[F15] ENGINEERING IN GENERAL -- F15 covers general engineering concepts and practices that apply across the mechanical engineering spectrum, including fundamental design principles, analysis methods, and common technical solutions not limited to a specific device or subsystem. It serves as a catch-all for inventions and innovations that address overall engineering problems rather than particular mechanical components.
[F15B] SYSTEMS ACTING BY MEANS OF FLUIDS IN GENERAL -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems transferring mechanical energy by means of a fluid under pressure using the principles of fluid statics or hydrostatics, i.e. hydraulic or pneumatic systems  **Glossary:** - Pneumatic: using air or an inert gas (except steam) as a pressure medium. - Hydraulic: using a liquid as the pressure medium. - Telemotor: system or device in which a substantially constant amount of fluid is trapped between an input member and an output member to act as a fluid link - Servomotor: fluid-pressure actuator, e.g. a piston and a cylinder, directly controlled by a valve or other device (e.g. pump) which is responsive to operation of an initial controlling member (e.g. joystick). The initial controlling member may be adjacent to the servomotor or at a distance and may be, e.g. a hand lever.
[F15B 1/00] Installations or systems with accumulators -- **Glossary:** - Accumulator: vessel for storing pressurised liquid (e.g. using a liquid and a gas chamber separated by a membrane or piston or using an elastic housing)
[F15B 1/02] Installations or systems with accumulators -- **Glossary:** - Accumulator: vessel for storing pressurised liquid (e.g. using a liquid and a gas chamber separated by a membrane or piston or using an elastic housing)
[F15B 1/021] used for damping -- This subclass covers installations or systems that use fluid accumulators primarily for damping, such as hydraulic shock absorbers, vibration isolators, or other fluid-based energy-dissipating devices. It includes designs where the accumulator absorbs kinetic energy and releases it gradually to reduce oscillations and control motion.
[F15B 1/022] used as an emergency power source, e.g. in case of pump failure -- A system or installation that incorporates accumulators to supply emergency power, for example when a pump fails, is covered by this subclass. It concerns fluid-based mechanisms that temporarily store energy to keep the equipment operating during a power loss.
[F15B 1/024] used as a supplementary power source, e.g. to store energy in idle periods to balance pump load -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Also for recuperation of hydraulic energy (as e.g. used in hydraulic excavators)
[F15B 1/025] used for thermal compensation, e.g. to collect expanded fluid and to return it to the system as the system fluid cools down -- F15B 1/025 covers devices that provide thermal compensation in fluid installations by collecting fluid that expands on heating and returning it to the system as it cools. Such mechanisms help maintain pressure and volume stability in pipelines, hydraulic circuits or other fluid-based systems.
[F15B 1/027] having accumulator charging devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. valves controlling flow of hydraulic fluid to and from the liquid chamber of an accumulator.
[F15B 1/0275] with two or more pilot valves, e.g. for independent setting of the cut-in and cut-out pressures -- The class covers fluid-accumulator installations that employ two or more pilot valves so that the cut-in pressure for charging and the cut-out pressure for discharging can be set independently, allowing separate control of the accumulator's start and stop thresholds in hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F15B 1/033] with electrical control means -- This subclass covers installations or systems that include accumulators whose operation is managed by electrical control devices such as sensors, switches, controllers or electronically actuated valves. It pertains to fluid-acting mechanical engineering systems where electrical means regulate the charging, discharging or pressure maintenance of the accumulator.
[F15B 1/04] Accumulators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Vessel for storing pressurised liquid (e.g. using a liquid and a gas chamber separated by a membrane or piston or using an elastic housing).
[F15B 1/045] Dead weight accumulators -- Dead-weight accumulators are hydraulic devices that store energy by using a column of fluid acting on a weighted piston or plate, allowing pressure to be maintained or released as the fluid moves. They smooth pressure fluctuations, provide standby power, or absorb shocks in fluid-power systems, relying on gravity for the acting force.
[F15B 1/08] using a gas cushion -- The code F15B 1/08 refers to installations or systems that employ a gas cushion as the energy-storage element. It covers fluid-based mechanical devices where a compressible gas accumulates to absorb shocks, provide spring action, or assist motion, and is situated under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, general engineering, and fluid-system technology.
[F15B 1/083] the accumulator having a fusible plug -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Accumulators with a safety plugs that melt at a certain temperature for relieving the pressure.
[F15B 1/086] the gas cushion being entirely enclosed by the separating means, e.g. foam or gas-filled balls -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Accumulators using bladders -> F15B1/165
[F15B 1/10] with flexible separating means -- F15B 1/10 refers to accumulators or similar fluid-power installations that employ flexible separating means, such as membranes, diaphragms or bellows, to divide the working fluid from a gas charge. The flexible element provides a seal while allowing volume change as the accumulator stores or releases energy, and the code is used for hydraulic and pneumatic systems that incorporate this type of separator.
[F15B 11/00] Servomotor systems without provision for follow-up action; -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Servomotor systems without provision for follow-up action and circuits therefor  **Glossary:** - Servomotor: fluid-pressure acutator, e.g. a piston and a cylinder, directly controlled by a valve or other device (e.g. pump) which is responsive to operation of an initial controlling member (e.g. joystick). The initial controlling member may be adjacent to the servomotor or at a distance and may be, e.g. a hand lever.
[F15B 11/003] Systems with load-holding valves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This class comprises releasable check valves and proportional braking valves.
[F15B 11/006] Hydraulic "Wheatstone bridge" circuits, i.e. with four nodes, P-A-T-B, and on-off or proportional valves in each link -- F15B 11/006 covers hydraulic Wheatstone bridge circuits, which use four interconnected nodes (P-A-T-B) and incorporate on-off or proportional valves in each branch to sense and control fluid pressure in servomotor systems lacking follow-up action.
[F15B 11/02] Systems essentially incorporating special features for controlling the speed or actuating force of an output member -- F15B 11/02 refers to servomotor systems that do not provide a follow-up action and incorporate special features for controlling the speed or actuating force of the output. These fluid-based mechanisms belong to the mechanical-engineering branch of systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 11/022] in which a rapid approach stroke is followed by a slower, high-force working stroke  F15B11/0325  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - fluid-pressure converters increasing the working force after an approach stroke -> F15B11/0325
[F15B 11/024] by means of differential connection of the servomotor lines, e.g. regenerative circuits -- F15B 11/024 covers servomotor systems that lack a follow-up mechanism and use a differential connection of the motor lines, such as regenerative circuits. This falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-actuated systems.
[F15B 11/028] for controlling the actuating force -- This CPC group refers to servo-motor systems that do not incorporate a feedback or follow-up action and are used to regulate the force applied by the actuator, placed within the broader classes of mechanical engineering, general engineering and fluid-power systems.
[F15B 1/103] the separating means being bellows -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  Fig. from US2004244857
[F15B 11/032] by means of fluid-pressure converters -- This subclass covers servomotor arrangements that operate without any feedback or follow-up mechanism and transmit motion through hydraulic or other fluid-pressure converters. It is situated within fluid-based system engineering under the broader mechanical engineering field.
[F15B 11/0325] the fluid-pressure converter increasing the working force after an approach stroke -- This CPC entry covers servomotor systems lacking follow-up action, specifically the fluid-pressure converter that boosts the output force after the piston's approach stroke. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and fluid-acting systems.
[F15B 11/036] by means of servomotors having a plurality of working chambers -- The class F15B 11/036 pertains to fluid-powered servomotor arrangements that employ several working chambers to produce motion, but do not include any follow-up or feedback action. It covers mechanical systems in which the plurality of chambers provides the driving force directly, without additional control loops, and is used for precise positioning or speed regulation in hydraulic or pneumatic applications.
[F15B 11/0365] Tandem constructions -- The classification F15B 11/0365 covers tandem constructions employed in servomotor systems that do not include any follow-up action. It refers to arrangements where two or more motor units are linked in series to deliver combined motion or force, often in fluid-driven or hydraulic actuation contexts. These constructions are used in mechanical engineering applications that operate without feedback or control loops.
[F15B 11/04] for controlling the speed -- F15B 11/04 covers speed control mechanisms for servomotor systems that operate without any follow-up action, typically using hydraulic or pneumatic power. The classification falls under mechanical engineering and applies to fluid-driven actuator arrangements where the motor speed is regulated by valves, throttles or other speed-adjusting devices.
[F15B 11/0406] during starting or stopping  F15B11/048  takes precedence -- F15B 11/0406 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to servomotor systems without follow-up action, specifically addressing aspects of operation during starting or stopping. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with mechanical engineering and fluid-based systems.
[F15B 11/0413] in one direction only, with no control in the reverse direction, e.g. check valve in parallel with a throttle valve -- The entry refers to servomotor systems that permit motion only in a single direction and provide no control for reverse motion, such as a check valve placed in parallel with a throttle valve. This configuration ensures one-way fluid actuation without follow-up action in the opposite direction.
[F15B 11/042] by means in the feed line -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Servomotor systems for controlling the speed of an output member, the speed depending on the position of the working member -> F15B11/046 - Servomotor systems specially adapted to maintain constant speed force of an output member -> F15B11/05
[F15B 11/0423] by controlling pump output or bypass, other than to maintain constant speed -- This CPC subclass deals with fluid-powered servomotor arrangements that lack a feedback or follow-up mechanism and regulate motion by varying the pump's output or by diverting flow, rather than by keeping the motor speed constant. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 11/0426] by controlling the number of pumps or parallel valves switched on -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. systems controlling the speed by means of so-called digital valves or by means of systems using pulse code modulation (PCM)
[F15B 11/044] by means in the return line -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Servomotor systems for controlling the speed of an output member, the speed depending on the position of the working member -> F15B11/046 - Servomotor systems specially adapted to maintain constant speed force of an output member -> F15B11/05
[F15B 11/0445] with counterbalance valves, e.g. to prevent overrunning or for braking -- This subclass covers fluid-powered servomotor arrangements that lack a feedback or follow-up mechanism but incorporate counterbalance valves to limit overspeed, prevent overrunning, or provide a braking function. Such valves are used to balance load forces and improve control in hydraulic or pneumatic actuation systems.
[F15B 11/046] depending on the position of the working member -- F15B 11/046 concerns servomotor systems that operate without follow-up action and whose control depends on the position of the working member. It includes arrangements where the actuator's output is varied or regulated according to the location of the moving part, typically using positional sensing rather than continuous feedback.
[F15B 11/048] with deceleration control -- F15B 11/048 covers fluid-power servomotor arrangements that lack any feedback or follow-up device yet incorporate a mechanism to control deceleration, such as braking or throttling, when the motor slows down. It is nested under mechanical engineering, general engineering and fluid-acting system categories.
[F15B 11/05] specially adapted to maintain constant speed, e.g. pressure-compensated, load-responsive -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - maintaining constant speed with sensing of servomotor demand and load, for two or more servomotors -> F15B11/161
[F15B 11/055] by adjusting the pump output or bypass -- This CPC subclass deals with servomotor systems that do not include a follow-up mechanism and control motion by varying the pump output or by using a bypass. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 1/106] characterised by the way housing components are assembled -- F15B 1/106 covers fluid-power installations that include accumulators and are distinguished by the specific method used to assemble the housing components of the system. The classification is nested under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, systems acting by means of fluids in general, and installations or systems with accumulators.
[F15B 11/06] involving features specific to the use of a compressible medium, e.g. air, steam -- F15B 11/06 covers servomotor systems that operate using a compressible medium such as air or steam, without follow-up action. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and fluid-based systems.
[F15B 11/064] with devices for saving the compressible medium -- The classification covers servomotor arrangements that lack an internal follow-up (feedback) function but include components such as accumulators, gas springs or other storage devices that retain the compressed fluid used to drive the motor, enabling the system to conserve or reuse the pressurised medium for improved efficiency and response.
[F15B 11/068] with valves for gradually putting pneumatic systems under pressure -- F15B 11/068 pertains to servomotor configurations that use valves to incrementally increase the pressure in pneumatic systems, operating without any follow-up or feedback control for motion or position.
[F15B 11/072] Combined pneumatic-hydraulic systems -- The F15B 11/072 subclass covers combined pneumatic-hydraulic servomotor arrangements that generate motion using both air and oil but do not include any follow-up or feedback mechanism. These systems are used where fluid-power actuation is required without closed-loop control, falling under mechanical engineering and fluid-system categories.
[F15B 11/0725] with the driving energy being derived from a pneumatic system, a subsequent hydraulic system displacing or controlling the output element -- F15B 11/0725 defines servomotor arrangements that generate motion from a pneumatic source and then transmit that energy to a hydraulic subsystem which moves or regulates the output element, without any feedback mechanism to adjust the motion in real time.
[F15B 11/076] with pneumatic drive or displacement and speed control or stopping by hydraulic braking -- This subclass covers servomotor systems that use a pneumatic actuator for movement and employ hydraulic braking to control speed or stop motion, without any feedback or follow-up mechanism. It falls under fluid-powered mechanical engineering categories in the CPC.
[F15B 11/08] with only one servomotor -- This subclass covers fluid-powered servomotor arrangements that employ a single servomotor and lack any mechanism for automatic follow-up or corrective action. It applies to systems where the motor directly controls a fluid actuator without feedback loops.
[F15B 11/10] in which the servomotor position is a function of the pressure -- The classification F15B 11/10 defines fluid-actuated servomotor systems where the motor's position is determined solely by the applied pressure, lacking any separate feedback or follow-up control. It falls under mechanical engineering topics dealing with general fluid-driven systems.
[F15B 11/12] providing distinct intermediate positions -- The CPC subclass F15B 11/12 covers servomotor systems that lack a follow-up action and specifies how to provide distinct intermediate positions within the motor's movement. It is situated under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 11/121] providing distinct intermediate positions  F15B11/13  takes precedence -- F15B 11/121 refers to servomotor systems that do not include a follow-up action mechanism but are capable of holding distinct intermediate positions, allowing the motor to stop at several predefined points instead of continuously tracking a signal. It is a narrower subclass of F15B 11/13, which covers servomotors without follow-up action in general.
[F15B 11/122] by means of actuators with multiple stops -- F15B 11/122 pertains to fluid-driven servomotor arrangements that lack continuous feedback control and achieve positioning by using actuators equipped with several preset stops. The design provides discrete movement steps rather than proportional or follow-up action.
[F15B 11/123] by means of actuators with fluid-operated stops -- F15B 11/123 pertains to servomotor systems that lack follow-up action and employ actuators equipped with fluid-operated stops. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-powered systems, and addresses the use of fluid-controlled stopping mechanisms to position or limit motion.
[F15B 11/125] by means of digital actuators, i.e. actuators in which the total stroke is the sum of individual strokes -- F15B 11/125 covers servomotor systems that lack follow-up action and use digital actuators, where the overall displacement results from a series of discrete strokes added together. These systems belong to the fluid-powered mechanical engineering group, specifically engineering in general and fluid-based system categories.
[F15B 11/126] by means of actuators of the standard type with special circuit controlling means  F15B11/125  takes precedence -- This subclass covers servomotor systems that operate without a follow-up action, where motion is produced by standard-type actuators directed by special control circuits. It is a refinement of F15B11/125 within fluid-powered system engineering.
[F15B 11/127] with step-by-step action -- The CPC subclass F15B 11/127 covers servomotor systems that operate with step-by-step action but lack any feedback or follow-up mechanism. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general, describing fluid-driven servomotors that move sequentially without closed-loop control.
[F15B 11/128] by means of actuators of the standard type with special circuit controlling means -- The subclass F15B 11/128 defines servomotor systems that operate without a feedback or follow-up mechanism and employ standard-type actuators together with a special control circuit. It covers hydraulic or pneumatic servos whose motion is directed solely by the circuit logic rather than by sensor-based correction, fitting within the broader category of fluid-based mechanical engineering systems.
[F15B 11/13] using chambers of predetermined volume -- This subclass covers servomotor systems that use chambers of a fixed predetermined volume to produce motion, without any feedback or follow-up control. The chambers are sized to deliver a set displacement or force when pressurised, enabling precise but open-loop operation.
[F15B 11/15] with special provision for automatic return -- F15B 11/15 refers to fluid-driven servomotor systems that incorporate a built-in mechanism to automatically return the output element to a reference position after actuation, eliminating the need for external follow-up control.
[F15B 11/16] with two or more servomotors -- F15B 11/16 covers servomotor systems that lack a follow-up action mechanism and employ two or more servomotors, typically used to control fluid-driven mechanisms such as valves or pumps. The class falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 11/161] with sensing of servomotor demand or load -- The entry refers to servomotor systems that do not incorporate automatic follow-up control but are equipped with a sensor that measures the motor's demand or load. It applies to mechanisms where the sensed load is used for monitoring or occasional adjustment rather than continuous feedback regulation.
[F15B 11/162] for giving priority to particular servomotors or users -- F15B 11/162 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to servomotor systems that prioritize specific servomotors or users, without follow-up action. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with fluid-based engineering systems.
[F15B 11/163] for sharing the pump output equally amongst users or groups of users, e.g. using anti-saturation, pressure compensation -- F15B 11/163 classifies hydraulic servomotor systems that operate without feedback or follow-up control and provide equal flow distribution to several users or user groups, employing techniques such as anti-saturation or pressure compensation to balance pump output across the loads.
[F15B 11/165] for adjusting the pump output or bypass in response to demand -- F15B 11/165 covers servomotor systems that lack a follow-up mechanism and are used to modify a pump's output or to operate a bypass line in line with changing demand, typically by adjusting flow or pressure automatically as the required performance varies. This falls under fluid-based mechanical engineering systems.
[F15B 11/166] Controlling a pilot pressure in response to the load, i.e. supply to at least one user is regulated by adjusting either the system pilot pressure or one or more of the individual pilot command pressures -- This CPC subclass F15B 11/166 covers servomotor hydraulic systems that regulate a pilot pressure in response to load changes, controlling the supply to a user by varying either the overall system pilot pressure or the individual pilot command pressures. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-powered systems.
[F15B 11/167] using pilot pressure to sense the demand -- This subclass covers servomotor systems that lack a follow-up mechanism and employ pilot pressure as a sensing signal to detect the demanded motion or force. The pilot-pressure feedback is used solely to sense demand, not to provide continuous corrective action.
[F15B 11/168] with an isolator valve (duplicating valve), i.e. at least one load sense [LS] pressure is derived from a work port load sense pressure but is not a work port pressure itself -- It covers hydraulic servomotor systems that lack follow-up control and incorporate an isolator or duplicating valve that provides a load-sense pressure derived from a work-port pressure, used for control but not as the actual work pressure.
[F15B 11/17] using two or more pumps -- F15B 11/17 concerns servomotor fluid-power arrangements that employ two or more pumps to supply the required pressure or flow, but do not include any follow-up or feedback action. The subclass addresses mechanical systems where multiple pumps are coordinated to drive a servomotor without closed-loop control.
[F15B 11/18] used in combination for obtaining stepwise operation of a single controlled member -- F15B 11/18 covers servomotor arrangements that lack feedback for continuous control and are combined to produce incremental motion of a single actuator. The classification is applied to systems that achieve stepwise positioning by linking multiple servomotors or using gear mechanisms to move one controlled member in discrete steps.
[F15B 11/183] Linear stepwise operation -- This class covers servomotor arrangements that produce linear motion in discrete steps without any feedback or automatic follow-up control. The devices are used in systems where motion is commanded stepwise, such as positioning or actuation mechanisms, and rely on fluid-driven actuation rather than continuous regulation. It falls under mechanical engineering, general engineering, and fluid-based system categories.
[F15B 11/186] Rotary stepwise operation -- F15B 11/186 covers rotary stepwise operation in servomotor systems that lack any follow-up action, meaning the motor moves in discrete angular increments without continuous control feedback. It falls under fluid-driven mechanical engineering for general fluid-actuated systems.
[F15B 1/12] attached at their periphery -- The subgroup addresses fluid-power installations in which one or more accumulators are mounted on the outer part of the system rather than inside the main housing, providing peripheral pressure regulation, energy storage, or shock-absorption functions.
[F15B 11/20] controlling several interacting or sequentially-operating members -- F15B 11/20 relates to control of multiple interacting or sequentially-operating members within servomotor systems that do not provide follow-up action. The subclass covers fluid-powered arrangements that coordinate the movement of several components to achieve a target function, such as hydraulic or pneumatic devices where the control logic synchronises successive or coupled actions.
[F15B 11/205] the position of the actuator controlling the fluid flow to the subsequent actuator -- F15B 11/205 concerns the spatial arrangement or setting of an actuator that governs the fluid flow to a downstream actuator in fluid-driven servomotor systems that do not include a follow-up or feedback mechanism. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically in the category of systems operating with fluids, and addresses how the controlling actuator is positioned to influence subsequent motion.
[F15B 11/22] Synchronisation of the movement of two or more servomotors -- This CPC subclass covers the coordination of two or more servomotors in fluid-driven mechanical systems, describing methods and devices that synchronize their motion without provision for follow-up action.
[F15B 1/125] characterised by the attachment means  F15B1/14  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass F15B 1/125 covers fluid-power installations or systems that include accumulators and are distinguished by the specific means used to attach those accumulators to the system, such as brackets, couplings or mounting structures. This subclass falls under the broader group for systems acting by means of fluids and inherits the general features of F15B 1/14, which must be read first.
[F15B 1/14] by means of a rigid annular supporting member -- F15B 1/14 classifies fluid-driven installations or systems with accumulators that incorporate a rigid annular supporting member, such as a circumferential ring or sleeve, to stabilize or guide moving components within the hydraulic or pneumatic circuit. The feature is used to enhance alignment or bearing capacity of the accumulator assembly.
[F15B 1/16] in the form of a tube -- F15B 1/16 classifies accumulator installations that are built as tubes, such as pipe-type accumulators used to store pressurised fluid. The code applies to fluid-storage systems where the storage element is a tubular container rather than a separate vessel or bladder.
[F15B 1/165] in the form of a bladder -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Accumulators using bladders, i.e. essentially cylindrical flexible separating means having a first open end portion which is normally rounded and a second end portion with a fluid inlet for separating a hydraulic fluid and a gas.  Fig. from US2006042707
[F15B 1/18] Anti-extrusion means -- F15B 1/18 covers anti-extrusion devices used in fluid systems with accumulators to prevent material from escaping the containment area. These means are employed in mechanical-engineering installations that store fluid pressure, such as hydraulic accumulators, and typically include seals, barriers or locking mechanisms.
[F15B 1/20] fixed to the separating means -- F15B 1/20 covers installations or systems that include accumulators in which a component is permanently attached to the separating element that divides the high-pressure and low-pressure chambers, such as a fixed piston, valve or membrane that remains stationary relative to the accumulator body.
[F15B 1/22] Liquid port constructions -- F15B 1/22 covers the design and arrangement of liquid ports used to introduce or discharge fluid in installations that contain accumulators, such as hydraulic or pneumatic storage tanks. It includes the shape, size, positioning and sealing features of inlet, outlet or vent connections that control the flow of liquid to and from the accumulator.
[F15B 1/24] with rigid separating means, e.g. pistons -- The subclass covers fluid-powered installations that incorporate accumulators using rigid separating elements such as pistons to separate the working fluid from a pressurised gas or spring. It pertains to hydraulic or pneumatic systems where the piston-type separator provides a defined, non-flexible barrier to store or release energy.
[F15B 1/26] Supply reservoir or sump assemblies -- The entry F15B 1/26 pertains to supply reservoir or sump assemblies used in fluid-powered installations, describing tanks, sumps and accumulator units that store and provide fluid to hydraulic or pneumatic systems, together with their associated piping and mounting arrangements.
[F15B 1/265] with pressurised main reservoir -- F15B 1/265 covers installations or systems that incorporate accumulators with a pressurised main reservoir, used to store fluid under pressure in hydraulic or pneumatic circuits for mechanical-engineering applications.
[F15B 13/00] Details of servomotor systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Components of hydraulic or pneumatic circuits such as valves and flow dividers.
[F15B 13/01] Locking-valves or other detent devices -- The CPC subclass F15B 13/01 covers locking-valves and other detent mechanisms used in servomotor systems that control fluid flow. It includes devices that retain a valve in a selected position without continuous power, such as mechanical catches or magnetic latches integrated into hydraulic or pneumatic actuators.
[F15B 13/015] using an enclosed pilot flow valve -- F15B 13/015 covers the use of an enclosed pilot flow valve within servomotor systems, detailing how the valve regulates fluid flow to control actuator motion. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, specifically systems acting by means of fluids in general, and addresses design features that isolate the pilot valve from the main fluid circuit.
[F15B 13/02] Fluid distribution or supply devices characterised by their adaptation to the control of servomotors -- F15B 13/02 denotes fluid-distribution or supply devices specially adapted for controlling servomotors, such as hydraulic or pneumatic circuits that regulate flow to drive precision servomotor systems. It is situated under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 13/021] Valves for interconnecting the fluid chambers of an actuator -- F15B 13/021 defines valves that interconnect the fluid chambers of an actuator, used in hydraulic or pneumatic servomotor systems to direct fluid between chambers and control motion. Such valves may be built into single or multiple chambers and are essential for bidirectional actuation and position regulation.
[F15B 13/022] Flow-dividers; Priority valves -- F15B 13/022 relates to flow-dividing devices and priority valves that control the distribution of fluid in hydraulic servomotor systems. These components direct fluid to multiple actuators or give precedence to a particular circuit, improving response and stability in fluid-powered mechanisms.
[F15B 13/023] Excess flow valves, e.g. for locking cylinders in case of hose burst -- F15B 13/023 concerns excess-flow valves that automatically limit fluid flow, such as devices that lock hydraulic cylinders when a hose bursts. These components are part of servomotor systems and fall under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-based systems. They protect equipment by detecting abnormal pressure drops and shutting off flow to prevent damage.
[F15B 13/024] Pressure relief valves -- Pressure relief valves are safety components that automatically open to discharge excess fluid pressure, preventing damage to hydraulic or pneumatic servomotor systems. They are designed to sense a preset pressure threshold and vent flow when that limit is exceeded, thereby protecting actuators and associated equipment from overload.
[F15B 13/025] Pressure reducing valves -- F15B 13/025 covers pressure reducing valves, which are components of servomotor systems that lower fluid pressure to a desired value, used in hydraulic circuits to control flow and protect downstream equipment.
[F15B 13/026] Pressure compensating valves -- F15B 13/026 covers pressure compensating valves used in hydraulic servomotor systems to automatically adjust flow and keep a set downstream pressure despite load changes. These devices typically employ springs or diaphragms that modulate the valve opening, ensuring stable actuator performance.
[F15B 13/027] Check valves -- F15B 13/027 designates check valves - one-way flow devices that automatically close to prevent backflow - as they are employed in fluid-driven servomotor systems. The code falls under mechanical engineering, specifically within engineering in general and systems acting by means of fluids. It covers the structure, operation and integration of check valves in such hydraulic or pneumatic actuator arrangements.
[F15B 13/028] Shuttle valves -- Shuttle valves are fluid-control components that connect a single output to one of two input lines, automatically passing the higher-pressure source while preventing backflow from the lower-pressure side. They are employed in servomotor and hydraulic circuits to switch power sources or protect the system from unwanted pressure changes.
[F15B 13/029] Counterbalance valves -- Counterbalance valves are hydraulic or pneumatic devices that regulate pressure to maintain a constant load or to halt motion when the load exceeds a set limit, providing safety and precise control in servomotor actuator systems. They fall under CPC code F15B 13/029, covering details of servomotor systems in fluid-acting mechanisms.
[F15B 13/04] for use with a single servomotor -- F15B 13/04 covers servomotor systems designed to operate with a single servomotor, including the mechanical arrangement, control linkages and power transmission that enable precise motion control in fluid-based engineering applications. It is used for devices where one motor provides the complete actuation function.
[F15B 13/0401] Valve members; Fluid interconnections therefor -- F15B 13/0401 covers valve elements and the fluid-flow connections that link them, specifically in servomotor systems used for precise motion control. It includes the design of valve bodies, ports, actuating mechanisms and associated piping that direct hydraulic or pneumatic fluids to achieve controlled movement.
[F15B 13/0402] for linearly sliding valves, e.g. spool valves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  Fig. from WO2008015752

This sub-group is superseded by the more specific F15B 13/0418, which refines the arrangement and operation of the secondary valve member.
[F15B 13/0405] for seat valves, i.e. poppet valves -- The CPC subclass F15B 13/0405 covers servomotor-driven seat or poppet valves, describing mechanisms where a servo actuator positions the valve seat to control fluid flow. It pertains to hydraulic or pneumatic systems in which the valve is actuated by a servo motor for precise flow regulation.
[F15B 13/0406] for rotary valves -- F15B 13/0406 covers rotary valves used in servomotor systems that control fluid flow. It falls within the mechanical engineering field, specifically systems acting by means of fluids, and relates to detailed aspects of servomotor operation. The classification addresses design, function, and integration of rotary valve mechanisms in fluid-powered actuation.
[F15B 13/0407] Means for damping the valve member movement -- F15B 13/0407 defines means for damping the movement of valve members in servomotor systems, such as hydraulic or pneumatic valves, using devices that absorb shock, limit overshoot, or control actuation speed. The subclass resides in the mechanical engineering section under engineering in general, systems acting by means of fluids, and details of servomotor systems.
[F15B 13/0416] with means or adapted for load sensing -- This subclass pertains to servomotor systems that incorporate load-sensing mechanisms, such as torque or force sensors, enabling the motor control to adjust speed, torque or other parameters in response to the detected load. It covers designs where load information is used to modulate performance.
[F15B 13/0417] Load sensing elements; Internal fluid connections therefor; Anti-saturation or pressure-compensation valves -- The entry covers hydraulic or pneumatic load-sensing elements used in servomotor systems, the internal fluid passages that connect them, and valves designed to prevent saturation or provide pressure compensation. These components enable precise control of fluid power by adjusting flow or pressure in response to load changes.
[F15B 13/0418] Load sensing elements sliding within a hollow main valve spool -- F15B 13/0418 concerns load-sensing components that move inside a hollow main valve spool of a fluid-driven servomotor. The sliding elements detect pressure or force changes and transmit this information to control the valve, enabling precise regulation of hydraulic or pneumatic power.
[F15B 13/042] operated by fluid pressure -- A servomotor system that derives its motion from fluid pressure, typically hydraulic or pneumatic, falls under CPC F15B 13/042. This subclass includes designs where fluid power is used to drive the actuator, control the speed or position of the motor, and provide precise mechanical output in various engineering applications.
[F15B 13/0422] with manually-operated pilot valves, e.g. joysticks -- This subclass defines servomotor arrangements that use manually-operated pilot valves, such as joysticks, to direct the fluid flow that drives the motor. It applies to hydraulic or pneumatic actuation systems where the operator directly controls the pilot valve.
[F15B 13/0424] the joysticks being provided with electrical switches or sensors -- F15B 13/0424 covers joysticks that incorporate electrical switches or sensors as part of servomotor systems that operate through fluid power. The classification applies to control devices where the joystick's position is detected or actuated electrically, enabling precise command of hydraulic or pneumatic mechanisms.
[F15B 13/0426] with fluid-operated pilot valves, i.e. multiple stage valves -- The F15B 13/0426 subclass concerns servomotor systems that employ fluid-operated pilot valves, i.e., multiple-stage valves, to control motion. [F15B 13/043] with electrically-controlled pilot valves -- F15B 13/043 covers servomotor systems that incorporate pilot valves whose operation is governed by electrical control signals. [F15B 13/0431] the electrical control resulting in an on-off function -- F15B 13/0431 relates to the electrical control that provides an on-off function in fluid-powered servomotor systems, covering the circuitry or switching mechanisms used to start and stop the motor based on electrical signals.
[F15B 13/0433] the pilot valves being pressure control valves  F15B13/0435 ,  F15B13/0436 ,  F15B13/0438  take precedence -- This subclass covers pilot valves that act as pressure control valves within servomotor systems, regulating fluid pressure to control actuator motion. [F15B 13/0436] the pilot valves being of the steerable jet type -- This subclass deals with pilot valves that use a steerable jet to direct fluid flow, allowing precise control of hydraulic or pneumatic servomotor systems. The design typically employs a movable nozzle that creates a fluid jet which can be angled to open or close the main valve.
[F15B 13/0438] the pilot valves being of the nozzle-flapper type -- This subclass covers servomotor systems that use pilot valves of the nozzle-flapper type, where fluid flow is regulated by a moving flapper positioned near a nozzle opening. It relates to fluid-actuated control mechanisms within broader mechanical engineering and fluid-system categories.
[F15B 13/044] operated by electrically-controlled means, e.g. solenoids, torque-motors -- F15B 13/044 covers servomotor systems that are driven by electrically-controlled devices such as solenoids or torque-motors, where the electrical actuation regulates the movement of fluid-driven mechanisms.
[F15B 13/0442] with proportional solenoid allowing stable intermediate positions -- This subclass covers servomotor mechanisms that incorporate a proportional solenoid to hold the rotor at selectable intermediate positions rather than only fully on or off. The solenoid provides a continuously variable magnetic force, enabling precise positioning and stable hold without external feedback.
[F15B 13/0444] with rotary electric motor -- F15B 13/0444 covers servomotor arrangements where the drive is provided by a rotary electric motor, used to control fluid-powered mechanisms. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically systems acting by means of fluids and the detailed design of servomotor systems.
[F15B 13/0446] with moving coil, e.g. voice coil -- F15B 13/0446 defines servomotor arrangements that generate motion using a moving coil within a magnetic field, such as voice-coil actuators, and covers the mechanical configuration of the coil and magnet that produces linear or rotary movement.
[F15B 13/06] for use with two or more servomotors -- The subclass F15B 13/06 covers fluid-actuated servomotor arrangements that employ two or more servomotors working together, often to share load, increase torque, or provide coordinated motion. It includes designs where the servomotors are mechanically linked or controlled in unison to achieve higher precision or power in hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F15B 13/07] in distinct sequence -- F15B 13/07 covers servomotor arrangements in which fluid power is delivered to the actuators in a defined order rather than simultaneously. The classification includes sequential valve networks, timing mechanisms and control circuits that cause each motor to engage one after another, enabling stepwise motion or staged operation in hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F15B 13/08] Assemblies of units, each for the control of a single servomotor only -- This class concerns assemblies made up of separate control modules, each designed to operate only one servomotor, typically employed in mechanical systems that manipulate fluids. The units are independent, providing individual feedback and drive for their respective motor, enabling precise coordination of multiple actuators within a larger fluid-based mechanism.
[F15B 13/0803] Modular units -- F15B 13/0803 pertains to modular units used in fluid-driven servomotor systems. The code covers interchangeable or pre-assembled modules that form part of the motion-control arrangement, allowing easy integration, reconfiguration or replacement within hydraulic or pneumatic servomechanisms.
[F15B 13/0807] Manifolds -- Manifolds are fluid distribution components used in servomotor systems to direct hydraulic or pneumatic fluid between pumps, valves and actuators, ensuring controlled flow and pressure for precise motion control. They may include chambers, ports and internal passages designed to balance flow, reduce pressure loss and protect the system from contamination. This classification covers the design, arrangement and functional aspects of such manifolds in fluid-driven servomechanisms.
[F15B 13/081] Laminated constructions -- F15B 13/081 pertains to laminated constructions used in servomotor systems, such as layered magnetic or structural components that enhance performance, heat management or mechanical rigidity. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-actuated systems, and addresses detailed design aspects of these laminated assemblies.
[F15B 13/0814] Monoblock manifolds -- F15B 13/0814 covers monoblock manifolds used in servomotor systems, where the manifold body and adjoining components are formed as a single piece to distribute fluid to multiple actuators. The classification pertains to fluid-powered mechanical engineering applications involving integrated manifold designs that simplify routing and reduce leakage.
[F15B 13/0817] Multiblock manifolds -- Multiblock manifolds are fluid-distribution structures composed of several interconnected manifold sections that form a single block, used to manage flow and pressure within servomotor hydraulic systems. The arrangement improves routing flexibility and performance of fluid-actuated mechanisms, and the classification covers their design, integration and functional details.
[F15B 13/0821] Attachment or sealing of modular units to each other -- The CPC subclass F15B 13/0821 covers methods and devices for attaching or sealing modular units together within servomotor or fluid-actuated systems, focusing on the interfaces that connect separate components to form a unified mechanism, often to prevent leakage or misalignment.
[F15B 13/0825] the modular elements being mounted on a common member, e.g. on a rail -- F15B 13/0825 covers servomotor fluid-power systems where individual modular elements are mounted on a common supporting member such as a rail, allowing interchangeable or scalable configurations. [F15B 13/0828] characterised by sealing means of the modular units -- F15B 13/0828 falls within the mechanical engineering section dealing with fluid-driven systems and specifically covers servomotor assemblies where the individual modular components are sealed against fluid leakage. [F15B 13/0832] Modular valves -- F15B 13/0832 covers modular valves used in servomotor systems that control fluid flow by interchangeable valve modules, allowing flexible configuration and maintenance of hydraulic or pneumatic actuation units. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 13/0835] Cartridge type valves -- Cartridge type valves are modular valve assemblies that can be inserted into a housing or block, providing a compact, replaceable unit for controlling fluid flow in servomotor and other fluid-power systems. They combine the valve body, seat and stem in a single sealed cartridge, enabling quick maintenance and standardized performance.
[F15B 13/0839] Stacked plate type valves -- F15B 13/0839 covers stacked plate type valves, which use a series of interleaved plates that shift relative to each other to create variable flow passages. These valves are employed in servomotor systems for fine control of fluid power in hydraulic or pneumatic mechanisms, allowing precise regulation of pressure and flow rate.
[F15B 13/0842] Monoblock type valves, e.g. with multiple valve spools in a common housing -- Monoblock type valves integrate two or more valve spools within a single housing, allowing compact construction and shared fluid passages for hydraulic or pneumatic servomotor systems. This design reduces the number of separate components, simplifies assembly, and improves reliability by minimizing external connections between individual valve units.
[F15B 13/0846] Electrical details -- F15B 13/0846 pertains to the electrical aspects of servomotor systems, including wiring, power supply, control electronics, and signal processing that drive fluid-powered actuators. [F15B 13/0871] Channels for fluid -- F15B 13/0871 pertains to the design and arrangement of fluid channels used in servomotor systems, covering ducts, pipes, passages and other conduits that direct hydraulic or pneumatic fluid to the moving elements of the motor. [F15B 13/10] Special arrangements for operating the actuated device without using fluid pressure, e.g. for emergency use -- F15B 13/10 covers special arrangements that let a servomotor or other actuated device operate without using fluid pressure, typically for emergency or backup use. These configurations provide a non-fluid fallback in systems that normally rely on fluid-driven actuation.

[F15B 13/16] Special measures for feedback -- F15B 13/16 covers special feedback mechanisms used in servomotor systems, such as sensors, control loops and correction devices that adjust motion based on measured output. [F15B 15/00] Fluid-actuated devices for displacing a member from one position to another -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hydraulic or pneumatic actuators with linear or non-continuous rotary output.
[F15B 15/02] Mechanical layout characterised by the means for converting the movement of the fluid-actuated element into movement of the finally-operated member -- F15B 15/02 covers mechanical arrangements that convert the motion of a fluid-driven actuator into the movement of the final work element, such as linkages, cams or gear trains that translate hydraulic or pneumatic displacement into linear or rotary output. This subclass falls within fluid-actuated devices for displacing a member, under the broader categories of systems acting by means of fluids.
[F15B 15/04] with oscillating cylinder -- This class covers mechanisms where a fluid-powered cylinder repeatedly moves back and forth, producing an oscillating motion that displaces a linked component. Typical applications include reciprocating pumps, pistons, and vibration generators that rely on hydraulic or pneumatic actuation.
[F15B 15/06] for mechanically converting rectilinear movement into non- rectilinear movement -- F15B 15/06 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers fluid-actuated devices that convert straight-line motion into curved or rotational motion, typically used in mechanical engineering systems.
[F15B 15/061] by unidirectional means -- F15B 15/061 covers fluid-actuated mechanisms that move a component in only one direction, typically using one-way valves, check valves or ratchet-type arrangements to permit motion forward while preventing return. Examples include hydraulic cylinders that extend but lock when the fluid pressure is removed, or pneumatic pistons that drive a load unidirectionally and then hold position without reverse flow.
[F15B 15/063] Actuator having both linear and rotary output, i.e. dual action actuator -- F15B 15/063 classifies actuators that generate both linear and rotary motion from a single fluid-driven source, providing dual-action output to move a member in translation and rotation.
[F15B 15/065] the motor being of the rack-and-pinion type -- F15B 15/065 classifies fluid-actuated devices whose motor employs a rack-and-pinion mechanism to convert rotary motion into linear displacement, typically used in hydraulic or pneumatic systems for moving a member between positions. The entry is part of the hierarchy covering mechanical engineering, engineering in general and fluid-driven systems.
[F15B 15/066] the motor being of the scotch yoke type -- This subclass describes fluid-actuated devices that employ a motor using the Scotch-yoke mechanism, in which a rotating shaft drives a reciprocating member through a slot-and-pin arrangement, converting rotary motion into linear displacement for hydraulic or pneumatic actuators.
[F15B 15/068] the motor being of the helical type -- F15B 15/068 classifies fluid-actuated devices that use a helical-type motor to produce linear motion, such as screws or augers driven by hydraulic or pneumatic power. The code falls under mechanical engineering and covers mechanisms that displace a member from one position to another by converting fluid energy into rotary-to-linear motion.
[F15B 15/08] Characterised by the construction of the motor unit -- F15B 15/08 belongs to the CPC class for fluid-actuated devices that move a member between positions and is defined by the particular construction of the motor unit that drives the motion, such as specific hydraulic or pneumatic motor arrangements.
[F15B 15/082] the motor being of the slotted cylinder type -- F15B 15/082 covers fluid-actuated devices that move a member between positions, specifically motors built as slotted cylinders. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems using fluids, describing the configuration and operation of a slotted-cylinder type hydraulic or pneumatic motor.
[F15B 15/084] the motor being of the rodless piston type, e.g. with cable, belt or chain -- F15B 15/084 covers fluid-actuated motors that employ a rodless piston arrangement, where the piston moves within a cavity without a connecting rod and transmits motion via a cable, belt or chain. This type of motor is used in systems that shift a component from one position to another, providing linear drive without the bulk of a traditional rod-linked piston.
[F15B 15/086] with magnetic coupling -- This CPC subclass classifies fluid-actuated devices that move a member by transmitting power through a magnetic coupling, allowing the driving fluid mechanism and the driven load to remain sealed from each other. It applies to hydraulic or pneumatic actuators where magnetic fields convey force without direct mechanical contact.
[F15B 15/088] the motor using combined actuation, e.g. electric and fluid actuation -- F15B 15/088 covers motors that combine electric and fluid power to produce motion, using both energy sources simultaneously to move a member from one position to another. It belongs to the fluid-actuated devices for displacing a member category within the broader mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F15B 15/10] the motor being of diaphragm type -- F15B 15/10 covers fluid-actuated displacement devices whose motor uses a diaphragm to convert pressure into motion. The diaphragm expands and contracts under fluid pressure, driving a mechanical member from one position to another, and is applied in pumps, actuators and similar hydraulic or pneumatic systems.

[F15B 15/1404] in clusters, e.g. multiple cylinders in one block -- F15B 15/1404 concerns fluid-actuated devices that displace a member by using several cylinders grouped together in a single block, such as clustered hydraulic or pneumatic actuators designed for coordinated motion.
[F15B 15/1409] with two or more independently movable working pistons -- This subclass covers fluid-driven actuators that move a component using two or more pistons that can operate independently of each other. [F15B 15/1414] with non-rotatable piston -- This CPC subclass covers fluid-actuated devices that move a load by linear displacement of a member using a piston that is fixed against rotation. [F15B 15/1428] Cylinders  F15B15/1438  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass F15B 15/1428 covers fluid-actuated cylinders used to move a member from one position to another, such as hydraulic or pneumatic pistons and rod assemblies. [F15B 15/1466] Hollow piston sliding over a stationary rod inside the cylinder -- A hollow piston that moves along a fixed rod within a cylinder, using fluid pressure to transfer motion, is covered by this subclass. [F15B 15/1471] Guiding means other than in the end cap  F15B15/1466  takes precedence -- The subclass F15B 15/1471 defines guiding components used in fluid-actuated devices that move a member, where the guides are positioned outside the end cap, such as guide rails, pins or sleeves that constrain motion.
