[F15B 15/1476] Special return means -- Special return means are mechanisms that automatically restore a displaced component to its original position after operation, using fluid pressure, spring force, or a combination of both. They are applied in hydraulic or pneumatic actuators to provide reliable reset without external control.
[F15B 15/148] Lost-motion means between the piston and the output -- F15B 15/148 covers fluid-actuated devices that incorporate a lost-motion mechanism between a piston and the driven member, allowing relative movement or a delay before power is transferred to the output. Typical solutions include couplings, levers, or gear arrangements that provide slip, take-up, or controlled disengagement to accommodate positional differences or reduce shock loads. This subclass falls under fluid-actuated devices for displacing a member from one position to another.
[F15B 15/1485] Special measures for cooling or heating -- The F15B 15/1485 subclass deals with fluid-actuated devices that move a component and incorporate special measures to control temperature, such as cooling or heating of the moved member or the working fluid during operation.
[F15B 15/149] Fluid interconnections, e.g. fluid connectors, passages -- F15B 15/149 covers fluid interconnections including connectors, couplings and passages that link fluid-actuated components to transmit pressure or flow for moving a member. It addresses the design and arrangement of such connections in hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid power systems.
[F15B 15/16] of the telescopic type -- F15B 15/16 covers fluid-actuated telescopic devices that extend or retract a member in a series of nested stages, such as hydraulic or pneumatic telescopic cylinders used for linear motion control. These mechanisms provide high stroke length in a compact form by sequentially unfolding the sections under fluid pressure.
[F15B 15/165] with synchronisation of sections -- F15B 15/165 covers fluid-actuated devices that move a component from one position to another while synchronising the motion of multiple sections. It applies to mechanisms such as hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders, pistons or similar movers where coordinated displacement of parts is required.
[F15B 15/17] of differential-piston type -- The F15B 15/17 subclass covers fluid-actuated devices that move a component by means of a differential-piston arrangement, where two pistons of differing areas create a net force to shift the member from one position to another. It falls under fluid-actuated displacement mechanisms within broader mechanical and engineering categories.
[F15B 15/18] Combined units comprising both motor and pump -- This subclass covers integrated assemblies that combine a fluid motor and a pump in a single unit, providing both power generation and fluid displacement in one package. It includes designs where the motor drives the pump or the pump drives the motor, enabling compact fluid-power systems for moving a member from one position to another.
[F15B 15/19] Pyrotechnical actuators -- F15B 15/19 covers pyrotechnical actuators, which are fluid-actuated devices that use the rapid expansion of gases from a pyrotechnic charge to move a member from one position to another, often for rapid release or safety functions in mechanical systems. This subclass falls under fluid-actuated devices for displacing a member within the broader category of mechanical engineering.
[F15B 15/20] Other details -- F15B 15/20 groups miscellaneous technical information concerning fluid-actuated devices that displace a member, covering features such as sealing arrangements, mounting configurations, control elements and other ancillary details not defined in more specific sub-groups. It captures the supplemental aspects of hydraulic or pneumatic actuators that facilitate movement but fall outside the primary functional categories.
[F15B 15/202] Externally-operated valves mounted in or on the actuator -- This subclass covers valves that are attached to or integrated into the actuator of a fluid-actuated mechanism and are operated from outside the actuator housing. Such externally-operated valves control the supply or release of fluid to move a component from one position to another. They are used in hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid-driven systems.
[F15B 15/204] Control means for piston speed or actuating force without external control, e.g. control valve inside the piston  F15B11/02 ,  F15B15/22  take precedence -- F15B 15/204 covers internal control mechanisms that regulate piston speed or actuating force without an external controller, such as a valve incorporated inside the piston itself. These features are applied in fluid-actuated devices that move a member from one position to another.
[F15B 15/22] for accelerating or decelerating the stroke -- F15B 15/22 covers fluid-actuated devices that move a component and include features for controlling the speed of the moving member, such as mechanisms that accelerate or decelerate the stroke. Typical implementations use hydraulic or pneumatic pistons with cushions, throttles or variable-area chambers to modify motion timing within the stroke.
[F15B 15/221] for accelerating the stroke, e.g. by area increase -- The subclass covers fluid-actuated devices that speed up the movement of a member by increasing the effective area during the stroke, such as variable-area pistons or tapered cylinders that boost acceleration through larger pressure surfaces. It relates to mechanisms that accelerate displacement using fluid pressure.
[F15B 15/222] having a piston with a piston extension or piston recess which throttles the main fluid outlet as the piston approaches its end position -- This subclass defines fluid-actuated devices where the piston includes an extension or recess that progressively throttles the main fluid outlet as the piston nears its end position, providing controlled deceleration or cushioning of the moving member.
[F15B 15/223] having a piston with a piston extension or piston recess which completely seals the main fluid outlet as the piston approaches its end position -- The subclass covers fluid-actuated devices where, as the piston reaches its final stroke, a piston extension or a recess in the piston fully blocks the main fluid outlet, providing a complete seal of the flow path at the end position.
[F15B 15/224] having a piston which closes off fluid outlets in the cylinder bore by its own movement -- F15B 15/224 pertains to fluid-actuated devices that employ a piston which, by its own movement, closes off fluid outlets in the cylinder bore, thereby displacing a member from one position to another. It falls under the subclass of fluid-actuated systems for member displacement within mechanical engineering.
[F15B 15/225] with valve stems operated by contact with the piston end face or with the cylinder wall -- This subclass covers hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders where the valve stems that control fluid flow are actuated directly by the moving piston end face or by contact with the cylinder wall, allowing automatic reversal or positioning. It belongs to fluid-actuated devices for moving a member and refines the broader category of systems acting by means of fluids.
[F15B 15/226] having elastic elements, e.g. springs, rubber pads -- This CPC subclass F15B 15/226 covers fluid-actuated devices that move a member by means of elastic elements such as springs or rubber pads. It is situated within mechanical engineering, under systems using fluids to displace components.
[F15B 15/227] having an auxiliary cushioning piston within the main piston or the cylinder end face -- A fluid-actuated cylinder that incorporates an auxiliary cushioning piston either inside the main piston or mounted on the cylinder end face, used to absorb shocks and moderate the motion of the rod during extension or retraction. This feature provides smoother operation and protects the system from impact loads in hydraulic or pneumatic actuators.
[F15B 15/228] having shock absorbers mounted outside the actuator housing -- The subclass F15B 15/228 refers to fluid-actuated devices that incorporate shock absorbers mounted externally to the actuator housing, providing damping for the moving member while it is displaced between positions. This arrangement is used in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders to control impact and vibration without increasing the size of the main actuator body.
[F15B 15/24] for restricting the stroke -- This CPC subclass covers fluid-actuated mechanisms that limit the travel distance of a moving component, such as hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders with stroke-limiting devices, used to control positioning in various engineering applications. It falls under the broader group of fluid-actuated devices for displacing members within mechanical engineering.
[F15B 15/26] Locking mechanisms -- F15B 15/26 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers locking mechanisms used in fluid-actuated devices designed to displace a member from one position to another. These mechanisms ensure that the device remains securely in place when not actively being moved by hydraulic or pneumatic pressure.
[F15B 15/261] using positive interengagement, e.g. balls and grooves, for locking in the end positions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locking mechanisms using positive interengagement for locking in any distinct position (not restricted to locking in the end positions).
[F15B 15/262] using friction, e.g. brake pads -- F15B 15/262 covers fluid-actuated devices that move a component by employing friction, such as brake pads that convert hydraulic or pneumatic pressure into a retarding force to shift a member from one position to another. The classification falls within mechanical engineering, specifically systems operating with fluids to produce displacement through frictional contact.
[F15B 15/264] Screw mechanisms attached to the piston -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This subgroup contains documents relating to locking mechanisms using screw mechanisms attached to the piston but not using friction.
[F15B 15/265] specially adapted for rodless pistons or slotted cylinders -- F15B 15/265 concerns fluid-actuated devices that are specially adapted for rodless pistons or slotted cylinders, enabling the displacement of a member between positions without a conventional piston rod. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids.
[F15B 15/28] Means for indicating the position, e.g. end of stroke -- The class covers devices that give a visual, tactile or electronic signal of the displacement limit of a fluid-driven actuator, such as stroke-end markers, limit switches, position displays or indicators that show when a piston, cylinder or similar member has reached its maximum or minimum travel. It is used in hydraulic or pneumatic systems to monitor or control motion.
[F15B 15/2807] Position switches, i.e. means for sensing of discrete positions only, e.g. limit switches -- The entry F15B 15/2807 covers position-switches that sense only discrete positions of a moving member, such as limit or end switches used to indicate when a component has reached a predefined location. These devices may employ mechanical, electrical, magnetic or optical means to generate a signal when the member arrives at the set point, and are applied in fluid-actuated systems for control and safety functions.
[F15B 15/2815] Position sensing, i.e. means for continuous measurement of position, e.g. LVDT -- This CPC subclass F15B 15/2815 covers mechanisms that continuously sense the position of a moving part, such as linear variable differential transformers, within fluid-actuated devices that displace a member from one location to another. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, general engineering, and systems using fluids for actuation.
[F15B 15/2823] by a screw mechanism attached to the piston -- The CPC class F15B 15/2823 covers fluid-actuated devices where the piston's motion is converted into linear displacement by a screw mechanism attached to the piston. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, systems acting by means of fluids, focusing on screw-driven fluid actuators.
[F15B 15/283] using a cable wrapped on a drum and attached to the piston -- F15B 15/283 refers to fluid-actuated devices that move a piston by means of a cable wound on a drum and attached to the piston, allowing the piston to be displaced from one position to another using hydraulic or pneumatic pressure. This arrangement is classified under fluid-actuated mechanisms within mechanical engineering.
[F15B 15/2838] with out using position sensors, e.g. by volume flow measurement or pump speed -- This CPC subclass pertains to fluid-actuated devices that move a member without using position sensors, instead determining displacement by measuring the volumetric flow of the fluid or the speed of the pump.
[F15B 15/2846] using detection of markings, e.g. markings on the piston rod -- This subclass covers fluid-actuated displacement devices that use detection of markings on the piston rod, such as visual or sensor-based identification of engraved or painted lines, to determine rod position or movement for control or feedback.
[F15B 15/2853] using potentiometers -- F15B 15/2853 covers fluid-actuated mechanisms that move a component from one position to another and incorporate a potentiometer to sense or control the displacement. It addresses devices where hydraulic or pneumatic power is combined with electrical position feedback for precise movement. This subclass falls under fluid-actuated devices for member displacement within mechanical engineering.
[F15B 15/2861] using magnetic means -- This subclass pertains to fluid-powered actuators that displace a member by employing magnetic forces, such as electromagnets or permanent magnets that attract or repel the moving part during operation. It falls under fluid-actuated devices for moving a member within the broader categories of mechanical engineering and systems acting by means of fluids.
[F15B 15/2869] using electromagnetic radiation, e.g. radar or microwaves -- F15B 15/2869 pertains to fluid-actuated mechanisms that employ electromagnetic radiation, such as radar or microwaves, to sense, guide or control the displacement of a member from one position to another.
[F15B 15/2876] using optical means, e.g. laser -- F15B 15/2876 classifies fluid-actuated devices that use optical means, for example lasers, to move a member from one position to another. It lies within mechanical engineering under systems acting by fluids and specifically covers displacement mechanisms that employ light-based energy for actuation.
[F15B 15/2884] using sound, e.g. ultrasound -- This subclass relates to fluid-actuated devices that use acoustic energy, such as ultrasound, to displace a member from one position to another. The method relies on sound-generated pressure waves in a fluid to produce the required movement.
[F15B 15/2892] characterised by the attachment means -- The subclass F15B 15/2892 covers fluid-actuated devices that move a member from one position to another where the defining feature is the way the moving element is attached to the drive or support structure, such as specific brackets, joints, couplings or other attachment means that determine the device's operation.
[F15B 17/00] Combinations of telemotor and servomotor systems -- F15B 17/00 pertains to arrangements that integrate telemotor devices with servomotor mechanisms for remote or feedback-controlled fluid-power transmission. It covers combined systems where a hydraulic telemotor is coupled to a servomotor to achieve precise positioning or speed regulation in hydraulic circuits.
[F15B 17/02] in which a telemotor operates the control member of a servomotor -- F15B 17/02 covers arrangements where a telemotor-a remote-operated hydraulic or pneumatic actuator-drives the control element of a servomotor, integrating the power transmission of the telemotor with the precise positioning function of the servomotor. This combination is used in fluid-powered systems to achieve accurate remote control of mechanical devices.
[F15B 18/00] Parallel arrangements of independent servomotor systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. redundant systems.  Fig. from DE1940946A1
[F15B 19/00] Testing; fluid-pressure systems or apparatus not otherwise provided for -- F15B 19/00, placed under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering in General → Systems Acting by Means of Fluids in General, addresses methods and devices for testing fluid-pressure systems or apparatus that are not otherwise provided for in the classification.
[F15B 19/002] Calibrating -- F15B 19/02 relates to calibrating fluid-pressure testing equipment, covering methods and devices used to adjust and verify the accuracy of pressure measurement systems in mechanical-engineering tests. It is applied to apparatus that generate, transmit or measure fluid pressure where precise calibration is required.
[F15B 19/005] Fault detection or monitoring -- F15B 19/005 covers methods and devices for detecting faults or monitoring performance in fluid-pressure testing systems, such as those used to assess the integrity and operation of hydraulic or pneumatic apparatus. It includes sensors, diagnostic techniques, and control schemes that identify leaks, pressure deviations, or component failures during testing. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, focusing on systems that employ fluids for functional evaluation.
[F15B 19/007] Simulation or modelling -- The CPC code F15B 19/007 refers to the use of simulation or modelling techniques to evaluate fluid-pressure systems or testing apparatus that are not covered elsewhere. It includes computer-based analysis, virtual prototypes and mathematical modelling of hydraulic or pneumatic test rigs to predict performance and behaviour.
[F15B 20/00] Safety arrangements for fluid actuator systems -- The CPC class F15B 20/00 covers safety devices and protective measures used in fluid-actuator systems, such as pressure relief valves, emergency shut-off mechanisms and overload protection. It pertains to mechanical engineering designs that control the safe operation of hydraulic or pneumatic actuators within broader fluid-powered system categories.
[F15B 20/001] Double valve requiring the use of both hands simultaneously -- F15B 20/001 covers double valves that must be operated with both hands at the same time, a safety measure for fluid-actuated systems. The dual-hand requirement prevents inadvertent opening or closing, enhancing control of hydraulic or pneumatic actuators in mechanical engineering applications.
[F15B 20/002] Electrical failure -- F15B 20/002 covers safety measures that address electrical failures in fluid-actuator systems, such as loss of power, short circuits or voltage spikes that could impair actuator control or cause hazardous operation. It includes detection, protection and shutdown methods designed to prevent unsafe fluid movements when electrical faults occur.
[F15B 20/004] Fluid pressure supply failure -- The F15B 20/004 class covers safety measures dealing with loss or interruption of fluid pressure in actuator systems. It includes devices, methods or control strategies that detect, prevent or mitigate the effects of a fluid pressure supply failure in hydraulic or pneumatic circuits.
[F15B 20/005] Leakage; Spillage; Hose burst -- The F15B 20/005 subclass addresses safety measures for unintended fluid loss in fluid-actuator systems, covering detection, containment and mitigation of leaks, spills and hose bursts. It includes designs and devices that prevent or limit fluid escape and protect equipment and personnel from the consequences of such failures.
[F15B 20/007] Overload -- The F15B 20/007 entry concerns overload safety features for fluid-actuated systems, detailing devices and methods that detect or limit excessive forces or pressures in hydraulic or pneumatic actuators. It includes pressure-relief valves, overload clutches, sensors and control mechanisms designed to prevent damage or hazardous operation.
[F15B 20/008] Valve failure -- This subclass addresses failures of valves in fluid-actuated systems, covering causes such as leakage, sticking, rupture, and associated detection or mitigation measures that impact safety and performance. It is part of safety arrangements for fluid actuator systems within mechanical engineering.
[F15B 2011/0243] the regenerative circuit being activated or deactivated automatically -- F15B 2011/0243 covers electric switching or relay devices that contain a regenerative circuit which is switched on or off automatically in response to operating conditions, such as load changes or fault detection, to improve efficiency, limit energy losses, or provide protective functions.
[F15B 2011/0246] with variable regeneration flow -- F15B 2011/0246 covers reciprocating pumps that incorporate a variable regeneration flow system, allowing the amount of fluid recirculated during the suction stroke to be adjusted for improved efficiency, capacity control, or reduced pulsation. The invention typically includes adjustable ports or passages that modify the regeneration flow rate in response to operating conditions. This feature is applied to pump designs where fine control of fluid displacement is required.
[F15B 2013/002] Modular valves, i.e. consisting of an assembly of interchangeable components -- F15B 2013/002 relates to modular valves constructed from interchangeable components that can be assembled in various configurations to regulate fluid flow. The code applies to designs where valve bodies, seats, actuators or seals are separate parts that can be swapped or combined, facilitating customization, easy maintenance, and adaptation for applications such as water, oil, gas and other fluid-handling systems.
[F15B 2013/004] Cartridge valves -- The CPC subclass F15B 2013/004 refers to cartridge valves, which are compact, modular fluid-control devices that can be inserted into a valve body or manifold to regulate the flow or pressure of liquids or gases. These valves typically integrate the sealing, guiding and regulating elements in a replaceable unit.
[F15B 2013/006] Modular components with multiple uses, e.g. kits for either normally-open or normally-closed valves, interchangeable or reprogrammable manifolds -- This CPC subgroup covers modular fluid-control components designed for interchangeable or reprogrammable use, such as kits that can be assembled with either normally-open or normally-closed valves and manifolds that can be swapped or reprogrammed to change flow configurations.
[F15B 2013/008] Throttling member profiles -- The code pertains to the design and geometry of throttling members such as orifices, nozzles or flow restrictors used in fluid-power systems to control flow rate or pressure. It covers specific cross-sectional profiles, shapes and dimensions that determine the flow characteristic of the throttling element.
[F15B 2013/0409] Position sensing or feedback of the valve member -- Covers devices and methods that detect or report the position of a valve member, such as sensors, transducers or feedback circuits that convey information on opening, closing or intermediate positions for control or monitoring. The claim area includes mechanical, electrical, hydraulic or pneumatic means of position-sensing and feedback.
[F15B 2013/041] with two positions -- F15B 2013/041 is a CPC group for lifting devices-cranes, hoists, winches and similar apparatus-featuring a mechanism that offers exactly two selectable operating positions, such as two speed settings, two height ranges or two load configurations.
[F15B 2013/0412] with three positions -- The CPC code F15B 2013/0412 designates mechanical devices for handling particulate material that offer three distinct selectable positions, such as loading, processing and discharge or three separate flow paths. It covers apparatuses where the configuration can be set to one of three predetermined states to control material movement.
[F15B 2013/0413] with four or more positions -- F15B 2013/0413 classifies couplings that provide four or more distinct operating positions, enabling multiple engagement or locking states within the same device. It is a subgroup of the CPC coupling category that addresses devices with elastic or flexible elements allowing varied positional settings.
[F15B 2013/0414] Dosing devices -- F15B covers devices that dispense measured quantities of fluids, gases, powders or pastes, ranging from metering pumps and injectors to consumer dispensers. The class includes mechanisms for controlling volume, flow rate and dosing timing, as well as adjustable or preset dosing units used in industrial, medical or household applications.
[F15B 2013/0428] with switchable internal or external pilot pressure source -- A pneumatic or hydraulic circuit in which the pilot pressure source can be switched between an internal reservoir and an external supply, enabling the system to obtain pilot pressure either from within the circuit or from an outside source for controlling valves or actuators.
[F15B 2013/0448] Actuation by solenoid and permanent magnet -- F15B 2013/0448 covers mechanisms that generate motion by using a solenoid together with a permanent magnet, where the magnetic field of the permanent magnet assists, opposes or modifies the force produced by the coil. Typical applications include valves, lock actuators and other devices that require controlled linear or rotary movement.
[F15B 2015/1495] with screw mechanism attached to the piston -- The CPC entry F15B 2015/1495 pertains to pistons that incorporate a screw mechanism directly mounted on the piston body. Such arrangements are used to adjust the piston's position, secure accessories, or provide a means of transmission to other components within an engine or pump.
[F15B 2015/206] Combined actuation, e.g. electric and fluid actuated -- The CPC subclass F15B 2015/206 covers devices that use two or more types of actuation together, such as electrical and fluid (hydraulic or pneumatic) mechanisms operating in concert to provide motion or control. It includes hybrid drive systems, combined motor-pump assemblies, and any apparatus integrating multiple energy sources for actuation.
[F15B 2015/208] Special fluid pressurisation means, e.g. thermal or electrolytic -- The CPC entry F15B 2015/208 covers devices that pressurise fluids by means other than conventional mechanical pumping, such as heating or cooling the fluid, applying electrolytic reactions, or other special energy inputs. These methods create pressure changes in hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid-power systems for purposes like actuation, flow control or energy storage. It is part of subclass F15B, which concerns fluid-pressure generation and transmission.
[F15B 2015/267] Manual locking or release -- The CPC subclass F15B 2015/267 relates to security devices that are engaged or disengaged by a person's direct manual action. It covers mechanisms such as handles, levers, knobs or key-operated parts that lock or release a lock without electric, hydraulic or pneumatic assistance.
[F15B 2015/268] Fluid supply for locking or release independent of actuator pressurisation -- This group covers arrangements that provide fluid to a locking or releasing mechanism without relying on the pressure used to drive the primary actuator. It includes auxiliary pumps, reservoirs or channels that operate separately to engage or disengage the lock-release, often found in hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F15B 21/00] Common features of fluid actuator systems -- The CPC subclass F15B 21/00 covers the shared elements and design principles used in fluid-actuator systems, such as hydraulic or pneumatic actuators, including common components like valves, pumps, reservoirs, accumulators and control mechanisms that are applicable to multiple actuator types within fluid-driven machinery.
[F15B 21/001] Servomotor systems with fluidic control -- This subclass covers servomotor arrangements where motion or position is regulated by hydraulic or pneumatic signals, integrating fluidic control valves, feedback sensors, and drive mechanisms to achieve precise closed-loop control. It includes systems that use fluid power to drive a servomotor and a fluidic controller that modulates flow or pressure in response to command signals.
[F15B 21/003] Systems with different interchangeable components, e.g. using preassembled kits -- F15B 21/003 covers fluid-actuated systems whose parts are designed to be interchangeable, such as those assembled from pre-made kits, allowing easy replacement or reconfiguration of components within the same system. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 21/005] Filling or draining of fluid systems -- F15B 21/005 relates to devices and methods for filling or draining fluid systems, covering mechanisms that introduce or remove liquids or gases in hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid-actuated assemblies. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, general engineering, and systems acting by means of fluids, and focuses on the specific operations required to control the volume of fluid within such actuator systems.
[F15B 21/006] Compensation or avoidance of ambient pressure variation -- The F15B 21/006 subclass covers methods and devices that counteract or prevent changes in ambient pressure from affecting fluid-actuated systems. It includes pressure-compensating chambers, regulators, sealed housings and control strategies that maintain consistent actuator performance despite external pressure variations.
[F15B 21/008] Reduction of noise or vibration -- F15B 21/008 covers methods and devices for reducing noise or vibration generated by fluid-actuated systems such as hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders, pistons and related components. It includes design features, damping elements, acoustic insulation and control strategies aimed at minimizing sound and oscillations during operation. This subclass falls under the broader category of common features of fluid actuator systems in mechanical engineering.
[F15B 21/02] Servomotor systems with program control derived from a store or timing device -- F15B 21/02 pertains to fluid-actuated servomotor systems in which the actuator's motion is governed by a program stored in a memory device or by a timing mechanism, enabling precise control of position, speed or torque. The classification applies to arrangements that integrate such stored-program or timing-based control within hydraulic or pneumatic actuator circuits.
[F15B 21/04] Special measures taken in connection with the properties of the fluid -- F15B 21/04 covers provisions applied to fluid-actuated systems to exploit or compensate for specific fluid characteristics. It includes heating, cooling, pressurising, filtering, deaerating, adding additives or other treatments that affect viscosity, density, compressibility or chemical stability, thereby ensuring reliable operation of the actuator.
[F15B 21/041] Removal or measurement of solid or liquid contamination, e.g. filtering -- F15B 21/041 addresses techniques and apparatus for eliminating or measuring solid or liquid contaminants within fluid actuator systems, including filtering methods and devices that detect debris or impurity levels.
[F15B 21/042] Controlling the temperature of the fluid -- F15B 21/042 covers methods and devices for regulating the temperature of a fluid within fluid-actuator systems, such as heating, cooling, or maintaining a set temperature to ensure proper operation. It includes control strategies, sensors, and heat exchangers integrated with hydraulic or pneumatic actuators.
[F15B 21/0423] Cooling -- F15B 21/0423 pertains to cooling methods applied to fluid-actuator systems, describing arrangements such as internal cooling passages, external heat exchangers or fluid recirculation that dissipate heat generated in hydraulic or pneumatic actuators to maintain performance and prevent overheating.
[F15B 21/0427] Heating -- F15B 21/0427 covers heating aspects of fluid-actuated systems, describing methods and devices that raise the temperature of the working fluid or the actuator components. It applies to mechanical engineering applications where fluid power is used to generate heat for operation, control or performance enhancement.
[F15B 21/044] Removal or measurement of undissolved gas, e.g. de-aeration, venting or bleeding -- The F15B 21/044 class covers fluid-actuator systems that incorporate means for removing or measuring undissolved gases, such as de-aeration, venting or bleeding devices, to maintain performance and prevent cavitation. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering in general, and more narrowly under systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 21/045] Compensating for variations in viscosity or temperature -- F15B 21/045 relates to fluid-actuated systems that include mechanisms to offset changes in fluid viscosity or temperature, ensuring consistent performance. It covers designs such as heating elements, thermostatic controls, variable-speed pumps or adaptive control algorithms that adjust pressure or flow in response to temperature-induced viscosity shifts.
[F15B 21/047] Preventing foaming, churning or cavitation -- This subclass covers measures for avoiding foam formation, excessive churn or cavitation in fluid-actuated mechanisms. It includes designs, control strategies or additives that smooth flow, vent gases, or limit pressure spikes to protect pumps, valves and cylinders. The aim is to maintain stable operation of hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F15B 21/048] Arrangements for compressed air preparation, e.g. comprising air driers, air condensers, filters, lubricators or pressure regulators -- F15B 21/048 covers arrangements for preparing compressed air in fluid-actuator systems, including air dryers, condensers, filters, lubricators and pressure regulators, and is situated within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for systems acting by means of fluids.
[F15B 21/06] Use of special fluids, e.g. liquid metal -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Actuators having special fluid pressurization means -> F15B2015/208
[F15B 21/065] Use of electro- or magnetosensitive fluids, e.g. electrorheological fluid -- The subclass covers fluid-actuated mechanisms that employ fluids whose rheological properties change when an electric or magnetic field is applied, such as electrorheological or magnetorheological fluids. These field-responsive fluids are used to modulate force, stiffness or motion in hydraulic or pneumatic systems, enabling rapid and controllable actuation within broader fluid-actuator technologies.
[F15B 21/08] Servomotor systems incorporating electrically operated control means -- F15B 21/08 covers servomotor arrangements where motion is regulated by electrical control devices. It includes systems that use feedback, electronic governors or other electrically operated means to adjust speed, position or torque of a fluid-driven actuator, applied to precision motion-control units.
[F15B 21/082] with different modes -- This subclass covers fluid-actuated systems that are capable of operating in more than one mode, for example switching between pressure-control, flow-control or displacement-control functions, or between different actuation principles within the same device. It addresses the design features that enable such mode-changing behaviour.
[F15B 21/085] using a data bus, e.g. "CANBUS" -- The classification covers fluid actuator systems that communicate through a data bus, such as CANBUS, for control, monitoring and coordination of motion. It includes integration of digital networking into hydraulic or pneumatic actuators, allowing signals to be transmitted over a common bus rather than separate wiring.
[F15B 21/087] Control strategy, e.g. with block diagram -- F15B 21/087 covers the design and documentation of control strategies for fluid-actuated systems, including the use of block diagrams to represent feedback, command and regulation functions. It addresses how pressure, flow or motion are monitored and adjusted to achieve desired performance in hydraulic or pneumatic actuators.
[F15B 21/10] Delay devices or arrangements -- F15B 21/10 refers to delay devices or arrangements in fluid actuator systems. It includes mechanisms that intentionally slow, retard, or hold the motion of hydraulic or pneumatic actuators, such as throttling valves, accumulators, or timing cams, used to control the timing of fluid-driven movements.
[F15B 21/12] Fluid oscillators or pulse generators -- F15B 21/12 covers devices that convert a steady fluid flow into periodic motion, including fluid oscillators and pulse generators that create rhythmic pressure or flow variations for use in hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F15B 21/125] by means of a rotating valve -- This subclass covers fluid-actuated mechanisms that control fluid power by means of a rotating valve, such as spool or rotary valve designs, and is used to regulate pressure, flow direction or speed in hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F15B 21/14] Energy-recuperation means -- F15B 21/14 concerns means for recovering energy in fluid-actuated systems, such as hydraulic accumulators, turbines, or regenerative-braking devices that capture and reuse work produced by the fluid's motion. The entry covers designs that store or convert that recovered energy for later use within the same system.
[F15B 2201/00] Accumulators -- Accumulators are devices that store fluid under pressure for later release, used to smooth out pulsations, store energy, or provide emergency power in hydraulic and pneumatic systems. In mechanical engineering they belong to the class of fluid-powered systems and are classified under CPC F15B 2201/00.
[F15B 2201/20] Accumulator cushioning means -- F15B 2201/20 covers devices that provide cushioning for hydraulic or pneumatic accumulators, using springs, diaphragms, or other elastic elements to absorb pressure fluctuations and protect system components. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-based systems and accumulator technology.
[F15B 2201/205] using gas -- F15B 2201/205 pertains to accumulators that employ gas as the storage medium within fluid-acting systems. It covers designs and methods for using compressed or pressurised gas to store hydraulic energy, smooth pressure fluctuations, or provide rapid power release in mechanical engineering applications. The code falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, general engineering, and fluid-based systems.
[F15B 2201/21] using springs -- The subclass F15B 2201/21 covers accumulator devices that employ springs to store and release energy, using the spring's restoring force either alone or in combination with fluid pressure. It applies to hydraulic or pneumatic systems where springs provide the primary or supplementary means of maintaining pressure or delivering stored energy.
[F15B 2201/215] using weights -- F15B 2201/215 relates to hydraulic or pneumatic accumulators that employ external masses or weights to store energy and regulate fluid pressure. The classification covers designs where gravity-driven weights compress a fluid-filled chamber, providing a predictable pressure source or shock-absorption function in fluid-based systems. It is used for engineering solutions that balance fluid dynamics with mechanical weight loading.
[F15B 2201/22] using elastic housings -- This CPC subclass deals with accumulators that incorporate elastic housings to store fluid energy. It covers designs where the container itself flexes or is made of a resilient material to accommodate pressure changes in hydraulic or pneumatic systems. The classification is used for patents relating to flexible-wall accumulators and similar devices.
[F15B 2201/30] Accumulator separating means -- This CPC subclass (F15B 2201/30) covers devices that isolate or separate accumulators within fluid-acting mechanical systems, such as valves, seals or partitions that prevent fluid exchange between an accumulator and the rest of the system. It belongs to the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering, Engineering in General, Systems acting by means of fluids in general, Accumulators.
[F15B 2201/305] without separating means -- F15B 2201/305 covers accumulators that store energy in a fluid without any separating means such as a valve, membrane or separator between the working fluid and the storage chamber. The subclass includes designs where the fluid directly contacts the storage medium, typically a gas cushion, to smooth pressure or release energy in hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F15B 2201/31] having rigid separating means, e.g. pistons -- The CPC subclass F15B 2201/31 refers to accumulators that use rigid separating means such as pistons to separate the working fluid from the stored fluid. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 2201/312] Sealings therefor, e.g. piston rings -- The subclass F15B 2201/312 relates to sealings for accumulators, such as piston rings that prevent fluid leakage between moving parts. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engineering in general and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 2201/315] having flexible separating means -- This subcategory of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers accumulators that use flexible separating elements, such as bellows or diaphragms, to isolate fluid chambers while allowing volume change and maintaining a seal. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 2201/3151] the flexible separating means being diaphragms or membranes -- This CPC class F15B 2201/3151 belongs to mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general, focusing on accumulators. It concerns flexible separating means such as diaphragms or membranes that isolate fluid chambers within accumulator devices.
[F15B 2201/3152] the flexible separating means being bladders -- F15B 2201/3152 refers to accumulators that employ flexible separating means such as bladders to segregate a fluid from a gas within the device. The bladder expands and contracts with pressure changes, enabling energy storage or pressure regulation in hydraulic and pneumatic systems.
[F15B 2201/3153] the flexible separating means being bellows -- A flexible separating means being bellows refers to a compliant, accordion-like component used in fluid-power accumulators to maintain a seal while allowing volume changes as pressure varies. The bellows expand and contract with fluid displacement, providing compliance, leakage prevention, and accommodation of pressure-induced movement within hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F15B 2201/3154] the flexible separating means being completely enclosed, e.g. using gas-filled balls or foam -- F15B 2201/3154 classifies accumulators that use a flexible separating means that is completely enclosed within the device, such as gas-filled balls or foam, to isolate fluid chambers while permitting volume changes.
[F15B 2201/3155] characterised by the material of the flexible separating means -- F15B 2201/3155 classifies accumulator devices that are distinguished by the material used for their flexible separating element, such as membranes, diaphragms or bellows, within the broader mechanical-engineering field of fluid-acting systems. It covers designs where the choice of polymer, metal, composite or elastomer determines performance, sealing and durability, and is part of the CPC hierarchy under systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 2201/3156] characterised by their attachment -- F15B 2201/3156 covers fluid accumulators that are distinguished by the manner in which they are attached to a hydraulic or pneumatic circuit, such as flanged, threaded, welded or bolted connections, and includes any structural features that facilitate mounting to pipes, tanks or other equipment.
[F15B 2201/3157] Sealings for the flexible separating means -- F15B 2201/3157 covers sealing arrangements for the flexible separating elements of fluid accumulators, such as membranes or diaphragms, designed to prevent leakage of hydraulic or pneumatic fluid while allowing movement. The seals may be static or dynamic and are used in pressure storage systems for energy absorption and release.
[F15B 2201/3158] Guides for the flexible separating means, e.g. for a collapsed bladder -- Guides designed to position, support or limit movement of flexible separating elements, such as a collapsed bladder, within fluid-acting accumulators or similar systems, ensuring proper sealing and operation during compression or expansion.

[F15B 2211/2652] without priority -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F15B 2211/2652 pertains to circuits for servomotor systems in fluid-based engineering, specifically noting configurations where the system operates without priority. [F15B 2211/2654] one or more pressure sources having priority -- F15B 2211/2654 covers circuit arrangements for servomotor systems that include two or more fluid pressure sources and implement a priority scheme to select which source supplies the actuator. The priority logic ensures that a designated source is used first, with others engaged only if the primary source fails or is unavailable.

[F15B 2211/2658] by control of the prime movers -- F15B 2211/2658 refers to servomotor circuit arrangements in which the prime mover-such as a hydraulic, pneumatic or electric motor-is regulated by control means that adjust its operation to achieve the required motion. [F15B 2211/305] characterised by the type of valves -- The subclass F15B 2211/305 covers servomotor system circuits that are distinguished by the specific type of valve used, such as directional, proportional or check valves, which determine how fluid flow is controlled and influence the system's dynamic response.
[F15B 2211/30505] Non-return valves, i.e. check valves -- The CPC code F15B 2211/30505 falls under mechanical engineering fluid-power circuits for servomotor systems and specifically covers non-return, or check, valves that permit fluid flow in one direction while preventing backflow in hydraulic or pneumatic circuits.
[F15B 2211/3051] Cross-check valves -- Cross-check valves are fluid control devices used in servomotor hydraulic circuits that permit flow in a forward direction while automatically blocking reverse flow, ensuring one-way movement of hydraulic fluid and protecting actuators from back-pressure. They are typically incorporated in mechanical engineering systems that rely on fluid power to coordinate precise motion.
[F15B 2211/30515] Load holding valves -- Load holding valves are hydraulic components that maintain pressure in servomotor circuits to keep a load stationary when power is removed. They are used in fluid-powered mechanisms to lock positions, prevent back-driving, and provide safety hold-in functions in machinery.
[F15B 2211/3052] Shuttle valves -- Shuttle valves are fluid-control components that automatically route the flow from two separate sources to a single downstream line, selecting the source with the higher pressure while isolating the other. In servomotor circuits they provide quick switching between supply ports without moving parts, ensuring continuous operation and protection against back-flow. This function is classified under F15B 2211/3052 within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for fluid-acting systems.
[F15B 2211/30525] Directional control valves, e.g. 4/3-directional control valve -- This CPC subclass covers directional control valves used in hydraulic or pneumatic circuits for servomotor systems, including four-to-three-way valves that direct fluid flow to control motion and position. It belongs to mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids, specifically circuits for servomotor systems.
[F15B 2211/3053] In combination with a pressure compensating valve -- F15B 2211/3053 pertains to fluid-based servomotor circuits that incorporate a pressure-compensating valve, which automatically balances fluid pressure to maintain consistent actuator performance in mechanical engineering systems.
[F15B 2211/30535] the pressure compensating valve is arranged between pressure source and directional control valve -- The code F15B 2211/30535 covers fluid-power circuits for servomotor systems in which a pressure-compensating valve is positioned between the pressure source and a directional control valve, regulating flow to maintain constant actuator speed or force. This arrangement is used in hydraulic or pneumatic servo mechanisms to improve performance and stability.
[F15B 2211/3054] the pressure compensating valve is arranged between directional control valve and output member -- This class covers hydraulic circuits where a pressure compensating valve is placed between a directional control valve and the actuator (output member) to maintain constant load pressure in servomotor systems. The arrangement ensures stable pressure despite varying flow demands and improves control precision.
[F15B 2211/30545] the pressure compensating valve is arranged between output member and directional control valve -- The subclass covers servomotor hydraulic circuits where a pressure-compensating valve is installed between the actuator's output member and the directional control valve. It specifies the arrangement of the valve to modulate system pressure and maintain consistent flow to the output. This classification applies to mechanical engineering fluid-powered control systems.
[F15B 2211/3055] the pressure compensating valve is arranged between directional control valve and return line -- The entry F15B 2211/3055 covers hydraulic circuits for servomotor systems where a pressure-compensating valve is placed between the directional control valve and the return line. This configuration keeps actuator pressure constant, improving speed control and energy efficiency.
[F15B 2211/30555] Inlet and outlet of the pressure compensating valve being connected to the directional control valve -- The code F15B 2211/30555 covers configurations where the inlet and outlet ports of a pressure-compensating valve are directly linked to a directional control valve in servomotor hydraulic circuits. [F15B 2211/3056] Assemblies of multiple valves -- F15B 2211/3056 belongs to the CPC class for mechanical engineering and covers assemblies that combine two or more valves within fluid-powered servomotor circuits. [F15B 2211/30565] having multiple valves for a single output member, e.g. for creating higher valve function by use of multiple valves like two 2/2-valves replacing a 5/3-valve -- This subclass covers fluid-power circuits that achieve a single output motion by using multiple basic valves in parallel or series, such as two 2-2 valves replacing a single 5-3 valve to provide higher valve functionality. The arrangement is employed in servomotor systems to improve reliability, modularity or control precision.
[F15B 2211/3057] having two valves, one for each port of a double-acting output member -- This subclass defines servomotor circuits that employ two separate control valves, each dedicated to one of the two ports of a double-acting actuator, allowing independent regulation of pressure flow to the piston sides. [F15B 2211/30575] in a Wheatstone Bridge arrangement (also half bridges) -- The code F15B 2211/30575 pertains to servomotor control circuits that use a Wheatstone bridge, including half-bridge configurations, to sense and regulate fluid power signals in mechanical engineering applications. [F15B 2211/3058] having additional valves for interconnecting the fluid chambers of a double-acting actuator, e.g. for regeneration mode or for floating mode -- This subclass covers double-acting hydraulic or pneumatic actuators that include additional valves linking the two fluid chambers, allowing a regeneration mode or a floating mode where energy is transferred between chambers. It pertains to servomotor circuit designs that improve efficiency or control in fluid-driven systems.
[F15B 2211/30585] having a single valve for multiple output members -- F15B 2211/30585 concerns fluid power circuits in which a single valve supplies fluid to several output members such as servomotor actuators. The code falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and fluid-based system categories.
[F15B 2211/3059] having multiple valves for multiple output members -- A circuit for servomotor systems that incorporates several valves, each dedicated to a separate output member, allowing independent control of multiple loads within a fluid-powered mechanical arrangement. [F15B 2211/30595] with additional valves between the groups of valves for multiple output members -- F15B 2211/30595 is a CPC subclass that covers fluid-power circuits for servomotor systems in which extra valves are placed between groups of valves to control several output members. [F15B 2211/31] characterised by the positions of the valve element -- F15B 2211/31 is a CPC subgroup for circuits of servomotor systems in which the distinguishing feature is the position of the valve element, covering fluid-actuated arrangements where valve placement determines flow paths, control functions, or the actuation state of the system.
[F15B 2211/3105] Neutral or centre positions -- F15B 2211/3105 designates neutral or centre positions in circuits for servomotor systems, defining the condition where the motor is held at a rest or zero-movement point. [F15B 2211/3111] the pump port being closed in the centre position, e.g. so-called closed centre -- F15B 2211/3111 classifies hydraulic or pneumatic servomotor circuits in which the pump port is closed when the control valve is in its centre (neutral) position, commonly referred to as a closed-centre arrangement. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering in General → Systems Acting by Means of Fluids in General → Circuits for Servomotor Systems.
[F15B 2211/3116] the pump port being open in the centre position, e.g. so-called open centre -- The code F15B 2211/3116 covers hydraulic circuits in which the pump port remains open when the servomotor is in its centre or neutral position, often called an open-centre configuration. In this arrangement fluid can flow freely through the pump when no demand is present, reducing load on the motor and allowing smooth start-up. It is typical for fluid-driven servomotor systems.
[F15B 2211/3122] Special positions other than the pump port being connected to working ports or the working ports being connected to the return line -- This subclass pertains to hydraulic circuits for servomotor systems in which a valve position other than the pump port is linked to the working ports, or the working ports are tied to the return line, defining non-standard flow paths within fluid-power devices.
[F15B 2211/3127] Floating position connecting the working ports and the return line -- A floating connection that links the working ports of a servomotor circuit to its return line, allowing the link to move or adjust with fluid pressure changes, is covered by this subclass.
[F15B 2211/3133] Regenerative position connecting the working ports or connecting the working ports to the pump, e.g. for high-speed approach stroke -- A regenerative connection that links the working ports of a fluid-power circuit, or ties them to the pump, to boost flow during the approach phase of a servomotor's motion, enabling higher-speed strokes in hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F15B 2211/3138] the positions being discrete -- This subclass covers servomotor circuit designs that drive an actuator to distinct, predefined positions rather than continuous motion, including control methods, positioning feedback and discrete-step actuation mechanisms. It lies within mechanical engineering, engineering in general and fluid-based system circuits.
[F15B 2211/3144] the positions being continuously variable, e.g. as realised by proportional valves -- F15B 2211/3144 covers circuits for servomotor systems in which the actuator position can be varied continuously, such as through the use of proportional valves that adjust fluid flow to achieve a range of intermediate positions rather than fixed steps. This enables precise, real-time control of mechanical motion.
[F15B 2211/315] characterised by the connections of the valve or valves in the circuit -- This CPC subgroup covers servomotor system circuits where the defining feature is how the valve or valves are connected within the fluid circuit. [F15B 2211/52] characterised by the type of actuation -- This sub-group of the CPC classifies servomotor circuit designs based on the type of actuation employed, such as hydraulic, pneumatic, electric, or piezoelectric. It distinguishes the actuation mechanism rather than the control strategy, fitting within fluid-based mechanical engineering systems.
[F15B 2211/521] mechanically -- F15B 2211/521 classifies circuits used in servomotor systems that achieve control through mechanical means rather than electronic or hydraulic methods. It covers designs where mechanical components such as gears, linkages or cam mechanisms provide the feedback or actuation required for precise motor positioning, fitting within the broader categories of mechanical engineering and fluid-based system control.
[F15B 2211/522] actuated by biasing means, e.g. spring-actuated -- F15B 2211/522 covers circuits used in servomotor systems that are driven by a biasing element such as a spring, where the stored mechanical energy provides the actuation force. The classification applies to designs that combine fluid-based mechanisms with spring-biased actuation to control motion or position. It falls within the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, general engineering, and fluid-based system technologies.
[F15B 2211/523] manually, e.g. by using a lever or pedal -- This subclass covers servomotor circuits that are operated manually, such as by a lever, pedal or other human-powered control element, rather than automatically or electrically. It relates to fluid-power systems where the operator directly changes the circuit's state.
[F15B 2211/524] actuated by an output member of the circuit -- The entry covers servomotor control circuits in which the actuation is triggered by the circuit's own output member, such as a moving part or shaft that provides feedback to initiate or regulate motion. It pertains to fluid-based mechanical systems where the output element directly influences the circuit's operation.
[F15B 2211/525] with follow-up action -- The F15B 2211/525 subclass concerns servomotor circuits that include a follow-up action, meaning an additional control or mechanical step performed after the primary movement, such as braking, positioning, or resetting, typically in fluid-driven hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F15B 2211/526] electrically or electronically -- F15B 2211/526 refers to circuits for servomotor systems that are electrically or electronically implemented. It covers designs of control and power circuitry used to operate fluid-driven servomotors, including sensors, drivers and signal processing elements that regulate motor speed, position or torque in mechanical engineering applications.
[F15B 2211/527] with signal modulation, e.g. pulse width modulation [PWM] -- F15B 2211/527 pertains to circuits in servomotor systems that utilize signal modulation techniques, such as pulse width modulation (PWM), for controlling the operation and performance of fluid-actuated systems.
[F15B 2211/528] actuated by fluid pressure -- This CPC subclass F15B 2211/528, located under mechanical engineering, engineering in general and systems acting by means of fluids, pertains to circuits for servomotor systems that are actuated by fluid pressure. It covers designs where hydraulic or pneumatic pressure directly drives the servomotor, including the associated control and power-distribution arrangements.
[F15B 2211/55] for limiting a pressure up to a maximum pressure, e.g. by using a pressure relief valve -- F15B 2211/55 covers circuits for servomotor systems that include means for limiting pressure to a preset maximum, typically achieved with a pressure-relief valve that opens when the pressure exceeds the set threshold. This subclass addresses safety or control functions that protect hydraulic or pneumatic servomotor components from over-pressure conditions.
[F15B 2211/555] for assuring a minimum pressure, e.g. by using a back pressure valve -- This subclass concerns fluid circuits for servomotor systems that include means to ensure a minimum pressure, such as the use of a back pressure valve.
[F15B 2211/56] Control of an upstream pressure -- F15B 2211/56 relates to control of an upstream pressure in fluid-driven servomotor circuits, covering mechanisms that adjust or regulate the pressure supplied to a servomotor before it reaches the actuator. It includes pressure-sensing, feedback, and valve-based regulation techniques used to ensure precise motion control in hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F15B 2211/565] Control of a downstream pressure -- The entry concerns devices and methods that regulate the pressure after a fluid-driven servomotor, using sensors, valves or feedback loops to maintain a set downstream pressure in hydraulic or pneumatic circuits. It covers control strategies, pressure-sensing elements and adjustment mechanisms applied to servomotor systems.
[F15B 2211/57] Control of a differential pressure -- The subclass F15B 2211/57 covers devices and methods for regulating a differential pressure within fluid-driven servomotor circuits, including mechanisms that sense and adjust pressure differences to control motion or force. It pertains to fluid-based control systems used in mechanical engineering applications where precise pressure management is required for actuation.
[F15B 2211/575] Pilot pressure control -- F15B 2211/575 relates to pilot pressure control devices used in hydraulic or pneumatic circuits that operate servomotors. It covers methods for adjusting a small pilot pressure to regulate a larger main pressure, enabling precise motion control in fluid-driven systems. The classification is part of the broader group dealing with fluid-based engineering mechanisms.
[F15B 2211/5753] for closing a valve -- This CPC subclass covers circuit arrangements that actuate a servomotor to close a valve, used in fluid-power or hydraulic systems. It falls under the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general, specifically circuits for servomotor systems. The code F15B 2211/5753 identifies devices and methods for valve-closing control.
[F15B 2211/5756] for opening a valve -- This subclass covers control circuits that use a servomotor to actuate the opening of a valve within fluid-driven mechanical systems. It pertains to the electrical or hydraulic circuitry that commands valve movement for regulation or flow control.
[F15B 2211/60] Circuit components or control therefor -- F15B 2211/60, within the Cooperative Patent Classification for mechanical engineering, relates to circuits for servomotor systems. It covers the components of electrical or electronic circuits and the methods of controlling them that manage the operation of servomotors powered by fluid-driven mechanisms.
[F15B 2211/605] Load sensing circuits -- F15B 2211/605 pertains to load-sensing circuits used in servomotor systems, which are part of fluid-actuated mechanical engineering applications. The entry covers electrical circuitry that detects and measures mechanical load on a motor to provide feedback for control and efficiency. It falls under the broader categories of engineering in general, mechanical engineering, and systems acting by means of fluids.
[F15B 2211/6051] having valve means between output member and the load sensing circuit -- F15B 2211/6051 refers to arrangements in which a valve is positioned between an output member and the load-sensing circuit of a servomotor system, enabling pressure regulation or isolation for feedback control in hydraulic circuits. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-based systems used for servomotor actuation.
[F15B 2211/6052] using check valves -- F15B 2211/6052 relates to the use of check valves within fluid-driven servomotor circuits, specifying how these one-way devices are employed to control flow direction, prevent backflow and improve system stability and efficiency in hydraulic or pneumatic actuation networks. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and fluid-based system categories.

It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.

[F15B 2211/6058] with isolator valves -- F15B 2211/6058 classifies circuits for servomotor systems that include isolator valves, which are used to disconnect or isolate sections of a fluid circuit for protection, maintenance or control purposes. [F15B 2211/61] Secondary circuits -- This subclass covers circuits that provide auxiliary functions to servomotor systems, such as control, feedback, power supply, or monitoring, separate from the main drive circuit. It includes secondary power distribution, signal processing, or regulation loops that support the primary servomotor operation.
[F15B 2211/611] Diverting circuits, e.g. for cooling or filtering -- The F15B 2211/611 entry refers to diverting circuits used in servomotor systems, such as those that route fluid for cooling or filtering purposes. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically in systems acting by means of fluids in general, and captures designs that manage fluid flow to control temperature or remove particulates.
[F15B 2211/613] Feeding circuits -- Feeding circuits are electrical networks that supply power to servomotor systems, encompassing devices such as power converters, voltage regulators, current limiters and connections that deliver the required energy to the motor and its control electronics. The classification covers designs that manage the distribution, transformation and conditioning of electrical energy for servomotors within fluid-acting mechanical systems.
[F15B 2211/615] Filtering means -- A filtering means in this class refers to a device that removes contaminants, debris or air bubbles from the fluid circulating in servomotor circuits, typically hydraulic or pneumatic lines, to protect the motor and associated components and ensure reliable operation.
[F15B 2211/62] Cooling or heating means -- F15B 2211/62 covers the incorporation of cooling or heating devices in the fluid-driven circuits that control servomotors, such as temperature regulation of hydraulic or pneumatic lines and components to maintain performance and prevent overheating.
[F15B 2211/625] Accumulators -- Accumulators are devices that store hydraulic or pneumatic energy in a fluid under pressure and release it to smooth out flow, absorb shocks, or provide supplemental power in servomotor circuits. They typically consist of a gas-filled bladder or piston that compresses as fluid is pumped in, delivering stored energy when demand spikes, improving system stability and response.
[F15B 2211/63] Electronic controllers -- F15B 2211/63 is a CPC subclass that falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general and systems acting by means of fluids, specifically circuits for servomotor systems. [F15B 2211/6303] using input signals -- F15B 2211/6303 defines circuitry for servomotor systems that employs input signals to command actuator motion. It covers electronic or electro-hydraulic/hydraulic circuits that interpret control inputs-such as voltage, current, or digital commands-and generate the necessary drive signals to position or regulate the servomotor's output.
[F15B 2211/6306] representing a pressure -- F15B 2211/6306 covers the representation of pressure within circuits for servomotor systems, including pressure transducers, signal conversion and digital or analogue encoding of pressure information for feedback and control. The subclass also includes methods for processing and utilizing pressure signals to regulate motor movement and system performance.
[F15B 2211/6309] the pressure being a pressure source supply pressure -- F15B 2211/6309 covers fluid circuits for servomotor systems in which the pressure itself acts as the source that supplies the required pressure to the actuator. It addresses configurations where the pressure generated elsewhere is used directly to drive the servomotor, defining the pressure-as-supply concept within fluid-powered control circuits.
[F15B 2211/6313] the pressure being a load pressure -- F15B 2211/6313 refers to servomotor fluid circuits in which the system's load is applied directly by the pressure of the working fluid. It covers designs where the pressure itself functions as the load pressure, typical of hydraulic or pneumatic position-control loops.
[F15B 2211/6316] the pressure being a pilot pressure -- The F15B 2211/6316 entry covers fluid-circuit arrangements for servomotor systems in which a low-power pilot pressure is employed to regulate or activate the main operating pressure. It includes designs that use pilot lines, valves or sensors to control motion or force in hydraulic or pneumatic servomechanisms.
[F15B 2211/632] representing a flow rate -- This CPC subclass deals with ways to indicate or calculate the flow rate of fluid in servomotor control circuits, covering sensors, transducers and signal-processing methods used to measure and represent fluid movement within such systems.
[F15B 2211/6323] the flow rate being a pressure source flow rate -- F15B 2211/6323 concerns servomotor fluid circuits in which the flow rate is determined by a pressure-source flow rate. It classifies arrangements where a pressure source supplies fluid and the resulting flow rate governs actuator performance in hydraulic or pneumatic servomotor systems.
[F15B 2211/6326] the flow rate being an output member flow rate -- This classification covers servomotor circuit designs where the regulated output is the fluid flow rate, such as hydraulic or pneumatic systems that use flow-rate set-points or feedback to control actuator motion.

[F15B 2211/635] Circuits providing pilot pressure to pilot pressure-controlled fluid circuit elements -- The CPC subclass F15B 2211/635 covers electrical circuits that generate and supply pilot pressure to fluid components whose operation is controlled by pilot pressure, such as servomotor valves and other fluid control devices. [F15B 2211/65] Methods of control of the load sensing pressure -- F15B 2211/65 pertains to methods for controlling load sensing pressure in circuits designed for servomotor systems, focusing on hydraulic or pneumatic applications within mechanical engineering. [F15B 2211/651] characterised by the way the load pressure is communicated to the load sensing circuit -- F15B 2211/651 relates to hydraulic or pneumatic servomotor systems where the load pressure is transmitted to the load-sensing circuit by a specific communication method, such as pressure-transmission lines, sensors or dedicated valves that convey the load pressure directly to the control unit.
[F15B 2211/652] the load sensing pressure being different from the load pressure -- In the Cooperative Patent Classification, F15B 2211/652 pertains to circuits in servomotor systems where the load sensing pressure is distinct from the actual load pressure. This classification highlights specific hydraulic or pneumatic control mechanisms designed to optimize system performance and efficiency.
[F15B 2211/653] the load sensing pressure being higher than the load pressure -- A servomotor circuit in which the pressure employed for load-sensing is set higher than the actual load pressure, allowing the control system to detect overload conditions and adjust motor output accordingly. This feature is used in fluid-powered mechanical systems to improve safety and performance.
[F15B 2211/654] the load sensing pressure being lower than the load pressure -- The entry F15B 2211/654 covers fluid-power circuit arrangements for servomotor systems in which the load-sensing pressure is intentionally set lower than the load pressure. This configuration is used to provide early detection of load changes, improve response speed, and protect components by ensuring the control pressure activates before the full load pressure is reached.
[F15B 2211/655] Methods of contamination control, i.e. methods of control of the cleanliness of circuit components or of the pressure fluid -- It covers contamination-control techniques for preventing or removing contaminants in hydraulic or pneumatic circuits that drive servomotors, including cleaning of the pressure fluid and the circuit components. Methods such as filtration, fluid treatment, and routine maintenance are included to preserve cleanliness and ensure reliable operation.
[F15B 2211/66] Temperature control methods -- The classification F15B 2211/66 covers methods for regulating temperature within circuits that drive servomotor systems, such as heating or cooling techniques applied to the motor, its controller, or associated fluid-based components. These methods may involve sensors, feedback loops, thermostatic devices, or fluid flow adjustments to maintain optimal operating temperatures and protect performance.
[F15B 2211/665] Methods of control using electronic components -- The entry covers techniques for regulating servomotor or fluid-power circuits by employing electronic devices such as sensors, microprocessors, solid-state switches or dedicated control units. [F15B 2211/6656] Closed loop control, i.e. control using feedback -- The entry covers electrical or hydraulic circuits that implement closed-loop, feedback-based control for servomotors, including sensor signal processing, a comparator, and actuator drive to maintain a desired position, speed or torque. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 2211/6657] Open loop control, i.e. control without feedback -- The CPC entry F15B 2211/6657 covers circuits for servomotor systems that use open-loop control, meaning the motor's operation is set without receiving feedback signals to adjust performance. [F15B 2211/6658] Control using different modes, e.g. four-quadrant-operation, working mode and transportation mode -- The CPC subclass F15B 2211/6658 covers control techniques for servomotor circuits that employ multiple operating modes, such as four-quadrant, working and transportation modes, to manage speed and torque. [F15B 2211/7051] Linear output members -- F15B 2211/7051 covers linear output members used in servomotor circuits, such as rods, pistons or sliders that convert hydraulic or pneumatic fluid power into linear motion. These components are part of fluid-driven mechanical systems that provide precise positioning in machines and equipment.
[F15B 2211/7052] Single-acting output members -- The CPC class F15B2211/7052 defines single-acting output members used in servomotor circuits, i.e., devices that generate motion in only one direction when energized, typical of hydraulic or pneumatic actuators that return by spring or external force. It falls under mechanical engineering, fluid-based system control.
[F15B 2211/7053] Double-acting output members -- Double-acting output members are components, such as cylinders or hydraulic motors, that generate output motion in both forward and reverse directions by using pressurized fluid alternately on opposite sides of a piston or rotor.
