[F15B 2211/7054] Having equal piston areas -- This subclass covers fluid-power circuit designs for servomotor systems in which the pistons on opposite sides of the cylinder have the same cross-sectional area, ensuring balanced pressure forces and symmetric motion. It includes hydraulic or pneumatic arrangements that exploit equal piston areas for precise control.
[F15B 2211/7055] having more than two chambers -- This CPC subclass covers servomotor circuit arrangements that employ actuators or pumps with more than two chambers, typically in hydraulic or pneumatic systems, allowing multi-stage control or simultaneous operation of several pistons.
[F15B 2211/7056] Tandem cylinders -- Tandem cylinders are hydraulic cylinders arranged in series, allowing a single rod to move through successive chambers to provide greater force or longer stroke in servomotor systems. They typically share a common piston rod and are used where compact, high-force actuation is required, such as in industrial machinery, aerospace, and precision positioning equipment. This CPC entry classifies the design and application of such combined-cylinder arrangements.
[F15B 2211/7057] being of the telescopic type -- The CPC subclass F15B 2211/7057 relates to electrical control circuits for servomotor systems that operate telescopic mechanisms, i.e., devices whose motion is produced by extending or retracting a series of nested members. It covers circuit designs that drive or regulate such telescopic actuators.
[F15B 2211/7058] Rotary output members -- This class pertains to rotary output members used in fluid-powered servomotor circuits, such as shafts, gears, or rotors that convey the motion generated by hydraulic or pneumatic actuators. It falls under mechanical engineering and covers components that convert fluid energy into controlled rotary movement.
[F15B 2211/71] Multiple output members, e.g. multiple hydraulic motors or cylinders -- The code F15B 2211/71 covers fluid-power systems that drive more than one actuator from a single source, such as arrangements with several hydraulic motors or cylinders operating together. It relates to the distribution of hydraulic energy to multiple output members within servomotor circuits for synchronized or independent motion control.
[F15B 2211/7107] the output members being mechanically linked -- This subclass deals with servomotor circuit designs in which the output members-such as shafts, pistons or actuators-are physically linked together so that the movement of one output mechanically drives the others, enabling coordinated motion within the fluid-powered system.
[F15B 2211/7114] with direct connection between the chambers of different actuators -- F15B 2211/7114 covers fluid-power circuits used in servomotor systems where the chambers of separate actuators are linked directly, allowing fluid to flow between them without intermediate control components. This arrangement enables coordinated movement or force sharing among the actuators within a hydraulic or pneumatic servomechanism.
[F15B 2211/7121] the chambers being connected in series -- This subclass covers hydraulic or pneumatic servomotor circuits in which the working chambers are linked one after another so the fluid flows sequentially through each chamber, describing a series connection of chambers in fluid-powered actuation systems.
[F15B 2211/7128] the chambers being connected in parallel -- In this subclass the focus is on fluid-power servomotor circuits where two or more chambers are linked in parallel, allowing the chambers to share the same fluid pressure or flow simultaneously. The arrangement is used to achieve concurrent actuation or load balancing within the system.
[F15B 2211/7135] Combinations of output members of different types, e.g. single-acting cylinders with rotary motors -- This subclass covers fluid-power circuits that drive mixed output devices, such as pairing a single-acting hydraulic cylinder with a rotary motor. [F15B 2211/7142] the output members being arranged in multiple groups -- The subclass F15B 2211/7142 deals with circuits for servomotor systems where the output members are arranged in multiple groups, enabling distribution of motion or force across several actuators. [F15B 2211/715] having braking means -- The subclass F15B 2211/715 covers circuits for servomotor systems that incorporate braking means. [F15B 2211/72] having locking means -- F15B 2211/72 designates circuits for servomotor systems that include locking means, which secure the motor's output or prevent movement when the drive is inactive. [F15B 2211/75] Control of speed of the output member -- The F15B 2211/75 subclass covers methods and devices for regulating the rotational speed of the output shaft in servomotor circuits, typically using feedback, variable voltage, or frequency control techniques. [F15B 2211/755] Control of acceleration or deceleration of the output member -- The code F15B 2211/755 falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-based systems and servomotor circuits, and covers methods for controlling the acceleration or deceleration of the output member in such systems. [F15B 2211/76] Control of force or torque of the output member -- F15B 2211/76 covers circuits for servomotor systems that regulate the force or torque applied to the output member. [F15B 2211/761] Control of a negative load, i.e. of a load generating hydraulic energy -- The code F15B 2211/761 covers methods and devices that regulate a negative load, meaning a load that returns hydraulic energy to the system, within servomotor hydraulic circuits. It focuses on controlling the flow or pressure to capture and reuse the generated hydraulic power.
[F15B 2211/763] Control of torque of the output member by means of a variable capacity motor, i.e. by a secondary control on the motor -- This CPC subclass deals with methods and devices for regulating the torque of a driven shaft by adjusting the motor's capacity, typically using a secondary control loop that varies motor parameters. [F15B 2211/765] Control of position or angle of the output member -- F15B 2211/765 covers methods and devices that regulate the position or angular displacement of a servomotor's output shaft. [F15B 2211/7653] at distinct positions, e.g. at the end position -- F15B 2211/7653 relates to fluid-power circuits for servomotor systems in which the actuators are placed at separate points, for example at the terminal or end positions of the mechanism, defining the layout and control of such distributed fluid devices.
[F15B 2211/7656] with continuous position control -- The CPC subclass F15B 2211/7656 relates to fluid-powered servomotor circuits that achieve continuous position control, enabling smooth and precise adjustment of a motor's shaft or actuator over a range of positions. It covers designs where the fluid system continuously regulates the motor to maintain the commanded position rather than moving in discrete steps. [F15B 2211/77] Control of direction of movement of the output member -- This class covers fluid-powered servomotor circuits that regulate the direction in which an actuator's output member moves, including the valve arrangements and control strategies used to reverse or steer the output shaft in hydraulic or pneumatic drive systems.
[F15B 2211/7708] in one direction only -- F15B 2211/7708 classifies fluid-power circuits for servomotor systems that are intended to operate only in a single direction, such as one-way valves or pumps that provide flow without reverse action. The entry falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids.
[F15B 2211/7716] with automatic return -- This CPC subgroup pertains to fluid-driven servomotor circuits that incorporate an automatic return feature, allowing the actuator to revert to its initial position without additional control input. The automatic return is typically achieved by spring loading, pressure release or similar mechanisms integrated in the hydraulic or pneumatic circuit.
[F15B 2211/7725] with automatic reciprocation -- The CPC subclass F15B 2211/7725 is located in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engineering in general and systems acting by means of fluids in general, and it pertains to circuits for servomotor systems that include automatic reciprocation, meaning the motion is repeatedly reversed by the system itself without external intervention.
[F15B 2211/7733] providing vibrating movement, e.g. dither control for emptying a bucket -- This entry covers mechanisms that generate a vibrating movement, such as dither control, to aid the emptying of a bucket within fluid-driven servomotor circuit systems, classified under mechanical engineering and general engineering of fluid-based systems.
[F15B 2211/7741] with floating mode, e.g. using a direct connection between both lines of a double-acting cylinder -- A circuit for a servomotor system that employs a double-acting cylinder operating in a floating mode, meaning both rod-side and rod-end lines are directly linked so the cylinder can move freely without pressure buildup. This arrangement is used to maintain position or allow smooth motion by balancing forces within the hydraulic circuit.
[F15B 2211/775] Combined control, e.g. control of speed and force for providing a high speed approach stroke with low force followed by a low speed working stroke with high force, e.g. for a hydraulic press -- This CPC entry covers hydraulic or servomotor circuits that combine speed and force control to produce a fast, low-force approach stroke followed by a slower, high-force working stroke, such as in hydraulic presses that need rapid positioning and then strong pressing force. It falls under fluid-based mechanical engineering systems.
[F15B 2211/78] Control of multiple output members -- This CPC subclass covers methods and devices for controlling several actuators or output elements from a servomotor circuit, allowing coordinated operation of multiple hydraulic or pneumatic members. It includes control strategies, valve arrangements, and feedback systems that manage the motion of two or more outputs simultaneously.
[F15B 2211/781] one or more output members having priority -- This subclass covers servomotor circuit designs where one or more output devices are designated as primary, receiving control signals before other outputs. It applies to fluid-powered or hydraulic systems that prioritize certain actuators within a servomechanism.
[F15B 2211/782] Concurrent control, e.g. synchronisation of two or more actuators -- This subclass covers methods and devices that coordinate the operation of multiple actuators so that they move in a synchronized or otherwise interdependent manner, typically using feedback from servomotor circuits. It is applied to fluid-powered or hybrid systems where concurrent control improves performance, precision or safety.
[F15B 2211/783] Sequential control -- The CPC subclass F15B 2211/783 covers sequential-control techniques applied to circuits that drive servomotor systems, especially those that operate by means of fluid power. It defines arrangements and control logic that cause motor actions to occur in a programmed order, enabling precise timing and coordination of fluid-driven mechanical movements.
[F15B 2211/785] Compensation of the difference in flow rate in closed fluid circuits using differential actuators -- The code F15B 2211/785 designates methods and devices for compensating flow-rate differences in closed fluid circuits by employing differential actuators, typically within servomotor systems that control mechanical movement. It addresses how varying pressures are balanced to maintain stable operation of hydraulic or pneumatic loops.
[F15B 2211/80] Other types of control related to particular problems or conditions -- F15B 2211/80 covers circuit designs for servomotor systems that employ control methods beyond the standard ones, addressing specific problems or operating conditions such as overload protection, fault detection, or environmental constraints, and is positioned within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, general engineering, and fluid-based system actuation.
[F15B 2211/85] Control during special operating conditions -- F15B 2211/85 covers control of servomotor circuits when operating under special conditions such as overload, temperature extremes, or emergency modes. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids, focusing on fluid-power servomotor control strategies.
[F15B 2211/851] during starting -- F15B 2211/851 classifies circuit arrangements for servomotor systems that address the conditions and control methods employed during the starting phase of operation. It pertains to fluid-based mechanical engineering where the start-up behavior of servomotors, such as initial flow regulation, pressure buildup, or torque control, is specifically described. This subclass falls under systems acting by means of fluids in general within the broader mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F15B 2211/853] during stopping -- The CPC code F15B 2211/853 covers hydraulic or pneumatic circuit designs that manage a servomotor's behavior when it is brought to a stop. It includes features such as flow-restriction, valve sequencing or pressure-relief arrangements that control deceleration, hold position or dissipate stored energy during stopping. This subclass falls within mechanical engineering, specifically systems operating by fluids and servomotor circuit technology.
[F15B 2211/855] Testing of fluid pressure systems -- This subclass addresses methods and apparatus for evaluating the performance and integrity of hydraulic or pneumatic pressure networks, particularly those driving servomotor circuits. It includes pressure measurement, leak detection, functional checks and diagnostic procedures for testing fluid-pressure systems used in mechanical engineering applications.
[F15B 2211/857] Monitoring of fluid pressure systems -- The entry covers technologies for observing and measuring pressure within fluid power circuits that drive servomotor systems, including sensors, transducers and control methods that detect pressure changes to maintain proper operation and safety of hydraulic or pneumatic circuits.
[F15B 2211/86] Control during or prevention of abnormal conditions -- F15B 2211/86 covers circuitry that monitors and intervenes to avoid or correct fault conditions in servomotor systems, such as over-temperature, over-current or positional errors. The classification addresses protective and corrective control functions that maintain safe operation of fluid-driven mechanical devices.
[F15B 2211/8603] the abnormal condition being an obstacle -- F15B 2211/8603 covers servomotor fluid circuits that monitor or respond to abnormal conditions when an obstacle is present. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids, specifically circuits for servomotor systems. The classification addresses obstacle-induced fault detection and control in hydraulic or pneumatic servomechanisms.
[F15B 2211/8606] the abnormal condition being a shock -- F15B 2211/8606 relates to fluid-driven servomotor circuits that include features for detecting or responding to an abnormal shock condition, such as sensors, protective devices or control strategies that mitigate the effects of sudden mechanical impacts on the system.
[F15B 2211/8609] the abnormal condition being cavitation -- F15B 2211/8609 addresses the abnormal condition of cavitation in fluid circuits that operate servomotor systems, focusing on its detection, mitigation and impact within hydraulic or pneumatic networks.
[F15B 2211/8613] the abnormal condition being oscillations -- F15B 2211/8613 covers the identification and handling of abnormal oscillatory behaviour in circuits that drive servomotor systems using fluids. It classifies patents that address vibration, instability or resonance phenomena affecting the performance of fluid-powered motion control mechanisms.
[F15B 2211/8616] the abnormal condition being noise or vibration -- F15B 2211/8616 covers abnormal conditions such as noise or vibration occurring within circuits that control servomotor systems, which are part of fluid-based mechanical engineering applications. The classification is used to identify patents that address detection, analysis, or mitigation of these undesirable acoustic or vibratory effects in servomotor control circuitry.
[F15B 2211/862] the abnormal condition being electric or electronic failure -- F15B 2211/862 classifies inventions that address abnormal conditions caused by electric or electronic failures in fluid-based servomotor circuits. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering in General → Systems Acting by Means of Fluids in General → Circuits for servomotor systems, and covers methods, devices or control strategies for detecting, protecting against or mitigating such electrical faults.
[F15B 2211/8623] Electric supply failure -- F15B 2211/8623 covers the loss or interruption of electrical power in circuits that drive servomotor systems, including detection, protection and fallback mechanisms to maintain operation of fluid-powered components when the supply fails.
[F15B 2211/8626] Electronic controller failure, e.g. software, EMV, electromagnetic interference -- F15B 2211/8626 covers problems caused by failures in electronic controllers of servomotor circuits, including software errors, electromagnetic compatibility issues and electromagnetic interference. It pertains to mechanical engineering systems that use fluid power and servomotors, focusing on diagnosing and mitigating controller malfunctions.
[F15B 2211/863] the abnormal condition being a hydraulic or pneumatic failure -- F15B 2211/863 covers the detection, analysis or response to abnormal conditions caused by hydraulic or pneumatic failures in servomotor system circuits, focusing on how such failures affect the performance, safety or control of the mechanical system. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically systems that operate by fluids, and addresses fault-diagnosis or mitigation measures for fluid-power actuation.
[F15B 2211/8633] Pressure source supply failure -- The code F15B 2211/8633 covers failures of the pressure source that supplies fluid to servomotor circuits, including loss of pressure, leaks or malfunctions of pumps and accumulators that impede system operation. It is used to classify patents dealing with detection, mitigation or design features addressing such supply failures in fluid-driven mechanical systems.
[F15B 2211/8636] Circuit failure, e.g. valve or hose failure -- F15B 2211/8636 covers failures in fluid-based servomotor circuits, such as broken valves or ruptured hoses, that interrupt power or control flow. The class is situated within mechanical engineering, specifically under engineering in general and systems acting by means of fluids, focusing on circuits for servomotor systems.
[F15B 2211/864] Failure of an output member, e.g. actuator or motor failure -- The CPC entry F15B 2211/864 covers failures of output components in servomotor circuits, such as an actuator or motor that does not operate correctly. It classifies problems where the output member itself malfunctions, affecting the performance of fluid-driven mechanical systems.
[F15B 2211/8643] the abnormal condition being a human failure -- F15B 2211/8643 pertains to servomotor fluid-circuit designs where the abnormal condition arises from human failure, such as operator error, mis-operation or procedural mistakes that affect system performance.
[F15B 2211/8646] the abnormal condition being hysteresis -- F15B 2211/8646 relates to circuits for servomotor systems that experience abnormal hysteresis conditions, covering detection, compensation, or control strategies for hysteresis effects in fluid-driven actuators. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and systems acting by means of fluids.
[F15B 2211/865] Prevention of failures -- F15B 2211/865 covers measures that prevent failures in circuits for servomotor systems, including protective devices, fault-detection methods, monitoring arrangements and control strategies designed to avoid damage or malfunction of fluid-powered servomotors. This subclass addresses reliability-enhancing features applied to the hydraulic or pneumatic circuitry that drives the servomotor.
[F15B 2211/87] Detection of failures -- The code F15B 2211/87 relates to methods and devices for detecting faults in servomotor system circuits, including the identification of abnormal conditions such as overload, short-circuit, or loss of position feedback. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-actuated systems and servomotor circuitry.
[F15B 2211/875] Control measures for coping with failures -- This CPC subclass covers protective and corrective strategies applied to servomotor circuits to detect, isolate, and mitigate component or system failures, ensuring continued operation or safe shutdown. It includes fault detection, redundancy, fallback control, and fault-tolerant design measures for hydraulic or pneumatic servomotor drives.
[F15B 2211/8752] Emergency operation mode, e.g. fail-safe operation mode -- The code F15B 2211/8752 covers servomotor circuit designs that include an emergency or fail-safe mode, automatically activating a safe operating condition when a fault or loss of control is detected, thereby maintaining function or ensuring a safe shutdown of the hydraulic or pneumatic system.
[F15B 2211/8755] Emergency shut-down -- A safety circuit designed to rapidly stop a servomotor system in an emergency, cutting power and preventing further motion. It typically incorporates sensors, relays or electronic controls that detect fault conditions and trigger an immediate shutdown to protect equipment and personnel.
[F15B 2211/8757] using redundant components or assemblies -- This subclass concerns servomotor control circuits that incorporate duplicate or backup components and assemblies to boost reliability and provide fault tolerance, ensuring continued operation if a part fails. It applies to fluid-based actuation systems where redundancy is built into the circuit architecture.
[F15B 2211/88] Control measures for saving energy -- F15B 2211/88 pertains to control measures for saving energy in fluid-powered servomotor circuits. It includes techniques for regulating pressure, flow or valve operation and feedback control strategies that minimise power use while maintaining performance. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering topics dealing with fluid-based systems.
[F15B 2211/885] Control specific to the type of fluid, e.g. specific to magnetorheological fluid -- F15B 2211/885 covers control techniques that are tailored to the particular fluid used in a servomotor circuit, such as magnetorheological or electrorheological fluids whose rheology can be altered by a magnetic or electric field. It falls within mechanical engineering, specifically systems that act by means of fluids, and addresses how fluid-type-specific properties are exploited for precise motor control.
[F15B 2211/8855] Compressible fluids, e.g. specific to pneumatics -- F15B 2211/8855 covers devices and methods that use compressible fluids such as air or gas to power or control servomotor circuits. It pertains to pneumatic actuation, pressure regulation, and fluid-driven transmission of motion within mechanical systems. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-based system circuits.
[F15B 2211/89] Control specific for achieving vacuum or "negative pressure" -- The code F15B 2211/89 covers control mechanisms that create or maintain a vacuum or negative pressure in fluid circuits, especially within servomotor systems, and includes devices such as vacuum pumps, pressure-reducing valves and electronic regulators designed to keep pressure below atmospheric levels for mechanical engineering applications.
[F15B 2211/895] Manual override -- F15B 2211/895 classifies manual-override devices that allow an operator to take direct control of a servomotor circuit, typically by disconnecting or bypassing the automatic control signal. The feature is used in fluid-powered mechanical systems where a manual input can supersede the normal automated operation.
[F15B 2215/00] Fluid-actuated devices for displacing a member from one position to another -- F15B 2215/00 classifies fluid-actuated devices that move a part from one position to another, such as hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders, pistons and similar actuators used in mechanical systems. The category includes mechanisms that employ pressure or flow of fluid to generate linear or rotary displacement of a member.
[F15B 2215/30] Constructional details thereof -- The subclass F15B 2215/30 addresses the specific structural and design elements of fluid-actuated devices that move a member, such as pistons, cylinders or actuators. It covers constructional details including housings, mounting arrangements, seals, guides, linkages and connection features that influence the device's operation and integration.
[F15B 2215/305] characterised by the use of special materials -- This CPC subgroup covers fluid-actuated devices that move a member and are distinguished by the use of special materials such as alloys, composites, polymers or other non-standard substances to achieve particular strength, corrosion resistance, temperature tolerance or other performance benefits. It applies to pistons, cylinders, hydraulic or pneumatic actuators whose material selection is a defining feature.
[F15B 3/00] Intensifiers or fluid-pressure converters, e.g. pressure exchangers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for converting fluid energy, i.e. from a flow of fluid having high pressure and low flow rate to a flow of fluid having low pressure and high flow rate or vice versa.  E.g. devices using pistons of different size or rotating fluid pumps and motors of different capacity.  Fig. from FR2822906  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fluid-driven pumps -> F04B9/08
[F15B 5/00] Transducers converting variations of physical quantities, e.g. expressed by variations in positions of members, into fluid-pressure variations or -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Servomotors with follow-up action -> F15B9/00
[F15B 5/003] characterised by variation of the pressure in a nozzle or the like, e.g. nozzle-flapper system -- This subclass covers fluid-pressure transducers whose output pressure is varied by changes in a nozzle or similar device, such as a nozzle-flapper arrangement, converting mechanical motion into a pressure signal. It sits within mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and fluid-actuated system categories.
[F15B 5/006] with electrical means, e.g. electropneumatic transducer  F15B5/003  takes precedence -- F15B 5/006 pertains to transducers that convert physical quantity variations into fluid pressure changes using electrical methods, such as electropneumatic transducers, with F15B 5/003 taking precedence in classification.
[F15B 7/00] Fluid-pressure actuator systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. systems wherein a master cylinder is directly connected to a subordinate cylinder. Illustrative example:  Fig. from WO2006129746  **Glossary:** - Telemotor: system or device in which a substantially constant amount of fluid is trapped between an input member and an output member to act as a fluid link, e.g. realised as a master cylinder which is directly connected to a subordinate cylinder.
[F15B 7/001] With multiple inputs, e.g. for dual control -- F15B 7/001 pertains to fluid-pressure actuator systems that are operated by more than one control input, such as dual-control arrangements. It covers mechanisms where multiple signals or commands influence the actuator's movement, enabling coordinated or redundant control of hydraulic or pneumatic devices.
[F15B 7/003] with multiple outputs -- This subclass concerns fluid-pressure actuators that supply more than one output simultaneously, such as hydraulic cylinders or pneumatic pistons driving multiple levers, rods or work members. It includes arrangements where a single pressure source is split to operate several outputs in coordinated motion.
[F15B 7/005] With rotary or crank input -- F15B 7/005 covers fluid-pressure actuator systems that are driven by a rotary or crank input, where the rotating motion is used to pressurise fluid to produce linear or rotary output forces. Such arrangements are typical in hydraulic pumps or motor-driven cylinders where a shaft or crankshaft supplies the energy.
[F15B 7/006] Rotary pump input -- F15B 7/006 concerns the inlet side of rotary pumps, detailing the arrangement of suction ports, inlet valves and the geometry that directs fluid into the rotating mechanism. It relates to fluid-pressure actuator systems within mechanical engineering.
[F15B 7/008] with rotary output -- The subclass F15B 7/008 covers fluid-pressure actuator systems that deliver rotary output, such as hydraulic or pneumatic motors that transform fluid power into a rotating shaft. It includes designs where the actuator's motion is transferred to gears, rotors, vanes or other mechanisms to produce continuous rotation.
[F15B 7/02] Systems with continuously-operating input and output apparatus -- F15B 7/02 covers fluid-pressure actuator systems in which both the input device (such as a pump or compressor) and the output device (such as a cylinder or motor) operate continuously, providing steady, uninterrupted flow of fluid to drive mechanical motion.
[F15B 7/04] In which the ratio between pump stroke and motor stroke varies with the resistance against the motor -- This subclass covers fluid-pressure actuator arrangements where the displacement of the pump is adjusted relative to the motor displacement as the motor experiences varying resistance, allowing the stroke ratio to change dynamically to match load conditions.
[F15B 7/06] Details -- F15B 7/06 covers the specific structural and functional details of fluid-pressure actuator systems, including the design of pistons, cylinders, valves, seals and mounting arrangements, as well as control and safety features that define the operation of hydraulic or pneumatic actuators.
[F15B 7/08] Input units -- F15B 7/08 covers the input devices that generate or control fluid pressure for hydraulic or pneumatic actuator systems, such as pumps, compressors, reservoirs, and pressure-regulating valves, which supply the energy needed to move the actuators. It pertains to fluid-pressure actuator systems within the broader categories of mechanical engineering and fluid-based system engineering.

[F15B 9/00] Servomotors with follow-up action, i.e. in which the position of the actuated member conforms with that of the controlling member -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fluid power drives using position feed-back  **Glossary:** - Servomotor: fluid-pressure acutator, e.g. a piston and a cylinder, directly controlled by a valve or other device (e.g. pump) which is responsive to operation of an initial controlling member (e.g. joystick). The initial controlling member may be adjacent to the servomotor or at a distance and may be, e.g. a hand lever.
[F15B 9/02] with servomotors of the reciprocatable or oscillatable type -- F15B 9/02 classifies servomotors that operate by reciprocating or oscillating motion, where the output member follows the position of the controlling member. These devices are used in fluid-powered systems to provide precise position control and are a subcategory of servomotors within the broader field of mechanical engineering.
[F15B 9/03] with electrical control means -- This subclass covers servomotors that use electrical control devices to ensure the driven member follows the position of the controlling member. It includes systems where feedback and actuation are achieved through electric circuits or electronic components.
[F15B 9/04] controlled by varying the output of a pump with variable capacity -- The subclass F15B 9/04 covers hydraulic or fluid-driven servomotors that provide follow-up action, where the driven element's position tracks that of a controlling element by varying the output of a pump with variable capacity, thereby adjusting flow to achieve the desired motion. It is situated under mechanical engineering, general engineering, and systems acting by means of fluids in general.
[F15B 9/06] controlled by means using a fluid jet -- The F15B 9/06 subclass covers servomotors whose follow-up action is produced by directing a fluid jet onto a surface or member to generate motion, so the position of the driven element follows the control input. These devices use fluid-jet actuation for precise positioning within mechanical systems.
[F15B 9/07] with electrical control means -- F15B 9/07 designates servomotors that use electrical control means to achieve follow-up action, meaning the driven member's position mirrors the controlling member's position. The classification applies to fluid-power actuators where electrical signals adjust flow or pressure to regulate motion.
[F15B 9/08] controlled by valves affecting the fluid feed or the fluid outlet of the servomotor -- The F15B 9/08 subclass covers servomotors whose motion is regulated by valves that modify the fluid entering or leaving the motor, so the driven element follows the controlling element's position. It lies within the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and fluid-based systems, and pertains to hydraulic or pneumatic actuation where valve control determines feed or outlet flow.
[F15B 9/09] with electrical control means -- F15B 9/09 covers servomotors that employ follow-up action, meaning the position of the driven member mirrors that of the controlling member, and the system is governed by electrical control means. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and fluid-based systems, specifying fluid-powered actuators whose motion is electrically regulated to achieve precise positioning.

[F15B 9/14] with rotary servomotors -- F15B 9/14 designates rotary servomotors that operate with follow-up action, where the driven shaft continuously matches the position of the controlling shaft. It applies to fluid-driven mechanical systems where the electric controller adjusts the actuator to follow the input command.
[F15C] FLUID-CIRCUIT ELEMENTS PREDOMINANTLY USED FOR COMPUTING OR CONTROL PURPOSES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Components for fluidic logic systems (fluidics), i.e. for systems using a fluid to perform analog or digital operations similar to those performed with electronics.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Nozzles for windscreens -> B60S1/52
[F15C 1/00] Circuit elements having no moving parts -- Group F15C1/22 takes precedence over groups F15C1/08 - F15C1/20 . This Note corresponds to IPC Note (1) relating to F15C1/08 - F15C1/20 .
[F15C 1/001] for punched-card machines  punched-card machines  G06K ; for typewriters  typewriters  B41J ; for keyboards; for conveying cards or tape; for conveying through tubes  transport through tubes  B65G51/00 ,  B65G53/00 ; for computers  non-electric computers  G06C ,  G06D ,  G06G ; for DC-AC transducers for information processing  DC-AC converters  H02M ; for signal transmission  telegraphic apparatus  H04L -- F15C 1/001 covers circuit elements that have no moving parts and are employed in devices such as punched-card machines, typewriters, keyboards, and mechanisms for transporting cards, tape or media through tubes, as well as in non-electric computers, DC-AC converters for information processing and telegraphic signal-transmission equipment. It groups these fluid-circuit components used primarily for computing or control purposes.
[F15C 1/002] for controlling engines, turbines, compressors (starting, speed regulation, temperature control or the like)  control of internal-combustion piston engines  F02D ; of turbines  F01D ,  F02C ; of fans  F04D27/00 ; speedometers  G01P -- F15C 1/002 covers circuit elements without moving parts that are used to start, regulate speed, or control temperature of engines, turbines, compressors, fans and related devices, including control of internal-combustion piston engines, gas-turbine units and speedometers.
[F15C 1/003] for process regulation, (e.g. chemical processes, in boilers or the like); for machine tool control (e.g. sewing machines, automatic washing machines); for liquid level control; for controlling various mechanisms; for alarm circuits; for AC-DC transducers for control purposes  special provisions on lathes  B23B25/00 ,  B23Q ; electric regulation of mechanical working machines  B23Q35/00 ,  G05B19/00 ; thread feeding devices for sewing machines  D05B51/00 ; automatic washing machines  D06F33/00 ; valve-controlled servomotors  F15B9/08 ; non-electric signal transmission  G08C23/00 -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F15C 1/003 pertains to fluid-circuit elements without moving parts, specifically for process regulation in various applications such as chemical processes, boilers, machine tool control, liquid level control, and alarm circuits. It also covers special provisions for lathes, electric regulation of mechanical working machines, thread feeding devices for sewing machines, automatic washing machines, valve-controlled servomotors, and non-electric signal transmission.
[F15C 1/005] for measurement techniques, e.g. measuring from a distance; for detection devices, e.g. for presence detection; for sorting measured properties (testing); for gyrometers; for analysis; for chromatography  fluid information or impulse transducers  F15B5/00 ; postal sorting according to size  B07C1/10 ; dial gauges, spherometers  G01B3/22 ,  G01B5/22 ; gyroscopic apparatus  G01C19/00 ; viscosimeters  G01N11/00 ; speed measurement, flowmeters  G01P -- F15C 1/005 pertains to fluid circuit elements without moving parts used for measurement techniques and detection devices, including presence detection, property sorting, gyrometers, analysis, and chromatography. It also covers related aspects such as postal sorting by size, dial gauges, gyroscopic apparatus, viscosimeters, and speed or flow measurements.
[F15C 1/006] for aeronautics; for rockets (drives, controls); for satellites; for air cushion vehicles; for controlling vessels or torpedoes  injectors  F04F5/00 ; aircraft control by jet reaction  B64C15/00 ; air pressure regulation in aircraft  B64D13/04 ; instruments adapted to be mounted in aircraft  B64D43/00 -- F15C 1/006 covers fluid-circuit elements without moving parts that are used for computing or control functions in aeronautics, rockets, satellites, air-cushion vehicles and maritime vessels or torpedoes, including jet-reaction aircraft controls, air-pressure regulation, onboard instruments and injectors.
[F15C 1/007] for indicating devices for fluid signals  output arrangements in electronic computers  G06F3/14 ; luminous advertising  G09F13/00 ; name or number plates with interchangeable characters  G09F7/00 ; fluid operating means for indicating or recording members in measuring instruments  G01D5/42 ; fluid information or pulse transducers for converting variations of fluid pressure into other physical quantities  F15B5/003 -- This classification code, F15C 1/007, pertains to circuit elements without moving parts that are used in indicating devices for fluid signals, including their application in electronic computer output arrangements, luminous advertising, name or number plates with interchangeable characters, and fluid-operated components in measuring instruments. It also covers transducers that convert fluid pressure variations into other physical quantities.
[F15C 1/008] Other applications, e.g. for air conditioning, medical applications, other than in respirators, derricks for underwater separation of materials by coanda effect, weapons -- F15C 1/008 covers circuit elements without moving parts that are used in applications such as air-conditioning, medical devices (excluding respirators), underwater material separation by the Coanda effect, and weapons.
[F15C 1/02] Details -- F15C 1/02 pertains to specific details of fluid-circuit elements without moving parts, used primarily for computing or control purposes in the field of mechanical engineering. This classification covers aspects such as design, materials, and operational features that enhance functionality and precision in fluid-based control systems.
[F15C 1/04] Means for controlling fluid streams to fluid devices, e.g. by electric signals -- F15C 1/04 covers fluid-circuit elements that regulate the flow of liquids or gases to other fluid devices by means of electrical signals, without employing moving mechanical parts. It includes electronically actuated valves, pumps or flow-control modules used for computing or control functions.
[F15C 1/06] Constructional details -- F15C 1/06 covers the constructional details of fluid-circuit components that have no moving parts and are primarily employed in computing or control systems. It addresses structural features such as housing, connections, sealing, and material selections for these static circuit elements.
[F15C 1/08] Boundary-layer devices, e.g. wall-attachment amplifiers -- The CPC subclass F15C 1/08 covers devices that modify a fluid's boundary layer to improve performance, such as wall-attachment amplifiers that increase lift or reduce drag, and are used in fluid-circuit elements without moving parts.
[F15C 1/10] for digital operation, e.g. to form a logical flip-flop, OR-gate, NOR-gate -- F15C 1/10 covers fluid-circuit components that perform digital logic functions without moving parts, such as forming flip-flops, OR-gates or NOR-gates. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically fluid-circuit elements used primarily for computing or control purposes, and is classified within the broader engineering-in-general hierarchy.
[F15C 1/12] Multiple arrangements thereof for performing operations of the same kind, e.g. majority gates, identity gates -- F15C 1/12 is a CPC sub-class within the Mechanical Engineering section that covers circuit elements without moving parts used for computing or control, specifically multiple configurations of the same type arranged to perform identical operations such as majority or identity gates. This entry groups together arrangements that execute the same logical function, enabling designers to reference standardized configurations in fluid-circuit or electronic control systems.
[F15C 1/14] Stream-interaction devices -- F15C 1/14 covers stream-interaction devices, which are components that modify, split, combine, or otherwise affect a fluid stream without any moving parts. They are used in fluid-circuit elements for computing or control functions, falling under mechanical engineering and general engineering.
[F15C 1/143] for digital operation, e.g. to form a logical flip-flop, OR-gate, NOR-gate, AND-gate  F15C1/10  takes precedence -- F15C 1/143 covers fluid-circuit components without moving parts that are designed to perform digital functions such as creating flip-flops or basic logic gates (OR, NOR, AND). These elements belong to the broader class F15C 1/10, which has precedence.
[F15C 1/146] multiple arrangements thereof, forming counting circuits, sliding registers, integration circuits or the like  F15C1/12  take precedence -- This CPC entry (F15C 1/146) covers fluid-circuit elements that have no moving parts and are combined in multiple configurations to create counting circuits, sliding registers, integrated circuits or similar devices, building on the broader category of fluid-circuit elements used for computing or control. It specifies the arrangement of several such elements into functional electronic-type structures.
[F15C 1/16] Vortex devices, i.e. devices in which use is made of the pressure drop associated with vortex motion in a fluid -- F15C 1/16 covers vortex devices that exploit the pressure drop generated by vortex motion in a fluid, such as vortex-tubes and ejectors, for purposes like cooling, mixing or flow control. These devices have no moving parts and fall under fluid-circuit elements used primarily for computing or control functions within mechanical engineering.
[F15C 1/18] Turbulence devices, i.e. devices in which a controlling stream will cause a laminar flow to become turbulent -- The code F15C 1/18 covers turbulence-inducing devices that convert a laminar flow into a turbulent one, typically by inserting a controlling stream or obstruction into the fluid circuit. These elements have no moving parts and are used in engineering applications where enhanced mixing, heat transfer, or flow resistance is required.
[F15C 1/20] Direct-impact devices i.e., devices in which two collinear opposing power streams are impacted -- F15C 1/20 pertains to direct-impact devices in fluid-circuit engineering, where two collinear opposing power streams are made to collide, converting their kinetic energy into a useful effect without any moving parts. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and fluid-circuit elements used mainly for computing or control purposes.
[F15C 1/22] Oscillators -- F15C 1/22 covers oscillators, which are circuit elements that generate periodic electrical signals without any moving parts. These devices are used primarily in computing or control applications to provide timing, waveform generation, or frequency reference functions within fluid-circuit systems. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering in general, and fluid-circuit elements predominantily used for computing or control purposes.
[F15C 3/00] Circuit elements having moving parts -- The class covers fluid-circuit components whose operation depends on movable parts, such as valves, pistons, diaphragms or actuators that regulate flow, pressure or direction in hydraulic or pneumatic systems used for control or computing functions. These elements may be manually or automatically operated and are intended primarily for system-level control rather than power transmission.
[F15C 3/002] using fluid droplets or similar deformable bodies  using solid balls  F15C3/06 -- This subclass covers fluidic circuit components that manipulate fluid droplets or other deformable bodies by employing solid spherical particles as carriers, actuators or valves. It includes devices where solid balls are used to transport, split or merge droplets in fluid-based computing or control systems.
[F15C 3/005] using loose plates or foils  using diaphragms  F15C3/04 -- F15C 3/005 pertains to fluid-circuit components that use movable loose plates, foils or diaphragms to regulate flow or pressure, typically in sensors, actuators or control valves. It classifies devices where such flexible elements act as the moving part that modulates the fluid circuit for computing or control functions.
[F15C 3/007] using a spiral spring which allows fluid bass upon deformation  using reeds  F15C3/08 -- F15C 3/007 covers fluid-circuit elements that incorporate a spiral spring and reed system, where deformation of the spring generates or modulates fluid pressure or flow. Such devices are used in sensing, actuation or control mechanisms that rely on moving-part fluid dynamics within mechanical engineering applications.
[F15C 3/02] using spool valves -- F15C 3/02 covers the use of spool valves, which are rotary or sliding valve elements that control fluid flow in hydraulic or pneumatic circuits, particularly in systems for computing or control functions. The classification addresses design, operation and integration of these moving-part valve mechanisms within mechanical engineering applications.
[F15C 3/04] using diaphragms -- F15C 3/04 covers fluid-circuit elements that incorporate diaphragms as the moving component, typically to transmit or control pressure changes in hydraulic or pneumatic systems. The diaphragm acts as a flexible barrier that separates fluid chambers, allowing precise displacement or modulation without direct mechanical linkage. This technology is used in devices such as pumps, valves and actuators for computing or control applications.
[F15C 3/06] using balls -- This subclass concerns fluid-circuit components that use a ball as a moving part to control flow, such as ball valves employed in computing or control applications. It covers designs where the ball opens, closes, or modulates a passage to regulate fluid movement.
[F15C 3/08] using reeds -- This subclass covers fluid-circuit components that employ reed switches or reed relays as the active element, using the magnetic reed to open or close the circuit for sensing, control or signalling functions within mechanical-engineering fluid systems.
[F15C 3/10] using nozzles or jet pipes -- This subclass covers fluid-circuit elements with moving parts that employ nozzles or jet pipes to create or direct fluid jets for control or computing functions, such as measurement, feedback or actuation. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering branch of fluid-circuit components primarily used in control systems.
[F15C 3/12] the nozzle or jet pipe being movable -- The code F15C 3/12 covers fluid-circuit components in which the nozzle or jet pipe can be repositioned, allowing adjustment of flow direction, shape or intensity. Such movable nozzles are used in engineering systems that require variable spray patterns or precise fluid control.
[F15C 3/14] the jet the nozzle being intercepted by a flap -- F15C 3/14 covers fluid-circuit devices in which a jet issued from a nozzle is intercepted by a movable flap to modify flow direction or intensity, typically for control or measurement functions. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, fluid-circuit elements used for computing or control, and circuit elements with moving parts.
[F15C 3/16] Oscillators -- F15C 3/16 covers oscillators, which are fluid-circuit components that produce periodic motion by converting a steady energy source into a repetitive movement. These devices typically involve pistons, diaphragms or other moving parts that generate regular oscillations for timing, signaling or control functions in mechanical or hydraulic systems.
[F15C 4/00] Circuit elements characterised by their special functions -- F15C 4/00 covers circuit elements whose purpose is defined by a specific function rather than by their physical form, including components such as valves, pumps, sensors or actuators that operate in fluid-circuits primarily for computing or control applications.
[F15C 5/00] Manufacture of fluid circuit elements -- The F15C 5/00 category covers methods and apparatus for producing fluid-circuit components-such as pumps, valves, channels and related parts-intended mainly for computing or control applications. It includes manufacturing steps, material choices and assembly techniques specific to these fluid-based devices.
[F15C 7/00] Hybrid elements, i.e. circuit elements having features according to groups -- F15C 7/00 is a CPC subgroup that deals with hybrid elements - circuit components that combine characteristics of two or more distinct groups within fluid-circuit devices used for computing or control. The classification covers devices such as combined valve-pump units, integrated sensors-actuators, and other multi-function components that perform several fluid-based operations in a single element.
[F15D] FLUID DYNAMICS, i.e. METHODS OR MEANS FOR INFLUENCING THE FLOW OF GASES OR LIQUIDS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means and methods not provided for in other classes, for influencing the flow of fluids relative to constraining surfaces and after leaving these surfaces, e.g. producing or removing turbulence, deflecting jets, guiding flow through bends in conduits, affecting distribution of fluid in a conduit, reducing fluid friction.
[F15D 1/00] Influencing flow of fluids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means and methods not provided for in other classes, for influencing the flow of fluids, i.e. the flow of liquids or gases relative to constraining surfaces and after leaving these surfaces, e.g. producing or removing turbulence, deflecting jets, guiding flow through bends in conduits, affecting distribution of fluid in a conduit, reducing fluid friction.
[F15D 1/0005] Baffle plates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Flow directing or obstructing plates, vanes or panels used to direct or modify a flow of fluid.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  2.  3.
[F15D 1/001] Flow of fluid from conduits such as pipes, sleeves, tubes, with equal distribution of fluid flow over the evacuation surface -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for equalising the velocity distribution across the cross section of a fluid conduit.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F15D 1/0015] Whirl chambers, e.g. vortex valves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A fluid flow influencing device situated between an inlet and an outlet, the device causing the fluid to form a swirl or vortex, without the need for moving parts to create the swirl or vortex.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  2.  3.
[F15D 1/002] by influencing the boundary layer -- F15D 1/002 covers inventions that alter a fluid's boundary layer to control its flow, such as surface shaping, suction, blowing or other means that modify shear stress and laminar-turbulent transition. These measures are used to improve performance of ducts, turbines, aircraft or other fluid-handling systems.
[F15D 1/0025] using passive means, i.e. without external energy supply -- F15D 1/0025 covers methods and devices that influence the flow of gases or liquids by passive means, meaning no external power source is applied. It includes the use of fixed shapes, gravity, pressure differentials, or other inherent fluid properties to control direction, speed, or distribution of the flow.
[F15D 1/003] comprising surface features, e.g. indentations or protrusions  F15D1/0055 ,  F15D1/006  take precedence -- This subclass covers devices or methods for influencing fluid flow that employ surface features such as indentations or protrusions to modify flow characteristics, and it is placed before the more specific subclasses dealing with particular feature geometries.
[F15D 1/0035] in the form of riblets -- F15D 1/0035 covers the use of riblets-small, streamwise surface protuberances-to modify fluid flow and reduce drag. The feature's geometry creates micro-scale grooves that influence boundary-layer behavior, improving efficiency in applications such as ship hulls, aircraft skins and pipe linings. This falls under methods for influencing the flow of gases or liquids within mechanical engineering.
[F15D 1/004] oriented essentially parallel to the direction of flow -- F15D 1/004 covers arrangements for influencing fluid flow where the functional elements are positioned essentially parallel to the direction of the stream, such as longitudinal vanes, ribs or slots that guide or shape the flow along its axis. This subclass lies within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under fluid-dynamics methods for affecting liquids or gases.
[F15D 1/0045] oriented essentially perpendicular to the direction of flow -- The subclass covers devices or structures that are positioned essentially perpendicular to the direction of fluid flow, such as baffles, plates or vanes placed across a stream. It relates to using this orientation to modify flow characteristics, pressure drop or mixing.
[F15D 1/005] in the form of dimples -- F15D 1/005 relates to methods or means for influencing fluid flow by using dimples on a surface. The dimple pattern creates localized turbulence that can reduce drag, improve lift or promote mixing in aerodynamic and hydraulic components, such as wings, blades or pipes.
[F15D 1/0055] comprising apertures in the surface, through which fluid is withdrawn from or injected into the flow -- A device or method that incorporates one or more openings in a surface to draw fluid from or inject fluid into a flowing stream, thereby influencing the flow characteristics. The apertures may be perforated plates, slots, nozzles or similar features designed for suction or injection within the fluid path. This arrangement is used in applications such as flow control, mixing, cooling or measurement in mechanical engineering fluid-dynamics systems.
[F15D 1/006] comprising moving surfaces, wherein the surface, or at least a portion thereof is moved or deformed by the fluid flow -- The class covers devices that incorporate surfaces which are set in motion or deformed directly by the flowing fluid, using that movement to influence the fluid's behavior. Typical examples include flexible blades, ribbons or other movable elements that react to the flow to regulate pressure, direction or thrust.
[F15D 1/0065] using active means, e.g. supplying external energy or injecting fluid -- F15D 1/0065 relates to fluid-flow control devices that employ active means, such as applying external energy or injecting a secondary fluid, to modify the movement of liquids or gases, for example in pumps, blowers, or jet actuators.
[F15D 1/007] comprising surfaces being moved by external supplied energy -- F15D 1/007 covers arrangements where surfaces that contact a fluid are driven by an externally supplied energy source to modify the flow characteristics. The moving surfaces may be blades, plates or other elements that actively alter fluid velocity or direction. This subclass falls within fluid dynamics methods for influencing flow within mechanical engineering.
[F15D 1/0075] comprising electromagnetic or electrostatic means for influencing the state of the fluid, e.g. for ionising the fluid or for generating a plasma -- F15D 1/0075 denotes devices that influence fluid flow by using electromagnetic or electrostatic techniques, such as ionising the fluid or generating plasma, to modify its state or behavior. It falls within the fluid-dynamics branch of mechanical engineering and covers methods that alter gas or liquid movement through electric or magnetic fields.
[F15D 1/008] comprising fluid injection or suction means -- The F15D 1/008 subclass comprises devices that modify fluid movement by means of fluid injection or suction, applying such mechanisms to control the flow of gases or liquids. It falls under mechanical engineering, fluid dynamics.
[F15D 1/0085] Methods of making characteristic surfaces for influencing the boundary layer -- The entry covers techniques for producing surface features such as riblets, dimples, or textured patterns that deliberately alter the boundary-layer flow of a liquid or gas. These characteristic surfaces are engineered to reduce drag, improve lift, or enhance mixing by affecting the fluid's near-wall behavior.
[F15D 1/009] by means of vortex rings -- F15D 1/009 pertains to technologies that influence fluid flow by creating vortex rings, which are toroidal swirls of fluid that can impart thrust, enhance mixing, or modify aerodynamic characteristics. It is classified under fluid-dynamics methods for controlling gases or liquids.
[F15D 1/0095] by means of injecting jet pulses of fluid wherein the injected fluid is taken from the fluid and re-injected again, e.g. synthetic jet actuators  F15D1/008  takes precedence -- This subclass deals with influencing fluid flow by injecting jet pulses of fluid that are drawn from the same flow and re-injected, as in synthetic jet actuators. It falls under F15D 1/0095, subordinate to F15D 1/008.
[F15D 1/02] in pipes or conduits -- This sub-group covers arrangements and devices for influencing the flow of liquids or gases inside pipes or other conduits, including valves, flow restrictors, and internal surface features designed to control velocity, pressure, or direction of the fluid.
[F15D 1/025] by means of orifice or throttle elements -- F15D 1/025 is a subclass of fluid-dynamics patents that concerns methods or devices for influencing the flow of liquids or gases by means of orifice or throttle elements such as plates, apertures or valves, creating a pressure drop to control flow rate.
[F15D 1/04] Arrangements of guide vanes in pipe elbows or duct bends -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Guiding elements arranged within pipe elbows or duct bends; the construction of a pipe conduit element for elbows (e.g. tee, wye), which typically reduces the flow losses.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  2.  3.
[F15D 1/06] by influencing the boundary layer -- F15D 1/06 concerns techniques that modify the boundary layer of a fluid in order to control its flow, such as devices or surface treatments that reduce drag, enhance lift, or promote mixing. The methods affect the thin region adjacent to solid surfaces, influencing overall fluid dynamics in mechanical engineering applications.
[F15D 1/065] Whereby an element is dispersed in a pipe over the whole length or whereby several elements are regularly distributed in a pipe -- F15D 1/065 relates to fluid-flow control in pipes where a material or device is continuously or periodically distributed along the pipe interior to modify the flow characteristics. The classification covers designs in which the dispersal element extends over the entire pipe length or appears at regular intervals, affecting turbulence, pressure drop, mixing or heat transfer.
[F15D 1/08] of jets leaving an orifice -- F15D 1/08 relates to devices or techniques that shape or control fluid jets as they leave an orifice, such as specific nozzle geometries, flow straighteners or other means used to modify the jet's direction, velocity, or pattern for improved performance.
[F15D 1/10] around bodies of solid material -- The F15D 1/10 subclass covers techniques, structures or devices that guide a fluid to pass around solid objects, such as ducts, pipes, baffles or other obstacles, in order to control the flow pattern, pressure loss or turbulence. It includes shaping, routing or shielding measures that influence how liquids or gases move when encountering solid material.
[F15D 1/12] by influencing the boundary layer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place.  1.  2.
[F15D 1/14] Diverting flow into alternative channels -- F15D 1/14 covers devices or methods that redirect a fluid stream into one or more alternative pathways, such as bypasses, splitters or distributers, to control the direction of flow in a system. This falls under fluid dynamics within mechanical engineering.
[F16] ENGINEERING ELEMENTS AND UNITS -- F16 in the Cooperative Patent Classification groups the basic mechanical components and assemblies used in engineering, such as bearings, gears, couplings, springs, seals and similar units that form part of machines or structures. It covers their design, construction, mounting and functional integration within broader mechanical systems.
[F16B] DEVICES FOR FASTENING OR SECURING CONSTRUCTIONAL ELEMENTS OR MACHINE PARTS TOGETHER, e.g. NAILS, BOLTS, CIRCLIPS, CLAMPS, CLIPS OR WEDGES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fastening elements for constructional parts per se, such as: clamps, clips, dowels, nails, staples, bolts without screw thread, pins, rivets, circlips, self-cutting screws, screws and bolts with thread, nuts, lock-nuts, threaded inserts, washers, hooks, suction cups  The connection of constructional elements such as key-type connections, shrinkage connections, joining of sheets and plates, connections of rods or tubes mutually or to flat surfaces at an angle, connections of constructional elements by sticking or pressing them together, joining of furniture, connections of constructional elements by a part of one element entering a hole in the other  The fastening elements or connection of elements in this group can be employed in multiple technical application fields. However, the focus is on the fastening element or the connection of elements itself.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Couplings transmitting rotation -> F16D  **Glossary:** - Connections using screw-thread elements: the connection is established by the screw-thread element - A screw acts on another element in order to establish a clamped connection: a connection using clamps
[F16B 1/00] Devices for securing together, or preventing relative movement between, constructional elements or machine parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Securing devices making use of shape memory alloys, hydraulic or pneumatic action or securing devices with marking colours or the like.
[F16B 1/02] Means for securing elements of mechanisms after operation -- This subclass covers devices that lock or hold moving parts of a machine in place after they have been positioned, preventing further motion. It includes mechanisms such as latches, catches, detents, brakes or other retaining features that engage after operation to keep components secured.
[F16B 1/04] disengaged by movement of the actuating member of the element -- F16B 1/04 covers fastening devices whose release is achieved by moving the actuating part of the element itself, such as bolts or clamps that unlock when the screw, lever or cam is turned or displaced. The subclass includes mechanisms where the actuation directly disengages the securing element without additional external forces.

[F16B 13/10] with separate gripping parts moved into their final position in relation to the body of the device by a separate operation -- This subclass (F16B 13/10) covers dowels or similar fasteners that are inserted into a pre-drilled hole and then have their gripping elements positioned by a separate operation after placement. The gripping parts are moved into their final location relative to the body of the device, providing a secure hold in the surrounding material.
[F16B 13/12] Separate metal dowel sleeves fastened by inserting the screw, nail or the like -- F16B 13/12 covers separate metal dowel sleeves that are secured in a hole by driving a screw, nail or similar fastener into the sleeve, providing a removable anchor for attaching components to walls or other structures.

[F16B 15/0023] Nail plates -- Nail plates are sheet-like fastening devices that join constructional elements or machine parts without using screw threads. They consist of a metal plate with protruding nails or pins that are driven into the materials to hold them together, offering a quick and secure joint for temporary or permanent assembly.

It falls within the F16B group for non-threaded fasteners and specifically concerns plates with teeth cut out of the material.

[F16B 19/04] Rivets -- F16B 19/04 designates rivets, a fast-ener type used in mechanical engineering to join components permanently by inserting a smooth-shank pin and then deforming its tail to secure the parts. The classification falls under devices for fastening or securing constructional elements or machine parts, specifically bolts that lack screw-thread. Rivets are employed where a non-removable, high-strength joint is required.
[F16B 19/05] Bolts fastening by swaged-on collars -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 19/06] Solid rivets made in one piece -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 19/08] Hollow rivets -- F16B 19/08 pertains to hollow rivets, a type of fastener that consists of a cylindrical sleeve with a hollow interior, designed to be deformed by a pressure tool to secure components together. The hollow cavity allows the rivet to be expanded or filled, providing a strong joint in sheet metal or lightweight structures, and may accommodate inserts or adhesives.
[F16B 19/083] Self-drilling rivets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 19/086] Self-piercing rivets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 19/10] fastened by expanding mechanically -- F16B 19/10 covers bolts that have no external screw-thread and are held in place by mechanical expansion of the shank or a surrounding sleeve. The expansion may be achieved by wedges, sleeves, or deformable sections that lock the bolt to the surrounding material.
[F16B 19/1027] Multi-part rivets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 19/1036] Blind rivets -- F16B 19/1036 designates blind rivets, a type of fastening device that can be installed from one side of a workpiece without access to the opposite side. They belong to the CPC subclass for bolts without screw-thread, under mechanical engineering elements for securing constructional or machine parts.
