[F16B 2/00] Fastenings for constructional elements or machine parts in general -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Clamps, i.e. gripping elements where the gripping action is effected by positive means other than the inheretent resistance to deformation of the material of the clamp  Clips, i.e. gripping elements where the gripping action is effected solely by inherent resistance to deformation of the material of the clip
[F16B 2/005] Means to increase the friction-coefficient -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 2005/0671] with unlocking by rotation -- This subclass covers mechanisms that release a locked sheet or page by a rotating element, such as a cam or knob, which when turned disengages the locking feature and permits the sheet to be moved.
[F16B 2005/0678] in abutting relationship -- The subclass F16B 2005/0678 covers transmission elements such as gears, pulleys or belts that are positioned side-by-side so that their working surfaces abut each other without intermeshing. It includes arrangements where elements engage by direct contact along a common line or surface rather than by overlapping teeth.
[F16B 2012/043] using carpentry joints other than mortise and tenon joints, e.g. using multiple tenons -- F16B 2012/043 covers the use of carpentry joints other than the traditional mortise-and-tenon, such as multiple-tenon or other fabricated wood-joint configurations. The classification applies to inventions that employ these alternative joint designs for fastening structural or mechanical components.
[F16B 2012/046] using mortise and tenon joints -- The CPC entry F16B 2012/046 covers machines or assemblies in which structural parts are connected using mortise-and-tenon joints, describing the design, construction or use of this type of interlocking joint to provide a secure, often reversible, mechanical connection.
[F16B 2012/103] Sleeves or dowels for connection fittings -- F16B 2012/103 covers sleeves or dowels designed to link or reinforce connection fittings in building or mechanical structures. These components are inserted into or around adjoining parts to transmit loads, align elements, or provide a removable joint without the need for welding or permanent fastening.
[F16B 2012/106] Connection bolts for connection fittings -- This CPC group covers bolts specifically designed to join connection fittings in fluid, hydraulic or pneumatic systems. The entry includes various forms of threaded fasteners that provide a secure, leak-tight link between pipes, valves or other components and may incorporate sealing or locking features.
[F16B 2012/145] Corner connections -- F16B 2012/145 covers corner-connection elements that join two structural members at a corner, such as brackets, clips, sleeves or fastening devices designed to link parts at right-angle or other angular positions. The classification is used for inventions that provide a means of securing or aligning adjoining components in constructions, machinery or assemblies where a corner joint is required.
[F16B 2012/2018] pin and drum securing device;  drum has cam surface to engage the head of the pin -- A pin-and-drum securing device consists of a drum that incorporates a cam surface designed to engage the head of a pin, locking the pin in place when the drum rotates. This arrangement is used in clamping, braking and similar mechanical applications to provide reliable, quick-release fastening.
[F16B 2012/2045] pin and drum securing device;  drum has screw to engage the head of the pin -- The code F16B 2012/2045 denotes a pin-and-drum securing device in which a screw mounted on the drum engages the head of a pin, locking the two together to transmit torque or prevent axial movement, commonly used in presses, rollers and similar machinery.
[F16B 2012/2072] Pin and drum securing devices;  Drums having lever with cam surface to engage the head of the pin -- This subclass covers mechanisms that lock a pin to a rotating drum using a lever equipped with a cam surface that catches the pin head, providing a simple, repeatable fastening for equipment such as conveyors, winches or rotary tools.
[F16B 2012/2081] having a fitting providing slanted access for a screwdriver as actuator -- F16B 2012/2081 falls within the pipe fitting and joining group and covers fittings that include a slanted opening designed to receive a screwdriver, allowing the screwdriver to act as the actuator for tightening or loosening the component. The angled access simplifies tool insertion and improves leverage during assembly.
[F16B 2012/209] having an integrated lever as actuator -- F16B 2012/209 classifies machines or devices for moving, handling, treating or supporting works that incorporate an integrated lever used as the actuator. The lever is built into the apparatus and directly provides the controlling force or motion, rather than being a separate external component. This feature is considered when searching patents for lever-actuated hydraulic, pneumatic or electric systems.
[F16B 2012/403] with inserts for joining tubes coaxially -- F16B 2012/403 covers pipe or tube fittings that join two or more tubes along a common axis by using an internal insert. The insert aligns the tube ends and provides a secure, coaxial connection, often in applications such as fluid transport, hydraulic systems, or structural tubing assemblies.
[F16B 2012/406] Cove joints for joining two cylindrical members -- Cove joints are a type of fixed joint used to connect two cylindrical parts, typically pipes or tubes, by providing a recessed, complementary surface that allows a tight, sealed assembly. They are employed in fluid-handling, hydraulic and pneumatic systems where a secure, leak-proof connection between aligned cylindrical members is required.
[F16B 2012/443] with two-dimensional corner element, the legs thereof being inserted in hollow frame members -- The entry covers a fastening component that features a two-dimensional corner element, such as an L-shaped head, whose legs are designed to be inserted into hollow frame members. This arrangement allows the element to secure or align frame sections by fitting the legs into the cavities of the frame profile. It falls under the CPC subclass for screws, bolts, nuts, studs and similar fasteners.
[F16B 2012/446] with three-dimensional corner element, the legs thereof being inserted in hollow frame members -- The CPC class F16B covers hinges, joints or other moving parts of frames. Subclass 2012/446 is for devices that incorporate a three-dimensional corner element whose legs are inserted into hollow frame members, providing a fixed angular connection without external fasteners. It is used in construction of frames, scaffolding or modular structures.
[F16B 2012/463] for wooden members without additional elements -- The CPC entry F16B 2012/463 covers inventions that use wooden members as functional elements in shaft, axle, drive or coupling arrangements without any auxiliary reinforcing parts. It pertains to mechanical components where the wood itself carries the load or transmits motion, distinguishing them from hybrid or metal-based solutions.
[F16B 2012/466] using mortise and tenon joints -- F16B 2012/466 concerns the use of mortise-and-tenon joints for mechanically fastening components. It covers designs where a projecting tenon is inserted into a matching mortise cavity to achieve a secure, often detachable, connection in machines, structures or other assemblies without relying on separate fasteners.
[F16B 2012/505] having a corner insert which is inserted in mitered profiled members -- F16B 2012/505 covers fluid-power pipe or tube joints that use a corner insert placed in mitered, profiled members to form the connection. The insert fits into the cut ends of the profiles, providing reinforcement, alignment and sealing for the joint. This subclass relates to designs where the insert is a distinct component that is inserted during assembly.
[F16B 2013/006] with sealing means -- F16B 2013/006 designates valves, gates, taps or similar fluid-control devices that incorporate sealing means to prevent leakage between moving and stationary parts, such as packings, gaskets, O-rings or other sealing arrangements.
[F16B 2013/007] to be fastened in undercut holes -- This subclass covers fastening elements designed to be secured inside recessed or undercut cavities, where the part locks by expanding or engaging a shape within the hole. It includes pins, clips, inserts or similar devices that achieve a form-fit hold by deformation or mechanical interlock when placed in an undercut hole.
[F16B 2013/008] used for mining purposes -- F16B 2013/008 designates inventions whose principal function is to serve mining operations, covering devices, arrangements or methods that control, direct or regulate fluids, gases or powders in activities such as drilling, ore transport, ventilation, de-watering or mineral processing. It groups patents that apply fluid-handling technology specifically to the extraction and treatment of mineral resources.
[F16B 2013/009] Double sleeve dowels, i.e. the first sleeve is fixed in a hole by the action of a second sleeve and one of the sleeves receives a nail, a screw or the like -- F16B 2013/009 relates to double-sleeve dowels, a fastening device where a first sleeve is secured in a hole by the expansion of a second sleeve that surrounds it. One of the sleeves is designed to receive a nail, screw or similar fastener, allowing the dowel to lock the fastener in place.
[F16B 2013/147] Grout with reinforcing elements or with fillers, e.g. fibres, flakes, balls, wires -- F16B 2013/147 covers grout mixtures that contain reinforcing elements or filler materials such as fibres, flakes, balls or wires. The classification is used for patents on compositions that improve the mechanical strength, durability or handling properties of grout in construction applications.
[F16B 2013/148] Means for inhibiting adhesion between dowel or anchor bolt parts and the surrounding grouting composition -- Means for preventing the bond between dowels, anchor bolts or similar fastening elements and the cementitious or resinous grout that surrounds them, such as release agents, coatings, sleeves, or surface treatments that keep the grout from adhering to the metal parts. This facilitates removal, adjustment or reuse of the anchored components after installation.
[F16B 2015/0061] Multipiece-plates -- F16B 2015/0061 covers multipiece plates, which are plates made from two or more separate components that can be assembled or disassembled, typically used in machinery, equipment or structural applications where modularity, adjustability, or ease of maintenance is required.
[F16B 2015/0069] with nails on both sides -- F16B 2015/0069 classifies fasteners that carry projecting nails on both faces of the element, allowing one piece to secure two adjoining sheets or panels in a single operation, a solution commonly employed in construction, flooring and other applications that join layered materials.
[F16B 2015/0076] with provisions for additional fastening means, e.g. hooks, holes for separate screws or nails, adhesive -- The CPC class F16B 2015/0076 designates fixing or mounting devices that incorporate extra fastening provisions such as hooks, pre-drilled holes for separate screws or nails, or adhesive-ready surfaces, enabling additional attachment methods beyond the primary securing element. This subclass is used for components where supplementary means of fixation are integral to the design.
[F16B 2015/0084] with marks to indicate where to strike with the hammer -- F16B 2015/0084 refers to fasteners such as screws or bolts that are provided with visual or tactile marks indicating the precise location where a hammer should be struck. The markings guide correct installation, helping to prevent damage or mis-alignment during assembly.
[F16B 2019/006] made in a plurality of pieces -- F16B 2019/006 classifies valves and pressure-regulating devices that are fabricated from several separate components rather than a single monolithic part. It covers designs where the overall assembly is built by joining multiple pieces.
[F16B 2019/045] Coated rivets -- F16B 2019/045 relates to rivets that have an applied coating to improve corrosion resistance, adhesion, wear resistance or electrical conductivity. The coating may be metallic, polymeric, ceramic or composite and is applied by plating, spraying, dipping or similar processes. The classification includes both the rivet and its manufacturing method.
[F16B 2019/055] deformed by an electro-magnetic action -- F16B 2019/055 relates to pumps whose working parts are intentionally deformed through electromagnetic forces to modify their shape or displacement characteristics. The classification covers devices and methods that use magnetic or electric fields to induce deformation of pump components, thereby influencing fluid handling performance.
[F16B 2019/1009] hollow or single-part rivets fastened by a pull-through mandrel -- This CPC subclass covers rivets that are hollow or formed as a single piece and are installed by inserting them through a workpiece and then pulling a mandrel through to expand or lock the rivet in place. The classification applies to the design and method of fastening using a pull-through mandrel.
[F16B 2019/1018] hollow or single-part rivets fastened by a drive pin -- The F16B 2019/1018 entry covers rivets that are either hollow or formed as a single piece and are secured by inserting a drive pin that expands or locks the rivet in place. It pertains to fastening elements where the pin provides the mechanical action needed to seat the rivet within the workpieces, enabling a permanent joint without additional fastening components.
[F16B 2/02] Clamps, i.e. with gripping action effected by positive means other than the inherent resistance to deformation of the material of the fastening -- F16B 2/02 refers to clamps that achieve a secure hold by applying a positive gripping force through a mechanical means such as jaws, levers, screws or hydraulic actuation, rather than relying on the inherent resistance of the material being fastened. These devices are used to fasten constructional elements or machine parts by actively squeezing or clamping the workpieces together.
[F16B 2031/002] Breakbolts loosening due to an electromagnetic action -- The CPC class F16B 2031/002 refers to fastening devices, specifically breakbolts, that are released or loosened by an electromagnetic action. It includes mechanisms where a magnetic or electric field generates a force to overcome the bolt's retaining torque, enabling automatic or remote disengagement for safety or control purposes.
[F16B 2031/022] using an ultrasonic transducer -- F16B 2031/022 covers fluid-handling components that incorporate ultrasonic transducers for purposes such as measuring flow, detecting level, or controlling pumps, valves or other hydraulic elements. It pertains to the use of ultrasonic sensing technology within hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F16B 2031/046] by means of an explosive charge -- F16B 2031/046 covers pumps in which the working fluid is moved by the sudden expansion of an explosive charge, such as a cartridge or detonator, rather than by conventional mechanical or hydraulic power sources. The classification applies to devices that use the explosion to generate a pressure pulse that drives a piston or diaphragm, typical in fire-extinguishing, rescue or artillery-loading equipment.
[F16B 2033/025] with left-hand thread -- F16B 2033/025 classifies fasteners that use a left-hand thread, meaning the thread advances toward the head when turned counter-clockwise. The code is applied to screws, bolts, nuts or other devices where a left-handed helix is required for assembly, operation or safety reasons. It falls under the CPC section for screw threads and related securing elements.
[F16B 2037/007] with a blind hole -- F16B 2037/007 refers to reciprocating pumps that incorporate a blind hole-an enclosed cavity without a through-passage-within the pump barrel, cylinder or piston assembly, typically used to house a valve, spring or other component while keeping the internal fluid sealed. This subcategory covers designs where the blind hole forms part of the pump's sealing or actuation mechanism.
[F16B 2/04] internal, i.e. with spreading action -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 2043/008] with a cavity for receiving the bolt head in order to make a flush surface -- F16B 2043/008 classifies fastening elements that include a cavity intended to receive the bolt head so the assembled part presents a flush surface, concealing the head for aesthetic or protective reasons.
[F16B 2/06] external, i.e. with contracting action -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 2/065] using screw-thread elements  F16B2/08  -  F16B2/12  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 2/08] using bands -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 2/10] using pivoting jaws -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 21/00] Means for preventing relative axial movement of a pin, spigot, shaft or the like and a member surrounding it -- **Definition:** This place covers:  - Means for preventing axial movement of a pin or the like by rotation, snap in features, engagement in a keyhole slot, separate parts, such as circlips and other locking members.  - Releasable stud-and-socket connections where the stud or the socket or both parts being resilient.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rivets or deformable spigots -> F16B19/04

[F16B 21/14] Details of locking-pins or split-pins -- F16B 21/14 covers the detailed design of locking-pins or split-pins that prevent axial movement of a pin, spigot, shaft or similar member within a surrounding component. It includes features such as retaining heads, spring mechanisms, tapered shanks and other means that secure the pin in place.
[F16B 21/16] with grooves or notches in the pin or shaft -- This subclass covers devices that prevent axial movement of a pin, spigot or shaft relative to a surrounding member by incorporating grooves or notches in the pin or shaft. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units and devices for fastening constructional elements or machine parts.

[F16B 21/205] the connecting means having gripping edges in the form of a helix -- F16B 21/205 covers fastening elements that stop axial movement of a pin, shaft or similar part by using gripping edges shaped as a helix. The helical surfaces engage the surrounding component to lock it in place.

[F16B 2/20] Clips, i.e. with gripping action effected solely by the inherent resistance to deformation of the material of the fastening -- Clips in this group fasten parts by using only the material's own resistance to deformation, without additional springs or separate moving components; the gripping action is achieved through elastic flexure of the clip itself, as seen in spring steel retaining rings, leaf clips and similar devices that secure constructional elements or machine parts.
[F16B 2200/00] Constructional details of connections not covered for in other groups of this subclass -- F16B 2200/00 covers the design and construction features of fastening connections that do not fall into any other specific group of the subclass, such as unusual or specialised arrangements of bolts, clips or wedges used in mechanical assemblies.

[F16B 2200/40] Clamping arrangements where clamping parts are received in recesses of elements to be connected -- F16B 2200/40 covers clamping arrangements in which the clamping element is placed into a recess, groove or cavity formed in the parts to be joined, creating a secure, hidden connection without external projections. It includes designs such as recessed bolt or screw heads, snap-in clamps, and other devices that lock by filling a machined or molded depression in the adjoining components.
[F16B 2200/403] Threaded clamping parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 2200/406] Clamping parts being collars, bushings or wedges -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 2200/50] Flanged connections -- F16B 2200/50 addresses the constructional details of flanged connections, where a protruding rim or collar on a component is joined to another part by bolting, welding, sealing or other fastening means. It covers specific features, reinforcement and assembly methods for these connections that are not covered in other groups of the subclass.
[F16B 2200/503] the flange being separate from the elements to be connected -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 2200/506] bolted or riveted -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 2200/509] clamped -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 2200/60] Coupler sealing means -- The F16B 2200/60 subclass covers sealing devices incorporated in couplers that join pipes, hoses or other conduits, ensuring leak-free connections under pressure, vibration or temperature changes. It includes gaskets, O-rings, sealing lips and related features designed to prevent fluid escape where the coupling members engage. This group addresses specific constructional details of such seals not covered in broader fastening categories.
[F16B 2200/63] Frangible connections -- F16B 2200/63 covers frangible connections, i.e., fastening or securing arrangements designed to break or release at a predetermined load to protect the surrounding structure. The classification includes the design, geometry and material features that cause the joint to fail in a controlled manner, such as breakaway bolts or shear pins.
[F16B 2200/65] Miter joints -- F16B 2200/65 covers miter joints, which are angled connections where two components are cut and joined at a complementary angle, typically 45°. The classification includes design and construction details of such joints used to fasten structural or machine parts when other specific connection groups do not apply.
[F16B 2200/67] Rigid angle couplings -- Rigid angle couplings are devices that securely join two shafts at a fixed angular relationship, typically 90°, 45° or other predetermined angles, without allowing relative angular movement. They transmit torque and axial loads while maintaining precise alignment, and are used in machinery where a permanent, robust connection is required.
[F16B 2200/69] Redundant disconnection blocking means -- F16B 2200/69 belongs to the subclass for mechanical fastening devices and covers redundant disconnection-blocking means that are incorporated into a joint to prevent unintended release of the connection. Such measures include secondary locks, interlocks or safety wedges that act if the primary fastener fails, ensuring continued structural integrity.
[F16B 2200/71] Blocking disengagement of catches or keys -- F16B 2200/71 pertains to fastening devices where a catch or key is intentionally prevented from unintentionally releasing, typically by a blocking or lockout element that holds the device in the engaged position. It covers mechanisms such as mechanical stops, secondary latches or wedges that obstruct disengagement.
[F16B 2200/73] Cam locks or thread locks -- F16B 2200/73 pertains to cam-locks and thread-locks, devices that secure parts by using a cam action or a threaded element to lock connections in place. It addresses constructional details of fastening that are not covered by other groups within the subclass for mechanical fastening elements.
[F16B 2200/75] Fasteners made by sintering powders -- The subclass F16B 2200/75 covers fasteners manufactured by sintering metal or other powders, such as bolts, pins or clips produced through powder-metallurgy techniques. These parts are used to join or secure constructional elements or machine components, and the classification applies where the connection details are not addressed in other groups of the subclass.
[F16B 2200/77] Use of a shape-memory material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group:
[F16B 2200/79] Friction-reducing coatings -- This subclass covers friction-reducing surface treatments or coatings applied to fastening devices such as bolts, clips, clamps or wedges to lower assembly resistance, improve ease of insertion and reduce wear during operation.
[F16B 2200/81] Use of a material of the hooks-and-loops type -- The entry F16B 2200/81 covers applications where a hook-and-loop (Velcro-type) material is employed to fasten or secure constructional elements or machine parts. It concerns the use of this reusable, non-permanent joining method in devices such as bolts, clips, clamps or wedges, when the connection details are not otherwise classified within the subclass.
[F16B 2200/83] Use of a magnetic material -- F16B 2200/83 covers fastening or securing devices that incorporate a magnetic material, describing constructional details of magnetic connections used to hold or release components, where such features are not captured by other groups in the subclass.
[F16B 2200/85] Ceramic-to-metal-connections -- The CPC subclass F16B 2200/85 concerns the design and manufacture of connections joining ceramic components to metal parts, including bonding, brazing, mechanical fastening, or other joining methods where a ceramic element is attached to a metal element. It falls under devices for fastening or securing constructional elements or machine parts together, specifically constructional details of connections not covered elsewhere in the subclass, such as ceramic inserts, metal pins, or composite fixtures.

[F16B 2200/99] Fasteners with means for avoiding incorrect assembly or positioning -- F16B 2200/99 covers fasteners that incorporate features to prevent incorrect assembly or misalignment, such as self-locking mechanisms, positioning guides, or error-proof designs. These devices are used in mechanical engineering to ensure secure, error-free connections where traditional fasteners might be assembled incorrectly.

[F16B 37/00] Nuts or like thread-engaging members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Nuts, i.e. fasteners with a threaded hole.
[F16B 37/002] cutting threads during screwing; removing paint or dirt layers covering threaded shanks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/005] into which threads are cut during screwing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/02] made of thin sheet material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Nuts made of thin sheet material used as lock-nuts -> F16B39/14
[F16B 37/04] Devices for fastening nuts to surfaces, e.g. sheets, plates -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Nuts fastened behind a wall by a toggle mechanism -> F16B13/0808 - Threaded inserts -> F16B37/122 - Measures against loss of bolts, nuts or pins -> F16B41/002
[F16B 37/041] Releasable devices  F16B37/044 ,  F16B37/045  take precedence -- F16B 37/041 covers nuts and comparable thread-engaging members used to secure fasteners, including split, lock, and self-locking types that mate with threaded elements to provide a releasable connection. Devices defined in the subordinate groups 37/044 and 37/045 are treated as having priority over this broader category.
[F16B 37/042] locking by rotation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/043] with snap action -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/044] Nut cages -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/045] specially adapted for fastening in channels, e.g. sliding bolts, channel nuts -- The subclass F16B 37/045 covers fastening elements that are built to fit within grooves or channels of a structure, such as sliding bolts and channel nuts. These devices allow secure attachment while permitting adjustment or movement along the channel and are used in mechanical assemblies and construction.
[F16B 37/046] with resilient means for urging the nut inside the channel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/047] Barrel nuts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/048] Non-releasable devices  F16B37/044 ,  F16B37/045  and  F16B37/06  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/06] by means of welding or riveting -- This subclass covers nuts or similar thread-engaging members that are attached to a component by welding or riveting rather than by screwing onto a bolt. It includes designs where the nut is permanently joined to the workpiece through fusion or a rivet-type mechanical bond.
[F16B 37/061] by means of welding -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/062] by means of riveting -- F16B 37/062 covers nuts or similar thread-engaging members that are attached to a workpiece by riveting rather than by being screwed onto a bolt, including the design of the rivet and the method of securing the nut in place. It falls under devices for fastening constructional elements and pertains to mechanical engineering applications where a permanent, rivet-based connection is required.
[F16B 37/064] with the use of separate rivets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/065] by deforming the material of the nut -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/067] the material of the nut being deformed by a threaded member generating axial movement of the threaded part of the nut, e.g. blind rivet type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/068] by deforming the material of the support, e.g. the sheet or plate -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/08] Quickly-detachable nuts, e.g. consisting of two or more parts -- F16B 37/08 covers nuts that can be removed or installed rapidly without tools, typically formed from two or more interlocking parts such as split, wing or cam-type nuts. These devices engage threads to secure components but allow quick release for maintenance or adjustment.
[F16B 37/0807] Nuts engaged from the end of the bolt, e.g. axially slidable nuts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/0814] movable along the bolt after tilting the nut -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.

[F16B 37/0835] with balls engaging threads or grooves on the shaft of the bolt -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/0842] fastened to the threaded bolt with snap-on-action, e.g. push-on nuts for stud bolts  F16B37/0857  takes precedence; snap-on-action of a pin, spigot, shaft or the like and a member surrounding it  F16B21/06 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/085] with at least one unthreaded portion in both the nut and the bolt -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/0857] with the threaded portions of the nut engaging the thread of the bolt by the action of one or more springs or resilient retaining members  F16B37/0821  and  F16B37/0835  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/0864] with the threaded portions of the nut engaging the thread of the bolt by pressing or rotating an external retaining member such as a cap, a nut, a ring or a sleeve  F16B37/0835  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 37/0871] engaging the bolt laterally, i.e. without the need to engage the end of the bolt -- The code refers to fastening devices that grip a bolt from the side of its shank rather than the head, using lateral engagement methods such as wedges, cams or split rings. This allows the bolt to be secured without needing access to its end, facilitating installation in confined spaces or where the bolt head is inaccessible.

[F16B 39/22] in which the locking takes place during screwing down or tightening -- F16B 39/22 covers fastening systems in which the locking of screws, bolts or nuts is achieved during the screwing-down or tightening action itself, rather than by a separate post-assembly step. It applies to devices that integrate a locking function directly into the tightening process, ensuring secure, vibration-resistant connections.

[F16B 45/002] Eyes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The figures below are illustrative examples which fall within the scope of this subgroup. In the figures are shown examples of eyes E.
[F16B 45/005] characterised by the material -- F16B 45/005 covers hooks used for fastening or securing constructional elements or machine parts in which the defining feature is the material from which the hook is made, such as metal, plastic, composite or other substances. The classification distinguishes these hooks from others based on the specific material properties that affect strength, durability, corrosion resistance or weight.
[F16B 45/008] plastics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The figures below are illustrative examples which fall within the scope of this subgroup. In the figures are shown examples of a plastic hook.
