[F16B 9/00] Connections of rods or tubular parts to flat surfaces at an angle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-torque transmitting connections of rods or tubular parts to flat surfaces at an angle, i.e. the rods or tubular parts are non-parallel with respect to the flat surfaces. The rods or tubular parts can be connected perpendicularly to the flat surfaces. The connections can involve an intermediate member.  The rods or tubular parts and the flat surfaces are constructional elements or machine parts.  The rods or tubular parts are elongated elements, of any cross-section, that do not convey fluids. Rods or tubular elements may be flexible, but not flaccid, e.g. a cord is not a rod or tubular element in the sense of F16B9/00 .  Flat surfaces are surfaces that are planar at least locally around the connection location.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Connections of rods to flat surfaces whereby a part of or on one member enters a hole in the other member and involving plastic deformation -> F16B17/006
[F16B 9/01] Welded or bonded connections -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 9/02] Detachable connections -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Connections that can be detached without breaking any part.  Detachable and releasable are synonyms.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Connections by way of an intermediate member -> F16B9/05 - Connections involving plastic or elastic deformation -> F16B9/07 - Connections where rods and flat surfaces interengage by projections and mating sockets -> F16B9/09
[F16B 9/05] by way of an intermediate member -- This subclass covers arrangements that join rods or tubular elements to a flat surface at an angle by inserting an intermediate member such as a bracket, sleeve, or fitting, which transmits the load between the two parts and provides the required angular connection.
[F16B 9/052] the intermediate member having a radial flange secured to the flat surface -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 9/054] the intermediate member being threaded -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 9/056] the intermediate member extending through the flat surface; the rod or tubular part extending through the flat surface -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 9/058] the intermediate member being secured to the rod by transverse fasteners -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16B 9/07] involving plastic or elastic deformation when assembling  involving plastic deformation with a part of or on one member entering a hole in the other  F16B17/006 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Connections with a part of or on one member entering a hole in the other and involving plastic deformation -> F16B17/006  **Glossary:** - Elastic deformation: The parts recover their initial shape after deformation - Plastic deformation: The parts do not recover their initial shape after deformation
[F16B 9/09] rods and flat surfaces interengaging by projections and mating sockets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16C] SHAFTS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The elements of Subclass F16C are various general mechanical engineering elements or units which have in common that they all relate to:  In general Subclass F16C it encompasses:  The elongated elements to transmit linear, rotary or combined movement, e.g. push-pull type movement, comprise:  The supporting elements allowing relative rotary, linear or combined motion comprise:  Since the individual mechanical engineering elements of Subclass F16C are not a coherent group they will be defined individually in more detail below.  General distribution of subject-matter in Subclass F16C (index):  Flexible Mechanical elements for transmitting movement / force F16C1/00 :  Support elements allowing relative displacement between parts:  Bearings  Bearings with sliding or rolling contact:  For parts which both rotate and move linearly F16C31/00  Other bearings ( F16C32/00 )  Details or accessories of bearings:  Bearings are general mechanical engineering elements which support or guide and are adapted to position one part moving with respect to another part of an arrangement, i.e. to allow constrained relative motion between the two parts. One of the parts supports the load imposed thereon by the other part. The bearing is intended to reduce friction between the two relatively moving parts, which are typically larger than the bearing itself.  It is noted that apart from bearings many other objects, such as vehicle wheels, conveyor rollers etc., also meet the above criteria. These objects are classified elsewhere.  Further it is noted here that in general the expression "bearing" is also used for supports between parts which are (quasi) stationary.  Due to the lack of relative movement such bearing are not bearings in the sense of this section of Subclass F16C .  Apart from the bearings per se the section "bearings" also covers:  Bearings are distinguished and classified in the respective groups and groups according to  Movement between the two parts:  Principle of operation, i.e. the means to reduce friction.  Sliding contact, e.g. plain bearings (bushings, journal bearings, sleeve bearings) including:  Loads:  Combination of principles can be used within a bearing or bearing arrangement, e.g. to support different load directions or to take account of various operating conditions, e.g. speed.  **Glossary:** - rotary engineering element: any engineering element, other than a rotating element used in gearing, clutches, couplings or brakes, which rotates so far as its features are affected only by the fact that it rotates. - bearing: a component of a machine or mechanism that is positioned between two relatively movable elements of the machine or mechanism and that has a surface formed for sliding or rolling contact with these elements when they move relative to each other for the sole function of reducing the friction that would otherwise result from their relative movement. - connecting-rod or link pivoted at both ends: a reciprocating or oscillating elongated member of a machine or mechanism that is intended to be pivotally connected to and positioned between two relatively movable elements of the machine or mechanism for the purpose of transmitting force or conveying motion between the elements when it reciprocates or oscillates. - axle: an elongated element to support a rotating part, e.g. a wheel or roll. loads are bending moments and radial load. It may be stationary or rotating with the part fixed thereto. - shaft: an elongated element, normally a rod of circular cross-section, that rotates about its longitudinal axis to transmit torque, e.g. by conveying motion from a gear wheel supported by it to another part of a machine or mechanism. - crankshaft: a shaft with one or more cranks for turning reciprocating or oscillating motion into rotary motion. - flexible shaft: an elongated element, that is bendable along its length and rotates about its longitudinal axis to transmit torque or convey rotary motion. - Bowden cable: a control cable consisting of an elongated member enclosed within a housing bendable along its length and shiftable along its longitudinal axis relative to its housing to transmit motion or force. - roll, drum, disc: an element of a machine or mechanism in the form of a roller that has a generally curved surface that contacts work or (linear) moving part and revolves about its axis when the work shifts relative to it.
[F16C 1/00] Flexible shafts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  There are two distinct groups of elements which, apart from being elongated and flexible, have a different function:  The shaft itself or the transmitting means themselves provide flexibility.  It is noted that:  The two groups of elements are thus related to different fields (see limiting and informative references below).
[F16C 1/02] for conveying rotary movements -- F16C 1/02 covers flexible shafts that transmit rotary motion between components whose axes are not collinear. These devices, like spiral or braided shafts, convey torque while accommodating angular, radial or axial misalignment, enabling power transfer in compact or constrained mechanical arrangements.
[F16C 1/04] Articulated shafts -- The CPC class F16C 1/04 covers articulated shafts, which are flexible power-transmission shafts made of linked segments that can bend relative to one another while still conveying torque. They are employed where a rigid shaft cannot follow the required path, such as in automotive drivetrains, robotics, and various industrial machines.
[F16C 1/06] with guiding sheathing, tube or box -- F16C 1/06 pertains to flexible shafts that are provided with a guiding sheathing, tube or box, which encases the shaft to protect it, maintain alignment and guide its motion while transmitting rotary power. This arrangement is used where a flexible power transmission must be shielded from contaminants or mechanical damage.
[F16C 1/08] End connections -- F16C 1/08 falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification for flexible shafts and covers end connections for such shafts, including couplings, fittings, sleeves, clamps or other means used to attach the shaft to a drive, load or other component. It addresses the design, geometry, securing method and material features that enable reliable torque transmission while accommodating the shaft's flexibility. This subclass groups inventions that improve connection strength, ease of assembly or alignment of flexible shaft ends.
[F16C 1/10] Means for transmitting linear movement in a flexible sheathing, e.g. "Bowden-mechanisms" -- F16C 1/10 classifies devices that convey linear motion through a flexible sheath, such as Bowden cables, by means of a moving core inside a protective tube. These mechanisms are used where straight push rods cannot be employed and are part of the broader shaft category in mechanical engineering.
[F16C 11/00] Pivots -- F16C 11/00 pertains to pivots, the rotational joints that connect a shaft to a supporting structure while allowing angular movement. It includes designs such as pins, hinges, and sleeves that transmit torque or motion between parts, and covers their mounting, bearing arrangements, and material choices.
[F16C 1/101] Intermediate connectors for joining portions of split flexible shafts and/or sheathings -- Intermediate connectors for joining portions of split flexible shafts or their sheathings are devices that link separated shaft segments while preserving torsional transmission and flexibility. The classification covers couplings, sleeves, clamps and similar fittings designed to securely re-assemble or extend split shafts and protect the enclosed cables or wires.
[F16C 1/102] Arrangements to mount end fittings of the sheathings to support walls or brackets -- F16C 1/102 covers solutions for attaching the terminal fittings of flexible shaft sheaths to supporting structures such as walls or brackets. These arrangements secure the sheath ends, facilitating reliable power transmission while allowing alignment and mounting flexibility.
[F16C 11/02] Trunnions -- F16C 11/02 covers trunnions, which are cylindrical sleeves or projections that serve as pivots or bearing points for rotating or oscillating parts in machinery. They provide a supported axis for shafts, wheels or other components and are typically fixed to a frame while allowing attached parts to turn or swing.
[F16C 1/103] to a hole in the wall or bracket -- The subclass F16C 1/103 relates to flexible shafts that are attached by being inserted through a hole in a wall, bracket or similar supporting part, providing a secured linking point for power transmission while allowing angular misalignment.
[F16C 11/04] Pivotal connections -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Articulated joints which are primarily designed to introduce push-pull movements to an elongated mechanical engineering element, such as connecting rods (cf. F16C7/00 ) and "Bowden-cables" (cf. F16C1/10 ).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Hinges for doors, windows, or wings -> E05D - Devices for moving wings into open or closed position -> E05F - Yielding couplings, i.e. with means permitting movement between the connected parts during the drive -> F16D3/00 - Rubber springs with stiff outer sleeve and inner sleeve or pin -> F16F1/38 - Hinges of laptops -> G06F1/1616 - Hinges of mobile phones -> H04M1/0206
[F16C 11/045] with at least a pair of arms pivoting relatively to at least one other arm, all arms being mounted on one pin  crank-pins  F16C11/02 -- F16C 11/045 pertains to mechanical engineering elements, specifically shafts and pivots. This classification covers designs with at least a pair of arms that pivot relative to another arm, all mounted on a single pin or crank-pin, as detailed in F16C11/02.
[F16C 1/105] to a slot in the bracket -- F16C 1/105 concerns flexible shafts that are secured to a bracket by fitting into a slot, allowing the shaft to pivot or translate while remaining guided. This arrangement is used in power transmission where compact, adjustable coupling between a rotating shaft and a mounted component is required.
[F16C 1/106] Plurality of transmitting means, e.g. two or more parallel "Bowden cables" -- F16C 1/106 covers devices that use two or more parallel Bowden-type cables or similar transmitting elements to convey motion or force along a flexible shaft. The classification includes arrangements where multiple cables share load, provide redundancy, or enable coordinated actuation in mechanical systems.
[F16C 11/06] Ball-joints -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Universal joints in which flexibility is produced by means of pivots or sliding or rolling connecting parts -> F16D3/16 - Supports for apparatus with ball-joint heads -> F16M11/14
[F16C 11/0604] Construction of the male part -- F16C 11/0604 pertains to the specific construction details of the male component in pivot mechanisms, which are a subset of shafts used in mechanical engineering. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system jointly maintained by the European Patent Office (EPO) and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO).
[F16C 11/0609] made from two or more parts -- F16C 11/0609 covers pivots that are fabricated by assembling two or more separate elements into a single shaft component. These composite pivots may be joined by welding, bolting, adhesives or other means to form a functional rotating joint within mechanical systems.
[F16C 11/0614] the female part of the joint being open on two sides -- A CPC subclass F16C 11/0614 covers pivot joints in which the female component of the connection is open on two opposite sides, allowing coupling to a male shaft while providing clearance for assembly or service. This feature is used in mechanical engineering shaft assemblies where a partially open housing simplifies insertion or removal of the rotating element.
[F16C 11/0619] the female part comprising a blind socket receiving the male part -- This CPC subclass (F16C 11/0619) relates to a pivot's female component that consists of a blind socket designed to receive the male part. It covers mechanical-engineering shaft elements where the socket is closed at one end, forming an enclosed receiving chamber for the corresponding male shaft.
[F16C 11/0623] Construction or details of the socket member -- The subclass F16C 11/0623 covers the design and construction of the socket component that receives a pivot or shaft, detailing features such as shape, attachment method, reinforcement, sealing or bearing arrangements that enable the socket to accommodate and guide the rotating or pivoting element.
[F16C 11/0628] with linings -- The subclass F16C 11/0628 covers pivots that incorporate linings, such as sleeves, bushings or other material inserts that provide a bearing surface, reduce wear or protect the pivot elements. It applies to shafts or rotating parts where a lining is integral to the pivot's function, typically to improve lubrication, adjust clearances or accommodate differing materials.
[F16C 11/0633] the linings being made of plastics -- The F16C 11/0633 subgroup covers shaft pivots whose internal linings are made of plastic materials, used to reduce wear and friction in mechanical engineering applications. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, pivots.
[F16C 11/0638] characterised by geometrical details -- F16C 11/0638 designates a subclass of pivots in shafts whose defining feature is the specific geometrical configuration of the pivot, such as shape, cross-section, or surface profile that influences its mechanical performance.
[F16C 11/0642] Special features of the plug or cover on the blind end of the socket -- The classification covers inventions that provide special designs or functions for the plug or protective cover placed on the closed, or blind, end of a socket used in shaft-type mechanical systems. It includes features that improve sealing, safety, ease of installation, or maintenance of the socket when the end is not intended for direct connection. The focus is on the plug or cover itself rather than the socket interior.
[F16C 11/0647] Special features relating to adjustment for wear or play; Wear indicators -- The classification covers pivot shafts that incorporate mechanisms for adjusting clearance as wear develops, as well as devices that signal wear levels. It includes built-in adjusters, compensating sleeves, and visual or tactile wear indicators designed to maintain proper function despite material loss.
[F16C 11/0652] combined with a damper other than elastic linings -- The CPC subclass F16C 11/0652 relates to pivots or shaft elements that incorporate a damping device which is not an elastic lining. It covers mechanisms where the pivot is combined with fluid, magnetic, hydraulic or other non-elastic dampers to control vibration or shock, typically integrated within the bearing or supporting structure. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, pivots.
[F16C 11/0657] the socket member being mainly made of plastics -- F16C 11/0657 classifies pivot assemblies in which the socket part of the joint is substantially made of plastic material, covering designs where the plastic component forms the receiving portion that engages the shaft or pin to allow rotational movement. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Pivots.
[F16C 11/0661] the two co-operative parts each having both convex and concave interfaces -- F16C 11/0661 pertains to pivot or shaft constructions in which the two cooperating parts each provide a convex surface that engages a corresponding concave surface on the other part, allowing reciprocal guidance and load sharing. This arrangement is used to achieve smooth rotational or oscillatory motion while accommodating misalignment.
[F16C 11/0666] Sealing means between the socket and the inner member shaft -- F16C 11/0666 covers sealing arrangements that are positioned between a socket (or housing) and an inner shaft member to prevent fluid leakage along the rotating interface. It includes various seal types such as O-rings, lip seals, and packing glands designed for shafts that transmit torque or motion while maintaining a pressure-tight seal.
[F16C 11/0671] allowing operative relative movement of joint parts due to flexing of the sealing means -- This subclass covers mechanical joints in which the sealing element itself flexes to permit relative movement of the connected parts, such as shafts or pivots, while still providing a seal against leakage. The design relies on the seal's elasticity to allow the joint to operate.
[F16C 11/0676] allowing operational relative movement of joint parts due to sliding between parts of the sealing means -- F16C 11/0676 covers pivots that permit relative movement of joint parts by means of sliding between components of the sealing arrangement. The subclass is applied to shaft assemblies where the seal slides to accommodate motion while maintaining a seal, typically in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 11/068] Special features relating to lubrication -- F16C 11/068 refers to special features that provide lubrication for shafts, pivots and related rotating elements. It includes designs such as oil reservoirs, channels, grooves, bearings or other mechanisms that deliver lubricating fluid to moving parts to reduce wear and improve efficiency.
[F16C 11/0685] Manufacture of ball-joints and parts thereof, e.g. assembly of ball-joints -- The F16C 11/0685 subclass covers methods and steps involved in producing ball-joints and their components, including the assembly of the spherical bearing, housing, and associated sealing or lubrication elements, with focus on manufacturing techniques that secure the joint's precise motion and durability.
[F16C 11/069] with at least one separate part to retain the ball member in the socket; Quick-release systems -- F16C 11/069 covers pivot joints in which the ball member is held in its socket by a distinct retaining element, often incorporating a quick-release mechanism that allows fast disengagement and re-assembly. This subclass falls under shafts and mechanical engineering elements for applications requiring rapid maintenance or adjustment.
[F16C 11/0695] Mounting of ball-joints, e.g. fixing them to a connecting rod -- The subclass deals with methods and structures for attaching ball-joints to components such as connecting rods, including clamping, bolting, welding or other fixation techniques used to secure the joint in mechanical assemblies.
[F16C 1/107] Sealing details -- F16C 1/107 covers sealing details for flexible shafts, including the design, arrangement and materials of seals that prevent fluid or contaminant ingress or egress along rotating or transmit-torsion shafts. It addresses sealing structures such as lip seals, mechanical seals, gland packs and associated lubrication or pressure-balancing features specific to flexible, torsion-transmitting shafts.
[F16C 1/108] Reducing or controlling of vibrations, e.g. by resilient damping of noise -- The CPC subclass F16C 1/108 addresses ways to reduce or control vibrations in flexible shafts, typically by using resilient damping or noise-attenuation measures. It includes designs that incorporate elastic elements, dampers or other vibration-absorbing structures to improve shaft performance.
[F16C 11/08] with resilient bearings -- F16C 11/08 classifies pivots on shafts that employ resilient bearings, meaning the rotating joint uses elastic or elastomeric components to provide flexibility, vibration damping, or shock absorption. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering - Engineering Elements and Units - Shafts - Pivots in the CPC system used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F16C 11/083] by means of parts of rubber or like materials -- This subgroup covers pivots or shaft connections that employ rubber or elastomeric components to provide bearing, sealing or damping functions. It includes arrangements where the rotational support is achieved through parts made of rubber or similar flexible materials, often used for vibration isolation or wear reduction.
[F16C 11/086] with an elastomeric member in the blind end of a socket -- F16C 11/086 covers pivot shafts in which a socket's blind end is filled with an elastomeric member to provide sealing, damping or flexible support. The arrangement is used to improve load transmission and reduce wear in mechanical linkages.
[F16C 11/10] Arrangements for locking -- F16C 11/10 covers devices and mechanisms that lock a pivot or shaft to prevent rotation or to hold it in a predetermined position, including locking collars, pins, detents and similar arrangements that secure the joint in place.
[F16C 11/103] frictionally clamped -- This subclass deals with pivot joints in shafts that are secured by friction forces, for example using clamping sleeves, collars or friction bearings that lock the rotating member without separate bolts or keys. It includes designs where the frictional contact provides the necessary holding torque.
[F16C 11/106] for ball joints -- The code F16C 11/106 designates ball-joint assemblies that connect rotating shafts or pivots, allowing angular movement while transmitting torque and accommodating misalignment. It applies to mechanical engineering elements where a spherical bearing provides articulation between linked components.
[F16C 11/12] incorporating flexible connections, e.g. leaf springs -- F16C 11/12 classifies pivots that incorporate flexible connections, such as leaf springs, to give compliance, damping or load distribution in rotating shafts. The code is applied to mechanical-engineering designs where a spring element is integrated into the pivot to accommodate movement or shock.
[F16C 1/12] Arrangements for transmitting movement to or from the flexible member -- F16C 1/12 covers devices that couple a flexible shaft to a drive or load, including couplings, clutches, gearings and other mechanisms that transfer rotation or linear motion while accommodating shaft flexibility. It also addresses mounting, tensioning and alignment features for such arrangements.
[F16C 1/14] Construction of the end-piece of the flexible member -- The CPC subclass F16C 1/14 covers the design of the terminal component of a flexible shaft, including methods for attaching or supporting the flexible member to a drive or load. It addresses end pieces such as couplings, bearings, collars or brackets that secure the shaft while allowing flexibility.
[F16C 1/145] Attachment of the end-piece to the flexible member -- F16C 1/145 concerns devices and methods for attaching an end-piece to a flexible shaft, such as couplings, fittings or clamps that secure the terminal component while allowing torque transmission and flexibility. The subclass includes various joining arrangements that connect the end element to the flexible member.
[F16C 1/16] in which the end-piece is guided rectilinearly -- This subgroup covers flexible shafts whose end-piece is constrained to move in a straight line rather than rotate or swing, providing rectilinear guidance of the terminating element. It applies to power-transmission devices where the drive or load end must be guided linearly, such as certain medical instruments, robotics, or precision tools.
[F16C 1/18] in which the end portion of the flexible member is laid along a curved surface of a pivoted member -- A flexible shaft whose terminal section is arranged to lie along the curved surface of a pivoted component, such as a rotating drum or cam, enabling motion transmission while the shaft follows the member's angular movement.
[F16C 1/20] Construction of flexible members moved to and fro in the sheathing -- F16C 1/20 classifies the construction of flexible members that are moved back-and-forth within a protective sheathing, such as cables, ropes, or bundled strands used for power, control or data transmission. The entry covers features that guide, support, secure, or seal the flexible element inside its sheath, including mounting arrangements, tensioning devices and protective coatings that enable reciprocal motion while maintaining alignment and preventing wear.
[F16C 1/205] Details of the outer surface of the flexible member, e.g. coatings -- The F16C 1/205 subclass addresses patents that concern the outer surface of flexible shafts, including any coatings, films, or surface treatments applied to the member. It covers protective, tribological, insulating or functional layers intended to modify wear, corrosion, friction or other performance aspects of the flexible shaft.
[F16C 1/22] Adjusting -- F16C 1/22 covers devices that vary the position, tension or length of flexible shafts to achieve precise motion control. The claim includes adjustable couplings, tensioning mechanisms and angle-setting arrangements that accommodate shaft bending while maintaining transmission efficiency. These solutions are used in robotics, machine tools and other systems requiring adaptable power transmission.
[F16C 1/223] by adjusting the effective length of the flexible member -- F16C 1/223 relates to flexible shafts whose effective length can be altered, for example by telescoping, inflatable sections or adjustable couplings, to modify torque transmission, speed ratio or vibration characteristics. The classification covers mechanisms that vary the shaft's length to achieve desired mechanical performance.
[F16C 1/226] by adjusting the effective length of the sheathing -- F16C 1/226 covers flexible shafts whose sheathing length can be varied, typically by mechanisms that adjust the effective length of the protective covering while the shaft remains in operation. This allows control of stiffness, reach or positioning of the shaft without changing the core rotary element.
[F16C 1/24] Lubrication -- F16C 1/24 relates to the lubrication of flexible shafts, covering devices, methods and arrangements for applying lubricants to rotating flexible shafts to minimise friction, wear and heat generation.
[F16C 1/26] Construction of guiding-sheathings or guiding-tubes -- F16C 1/26 covers the design and construction of guiding sheaths or tubes that direct flexible shafts, focusing on the geometry, mounting, and protective features that ensure precise motion transmission and alignment in mechanical systems.
[F16C 1/262] End fittings; Attachment thereof to the sheathing or tube -- The CPC class F16C 1/262 refers to the design of end fittings for flexible shafts and the methods used to attach these fittings to the protective sheathing or tube that encloses the shaft. It includes various coupling, clamping and sealing arrangements that secure the shaft ends while maintaining flexibility and protection.
[F16C 1/265] with a swivel tube connected to the end-fitting of a sheathing, e.g. with a spherical joint -- This subclass covers flexible shafts that incorporate a swivel tube attached to the end of a sheathing, allowing the shaft's terminal fitting to pivot or rotate. The swivel arrangement may be realized with a spherical joint or similar device, providing angular freedom while maintaining the transmission of torque along the flexible shaft.
[F16C 1/267] Details of the inner surface of the sheathing or tube, e.g. coatings -- F16C 1/267 concerns the treatment or modification of the inner wall of a flexible shaft's protective tube, including any applied coatings, linings or surface finishes that improve wear resistance, friction or corrosion protection.
[F16C 1/28] with built in bearings -- This subclass covers flexible shafts that incorporate bearings integral to the shaft structure, providing support and reducing friction along the length. It includes designs where bearing elements are embedded or formed as part of the shaft, typically used in power transmission where flexibility and alignment are required.
[F16C 13/00] Rolls, drums, discs, or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rotary bodies shaped substantially as a body of revolution, i.e. axisymmetrical bodies respectively bodies having rotational symmetry, with an outer or inner functional surface such as:  The rotary body allows relative movement of another part or product tangentially along the rotary body, particularly along a linear path, i.e. it is used to support, transport and/or treat another part or product which moves relatively along the functional surface.
[F16C 13/003] Bowed or curved rolls  rollers with a bowed axis as tentering devices for tensioning, smoothing or guiding webs  B65H23/0258 -- F16C 13/003 covers rolled elements whose axis is bowed or curved, used as tentering devices to tension, smooth or guide web materials. These rolls are employed in processes such as paper or film handling to maintain uniform width and surface quality.
[F16C 13/006] Guiding rollers, wheels or the like, formed by or on the outer element of a single bearing or bearing unit, e.g. two adjacent bearings, whose ratio of length to diameter is generally less than one -- A guiding roller, wheel or similar component that is formed by, or mounted on, the outer element of a single bearing or bearing unit, such as two adjacent bearings, where the length-to-diameter ratio of the element is generally less than one, is covered by CPC entry F16C 13/006. This subclass pertains to mechanical engineering elements used for guiding motion in shafts and related assemblies.
[F16C 13/02] Bearings -- F16C 13/02 refers to bearings in mechanical engineering. It is a subset of shafts and related components. This code is used for rolls, drums, discs, or similar elements.
[F16C 13/022] supporting a hollow roll mantle rotating with respect to a yoke or axle -- The code F16C 13/022 concerns devices that support a hollow roll mantle so that it can rotate about a yoke or axle. Such arrangements are used to locate and guide rolls in printing, coating or material-handling machines, allowing the roll to be driven while its interior remains free for suction or fluid passage.
[F16C 13/024] adjustable for positioning, e.g. radial movable bearings for controlling the deflection along the length of the roll mantle -- The CPC subclass F16C 13/024 relates to adjustable positioning devices for rolls, drums or discs, specifically radial movable bearings that can control deflection along the length of the roll mantle. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → shafts → rolls, drums, discs or the like.
[F16C 13/026] by fluid pressure -- F16C 13/026 refers to shafts, rolls, drums or discs that are driven or operated by fluid pressure, such as hydraulic or pneumatic power, within mechanical engineering. The classification includes mechanisms where fluid pressure is used to rotate or move the shaft element.
[F16C 13/028] with a plurality of supports along the length of the roll mantle, e.g. hydraulic jacks -- A roll, drum or disc whose mantle is reinforced by several supports spaced along its length, such as hydraulic jacks, to maintain stiffness, control deflection and distribute loads during operation. This arrangement improves performance in mechanical engineering applications where uniform pressure or precise positioning of the roll surface is required.
[F16C 13/04] Bearings with only partial enclosure of the member to be borne -- The subclass F16C 13/04 refers to bearings in which the shaft or rotating member is only partly enclosed by a housing or supporting structure, providing limited protection while still delivering support, alignment and load-carrying capacity.
[F16C 13/06] self-adjusting -- F16C 13/06 covers self-adjusting rotary elements such as rolls, drums or discs that automatically modify their position, pressure or clearance during operation, often using springs, hydraulic or pneumatic devices to maintain optimal contact or traction without manual intervention. This subclass is used for mechanical engineering components that adapt themselves to varying loads or material thicknesses.
[F16C 15/00] Construction of rotary bodies to resist centrifugal force -- F16C 15/00 covers the design and construction of rotating bodies such as disks, flywheels, rotors and other shafts that must endure centrifugal forces. It includes methods for selecting materials, shaping geometry, adding reinforcement, and balancing to ensure structural integrity while the component spins at high speed.
[F16C 17/00] Bearings for rotary parts -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Bearing surfaces of pistons -> F16J1/02 - Particular materials of piston rings -> F16J9/26
[F16C 17/02] for radial load only -- This CPC subclass covers rotary bearings that support shafts and are intended solely for radial-load-only operation, not axial or combined loads. It falls under the mechanical engineering section dealing with engineering elements and units, specifically shafts.
[F16C 17/022] with a pair of essentially semicircular bearing sleeves -- F16C 17/022 covers rotary shaft bearings that use two essentially semicircular bearing sleeves positioned opposite each other. The design provides support for the shaft while allowing limited radial and axial movement, typically in applications where space constraints or load distribution benefit from a split sleeve arrangement.
[F16C 17/024] with flexible leaves to create hydrodynamic wedge, e.g. radial foil bearings -- These are radial foil bearings that employ flexible leaf elements to generate a hydrodynamic wedge film, supporting rotating shafts without solid contact. The flexible leaves deform under load, creating a pressure zone that carries load and reduces friction.
[F16C 17/026] with helical grooves in the bearing surface to generate hydrodynamic pressure, e.g. herringbone grooves -- F16C 17/026 pertains to bearings for rotary parts that have helical or herringbone grooves cut into the bearing surface to create hydrodynamic pressure. The grooves produce a fluid wedge that supports the load, improves lubrication and reduces friction in rotating shafts.
[F16C 17/028] with fixed wedges to generate hydrodynamic pressure, e.g. multi-lobe bearings -- F16C 17/028 relates to bearings for rotary shafts that incorporate fixed wedge profiles to generate hydrodynamic pressure, such as multi-lobe designs. The fixed wedges force lubricant into converging gaps, creating a fluid film that supports load and reduces friction without direct metal-to-metal contact. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts.
[F16C 17/03] with tiltably-supported segments, e.g. Michell bearings -- F16C 17/03 designates rotary bearings whose load-carrying surfaces are mounted on tiltably-supported segments, such as the fluid-film Michell bearing. The tilting pads allow each segment to adjust its angle under load, improving alignment and reducing friction in rotating shafts. This subclass falls under shafts and bearings within mechanical engineering.
[F16C 17/035] the segments being integrally formed with, or rigidly fixed to, a support-element -- F16C 17/035 covers bearing segments that are manufactured as a single piece with, or permanently attached to, a supporting element such as a housing or shaft. The classification pertains to designs where the bearing elements cannot be removed or repositioned independently of the support structure, ensuring a fixed geometric relationship in rotary bearing assemblies.
[F16C 17/04] for axial load only -- F16C 17/04 designates bearings for rotary parts that are intended to support axial loads only. It falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Bearings for rotary parts, and covers devices such as thrust bearings that transmit force parallel to the shaft axis.
[F16C 17/042] with flexible leaves to create hydrodynamic wedge, e.g. axial foil bearings -- F16C 17/042 pertains to rotary bearings that employ flexible leaf or foil elements which deform under operation to create a hydrodynamic wedge film, as seen in axial foil bearings. This design enhances load support and reduces friction while allowing compact, lightweight bearing assemblies.
[F16C 17/045] with grooves in the bearing surface to generate hydrodynamic pressure, e.g. spiral groove thrust bearings -- A subclass of rotary-part bearings in which the bearing surface carries grooves that create hydrodynamic pressure to support loads. Typical examples are spiral-groove thrust bearings that use fluid-film lubrication.
[F16C 17/047] with fixed wedges to generate hydrodynamic pressure -- F16C 17/047 covers fluid-film bearings for rotary shafts that create supporting pressure by means of fixed wedge-shaped surfaces. The wedges generate a hydrodynamic film of lubricant, allowing the bearing to carry load without direct metal-to-metal contact. This subclass falls under bearings for rotary parts within mechanical engineering shaft elements.
[F16C 17/06] with tiltably-supported segments, e.g. Michell bearings -- F16C 17/06 designates bearings for rotary parts that employ tiltably-supported segments, such as Michell bearings, allowing the bearing elements to pivot and accommodate angular misalignment while maintaining low friction and stable load support.
[F16C 17/065] the segments being integrally formed with, or rigidly fixed to, a support-element -- F16C 17/065 relates to bearings for rotary parts in which the bearing segments are produced as a single-piece with, or are permanently attached to, the supporting element such as a shaft or housing. The classification covers designs where the segments form an integral or rigidly fixed structure, improving alignment and load transfer in mechanical assemblies.
[F16C 17/08] for supporting the end face of a shaft or other member, e.g. footstep bearings -- F16C 17/08 covers bearings that support the end-face of a shaft or similar member, known as footstep bearings. These devices provide axial support and limit movement, often used where a shaft terminates against a housing or frame.
[F16C 17/10] for both radial and axial load -- F16C 17/10 denotes bearings designed to support both radial and axial loads on rotating shafts. These units combine traditional bearing functions with thrust capabilities, allowing them to carry loads perpendicular and parallel to the shaft axis in mechanical systems.
[F16C 17/102] with grooves in the bearing surface to generate hydrodynamic pressure -- F16C 17/102 designates bearings for rotary parts that incorporate grooves in the bearing surface to generate hydrodynamic pressure, creating a fluid film that supports the shaft and reduces friction. The grooved geometry enhances load-carrying capacity and is commonly applied in journal and thrust bearing designs.
[F16C 17/105] with at least one bearing surface providing angular contact, e.g. conical or spherical bearing surfaces -- F16C 17/105 classifies rotary shaft bearings that incorporate at least one angular-contact surface, such as conical or spherical shapes, allowing the bearing to accommodate combined radial and axial loads.
[F16C 17/107] with at least one surface for radial load and at least one surface for axial load -- This subclass covers bearings that provide separate surfaces to support radial and axial forces on a rotating shaft, combining load-bearing features in a single bearing assembly. They are used where combined loads occur, such as in gearboxes or automotive wheels.
[F16C 17/12] characterised by features not related to the direction of the load -- This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, bearings for rotary parts. It covers bearings whose distinguishing features are independent of load direction, such as design aspects that affect performance regardless of whether the load is radial, axial, or combined.
[F16C 17/14] specially adapted for operating in water -- F16C 17/14 covers bearings for rotary shafts that are specifically designed for use in water or submerged environments. The classification includes adaptations such as corrosion-resistant materials, water-tight sealing, and lubrication methods that maintain performance despite constant exposure to liquid. It is a subcategory of bearings for rotary parts within mechanical engineering.
[F16C 17/18] with floating brasses or brushing, rotatable at a reduced speed -- This subclass covers bearings used on rotating shafts that incorporate floating brass or brush elements and are designed to run at reduced speeds. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 17/20] with emergency supports or bearings -- F16C 17/20 covers bearings for rotary shafts that incorporate emergency support features, such as backup or safety bearings designed to carry load if the primary bearing fails. These devices provide temporary load-bearing capacity to protect machinery during abnormal conditions, ensuring continued operation or safe shutdown.
[F16C 17/22] with arrangements compensating for thermal expansion -- F16C 17/22 covers bearings for rotary parts that incorporate features to accommodate or compensate for thermal expansion. It includes designs such as adjustable clearances, self-aligning elements, or temperature-responsive mechanisms that maintain proper bearing operation despite dimensional changes caused by heat. This subclass falls under shafts within engineering elements and units in the mechanical engineering section.
[F16C 17/24] with devices affected by abnormal or undesired positions, e.g. for preventing overheating, for safety -- F16C 17/24 classifies bearings for rotating shafts that include mechanisms reacting to abnormal or undesired positions, such as devices that interrupt operation, adjust load or provide cooling to prevent overheating and enhance safety. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of engineering elements and units, specifically shafts.
[F16C 17/243] related to temperature and heat, e.g. for preventing overheating -- F16C 17/243 covers bearings designed with provisions to manage temperature and heat, such as cooling mechanisms, thermal insulation, or materials that prevent overheating of rotary shafts.
[F16C 17/246] related to wear, e.g. sensors for measuring wear -- F16C 17/246 covers bearings for rotary parts that incorporate wear-related features such as sensors to detect or measure wear. It sits within Mechanical Engineering, Engineering Elements and Units, Shafts. The subclass focuses on technologies for monitoring bearing condition to aid performance and maintenance.
[F16C 17/26] Systems consisting of a plurality of sliding-contact bearings -- F16C 17/26 classifies systems that incorporate several sliding-contact bearings operating together to support a rotating shaft or other component. It includes bearing groups, bearing lines, and combined bearing assemblies that distribute loads across multiple elements. These arrangements are used to improve stiffness, load capacity, or alignment in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 19/00] Bearings with rolling contact, for exclusively rotary movement -- F16C 19/00 designates bearings that employ rolling elements such as balls, rollers or needles and are intended solely for rotary motion between a shaft and its supporting structure. These bearings transmit torque while eliminating sliding friction, and are classified under mechanical engineering, shaft elements and units.
[F16C 19/02] with bearing balls essentially of the same size in one or more circular rows -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ball bearings whereby all of the following requirements are met:
[F16C 19/04] for radial load mainly -- F16C 19/04 covers rolling-contact bearings intended for exclusively rotary movement that are primarily designed to carry radial loads, fitting within the shaft sub-category of mechanical engineering elements.
[F16C 19/06] with a single row or balls -- F16C 19/06 classifies rolling-contact bearings designed for purely rotary motion that employ a single row of balls. These bearings are used in shaft assemblies to support radial loads while allowing smooth rotation, and they fall under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 19/08] with two or more rows of balls -- F16C 19/08 pertains to bearings that employ rolling contact for exclusively rotary motion and are constructed with two or more parallel rows of balls, a specific type of shaft-related component within mechanical engineering.
[F16C 19/10] for axial load mainly -- F16C 19/10 refers to rolling-contact bearings designed for exclusively rotary movement that principally support axial loads. These bearings are used where the load acts parallel to the shaft axis, providing smooth rotation while handling thrust forces.
[F16C 19/12] for supporting the end face of a shaft or other member, e.g. footstep bearings -- F16C 19/12 covers rolling-contact bearings intended to support the end face of a shaft or similar member, commonly called footstep bearings. These bearings provide a bearing surface at the shaft's tip for exclusively rotary movement, allowing load transmission and stability without additional supporting structures.
[F16C 19/14] for both radial and axial load -- F16C 19/14 covers rolling-contact bearings designed to support loads that act both radially and axially on a rotating shaft. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts in the CPC system used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F16C 19/16] with a single row of balls -- F16C 19/16 refers to rolling-contact bearings that provide exclusively rotary motion and contain a single row of balls as the rolling elements. These bearings are used where precise, low-friction rotational support is required, typically in shafts and similar mechanical components.
[F16C 19/163] with angular contact -- This subclass concerns bearings that use rolling elements and are intended for exclusively rotary movement, where the raceways are angled to provide angular contact. The design enables the bearing to support both radial and axial loads, improving stiffness and load-carrying capacity in shaft applications.
[F16C 19/166] Four-point-contact ball bearings -- Four-point-contact ball bearings are rolling-contact bearings that support loads through four contact points on the balls, providing high stiffness and precise radial and axial load handling for exclusively rotary shafts. They fall under mechanical engineering elements and units, specifically the shaft category, and are used in applications requiring accurate positioning and minimal deflection.
[F16C 19/18] with two or more rows of balls -- F16C 19/18 covers rotary bearings that use rolling contact and contain two or more rows of balls. These bearings are designed for exclusively rotary motion, typically mounted on shafts to support radial loads while allowing smooth rotation.
[F16C 19/181] with angular contact -- F16C 19/181 covers angular-contact rolling bearings, a subset of bearings with rolling elements designed for exclusively rotary movement. These bearings have contact angles that allow them to support combined radial and axial loads, making them suitable for high-speed shafts where precise alignment and load-carrying capacity are required.
[F16C 19/182] in tandem arrangement -- A tandem-arranged bearing with rolling contact is a mechanical component that uses rolling elements such as balls or rollers to support exclusively rotary motion of a shaft, with two or more bearing units placed in series to share loads and improve stability. This configuration falls under the CPC code F16C 19/182, covering rolling-contact bearings used in purely rotational applications.
[F16C 19/183] with two rows at opposite angles -- F16C 19/183 is a Cooperative Patent Classification for bearings that provide rolling contact in purely rotary motion, specifically designs that incorporate two rows of rolling elements set at opposite angles to each other. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of engineering elements and units, within the shaft category.
[F16C 19/184] in O-arrangement -- F16C 19/184 covers bearings that use rolling elements and are designed for exclusively rotary motion, where the bearing components are arranged in an "O" configuration-that is, the rolling elements are positioned around a central shaft forming a circular, closed loop. This sub-class falls under the shaft bearings section of mechanical engineering.
[F16C 19/185] with two raceways provided integrally on a part other than a race ring, e.g. a shaft or housing -- The subclass F16C 19/185 pertains to rolling-contact bearings that provide two raceways formed integrally on a part other than a separate race ring, such as being machined or molded directly on a shaft or housing, and are intended for exclusively rotary movement. It falls under the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 19/186] with three raceways provided integrally on parts other than race rings, e.g. third generation hubs -- The subclass F16C 19/186 covers rolling-contact bearings intended for exclusively rotary movement that incorporate three raceways integral to elements other than separate race rings, such as the hubs of third-generation designs. These bearings provide the three raceways as part of the hub or other supporting part rather than using distinct race rings.
[F16C 19/187] with all four raceways integrated on parts other than race rings, e.g. fourth generation hubs -- F16C 19/187 pertains to bearings with rolling contact designed for purely rotary motion where all four raceways are formed as integral features of components other than separate race rings, a concept exemplified by fourth-generation hub designs. This subcategory falls under shafts within the broader engineering elements and units of mechanical engineering.
[F16C 19/188] with at least one row for radial load in combination with at least one row for axial load -- This subclass covers rolling-contact bearings intended solely for rotary motion that incorporate at least one row of rolling elements to support radial loads together with at least one row to support axial loads, combining radial and thrust load-carrying capabilities within a single bearing assembly.
[F16C 19/20] with loose spacing bodies, e.g. balls, between the bearing balls -- This subclass of F16C 19/20 concerns bearings that use separate rolling elements, such as balls, positioned between the main bearing balls to provide spacing and improve load distribution during rotary motion. The loose balls act as spacers, reducing friction and wear while maintaining exclusively rotary movement of the shaft.
[F16C 19/22] with bearing rollers essentially of the same size in one or more circular rows, e.g. needle bearings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Roller bearings whereby all of the following requirements are met:
[F16C 19/225] Details of the ribs supporting the end of the rollers -- F16C 19/225 covers the specific design features of ribs that support the ends of rollers in purely rotary bearings. It includes the shape, placement and attachment of these reinforcing ribs, which enhance roller stability and load distribution within the bearing assembly. This subclass refines broader categories of shafts and rolling-contact bearings.
[F16C 19/24] for radial load mainly -- F16C 19/24 covers rolling-contact bearings designed for purely rotary motion that bear mainly radial loads. It includes ball, roller or needle bearings used in shafts where the load acts perpendicular to the axis, distinguishing them from bearings intended for axial or combined loading.
[F16C 19/26] with a single row of rollers -- This CPC subclass covers bearings that use a single row of rollers to support a rotating shaft, providing rolling contact for pure rotary motion. It includes designs where the rollers are arranged in one line around the shaft, typically for load carrying and reduced friction.
[F16C 19/28] with two or more rows of rollers -- A bearing of the rolling-contact type designed for purely rotary motion that incorporates two or more parallel rows of rollers, providing greater load capacity and stability than single-row designs. This configuration is classified under CPC code F16C 19/28 within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for shafts and bearing elements.
[F16C 19/30] for axial load mainly -- F16C 19/30 covers bearings with rolling contact that are intended mainly to carry axial loads. These are thrust-type bearings such as ball, roller or tapered-roller bearings used on rotating shafts where the load direction is parallel to the axis.
[F16C 19/305] consisting of rollers held in a cage -- F16C 19/305 designates rolling-contact bearings used for purely rotary motion in which the rollers are retained in a cage. These units belong to the shaft category of mechanical engineering and are covered by the CPC system of the EPO and USPTO.
[F16C 19/32] for supporting the end face of a shaft or other member, e.g. footstep bearings -- F16C 19/32 covers rolling-contact bearings designed to support the end face of a rotating shaft or similar member, such as footstep bearings. These bearings provide a load-bearing surface at the shaft's end, allowing exclusively rotary movement while bearing axial and radial forces. They are a sub-category of shaft bearings within the mechanical engineering classification.
[F16C 19/34] for both radial and axial load -- The code F16C 19/34 covers rolling-contact bearings designed to support loads that act simultaneously in radial and axial directions. These bearings are used where shafts experience combined forces, such as in gearboxes or turbine shafts, and may include ball or roller arrangements that accommodate both load components.
[F16C 19/36] with a single row of rollers -- This subclass concerns bearings that rely on rolling contact for purely rotary motion and employ a single row of rollers. It is situated under mechanical-engineering shafts and covers rolling-contact bearings designed with one roller row to support rotational loads.
[F16C 19/361] with cylindrical rollers -- F16C 19/361 refers to bearings that employ cylindrical rollers to provide rolling contact for exclusively rotary movement. These devices are a subset of shaft-related components within mechanical engineering, classified under engineering elements and units. The code is used in both the Cooperative Patent Classification system of the EPO and USPTO.
[F16C 19/362] the rollers being crossed within the single row -- F16C 19/362 refers to single-row rolling-element bearings in which the rollers are crossed within the row. The crossed-roller arrangement enhances load capacity and stiffness for shafts that rotate exclusively.
[F16C 19/364] with tapered rollers, i.e. rollers having essentially the shape of a truncated cone -- This subclass covers rotary bearings that employ tapered rollers-rolling elements shaped like truncated cones-to transmit loads between rotating parts. The tapered geometry enables the bearing to support combined radial and axial forces while maintaining low friction.
[F16C 19/38] with two or more rows of rollers -- F16C 19/38 designates bearing units that use rolling contact for purely rotary motion and contain two or more rows of rollers. These bearings are classified under mechanical engineering, specifically within engineering elements and units for shafts. They are intended for applications requiring high load capacity and stability across multiple roller rows.
[F16C 19/381] with at least one row for radial load in combination with at least one row for axial load -- F16C 19/381 pertains to bearings with rolling contact designed for exclusively rotary movement, specifically those featuring at least one row configured for radial loads and another for axial loads. This classification is part of the Mechanical Engineering section under Engineering Elements and Units, focusing on shafts and their components.
[F16C 19/383] with tapered rollers, i.e. rollers having essentially the shape of a truncated cone -- This CPC subclass designates bearings that provide purely rotary motion by means of rolling elements shaped as tapered rollers, i.e., rollers that approximate a truncated cone. Such tapered rollers allow combined radial and axial load support in shaft applications.
[F16C 19/385] with two rows, i.e. double-row tapered roller bearings -- The classification F16C 19/385 covers bearings that employ rolling contact and consist of two parallel rows of tapered rollers, known as double-row tapered roller bearings. These components are designed for exclusively rotary movement and fall under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 19/386] in O-arrangement -- F16C 19/386 covers bearings that provide rolling contact for exclusively rotary movement in an O-arrangement, meaning the bearing elements are positioned in a circular or ring configuration around the shaft. These devices are used in mechanical engineering where axial loads are minimal and rotational motion is the primary function.
[F16C 19/388] with four rows, i.e. four row tapered roller bearings -- The CPC class F16C 19/388 designates tapered-roller bearings that contain four rows of rollers, providing rolling contact for purely rotary movement. Such bearings are used in shafts to carry high radial and axial loads while maintaining precise alignment, and they belong to the broader group of mechanical engineering elements.
[F16C 19/40] with loose spacing bodies between the rollers -- F16C 19/40 covers rotary rolling bearings in which the rollers are separated by loosely fitting spacing bodies such as cages or webs that maintain a small clearance while allowing the rollers to move independently. This arrangement reduces friction and improves load distribution in shafts.
[F16C 19/44] Needle bearings -- F16C 19/44 denotes needle bearings, a class of rolling-contact bearings that use long, thin cylindrical rollers to support exclusively rotary motion of shafts. They provide high load capacity in a compact form and are widely used in mechanical-engineering applications where space is limited and precise rotation is required.
[F16C 19/46] with one row or needles -- F16C 19/46 classifies rolling-contact bearings designed for exclusively rotary motion that incorporate a single row of needle-type rolling elements. These bearings belong to the shaft category within mechanical engineering and are used where compact, high-load capacity is required.
[F16C 19/463] consisting of needle rollers held in a cage, i.e. subunit without race rings -- F16C 19/463 covers subunits of needle-roller bearings where the needle rollers are retained in a cage but the bearing lacks the outer and inner race rings. This configuration provides a compact, modular element that can be placed in a housing or combined with separate race components, useful when space is limited or custom race designs are required.
[F16C 19/466] comprising needle rollers and an outer ring, i.e. subunit without inner ring -- The subclass defines rotary bearings that employ needle rollers and only an outer ring, omitting an inner ring to form a subassembly that can be fitted around a shaft. It applies to rolling-contact bearings used for exclusively rotary motion within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F16C 19/48] with two or more rows of needles -- This subclass covers needle roller bearings that have two or more parallel rows of needles, designed for exclusively rotary motion. Such bearings provide high load capacity and stiffness in compact shafts.
[F16C 19/49] Bearings with both balls and rollers -- F16C 19/49 covers bearings that incorporate both balls and rollers to transmit loads in exclusively rotary applications. By combining point-contact of balls with line-contact of rollers, these hybrid bearings provide increased load capacity, stiffness and speed capability compared with pure ball or roller types, and are used in shafts requiring versatile performance.
[F16C 19/492] with two or more rows with angular contact -- This subclass covers rolling-element bearings intended for exclusively rotary movement that contain two or more concentric rows of angular-contact balls or rollers. Such bearings combine high radial and axial load capacity in a compact shaft assembly.
[F16C 19/495] with two rows -- F16C 19/495 is a CPC subclass under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts that pertains to bearings with rolling contact used for exclusively rotary movement. It specifically classifies bearings that incorporate two rows of rolling elements.
[F16C 19/497] in O-arrangement -- This classification covers bearings with rolling contact that are intended for exclusively rotary movement and are arranged in an O-shape configuration. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts and is coded F16C 19/497.

[F16C 19/502] with rolling elements in rows not forming a full circle -- A bearing of the type that provides rolling contact for a shaft that rotates only, where the rolling elements are arranged in one or more linear rows rather than in a complete circular pattern. Such designs are used when space or load-distribution requirements favor partial rows of balls, rollers or needles instead of a full-circle array.
[F16C 19/505] with the diameter of the rolling elements of one row differing from the diameter of those of another row -- This subclass covers rotary bearings that use rolling elements in multiple rows, with the elements in one row having a different diameter than those in another row, allowing varied load distribution or speed characteristics.
[F16C 19/507] with rolling elements journaled in one of the moving parts, e.g. stationary rollers to support a rotating part -- F16C 19/507 covers bearings that provide exclusive rotary motion using rolling elements that are fixed in one of the moving components, such as stationary rollers that support a rotating part. The classification applies to designs where the rolling elements are journaled in either the shaft or the hub to maintain precise, low-friction rotation.
[F16C 19/52] with devices affected by abnormal or undesired conditions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Measures taken in view of conditions which are not normal, i.e. not relating to the proper functioning of the bearing at its operating speed, and which are not already covered by other groups. A few examples are:  Examples of groups dealing with a variety of undesired conditions:
[F16C 19/522] related to load on the bearing, e.g. bearings with load sensors or means to protect the bearing against overload -- F16C 19/522 covers bearings with rolling contact designed for purely rotary motion that incorporate features to monitor or manage the load they carry, such as integrated load sensors or overload-protective devices.
[F16C 19/525] related to temperature and heat, e.g. insulation -- F16C 19/525 covers bearings with rolling elements that are designed to manage temperature effects, such as incorporating thermal insulation or heat-dissipating features, to maintain performance during exclusive rotary motion.
[F16C 19/527] related to vibration and noise -- F16C 19/527 covers bearings with rolling contact designed for exclusively rotary motion where vibration and noise are addressed, including features or methods that reduce or control acoustic emission and oscillations. It falls within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, specifically shafts and their bearing components.
[F16C 19/54] Systems consisting of a plurality of bearings with rolling friction -- F16C 19/54 covers configurations that integrate several rolling-element bearings into a single system to support a rotating shaft or component. The design distributes loads, ensures alignment and may provide redundancy, often incorporating bearing housings, intermediate shafts or protective elements for exclusive rotary movement.
[F16C 19/541] Systems consisting of juxtaposed rolling bearings including at least one angular contact bearing -- F16C 19/541 covers arrangements where two or more rolling-contact bearings are mounted side by side on a shaft, with at least one bearing being an angular contact type, to support exclusively rotary motion and improve load handling or stiffness.
[F16C 19/542] with two rolling bearings with angular contact -- The subclass F16C 19/542 pertains to shafts that incorporate a pair of angular-contact rolling bearings, providing combined axial and radial support for exclusively rotary movement. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the category of shafts with rolling-contact bearings.
[F16C 19/543] in O-arrangement -- F16C 19/543 covers bearings that use rolling elements and are configured in an O-shaped arrangement, meaning the rolling components are arranged around a circular path that forms a closed loop. This subclass falls under shafts and rolling-contact bearings used for purely rotary motion within mechanical engineering.
[F16C 19/545] Systems comprising at least one rolling bearing for radial load in combination with at least one rolling bearing for axial load -- In the Cooperative Patent Classification of the EPO and USPTO, F16C 19/545 covers mechanical systems that combine at least one rolling bearing for radial loads with at least one rolling bearing for axial loads. Such arrangements are used on shafts subjected to both radial and axial forces to provide stable rotary movement.
[F16C 19/546] Systems with spaced apart rolling bearings including at least one angular contact bearing -- F16C 19/546 pertains to mechanical systems where two or more rolling bearings are spaced apart on a shaft and at least one of them is an angular contact bearing, providing combined radial and axial support for rotary movement.
[F16C 19/547] with two angular contact rolling bearings -- F16C 19/547 refers to bearings with rolling contact for rotary movement. It involves two angular contact rolling bearings. This code is under mechanical engineering.
[F16C 19/548] in O-arrangement -- F16C 19/548 covers bearings with rolling contact that employ an O-arrangement, meaning the rolling elements are positioned in a circular or ring-like configuration around the shaft to support exclusively rotary motion. This design is part of the shaft bearing subgroup within mechanical engineering.
[F16C 19/55] with intermediate floating rings rotating at reduced speed -- A bearing type in which rolling elements are separated by one or more intermediate floating rings that rotate at a lower speed than the inner and outer races, allowing controlled slip and reduced friction. This design is used for purely rotary motion where a gradual speed transition between races is required.
[F16C 19/56] in which the rolling bodies of one bearing differ in diameter from those of another -- A bearing in which the rolling elements of one bearing have a different diameter than those of another bearing, used for exclusively rotary movement. This design variation allows for tailored load distribution and stiffness within shaft assemblies, falling under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 21/00] Combinations of sliding-contact bearings with ball or roller bearings, for exclusively rotary movement -- The CPC class F16C 21/00 covers hybrid bearing assemblies that join sliding-contact (plain) bearings with ball or roller bearings, designed to support exclusively rotary motion. It addresses configurations where the two bearing types work together to improve load capacity, stiffness, or damping in rotating shafts.
[F16C 21/005] the external zone of a bearing with rolling members, e.g. needles, being cup-shaped, with or without a separate thrust-bearing disc or ring, e.g. for universal joints  seals  F16C33/72 ,  F16D3/38 -- F16C 21/005 covers bearing designs where the outer race is cup-shaped and contains rolling elements such as needles, and may include a separate thrust-bearing disc or ring. It is used for applications like universal joints and sealing arrangements.
[F16C 2202/00] Solid materials defined by their properties -- F16C 2202/00 is a CPC subclass under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts that covers solid materials identified by their intrinsic properties, such as strength, hardness, corrosion resistance or elasticity, when used for shaft construction. It groups patents that specify material characteristics rather than specific alloy compositions.
[F16C 2202/02] Mechanical properties -- F16C 2202/02 is a subclass in the Cooperative Patent Classification covering the mechanical properties of solid materials defined by their properties, situated under the shaft category of engineering elements and units within mechanical engineering. It addresses how material characteristics such as strength, elasticity, hardness and fatigue behavior affect shaft performance and design.
[F16C 2202/04] Hardness -- F16C 2202/04 pertains to the hardness of solid materials employed in shafts, defining the material's resistance to deformation or indentation. The code is used to classify patents that disclose methods, measurements or treatments concerning material hardness in mechanical-engineering applications.
[F16C 2202/06] Strength or rigidity -- F16C 2202/06 covers the strength or rigidity of solid materials used in shafts. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and addresses how material properties affect a shaft's resistance to deformation under load.
[F16C 2202/08] Resilience, elasticity, super-elasticity -- F16C 2202/08 relates to the resilience, elasticity and super-elasticity of solid shaft materials used in mechanical engineering. It covers materials that can recover their shape after deformation, including spring-type steels and shape memory alloys, and addresses design considerations for shafts that must flex or absorb energy.
[F16C 2202/10] Porosity -- F16C 2202/10 relates to the porosity of solid shaft materials, covering the presence, measurement, control and effects of voids or pores within shafts used in mechanical engineering. It addresses how porosity influences mechanical strength, fatigue resistance and manufacturing processes.
[F16C 2202/20] Thermal properties -- F16C 2202/20 covers the thermal characteristics of solid shaft materials, including heat conductivity, thermal expansion, specific heat capacity and related effects that influence shaft performance under temperature variations.
[F16C 2202/22] Coefficient of expansion -- The CPC code F16C 2202/22 covers the coefficient of expansion of solid shaft materials. It pertains to mechanical engineering elements where the dimensional change of a shaft due to temperature variations is specified. This designation is used to classify patents dealing with material property data and related calculations for shaft design.
[F16C 2202/24] Insulating -- F16C 2202/24 classifies shafts that are provided with or made from solid insulating materials to limit heat transfer or protect the shaft surface. It covers mechanical-engineering elements where the shaft itself, or an attached layer, functions as an insulator defined by its material properties.
[F16C 2202/28] Shape memory material -- F16C 2202/28 concerns shafts fashioned from shape-memory materials, which are solid substances that regain a predefined geometry after being deformed by temperature or stress changes. The classification covers the design, manufacture and application of such adaptive shafts in mechanical systems.
[F16C 2202/30] Electric properties -- The CPC code F16C 2202/30 covers the electric properties of solid materials defined by their intrinsic characteristics, such as conductivity, permittivity and resistivity, when these materials are used in shafts or similar mechanical elements.
[F16C 2202/32] Conductivity -- F16C 2202/32 is the CPC code for shafts whose solid material is characterized by its conductivity, addressing both electrical and thermal conduction properties. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and the subgroup of solid materials defined by their properties. The entry is used to classify patents dealing with conductive shaft materials and related manufacturing processes.
[F16C 2202/34] Superconductivity -- F16C 2202/34 designates shafts made from solid materials that exhibit superconductivity, focusing on the use of superconducting properties to improve mechanical performance such as reduced friction or magnetic levitation. The code applies to inventions where the shaft's superconducting nature is a defining functional feature.
[F16C 2202/36] Piezoelectric -- F16C 2202/36 (Piezoelectric) covers shafts or drive shafts that incorporate piezoelectric solid materials, such as crystals or polymers, to generate or sense electric signals when mechanically stressed. The classification is applied to devices where the piezoelectric effect is used for monitoring, control, or actuation within rotating or linear shaft components.
[F16C 2202/40] Magnetic -- F16C 2202/40 relates to shafts made of solid magnetic materials, such as ferromagnetic metals or alloys, whose magnetic properties are utilized in the device. The classification covers shafts used in magnetic couplings, actuators, sensors or other mechanisms where magnetic interaction is a functional requirement.
[F16C 2202/42] soft-magnetic, ferromagnetic -- F16C 2202/42 covers shafts fabricated from soft-magnetic or ferromagnetic solid materials, where the material's magnetic properties are a defining characteristic. Such shafts are used in devices that require magnetic transmission of torque or integration with electromagnetic fields, for example in electric motors, generators or magnetic coupling systems.
[F16C 2202/44] hard-magnetic, permanent magnetic, e.g. samarium-cobalt -- F16C 2202/44 classifies shafts fabricated from hard-magnetic permanent materials, such as samarium-cobalt, where the magnetic properties are essential to the component's operation. The code is part of the mechanical engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, focusing on solid materials defined by their magnetic characteristics.
[F16C 2202/50] Lubricating properties -- F16C 2202/50 covers the lubricating properties of solid materials employed in shafts, focusing on how these materials reduce friction and wear. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts, and addresses the performance characteristics of solids defined by their lubricating behavior.
[F16C 2202/52] Graphite -- F16C 2202/52 is a CPC subclass that covers the use of graphite as a solid material in shaft components. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and solid materials defined by their properties. The classification is used by EPO and USPTO to index patents dealing with graphite-based shaft designs or treatments.
[F16C 2202/54] Molybdenum disulfide -- F16C 2202/54 covers molybdenum disulfide, a solid-lubricant material applied to shafts and similar mechanical elements to lessen friction and protect against wear.
[F16C 2202/60] Oil repelling -- F16C 2202/60 classifies solid shaft materials that are engineered to be oil-repelling, reducing friction and preventing lubrication buildup. It falls under the mechanical engineering hierarchy of engineering elements and units, specifically shafts. This code is used in EPC/USPTO patent classification.
[F16C 2202/64] Water absorbing -- The F16C 2202/64 entry relates to shafts or rotating components made of, coated with, or incorporating solid materials that absorb water, such as hydrophilic polymers or treated metals. It addresses how water-absorbing properties influence performance, lubrication, corrosion resistance or dimensional stability of the element.
[F16C 2202/66] Water repelling -- The CPC subclass F16C 2202/66 covers shafts that are constructed from or treated with water-repellent solid materials.
