It includes designs, coatings or surface treatments that give the shaft hydrophobic properties to prevent water penetration, corrosion or degradation. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 2202/70] Anti-bacterial, anti-microbial -- F16C 2202/70 covers shafts made from solid materials that are inherently anti-bacterial or anti-microbial, meaning the material inhibits the growth of bacteria and other microorganisms on the shaft surface. Such shafts are used where hygiene is critical, for example in medical devices, food-processing machinery, or sanitary installations.
[F16C 2204/00] Metallic materials -- F16C 2204/00 relates to the selection and use of metallic materials for shafts in mechanical engineering. It covers the properties, composition, heat-treatment and performance criteria of steels, alloys and other metals that are suitable for transmitting torque, supporting loads and resisting wear in rotating or stationary shaft applications.
[F16C 2204/02] Noble metals -- F16C 2204/02 covers shafts and similar rotating elements that are made from or clad with noble metals such as gold, silver, platinum-group metals. It is applied to inventions where the shaft material is selected for corrosion resistance, electrical conductivity, or other special surface properties in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 2204/04] based on silver -- F16C 2204/04 covers shafts that are manufactured from or incorporate silver as the primary metallic material. It includes designs where silver or silver-based alloys are used for the shaft body or coating to provide specific properties such as conductivity or corrosion resistance.
[F16C 2204/10] Alloys based on copper -- This classification covers mechanical shafts made from copper-based alloys, including bronze, brass and other copper alloys used for shafts in machines. It pertains to engineering elements and units in mechanical engineering, focusing on metallic material properties, corrosion resistance, and suitability for bearing and rotational components.
[F16C 2204/12] with tin as the next major constituent -- The CPC entry F16C 2204/12 designates shafts made of metallic material in which tin is the next major constituent after the primary element, defining a subclass of tin-based alloys such as bronze or tin-plated steels used for rotating or transmitting components.
[F16C 2204/14] with zinc as the next major constituent -- This subclass pertains to shafts whose primary material is a metal alloy that includes zinc as the next major constituent, meaning zinc is the second most significant element after the base metal. It is used for patents describing the design, manufacture or treatment of such zinc-containing metal shafts.
[F16C 2204/16] with lead as the next major constituent -- F16C 2204/16 designates shafts made of a metallic material in which lead is the next major constituent, typically referring to lead-containing alloys or lead-lined shafts used for damping, corrosion resistance, or radiation shielding.
[F16C 2204/18] with bismuth as the next major constituent -- F16C 2204/18 is a CPC subclass used by the EPO and USPTO for shafts made of metallic material in which bismuth is the next major constituent after the primary metal. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Metallic materials.
[F16C 2204/20] Alloys based on aluminium -- F16C 2204/20 covers shafts made of aluminium-based alloys, including those where aluminium is the principal constituent and other metals are added to improve strength, corrosion resistance or mechanical properties. The code is used for patents describing the design, manufacturing or treatment of metallic shafts composed of aluminium alloys.
[F16C 2204/22] with tin as the next major constituent -- The subclass F16C 2204/22 refers to shafts made of metallic materials in which tin is the secondary major constituent after the primary metal. It covers designs, manufacturing methods and applications of such tin-containing alloy shafts used in mechanical engineering.
[F16C 2204/24] with lead as the next major constituent -- F16C 2204/24 classifies metallic shafts in which lead is the secondary principal material after the primary metal. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts, and applies to any shaft where lead is present in a significant amount as the next major constituent.
[F16C 2204/26] Alloys based on magnesium -- F16C 2204/26 relates to metallic materials for shafts that are primarily composed of magnesium alloys. It covers magnesium-based compositions, their processing and properties, and their use in lightweight mechanical components such as rotating shafts where strength-to-weight and corrosion resistance are important.
[F16C 2204/30] Alloys based on one of tin, lead, antimony, bismuth, indium, e.g. materials for providing sliding surfaces -- F16C 2204/30 covers alloys whose primary constituent is tin, lead, antimony, bismuth, indium or similar soft metals, intended for use as sliding-surface materials on shafts and other mechanical elements. These compositions are typically employed where low friction, wear resistance or self-lubrication is required, such as bearings, bushings and other contact surfaces.
[F16C 2204/32] Alloys based on lead -- F16C 2204/32 covers metallic alloys where lead is the principal component, intended for use in shafts and other rotating elements. These lead-rich compositions may be combined with small amounts of other metals to modify properties such as strength, wear resistance or machinability while retaining the characteristic high density and low melting point of lead.
[F16C 2204/34] Alloys based on tin -- Alloys based on tin are metallic materials used in shafts and other engineering elements, offering corrosion resistance and low-temperature strength. This CPC subclass groups tin-based alloys employed in mechanical engineering applications such as bearings, gears, and other rotating components. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 2204/36] Alloys based on bismuth -- F16C 2204/36 pertains to metallic materials for shafts, specifically alloys whose principal constituent is bismuth. It includes bismuth-based compositions designed for mechanical-engineering components such as bearings, bushings or other rotating parts where low-melting, self-lubricating properties are advantageous.
[F16C 2204/40] Alloys based on refractory metals -- F16C 2204/40 covers alloys that are primarily composed of refractory metals such as tungsten, molybdenum, tantalum, niobium or rhenium, used in shafts and other mechanical engineering components. These alloys are distinguished by very high melting points, excellent strength at elevated temperatures and strong resistance to wear, corrosion and thermal fatigue.
[F16C 2204/42] Alloys based on titanium -- Alloys based on titanium are metallic materials employed in shafts and related mechanical-engineering components, covering titanium-based compositions, processing and properties. They are classified under CPC code F16C 2204/42 within the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Metallic Materials.
[F16C 2204/44] Alloys based on chromium -- The CPC subclass F16C 2204/44 designates shafts that are made from metallic materials consisting of chromium-based alloys, covering their composition, manufacturing methods and related treatment within mechanical engineering.
[F16C 2204/46] Alloys based on molybdenum -- F16C 2204/46 designates metallic alloys whose primary component is molybdenum and that are applied to shafts and similar rotating-elements. These alloys are covered in the CPC under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and metallic materials.
[F16C 2204/50] Alloys based on zinc -- The CPC subclass F16C 2204/50 covers zinc-based alloys employed in shafts and other engineering elements, focusing on their composition, properties, and applications within mechanical engineering. It addresses metallic materials where zinc is the principal component, detailing alloying elements and performance characteristics relevant to shaft design and manufacturing.
[F16C 2204/52] Alloys based on nickel, e.g. Inconel -- F16C 2204/52 covers shafts constructed from nickel-based alloys, such as Inconel, prized for high temperature strength and corrosion resistance in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 2204/60] Ferrous alloys, e.g. steel alloys -- F16C 2204/60 designates ferrous alloys, such as steel alloys, employed in shafts and related mechanical engineering components. It resides within the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and metallic materials, and is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F16C 2204/62] Low carbon steel, i.e. carbon content below 0.4 wt% -- F16C 2204/62 designates low-carbon steel with a carbon content below 0.4 wt % and is used for shafts and other engineering elements, placing it in the metallic materials subclass of mechanical engineering.
[F16C 2204/64] Medium carbon steel, i.e. carbon content from 0.4 to 0,8 wt% -- F16C 2204/64 designates medium carbon steel, containing 0.4 to 0.8 wt% carbon, as a material for shafts and other mechanical engineering components. It falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering - Engineering Elements and Units - Shafts - Metallic Materials.
[F16C 2204/66] High carbon steel, i.e. carbon content above 0.8 wt%, e.g. through-hardenable steel -- F16C 2204/66 covers high-carbon steel - steel with a carbon content above 0.8 wt% such as through-hardenable grades - used for shafts and other mechanical engineering components.
[F16C 2204/70] with chromium as the next major constituent -- This subclass concerns metal shafts whose composition includes chromium as the second-most abundant element after the base metal, indicating an alloy designed for enhanced corrosion resistance or strength. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → shafts → metallic materials.
[F16C 2204/72] with nickel as further constituent, e.g. stainless steel -- F16C 2204/72 designates shafts made from metallic alloys that contain nickel, such as stainless-steel compositions. It covers the use of nickel as an additional element in the material of shafts, distinguishing these from shafts of plain iron or other non-nickel alloys. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and metallic materials.
[F16C 2204/74] with manganese as the next major constituent -- F16C 2204/74 designates shafts of metallic composition in which manganese forms the second most abundant element after the base metal, covering design and manufacturing aspects of such shafts within mechanical engineering.
[F16C 2204/80] Amorphous alloys -- F16C 2204/80 designates amorphous alloys, also known as metallic glasses, employed in shafts and other mechanical components. These non-crystalline metallic materials provide high strength, elastic limit and corrosion resistance while enabling compact designs. The classification is used for patents concerning their composition, production and shaft-specific applications.
[F16C 2206/00] Materials with ceramics, cermets, hard carbon or similar non-metallic hard materials as main constituents -- F16C 2206/00 designates shafts whose primary material is a ceramic, cermet, hard carbon or other non-metallic hard substance, emphasizing the use of these wear-resistant, high-strength materials in mechanical engineering shaft components.
[F16C 2206/02] Carbon based material -- F16C 2206/02 classifies shafts whose principal constituent is a carbon-based material such as graphite or carbon-fiber composites, used in mechanical engineering applications where high hardness, wear resistance, or low weight is required. The code falls under the broader CPC hierarchy of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and specifically addresses non-metallic hard materials.
[F16C 2206/04] Diamond like carbon [DLC] -- F16C 2206/04 classifies shafts whose principal material is diamond-like carbon, an amorphous hard carbon coating that mimics the hardness and low friction of diamond. The code is used for mechanical elements that require wear-resistant, non-metallic surfaces in engineering applications.
[F16C 2206/06] Composite carbon material, e.g. carbon fibre reinforced carbon (C/C) -- This subclass pertains to shafts whose principal constituent is a composite carbon material, for example carbon-fibre reinforced carbon (C/C). The material combines carbon fibres within a carbon matrix to deliver high strength, stiffness and thermal resistance, making it suitable for demanding mechanical-engineering applications.
[F16C 2206/40] Ceramics, e.g. carbides, nitrides, oxides, borides of a metal -- F16C 2206/40 pertains to shafts whose principal material is a ceramic, including metal carbides, nitrides, oxides or borides, used in mechanical engineering applications. The entry lies under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts, grouping non-metallic hard materials as the main constituent.
[F16C 2206/42] based on ceramic oxides -- F16C 2206/42 classifies shafts whose principal construction material consists of ceramic oxides such as alumina or zirconia, forming a non-metallic hard material for mechanical engineering applications. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts, and addresses components where ceramics, cermets or hard carbon are the main constituents.
[F16C 2206/44] based on aluminium oxide (Al -- The CPC subclass F16C 2206/44 covers shafts whose principal material is a ceramic composed mainly of aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃). It pertains to mechanical-engineering components where the shaft is manufactured from this hard, non-metallic material to provide wear resistance and high strength. This entry falls under the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and materials with ceramics, cermets, hard carbon or similar constituents.
[F16C 2206/48] based on zirconia (ZrO -- This subclass covers shaft elements whose principal material is a zirconia-based ceramic, such as ZrO₂, often used for its high hardness and wear resistance. It includes designs, manufacturing methods and applications of ceramic, cermet or hard-carbon shafts in mechanical engineering.
[F16C 2206/56] based on ceramic carbides, e.g. silicon carbide (SiC) -- A shaft whose primary material consists of ceramic carbides, such as silicon carbide (SiC), falls under this classification. It pertains to mechanical engineering elements where the shaft is made from hard, non-metallic composites to provide high wear resistance, temperature tolerance and strength, distinguishing it from metal-based shafts.
[F16C 2206/58] based on ceramic nitrides -- F16C 2206/58 covers shafts whose primary material is a ceramic nitride, such as silicon nitride or other nitride-based ceramics. These non-metallic hard materials provide high hardness, wear resistance and thermal stability, making the shafts suitable for demanding mechanical engineering applications where conventional metals would be inadequate.
[F16C 2206/60] Silicon nitride (Si -- F16C 2206/60 covers shafts whose primary material is silicon nitride, a hard ceramic composed of silicon and nitrogen. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering elements for shafts made of non-metallic hard materials such as ceramics, cermets or hard carbon, specifying silicon nitride as the constituent.
[F16C 2206/80] Cermets, i.e. composites of ceramics and metal -- F16C 2206/80 pertains to shafts whose material is primarily a cermet, a composite of ceramic and metal components. It includes any shaft where the hard, non-metallic phase such as ceramics, cermets, hard carbon or similar is the main constituent, focusing on the use of these wear-resistant composites in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 2206/82] based on tungsten carbide [WC] -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16C 2206/82 pertains to shafts in mechanical engineering, specifically addressing materials composed primarily of tungsten carbide. This classification is used for inventions involving components made from this hard, wear-resistant material.
[F16C 2208/00] Plastics -- This CPC subclass covers shafts made from plastic materials, including their design, manufacturing methods and related applications in mechanical engineering. It is placed under mechanical engineering > engineering elements and units > shafts and is used to classify inventions dealing with polymeric shaft components.
[F16C 2208/02] comprising fillers, fibres -- F16C 2208/02 covers plastic shafts that contain fillers or fibres, which are added to modify mechanical strength, stiffness, or wear resistance. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts, specifying composite plastic materials used in rotating or transmitting components.
[F16C 2208/04] Glass fibres -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16C 2208/04, titled "Glass fibres," is part of the broader category dealing with mechanical engineering, specifically focusing on shafts and their components. This classification pertains to the use or inclusion of glass fibers in engineering elements and units related to shafts.
[F16C 2208/10] Elastomers -- F16C 2208/10 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the European and United States patent offices, categorizes elastomers specifically within the context of shafts under mechanical engineering. This code is part of a detailed hierarchy that narrows down from broader categories like mechanical engineering to specific materials such as elastomers used in shaft components.
[F16C 2208/12] Polyurethan [PU] -- F16C 2208/12 classifies shafts that are formed from polyurethane plastic, a material valued for its flexibility, wear resistance and lightweight properties. The entry covers the design, manufacture and use of such polyurethane shafts in mechanical power transmission and similar applications.
[F16C 2208/14] Silicone rubber -- F16C 2208/14 refers to shafts or other rotating mechanical elements that are made principally of silicone rubber, a resilient polymer valued for its flexibility, temperature stability and low-friction properties in engineering applications.
[F16C 2208/20] Thermoplastic resins -- The CPC subclass F16C 2208/20, located under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Plastics, relates to thermoplastic resins used in shaft construction or related components. It covers the selection, formulation and processing of thermoplastic materials for such engineering applications.
[F16C 2208/22] comprising two or more thermoplastics -- The F16C 2208/22 subclass of the CPC concerns plastic shafts that are formed from a combination of two or more thermoplastic materials, typically to achieve enhanced mechanical or chemical properties. It covers designs and manufacturing methods where distinct thermoplastic polymers are blended or layered within a single shaft element. This classification is used for inventions involving composite or multi-material plastic shafts in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 2208/30] Fluoropolymers -- F16C 2208/30 covers the use of fluoropolymers in shaft constructions, part of the CPC class for mechanical engineering elements. It concerns plastic components, specifically fluoropolymer materials, employed to improve wear resistance, chemical stability, and low friction in rotating or sliding shafts.
[F16C 2208/32] Polytetrafluorethylene [PTFE] -- F16C 2208/32 designates shafts that are made of polytetrafluoroethylene, a plastic known for low friction and chemical resistance. The classification covers mechanical engineering components where PTFE is used as the shaft material.
[F16C 2208/34] Polyvinylidenefluoride [PVDF] -- F16C 2208/34 covers shafts, rods or similar components that are manufactured from the fluoropolymer polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF). PVDF provides high chemical resistance, temperature stability and good mechanical strength, making it suitable for demanding mechanical engineering applications. The code is used to classify inventions that employ PVDF as the material for rotating or fixed shafts.
[F16C 2208/36] Polyarylene ether ketones [PAEK], e.g. PEK, PEEK -- F16C 2208/36 covers shafts formed from polyarylene ether ketones such as PEK and PEEK, high-performance thermoplastic polymers known for excellent strength, stiffness and heat resistance, making them suitable for demanding mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 2208/40] Imides, e.g. polyimide [PI], polyetherimide [PEI] -- F16C 2208/40 in the Cooperative Patent Classification (EPO/USPTO) pertains to the use of imides, such as polyimide and polyetherimide, in mechanical engineering elements and units, specifically for shafts. These materials are noted for their high strength and thermal stability.
[F16C 2208/42] Polyamideimide [PAI] -- Polyamideimide (PAI) is a high-temperature, high-strength engineering plastic employed for manufacturing shafts and other mechanical components where wear resistance and rigidity are required. It combines an aromatic polyimide backbone with flexible aliphatic segments, giving excellent chemical resistance and dimensional stability. The CPC entry F16C 2208/42 covers such PAI materials used in shaft applications.
[F16C 2208/44] Polybenzimidazole [PBI] -- The Cooperative Patent Classification (F16C 2208/44) under the category of Mechanical Engineering, specifically for shafts and plastics, designates polybenzimidazole [PBI] as a material used in engineering elements and units. This classification is jointly maintained by the European Patent Office (EPO) and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO).
[F16C 2208/48] Liquid crystal polymers [LCP] -- The CPC subclass F16C 2208/48 covers shafts that are manufactured from liquid crystal polymers, a high-performance plastic known for exceptional strength, stiffness, dimensional stability and heat resistance. It includes devices such as bearings, rods or other rotating elements where the unique anisotropic properties of LCPs improve performance in demanding mechanical-engineering applications.
[F16C 2208/52] Polyphenylene sulphide [PPS] -- Polyphenylene sulphide (PPS) is a high-temperature, chemically resistant thermoplastic used for precision shafts and other mechanical elements. Its excellent dimensional stability, low friction and good wear resistance make it suitable where metal shafts would be corroded or overloaded. In the Cooperative Patent Classification this material is catalogued under F16C 2208/52 within the mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, plastics hierarchy.
[F16C 2208/54] Polysulphones, e.g. polysulphone [PSU], polyethersulphone [PES], polyethersulphone-block copolymer [PPSU] -- F16C 2208/54 classifies shafts that are manufactured from polysulphones such as polysulphone (PSU), polyethersulphone (PES) and polyethersulphone-block copolymer (PPSU). These plastics are used for mechanical engineering components requiring high temperature resistance and strength. The entry falls under the broader CPC hierarchy of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and plastics.
[F16C 2208/58] Several materials as provided for in -- F16C 2208/58 is a CPC subclass for shafts that are manufactured from a range of plastic or polymeric materials, including combinations of different plastics. It covers shafts where the material composition is specified as "several materials as provided for in" the relevant standards, addressing the use of multiple or composite plastics in mechanical engineering shaft applications.
[F16C 2208/60] Polyamides [PA] -- F16C 2208/60 is a CPC subclass that groups polyamide (PA) plastics employed in shafts and other mechanical engineering elements. It covers the material properties, manufacturing methods and applications of these synthetic polymers within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 2208/62] high performance polyamides, e.g. PA12, PA46 -- F16C 2208/62 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system refers to the use of high-performance polyamides, such as PA12 and PA46, specifically for shafts in mechanical engineering applications. These materials are noted for their durability and strength under various conditions.
[F16C 2208/66] Acetals, e.g. polyoxymethylene [POM] -- F16C 2208/66 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system refers to the use of acetals, such as polyoxymethylene (POM), in mechanical engineering elements and units, specifically for shafts. This code is used by the European Patent Office (EPO) and United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) to classify patents involving these materials.
[F16C 2208/70] Polyesters, e.g. polyethylene-terephthlate [PET], polybutylene-terephthlate [PBT] -- F16C 2208/70 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system refers to the use of polyesters, such as polyethylene terephthalate (PET) and polybutylene terephthalate (PBT), specifically for shafts in mechanical engineering. This classification is part of a broader category that includes various engineering elements and units.
[F16C 2208/72] Acrylics, e.g. polymethylmethacrylate [PMMA] -- F16C 2208/72 classifies shafts that are manufactured from acrylic plastics, primarily polymethylmethacrylate. It groups mechanical engineering components where transparent or lightweight polymer shafts are employed, covering design, production and use of PMMA shafts in devices.
[F16C 2208/76] Polyolefins, e.g. polyproylene [PP] -- The Cooperative Patent Classification (F16C 2208/76) under the category of Mechanical Engineering, specifically for shafts and plastics, refers to the use of polyolefins such as polypropylene in engineering components. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office to categorize patent applications involving these materials.
[F16C 2208/78] Polyethylene [PE], e.g. ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene [UHMWPE] -- F16C 2208/78 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the EPO and USPTO, specifies the use of polyethylene, including ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene, for shafts in mechanical engineering. This classification is part of a broader hierarchy that includes mechanical engineering elements and units.
[F16C 2208/80] Thermosetting resins -- F16C 2208/80 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the European and United States patent offices, specifies thermosetting resins for shafts within mechanical engineering. This classification is part of a broader category that includes various materials and components for engineering elements and units.
[F16C 2208/82] Composites, i.e. fibre reinforced thermosetting resins -- F16C 2208/82 classifies composites made from fibre-reinforced thermosetting resins that are employed in shaft constructions. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the engineering elements and units category for shafts and plastics, covering the use of reinforced polymer materials to enhance strength, stiffness and durability in rotating or static shaft components.
[F16C 2208/86] Epoxy resins -- Epoxy resins are thermosetting polymers that cure to form a hard, chemically resistant plastic, often employed as a coating or matrix material for mechanical shafts. In mechanical engineering they provide high strength, adhesion and corrosion protection for rotating elements and can be molded into complex shaft geometries.
[F16C 2208/90] Phenolic resin -- F16C 2208/90 designates phenolic resin used in shaft components, covering polymeric phenolic materials employed within mechanical engineering shafts and related plastic parts. The entry addresses the formulation, processing and performance characteristics of these thermosetting resins, which provide high heat resistance, electrical insulation and dimensional stability for shafts and similar elements.
[F16C 2210/00] Fluids -- The CPC subclass F16C 2210/00 covers shafts that incorporate or transport fluids, including designs where fluid passage is integral to the shaft for lubrication, cooling, power transmission or sealing functions. It encompasses members such as fluid-filled driveshafts, lubricated rotating rods and shafts with built-in channels for fluid flow.
[F16C 2210/02] defined by their properties -- F16C 2210/02 is a CPC subclass for shafts used in fluid-handling equipment that are classified according to their inherent properties such as material composition, geometry, strength, corrosion resistance or surface finish rather than by specific function. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Fluids.
[F16C 2210/04] by viscosity -- F16C 2210/04 covers shaft designs and applications in which the fluid's viscosity is the defining characteristic, such as hydraulic or lubricated shafts where viscosity determines load-bearing capacity, speed control, or energy transmission. It classifies inventions that exploit or adjust fluid viscosity to optimise shaft performance.
[F16C 2210/06] magnetic fluids -- Magnetic fluids, or ferrofluids, are stable colloidal suspensions of magnetic nanoparticles in a liquid carrier that respond to magnetic fields. In shaft applications they provide sealant, damping or lubrication by forming a controllable fluid film that remains attached to rotating components. The technology is classified under CPC F16C 2210/06 for fluids used with shafts.
[F16C 2210/08] molten metals -- F16C 2210/08 covers shafts and rotating elements intended for use with molten metals, including designs that transport, support, or withstand liquid metal streams and incorporate heating, cooling, or protective features. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, fluids.
[F16C 2210/10] water based -- F16C 2210/10 relates to shafts and associated components that employ water-based fluids, such as water-lubricated bearings, cooling passages or hydraulic systems where water serves as the working or lubricating medium.
[F16C 2212/00] Natural materials, i.e. based on animal or plant products such as leather, wood or cotton or extracted therefrom, e.g. lignin -- The classification covers shafts that are manufactured from natural, animal- or plant-derived materials such as leather, wood, cotton, lignin or extracts thereof, used as mechanical engineering elements. It pertains to the design, production, and application of such biologically sourced shaft components.
[F16C 2212/04] Wood -- The CPC subclass F16C 2212/04 classifies wooden shafts and similar rotating members in mechanical engineering, covering any shaft or spindle whose structural material is wood-a natural, plant-derived material. It includes designs, manufacturing methods and applications where wood is used as the primary shaft material.
[F16C 2212/08] Woven, unwoven fabrics, e.g. felt -- F16C 2212/08 classifies shafts that utilize woven or unwoven fabrics, such as felt, which are natural materials derived from animal or plant products. These materials are used for specific engineering applications where their unique properties provide advantages in performance or functionality.
[F16C 2220/00] Shaping -- F16C 2220/00 covers methods and apparatus for shaping shafts, including machining, grinding, or other material removal techniques applied to cylindrical components. It classifies inventions that modify the external profile of a shaft to achieve specific functional or geometric requirements.
[F16C 2220/02] by casting -- The code F16C 2220/02 refers to shafts that are formed by a casting process, meaning the metal is poured into a mould and allowed to solidify into the required shape. It is a subcategory of shaping within the shaft element group of mechanical engineering.
[F16C 2220/04] by injection-moulding -- F16C 2220/04 designates the production of shafts by injection-moulding, where molten polymer or metal is forced into a mould cavity to create the shaft's geometry in a single step. This method replaces conventional machining or forming operations for shaft shaping and is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, shaping.
[F16C 2220/06] in situ  casting or moulding -- This subclass of F16C covers shafts whose shaping is achieved by casting or moulding the material directly in place, i.e., in situ processes that form the shaft without separate machining. It includes methods where molten metal or polymer is poured into a mould that creates the shaft on the component itself, often used for repair or integrated manufacturing.
[F16C 2220/08] by compression-moulding -- F16C 2220/08 covers methods of shaping shafts by compression-moulding, where a material-often a polymer or composite-is placed in a mould and formed into a shaft or similar component under pressure. This technique is used to produce precise shaft geometries with improved mechanical properties and surface finish.
[F16C 2220/20] by sintering pulverised material, e.g. powder metallurgy -- F16C 2220/20 pertains to the shaping of shafts through the process of sintering pulverised materials, commonly known as powder metallurgy, which is a technique used in mechanical engineering for manufacturing components with specific properties.
[F16C 2220/24] by built-up welding -- The code F16C 2220/24 covers shaping of shafts by built-up welding, a process in which additional material is welded onto a shaft to obtain the required dimensions or profile. This method is used for manufacturing or repairing shafts where material removal alone cannot achieve the desired shape.
[F16C 2220/28] by winding impregnated fibres -- The subclass F16C 2220/28 covers shaping shafts through the winding of fibres that have been impregnated with resin or similar binding agents, forming a composite structure around a core. This technique is used to produce lightweight, high-strength shafts for mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 2220/40] by deformation without removing material -- F16C 2220/40 covers processes for shaping a shaft by deforming the material instead of removing it, including techniques such as cold forming, bending, rolling or upsetting that alter the shaft's geometry while retaining its mass.
[F16C 2220/42] by working of thin-walled material such as sheet or tube -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F16C 2220/42 pertains to the shaping of shafts through processes that involve working with thin-walled materials like sheets or tubes, focusing on mechanical engineering elements and units.
[F16C 2220/44] by rolling -- The subclass F16C 2220/44 relates to shaping shafts by rolling, where rollers deform the shaft material to produce the desired external or internal profile. It covers processes and devices that achieve the final geometry through plastic deformation rather than cutting or machining. This classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 2220/46] by forging -- F16C 2220/46 covers shafts that are formed through forging processes. This sub-group falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units, shafts, and shaping, and addresses the manufacturing of forged shafts.
[F16C 2220/48] by extrusion, e.g. of metallic profiles -- This subclass covers methods of shaping shafts by extrusion, especially the production of metallic profiles, where a metal workpiece is forced through a die to obtain the desired cross-section. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → shafts → shaping.
[F16C 2220/60] by removing material, e.g. machining -- F16C 2220/60 relates to shaping shafts by removing material, covering machining processes such as cutting, grinding, or other subtractive methods that alter the shaft's dimensions or profile. This subclass groups techniques that achieve the desired shape through material removal rather than deformation or additive means.
[F16C 2220/62] by turning, boring, drilling -- F16C 2220/62 classifies shaping processes applied to shafts that involve turning, boring or drilling operations, covering mechanical-engineering methods for contouring and material removal from rotating or stationary shafts.
[F16C 2220/66] by milling -- The F16C 2220/66 subclass concerns shaping of shafts by milling, where a rotating cutting tool removes material to form the required external or internal profile. It includes any milling operations applied to shaft elements to achieve dimensional or surface-finish specifications.
[F16C 2220/68] by electrical discharge or electrochemical machining -- This CPC subclass (F16C 2220/68) covers shaping of shafts using electrical discharge or electrochemical machining, where material is removed by spark erosion or electrochemically driven dissolution. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, shaping.
[F16C 2220/70] by grinding -- The CPC entry F16C 2220/70 pertains to shaping shafts by grinding. It covers methods that remove material from a shaft through abrasive grinding to achieve specified dimensions, geometry or surface finish.
[F16C 2220/80] by separating parts, e.g. by severing, cracking -- F16C 2220/80 covers shaping of shafts by separating the material, for example through severing, cracking or other breakage techniques that divide the shaft into distinct parts. The subclass is located under Mechanical Engineering - Engineering Elements and Units - Shafts - Shaping.
[F16C 2220/82] by cutting -- F16C 2220/82 classifies methods for shaping shafts by cutting, covering processes that remove material to form or refine the external or internal profile of a rotating element. It includes machining techniques such as turning, milling or grinding that are applied to shafts to achieve required dimensions, tolerances or surface finishes in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 2220/84] by perforating -- F16C 2220/84 covers the shaping of shafts through perforating techniques, where holes are introduced to modify dimensions, weight, or mechanical properties. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Shaping, and pertains to methods that create or enlarge perforations in rotating or stationary shaft components.
[F16C 2223/00] Surface treatments -- F16C 2223/00 pertains to surface treatments applied to shafts and other rotating elements. It includes processes such as coating, heat-treating, plating, ion implantation or shot peening that modify the shaft's exterior to improve wear resistance, corrosion protection, fatigue life, or dimensional stability.
[F16C 2223/02] Mechanical treatment, e.g. finishing -- F16C 2223/02 refers to mechanical surface-treatment processes applied to shafts, such as finishing operations that modify the shaft's outer layer to improve texture, hardness or dimensional accuracy. It is classified under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → shafts → surface treatments.
[F16C 2223/04] by sizing, by shaping to final size by small plastic deformation, e.g. by calibrating or coining -- F16C 2223/04 covers methods of finishing shafts by altering their dimensions through minor plastic deformation. The process includes techniques such as calibrating or coining to achieve the final size and surface characteristics.
[F16C 2223/06] polishing -- F16C 2223/06 covers polishing processes applied to shafts, including techniques that improve surface smoothness, reduce friction and enhance wear resistance. It is classified under surface-treatment of shafts within the broader mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F16C 2223/08] shot-peening, blasting -- F16C 2223/08 classifies surface-treatment methods for shafts, specifically shot-peening and blasting processes that impart compressive residual stresses to enhance fatigue strength and surface integrity. This falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Surface treatments.
[F16C 2223/10] Hardening, e.g. carburizing, carbo-nitriding -- F16C 2223/10 covers surface-treatment processes applied to shafts, specifically hardening techniques such as carburizing and carbo-nitriding that increase surface hardness and wear resistance. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and deals with methods for improving shaft performance through diffusion-based hardening.
[F16C 2223/12] with carburizing -- F16C 2223/12 classifies shafts that have been subjected to carburizing, a heat-treatment process that diffuses carbon into the surface to increase hardness and wear resistance. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Surface treatments.
[F16C 2223/14] with nitriding -- F16C 2223/14 relates to shafts that are treated by nitriding, a surface-hardening process in which nitrogen diffuses into the metal to form a hard, wear-resistant layer. This classification covers the use of nitriding on rotating or stationary shafts to improve fatigue strength, wear resistance and corrosion protection, typically applied in automotive, aerospace and industrial machinery components.
[F16C 2223/16] with carbo-nitriding -- The subclass F16C 2223/16 refers to shafts that are treated by carbo-nitriding, a thermochemical process that diffuses carbon and nitrogen into the surface layer. This treatment creates a hard, wear-resistant case while retaining a tougher core, enhancing the shaft's fatigue strength and service life.
[F16C 2223/18] with induction hardening -- F16C 2223/18 covers shafts that have been surface-treated by induction hardening, a method in which electromagnetic induction rapidly heats the outer layer, followed by quenching to produce a hardened, wear-resistant surface. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Surface treatments.
[F16C 2223/30] Coating surfaces -- F16C 2223/30 classifies the application of coating materials to the surfaces of shafts and other rotating mechanical elements. It includes methods and compositions for depositing protective, lubricating or functional layers to enhance wear-resistance, corrosion protection or performance.
[F16C 2223/32] by attaching pre-existing layers, e.g. resin sheets or foils by adhesion to a substrate -- F16C 2223/32 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to methods of surface treatment for shafts involving the attachment of pre-existing layers, such as resin sheets or foils, through adhesion to a substrate. This process is used to enhance the mechanical properties or protective qualities of the shaft's surface.
[F16C 2223/40] by dipping in molten material -- F16C 2223/40 designates methods for treating the surface of shafts by immersing them in a molten material, such as a metal or alloy, to create a coating or hardened layer. The process typically involves dipping the shaft into a bath of the molten substance, resulting in a metallurgical bond that improves wear-resistance, corrosion protection, or other functional properties.
[F16C 2223/42] by spraying the coating material, e.g. plasma spraying -- The CPC subclass F16C 2223/42 covers surface treatment of shafts in which a coating is applied by spraying techniques, such as plasma spraying, to modify the shaft's surface properties. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and surface treatments.
[F16C 2223/44] by casting molten material on the substrate -- This subclass covers surface-treatment methods for shafts in which molten material is poured or cast directly onto the shaft's substrate to form a coating, overlay or protective layer, often to improve wear resistance, corrosion protection or mechanical properties.
[F16C 2223/46] by welding, e.g. by using a laser to build a layer -- This subclass covers surface treatment of shafts performed by welding processes, such as laser cladding that builds a material layer on the shaft surface. It includes methods where a weld deposit is applied to modify wear resistance, corrosion protection, or mechanical properties of the shaft.
[F16C 2223/60] by vapour deposition, e.g. PVD, CVD -- The subclass F16C 2223/60 covers surface treatments applied to shafts that are achieved through vapor-phase methods, such as physical or chemical vapour deposition. It includes processes where metal, ceramic or composite coatings are formed on the shaft surface by depositing material from a gas or plasma. This technique is used to enhance wear resistance, corrosion protection or functional properties of the shaft.
[F16C 2223/70] by electroplating or electrolytic coating, e.g. anodising, galvanising -- F16C 2223/70 refers to the surface treatment of shafts by electroplating or electrolytic coating, including processes such as anodising and galvanising. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and surface treatments.
[F16C 2223/80] by powder coating -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F16C 2223/80 pertains to the surface treatment of shafts through powder coating, a process used in mechanical engineering to apply a protective and durable finish. This classification is part of a detailed system maintained by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office.
[F16C 2226/00] Joining parts -- F16C 2226/00 covers methods and devices for joining shaft components, including couplings, flanges, and other means of mechanically linking rotating parts. It addresses designs that secure shafts together while transmitting torque and accommodating alignment or vibration. The classification is used for patents involving shaft coupling technologies.
[F16C 2226/10] Force connections, e.g. clamping -- F16C 2226/10 covers force-type connections that join shafts, such as clamping or squeezing devices that secure one shaft to another or to a housing without permanent deformation. It includes mechanisms that apply compressive or tensile forces, like set-screws, collars, and pneumatic or hydraulic clamps, to achieve a reversible or adjustable attachment.
[F16C 2226/12] by press-fit, e.g. plug-in -- The CPC subclass F16C 2226/12 concerns shafts that are joined to other parts through a press-fit or plug-in method, where the components are inserted into one another and held together by an interference fit rather than by screws, bolts or adhesives. It covers designs that rely on the friction and deformation produced by the tight fit to secure the connection.
[F16C 2226/14] by shrink fit, i.e. heating and shrinking part to allow assembly -- F16C 2226/14 classifies methods of joining shaft components by shrink fit, where a part is heated to expand or a mating part is cooled to contract, allowing it to be inserted and then solidly locked as temperatures equalize. This technique is used for assembling mechanical elements without additional fasteners.
[F16C 2226/16] by wedge action, e.g. by tapered or conical parts -- This CPC subclass covers shaft-joining methods that use a wedge principle, typically employing tapered or conical elements to lock the parts together. It resides in the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Joining parts.
[F16C 2226/18] by magnets, i.e. magnetic attraction to hold parts together -- F16C 2226/18 covers shafts that are joined by magnetic forces, where magnets are used to hold the parts together without mechanical fasteners. This includes designs that rely on magnetic attraction to secure rotating or stationary components, often for quick assembly, disassembly, or torque transmission in machines.
[F16C 2226/30] Material joints -- F16C 2226/30 relates to the joining of shafts by material joints, where the connection is achieved through the material of the parts themselves rather than by separate fasteners such as screws or pins. This includes methods such as welding, brazing, adhesive bonding or casting that create a permanent, load-bearing union between rotating elements. The classification covers the design, manufacturing and testing of these material-based shaft connections.
[F16C 2226/32] by soldering -- The CPC subclass F16C 2226/32 relates to joining shaft components by soldering, a process where a filler metal is melted to bond the parts without melting the base material. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for shafts, and addresses methods of assembling shafts using soldered joints.
[F16C 2226/34] by brazing -- F16C 2226/34 designates methods for joining shafts through brazing, a process that uses a filler metal melted below the base metal's melting point to create a strong bond. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units, shafts, and joining parts. This classification is used in patent documents to identify inventions that employ brazing for shaft connections.
[F16C 2226/36] by welding -- F16C 2226/36 covers the welding of shaft components, including processes, apparatus and arrangements used to join shafts by fusion of metal parts to other elements or to each other in mechanical engineering.
[F16C 2226/38] with ultrasonic welding -- This subclass refers to shafts that are joined by ultrasonic welding, a technique that uses high-frequency vibrations to fuse the material surfaces together without filler or external heat sources. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and joining parts, specifying the use of ultrasonic energy for the permanent connection of shaft components.
[F16C 2226/40] with adhesive -- F16C 2226/40 covers shafts that are joined to other components using an adhesive bond. The classification includes any method where the shaft is attached by glue, resin, or similar chemical fastening rather than by welding, screwing or riveting. It applies to mechanical engineering designs where adhesion provides the primary joining action.
[F16C 2226/50] Positive connections -- F16C 2226/50 covers positive connections for shafts, i.e., mechanical means that securely lock two rotating elements together to transmit torque, such as splines, keyways, pins or wedges. These devices provide a non-slipping, indexed engagement and are classified under Mechanical Engineering - Engineering Elements and Units - Shafts - Joining parts.
[F16C 2226/52] with plastic deformation, e.g. caulking or staking -- F16C 2226/52 classifies shafts that are joined to other parts by locally deforming the material, typically using caulking or staking to create a permanent mechanical lock. The technique relies on plastic deformation of the shaft or adjoining component to form a secure connection without additional fasteners.
[F16C 2226/54] with rivets -- The CPC subclass F16C 2226/54 relates to methods and devices for joining shaft sections by means of rivets. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and joining parts. The classification covers rivet-based connections, including the design, assembly and securing of riveted shaft components.
[F16C 2226/60] with threaded parts, e.g. bolt and nut connections -- The F16C 2226/60 subclass pertains to shafts that are joined by threaded components such as bolts and nuts. It covers mechanical engineering designs where the connection relies on screw threads to secure the shaft to other parts, including various arrangements of threaded fasteners and their integration into shaft assemblies.
[F16C 2226/62] with pins, bolts or dowels -- The CPC subclass F16C 2226/62 defines shafts that are joined to other components by means of pins, bolts or dowels. It resides in the hierarchy under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Joining Parts, and addresses designs that use these fasteners for mechanical coupling.
[F16C 2226/70] with complementary interlocking parts -- F16C 2226/70 covers shafts that are joined to other components by complementary interlocking parts, such as splines, keyways, or matching protrusions and recesses that mesh together to transmit torque or motion without separate fasteners. This subgroup falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and joining parts.
[F16C 2226/72] with bayonet joints, i.e. parts are rotated to create positive interlock -- F16C 2226/72 covers shafts that incorporate bayonet joints, where the components are rotated relative to each other to create a positive interlock, used for secure mechanical connections of rotating parts.
[F16C 2226/74] with snap-fit, e.g. by clips -- F16C 2226/74 covers shafts that are joined to other components by snap-fit methods, such as using clips that lock the parts together without additional fasteners. The classification includes designs where the shaft has features that allow it to snap into place, enabling quick assembly and easy disassembly.
[F16C 2226/76] with tongue and groove or key and slot -- This CPC subclass defines shafts that are joined to other parts by a tongue-and-groove or key-and-slot arrangement, specifying the geometric features and methods used to transmit torque between components.
[F16C 2226/78] of jigsaw-puzzle type -- The code F16C 2226/78 covers shafts that are joined using interlocking, jigsaw-puzzle-type connections. It applies to mechanical-engineering components where the mating parts are shaped to fit together like puzzle pieces, providing a secure, non-welding method of assembly.
[F16C 2226/80] with splines, serrations or similar profiles to prevent movement between joined parts -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F16C 2226/80 pertains to mechanical engineering, specifically addressing shafts and their joining parts. It focuses on the use of splines, serrations, or similar profiles designed to prevent relative movement between the connected components.
[F16C 2229/00] Setting preload -- The code F16C 2229/00 relates to techniques for applying a predetermined axial preload to a shaft, such as tightening, stretching or otherwise pre-loading a shaft to improve its operational stiffness or bearing performance. It covers devices, methods and adjustments used to set and maintain that preload during assembly or service.
[F16C 2231/00] Running-in -- F16C 2231/00 concerns the running-in of shafts, the initial wear or surface-conditioning phase after assembly or installation. It covers methods, devices or procedures that promote controlled friction, material transfer and geometry adjustment to achieve a stable operating clearance and improve performance.
[F16C 2233/00] Monitoring condition, e.g. temperature, load, vibration -- F16C 2233/00 falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts. It covers systems or methods that monitor the condition of a shaft, including temperature, load, and vibration, using sensors or diagnostic techniques. The classification is used for patents related to condition-monitoring devices integrated with rotating shafts.
[F16C 2235/00] Cleaning -- F16C 2235/00 relates to cleaning of shafts, covering devices, methods or systems that remove contaminants, lubricants, scale or other deposits from rotating or stationary shafts, using mechanical, chemical, ultrasonic or other cleaning actions. It is a subclass of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts.
[F16C 2237/00] Repair or replacement -- The CPC subclass F16C 2237/00 relates to the repair or replacement of shafts, encompassing methods, devices and procedures for restoring, refurbishing or substituting damaged, worn or broken shafts in mechanical systems. This includes techniques for reconditioning shaft surfaces, joining or fitting new shaft sections, and any apparatus designed to facilitate shaft maintenance or replacement.
[F16C 2240/00] Specified values or numerical ranges of parameters -- The CPC group F16C 2240/00 covers shaft-related inventions in which the design or operation is defined by explicit numerical values or ranges for technical parameters. It includes specifications such as dimensions, material limits, speed, torque, stress, and other quantified characteristics that define the shaft's performance or geometry.
[F16C 2240/02] Flow, e.g. volume flow or mass flow -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16C 2240/02 pertains to the specification of flow parameters, such as volume or mass flow, in the context of shafts within mechanical engineering. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office for detailed indexing of patent documents.
[F16C 2240/06] Temperature -- F16C 2240/06 covers temperature aspects of shafts, including specified temperature ranges, measurement and control, and thermal effects such as expansion or stress. It is used for inventions that define or limit shaft temperature during operation or testing.
[F16C 2240/08] Time -- F16C 2240/08 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the EPO and USPTO, specifies time-related parameters for shafts within mechanical engineering. This classification covers aspects such as timing of operations or duration that are pertinent to the function or testing of shafts.
[F16C 2240/12] Force, load, stress, pressure -- In the CPC hierarchy for mechanical engineering-engineering elements-shafts, F16C 2240/12 designates documents that give specific numerical values or ranges for force, load, stress or pressure applied to shafts. It is used to index technical literature that reports such quantitative data.
[F16C 2240/14] Preload -- F16C 2240/14 covers shafts that are subjected to a preload, describing inventions that apply an initial axial or torsional load and specify the magnitude or range of that load. The classification is used for designs that use preloading to improve stiffness, reduce backlash, or control vibration.
[F16C 2240/18] Stress -- F16C 2240/18 is a CPC subclass that covers patents dealing with the stress characteristics of shafts, including the specification of stress values or numerical stress ranges. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Specified values or numerical ranges of parameters, focusing on how stress influences shaft performance and design.
[F16C 2240/22] Fluid pressure -- F16C 2240/22 designates the definition of fluid-pressure parameters for shafts, including specified values, ranges, or limits of pressure applied to or generated by a rotating shaft. It addresses the quantitative aspects of hydraulic or pneumatic forces that affect shaft performance, maintenance, or safety. The code is used to classify patents that disclose pressure thresholds, measurement methods, or pressure-dependent design criteria for shaft components.
[F16C 2240/26] Speed, e.g. rotational speed -- The CPC subclass F16C 2240/26 covers the specification of speed parameters, such as rotational speed, for shafts and other mechanical elements. It includes the definition of numeric limits, ranges or values that describe how fast a shaft is intended to operate within a mechanical system. This subclass is used to classify patents that define or control shaft speed characteristics.
[F16C 2240/30] Angles, e.g. inclinations -- F16C 2240/30 is a subclass of the CPC for mechanical engineering that deals with shafts and specifies the numerical ranges or values of angular parameters related to them. It covers the definition, measurement and limits of inclinations, pitches, or other oriented angles that affect shaft design, performance or alignment.
[F16C 2240/34] Contact angles -- The CPC subgroup F16C 2240/34 covers patents that specify the contact angle between a shaft (or axle) and a bearing, groove or supporting surface, defining the geometric relationship that influences load distribution, friction or lubrication. It includes the measurement, calculation or prescribed range of that angle.
[F16C 2240/40] Linear dimensions, e.g. length, radius, thickness, gap -- F16C 2240/40 is a CPC subclass for shaft components in which the patent specifies exact linear dimensions such as overall length, radius, wall thickness or clearance gaps. It groups inventions that disclose measured values or defined ranges for these parameters in mechanical engineering shaft designs.
[F16C 2240/42] Groove sizes -- The F16C 2240/42 class defines the dimensional parameters of grooves cut or formed on shafts, covering specified ranges for groove width, depth, profile and spacing used for keyways, bearing seats, or other functional features. It is applied when the exact size of these grooves is a determining factor in the shaft's design or performance.
[F16C 2240/44] Hole or pocket sizes -- F16C 2240/44 concerns the dimensional specifications of holes or pockets in shafts, defining the size ranges, tolerances and related numerical limits for such features. It is used to classify inventions that address how these openings are sized, measured or controlled within mechanical engineering components.
[F16C 2240/46] Gap sizes or clearances -- F16C 2240/46 relates to shafts in mechanical engineering and covers the specification of gap sizes or clearances between moving parts. It defines the numerical ranges or values of clearances that influence shaft performance and interaction with other components.
[F16C 2240/48] Particle sizes -- F16C 2240/48 classifies the definition of particle-size ranges or specific values for parameters related to shafts, such as grain-size distribution, abrasive particles in wear-resistant coatings, or other particulate characteristics that affect shaft performance. It is used to index documents that detail these numerical specifications.
[F16C 2240/50] Crowning, e.g. crowning height or crowning radius -- F16C 2240/50 covers the specification of crowning for shafts, such as the crowning height or the crowning radius, defining the allowable geometric deviation along the shaft length. It falls under mechanical engineering, within engineering elements and units, specifically the shaft category and the sub-group for numerical parameter ranges.
[F16C 2240/54] Surface roughness -- F16C 2240/54 classifies patents that deal with determining, specifying or controlling the surface roughness of shafts, covering measurement methods, roughness parameters and allowable tolerance ranges.
[F16C 2240/56] Tolerances -- F16C 2240/56 falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts, and it addresses the specification of tolerance values or numerical ranges for shaft parameters such as dimensions, straightness, roundness and surface finish. These tolerances define acceptable deviations to ensure proper function, interchangeability and compliance with design standards.
[F16C 2240/60] Thickness, e.g. thickness of coatings -- F16C 2240/60 pertains to the specification of dimensional parameters, specifically the thickness of a shaft element or its surface coating. It covers methods, devices or criteria for measuring or defining coating thickness, as well as tolerance ranges relevant to mechanical engineering applications. This subclass is used to classify patents that address thickness-related features of shafts or their protective layers.
[F16C 2240/64] in the nanometer range -- F16C 2240/64 classifies shafts whose dimensional parameters are defined within the nanometer range, covering mechanical engineering elements where extremely small diameters, lengths or tolerances are specified. It falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts and addresses the precise measurement limits for nano-scale shaft components.
[F16C 2240/70] Diameters -- The CPC subclass F16C 2240/70 covers the specification of shaft diameters, including the definition of numerical ranges or exact values for the diameter of rotating or stationary shafts. It is used to classify patents that disclose or claim particular diameter dimensions, tolerances, or size ranges for shafts in mechanical systems.
[F16C 2240/76] Osculation, i.e. relation between radii of balls and raceway groove -- F16C 2240/76 covers the geometrical relationship-known as osculation-between the radii of rolling elements such as balls and the groove of the raceway in a shaft bearing. It specifies how the curvature of the balls matches that of the groove to ensure proper contact and load distribution. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and deals with defined parameter values or ranges.
[F16C 2240/80] Pitch circle diameters [PCD] -- The code F16C 2240/80 designates information that specifies the pitch-circle diameter (PCD) of a shaft, defining the circular path on which gear teeth or other elements are positioned. It is used in mechanical-engineering classifications that address shaft dimensions and related parameters.
[F16C 2240/82] Degree of filling, i.e. sum of diameters of rolling elements in relation to PCD -- F16C 2240/82 relates to the degree of filling of rolling-element shafts, defined as the sum of the rolling-element diameters divided by the pitch-circle diameter. The code is used to specify numerical ranges for this parameter, which influences bearing load capacity and stiffness.
[F16C 2240/84] with full complement of balls or rollers, i.e. sum of clearances less than diameter of one rolling element -- The subclass F16C 2240/84 covers shafts whose bearing surfaces are completely filled with balls or rollers, so that the total clearance between adjacent rolling elements is smaller than the diameter of a single ball or roller. This tight-clearance arrangement ensures that the rolling elements are essentially in continuous contact with the shaft, providing high load-carrying capacity and precision.
[F16C 2240/90] Surface areas -- F16C 2240/90 covers the specification of surface-area values or ranges for shaft elements, defining how the total or partial surface of a shaft is quantified for engineering and patent purposes. It is used to describe dimensional parameters of shafts in mechanical designs.
[F16C 2240/94] Volume -- F16C 2240/94 relates to the volume of shafts, covering the specification of numerical ranges or values for shaft volume, including cross-sectional area, total displacement and methods for measuring or calculating these parameters in mechanical engineering.
[F16C 23/00] Bearings for exclusively rotary movement adjustable for aligning or positioning -- F16C 23/00 covers bearings intended for purely rotary movement that can be adjusted to align or position the shaft. These adjustable bearings allow fine-tuning of shaft orientation and clearance during operation.
[F16C 2300/00] Application independent of particular apparatuses -- F16C 2300/00 covers inventions related to the use of shafts where the application is described without reference to a particular device, focusing on generic functional aspects rather than specific machinery. It includes any shaft-based solution applicable across multiple machines or systems.
[F16C 2300/02] General use or purpose, i.e. no use, purpose, special adaptation or modification indicated or a wide variety of uses mentioned -- F16C 2300/02 covers shafts whose function is not tied to a specific device or application. The classification is used when the shaft is described in general terms without indicating a particular use, purpose, or special adaptation, or when many possible uses are mentioned.
[F16C 2300/10] related to size -- F16C 2300/10 is a CPC subclass for shafts that focuses on size-related features such as diameter, length, overall dimensions or dimensional tolerances, and is applied independently of any specific machine or device. It sits under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts.
[F16C 2300/12] Small applications, e.g. miniature bearings -- F16C 2300/12 covers shafts designed for small-scale uses such as miniature bearings, where the shaft dimensions and loads are reduced compared to standard industrial sizes. The classification applies to any device that uses such shafts regardless of the surrounding apparatus, focusing on the element itself rather than the overall machine.
[F16C 2300/14] Large applications, e.g. bearings having an inner diameter exceeding 500 mm -- The CPC subclass F16C 2300/14 covers shaft-related inventions designed for very large applications, such as bearings whose inner diameter exceeds 500 mm. It groups technologies where the size of the component is a defining feature, independent of the specific machine or apparatus in which the shaft is used. This helps identify solutions for heavy-load, high-capacity mechanical systems.
[F16C 2300/20] related to type of movement -- F16C 2300/20 classifies shafts where the emphasis is on the type of movement they generate or transmit, such as rotational, reciprocating or oscillatory motion, independent of any particular device. It applies to mechanical-engineering designs that specify movement characteristics rather than a complete apparatus.
[F16C 2300/22] High-speed rotation -- F16C 2300/22 designates technologies related to shafts or rotating elements that operate at high rotational speeds, independent of any specific machine. It includes features such as balancing, material selection, bearing arrangements and methods to mitigate stress, vibration and thermal effects associated with rapid rotation.
[F16C 2300/28] Reciprocating movement -- The CPC group F16C 2300/28 covers shafts designed to produce a reciprocating motion, meaning the shaft moves back and forth along its axis rather than rotating continuously. This classification applies to any device or mechanism that uses such linear oscillation, independent of the surrounding apparatus.
[F16C 2300/30] related to direction with respect to gravity -- F16C 2300/30 classifies shafts whose orientation or operational direction is defined with respect to gravity, such as vertical, inclined, or horizontal shafts, and the design considerations arising from gravitational forces on rotation, support, and load transmission. It applies to any mechanical system where shaft direction relative to gravity influences performance, independent of a specific apparatus.
[F16C 2300/32] Horizontal, e.g. bearings for supporting a horizontal shaft -- F16C 2300/32 covers bearings designed to support a horizontal shaft, such as those used in machines where the shaft extends parallel to the ground. It includes bearing configurations that accommodate loads while the shaft remains in a horizontal orientation, independent of any specific apparatus. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 2300/34] Vertical, e.g. bearings for supporting a vertical shaft -- F16C 2300/34 relates to bearings and similar devices that support a shaft oriented vertically, such as vertical bearings used in lifts, gear units or other machinery. The classification is applied independently of any particular apparatus, fitting within the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 2300/40] related to environment, i.e. operating conditions -- This CPC subclass concerns shafts whose design or use is influenced by environmental or operating conditions, without reference to a specific machine. It covers features such as temperature, pressure, corrosive media, or vibration that affect shaft performance. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and is application independent.
[F16C 2300/42] corrosive, i.e. with aggressive media or harsh conditions -- The CPC entry F16C 2300/42 falls under mechanical engineering shafts and addresses shafts designed for use in corrosive environments, where they encounter aggressive media or harsh conditions that can cause material degradation. It covers design, material selection and protective measures to ensure reliable operation despite exposure to chemicals, moisture, salts or extreme temperature.
[F16C 2300/52] low temperature, e.g. cryogenic temperature -- The subclass covers shafts that are intended to function at low or cryogenic temperatures, addressing material choices, design features and treatment methods that ensure reliable operation under such conditions, without reference to any particular machine or device.
[F16C 2300/54] high-temperature -- F16C 2300/54 pertains to shafts that are intended for use at elevated temperatures, addressing the material selection, heat-resistant design features and thermal-management methods required for reliable operation. It applies regardless of the specific machine or apparatus in which the shaft is employed, focusing on the functional aspects of high-temperature performance rather than a particular product. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 2300/62] low pressure, e.g. elements operating under vacuum conditions -- F16C 2300/62 covers shafts and related mechanical elements designed to operate at low pressure, such as in vacuum environments, without being tied to any particular machine or device. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for shafts, and addresses applications where reduced atmospheric pressure is a key operating condition.
[F16C 2300/64] high pressure, e.g. elements exposed to high pressure gases or fluids -- F16C 2300/64 covers shafts and related rotary parts designed to operate under high pressure, such as those exposed to high-pressure gases or fluids. The classification applies regardless of the specific apparatus in which the shaft is used, focusing on the pressure conditions the element must withstand.
[F16C 23/02] Sliding-contact bearings -- F16C 23/02 classifies sliding-contact bearings designed for purely rotary motion that can be adjusted to align or position the shaft. These bearings rely on a sliding surface rather than rolling elements and are used where precise angular placement or fine-tuning of the shaft is required. They fall under the mechanical engineering hierarchy of engineering elements and units, specifically within shafts.
[F16C 23/04] self-adjusting -- The code F16C 23/04 designates self-adjusting bearings that provide exclusively rotary movement and can be altered to align or position the shaft they support. These bearings automatically compensate for misalignment, ensuring smooth rotation and reducing wear. They belong to the mechanical-engineering class for shafts and bearing elements.
[F16C 23/041] with edge relief -- F16C 23/041 refers to bearings designed for purely rotary motion that can be adjusted to align or position the shaft, and that include edge-relief features to reduce stress concentrations and improve load distribution.
[F16C 23/043] with spherical surfaces, e.g. spherical plain bearings -- F16C 23/043 pertains to bearings designed for exclusively rotary movement that incorporate spherical surfaces, such as spherical plain bearings, and can be adjusted to align or position rotating shafts. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts.
[F16C 23/045] for radial load mainly, e.g. radial spherical plain bearings -- F16C 23/045 covers bearings that allow exclusively rotary motion and can be adjusted to align or position the shaft, specifically designed to carry mainly radial loads such as radial spherical plain bearings. This falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts.
[F16C 23/046] with split outer rings -- F16C 23/046 denotes rotary bearings designed for exclusively rotary movement that can be adjusted for alignment or positioning by means of a split outer ring. The split ring allows the bearing housing to be opened and reassembled around a shaft, facilitating precise placement and ease of installation. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 23/048] for axial load mainly -- F16C 23/048 covers bearings that are adjustable for shaft alignment or positioning and are intended mainly to carry axial loads. These are rotary bearings within mechanical engineering, specifically for shafts where axial force predominates.
[F16C 23/06] Ball or roller bearings -- F16C 23/06 refers to ball or roller bearings that are mounted on shafts to provide exclusive rotary movement and can be adjusted to align or position the shaft precisely. These bearings are mechanical elements used to support rotating parts while allowing fine positional tuning.
[F16C 23/08] self-adjusting -- F16C 23/08 refers to self-adjusting bearings designed for exclusively rotary movement that can automatically align or reposition themselves to compensate for misalignment, ensuring smooth and reliable rotation in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 23/082] by means of at least one substantially spherical surface -- F16C 23/082 concerns rotary bearings designed for exclusively rotary movement that can be adjusted for aligning or positioning by incorporating at least one substantially spherical surface, which permits angular compensation while maintaining the bearing's support function.
[F16C 23/084] sliding on a complementary spherical surface -- The code F16C 23/084 refers to bearings designed for purely rotary motion that can be adjusted to align or position a shaft, where the bearing elements slide against a matching spherical surface to provide precise angular adjustment. This type of bearing is used in mechanical engineering applications requiring fine positioning while maintaining rotary freedom.
[F16C 23/086] forming a track for rolling elements -- The F16C 23/086 subclass covers techniques for forming the raceway or track that guides rolling elements in adjustable rotary bearings. It includes methods and structures that shape, machine or otherwise create the path for balls or rollers, enabling precise alignment or positioning of the bearing assembly.
[F16C 23/088] by means of crowning -- F16C 23/088 covers bearings designed for exclusively rotary motion that can be adjusted to align or position the rotating shaft, employing a crowning method to achieve the adjustment. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 23/10] Bearings, parts of which are eccentrically adjustable with respect to each other -- This subclass pertains to bearings whose components can be shifted eccentrically to adjust the alignment or positioning of a rotating shaft, allowing fine tuning of bearing clearance by moving parts relative to one another.
[F16C 2310/00] Agricultural machines -- F16C 2310/00 is a subclass of the Mechanical Engineering section that deals with shafts and related power-transmission components specifically for agricultural machines, covering the design and use of rotary elements in farm equipment such as harvesters, tractors and processing units.
[F16C 2314/00] Personal or domestic articles, e.g. household appliances such as washing machines, dryers -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F16C 2314/00 pertains to personal or domestic articles, specifically focusing on household appliances like washing machines and dryers, detailing the mechanical engineering aspects related to shafts in these devices.
[F16C 2314/70] Furniture -- F16C 2314/70 covers shafts incorporated in furniture items, such as rotating or adjustable mechanisms used in chairs, tables, cabinets and other household fixtures.
