The classification addresses the design, construction and function of these shafts when they form part of personal or domestic articles.
[F16C 2314/72] Drawers -- F16C 2314/72 pertains to drawer components that function as shaft-like elements in personal or domestic articles such as washing machines and dryers. The classification covers the design, construction or mounting of drawer mechanisms within these appliances, focusing on their mechanical integration with the overall shaft system.
[F16C 2314/73] Chairs -- The F16C 2314/73 subclass pertains to chairs classified as personal or domestic articles whose principal functional element is a shaft, such as rotating, tilting or height-adjusting components. It resides within mechanical engineering, under engineering elements and units, specifically shafts, and captures chair designs where shaft-based mechanisms are central to operation.
[F16C 2316/00] Apparatus in health or amusement -- F16C 2316/00 covers apparatus used in medical or recreational contexts that incorporate shafts, axles, spindles or similar rotating elements, such as therapy devices, fitness machines, or components of amusement rides.
[F16C 2316/10] in medical appliances, e.g. in diagnosis, dentistry, instruments, prostheses, medical imaging appliances -- F16C 2316/10 covers shafts that are integral to medical appliances, including those used for diagnosis, dentistry, prosthetic devices and medical imaging equipment. The classification applies to any rotational or linear shaft element designed to transmit power, motion or fluids within such health-related or amusement apparatus.
[F16C 2316/13] Dental machines -- F16C 2316/13 classifies dental machines, including hand-pieces, drills, cleaning and polishing equipment, and other apparatus used for dental treatment or research. These devices are mechanical systems with rotating shafts designed for health applications, falling under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 2316/18] Pumps for pumping blood -- F16C 2316/18 designates pumps intended to move blood, typically employed in medical circulatory support such as heart-lung machines, ventricular assist devices, or extracorporeal circulation systems. The entry is part of the CPC group for hydraulic or pneumatic pumps, situated under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and apparatus in health or amusement.
[F16C 2316/30] Articles for sports, games and amusement, e.g. roller skates, toys
[F16C 2320/00] Apparatus used in separating or mixing -- F16C 2320/00 covers machines that employ a rotating shaft to separate or mix substances, such as mixers, grinders, homogenisers, centrifugal separators and similar apparatus where the shaft drives the working elements that effect the separation or blending of material.
[F16C 2320/16] Mixing apparatus -- F16C 2320/16 covers mixing apparatus designed for use with shafts, typically part of mechanical engineering equipment that combines or homogenizes materials. The classification includes devices that agitate, blend, or stir substances, often employing rotary or reciprocating motion, and is applied to patents involving industrial mixers.
[F16C 2320/23] Milling apparatus -- F16C 2320/23 covers milling apparatus, i.e., machines that use rotating shafts to grind, crush, or pulverize solid materials for processing or separation. The classification includes devices where the shaft drives cutting tools or rollers to achieve particle size reduction, commonly used in mineral, food, or chemical industries.
[F16C 2320/42] Centrifuges -- F16C 2320/42 classifies centrifuges, machines that rotate a shaft to create centrifugal force for separating liquids, solids or gases, or for mixing substances. The classification applies to apparatus where the rotating shaft is the primary element in the separation or mixing operation.
[F16C 2322/00] Apparatus used in shaping articles -- The CPC subclass F16C 2322/00 covers devices and machinery used to shape workpieces, including lathes, shaping machines and similar equipment that mechanically modify the form of articles. It is situated in the mechanical engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, specifically shafts.
[F16C 2322/12] Rolling apparatus, e.g. rolling stands, rolls -- The CPC subclass F16C 2322/12 covers rolling apparatus such as rolling stands and individual rolls used to shape articles by passing material between rotating cylinders, a type of shaft-based equipment within mechanical engineering. It includes devices designed for continuous or batch rolling operations that deform workpieces through controlled compression.
[F16C 2322/14] Stamping, deep-drawing or punching, e.g. die sets -- F16C 2322/14 designates apparatus used for stamping, deep-drawing or punching operations, including die sets that shape metal or other sheet materials. The subclass is nested within mechanical engineering, under engineering elements and units for shafts and devices employed in shaping articles.
[F16C 2322/34] Sawing machines -- F16C 2322/34 falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Apparatus used in shaping articles and designates sawing machines, i.e., devices that employ saw blades-reciprocating, circular or other types-to cut, trim or shape workpieces in manufacturing and processing operations.
[F16C 2322/39] General buildup of machine tools, e.g. spindles, slides, actuators -- This class covers the basic building blocks of machine tools that provide motion and positioning, such as spindles, slides and actuators. It includes the design, arrangement and integration of these shaft-based elements in apparatus used for shaping articles.
[F16C 2322/50] Hand tools, workshop equipment or manipulators -- F16C 2322/50 designates hand tools, workshop equipment or manipulators employed in shaping articles, falling under shafts within engineering elements and units of mechanical engineering. [F16C 2322/59] Manipulators, e.g. robot arms -- A manipulator such as a robot arm designed to position, move or orient workpieces or tooling during shaping operations, typically mounted on a shaft or similar support, and used to automate material removal, forming or finishing processes.
[F16C 2324/00] Apparatus used in printing -- F16C 2324/00 classifies devices in which shafts are employed as part of printing equipment, such as rollers, cylinders or other rotating elements that convey ink, paper or substrate through a press.

It includes the rotating shafts, feeding mechanisms and other engineering elements that drive printing operations.
[F16C 2326/00] Articles relating to transporting -- The code refers to shafts employed in devices that move goods, such as conveyor belts, rollers, or other transport mechanisms. It includes shafts integral to the structure or drive of material-handling equipment.
[F16C 2326/01] Parts of vehicles in general -- This CPC subclass deals with any component that forms part of a vehicle, irrespective of the vehicle's specific function or propulsion system. It includes shafts, axles, frames and other structural or mechanical elements used in the construction or operation of land, sea or air vehicles.
[F16C 2326/02] Wheel hubs or castors -- This CPC subgroup covers wheel hubs and casters, the components that connect a wheel to an axle or support a load for transport. They are elements of shaft assemblies used in mechanical engineering to enable rolling motion or directional movement.
[F16C 2326/05] Vehicle suspensions, e.g. bearings, pivots or connecting rods used therein -- F16C 2326/05 classifies vehicle suspension parts, specifically bearings, pivots and connecting rods that link wheels to the chassis and provide isolation of road forces. The entry falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts, and relates to components used in transport machinery.
[F16C 2326/06] Drive shafts -- The CPC subclass F16C 2326/06 falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and articles relating to transporting. It covers drive shafts, the rotating power-transmission components that convey torque from a power source to driven wheels or machinery in vehicles and other transport devices. This includes design, construction and integration of such shafts for automotive, railway, marine and aerospace applications.
[F16C 2326/08] Vehicle seats, e.g. in linear movable seats -- F16C 2326/08 covers vehicle seats that are integrated with or move along a shaft, such as linear adjustable seats in automobiles or other transport vehicles. [F16C 2326/09] Windscreen wipers, e.g. pivots therefore -- F16C 2326/09 classifies windscreen-wiper mechanisms that use shafts, pivots or similar rotating elements to move a wiping blade on a vehicle. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and pertains to articles related to transporting.
[F16C 2326/10] Railway vehicles -- F16C 2326/10 pertains to railway vehicles, specifically the shafts and related components used in their propulsion and transmission systems. It includes designs, arrangements, and technical features of shafts that transmit power within locomotives and train cars, covering both mechanical and functional aspects of railway transport.
[F16C 2326/20] Land vehicles -- F16C 2326/20 designates land vehicles that employ shafts as part of their mechanical transmission system. It lies within the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts, and covers vehicles used for transporting goods or passengers on solid ground.
[F16C 2326/24] Steering systems, e.g. steering rods or columns -- The CPC subclass F16C 2326/24 pertains to steering systems, including components such as steering rods and columns that convey driver input to wheel assemblies in vehicles. It is classified under mechanical engineering, specifically within engineering elements and units for shafts used in transport-related articles.
[F16C 2326/26] Bicycle steering or suspension -- The CPC subclass F16C 2326/26 covers mechanisms that control the direction or absorb shocks on bicycles, including steering linkages, handlebars, forks and suspension components. It pertains to shaft-type elements used to transmit motion or forces for steering or for providing ride comfort in two-wheeled human-powered vehicles.

It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 2326/43] Aeroplanes

These bearings are designed to operate under the vacuum, temperature extremes and unique load conditions encountered in space.

[F16C 2340/00] Apparatus for treating textiles -- The CPC code F16C 2340/00 covers shaft-driven apparatus used in textile treatment, such as machines that incorporate rotating shafts to wind, tension, stretch, clean, dye or otherwise process fabrics and yarns. [F16C 2340/24] Godet rolls -- F16C 2340/24 covers godet rolls, cylindrical shafts or rollers incorporated into textile-treating machines to guide, tension, drive or heat fabrics as they pass through processes such as weaving, dyeing or drying. [F16C 2350/26] Excavators -- F16C 2350/26 classifies excavators, which are construction machines designed to dig, lift and move earth or rock using a boom, arm and bucket mounted on a rotating platform. [F16C 2350/52] Locks, e.g. cables to actuate door locks -- The CPC entry F16C 2350/52 falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts and concerns devices that use cables or similar shaft mechanisms to operate door locks. It covers lock systems where the actuating force is transmitted through a cable or comparable flexible shaft.
[F16C 2350/54] Hinges, e.g. sliding bearings for hinges -- This CPC subclass F16C 2350/54 covers hinges and related sliding-bearing mechanisms that join rotating or moving parts, typically in machines or structural components. It includes designs where a bearing slides within the hinge to reduce friction and enable smooth motion, and it falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 2352/00] Apparatus for drilling -- F16C 2352/00 covers apparatus that uses rotating shafts to drive drill bits for creating holes in various materials, including systems with feed mechanisms, suction or fluid delivery, and means for stabilising the drill during operation. It pertains to machines such as rock, mineral or soil drills where the shaft transmits power from a motor to the cutting tool, often incorporating control and safety features specific to drilling applications.
[F16C 2360/00] Engines or pumps -- F16C 2360/00 classifies shafts that are integral to engines or pumps, covering the mechanical elements that transmit power within these devices. It includes designs, constructions and arrangements of rotating shafts that directly drive pistons, rotors or impellers in internal-combustion engines, electric motors and various pump types. The entry is used to index patents relating to the coupling of shaft and power-generation or fluid-moving machinery.
[F16C 2360/18] Camshafts -- F16C 2360/18 covers camshafts, which are rotating shafts with raised lobes that convert rotary motion into timed reciprocating motion for opening and closing engine valves. They are employed mainly in internal combustion engines and, in some cases, in pump mechanisms.
[F16C 2360/22] Internal combustion engines -- F16C 2360/22 classifies internal combustion engines, covering the design, construction and operation of engines that convert fuel into mechanical power for use in vehicles, machinery and power-generation equipment. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically within the shaft and engine or pump categories.
[F16C 2360/23] Gas turbine engines -- F16C 2360/23 defines gas turbine engines, a type of shaft-driven engine that generates power by expanding hot combustion gases through a turbine. The classification covers mechanically integrated turbine, compressor and combustion sections used in aircraft propulsion, power generation and industrial applications, emphasizing the shaft and rotating components that transmit turbine output to a load.
[F16C 2360/24] Turbochargers -- F16C 2360/24 covers turbochargers, which are devices that use exhaust gas energy to drive a turbine that compresses intake air, increasing engine power and efficiency. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units relating to shafts for engines or pumps.
[F16C 2360/31] Wind motors -- F16C 2360/31 relates to wind motors, devices that transform wind energy into rotational motion via a shaft driven by aerodynamic forces. These machines are classified under engines or pumps within the shaft category of mechanical engineering.
[F16C 2360/42] Pumps with cylinders or pistons -- F16C 2360/42 refers to pumps that employ one or more cylinders or pistons to transfer fluid, encompassing reciprocating, rotary-piston and similar designs used in hydraulic or mechanical power transmission systems.
[F16C 2360/43] Screw compressors -- Screw compressors are rotary positive-displacement machines that compress gas by meshing two helical rotors within a housing. They belong to the shaft-driven engine or pump family and are widely used in industrial refrigeration, air-conditioning and process plants for efficient continuous compression.
[F16C 2360/44] Centrifugal pumps -- F16C 2360/44 designates centrifugal pumps in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, covering impeller-driven fluid movers that generate flow by rotation of a shaft-driven impeller. The entry falls under Mechanical Engineering, Engineering Elements and Units, Shafts, Engines or pumps, and is used to classify patents related to pump design, performance, and integration with driven shafts.
[F16C 2360/45] Turbo-molecular pumps -- Turbo-molecular pumps are high-vacuum devices that use rapidly rotating rotor blades to impart momentum to gas molecules, moving them from the inlet to the exhaust side and achieving pressures down to 10⁻⁹ Pa. They are classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, engines or pumps, with CPC code F16C 2360/45.
[F16C 2360/46] Fans, e.g. ventilators -- F16C 2360/46 covers fans and ventilators, devices that generate airflow by rotating blades mounted on a shaft, typically used for ventilation, cooling, or air circulation in industrial, commercial, or residential settings. The code falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and engines or pumps.
[F16C 2361/00] Apparatus or articles in engineering in general -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F16C 2361/00, used by the European and United States patent offices, categorizes apparatus or articles that are broadly applicable in mechanical engineering, particularly those related to shafts and their components. This classification helps in organizing and searching for patents involving specific mechanical elements and units.
[F16C 2361/31] Axle -- F16C 2361/31 classifies axles, the rotating shafts that support and transmit loads between wheels or other rotating members in mechanical systems. It covers designs, structures and mounting arrangements of axles used in vehicles, machinery and related engineering applications.
[F16C 2361/41] Couplings -- F16C 2361/41 covers couplings - devices that join two rotating shafts to transmit torque while accommodating misalignment, vibration damping, or speed changes. The class includes rigid, flexible, fluid and universal couplings used in machinery and power-transmission systems.
[F16C 2361/43] Clutches, e.g. disengaging bearing -- This subclass covers clutches that incorporate a disengaging bearing, designed to transmit torque between rotating shafts and allow controlled separation when disengaged. It includes devices where a bearing element provides the disengagement function, typically used in automotive or industrial power transmission.
[F16C 2361/45] Brakes -- The F16C 2361/45 subclass covers brake devices that are incorporated into or act upon rotating shafts, including friction, hydraulic, pneumatic or electromagnetic mechanisms that slow or stop shaft motion. It applies to engineering components where the brake is integral to the shaft assembly.
[F16C 2361/53] Spring-damper, e.g. gas springs -- A spring-damper (such as a gas spring) is an integrated device that provides both elastic restoring force and controlled damping, typically using compressed gas or hydraulic fluid to deliver smooth, adjustable resistance. It is employed in mechanical systems to support or counteract motion, often found in automotive suspensions, seat recliners, and industrial equipment where precise force control is required.
[F16C 2361/55] Flywheel systems -- F16C 2361/55 relates to flywheel systems, rotating assemblies that store kinetic energy and provide inertia for smoothing power variations in machines. These devices are used as energy-storage or torque-stabilizing components in mechanical and engineering applications.
[F16C 2361/61] Toothed gear systems, e.g. support of pinion shafts -- This CPC subclass covers devices and arrangements for toothed gear systems that include means for supporting pinion shafts, such as bearings, housings or other structural elements that hold the gear shafts in place, focusing on the integration of shaft support within the gear assembly.
[F16C 2361/63] Gears with belts and pulleys -- The F16C 2361/63 subclass concerns gear mechanisms that incorporate belts and pulleys to transmit motion or power between shafts, combining toothed gear elements with belt-driven pulley arrangements for speed change, torque conversion or flexible power transmission.
[F16C 2361/65] Gear shifting, change speed gear, gear box -- F16C 2361/65 covers mechanisms for changing gear ratios in gearboxes, including gear-shifting devices and speed-change arrangements. It pertains to shafts and related components used in mechanical engineering to transmit power and alter rotational speed. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts.
[F16C 2361/71] Chains -- F16C 2361/71 covers chains employed in mechanical engineering, especially for transmitting power or motion between shafts and other components. It includes design, construction, and application of various chain types such as roller, leaf, and toothed chains used in drive systems.
[F16C 2361/91] Valves -- F16C 2361/91 denotes valves that are incorporated in or directly actuate rotary shafts, such as those used in pumps, gearboxes, or rotating machinery. It covers valve designs where the valve body, seat or operating mechanism is integral to the shaft or is mounted on the shaft and moves with it, as well as related sealing and control features specific to rotating applications.
[F16C 2362/00] Apparatus for lighting or heating -- The CPC class F16C 2362/00 covers devices that combine a shaft with systems for emitting light or generating heat. It includes mechanisms where the rotating or stationary shaft integrates lamps, heaters or related components to illuminate or warm a surrounding area, often used in industrial or construction equipment.
[F16C 2362/40] Ovens or other heatings -- F16C 2362/40 covers ovens or other heating devices that incorporate shafts as functional elements, such as rotating racks, stirrers or conveyors, used in mechanical engineering applications for baking, thermal processing or heat treatment.
[F16C 2362/52] Compressors of refrigerators, e.g. air-conditioners -- F16C 2362/52 covers compressors employed in refrigeration and air-conditioning systems, specifically the types used to raise the pressure of refrigerant gas in household or commercial appliances. It falls under mechanical engineering shafts and relates to devices that provide cooling or heating by cyclic compression of refrigerants.
[F16C 2370/00] Apparatus relating to physics, e.g. instruments -- F16C 2370/00 falls under the CPC section for mechanical engineering, specifically the shaft group. It designates shafts incorporated in apparatuses used for physical experiments or measurements, such as scientific instruments and testing equipment. The class covers any shaft-based component that forms part of such physics-related devices.
[F16C 2370/12] Hard disk drives or the like -- F16C 2370/12 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system refers to hard disk drives or similar devices, focusing on their mechanical aspects and components such as shafts and bearings. This classification is used for inventions involving the physical structure and operation of data storage devices.
[F16C 2370/20] Optical, e.g. movable lenses or mirrors -- The F16C 2370/20 subclass pertains to shafts that incorporate optical elements such as movable lenses or mirrors, enabling the adjustment, focusing or redirection of light within mechanical devices. It covers apparatus where these optical components are integrated with rotating or translating shafts for measurement, alignment or other physical-instrument functions.
[F16C 2370/22] Polygon mirror -- F16C 2370/22 covers polygon mirrors, which are multi-faceted rotating mirrors mounted on a shaft and used to deflect light beams in optical scanning, laser printing and projection systems. The classification applies to the mechanical arrangement of the mirror and its drive mechanism within devices that manipulate light.
[F16C 2370/38] Electrographic apparatus -- The F16C 2370/38 subclass groups electrographic apparatus that incorporate rotating or sliding shafts to generate or record images by electrostatic or electrophotographic processes, including printers, copiers and plotters where the shaft forms part of the developing, transfer or feeding mechanisms. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, apparatus relating to physics.
[F16C 2380/00] Electrical apparatus -- F16C 2380/00 refers to electrical apparatus that is incorporated into or mounted on rotating shafts, such as generators, motors, sensors or power-transmission devices integrated with the shaft assembly in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 2380/16] X-ray tubes -- F16C 2380/16 designates X-ray tubes, devices that produce X-rays by accelerating electrons in a vacuum and striking a target, often incorporating rotating shafts for cooling or target movement. [F16C 2380/18] Handling tools for semiconductor devices -- F16C 2380/18 covers tools designed to grip, move, position or transport semiconductor devices during manufacturing or testing, typically using specialized shafts or fixtures that handle delicate components without damage. The classification falls under mechanical engineering shafts within the broader category of electrical apparatus, addressing handling equipment specific to microelectronic parts.
[F16C 2380/26] Dynamo-electric machines or combinations therewith, e.g. electro-motors and generators -- This subclass deals with shafts that are part of or directly linked to dynamo-electric machines such as electric motors and generators, including combined motor-generator units and their mechanical coupling arrangements. It covers structural and functional aspects of the rotating shaft within these electrical apparatuses.
[F16C 2380/27] Motor coupled with a gear, e.g. worm gears -- F16C 2380/27 covers motor-driven units where the motor is directly coupled to a gear mechanism, including configurations such as worm gears, used to transmit power through a shaft system. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and relates to electrical apparatus.
[F16C 2380/28] Motor, generator coupled with a flywheel -- This class covers devices in which a motor or generator is mechanically linked to a flywheel, the flywheel storing kinetic energy to assist the machine during power fluctuations or to improve efficiency. Such arrangements are used for load-leveling, start-up assistance, or energy recovery in mechanical systems.
[F16C 25/00] Bearings for exclusively rotary movement adjustable for wear or play -- A subclass of mechanical engineering that covers bearings intended solely for rotary motion and equipped with mechanisms to adjust clearance for wear or play. These adjustable rotary bearings are used on shafts to maintain precise alignment and functional performance as components degrade over time.
[F16C 25/02] Sliding-contact bearings -- F16C 25/02 relates to sliding-contact bearings used for purely rotary movement that can be adjusted to compensate for wear or play. These bearings provide a low-friction interface between a rotating shaft and its housing, allowing the clearance to be fine-tuned for smooth operation as components age.
[F16C 25/04] self-adjusting -- The F16C 25/04 subclass covers bearings designed for exclusively rotary motion that incorporate a self-adjusting mechanism to compensate for wear or play. These devices automatically modify clearance or position, maintaining proper alignment and load distribution without manual intervention.
[F16C 25/045] with magnetic means to preload the bearing -- F16C 25/045 covers bearings that allow only rotary motion and can be adjusted for wear or play, where a magnetic system is used to apply a preload to the bearing surfaces. The magnetic preload improves stiffness and reduces clearance, enhancing performance in precision machinery.
[F16C 25/06] Ball or roller bearings -- F16C 25/06 relates to ball or roller bearings that provide low-friction support for shafts undergoing exclusively rotary movement. The classification covers bearings that can be adjusted to compensate for wear or play, ensuring stable operation and precise alignment in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 25/08] self-adjusting -- F16C 25/08 covers self-adjusting bearings intended for exclusively rotary movement that automatically compensate for wear or play by altering their clearance or dimensions. These bearings keep proper alignment and load distribution throughout service without manual adjustment.
[F16C 25/083] with resilient means acting axially on a race ring to preload the bearing -- This subclass covers shaft bearings designed for purely rotary motion that incorporate a resilient element applying axial force to a race ring to maintain preload. The arrangement adjusts for wear or play, ensuring consistent bearing performance over its service life. It falls within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 25/086] with magnetic means to preload the bearing -- The CPC subclass F16C 25/086 covers bearings that allow exclusive rotary movement and can be adjusted for wear or play, where the adjustment is achieved by magnetic means that preload the bearing. This falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts, and pertains to devices that use magnetic force to maintain optimal bearing clearance during operation.
[F16C 27/00] Elastic or yielding bearings or bearing supports, for exclusively rotary movement -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Elastic hydrostatic bearings -> F16C32/067 - Shock-damping bearings for watches or clocks -> G04B31/02
[F16C 27/02] Sliding-contact bearings -- F16C 27/02 covers sliding-contact bearings, which support rotating shafts by allowing surfaces to slide against each other rather than roll, typically using lubricated metals, polymers or composites to reduce friction and wear.
[F16C 27/04] Ball or roller bearings, e.g. with resilient rolling bodies -- F16C 27/04 classifies ball or roller bearings that incorporate elastic or yielding rolling bodies, such as those with resilient elements, for purely rotary motion. These bearings are a type of shaft support within the broader mechanical-engineering category of engineering elements and units.
[F16C 27/045] with a fluid film, e.g. squeeze film damping -- This subclass covers elastic or yielding rotary bearings that incorporate a thin fluid film to provide squeeze-film damping, enhancing load support and vibration attenuation in rotating shafts. It focuses on designs where the fluid film works together with the bearing element to control motion.
[F16C 27/06] by means of parts of rubber or like materials -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Bearings with sliding surfaces of rubber or synthetic rubber -> F16C33/22
[F16C 27/063] Sliding contact bearings -- The CPC group F16C 27/063 covers sliding-contact bearings that are elastic or yielding and are employed exclusively for rotary movement. These bearings provide a sliding interface between rotating shafts and their supports, allowing flexibility and efficient load distribution.
[F16C 27/066] Ball or roller bearings -- F16C 27/066 relates to ball or roller bearings, which are elastic or yielding supports that enable exclusively rotary movement of shafts. The bearings use spherical or cylindrical rolling elements to separate moving parts, reducing friction and supporting loads in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 27/08] primarily for axial load, e.g. for vertically-arranged shafts -- F16C 27/08 covers elastic or yielding bearings and bearing supports intended for purely rotary motion but primarily designed to carry axial loads, such as those used on vertically arranged shafts, allowing limited movement while supporting the shaft's weight or thrust.
[F16C 29/00] Other bearings -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** This place does not cover: Complete systems such as railways and conveyor systems are similar to linear bearings ( F16C29/00 ) but do not qualify as "engineering elements or units" in the sense of Class F16 . They are dealt with in the appropriate parts of "Transporting" in Section B , particularly in Subclass B61 ("Railways") and B65 ("Conveying; ... ").
[F16C 29/001] adjustable for alignment or positioning -- F16C 29/001 designates bearings that can be adjusted to fine-tune the alignment or position of a rotating shaft. The class covers mechanisms allowing lateral or angular displacement so the shaft geometry can be corrected during assembly or operation.
[F16C 29/002] Elastic or yielding linear bearings or bearing supports -- F16C 29/002 covers elastic or yielding linear bearings and bearing supports that provide compliance in one direction while restricting motion in others, typically using polymer pads, elastomeric inserts or spring elements to accommodate misalignment and absorb shock. These components belong to the shaft family of mechanical engineering and are classified under other bearings.
[F16C 29/004] Fixing of a carriage or rail, e.g. rigid mounting to a support structure or a movable part -- F16C 29/004 pertains to the secure attachment of a carriage or rail, such as rigid mounting to a support structure or to a moving part, used in machine tools and similar equipment to provide precise guidance and stability.
[F16C 29/005] Guide rails or tracks for a linear bearing, i.e. adapted for movement of a carriage or bearing body there along -- F16C 29/005 designates guide rails or tracks that form the bearing surface for a linear bearing, enabling a carriage or bearing body to move smoothly along a defined path. It falls under mechanical engineering shafts and other bearings, covering rail-based linear motion systems.
[F16C 29/007] Hybrid linear bearings, i.e. including more than one bearing type, e.g. sliding contact bearings as well as rolling contact bearings -- A hybrid linear bearing combines two or more bearing principles, typically integrating a sliding contact surface with rolling elements within the same linear guide. This arrangement provides low-friction operation while supporting loads and improving stiffness and durability compared to single-type bearings.
[F16C 29/008] Systems with a plurality of bearings, e.g. four carriages supporting a slide on two parallel rails -- F16C 29/008 refers to mechanical systems that employ several bearings together to support a moving part, such as a slide guided by two parallel rails and held by four carriage bearings. The arrangement distributes loads and improves stability and precision of linear motion, and is used where a single bearing would be insufficient.
[F16C 29/02] Sliding-contact bearings -- F16C 29/02 covers sliding-contact bearings, which are bearing types where the moving parts maintain contact across a sliding surface rather than rolling. These bearings are used to support rotating shafts and typically include plain or journal bearings made of low-friction materials. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, other bearings.
[F16C 29/025] Hydrostatic or aerostatic  this type of bearing for rotary parts  F16C32/06 -- F16C 29/025 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the European and United States patent offices, specifies hydrostatic or aerostatic bearings designed for rotary parts. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with mechanical engineering elements and units, specifically shafts and other types of bearings.
[F16C 29/04] Ball or roller bearings -- F16C 29/04 deals with ball or roller bearings, a subclass of other bearings for shafts in mechanical engineering. It covers designs, arrangements and supporting structures that use spherical or cylindrical rolling elements to reduce friction and carry radial and axial loads, and is applied to patents describing these bearing types and their specific configurations.
[F16C 29/041] having rollers crossed within a row -- F16C 29/041 refers to bearings in which the rollers of a single row are crossed, meaning that the axes of adjacent rollers intersect to form a criss-cross pattern. This arrangement improves load distribution, rigidity and wear resistance, making it suitable for high-load mechanical applications.
[F16C 29/043] with two massive rectangular rails having facing grooves -- F16C 29/043 covers shaft designs that are supported by a pair of large rectangular rails whose inner surfaces are grooved and face one another. The grooves guide and locate the shaft, providing precise linear guidance and bearing capacity for heavy loads in mechanical engineering applications. This configuration is used where strong, well-aligned support is required.
[F16C 29/045] having rolling elements journaled in one of the moving parts -- F16C 29/045 classifies bearings in which the rolling elements-balls, rollers or needles-are mounted on a journal that is integral to one of the moving parts, so the journal itself rotates relative to the counterpart component. This arrangement provides low-friction support for shafts in gearboxes, turbines and similar mechanical systems.
[F16C 29/046] with balls journaled in pockets -- The F16C 29/046 subclass covers shafts that incorporate ball bearings where each ball is seated in a separate pocket or raceway, allowing the balls to roll while the shaft rotates. This design is used in mechanical engineering applications that require precise, low-friction rotation, such as spindle or turbine shafts, and falls under the broader categories of engineering elements, shafts, and other bearings.
[F16C 29/048] with thin walled races, e.g. tracks of sheet metal -- This sub-group covers bearings whose raceways are formed as thin-walled tracks, such as sheet-metal or stamped elements, rather than solid rings. It includes designs where the race is a lightweight, sheet-like component that carries rolling elements.
[F16C 29/06] in which the rolling bodies circulate partly without carrying load -- F16C 29/06 covers shafts that use bearings in which the rolling elements travel partly without carrying load, such as zones where the balls or rollers circulate freely before engaging the load-bearing region. The classification applies to designs that separate load-supporting sections from clearance or transitional sections within the same bearing assembly.
[F16C 29/0602] Details of the bearing body or carriage or parts thereof, e.g. methods for manufacturing or assembly -- F16C 29/0602 covers the detailed design and construction of bearing bodies or carriers and their constituent parts, focusing on manufacturing methods, assembly processes and related technical features. This subclass is used to classify inventions that improve how bearing structures are produced or put together.
[F16C 29/0604] of the load bearing section -- F16C 29/0604 pertains to the load-bearing section of other bearings, focusing on the part of a shaft assembly that supports axial or radial forces. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts, and addresses designs that transmit and withstand loads within bearing structures.
[F16C 29/0607] of parts or members for retaining the rolling elements, i.e. members to prevent the rolling elements from falling out of the bearing body or carriage -- The subgroup F16C 29/0607 covers components that retain the rolling elements inside a bearing, preventing balls or rollers from falling out of the bearing body or carriage. These parts include cages, retainers and other structures that secure the rolling elements within shaft-bearing assemblies.
[F16C 29/0609] of the ends of the bearing body or carriage where the rolling elements change direction, e.g. end caps -- F16C 29/0609 covers the design of the terminal portions of a bearing housing or carriage that guide rolling elements as they reverse direction, such as end caps or similar structures that form the ends of the bearing body. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, other bearings.
[F16C 29/0611] of the return passages, i.e. the passages where the rolling elements do not carry load -- This CPC subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically shafts and other bearings. It covers features of return passages in bearing assemblies, meaning the channels through which rolling elements travel when they are not bearing load. It addresses design aspects of these unloaded sections of rolling-element paths.
[F16C 29/0614] with a shoe type bearing body, e.g. a body facing one side of the guide rail or track only -- The code F16C 29/0614 covers a shoe-type bearing body in which the bearing element contacts only one side of a guide rail or track, providing support for rotating shafts while allowing easy alignment and limited surface engagement. This arrangement is used in mechanical systems where a simple, unidirectional bearing surface is preferred.
[F16C 29/0616] for supporting load essentially in a single direction -- F16C 29/0616 covers bearings designed to carry a load that acts essentially in a single direction, typically axial or thrust loads. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, shafts, and other bearings, and includes devices such as thrust or tilting pads that transmit load predominantly along one axis.
[F16C 29/0619] with rollers or needles -- F16C 29/0619 classifies bearings for shafts that employ rollers or needle rollers as the rolling elements, distinguishing them from other bearing types within the "other bearings" subgroup. This sub-class focuses on configurations where the rolling components are cylindrical or needle-shaped to reduce friction and support axial or radial loads.
[F16C 29/0621] for supporting load in essentially two directions, e.g. by multiple points of contact or two rows of rolling elements -- This subclass defines bearings that support a load essentially in two directions, achieved by multiple contact points or by arranging two rows of rolling elements, such as certain thrust or angular-contact bearing designs that distribute forces in two orthogonal planes.
[F16C 29/0623] with balls -- F16C 29/0623 designates bearings that employ spherical rolling elements - balls - to support a rotating shaft. It covers constructions where the bearing assembly incorporates balls as the primary rolling components, distinct from roller-type or plain bearings.
[F16C 29/0626] with rollers -- F16C 29/0626 designates shaft bearings that employ rollers to locate and support a rotating shaft. The subclass includes cylindrical, tapered, spherical or other roller types used where the bearing element rolls rather than slides, providing reduced friction and higher load capacity compared with plain bearings.
[F16C 29/0628] crossed within a row -- It covers bearing assemblies in which several shafts intersect or cross within a single row of bearings, providing support for intersecting rotating members. This configuration is used when space limits require shafts to be arranged in a crossed layout while being held by a common bearing row.
[F16C 29/063] with a bearing body, e.g. a carriage or part thereof, provided between the legs of a U-shaped guide rail or track -- F16C 29/063 pertains to mechanical engineering elements, specifically shafts and other bearings, detailing designs where a bearing body, such as a carriage or its components, is positioned between the legs of a U-shaped guide rail or track.
[F16C 29/0633] with a bearing body defining a U-shaped carriage, i.e. surrounding a guide rail or track on three sides -- F16C 29/0633 covers shafts that incorporate a bearing body shaped as a U-shaped carriage, which surrounds a guide rail or track on three sides to provide support and guidance while the shaft rotates. This sub-category falls under other bearings within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F16C 29/0635] whereby the return paths are provided as bores in a main body of the U-shaped carriage, e.g. the main body of the U-shaped carriage is a single part with end caps provided at each end -- This subclass describes bearing arrangements where a U-shaped carriage is formed as a single solid part and the return passages for fluid or lubrication are machined as bores within the main body, with end caps sealing each end. The design integrates the return paths directly into the shaft bearing unit.
[F16C 29/0638] with balls -- F16C 29/0638 classifies shafts equipped with bearings that employ spherical balls as rolling elements. These ball bearings support rotating or oscillating shafts, allowing smooth motion while carrying radial and axial loads. The code is used for designs such as deep-groove, angular-contact, and self-aligning ball bearings.
[F16C 29/064] with two rows of balls, one on each side of the rail -- F16C 29/064 classifies bearings that employ two parallel rows of balls, each row positioned on opposite sides of the rail or raceway. This arrangement provides increased load-bearing capacity and stiffness compared with single-row designs, and is commonly used in linear guide systems and similar mechanical applications.
[F16C 29/0642] with four rows of balls -- The CPC subgroup F16C 29/0642 pertains to shaft bearings that employ four concentric rows of ball rolling elements. It falls under the "other bearings" category within mechanical engineering shaft components.
[F16C 29/0645] with load directions in O-arrangement -- F16C 29/0645 covers shafts that use other (non-rolling-element) bearings in which the applied loads are directed in an O-arrangement, meaning the load paths are oriented orthogonally around the shaft axis. This subclass falls under the broader category of shafts with other bearings in mechanical engineering.
[F16C 29/0647] with load directions in X-arrangement -- F16C 29/0647 classifies bearings that are arranged to transmit loads in an X-shaped configuration, specifying how forces acting along intersecting axes are accommodated within the bearing design. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically within engineering elements and units for shafts and other bearings, and addresses the geometry and load-direction characteristics of such X-arranged bearing assemblies.
[F16C 29/065] with rollers -- F16C 29/065 covers shafts that are supported by bearings incorporating rollers, a type of non-rotating bearing where rolling elements are used to reduce friction between moving parts. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the engineering elements and units for shafts, and is classified as an "other bearing" with rollers.
[F16C 29/0652] whereby the return paths are at least partly defined by separate parts, e.g. covers attached to the legs of the main body of the U-shaped carriage -- F16C 29/0652 covers bearings where the return path for the load is formed at least partly by separate elements, such as covers or side plates attached to the legs of a U-shaped carriage. This arrangement isolates the load path from the main shaft, enhancing rigidity and protecting moving parts.
[F16C 29/0654] with balls -- F16C 29/0654 designates a subcategory of shafts that incorporate other bearings employing balls. It is part of the mechanical engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, shafts, other bearings, and covers inventions related to ball-type bearing configurations for rotating shafts.
[F16C 29/0657] with two rows of balls, one on each side of the rail -- F16C 29/0657 covers linear ball bearings that use two parallel rows of balls, each row positioned on opposite sides of a guiding rail, allowing smooth low-friction motion along the shaft.
[F16C 29/0659] with four rows of balls -- F16C 29/0659 classifies bearings that incorporate four parallel rows of balls, a multi-row ball bearing design used to increase load capacity and stiffness. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and other bearings, indicating a specific type of rotary bearing with four ball races.
[F16C 29/0661] with load directions in O-arrangement -- F16C 29/0661 covers bearings that handle loads applied in an O-arrangement, meaning the load vectors are distributed around the bearing in a circular or oval pattern. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically shafts and other bearings, and relates to designs where load direction follows this O-shaped configuration.
[F16C 29/0664] with load directions in X-arrangement -- F16C 29/0664 designates bearings that accommodate loads whose directions are arranged in an X-pattern, meaning the forces act along intersecting axes rather than a single line. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Other bearings, covering devices that manage cross-directional loading on rotating shafts.
[F16C 29/0666] with rollers -- F16C 29/0666 covers bearings that use rollers to support rotating shafts, allowing reduced friction and load distribution. These roller-type bearings may include cylindrical, tapered, spherical or needle rollers and are used in various mechanical applications.
[F16C 29/0669] whereby the main body of the U-shaped carriage is an assembly of at least three major parts, e.g. an assembly of a top plate with two separate legs attached thereto in the form of bearing shoes  bearing shoes  per se   F16C29/0614 -- F16C 29/0669 pertains to U-shaped carriage designs where the main body is composed of at least three major parts, such as a top plate with two separate legs functioning as bearing shoes. This classification specifically addresses the construction and assembly aspects of these components within mechanical engineering.
[F16C 29/0671] with balls -- F16C 29/0671 pertains to shafts and other bearings, specifically detailing components that utilize balls in their design for reducing friction and supporting loads. This classification is part of the broader Mechanical Engineering section, focusing on engineering elements and units.
[F16C 29/0673] with rollers -- F16C 29/0673 covers shaft bearings that incorporate rollers, a type of rolling-element bearing where the load is transmitted through cylindrical or conical rollers rather than balls. This subgroup falls under other bearings within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy.
[F16C 29/0676] with a bearing body or carriage almost fully embracing the guide rail or track, e.g. a circular sleeve with a longitudinal slot for the support posts of the rail -- F16C 29/0676 classifies shafts whose bearing body or carriage nearly completely surrounds a guide rail or track, for example a circular sleeve that includes a longitudinal slot to receive the rail's support posts. This arrangement provides guided movement while protecting the rail and allowing easy installation.
[F16C 29/0678] with a bearing body, i.e. the body carrying the circulating rolling elements, provided in the interior of a sleeve-like guide member defining the opposing raceways, e.g. in a telescopic shaft  telescopic shafts with built-in bearings  F16C3/035 ; yielding coupling allowing axial displacement by rolling elements  F16D3/065 -- This classification concerns shafts in which the bearing body that carries the rolling elements is located inside a sleeve-like guide member that forms the opposing raceways, such as telescopic shafts with built-in bearings. It also includes yielding couplings that permit axial displacement by rolling elements.
[F16C 29/068] with the bearing body fully encircling the guide rail or track -- F16C 29/068 classifies bearings in which the bearing body completely surrounds a guide rail or track, providing full encirclement of the supporting element for shaft movement. This subcategory falls under Other bearings within the Shafts group of Mechanical Engineering, describing designs that integrate the bearing housing around the rail to ensure stable guidance and load distribution.
[F16C 29/0683] the bearing body encircles a rail or rod of circular cross-section, i.e. the linear bearing is not suited to transmit torque -- The code F16C 29/0683 classifies bearings whose housing completely surrounds a circular rail or rod, forming a linear bearing that is not intended to transmit torque. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and other bearings.
[F16C 29/0685] with balls -- F16C 29/0685 designates shafts that incorporate ball bearings, using spherical rolling elements to support rotation and minimise friction between moving parts. It is a sub-category of "other bearings" within the mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F16C 29/0688] whereby a sleeve surrounds the circulating balls and thicker part of the sleeve form the load bearing tracks -- The subclass F16C 29/0688 covers bearings in which a sleeve encloses the circulating balls, with a thicker portion of the sleeve acting as the load-bearing tracks that guide and support the balls during rotation.
[F16C 29/069] whereby discrete load bearing elements, e.g. discrete load bearing plates or discrete rods, are provided in a retainer and form the load bearing tracks -- This subclass concerns shafts or other bearings that incorporate separate load-bearing components, such as plates or rods, positioned within a retainer to create distinct load-bearing tracks. The design provides discrete elements to carry loads along the shaft, improving support and wear distribution.
[F16C 29/0692] the bearing body encircles a guide rail or track of non-circular cross-section, e.g. with grooves or protrusions, i.e. the linear bearing is suited to transmit torque  telescopic shafts with built-in bearings  F16C3/035 ; yielding coupling allowing axial displacement by rolling elements  F16D3/065 -- F16C 29/0692 covers bearings whose body surrounds a non-circular guide rail or track, such as grooved or protruding profiles, allowing the linear bearing to transmit torque. It includes telescopic shafts that incorporate built-in bearings and coupling arrangements that permit axial movement by rolling elements.
[F16C 29/0695] with balls -- F16C 29/0695 covers shafts that incorporate ball-type bearings, where the bearing elements are balls that support rotation of the shaft relative to a housing or other component. This subclass falls under other bearings in the shaft group of mechanical engineering elements.
[F16C 29/0697] with polygonal guide rail or track -- F16C 29/0697 covers bearings that incorporate a polygonal guide rail or track to locate and support a rotating shaft, providing precise positional control while allowing motion. The classification applies to designs where the bearing housing or outer ring includes a shaped groove or rail with straight or angular sides that the shaft or inner element follows, typically used in mechanical systems requiring accurate alignment and reduced play.
[F16C 29/08] Arrangements for covering or protecting the ways -- F16C 29/08 covers devices and methods that shield or enclose the ways along which shafts or linear guides move, such as protective covers, housings, shields or sealed chambers designed to keep contaminants out, reduce wear and improve alignment. These arrangements are used in machinery where precise linear motion must be maintained despite exposure to dust, debris or fluid.
[F16C 29/082] fixed to the way -- F16C 29/082 covers bearings in which the bearing component is rigidly attached to the guiding surface, so that the shaft or rolling element is fixed to the way rather than freely rotating. It applies to designs where the bearing is integral with the way to provide precise linear motion.
[F16C 29/084] fixed to the carriage or bearing body movable along the guide rail or track -- A component that is permanently attached to a carriage or bearing body but can travel along a guide rail or track, typically providing support or motion control in mechanical systems. This subgroup covers such fixed-to-moving elements used in shafts, other bearings, and related engineering applications.
[F16C 29/086] Seals being essentially U-shaped, e.g. for a U-shaped carriage -- This CPC subclass concerns seals that have an overall U-shaped form, such as those used to encircle a U-shaped carriage on a shaft or bearing assembly. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, other bearings.
[F16C 29/088] Seals extending in the longitudinal direction of the carriage or bearing body -- The CPC class F16C 29/088 covers seals that extend in the longitudinal direction of a carriage or bearing body, providing a barrier against fluid ingress or egress along the shaft or bearing assembly.
[F16C 29/10] Arrangements for locking the bearings -- F16C 29/10 covers designs that secure bearings in place on a rotating shaft, preventing axial or radial movement during operation. It includes locking rings, wedge mechanisms, clamping devices and other arrangements that engage the bearing race or housing to hold it firmly, especially for maintenance, safety or performance reasons.
[F16C 29/12] Arrangements for adjusting play -- F16C 29/12 concerns arrangements for adjusting the clearance (play) between rotating components in other types of bearings. It includes mechanisms such as shims, wedges, adjustable collars and preload devices that allow fine tuning of bearing clearance during assembly or operation.
[F16C 29/123] using elastic means -- F16C 29/123 covers bearings that incorporate elastic elements such as springs, elastomeric pads or flexible membranes to accommodate shaft movement, absorb shocks, or maintain alignment. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, other bearings, and deals with designs that use elasticity to improve bearing performance.
[F16C 29/126] using tapered surfaces or wedges -- F16C 29/126 addresses devices that use tapered surfaces or wedges to transmit motion, load or torque in shafts and related bearings. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts, and is used by the EPO and USPTO to classify patents involving tapered or wedge-shaped bearing components.
[F16C 3/00] Shafts -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Flexible shafts -> F16C1/02 - Camshafts -> F16H53/02
[F16C 3/02] Shafts -- F16C 3/02 covers shafts, the rotating cylindrical members that transmit torque or rotation between machines or mechanical units. It includes solid, hollow or splined shafts and their basic geometric features, but excludes details of bearings, couplings or gearings that are treated in separate subclasses. This subclass is used for patents focusing on shaft design, material selection, manufacturing or stress-relief features.
[F16C 3/023] made of several parts, e.g. by welding -- The F16C 3/023 entry covers shafts that are built from multiple components rather than a single solid piece, typically assembled by welding or similar joining techniques. It applies to mechanical-engineering applications where a shaft is fabricated from several parts to achieve desired strength, dimensions or material combinations.
[F16C 3/026] Shafts made of fibre reinforced resin -- F16C 3/026 pertains to shafts that are constructed from fiber-reinforced resin composites. The classification includes designs and manufacturing methods for rotating or stationary shafts where the load-bearing element is a polymer matrix reinforced with glass, carbon or other fibres, offering high strength-to-weight and corrosion resistance for mechanical applications.
[F16C 3/03] telescopic -- F16C 3/03 covers telescopic shafts, which consist of two or more hollow cylindrical members that can slide within each other to vary length while transmitting torque. These adjustable shafts are used in machinery where variable reach or compact storage is required, such as in drilling equipment, telescopic booms, and lift mechanisms.
[F16C 3/035] with built-in bearings -- F16C 3/035 covers shafts that incorporate bearings directly within the shaft body, forming an integrated rotating element. The built-in bearings support axial and radial loads without separate bearing housings, simplifying construction and improving alignment in mechanical systems. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts.
[F16C 3/04] Crankshafts, eccentric-shafts -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Crank gearings -> F16H21/18 - Crank gearings with adjustment of throw -> F16H21/20
[F16C 3/06] Crankshafts -- The F16C 3/06 subclass defines crankshafts, the rotating shafts with offset throws that transform reciprocating piston motion into rotary motion in internal-combustion or comparable engines. It covers their geometric design, balancing, material selection, manufacturing methods and mounting features within the broader shaft category of mechanical engineering.
[F16C 3/08] made in one piece -- F16C 3/08 covers shafts that are formed as a single, integral piece rather than assembled from multiple components. This includes solid, forged, extruded or otherwise monolithic shafts used in mechanical power transmission.
[F16C 3/10] assembled of several parts, e.g. by welding -- F16C 3/10 pertains to shafts that are fabricated by joining multiple components, for example through welding, rather than being produced as a single solid piece. It includes designs where the assembled parts function as a continuous rotating element in mechanical systems. This classification covers the methods and structures of such multi-part shaft constructions.
[F16C 31/00] Bearings for parts which both rotate and move linearly -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Screw mechanisms, e.g. with balls or rollers -> F16H25/20
[F16C 31/02] Sliding-contact bearings -- Sliding-contact bearings are bearing elements that provide support through direct surface contact between moving parts, allowing both rotational and linear motion along a shaft. They typically consist of a stationary race and a rotating or sliding element, and they rely on lubrication to reduce friction and wear.
[F16C 31/04] Ball or roller bearings -- F16C 31/04 relates to ball or roller bearings that support components which rotate while also moving linearly along a shaft. The entry covers designs, arrangements and variants of such bearings, including angular-contact and tapered or cylindrical rollers, intended for applications where combined rotary-reciprocating motion must be accommodated.
[F16C 31/06] in which the rolling bodies circulate partly without carrying load -- F16C 31/06 concerns bearings in which some of the rolling elements circulate without carrying load, providing guidance or reduced friction while the load-bearing elements remain engaged.
[F16C 3/12] releasably connected -- F16C 3/12 pertains to shafts that are releasably connected, covering devices and arrangements that enable temporary or detachable coupling of rotating shafts. It includes splines, clutches, couplings, and other mechanisms that allow shafts to be joined and separated without permanent fastening.
[F16C 3/14] Features relating to lubrication -- F16C 3/14 addresses lubrication features incorporated into shafts, including channels, grooves, bearings or other devices that supply oil or grease to moving parts, and any mechanisms that facilitate continuous or intermittent lubrication of the shaft.
[F16C 3/16] Features relating to cooling -- F16C 3/16 covers any cooling arrangements incorporated into shafts, such as internal passages, external fins, jackets or sprays that dissipate heat generated during operation. It includes design features that facilitate heat removal to prevent overheating and maintain mechanical performance.
[F16C 3/18] Eccentric-shafts -- F16C 3/18 pertains to eccentric shafts, which are rotating shafts whose axis is deliberately offset from the centerline of the driven component, producing a cyclical change in radial position. They are employed to generate reciprocating motion, balance loads or transmit power in devices such as pumps, compressors and engines.
[F16C 3/20] Shape of crankshafts or eccentric-shafts having regard to balancing -- F16C 3/20 covers the design of crankshafts and eccentric shafts whose shape is selected to improve dynamic balance, reducing vibration and stress during rotation. It includes features such as counterweights, profiling, and material distribution aimed at achieving static and running balance in mechanical power-transmission systems.
[F16C 32/00] Bearings not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details for all types of bearings, i.e. they are not limited to rotary sliding contact or rolling element bearings but, as far as applicable, they also cover the other bearing types (e.g. linear, magnetic, hydrostatic etc.).
[F16C 32/02] Knife-edge bearings -- F16C 32/02 designates knife-edge bearings, a type of bearing where a sharp, hardened edge contacts a mating surface to support a shaft or rotating element. This arrangement provides a low-friction, precise pivot point and is used in applications requiring accurate angular positioning that are not covered by other bearing categories.
[F16C 32/04] using magnetic or electric supporting means -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Magnetic levitation devices -> H02N15/00
[F16C 32/0402] combined with other supporting means, e.g. hybrid bearings with both magnetic and fluid supporting means -- F16C 32/0402 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system refers to bearings that integrate multiple support mechanisms, such as combining magnetic and fluid-based supports, for enhanced performance or specific application requirements.
[F16C 32/0404] Electrostatic bearings -- Electrostatic bearings are bearing devices that support rotating shafts using electrostatic forces instead of conventional contact surfaces, providing friction-free or low-wear operation for precision or high-speed machinery. The classification includes designs, arrangements and control methods for such bearings.
[F16C 32/0406] Magnetic bearings -- Magnetic bearings are non-contact shaft supports that use magnetic fields to levitate and stabilize a rotating shaft, eliminating friction, wear and the need for lubrication. They are employed in high-speed or precision machinery where conventional bearings are unsuitable, and are classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, bearings not otherwise provided for.
[F16C 32/0408] Passive magnetic bearings -- F16C 32/0408 covers passive magnetic bearings, which support rotating shafts by using permanent-magnet or permanent-field forces without active control systems. The technology provides non-contact, friction-free support for shafts where conventional bearings are not specified, fitting within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of engineering elements, shafts, and bearings not otherwise provided for.
[F16C 32/041] with permanent magnets on one part attracting the other part -- F16C 32/041 classifies shafts that incorporate a bearing arrangement where a permanent magnet mounted on one component attracts the other component, providing a non-lubricated holding or positioning function. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units, shafts, and bearings not otherwise provided for. This code is used in both the EPC and USPTO patent classification systems.
[F16C 32/0412] for radial load mainly -- F16C 32/0412 classifies bearings that are not otherwise specified and are designed principally to carry radial loads. It applies to shaft installations where the load acts perpendicular to the axis, typical of journal or plain bearings used in mechanical engineering.
[F16C 32/0414] with facing axial projections -- F16C 32/0414 covers bearings that are not otherwise provided for, specifically those that incorporate facing axial projections to locate or support the bearing within a shaft assembly. The axial projections are arranged to face each other, providing precise axial positioning and load distribution.
[F16C 32/0417] for axial load mainly -- F16C 32/0417 designates bearings that are primarily intended to carry axial loads along the shaft axis, as opposed to radial loads. These bearings are used where the load direction is mainly axial and are not otherwise classified elsewhere.
[F16C 32/0419] with facing radial projections -- F16C 32/0419 covers bearings that incorporate facing radial projections - protrusions extending outward from opposing bearing components that engage each other to limit axial movement and increase load capacity. The subclass is applied to shaft-bearing designs where standard bearing configurations are not otherwise specified.
[F16C 32/0421] for both radial and axial load -- F16C 32/0421 classifies bearings that are not otherwise specified and are designed to support loads in both radial and axial directions. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts, covering hybrid bearing concepts that combine the functions of supporting side-wise and end-wise forces.
[F16C 32/0423] with permanent magnets on both parts repelling each other -- This sub-group of F16C deals with bearings where permanent magnets mounted on the inner and outer members generate repulsive forces to support the shaft without contact, reducing friction and wear. It applies to designs where both parts carry magnets that oppose each other to maintain clearance.
[F16C 32/0425] for radial load mainly -- F16C 32/0425 refers to bearings that are intended principally to carry radial loads, meaning forces acting perpendicular to the shaft axis. These devices are used where support for axial forces is not required and include various plain or rolling-bearing designs that focus on resisting mainly radial pressure.
[F16C 32/0427] for axial load mainly -- The subclass F16C 32/0427 refers to bearings that are intended mainly to carry axial loads and are not otherwise specified by a more detailed bearing type, applied to shafts in mechanical engineering.
[F16C 32/0429] for both radial and axial load, e.g. conical magnets -- This subclass covers bearing arrangements that can sustain both radial and axial loads, exemplified by designs using conical magnetic elements, and is placed under mechanical engineering, shafts, bearings not otherwise provided for.
[F16C 32/0431] with bearings for axial load combined with bearings for radial load -- The F16C 32/0431 subclass covers shafts that incorporate bearing arrangements designed to support axial loads together with bearings for radial loads. It applies to mechanical-engineering elements where combined loading conditions are addressed, typically in rotating equipment that requires simultaneous handling of thrust and radial forces.
[F16C 32/0434] for parts moving linearly -- F16C 32/0434 refers to bearings that are not otherwise specified and are intended to support parts that move linearly along a shaft. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts. The subclass addresses linear motion applications of such bearings.
[F16C 32/0436] with a conductor on one part movable with respect to a magnetic field, e.g. a body of copper on one part and a permanent magnet on the other part -- F16C 32/0436 covers shaft bearing arrangements where a conductive element, such as a copper body, moves relative to a magnetic field produced by a permanent magnet on the opposite part. The relative motion creates electromagnetic forces that can transmit torque, brake or damp motion without direct mechanical contact. This subclass falls under bearings not otherwise provided for in the mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F16C 32/0438] with a superconducting body, e.g. a body made of high temperature superconducting material such as YBaCuO -- This CPC subclass covers bearings that incorporate a superconducting body, such as one made from high-temperature superconductors like YBaCuO. The superconducting material may be used to provide magnetic levitation, frictionless support, or other performance benefits within the bearing assembly.
[F16C 32/044] Active magnetic bearings -- Active magnetic bearings are non-contact shaft supports that use electromagnets to generate controlled magnetic fields, levitating the shaft and eliminating friction and wear associated with conventional bearings. The technology provides precise positional control, load capacity and damping, making it suitable for high-speed or high-precision rotating equipment where traditional bearings are impractical.
[F16C 32/0442] with devices affected by abnormal, undesired or non-standard conditions such as shock-load, power outage, start-up or touchdown -- This subclass deals with bearings that include protective or compensating features to operate safely under abnormal or non-standard conditions such as shock-load, power outage, start-up or touchdown. The inventions aim to maintain performance or prevent damage when the bearing experiences sudden or irregular forces.
[F16C 32/0444] Details of devices to control the actuation of the electromagnets -- The F16C 32/0444 subclass deals with devices and control arrangements that regulate the actuation of electromagnets in shaft or bearing systems, including circuits, sensors, and mechanical linkages used to start, stop, or modulate magnetic force. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for shafts and bearings not otherwise provided for.
[F16C 32/0446] Determination of the actual position of the moving member, e.g. details of sensors -- This subclass concerns techniques and devices for detecting the real-time location of a moving shaft or bearing element, such as position sensors, encoders, magnetic or optical detectors, and associated signal processing. It is used to monitor and control the operation of bearings that are not otherwise provided for.
[F16C 32/0448] by using the electromagnet itself as sensor, e.g. sensorless magnetic bearings -- F16C 32/0448 designates magnetic bearings that perform position sensing by exploiting the electromagnet that generates the supporting force, thereby providing sensorless operation. The approach eliminates separate displacement sensors, simplifying the bearing structure while maintaining control of shaft dynamics.
[F16C 32/0451] Details of controllers, i.e. the units determining the power to be supplied, e.g. comparing elements, feedback arrangements with P.I.D. control -- F16C 32/0451 covers the detailed design of control units that determine the power supplied to shaft bearings, including comparator elements and feedback mechanisms such as proportional-integral-derivative (PID) control. The subclass addresses how these controllers adjust torque or speed to maintain bearing performance.
[F16C 32/0453] for controlling two axes, i.e. combined control of x-axis and y-axis -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16C 32/0453 pertains to bearings designed for the simultaneous control of movement along both the x-axis and y-axis, falling under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 32/0455] including digital signal processing [DSP] and analog/digital conversion [A/D, D/A] -- The CPC subclass F16C 32/0455 belongs to mechanical engineering, shafts and bearings not otherwise provided for, and specifies bearings that incorporate digital signal processing and analog-digital conversion. It covers smart or sensor-enhanced bearings in which DSP chips and A/D or D/A converters are integrated to monitor, control or optimise bearing performance.
[F16C 32/0457] Details of the power supply to the electromagnets -- This subclass concerns the electrical provision for electromagnets used in bearing systems, detailing aspects such as voltage levels, current ratings, wiring configurations, control circuitry and power source arrangements that affect the operation, reliability and safety of magnet-actuated bearings.
[F16C 32/0459] Details of the magnetic circuit -- F16C 32/0459 concerns the magnetic circuit of bearings that are not otherwise specified, including the arrangement of magnetic poles, flux paths and materials used to generate and control magnetic forces in shaft-bearing assemblies. It addresses design details that enable magnetic levitation or torque transmission.
[F16C 32/0461] of stationary parts of the magnetic circuit -- F16C 32/0461 designates inventions concerning the stationary components of a magnetic circuit used in bearings that are not otherwise specified. It includes magnetic plates, yokes or housings that provide magnetic assistance to the shaft or bearing assembly while remaining fixed in place.
[F16C 32/0463] with electromagnetic bias, e.g. by extra bias windings -- F16C 32/0463 covers bearings that incorporate an electromagnetic bias, typically achieved with additional bias windings, to influence load distribution or stiffness. The electromagnetic system can be used to adjust bearing clearance, reduce wear, or improve dynamic response in applications such as high-speed or precision machinery. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and bearings not otherwise provided for.
[F16C 32/0465] with permanent magnets provided in the magnetic circuit of the electromagnets -- A bearing of the "not otherwise provided for" type that incorporates permanent magnets within the magnetic circuit of its electromagnets, using the magnets to create or enhance the magnetic field that supports the shaft or rotor. This arrangement can improve load capacity, reduce wear or enable contact-less operation.
[F16C 32/0468] of moving parts of the magnetic circuit, e.g. of the rotor -- This subclass covers bearings that support moving parts of a magnetic circuit, for example the rotor of an electric machine, when the bearing arrangement is not covered by any other specific bearing category. It falls under mechanical-engineering shafts and relates to specialized bearing provision for magnetic circuit components.
[F16C 32/047] Details of housings; Mounting of active magnetic bearings -- F16C 32/047 covers the design and construction of housings and mounting arrangements for active magnetic bearings, including how the bearing is supported, integrated with surrounding structures, and provided with cooling, sealing and alignment features. It relates to mechanical engineering elements that ensure stable operation of magnetic bearing systems.
[F16C 32/0472] for linear movement -- F16C 32/0472 covers bearings that are specifically intended for linear movement rather than rotation. It includes slide bearings, linear guides and other devices that allow a shaft or element to travel straight while supporting loads, falling under the broader group of bearings not otherwise provided for.
[F16C 32/0474] for rotary movement -- F16C 32/0474 designates bearings that enable rotary movement but are not otherwise covered by specific bearing types, falling under mechanical engineering shafts and engineering elements and units. This subclass groups devices that support rotation where standard bearing categories are insufficient. It is used in the Cooperative Patent Classification system for both EPO and USPTO filings.
[F16C 32/0476] with active support of one degree of freedom, e.g. axial magnetic bearings -- This subclass covers bearings that provide active axial support for rotating shafts, typically using magnetic fields to control one degree of freedom. It applies to systems where the axial load is managed by active means rather than conventional contact surfaces.
[F16C 32/0478] with permanent magnets to support radial load -- The subclass F16C 32/0478 defines shaft bearings that employ permanent magnets to carry radial loads, providing load support in applications where conventional bearings are not otherwise specified. It falls within mechanical engineering, under engineering elements and units for shafts.
