[F16C 32/048] with active support of two degrees of freedom, e.g. radial magnetic bearings -- F16C 32/048 covers bearings that provide active support in two degrees of freedom, such as radial magnetic bearings, where the load is sustained by controlled electromagnetic forces rather than static geometry. These devices are used on shafts to maintain position and reduce friction while allowing movement in the radial direction.
[F16C 32/0482] with three electromagnets to control the two degrees of freedom -- A bearing assembly of this subclass employs three electromagnets to actively control motion in two orthogonal degrees of freedom, allowing precise adjustment of radial and axial displacement. The magnetic forces are used to counteract loads, stabilise the shaft and provide dynamic positioning or vibration suppression beyond what passive bearings can achieve.
[F16C 32/0485] with active support of three degrees of freedom -- F16C 32/0485 covers bearings that provide active support in three degrees of freedom, allowing controlled motion or load compensation along three independent axes, usually by magnetic, hydraulic or electromechanical means. These bearings are employed where passive solutions cannot achieve the required positional stability or force regulation.
[F16C 32/0487] with active support of four degrees of freedom -- F16C 32/0487 covers bearings that are not otherwise specified and that incorporate an actively controlled support system providing four independent degrees of freedom. The active mechanism-typically electromagnetic, hydraulic or pneumatic-maintains load-bearing capacity while allowing translation in two axes and rotation about two perpendicular axes, enabling precise positioning and vibration damping.
[F16C 32/0489] with active support of five degrees of freedom, e.g. two radial magnetic bearings combined with an axial bearing -- This subclass refers to shaft bearings that are not otherwise specified and that provide active support in five degrees of freedom, typically by combining two radial magnetic bearings with an axial bearing. The arrangement allows precise, contact-less positioning and load handling of the shaft.
[F16C 32/0491] with electromagnets acting in axial and radial direction, e.g. with conical magnets -- A subclass of bearings not otherwise provided for, defined under mechanical engineering shafts, that incorporates electromagnets acting in both axial and radial directions, for example using conical magnets to generate magnetic levitation or support. This classification covers designs where magnetic forces replace or supplement traditional bearing contact.
[F16C 32/0493] integrated in an electrodynamic machine, e.g. self-bearing motor -- F16C 32/0493 covers bearings that are built into the structure of an electrodynamic machine, such as a self-bearing motor, where the bearing function is achieved by electromagnetic forces rather than conventional mechanical components. These integrated bearings are considered part of the machine's shaft system and are not provided separately.
[F16C 32/0495] generating torque and axial force -- The subclass covers bearings that are deliberately designed to produce torque and/or an axial loading force while supporting rotation, such as torque-transmitting or thrust-type bearings that combine a bearing function with force generation for power transmission or load-carrying applications.
[F16C 32/0497] generating torque and radial force -- This CPC subclass concerns devices or methods that generate torque together with a radial force in bearings that are not otherwise specified, typically applied to shafts. It includes mechanisms such as cams, eccentrics or gear arrangements that simultaneously produce rotational driving and outward pressure on the bearing elements.
[F16C 32/06] with moving member supported by a fluid cushion formed, at least to a large extent, otherwise than by movement of the shaft, e.g. hydrostatic air-cushion bearings -- F16C 32/06 covers shaft bearings in which the moving part is supported primarily by a fluid cushion that is generated independently of shaft motion, such as hydrostatic or air-cushion bearings. These designs provide low-friction support without relying on the shaft's own movement to create the bearing film.
[F16C 32/0603] supported by a gas cushion, e.g. an air cushion -- The CPC subclass F16C 32/0603 covers shafts whose bearings are not otherwise specified and are supported by a gas cushion, such as an air film, to provide low-friction support and load bearing. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → shafts.
[F16C 32/0607] the gas being retained in a gap, e.g. squeeze film bearings -- This subclass covers bearings that retain a thin layer of gas in the clearance between moving surfaces, forming a squeeze-film or hydrodynamic film that carries load. It includes designs, operating principles and devices that deliberately trap gas to provide lubrication, damping or support in mechanical shafts.
[F16C 32/0611] by means of vibrations -- F16C 32/0611 covers shaft bearings that are operated or assisted by vibratory motion, where vibrations are deliberately used to generate or maintain the bearing function for shafts that lack conventional bearing provisions. This subclass includes designs that employ acoustic, ultrasonic or resonant vibrations to create a supporting film, reduce friction or provide dynamic stabilization of the rotating element.
[F16C 32/0614] the gas being supplied under pressure, e.g. aerostatic bearings -- The subclass deals with bearings that rely on a pressurised gas film to support loads, such as aerostatic bearings, and covers the means of supplying the gas under pressure to create the bearing film.
[F16C 32/0618] via porous material -- F16C 32/0618 designates bearings that are formed from or incorporate a porous material, allowing the material itself to retain and supply lubricant to the moving shaft. Such bearings are used where traditional lubrication mechanisms are not provided.
[F16C 32/0622] via nozzles, restrictors -- F16C 32/0622 covers bearing arrangements that incorporate flow-controlling elements such as nozzles or restrictors to limit the passage of lubricant, gas or liquid through the bearing housing or shaft. The subclass applies to designs where these devices are integrated into bearings that are not otherwise specified.
[F16C 32/0625] via supply slits -- F16C 32/0625 covers bearings that are introduced into a shaft via narrow supply slits, allowing the bearing elements to be inserted directly through the shaft material without a separate housing. This subclass addresses the design of such slit-fed bearing arrangements used where conventional bearing provision is not provided.
[F16C 32/0629] supported by a liquid cushion, e.g. oil cushion -- F16C 32/0629 covers bearings that are supported by a liquid film, typically an oil cushion, which separates moving parts and carries the load. The classification includes hydrostatic and hydrodynamic fluid-film bearings where the liquid pressure is generated by external pumps or by relative motion, providing low friction and wear resistance.
[F16C 32/0633] the liquid being retained in a gap -- F16C 32/0633 covers bearings in which a liquid is deliberately retained in a clearance to form a fluid film that carries load, typically used on rotating shafts. The retained fluid reduces friction and wear while supporting the shaft, and the classification applies to designs where the liquid is trapped in a gap rather than supplied continuously.
[F16C 32/0637] by a magnetic field, e.g. ferrofluid bearings -- The code covers shafts that are supported by bearings formed with a magnetic field, for example ferrofluid bearings, which provide non-contact load carrying. These bearings are classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts where bearings are not otherwise provided for.
[F16C 32/064] the liquid being supplied under pressure -- F16C 32/064 covers bearings that are lubricated or cooled by a liquid delivered under pressure rather than by conventional oil baths or greases. The classification addresses designs where the pressurised fluid is supplied directly to the bearing surfaces to reduce friction and heat.
[F16C 32/0644] Details of devices to control the supply of liquids to the bearings -- The code F16C 32/0644 covers devices that regulate the flow of liquids to bearings that are not otherwise supplied with lubrication, such as pumps, metering valves, channels or control circuits that ensure a steady or variable supply of oil or coolant to maintain bearing performance.
[F16C 32/0648] by sensors or pressure-responsive control devices in or near the bearings -- This subclass concerns bearings that incorporate sensors or pressure-responsive control devices located in or near the bearing assembly, allowing monitoring or automatic adjustment of load, temperature, vibration, or lubrication conditions. It applies to bearings not otherwise provided for in other subclasses.
[F16C 32/0651] Details of the bearing area  per se -- This CPC subclass deals with the design and specification of the bearing surface itself, such as geometry, dimensions, surface finish and lubricating features of the shaft area that supports a bearing, without addressing the bearing housing or complete bearing unit.
[F16C 32/0655] of supply openings -- F16C 32/0655 is a CPC subclass in the mechanical-engineering section that deals with shafts where bearings are not specifically provided. It covers designs that incorporate supply openings-such as holes, ports or passages-through the shaft to deliver fluids, lubricants or other substances needed for operation, without dedicated bearing housings. This classification aids in locating inventions focused on integrating supply channels into rotating shafts.
[F16C 32/0659] of pockets or grooves -- This subclass concerns bearings that incorporate pockets or grooves as the means of locating or supporting the rotating element, rather than conventional bearing types. It covers design features where the bearing surface is formed by recessed grooves or pockets in a shaft or housing to provide load support.
[F16C 32/0662] Details of hydrostatic bearings independent of fluid supply or direction of load -- F16C 32/0662 concerns the design and characteristics of hydrostatic bearings where the description focuses on the bearing itself rather than the fluid delivery system or the direction of applied loads. It covers aspects such as geometry, material selection, pressure distribution and performance parameters of the bearing independent of its supply arrangement.
[F16C 32/0666] of bearing pads -- The CPC entry F16C 32/0666 pertains to bearing pads used on shafts where the bearings are not otherwise specified. It covers designs and arrangements of pads that support, locate or guide rotating shafts, including split, slip-on or fixed pads that transmit loads or reduce friction without constituting a complete bearing assembly. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Bearings not otherwise provided for.
[F16C 32/067] of bearings adjustable for aligning, positioning, wear or play -- F16C 32/067 concerns bearings that can be adjusted to correct alignment, set their position, compensate for wear or control the amount of play. These adjustable bearings are used on shafts to maintain precise positioning and functional clearance in mechanical assemblies.
[F16C 32/0674] by means of pre-load on the fluid bearings -- In the CPC class F16C 32/0674, under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Bearings not otherwise provided for, the invention concerns fluid bearings that are pre-loaded. The preload is applied to the fluid film to increase bearing stiffness, limit clearance and improve load-carrying capacity during operation.
[F16C 32/0677] of elastic or yielding bearings or bearing supports -- This class refers to elastic or yielding bearings and bearing supports, i.e., bearing elements that deform under load to provide compliance and shock absorption for rotating shafts. It covers designs where the bearing material yields to distribute stresses.
[F16C 32/0681] Construction or mounting aspects of hydrostatic bearings, for exclusively rotary movement, related to the direction of load -- This CPC subclass covers technical features concerning how hydrostatic bearings that allow only rotary motion are designed or mounted, specifically addressing the orientation of the applied load. It pertains to the construction details and mounting arrangements of such bearings within shaft assemblies.
[F16C 32/0685] for radial load only -- This subclass pertains to bearings designed specifically for radial loads that are not otherwise classified elsewhere, typically used on shafts in mechanical engineering. It falls under CPC group F16C 32/0685, covering bearings not otherwise provided for, for radial load only.
[F16C 32/0688] with floating bearing elements -- The CPC entry F16C 32/0688 classifies shafts that incorporate floating bearing elements, a type of bearing not otherwise specified in other categories. It falls under Mechanical Engineering - Engineering Elements and Units - Shafts - Bearings not otherwise provided for. The code is used by the EPO and USPTO to index inventions where the bearing can move relative to the shaft to accommodate misalignment.
[F16C 32/0692] for axial load only -- This CPC subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and covers bearings not otherwise provided for, specifically designed to support only axial-loads.
[F16C 32/0696] for both radial and axial load -- The CPC subclass F16C 32/0696 defines bearings that are capable of simultaneously supporting radial and axial loads, extending the category of shafts to include those not otherwise specified. It applies to mechanical-engineering elements where combined load bearing is required.
[F16C 3/22] Cranks -- F16C 3/22 covers cranks, the rotating arms or levers attached to a shaft that convert rotary motion into reciprocating motion or vice-versa. It includes designs of crank throws, offset pins and bearing arrangements that enable efficient power transmission in engines, pumps and other mechanical systems. The classification groups these elements within the broader categories of shafts and mechanical engineering components.
[F16C 3/24] with return cranks, i.e. a second crank carried by the crank-pin -- F16C 3/24 covers shafts that incorporate a return crank, meaning a second crank is mounted on the crank-pin of the primary crank. This configuration provides a reciprocating motion to a follower link and is used in devices such as pumps, presses and internal-combustion engines.
[F16C 3/26] Elastic crank-webs -- Elastic crank-webs are flexible sections of a crankshaft that connect the crank throws while providing torsional compliance and vibration damping. They are designed to deform elastically under load, improving the dynamic balance and longevity of the shaft in engines and machinery. This subclass covers such elastic web designs and related manufacturing methods.
[F16C 3/28] Adjustable cranks or eccentrics -- F16C 3/28 relates to mechanical devices where a crank or eccentric can be manually or automatically changed in length, offset or angular position to vary the stroke or timing of a rotating member. The classification includes adjustable cranks, adjustable eccentric cams and mechanisms that permit fine tuning of motion through selectable or movable geometry.
[F16C 3/30] with arrangements for overcoming dead-centres -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F16C 3/30 pertains to shafts in mechanical engineering, specifically addressing designs that include mechanisms or features intended to overcome dead-center positions, enhancing operational efficiency and reliability.
[F16C 33/00] Details or accessories of bearings -- The CPC subclass F16C 33/00 concerns auxiliary parts and specific features that complement bearings, such as seals, retainers, lubrication devices, protective covers, mounting fixtures and other accessories that enhance bearing performance, protect against contamination or simplify installation. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 33/02] Parts of sliding-contact bearings -- F16C 33/02 covers components used in sliding-contact bearings, such as sleeves, journals, liners and inserts that facilitate relative motion between bearing surfaces. These parts are designed to reduce friction and wear in mechanical assemblies where shafts rotate within supporting housings. The classification is part of the mechanical engineering section dealing with shafts and bearing accessories.
[F16C 33/04] Brasses -- F16C 33/04 - Brasses covers brass components that serve as liners, sleeves or protective inserts in shaft bearings, falling under the mechanical-engineering category of bearing accessories. It classifies details such as brass bushings used to reduce wear and improve lubrication in rotating assemblies.
[F16C 33/043] Sliding surface consisting mainly of ceramics, cermets or hard carbon, e.g. diamond like carbon [DLC] -- This CPC subgroup F16C 33/043 pertains to bearing accessories in which the sliding surface is made primarily of ceramics, cermets or hard-carbon materials such as diamond-like carbon, offering low wear and high hardness for improved performance.
[F16C 33/046] divided or split, e.g. half-bearings or rolled sleeves -- F16C 33/046 pertains to bearing elements that are divided or split, such as half-bearings or rolled sleeves, which can be assembled around a shaft or mounted in separate sections to facilitate installation or accommodate space constraints.
[F16C 33/06] Sliding surface mainly made of metal -- A sliding surface that is mainly metal, used in mechanical-engineering shafts and bearing accessories to provide a low-friction contact area such as metal liners, sleeves or similar components. This subclass covers designs where metal constitutes the primary material of the sliding interface.
[F16C 33/08] Attachment of brasses, bushes or linings to the bearing housing -- The code F16C 33/08 covers the attachment of brass, bush or lining components to a bearing housing, describing methods and devices for fixing these wear-resistant elements to form a bearing assembly. It falls within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and bearing accessories.
[F16C 33/10] Construction relative to lubrication -- F16C 33/10 covers the design of bearing components and accessories that relate to lubrication, such as oil-or grease delivery channels, reservoirs, seals and lubrication-system integration with shafts. It addresses how these features are constructed to ensure proper fluid flow, reduce friction and protect bearing surfaces.
[F16C 33/1005] with gas, e.g. air, as lubricant -- F16C 33/1005 covers accessories for shafts that use a gas such as air as a bearing lubricant. It relates to mechanical engineering components where the bearing fluid is a gas rather than oil or grease, providing low-friction operation in specialized shaft applications.
[F16C 33/101] Details of the bearing surface, e.g. means to generate pressure such as lobes or wedges -- F16C 33/101 relates to modifications of bearing surfaces that create pressure, such as protruding lobes, wedges or similar shaping elements, designed to improve load distribution, increase friction or otherwise enhance the performance of the bearing.
[F16C 33/1015] Pressure generating grooves -- F16C 33/1015 pertains to grooves cut or formed on shafts or bearing components that generate pressure, typically by directing fluid flow to create hydraulic or pneumatic forces. These pressure-generating grooves are used to enhance lubrication, support loads or control movement in mechanical systems.
[F16C 33/102] with grease as lubricant -- A subclass of bearing accessories in which grease serves as the lubricating medium, covering grease-filled bearings, grease lubrication systems and related devices for shafts and rotating components.
[F16C 33/1025] with liquid, e.g. oil, as lubricant -- The F16C 33/1025 subclass concerns accessories for bearings that use a liquid, typically oil, to provide lubrication. It includes devices and arrangements that deliver the lubricant to shafts or bearing elements, such as oil-filled baths, oil jets, and circulation systems integrated with the bearing assembly. This falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/103] retained in or near the bearing -- The subclass F16C 33/103 covers devices that are retained in or positioned near a bearing, such as retainers, cages, or other accessories that hold or align the bearing relative to a shaft. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → shafts → details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/1035] by a magnetic field acting on a magnetic liquid -- The subclass F16C 33/1035 covers bearing accessories that employ a magnetic field acting on a magnetic liquid, such as ferrofluid-based seals, couplings or dampers, to transmit forces, provide lubrication or reduce vibration in shaft assemblies. These devices use the field-responsive fluid to create controllable stiffness or damping directly within the bearing arrangement.
[F16C 33/104] in a porous body, e.g. oil impregnated sintered sleeve -- The code covers bearing components that incorporate a porous material, such as an oil-impregnated sintered sleeve, forming a lubricating reservoir or bearing surface. The porous body supplies continuous lubrication to the shaft, reducing friction and wear in the bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/1045] Details of supply of the liquid to the bearing -- The CPC subclass F16C 33/1045 relates to components and arrangements that provide liquid to a bearing, such as lubrication channels, feed ducts, pumps, or distribution devices integrated with shafts or bearing housings, ensuring proper lubrication and cooling of the bearing.
[F16C 33/105] Conditioning, e.g. metering, cooling, filtering -- F16C 33/105 covers devices or methods that condition the lubricant or working fluid in bearings, including metering, cooling and filtering of the fluid to maintain proper operation. It applies to shaft bearings and related accessories where fluid treatment is integrated with the bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/1055] from radial inside, e.g. via a passage through the shaft and/or inner sleeve -- This sub-group covers shafts that provide a radial internal path, such as a hole through the shaft or an inner sleeve, allowing bearings or accessories to be accessed from the inside. The feature enables fluid, coolant, lubrication or load transmission via the passage.
[F16C 33/106] Details of distribution or circulation inside the bearings, e.g. details of the bearing surfaces to affect flow or pressure of the liquid -- F16C 33/106 refers to features inside bearings that manage fluid flow or pressure, such as specially shaped bearing surfaces or ducts that direct lubricating liquid. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/1065] Grooves on a bearing surface for distributing or collecting the liquid -- F16C 33/1065 covers bearing surfaces that incorporate grooves to spread or collect lubricating or cooling liquids. It belongs to the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering, Engineering Elements and Units, Shafts, Details or accessories of bearings, and is used in the Cooperative Patent Classification system of both the EPO and USPTO.
[F16C 33/107] Grooves for generating pressure -- The CPC entry F16C 33/107 covers grooves cut or formed on shafts or bearing components that create fluid pressure for lubrication, sealing or thrust-bearing functions. These pressure-generating grooves are detailed accessories of bearings used to enhance performance in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 33/1075] Wedges, e.g. ramps or lobes, for generating pressure -- The CPC subclass F16C 33/1075 covers wedges such as ramps or lobes used to generate pressure in bearing assemblies and other shaft-related mechanisms. These components are designed to transmit or increase load by altering geometry within the bearing system.
[F16C 33/108] with a plurality of elements forming the bearing surfaces, e.g. bearing pads -- The classification F16C 33/108 covers shaft accessories where the bearing surfaces consist of multiple separate elements, such as bearing pads, that together form the bearing interface and distribute loads across the shaft.
[F16C 33/1085] Channels or passages to recirculate the liquid in the bearing -- This CPC subclass covers channels or passages designed to recirculate lubricating liquid within a bearing to enhance cooling and lubrication performance. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, details or accessories of bearings, and is coded F16C 33/1085.
[F16C 33/109] Lubricant compositions or properties, e.g. viscosity -- The CPC code F16C 33/109 is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, details or accessories of bearings, and relates to lubricant compositions or properties such as viscosity.
[F16C 33/1095] with solids as lubricant, e.g. dry coatings, powder -- F16C 33/1095 covers bearing accessories that use solid lubricants such as dry coatings or powder applied to the bearing surfaces. It pertains to mechanical engineering shafts where solids replace liquid oils to reduce friction and wear. This subclass is part of the CPC hierarchy under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/12] Structural composition -- F16C 33/12 relates to the structural composition of bearing details or accessories, specifying the materials, geometry and internal arrangement used in shaft-bearing assemblies. It addresses how these elements are constructed to achieve required strength, rigidity, wear resistance and other mechanical performance criteria.
[F16C 33/121] Use of special materials -- The subclass F16C 33/121 covers inventions that employ special or unconventional materials in bearing components or accessories for shafts, such as advanced alloys, composites, or surface-treated elements, aimed at improving durability, load capacity, lubrication or other performance characteristics.
[F16C 33/122] Multilayer structures of sleeves, washers or liners -- F16C 33/122 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system refers to multilayer structures of sleeves, washers, or liners used as details or accessories in bearings. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with mechanical engineering and specifically addresses components that enhance bearing functionality through layered designs.
[F16C 33/124] Details of overlays -- F16C 33/124 covers the specific details of overlay components used on bearings, such as protective or friction-reducing layers applied to shafts. It pertains to the design and material features that enhance bearing performance and durability within mechanical engineering.
[F16C 33/125] Details of bearing layers, i.e. the lining -- F16C 33/125 relates to the design and application of lining or protective layers inside bearings, including the materials, coatings or inserts that form the bearing's internal surface. It addresses details of bearing layers that reduce wear, support loads and improve operational performance.
[F16C 33/127] Details of intermediate layers, e.g. nickel dams -- This subclass covers intermediate layers added to shafts or bearing components, such as nickel dams that provide corrosion resistance, improve load distribution, or act as diffusion barriers between mating surfaces.
[F16C 33/128] Porous bearings, e.g. bushes of sintered alloy -- F16C 33/128 covers porous bearings such as bushes made from sintered alloy in which the material contains interconnected voids that retain lubricant and improve load distribution. The design provides self-lubricating characteristics and reduces wear in rotating shafts and other mechanical elements.
[F16C 33/14] Special methods of manufacture -- F16C 33/14 classifies special manufacturing techniques applied to bearing accessories and related details of shafts, including unconventional or innovative processes for producing or finishing these components. It covers methods such as precision grinding, laser machining, surface coating or additive-manufacturing steps that differ from standard bearing production.
[F16C 33/145] of sintered porous bearings -- This subclass covers bearings that are produced by sintering a porous metal or ceramic structure, allowing oil or lubricant to be retained within the material for improved load-carrying capacity and reduced friction. It includes the design, manufacturing methods and applications of such porous bearings in mechanical systems.
[F16C 33/16] Sliding surface consisting mainly of graphite -- F16C 33/16 covers shafts or bearing components whose sliding surfaces are formed primarily from graphite, providing low friction and self-lubricating properties. Such graphite-based surfaces are used in bearings, bushings and other sliding elements to enhance wear resistance and reduce the need for oil lubrication.
[F16C 33/18] Sliding surface consisting mainly of wood or fibrous material -- The CPC entry F16C 33/18 covers bearings whose sliding surface is formed primarily from wood or other fibrous materials. It pertains to mechanical-engineering shaft bearings where the contact area is composed of these organic substances rather than metal or polymer. This classification groups designs that use such materials to provide low-friction sliding support.
[F16C 33/20] Sliding surface consisting mainly of plastics -- F16C 33/20 pertains to shaft bearing accessories whose sliding surface is made chiefly of plastic, such as polymer liners, jackets or inserts that provide low-friction, wear-resistant contact in rotating assemblies. The classification covers designs that exploit the material's self-lubricating and corrosion-resistant properties.
[F16C 33/201] Composition of the plastic -- F16C 33/201 pertains to the composition of plastics employed in bearing accessories such as sleeves, inserts or other detail parts for shafts. It addresses polymer types, fillers, reinforcements and processing methods that give the material suitable wear resistance, friction characteristics and mechanical strength for bearing applications.
[F16C 33/203] Multilayer structures, e.g. sleeves comprising a plastic lining -- F16C33/203 covers multilayer components such as sleeves that are mounted on shafts and incorporate a plastic lining, typically to provide wear resistance, corrosion protection or reduced friction. The entry includes designs where a metal or other core is overlaid with one or more polymer layers to form a functional protective sleeve.
[F16C 33/205] with two layers -- F16C 33/205 designates shafts that incorporate two concentric layers, typically a core and an outer sleeve, used as detail or accessory components of bearings. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units, then shafts, and focuses on multi-layer shaft constructions for improved strength, stiffness or wear resistance.
[F16C 33/206] with three layers -- The F16C 33/206 subclass addresses shafts that incorporate a three-layer bearing accessory, where the bearing element is built from three concentric or stacked layers of material. This arrangement is used to enhance load distribution, damping, or wear resistance in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 33/208] Methods of manufacture, e.g. shaping, applying coatings -- F16C 33/208 classifies manufacturing techniques for bearing details or accessories of shafts, encompassing processes such as shaping, forming and applying protective or functional coatings. The code is applied to inventions that improve or create such components through these methods.
[F16C 33/22] Sliding surface consisting mainly of rubber or synthetic rubber -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Elastic or yielding bearings whereby rubber is of interest apart from the surface itself -> F16C27/063
[F16C 33/24] with different areas of the sliding surface consisting of different materials -- F16C 33/24 concerns bearings or related accessories whose sliding surfaces are divided into zones made of different materials. It addresses designs where each area of the bearing race or journal uses a distinct material to optimise performance.
[F16C 33/26] made from wire coils -- F16C 33/26 covers shafts or related bearing accessories that are manufactured as wire-coil constructions, such as helical springs or flexible coils used to transmit torque or support loads within mechanical assemblies. This sub-group falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/28] with embedded reinforcements shaped as frames or meshed materials -- F16C 33/28 covers shafts or bearing accessories that incorporate reinforcing elements formed as frames or mesh-like materials. The embedded reinforcements are integrated into the component to increase strength, stiffness or wear resistance, especially in rotating or load-carrying applications. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/30] Parts of ball or roller bearings -- F16C 33/30 covers components that make up ball or roller bearings, such as inner and outer rings, cages, retainers and seals that hold the rolling elements in place. These parts are designed to support rotational motion while minimizing friction and wear within mechanical systems.
[F16C 33/303] of hybrid bearings, e.g. rolling bearings with steel races and ceramic rolling elements -- The F16C 33/303 subclass covers hybrid bearings, where the bearing races are made of steel while the rolling elements such as balls or rollers are ceramic, providing improved wear resistance and reduced friction for mechanical engineer­ing applications involving shafts and bearing accessories.
[F16C 33/306] Means to synchronise movements -- F16C 33/306 covers devices that coordinate the rotation of two or more shafts to ensure they move together, such as synchronising couplings, gear trains, or other mechanisms that transmit speed and phase between shafts. These means are regarded as accessories of bearings and are used to achieve precise timing or uniform motion in mechanical systems.
[F16C 33/32] Balls -- This CPC entry pertains to spherical balls employed as rolling elements in bearings, covering their design, material choice, dimensions and related manufacturing aspects. It is situated under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/34] Rollers -- Rollers are cylindrical bearing components that roll between raceways to support radial and axial loads while minimizing friction. They serve as accessories in shaft assemblies, appearing in straight, tapered or cylindrical forms and are essential for smooth rotation in mechanical systems.
[F16C 33/36] with bearing-surfaces other than cylindrical, e.g. tapered -- F16C 33/36 covers shafts whose bearing surfaces are shaped other than cylindrical, such as tapered or conical forms. These non-cylindrical surfaces are used to provide specific load-carrying characteristics, improve alignment, or facilitate assembly in bearing arrangements.
[F16C 33/363] with grooves in the bearing-surfaces -- F16C 33/363 refers to shafts whose bearing surfaces are provided with longitudinal or circumferential grooves. The grooves are designed to enhance lubrication distribution, reduce friction or aid in the removal of contaminants in bearing assemblies.
[F16C 33/366] Tapered rollers, i.e. rollers generally shaped as truncated cones -- F16C 33/366 pertains to tapered roller bearings, which use rollers shaped as truncated cones to transmit radial loads while permitting axial movement. The code is applied to details or accessories of bearings in mechanical-engineering shafts.
[F16C 33/37] Loose spacing bodies -- F16C 33/37 covers loose spacing bodies used as accessories for bearings on shafts. These components are non-integral elements that provide axial or radial clearance between bearing parts, typically in mechanical devices to absorb shock or accommodate misalignment.
[F16C 33/3706] with concave surfaces conforming to the shape of the rolling elements, e.g. the spacing bodies are in sliding contact with the rolling elements -- The entry covers bearing components that feature concave surfaces shaped to match the rolling elements, such as spacing bodies that remain in sliding contact with those elements.
[F16C 33/3713] with other rolling elements serving as spacing bodies, e.g. the spacing bodies are in rolling contact with the load carrying rolling elements -- F16C 33/3713 designates shaft components or bearing accessories that use additional rolling elements as spacing bodies. These spacers are in rolling contact with the load-carrying rolling elements, providing precise separation and load distribution within the bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/372] rigid -- F16C 33/372 concerns rigid details or accessories attached to bearings in shafts, covering components such as housings, collars or seals that are fixed and non-flexible, and form part of the bearing assembly. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 33/374] resilient -- The code F16C 33/374 covers shafts that include resilient elements such as elastomeric sections, flexible couplings or built-in shock-absorbing devices, used as accessories for bearings to damp vibration, accommodate misalignment and reduce impact loads in rotating machinery.
[F16C 33/38] Ball cages -- F16C 33/38 covers ball cages, the devices that retain and space the balls within rolling-element bearings. It includes design, material and manufacturing aspects of cages that keep the balls separated and properly aligned for reliable bearing operation.
[F16C 33/3806] Details of interaction of cage and race, e.g. retention, centring -- F16C 33/3806 covers specific features that manage how a bearing cage engages with the race, including mechanisms for retaining or centring the cage within the bearing assembly. These details improve stability and positional accuracy of rotating shafts.
[F16C 33/3812] formed of interconnected segments, e.g. chains -- F16C 33/3812 covers shafts or bearing components that are constructed from a series of linked elements, such as chain-like segments, providing flexibility or modularity. The classification applies to designs where the shaft is formed by interconnected pieces rather than a single solid body.
[F16C 33/3818] formed of unconnected segments -- The CPC entry F16C 33/3818 covers shafts or bearing accessories that are constructed from separate, unconnected segments which are assembled to form the final component. It applies to modular or segmented designs that allow individual pieces to be joined for a complete shaft or bearing element.
[F16C 33/3825] formed as a flexible belt, e.g. spacers connected by a thin film -- A CPC subclass for details or accessories of bearings that are made as a flexible belt, for example spacers joined by a thin film, used to maintain bearing spacing or alignment. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under shafts.
[F16C 33/3831] with hybrid structure, i.e. with parts made of distinct materials -- F16C 33/3831 classifies bearing components or accessories that are constructed as hybrid structures, meaning the part incorporates two or more different materials, such as metal combined with polymer or ceramic, to achieve specific mechanical or wear-resistant properties.
[F16C 33/3837] Massive or moulded cages having cage pockets surrounding the balls, e.g. machined window cages -- A massive or moulded bearing cage that incorporates pockets surrounding each ball, such as a machined window-type cage, providing individual ball retention within the cage structure.
[F16C 33/3843] formed as one-piece cages, i.e. monoblock cages -- F16C 33/3843 designates bearing cages that are produced as a single, integral component, commonly referred to as monoblock cages, eliminating the need for separate parts or assembly. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering - engineering elements and units - shafts - details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/385] made from metal, e.g. cast or machined window cages -- F16C 33/385 covers metal window cages that are cast or machined as accessories for bearings. These cages are positioned on shafts to retain or guide bearing elements and protect them from debris.
[F16C 33/3856] made from plastic, e.g. injection moulded window cages -- F16C 33/3856 covers plastic components that serve as details or accessories for bearings, particularly those formed by injection moulding such as window cages. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of shafts and bearing-related parts, specifying polymer-based solutions.
[F16C 33/3862] comprising two annular parts joined together -- A bearing accessory consisting of two annular components that are joined together, forming a shaft-type element used to support or locate rotating parts within a mechanical system.
[F16C 33/3868] made from metal, e.g. two cast parts joined by rivets -- A metal bearing accessory or detail formed by joining two cast metal pieces, typically with rivets, such as a shaft collar or housing that is integral to a bearing assembly. This entry specifies that the component is fabricated from metal and created by riveted casting.
[F16C 33/3875] made from plastic, e.g. two injection moulded parts joined by a snap fit -- F16C 33/3875 classifies plastic components used as details or accessories of bearings, specifically when they are produced as two injection-moulded parts that are joined together by a snap-fit. This falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of shafts.
[F16C 33/3881] with more than three parts, e.g. two end rings connected by individual stays -- F16C 33/3881 covers bearing accessories made up of more than three separate parts, such as two end rings joined by individual stays. These multi-component arrangements are used to support or guide rotating shafts and improve load distribution in mechanical assemblies.
[F16C 33/3887] Details of individual pockets, e.g. shape or ball retaining means -- The CPC subclass F16C 33/3887 covers design details of the individual pockets in rolling-element bearings, such as the shape of the pockets and any means used to retain the balls. It addresses features that affect how the balls are held within the bearing raceway.
[F16C 33/3893] with rolling elements with smaller diameter than the load carrying balls, e.g. cages with counter-rotating spacers -- The code F16C 33/3893 refers to accessories for bearings in which additional rolling elements that are smaller than the main load-bearing balls are employed, typically in cages that incorporate counter-rotating spacers to improve load distribution and stability. This falls under mechanical engineering, specifically shafts and bearing details.
[F16C 33/40] for multiple rows of balls -- F16C 33/40 deals with ball bearings that contain two or more concentric rows of balls, providing higher load capacity and greater rigidity for rotating shafts. These multi-row ball bearings are used in mechanical engineering applications where both radial and axial forces are significant.
[F16C 33/405] with two or more juxtaposed cages joined together or interacting with each other -- The subclass F16C 33/405 covers bearing assemblies that incorporate two or more cages positioned side-by-side and either joined together or interacting with one another, describing details or accessories of such multi-cage bearing configurations.
[F16C 33/41] comb-shaped -- The comb-shaped subclass covers bearing accessories that feature a series of parallel teeth or projections resembling a comb. These elements are used to space, support or guide rotating shafts and to distribute loads across multiple points within a bearing assembly. They are typical in gearboxes and other mechanical systems requiring precise shaft alignment.
[F16C 33/412] Massive or moulded comb cages, e.g. snap ball cages -- The entry F16C 33/412 refers to massive or moulded comb cages, such as snap-ball cages, used as components of bearing assemblies. These cages are formed as a single piece or by molding and serve to retain balls or rollers within a bearing.
[F16C 33/414] formed as one-piece cages, i.e. monoblock comb cages -- The subclass F16C 33/414 covers bearing cages that are produced as a single, integral piece, commonly known as monoblock or comb cages, which serve to separate and guide rolling elements within a bearing. It is situated in the hierarchy under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → shafts → details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/416] made from plastic, e.g. injection moulded comb cages -- This CPC subclass falls under mechanical engineering, dealing with shaft bearing accessories, and defines bearing cages that are manufactured from plastic, for example injection-moulded comb-type cages used to retain rolling elements.
[F16C 33/418] Details of individual pockets, e.g. shape or ball retaining means -- The CPC entry F16C 33/418 covers design features of individual bearing pockets, such as the geometry of the cavity or mechanisms that retain the balls within the pocket. It pertains to details of bearing accessories that affect the positioning, support or locking of rolling elements in mechanical shafts.
[F16C 33/42] made from wire or sheet metal strips -- F16C 33/42 covers details or accessories of bearings that are manufactured from wire or sheet-metal strips, such as cages, retainers or other supporting elements used in shaft assemblies. These components are formed by shaping strips into the required geometry for bearing applications.
[F16C 33/422] made from sheet metal -- F16C 33/422 designates bearing details or accessories that are manufactured from sheet metal. It includes components such as flanged collars, plates, or shields that are stamped, bent or formed to support or protect bearings on shafts. The subclass is part of the mechanical engineering hierarchy for shafts and bearing elements.
[F16C 33/425] from a single part, e.g. ribbon cages with one corrugated annular part -- F16C 33/425 covers bearing cages that are made as a single integral part, such as ribbon-type cages formed from one corrugated annular piece. The code is applied to designs where the cage structure is produced as one continuous component rather than assembled from separate elements.
[F16C 33/427] from two parts, e.g. ribbon cages with two corrugated annular parts -- F16C33/427 covers bearing accessories that are formed from two parts, such as ribbon cages composed of two corrugated annular sections. These components are used to retain rolling elements within a shaft bearing and provide improved flexibility and load distribution.
[F16C 33/44] Selection of substances -- F16C 33/44 pertains to the choice of materials or substances for bearing components and related accessories, including metals, alloys, polymers and lubricants, with the aim of attaining required load capacity, wear resistance, corrosion protection and other performance characteristics.
[F16C 33/445] Coatings -- F16C 33/445 covers coatings applied to bearing accessories such as collars, seals or retainers that are part of shaft assemblies. The classification addresses methods, materials or functional layers designed to reduce wear, friction, corrosion or improve load capacity. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/46] Cages for rollers or needles -- This subclass covers devices that retain and separate individual rollers or needle elements within a bearing assembly, providing a cage or retaining ring that maintains correct spacing and alignment, often made of metal or polymer, and designed for shafts.
[F16C 33/4605] Details of interaction of cage and race, e.g. retention or centring -- The F16C 33/4605 subclass covers specific features that manage how a bearing cage contacts the inner or outer race, such as devices that retain the cage in place or centre it during operation. These details relate to the interaction between cage and race to ensure proper positioning and stability of the bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/4611] with hybrid structure, i.e. with parts made of distinct materials -- F16C 33/4611 designates shafts or bearing accessories that incorporate a hybrid structure, meaning they are built from two or more distinct materials, for example metal combined with polymer, to obtain improved mechanical or tribological properties. Such hybrid designs aim to exploit the strengths of each material while mitigating their weaknesses.
[F16C 33/4617] Massive or moulded cages having cage pockets surrounding the rollers, e.g. machined window cages -- This CPC entry covers large or moulded bearing cages that incorporate pockets surrounding the rollers, such as machined window cages. It relates to mechanical engineering shaft components and describes cage designs that improve roller retention and support.
[F16C 33/4623] formed as one-piece cages, i.e. monoblock cages -- F16C 33/4623 designates bearing cages that are produced as a single, monoblock component rather than assembled from separate parts, integrating the cage structure in one cast, forged or molded piece to enhance strength and simplify installation.
[F16C 33/4629] made from metal, e.g. cast or machined window cages -- The CPC subclass F16C 33/4629 covers metal-made window cages used as accessories of bearings in shaft assemblies, including cast or machined metal cages that hold rolling elements.

The classification addresses the design and manufacture of these polymer-based elements in mechanical engineering.
[F16C 33/4641] comprising two annular parts joined together -- A bearing accessory formed from two annular components that are joined together, such as a combined inner and outer race or a two-piece bearing housing, intended to support or guide rotating shafts. This falls under mechanical engineering, shafts, and bearing details.
[F16C 33/4647] made from metal, e.g. two cast parts joined by rivets -- The F16C 33/4647 entry concerns metal bearing components that are fabricated as two cast parts fastened together with rivets. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings, defining a specific assembly method for metal bearing parts.
[F16C 33/4652] made from plastic, e.g. two injection moulded parts joined by a snap fit -- This subclass addresses bearing components fabricated from plastic, typically produced as two injection-moulded parts that are joined together by a snap-fit connection rather than by screws, adhesives or other fasteners, and relating to shaft-related bearing accessories.
[F16C 33/4658] comprising three annular parts, i.e. three piece roller cages -- F16C 33/4658 covers bearing accessories that comprise three annular parts forming a three-piece roller cage. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and describes cage designs made from three separate rings.
[F16C 33/4664] with more than three parts, e.g. two end rings connected by individual stays -- F16C 33/4664 covers bearing accessories that consist of more than three components, such as two end rings linked by separate stays, and falls under the shaft and bearing details category of mechanical engineering.
[F16C 33/467] Details of individual pockets, e.g. shape or roller retaining means -- F16C 33/467 covers design features of the pockets within bearing assemblies, such as the geometry of the cavities that hold rollers or the mechanisms that retain them in place. It relates to individual pocket shapes and roller-retaining means in shafts.
[F16C 33/4676] of the stays separating adjacent cage pockets, e.g. guide means for the bearing-surface of the rollers -- The code F16C 33/4676 relates to small guiding or staying elements placed between adjacent pockets of a bearing cage, such as guides that keep rollers correctly positioned on the bearing surface. These stays separate the pockets and prevent roller-cage interference, serving as accessories to bearing assemblies in mechanical shaft applications.
[F16C 33/4682] of the end walls, e.g. interaction with the end faces of the rollers -- The classification F16C 33/4682 concerns shaft end-wall features that interact with the end faces of rollers in bearing assemblies, including contact surfaces, support geometry and any provisions for lubrication or stress distribution at those interfaces. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/4688] with rolling elements with smaller diameter than the load carrying rollers, e.g. cages with counter-rotating spacers -- F16C 33/4688 refers to shaft components that incorporate bearing accessories using rolling elements whose diameter is smaller than the load-carrying rollers, typically realized as cages with counter-rotating spacers. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts, and covers designs that improve bearing performance by separating and guiding the primary rollers with auxiliary, smaller elements.
[F16C 33/4694] Single-split roller or needle cages -- The code F16C 33/4694 designates single-split cages for roller or needle bearings, which are made as one piece with a single opening to separate the rolling elements, facilitate assembly and allow lubricant distribution while keeping the elements evenly spaced within shafts.
[F16C 33/48] for multiple rows of rollers or needles -- F16C 33/48 covers bearing designs that incorporate several parallel rows of rollers or needles, providing increased load capacity and stability compared with single-row arrangements. These multi-row configurations are used in shafts and other mechanical elements where higher rigidity and reduced wear are required.
[F16C 33/485] with two or more juxtaposed cages joined together or interacting with each other -- This CPC subclass covers bearing accessories that use a cage formed from two or more juxtaposed cages that are joined together or interact with each other. It resides in the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Details or Accessories of Bearings, and is designated F16C 33/485.
[F16C 33/49] comb-shaped -- F16C 33/49 designates comb-shaped details or accessories used with bearings, classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts. It covers components whose shape resembles a comb and that are intended to support, guide, or adjust bearing operation.
[F16C 33/491] applied as pairs for retaining both ends of the rollers or needles -- F16C 33/491 covers a bearing accessory that is provided as a pair to secure both ends of rollers or needle bearings. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering class for shafts and bearing components, specifying devices that retain the rolling elements within the bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/492] joined by rods -- F16C 33/492 relates to bearing assemblies in which the housing or associated parts are linked by rods, providing a rigid connection that transmits loads between bearing elements and secures the overall shaft support structure.
[F16C 33/494] Massive or moulded comb cages -- F16C 33/494 covers massive or moulded comb cages that serve as accessories for bearings. These cages are solid or injection-moulded components designed to space and guide rolling elements within a shaft assembly, improving load distribution and reducing friction in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 33/495] formed as one piece cages, i.e. monoblock comb cages -- F16C 33/495 covers bearing cages that are produced as a single, integral piece, often called monoblock or comb cages. Such cages provide spacing and guide the rolling elements within a bearing and are typically formed by machining, casting, or injection molding as a unitary component.
[F16C 33/497] made from metal, e.g. cast or machined comb cages -- The subclass F16C 33/497 covers metal comb cages that serve as bearing accessories, such as retainers or separators, in shaft bearings and are fabricated by casting, machining or similar metal-working processes. These components form part of the detailed elements of bearings within the mechanical engineering field.
[F16C 33/498] made from plastic, e.g. injection moulded comb cages -- F16C 33/498 covers bearing accessories that are manufactured from plastic, especially injection-moulded components such as comb-type cages. These parts are used on shafts and other mechanical engineering elements to separate rolling elements, reduce friction and retain lubricants, offering low weight and corrosion resistance.
[F16C 33/50] formed of interconnected members, e.g. chains -- The subclass F16C 33/50 concerns shafts that are built from a series of linked members, such as chains, where each link is joined to form a continuous rotating element. It applies to mechanical components that use interconnected members instead of a solid shaft, typically in power-transmission or bearing accessory arrangements.
[F16C 33/502] formed of arcuate segments retaining one or more rollers or needles -- F16C 33/502 covers shaft components in which the bearing accessory is formed from one or more curved (arcuate) segments that serve to retain rollers or needle-type rolling elements. The design integrates the segments into the shaft structure to provide a compact, self-supporting bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/504] with two segments, e.g. two semicircular cage parts -- The subclass F16C 33/504 covers bearing cages that are formed from two separate segments, such as a pair of semicircular parts that together form the cage structure. This design is used to simplify assembly, allow thermal expansion, or provide easier access to the bearing interior. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically within shaft elements and bearing accessories.
[F16C 33/506] formed as a flexible belt -- F16C 33/506 covers shaft accessories or bearing details that are formed as flexible belts, allowing rotary motion or load support to be transmitted through an elastic, looped element rather than a rigid shaft.
[F16C 33/508] formed of links having an H-shape, i.e. links with a single stay placed between two rollers and with two end portions extending along the end faces of the two rollers -- F16C 33/508 classifies bearing accessories where the supporting links are H-shaped, consisting of a single central stay positioned between two rollers and two end portions that extend along the rollers' end faces, forming a link structure for mechanical shafts.
[F16C 33/51] formed of unconnected members -- F16C 33/51 covers bearing accessories that are assembled from separate, non-connected components such as cages, retainers or inserts that hold rolling elements in place. These parts are not integral to the shaft or bearing housing.
[F16C 33/513] formed of arcuate segments for carrying one or more rollers -- F16C 33/513 designates shaft accessories formed from arcuate segment pieces that together create a carrier for one or more rollers, used to support or guide rotating components within bearing assemblies.
[F16C 33/516] with two segments, e.g. double-split cages with two semicircular parts -- F16C 33/516 covers bearing accessories that feature double-split cages formed from two semicircular segments. These components are designed to hold rolling elements in a shaft-type bearing and provide improved load distribution or ease of assembly. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 33/52] with no part entering between, or touching, the bearing surfaces of the rollers -- The CPC subclass F16C 33/52 covers devices in which rollers are supported in a bearing without any component passing between or touching the bearing surfaces of the rollers. It relates to shaft-related mechanical engineering elements where the bearing design eliminates interfering parts.
[F16C 33/523] with pins extending into holes or bores on the axis of the rollers -- The entry F16C 33/523 classifies bearing accessories that use pins projecting into holes or bores positioned on the roller axis, securing or locating the rollers within shafts or other mechanical elements.
[F16C 33/526] extending through the rollers and joining two lateral cage parts -- F16C 33/526 refers to bearing accessories that pass through the rollers of a cage and serve to join two lateral cage parts, creating a continuous structural link across the shaft. This element is used in mechanical engineering to improve alignment and stability of rotary bearings.
[F16C 33/54] made from wire, strips, or sheet metal -- The subclass F16C 33/54 applies to bearings or bearing components that are manufactured from wire, strip or sheet metal, often produced by stamping, weaving or rolling thin metal forms, and it falls under shaft-related details within mechanical engineering.
[F16C 33/541] Details of individual pockets, e.g. shape or roller retaining means -- F16C 33/541 pertains to the design of the individual pockets within bearing assemblies, including their shape and the means for retaining rollers or balls. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, details or accessories of bearings, covering specific features that define how rolling elements are housed and guided.
[F16C 33/542] made from sheet metal -- F16C 33/542 covers bearing details or accessories made from sheet-metal, such as rings, caps, liners or other fittings that are attached to shafts. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts.
[F16C 33/543] from a single part -- F16C 33/543 pertains to bearing details or accessories that are produced as a single-part and mounted on a shaft. Examples include integrated cages, seals, retainers or other components formed in one piece to support or protect the bearing.
[F16C 33/545] rolled from a band -- F16C 33/545 relates to shafts that are formed by rolling a metal band into a solid cylindrical shape, used as details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/546] with a M- or W-shaped cross section -- The CPC subclass F16C 33/546 designates shafts or bearing components that feature an M- or W-shaped cross-section. This geometry is employed to enhance stiffness, load distribution or to accommodate specific mounting requirements in mechanical engineering applications. It falls under the broader categories of engineering elements, units and shafts.
[F16C 33/547] from two parts, e.g. two discs or rings joined together -- F16C 33/547 covers bearing accessories that are formed from two separate elements, such as two discs or rings that are joined together to create a single component. The entry applies to details or attachments for shafts where the combined parts function as a unit within a bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/548] with more than three parts, e.g. two end rings connected by a plurality of stays or pins -- F16C 33/548 covers bearing accessories composed of more than three components, such as two end rings linked by a plurality of stays or pins. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and deals with complex multi-part bearing arrangements.
[F16C 33/56] Selection of substances -- F16C 33/56 deals with the criteria and methods for selecting materials used in bearing components and related accessories, emphasizing properties such as wear resistance, friction, corrosion protection and load-bearing capacity. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/565] Coatings -- F16C 33/565 relates to protective or functional coatings applied to bearing components and associated shaft accessories. It covers surface treatments, plating or films that improve wear resistance, corrosion protection, lubrication or friction reduction of bearings and their supporting parts.
[F16C 33/58] Raceways -- F16C 33/58 designates raceways, the precisely contoured tracks that guide rolling elements within a bearing and form part of the bearing housing or related accessories. The code is situated under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/581] integral with other parts, e.g. with housings or machine elements such as shafts or gear wheels -- The CPC subclass F16C 33/581 covers bearing accessories that are manufactured as a single, integral component together with other parts such as housings, shafts or gear wheels, rather than being separate items. It applies to designs where the bearing element and the adjoining machine element are formed as one piece to improve assembly, strength or functionality.
[F16C 33/583] Details of specific parts of races -- F16C 33/583 relates to the detailed components of bearing races, including specific profile modifications, inserts, protective elements or other parts that form the raceway for rotating shafts. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/585] of raceways, e.g. ribs to guide the rollers -- F16C 33/585 covers features added to bearing raceways that help direct the motion of rollers, such as ribs or other guiding elements. These accessories improve the stability and load distribution of rolling-element bearings by shaping the raceway surface. They are a subcategory of shaft-related bearing details within the mechanical engineering classification.
[F16C 33/586] outside the space between the races, e.g. end faces or bore of inner ring -- F16C 33/586 deals with bearing components that are situated outside the space between the races, for example modifications to the end faces or the bore of the inner ring. These elements are not part of the rolling contact zone but serve to support, seal, or adjust the bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/588] Races of sheet metal -- The entry covers bearing races made from sheet-metal material, including their design, formation and methods of manufacturing. It addresses the use of stamped or cut sheet-metal components as the inner or outer race in shafts and related assemblies, specifying forms, dimensions and fastening features for mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 33/60] divided -- F16C 33/60 pertains to shafts that are divided into separate parts, typically to accommodate bearing components or to allow assembly around other elements. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the category of engineering elements and units dealing with shafts and bearing accessories. The class covers designs where a shaft is split or segmented for functional or manufacturing purposes.
[F16C 33/605] with a separate retaining member, e.g. flange, shoulder, guide ring, secured to a race ring, adjacent to the race surface, so as to abut the end of the rolling elements, e.g. rollers, or the cage -- F16C 33/605 concerns bearing accessories in which a separate retaining member such as a flange, shoulder or guide ring is attached to the race ring near the race surface to stop the ends of rolling elements like rollers or the cage.
[F16C 33/61] formed by wires -- This CPC subgroup covers bearing components that are manufactured as wire-formed elements, such as wire cages, wire rings or other wire-shaped parts used to support or retain rolling elements within a bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/62] Selection of substances -- The F16C 33/62 entry covers the criteria and methods for selecting materials used in bearing components and accessories, such as alloys, polymers, and lubricants, to achieve desired performance characteristics like load capacity, wear resistance, and corrosion protection. It guides engineers in choosing optimal substances for shaft-bearing assemblies.
[F16C 33/64] Special methods of manufacture -- F16C 33/64 covers special manufacturing techniques for details or accessories of bearings, including unconventional processes, material modifications, or advanced treatments used to produce rollers, cages, seals and other bearing components. It addresses methods that differ from standard machining or forming in order to achieve specific performance or design objectives.
[F16C 33/66] Special parts or details in view of lubrication -- F16C 33/66 pertains to special parts or details of shaft bearings designed to facilitate lubrication. It includes features such as oil grooves, pockets, channels or other devices that distribute lubricant to the bearing surfaces, improving performance and reducing wear.
[F16C 33/6603] with grease as lubricant -- F16C 33/6603 covers bearing components that are supplied with grease as the lubricant. It pertains to accessories or details of bearings used in shafts within mechanical engineering.
[F16C 33/6607] Retaining the grease in or near the bearing -- The classification F16C 33/6607 pertains to bearing designs that incorporate features to keep grease confined within the bearing or in its immediate vicinity, preventing lubricant loss during operation. It includes seals, caps, labyrinths or other retaining mechanisms that maintain effective lubrication of the bearing.
[F16C 33/6611] in a porous or resinous body, e.g. a cage impregnated with the grease -- The code covers bearing cages made of a porous or resinous material that are pre-impregnated with grease, providing built-in lubrication for rotating shafts. Such cages gradually release grease to the rolling elements, extending bearing life and eliminating separate lubrication steps.
[F16C 33/6614] in recesses or cavities provided in retainers, races or rolling elements -- The subclass F16C 33/6614 refers to details or accessories of bearings that are placed in recesses or cavities formed in retainers, races or rolling elements. It captures designs where components such as pins, plugs or sealing elements are integrated into these internal pockets to improve bearing performance or assembly.
[F16C 33/6618] in a reservoir in the sealing means -- This CPC subclass F16C 33/6618 covers bearing shafts that incorporate a reservoir as part of the sealing arrangement. It relates to details or accessories of bearings where the sealing means includes a fluid-holding cavity to improve lubrication or prevent contamination. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → shafts.
[F16C 33/6622] Details of supply and/or removal of the grease, e.g. purging grease -- The class covers devices or methods for supplying or removing grease in bearing assemblies, including grease purging, lubricating ports, injection or extraction mechanisms, and related accessories that facilitate grease flow to or from shafts.
[F16C 33/6625] Controlling or conditioning the grease supply -- F16C 33/6625 covers devices or mechanisms that regulate or condition the grease supply to bearings, including flow control, metering and temperature regulation to maintain optimal lubrication performance. These solutions are applied to shafts and related bearing components to ensure reliable operation and reduce wear.
[F16C 33/6629] Details of distribution or circulation inside the bearing, e.g. grooves on the cage or passages in the rolling elements -- F16C 33/6629 classifies internal fluid-distribution features of bearings, such as grooves cut into the cage or passages formed in the rolling elements that guide oil, grease or coolant throughout the bearing.
[F16C 33/6633] Grease properties or compositions, e.g. rheological properties -- The CPC subclass F16C 33/6633 relates to the properties and formulation of greases used with bearing accessories, especially shafts and other mechanical engineering elements. It covers aspects such as rheology, viscosity, thixotropy and other performance characteristics that affect lubrication and bearing operation.
[F16C 33/6637] with liquid lubricant -- F16C 33/6637 relates to shafts that incorporate bearing accessories or details which are supplied with a liquid lubricant. The classification covers designs in which oil, grease or other fluid is used to lubricate bearings mounted on or integral to the shaft for reduced friction and wear.
[F16C 33/664] Retaining the liquid in or near the bearing -- The invention relates to solutions for keeping lubricant or other liquids confined in or close to a bearing, such as seals, caps, reservoirs or other structures that prevent fluid loss from the bearing assembly. It addresses methods and devices that maintain a stable liquid film around the bearing during operation.
[F16C 33/6644] by a magnetic field acting on a magnetic liquid -- F16C 33/6644 relates to shaft bearings or their accessories that employ a magnetic field acting on a magnetic liquid, such as ferrofluid, to create a lubricating film, provide damping, or support loads. The classification covers devices where the magnetic field controls the liquid's behaviour to enhance bearing performance.
[F16C 33/6648] in a porous or resinous body, e.g. a cage impregnated with the liquid -- The code F16C 33/6648 covers bearing accessories such as cages that are formed from a porous or resin-based material and are impregnated with a liquid, typically for lubrication or sealing purposes. It relates to shafts and their detailed elements where the porous body or resinous cage retains the liquid to improve bearing performance.
[F16C 33/6651] in recesses or cavities provided in retainers, races or rolling elements -- F16C 33/6651 relates to bearing designs that incorporate recesses or cavities formed in retainers, races or rolling elements. Such features are used to hold lubrication, accommodate sensors, or provide mounting points, improving load distribution and service life of the bearing. The code falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/6655] in a reservoir in the sealing means -- The CPC subclass F16C 33/6655 relates to bearing accessories that incorporate a reservoir within the sealing means, such as seals attached to a shaft bearing that contain a lubricating-oil or grease reservoir to continuously feed the bearing surfaces. This feature is applied to improve lubrication retention and reduce contamination in mechanical shaft assemblies.
[F16C 33/6659] Details of supply of the liquid to the bearing, e.g. passages or nozzles -- F16C 33/6659 relates to means for supplying liquid to a bearing, such as passages, channels or nozzles that provide lubrication or cooling during operation. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/6662] the liquid being carried by air or other gases, e.g. mist lubrication -- This subclass concerns bearing lubrication in which the liquid is transported by a stream of air or other gases, such as mist or aerosol lubrication. It includes devices and methods that generate and direct a lubricating mist to the bearing surfaces.
[F16C 33/6666] from an oil bath in the bearing housing, e.g. by an oil ring or centrifugal disc -- The subclass F16C 33/6666 concerns bearings that receive lubrication directly from an oil bath contained in the bearing housing, employing devices such as oil rings or centrifugal discs to feed oil onto the bearing surfaces. It addresses the means of extracting and delivering oil from the housing's oil reservoir to ensure continuous lubrication of the shaft or rotating element.
[F16C 33/667] related to conditioning, e.g. cooling, filtering -- F16C 33/667 covers accessories for bearings that condition the working fluid, such as devices for cooling, filtering, or other fluid treatment directly associated with the shaft. It includes mechanisms that manage temperature, remove contaminants, or otherwise maintain fluid quality to ensure reliable bearing performance.
[F16C 33/6674] related to the amount supplied, e.g. gaps to restrict flow of the liquid -- F16C 33/6674 refers to bearing accessories that regulate the quantity of fluid supplied, such as designed gaps or clearances that limit liquid flow through or around the bearing, affecting lubrication or cooling in shaft assemblies.
[F16C 33/6677] from radial inside, e.g. via a passage through the shaft and/or inner ring -- This CPC subclass F16C 33/6677 concerns bearing details or accessories that are introduced from the radial interior of a shaft, for example through a passage drilled in the shaft or the inner race. It covers components inserted radially inward to support, adjust or transmit loads within the bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/6681] Details of distribution or circulation inside the bearing, e.g. grooves on the cage or passages in the rolling elements -- The code F16C 33/6681 covers features that create internal fluid or lubricant pathways within a bearing, such as grooves machined on the cage or passages formed in the rolling elements, to improve distribution or circulation of the lubricating medium.
[F16C 33/6685] Details of collecting or draining, e.g. returning the liquid to a sump -- F16C 33/6685 relates to bearing accessories that collect or drain lubricant, including channels or mechanisms that return the liquid to a sump. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts.
[F16C 33/6688] Lubricant compositions or properties, e.g. viscosity -- The class covers lubricating substances or formulations intended for bearings on shafts, emphasizing their composition and key properties such as viscosity that influence bearing operation and performance. It includes details on how these lubricants are selected, applied, and evaluated to ensure proper friction reduction and wear protection.
[F16C 33/6692] Liquids other than oil, e.g. water, refrigerants, liquid metal -- This CPC subclass F16C33/6692 covers bearing accessories designed for use with non-oil liquids such as water, refrigerants or liquid metals. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically shafts and bearing details, and addresses the handling, lubrication or cooling functions for bearing systems employing these alternative fluids.
[F16C 33/6696] with solids as lubricant, e.g. dry coatings, powder -- The subclass F16C 33/6696 deals with bearing details or accessories that employ solid lubricants-such as dry coatings or powder particles-to reduce friction and wear, instead of liquid oils or greases, in shaft-bearing assemblies.
[F16C 33/72] Sealings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bearings with details of the sealing arrangements.  Seals specifically constructed to be incorporated in or which are directly associated with bearings.
[F16C 33/723] Shaft end sealing means, e.g. cup-shaped caps or covers -- This subclass covers devices that seal the end of a rotating shaft, such as cup-shaped caps, covers or other end-sealing means that prevent the entry of fluids, dust or debris while allowing the shaft to turn.
