[F16C 33/726] with means to vent the interior of the bearing -- The code F16C 33/726 designates bearings that include a means to vent the interior, allowing trapped fluids or gases to escape and thus preventing pressure buildup while improving lubrication. It is categorized under mechanical engineering, shafts and bearing accessories.
[F16C 33/74] of sliding-contact bearings -- F16C 33/74 covers details or accessories related to sliding-contact bearings, such as liners, guides, sealing elements or other components that improve performance, reduce wear or facilitate installation of the bearing within a shaft system. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts, focusing on supplemental parts that support or enhance sliding-contact bearing operation.
[F16C 33/741] by means of a fluid -- F16C 33/741 covers shafts that are equipped with bearing accessories operated or lubricated by a fluid, such as hydraulic or pneumatic devices that transmit power, control motion, or provide cooling. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and shafts, addressing fluid-based means for bearing support or actuation.
[F16C 33/743] retained in the sealing gap -- F16C 33/743 covers components or arrangements that are retained within the sealing gap of a bearing assembly, ensuring the seal remains effective while allowing movement of the shaft. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/745] by capillary action -- The CPC subclass F16C 33/745 covers accessories or details of bearings that employ capillary action to move or retain lubricating fluid, typically on shafts. It includes devices that use capillary forces for lubrication, sealing or fluid distribution within bearing assemblies.
[F16C 33/746] by a magnetic field -- The F16C 33/746 subclass covers bearings or related accessories whose operation, control, or load-support is achieved by a magnetic field. It includes magnetic bearings, magnetic damping, or field-controlled lubrication systems used on rotating shafts, and falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → shafts → details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/748] flowing to or from the sealing gap, e.g. vacuum seals with differential exhaust -- F16C 33/748 pertains to mechanical engineering, specifically addressing details and accessories of bearings related to fluid dynamics in sealing gaps. This classification covers mechanisms where fluids flow into or out of these gaps, including designs that utilize vacuum effects for enhanced sealing performance through differential exhaust systems.
[F16C 33/76] of ball or roller bearings -- F16C 33/76 covers accessories and auxiliary components designed for ball or roller bearings, such as seals, cages, retainers, lubrication devices, or mounting elements that support, protect, or adjust bearing operation. It falls within the shaft category of mechanical engineering, focusing on details that enhance bearing performance or facilitate integration into machinery.
[F16C 33/761] specifically for bearings with purely axial load -- F16C 33/761 is a CPC subclass within mechanical engineering that addresses details or accessories of bearings, specifically those designed to support purely axial loads. It covers design features, mounting arrangements and related components for axially loaded bearing applications.
[F16C 33/762] by means of a fluid -- F16C 33/762 pertains to details or accessories of bearings, specifically those that involve the use of a fluid for operational purposes such as lubrication or cooling. This classification is part of the broader categories of mechanical engineering and engineering elements and units, focusing on shafts and their components.
[F16C 33/763] retained in the sealing gap -- F16C 33/763 is a CPC subclass in the Mechanical Engineering section that deals with details or accessories of bearings, specifically elements that are retained in the sealing gap of a shaft assembly to protect or support the bearing.
[F16C 33/765] by a magnetic field -- F16C 33/765 covers shafts, spindles or axles that incorporate bearing accessories which are driven or controlled by a magnetic field, using magnetic forces to support, lift, position or otherwise influence the bearing operation.
[F16C 33/766] by pumping action -- F16C 33/766 designates devices that lubricate or otherwise treat bearing surfaces by a pumping action, covering details or accessories of bearings that actively deliver fluid, grease, or other lubricants into the bearing clearance to maintain performance and reduce wear.
[F16C 33/767] integral with the race -- F16C 33/767 pertains to shafts or similar members that incorporate the bearing race as an integral part of the component, eliminating the need for a separate race. It addresses designs where the race is formed directly on or within the shaft, simplifying assembly and improving load transmission.
[F16C 33/768] between relatively stationary parts, i.e. static seals -- F16C 33/768 relates to static seals positioned between relatively stationary parts of shaft assemblies, typically used as accessories of bearings. The entry falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/78] with a diaphragm, disc, or ring, with or without resilient members -- A shaft bearing accessory that incorporates a diaphragm, disc or ring, optionally combined with resilient elements, to control lubrication, pressure or vibration. This design is used in mechanical engineering applications where precise bearing performance is required. It falls under the CPC class F16C 33/78 for such bearing details.
[F16C 33/7803] suited for particular types of rolling bearings -- F16C 33/7803 relates to components-typically shafts or accessories-specifically adapted to particular types of rolling bearings, ensuring proper fit, load transmission and optimal performance for the selected bearing design.
[F16C 33/7806] for spherical roller bearings -- F16C 33/7806 covers spherical roller bearings, a type of bearing whose outer ring has a spherical raceway that permits angular misalignment between the shaft and housing. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Details or accessories of bearings, addressing design and application of these bearings for load handling and self-aligning capabilities.
[F16C 33/7809] for needle roller bearings -- F16C 33/7809 is a CPC subclass that deals with details or accessories specifically for needle-roller bearings, a type of rolling-element bearing used on shafts in mechanical systems. It falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts.
[F16C 33/7813] for tapered roller bearings -- F16C 33/7813 is a CPC subclass that deals with accessories and detailed components for tapered-roller bearings, such as cages, retainers, seals and lubrication devices. It falls under the shaft and bearing elements category of mechanical engineering and is used by both the EPO and USPTO.
[F16C 33/7816] Details of the sealing or parts thereof, e.g. geometry, material -- The CPC subclass F16C 33/7816 concerns the design and characteristics of sealing elements that are part of bearing assemblies, including their geometry, material selection and related components. It is positioned within mechanical engineering under engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/782] of the sealing region -- The CPC subclass F16C 33/782 covers details and accessories of bearings that pertain to the sealing region of a shaft assembly. It includes designs for seals, labyrinths and other sealing components integrated with or attached to bearing housings.
[F16C 33/7823] of sealing lips -- The subclass F16C 33/7823 covers sealing lips that are fitted to bearing components, describing their form, material and function for preventing lubricant loss and contaminant ingress. It is situated under shafts within engineering elements and units of mechanical engineering.
[F16C 33/7826] of the opposing surface cooperating with the seal, e.g. a shoulder surface of a bearing ring -- F16C 33/7826 covers features of a bearing in which the surface opposite the seal, for example a shoulder surface on a bearing ring, is shaped to cooperate with the sealing element. It pertains to details or accessories of shafts that enhance sealing performance.
[F16C 33/783] of the mounting region -- F16C 33/783 covers components and accessories that form or are attached to the mounting region of a bearing on a shaft, including sleeves, retainers, spacers, seals or other elements that secure the bearing in place within mechanical engineering assemblies.
[F16C 33/7833] Special methods of manufacture -- F16C 33/7833 pertains to specialised manufacturing techniques for details or accessories of bearings, covering processes that produce or modify bearing components to achieve particular functional or performance characteristics. It lies within the shaft sub-group of engineering elements in the mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F16C 33/7836] floating with respect to both races -- F16C 33/7836 is a CPC entry under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings. It covers bearing accessories that enable a component to float with respect to both inner and outer races, improving load distribution and reducing friction.
[F16C 33/784] mounted to a groove in the inner surface of the outer race and extending toward the inner race -- F16C 33/784 pertains to bearing accessories that are installed in a groove machined on the inner surface of the outer race and project toward the inner race. Typical examples include retainers, cages or other devices that secure rolling elements within the bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/7843] with a single annular sealing disc -- A shaft design that incorporates a single annular sealing disc as a bearing accessory, aimed at retaining lubricants or excluding contaminants. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → shafts → details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/7846] with a gap between the annular disc and the inner race -- The subclass deals with bearing components that incorporate a deliberate clearance between the annular disc and the inner race, allowing space for lubrication, thermal expansion or debris accommodation. Such a gap is typical in certain thrust or rotary bearing designs where controlled movement between the disc and race improves performance and durability. This falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and details or accessories of bearings.
[F16C 33/785] Bearing shields made of sheet metal -- A bearing shield fabricated from sheet metal is a detail or accessory that protects the rolling elements and raceways of a bearing from contamination, debris, and lubricant loss. It is classified under mechanical engineering, specifically within engineering elements and units for shafts, as a component that encloses or covers the bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/7853] with one or more sealing lips to contact the inner race -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16C 33/7853 pertains to mechanical engineering, specifically addressing details or accessories of bearings. It focuses on designs that include one or more sealing lips intended to make contact with the inner race, enhancing the bearing's protection against contaminants and lubricant retention.
[F16C 33/7856] with a single sealing lip -- F16C 33/7856 is a subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification covering details or accessories of bearings that include a single sealing lip. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts. This code is used by the EPO and USPTO to identify patents relating to shafts with a single sealing lip for bearing protection.
[F16C 33/7859] with a further sealing element -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16C 33/7859 pertains to details or accessories of bearings, specifically addressing designs that incorporate an additional sealing element. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office for organizing patent documents related to mechanical engineering, particularly those involving shafts and bearing components.
[F16C 33/7863] mounted to the inner race, e.g. a flinger to use centrifugal effect -- F16C 33/7863 covers devices mounted on the inner race of a bearing, such as flingers that create a centrifugal force to expel contaminants or lubricants. These accessories are intended to improve bearing performance by using the rotation of the shaft to generate a cleaning or sealing action.
[F16C 33/7866] with sealing lips -- F16C 33/7866 designates bearing accessories that incorporate sealing lips, used to retain lubrication and protect against contaminants. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for shafts, and covers details that enhance bearing performance and longevity.
[F16C 33/7869] mounted with a cylindrical portion to the inner surface of the outer race and having a radial portion extending inward -- F16C 33/7869 pertains to bearing accessories that are attached to the inner surface of the outer race. The device includes a cylindrical portion that fits against the race and a radial portion that projects inward toward the inner race, forming a detail of shafts in mechanical engineering.
[F16C 33/7873] with a single sealing ring of generally L-shaped cross-section -- F16C 33/7873 classifies shafts equipped with a single sealing ring that has a generally L-shaped cross-section, serving as a bearing accessory to provide sealing while allowing rotation.
[F16C 33/7876] with sealing lips -- F16C 33/7876 designates bearing accessories that incorporate sealing lips to retain lubricant and keep out contaminants on rotating shafts. It is a sub-category of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, specifically detailing sealing features for bearings.
[F16C 33/7879] with a further sealing ring -- F16C 33/7879 pertains to shafts that include an additional sealing ring as a bearing accessory. It classifies designs where a further sealing element is integrated with the shaft to improve protection against contaminants and retain lubrication.
[F16C 33/7883] mounted to the inner race and of generally L-shape, the two sealing rings defining a sealing with box-shaped cross-section -- The code F16C 33/7883 designates a bearing accessory mounted on the inner race that has an overall L-shape, using two sealing rings that together create a box-shaped cross-section to form a seal.
[F16C 33/7886] mounted outside the gap between the inner and outer races, e.g. sealing rings mounted to an end face or outer surface of a race -- The code classifies bearing accessories that are fitted on the exterior of the space between the inner and outer races, such as sealing rings attached to an end face or the outer surface of a race. These components are used to protect, retain or control lubricants in shafts and other mechanical-engineering applications.
[F16C 33/7889] mounted to an inner race and extending toward the outer race -- F16C 33/7889 refers to bearing accessories that are fixed to the inner race and project outward toward the outer race, such as extensions, collars or positioning elements that influence load transfer or alignment. These components are typically integrated with shafts to provide support, damping or precise positioning within the bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/7893] mounted to a cage or integral therewith -- F16C 33/7893 refers to bearing accessories that are mounted on, or formed as an integral part of, the bearing cage. It includes cage-mounted features such as integrated lubrication, sealing or mounting elements that become part of the shaft-bearing assembly.
[F16C 33/7896] with two or more discrete sealings arranged in series -- F16C 33/7896 pertains to shafts equipped with two or more separate sealing elements positioned in series to protect the bearing assembly. This arrangement provides enhanced containment of lubricants and exclusion of contaminants by using multiple seals in a consecutive configuration.
[F16C 33/80] Labyrinth sealings -- Labyrinth sealings are non-contact sealing arrangements mounted on rotating shafts to restrict the passage of fluids or gases, typically as accessories of bearings. They employ a series of interlocking teeth or ridges that create a tortuous path, limiting leakage while allowing continuous shaft rotation.
[F16C 33/805] in addition to other sealings, e.g. dirt guards to protect sealings with sealing lips -- F16C 33/805 pertains to additional protective features for seals in mechanical engineering, specifically focusing on components like dirt guards that safeguard sealing lips from contaminants and wear. This classification is part of the broader category dealing with details or accessories of bearings within the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F16C 33/82] Arrangements for electrostatic or magnetic action against dust or other particles -- The subclass F16C 33/82 covers arrangements that employ electrostatic or magnetic fields to repel, attract, collect or otherwise act on dust, powders or other particulate matter, typically in conjunction with shafts, bearings or related mechanical elements to keep surfaces clean or maintain performance.
[F16C 35/00] Rigid support of bearing units -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mounting of a bearing to surrounding parts in a fixed way.  Housings such as bearings shields which can be handled as a unit together with the bearing.
[F16C 35/02] in the case of sliding-contact bearings -- F16C 35/02 covers shafts that serve as a rigid support for sliding-contact bearings, detailing the mounting, housing and alignment methods that secure the bearing on the shaft while ensuring proper load transmission and limiting deflection. It addresses design features that maintain shaft rigidity in bearing applications.
[F16C 35/04] in the case of ball or roller bearings -- The code F16C 35/04 concerns the rigid support structures for bearing units that employ ball or roller bearings, covering housings, flanges, backs and other fixed elements that secure the bearing to a shaft or machine frame. It addresses the design and arrangement of these supports to ensure proper alignment and load transmission in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 35/042] Housings for rolling element bearings for rotary movement -- Housings for rolling-element bearings designed for rotary movement are rigid enclosures that support and retain bearing units while allowing smooth rotation. They protect the bearings from contamination, transmit axial and radial loads, and secure the bearing assembly within shafts or other mechanical structures.
[F16C 35/045] with a radial flange to mount the housing -- F16C 35/045 pertains to rigid supports for bearing units that incorporate a radial flange used to attach or mount the bearing housing, facilitating secure positioning within mechanical assemblies.
[F16C 35/047] with a base plate substantially parallel to the axis of rotation, e.g. horizontally mounted pillow blocks -- The subclass F16C 35/047 pertains to rigid shaft supports that incorporate a base plate positioned substantially parallel to the shaft's axis, as found in horizontally mounted pillow-block bearing units. It classifies bearing housings where the supporting plate lies in a horizontal plane relative to the rotating shaft.
[F16C 35/06] Mounting of ball or roller bearings -- The subclass F16C 35/06 relates to the mounting of ball or roller bearings on shafts, emphasizing rigid supports that secure bearing units in place. It covers fixtures, arrangements and methods for fixing bearings to shafts or housings to maintain alignment and transmit loads.
[F16C 35/061] mounting a plurality of bearings side by side -- F16C 35/061 covers the arrangement and mounting of several bearings placed side by side to form a rigid support for a shaft or other rotating element. The classification includes designs that align bearings in parallel or series so they share loads and maintain precise positioning within the supporting structure.
[F16C 35/062] Dismounting of ball or roller bearings -- F16C 35/062 concerns methods and devices for removing ball or roller bearings from shafts, housings or other rigid supports. It addresses dismounting techniques that separate the bearing elements without damaging the supporting structure, falling under the subclass for rigid support of bearing units.
[F16C 35/063] Fixing them on the shaft -- F16C 35/063 deals with the means of securing bearing units directly to a rotating shaft. It covers clamps, keys, set screws, welding, adhesives and similar fastening methods that provide a rigid, non-removable or releasable connection, ensuring proper load transmission and alignment.
[F16C 35/0635] the bore of the inner ring being of special non-cylindrical shape which co-operates with a complementary shape on the shaft, e.g. teeth, polygonal sections -- This CPC subclass covers shafts whose inner-ring bore is formed with a special non-cylindrical shape that mates with a complementary shape on the shaft, such as teeth or polygonal sections, to provide enhanced torque transmission or positional stability.
[F16C 35/067] Fixing them in a housing -- F16C 35/067 covers the fixing of rigid support shafts that hold bearing units within a housing, detailing the means of securing the shaft to the surrounding structure. It includes arrangements such as screws, pins, clamps or bonding that immobilize the shaft while allowing it to transmit loads to the bearing assembly. This subclass is part of the mechanical engineering domain dealing with shaft supports.
[F16C 35/07] Fixing them on the shaft or housing with interposition of an element -- F16C 35/07 covers methods of securing bearing units to a shaft or its housing by placing an intermediate component such as a spacer, sleeve or locking element between them. This approach provides a rigid support for the bearing while allowing assembly or adjustment.
[F16C 35/073] between shaft and inner race ring -- F16C 35/073 concerns rigid supports that join a shaft directly to the inner race of a bearing, forming a fixed bearing assembly with no relative movement. It includes designs using sleeves, brackets, clamps or similar elements to hold the shaft against the inner race and maintain alignment and preload.
[F16C 35/077] between housing and outer race ring -- F16C 35/077 refers to a rigid support located between a bearing housing and the outer race ring of the bearing. The support typically takes the form of a collar, sleeve or similar element that transfers loads directly from the housing to the outer race, ensuring precise alignment and load-bearing capacity.
[F16C 35/078] using pressure fluid as mounting aid -- This subclass covers methods that use hydraulic or pneumatic pressure fluid to aid the mounting of shafts or bearing units, employing the fluid as a temporary support, cushion or alignment aid during installation, which simplifies positioning and reduces friction or mis-alignment.
[F16C 35/08] for spindles -- F16C 35/08 pertains to the rigid support of bearing units specifically for spindles, falling under mechanical engineering shaft elements. It addresses design features that securely mount spindle bearings to ensure stable, precise operation.
[F16C 35/10] with sliding-contact bearings -- F16C 35/10 covers rigid supports for shafts that incorporate sliding-contact bearings, where the rotating element rests on a surface that slides rather than rolls, such as journal or plain bearings. These designs provide stable bearing support while allowing axial and radial loads to be transmitted through metal-to-metal contact, often used in heavy-duty machinery.
[F16C 35/12] with ball or roller bearings -- F16C 35/12 relates to shafts that are rigidly supported by bearing units incorporating ball or roller bearings. The classification covers designs where the bearing elements are integral to the shaft support, such as hollow shafts, flanged sections, or tapered sleeves that house the rolling elements to transmit loads while allowing rotation.
[F16C 37/00] Cooling of bearings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means to remove heat from the bearing area, i.e. by radiation, conduction or heat exchange, excluding cooling lubricant or by means of lubricant (cf. groups F16C33/10 and F16C33/66 ).
[F16C 37/002] of fluid bearings -- The subclass F16C 37/002 deals with fluid-lubricated bearings that cool rotating shafts. It covers designs where a liquid or gas film supplies both lubrication and heat removal to the bearing surfaces, improving performance and extending service life of the shaft.
[F16C 37/005] of magnetic bearings -- F16C 37/005 concerns magnetic bearings employed in shaft systems to provide support without mechanical contact, thereby reducing friction and heat. The subclass includes designs where the magnetic bearing itself contributes to bearing cooling or operates in conjunction with cooling mechanisms.
[F16C 37/007] of rolling bearings -- F16C 37/007 concerns cooling of rolling bearings, i.e., techniques and devices that remove heat generated during operation to maintain performance and prolong life. It includes methods such as forced oil circulation, air flow, heat-conductive sleeves, and integrated cooling passages within the bearing or housing.
[F16C 39/00] Relieving load on bearings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means exerting a force on the relative moving part directed against the load and cooperating in the load direction with the actual bearing which attains the desired position.  Means providing a preload on the bearing, which could be considered as negative relief, actually serve to reduce play and are e.g. to be classified in the appropriate groups (e.g. F16C25/00 , F16C29/12 , F16C32/067 ).
[F16C 39/02] using mechanical means -- F16C 39/02 pertains to methods and devices for relieving the load on bearings by employing mechanical means, such as springs or levers, within the broader context of shafts in mechanical engineering. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office.
[F16C 39/04] using hydraulic or pneumatic means -- F16C 39/04 covers shafts in which the load on the bearings is reduced by hydraulic or pneumatic devices. It includes systems that use fluid pressure to support or balance the shaft, thereby lessening bearing stress during operation. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, relieving load on bearings.
[F16C 39/06] using magnetic means -- F16C 39/06 covers shaft arrangements that employ magnetic means to relieve bearing load. It includes magnetic bearings, magnetic suspension or levitation systems that reduce mechanical contact and friction, thereby improving efficiency and extending the service life of rotating components.
[F16C 39/063] Permanent magnets -- F16C 39/063 covers the application of permanent magnets to reduce or balance the forces acting on rotating shafts, thereby relieving bearing loads. It includes designs where magnetic attraction or repulsion provides axial or radial support, minimizing friction and wear on the bearing assembly.
[F16C 39/066] with opposing permanent magnets repelling each other -- F16C 39/066 refers to shaft arrangements that incorporate opposing permanent magnets arranged to repel each other, creating a magnetic thrust that helps support the shaft. This magnetic support lessens the mechanical load carried by conventional bearings, improving efficiency and reducing wear.
[F16C 41/00] Other accessories, -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Elements which are not directly related to the bearing function, i.e. parts mounted to or integrated with the bearing but providing an additional functionality such as speed sensors, as far as there integration in the bearing is of relevance.  Load-equalizing elements.  Elements involved in protecting the bearing when not in use.
[F16C 41/001] Integrated brakes or clutches for stopping or coupling the relatively movable parts -- F16C 41/001 covers devices that combine a brake and a clutch into a single unit to either stop or connect rotating shafts or other relatively movable components. These integrated mechanisms are used where space or weight savings are important and can apply torque transmission or dissipation in one assembly.
[F16C 41/002] Conductive elements, e.g. to prevent static electricity -- The F16C 41/002 subclass covers conductive accessories mounted on shafts to eliminate or reduce static electricity build-up. It includes items such as conductive rings, brushes, coatings or other devices that provide a path for charge dissipation along rotating or sliding shafts. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts.
[F16C 41/004] Electro-dynamic machines, e.g. motors, generators, actuators -- F16C 41/004 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system covers electro-dynamic machines such as motors, generators, and actuators, specifically focusing on their integration with shafts and related accessories. This classification is used by the European Patent Office (EPO) and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO).
[F16C 41/005] Fluid passages not relating to lubrication or cooling -- The CPC subclass F16C 41/005 falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Other accessories. It covers designs of fluid passages incorporated in shaft-related components that convey fluids other than lubricants or cooling media, such as hydraulic or pneumatic lines integrated in the shaft assembly.
[F16C 41/007] Encoders, e.g. parts with a plurality of alternating magnetic poles -- F16C 41/007 pertains to encoders used as accessories for shafts, specifically devices that employ a series of alternating magnetic poles to generate position or speed signals for monitoring and control.
[F16C 41/008] Identification means, e.g. markings, RFID-tags; Data transfer means -- The subclass F16C 41/008 covers identification and data-transfer devices applied to shafts or their accessories, such as visual markings, RFID tags, or other means that convey information about the shaft's type, condition, or ownership. These features enable automated recognition, tracking, or communication of relevant data linked to the mechanical element.
[F16C 41/02] Arrangements for equalising the load on a plurality of bearings or their elements -- This subclass concerns designs that distribute the load among several bearings or bearing components, using devices such as load-equalising rings, flexible couplings or supporting structures to balance forces and reduce uneven wear. It falls under mechanical engineering, shafts, other accessories.
[F16C 41/04] Preventing damage to bearings during storage or transport thereof or when otherwise out of use -- F16C 41/04 covers measures that protect bearings from damage while they are stored, transported, or otherwise not in service. It includes devices, packaging, or handling procedures designed to prevent contamination, deformation or premature wear of bearing surfaces. The class falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, other accessories.
[F16C 41/045] Devices for provisionally retaining needles or rollers in a bearing race before mounting of the bearing on a shaft -- These devices temporarily retain needle or roller elements within a bearing race during assembly, preventing their movement and ensuring proper alignment before the bearing is mounted on a shaft, thereby facilitating safe and accurate installation.
[F16C 43/00] Assembling bearings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Joining of the parts of a bearing, optionally simultaneously with mounting. It also embraces joining bearing parts which are split.  For a slide bearing this involves typically joining bearing bushes, shells and washers; optionally providing other parts such as seals.  For a rolling element bearing this involves typically providing rolling elements between the races, optionally providing other parts such as cages and seals.
[F16C 43/02] Assembling sliding-contact bearings -- F16C 43/02 covers methods and apparatus for assembling sliding-contact bearings, such as ball or roller bearings, onto shafts. The classification includes techniques for fitting, securing and aligning bearing races and rolling elements during installation.
[F16C 43/04] Assembling rolling-contact bearings -- F16C 43/04 relates to the assembly of rolling-contact bearings, covering techniques and apparatus for fitting bearing elements such as rollers or balls onto shafts, housings or races. It includes methods for inserting, positioning, and securing these components to achieve proper alignment and load distribution in mechanical systems.
[F16C 43/045] Mounting or replacing seals -- F16C 43/045 pertains to the techniques, devices and tools used to mount or replace seals on rotating shafts, especially within bearing assemblies. It includes methods for positioning, securing and aligning seals to ensure proper lubrication retention, prevent contaminant ingress and maintain reliable shaft operation.
[F16C 43/06] Placing rolling bodies in cages or bearings -- F16C 43/06 covers methods and apparatus for placing rolling elements such as balls or rollers into cages or bearing assemblies. It pertains to the manufacturing step of inserting the rolling bodies into the bearing structure, a subcategory of shaft-related mechanical engineering.
[F16C 43/065] in cages -- F16C 43/065 relates to the assembly of bearings in which the rolling elements are positioned inside cages that retain them in fixed locations, facilitating installation and operation. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for shafts, and addresses cage structures used in bearing construction.
[F16C 43/08] by deforming the cages or the races -- This subclass covers methods of assembling bearings in which the cage or the race is permanently deformed to secure the rolling elements. It includes techniques such as squeezing, bending or otherwise shaping the cage or race to lock the components in place.
[F16C 43/083] by plastic deformation of the cage -- The subgroup F16C 43/083 concerns bearing assembly methods in which the cage or retainer is produced or secured by plastically deforming the material, for example by stamping, pressing or reshaping the cage during installation. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, shafts, and assembling bearings.
[F16C 43/086] by plastic deformation of the race -- The code F16C 43/086 refers to methods of assembling bearings in which the bearing race is shaped or secured by plastic deformation. This includes processes such as rolling, pressing or swaging that permanently alter the race to fit the shaft or housing.
[F16C 5/00] Crossheads -- F16C 5/00 refers to crossheads, the sliding or hinged members that connect a reciprocating piston rod to a crank or connecting rod in mechanical engines and machines. They guide linear motion while allowing the conversion to rotary motion, and are classified under shafts within engineering elements and units.
[F16C 7/00] Connecting-rods or like links pivoted at both ends -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Heads rigid with cross heads -> F16C5/00 - Inhibiting shift in gearing during unfavourable conditions -> F16H61/16 - Pivotal connection of pistons with connecting-rods -> F16J1/14 - Piston-rods, i.e. rods rigidly connected to the piston -> F16J7/00
[F16C 7/02] Constructions of connecting-rods with constant length -- F16C 7/02 covers designs of connecting-rods or similar pivoted links whose length does not change during operation. These constructions are used in mechanisms such as reciprocating engines and compressors to transform linear motion while keeping a fixed distance between the pivot points, ensuring reliable power transmission.
[F16C 7/023] for piston engines, pumps or the like -- The CPC subclass F16C 7/023 pertains to connecting-rods or similarly pivoted links used in piston engines, pumps and comparable machines, focusing on the structural features that join reciprocating components within the shaft assembly of such mechanical systems.
[F16C 7/026] made of fibre reinforced resin -- This subclass covers connecting rods or similar pivoted links that are constructed from fiber-reinforced resin materials. It includes designs where the structural component is primarily a composite resin matrix reinforced with fibers for strength and weight reduction. The classification is used for patents describing such resin-based mechanical linkages.
[F16C 7/04] with elastic intermediate part of fluid cushion -- F16C 7/04 pertains to connecting-rods or similar links pivoted at both ends that incorporate an elastic intermediate portion filled with a fluid cushion, providing shock absorption and flexibility within the shaft assembly. This feature is used to reduce vibration and wear in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16C 7/06] Adjustable connecting-rods -- Adjustable connecting-rods are shaft elements that link two rotating parts, such as crankshafts and pistons, while allowing length or position changes to accommodate wear, thermal expansion, or variable geometry. They typically incorporate threaded, telescopic, or sliding sections that can be finely tuned during assembly or maintenance, improving engine efficiency and serviceability.
[F16C 7/08] made from sheet metal -- F16C 7/08 pertains to connecting rods or similar links that are pivoted at both ends and are fabricated from sheet metal. It falls under mechanical engineering, shaft elements, and addresses the manufacturing of these components using sheet metal forming techniques.
[F16C 9/00] Bearings for crankshafts or connecting-rods -- F16C 9/00 covers bearings designed specifically for use with crankshafts or connecting rods in mechanical power-transmission systems. These bearings are engineered to support rotating or reciprocating shafts and manage the loads generated by engine operation.
[F16C 9/02] Crankshaft bearings -- F16C 9/02 covers bearings designed specifically for supporting crankshafts or connecting-rods in engines and other rotating machinery. These bearings mitigate axial and radial loads on the shaft, and may be plain, roller, or hydrodynamic types engineered for high-speed, high-torque applications.
[F16C 9/03] Arrangements for adjusting play -- This subclass covers devices or mechanisms that permit fine adjustment of clearance or radial/axial play in crankshaft or connecting-rod bearings, such as shims, hydraulic adjusters, or eccentric sleeves. It relates to ensuring optimal bearing alignment and load distribution in mechanical power-transmission systems.
[F16C 9/04] Connecting-rod bearings -- F16C 9/04 is a CPC subclass for connecting-rod bearings, situated under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Shafts → Bearings for crankshafts or connecting-rods. It covers the design, construction and material choices of bearings that support connecting rods in reciprocating engines, including various bearing types, lubrication provisions and mounting methods.
[F16C 9/045] the bearing cap of the connecting rod being split by fracturing -- F16C 9/045 covers connecting-rod bearing caps that are intended to separate by fracturing. The design provides a controlled split of the cap to protect the crankcase and reduce damage in overload conditions. It is used in internal-combustion engines where the bearing cap serves as a sacrificial element.
[F16C 9/06] Arrangements for adjusting play in bearings, operating either automatically or not -- F16C 9/06 is a subclass of the mechanical engineering shaft category that relates to devices that adjust the clearance (play) in bearings used for crankshafts or connecting rods. The arrangements may be automatic, self-acting, or manually controlled to maintain proper bearing fit during operation.
[F16D] COUPLINGS FOR TRANSMITTING ROTATION -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems of clutches or couplings for transmitting rotation used within a mechanism of general utility.  Combinations of clutches and brakes used within a mechanism of general utility.  Combinations couplings for transmitting rotation from one element to another and brakes used in conjunction with each other in a mechanism of general utility.  Combinations of couplings for transmitting rotation from one element to another and a clutch used in conjunction with each other in a mechanism of general utility.  Couplings per se for transmitting rotation of the following types:  Clutches per se of the following types:  Clutch components or special features related to clutches:  Brakes per se of the following types:  Brake components or special features related to brakes:  Friction linings in general:  Other types of couplings for transmitting rotation, clutches or brakes not provided for in another subclass of the CPC.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electrodynamic brake systems for vehicles in general -> B60L7/00 - Vehicle brake control systems -> B60T - Conjoint control of vehicle sub-units of different type or different function, e.g. when at least one sub-unit is a clutch or a brake -> B60W - Fluid gearings -> F16H39/00 , F16H41/00 , F16H43/00 - Combinations of fluid gearings with clutches or couplings -> F16H45/00 - Combinations of mechanical gearings with fluid clutches -> F16H47/00 - Differential gearings, e.g. with freewheels or other clutches -> F16H48/00 - Dynamo-electric brakes or clutches, e.g. of the Eddy-current hysteresis type -> H02K49/00 - Clutches or holding devices using electrostatic attraction, e.g. using Johnson-Rahbek effect -> H02N13/00  **Glossary:** - actuator: a component of a mechanism that causes movement of a motion-conveying component or motion-inhibiting component but does not itself directly convey or inhibit motion. - brake: a mechanism with at least one component that is moved by an actuator to selectively or automatically engage or stop a member with respect to a stationary part. - clutch: a mechanism with at least two relatively movable components that are selectively or automatically engaged with each other by control means, e.g. an actuator, to transmit mechanical power or convey motion from a first member to a second member or disengaged by control means to stop transmitting mechanical power or conveying motion. - coupling for transmitting rotation: a mechanism that transmits torque or conveys rotary motion from a first shaft to a second shaft or other rotating member and that either does not include relatively moving components or, if it has relatively moving components, the components move without the use of control means.
[F16D 1/00] Couplings -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Attachment of cranks to their shafts -> F16C3/10  **Glossary:** - Rigidly: "rigidly" means that there is no play or clearance intended in the connection (DE:spiel, FR:jeu).
[F16D 1/02] for connecting two abutting shafts or the like -- F16D 1/02 covers couplings designed to connect two adjoining shafts or similar rotating elements, allowing direct transmission of torque between them. These devices include splined, keyed, or other mechanical connections that join the shafts end-to-end without intervening intermediate components.
[F16D 1/027] non-disconnectable, e.g. involving gluing, welding or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for adhesive connection or welding, but not limited thereto. Rivets are also non-disconnectable therefore this type of connection would also be classified here. But bolts are usually disconnectable and would be classified rather in F16D1/033 when connecting two faces perpendicular to the axis of rotation.
[F16D 1/033] by clamping together two faces perpendicular to the axis of rotation, e.g. with bolted flanges -- F16D 1/033 refers to rotational couplings that transmit torque by clamping two mating faces that lie at right angles to the shaft axis, typically using bolted or otherwise fastened flanges. This subclass groups devices where the connection is achieved through face-to-face pressure rather than interlocking keys or splines.
[F16D 1/04] with clamping hub -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for connecting two abutting shafts or the like by means of a clamping hub or hub with a longitudinal key. Examples of clamping hubs (radial clamping/loading) and keys can be found in the titles of the subgroups of F16D1/08 .
[F16D 1/05] with radial clamping due to axial loading of at least one pair of conical surfaces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for connecting two abutting shafts or the like by means of radial clamping due to axial loading of at least one pair of conical surfaces. Examples of this kind of clamping can be found in the subgroups of F16D1/09 .
[F16D 1/06] for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft-end -- The F16D 1/06 subclass covers couplings that attach a member directly to a rotating shaft or to the end of a shaft, using devices such as set screws, pins, splines, clamping or other securing means to transmit torque while permitting assembly or removal.
[F16D 1/064] non-disconnectable -- Non-disconnectable couplings are designed so that the connected shafts cannot be separated without breaking or otherwise damaging the coupling, often using permanent fastening methods such as welding, crimping, or solid interlocking elements. They are used where a reliable, maintenance-free transmission of torque is required and where disassembly is not intended during the service life of the equipment.
[F16D 1/068] involving gluing, welding or the like -- This subclass covers rotational couplings whose components are joined by adhesive, welding, brazing, soldering or similar processes rather than mechanical fasteners. It includes designs where the coupling elements are permanently bonded to transmit torque.
[F16D 1/072] involving plastic deformation -- This subclass of CPC F16D covers couplings that achieve torque transmission by plastic deformation of the material, such as split, swaged or crimped designs where the component is permanently deformed to lock together, providing a secure mechanical connection.
[F16D 1/076] by clamping together two faces perpendicular to the axis of rotation, e.g. with bolted flanges -- This subclass covers couplings that join two mating faces oriented perpendicular to the rotation axis by clamping, typically using bolted flanges. The design transmits torque while the faces are pressed together, providing a rigid sealed connection. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 1/08] with clamping hub -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a clamping hub or a hub with a longitudinal key.
[F16D 1/0805] with radial clamping due to deformation of a resilient body or a body of fluid  F16D1/091  takes precedence; elastic couplings  F16D3/80 ; fluid pressure clutches  F16D25/04 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping hub due to deformation of a resilient body or a body of fluid, e.g. used for hubs comprising cavities which can be pressurised by fluid, so that a wall of the hub can expand towards the shaft in order to radially clamp the shaft.
[F16D 1/0811] with radial clamping due to tilting of a hub part or ring about a diametral axis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping hub due to tilting of a hub part or ring about a diametral axis, meaning that radially clamping is achieved by means of tilting of a hub part or ring about an axis perpendicularly crossing the rotational axis.
[F16D 1/0817] with radial clamping due to rotation along an eccentric surface, e.g. arcuate wedging elements  similar clutches  F16D17/00 ; similar free-wheel clutches  F16D41/06 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping hub due to rotation along an eccentric surface, e.g. arcuate wedging elements on the inner surface of the hub and/or the mating outer surface of the shaft, whereby clamping is achieved when the shaft and hub are relatively rotated and the elements wedge.
[F16D 1/0823] with radial clamping of a helical wrap spring on the shaft or in the hub bore  similar clutches  F16D13/025 ,  F16D13/08 ,  F16D27/025 ,  F16D27/105 ; similar slip couplings  F16D7/022 ; similar free-wheel clutches  F16D41/206 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping hub due to clamping of a helical wrap spring on the shaft or in the hub bore, e.g. by expanding the wrap spring against the hub bore due to relative rotation of the spring ends around the rotational axis.
[F16D 1/0829] with radial loading of both hub and shaft by an intermediate ring or sleeve  F16D1/0817 ,  F16D1/0823 ,  F16D1/093  take precedence -- This CPC subclass covers couplings that transmit rotation while bearing radial loads on both the hub and the shaft through an intermediate ring or sleeve. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation, and refines the more general subclasses F16D1/0817, F16D1/0823 and F16D1/093 which take precedence.
[F16D 1/0835] due to the elasticity of the ring or sleeve -- The F16D 1/0835 entry covers couplings that transmit rotation by exploiting the elasticity of a ring or sleeve, which deforms to accommodate misalignment or torque variations while maintaining a secure connection. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 1/0841] due to axial loading of the ring or sleeve, e.g. Belleville washers -- This subclass covers couplings in which axial loading of a ring or sleeve is used to provide spring or cushioning action, such as arrangements employing Belleville washers. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 1/0847] with radial clamping due to a radial screw -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping hub due to clamping by a set screw, e.g. a screw screwed in the hub in a radial direction through the hub and ending in the hub bore and thereby hitting the shaft outer surface.
[F16D 1/0852] with radial clamping between the mating surfaces of the hub and shaft  F16D1/0805  -  F16D1/0817 ,  F16D1/09  take precedence -- F16D 1/0852 refers to mechanical couplings that achieve radial clamping between the hub and shaft mating surfaces, ensuring secure transmission of rotation. This feature is defined within the broader group F16D 1/0805-F16D 1/0817 and takes precedence over the more general F16D 1/09 category.
[F16D 1/0858] due to the elasticity of the hub (including shrink fits) -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping between the mating surfaces of the hub and the shaft due to the elasticity of the hub, e.g. a hub is heated (to enlarge its bore), is then put on the shaft and subsequently cooled in order to crimp or e.g. a hub is press fit on a shaft.
[F16D 1/0864] due to tangential loading of the hub, e.g. a split hub -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Used for attachment of a member on a shaft or on a shaft end by means of a radial clamping between the mating surfaces of the hub and the shaft due to tangential loading of the hub, e.g. a split hub, meaning e.g. a hub which has a radial split in the direction of the rotational axis, whereby the parts of the hub adjacent to the split and facing each other are clamped together by e.g. a screw so that the bore of the hub clamps around the shaft.
[F16D 1/087] due to other loading elements in the hub or shaft -- This subclass covers couplings whose hub or shaft incorporates additional load-bearing elements, such as pins, keys, or other components that share the transmitted torque. It pertains to designs where the coupling works together with these auxiliary loading parts to transmit rotation.
[F16D 1/0876] with axial keys and no other radial clamping -- This CPC subgroup covers rotational couplings that transmit torque by means of axial keys inserted along the shaft axis, without any other radial clamping devices. The coupling relies solely on the axial key to lock the components together, providing a simple, compact means of torque transmission.
[F16D 1/0882] the key being axially tapered and tightening when loaded axially -- F16D 1/0882 covers mechanical couplings that transmit rotation by using a key whose profile is axially tapered so that axial loading forces the key to wedge tighter between shaft and hub. This design provides self-tightening action, improving torque transmission and preventing slippage under load.
[F16D 1/0888] the key having two axially tapered interengaging parts -- F16D 1/0888 covers couplings that transmit rotational motion by means of a key formed from two axially tapered interengaging components which lock together to transfer torque. Such designs are used where compact, self-locking connections are required in shafts and hubs.
[F16D 1/0894] with other than axial keys, e.g. diametral pins, cotter pins and no other radial clamping -- This subgroup covers couplings that transmit torque by using non-axial securing elements such as diametral pins or cotter pins, rather than traditional axial keys, and that do not employ additional radial clamping mechanisms. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 1/09] with radial clamping due to axial loading of at least one pair of conical surfaces -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Tapered keys -> F16D1/0882
[F16D 1/091] and comprising a chamber including a tapered piston moved axially by fluid pressure to effect clamping -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in F16D1/091 .
[F16D 1/092] the pair of conical mating surfaces being provided on the coupled hub and shaft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in F16D1/092 .
[F16D 1/093] using one or more elastic segmented conical rings forming at least one of the conical surfaces, the rings being expanded or contracted to effect clamping -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Couplings comprising a chamber including a tapered piston moved axially by fluid pressure to effect clamping -> F16D1/091
[F16D 1/094] using one or more pairs of elastic or segmented rings with mutually mating conical surfaces, one of the mating rings being contracted and the other being expanded -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in F16D1/094 .
