[F16D 2200/0047] Ceramic composite, e.g. C/C composite infiltrated with Si or B, or ceramic matrix infiltrated with metal -- The code F16D 2200/0047 covers couplings or rotating-transmission components that incorporate ceramic composites, such as carbon-carbon structures infiltrated with silicon or boron, or ceramic matrices reinforced by metal infiltration. It classifies devices where these advanced ceramic-metal materials are employed to improve strength, wear resistance and thermal stability.
[F16D 2200/0052] Carbon -- F16D 2200/0052 designates couplings for transmitting rotation that are made wholly or partly of carbon materials, including carbon fibre, carbon composites and other carbon-based substances, used to achieve high strength-to-weight ratios, corrosion resistance and thermal stability in mechanical power transmission.
[F16D 2200/0056] Elastomers -- The subclass F16D 2200/0056 focuses on couplings that employ elastomeric materials-rubber, synthetic polymers or similar flexible substances-to transmit rotational motion while absorbing vibration, accommodating misalignment and compensating for tolerances. It includes designs where elastomers form flexible elements, bushings, inserts or seals within the coupling structure.
[F16D 2200/006] containing fibres or particles -- F16D 2200/006 covers couplings for transmitting rotation that are made from or incorporate fibres or particulate materials. These couplings use such inclusions to provide flexibility, damping or strength, often in flexible or elastic coupling designs. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and specifically the materials sub-category for rotational couplings.
[F16D 2200/0065] Inorganic, e.g. non-asbestos mineral fibres -- F16D 2200/0065 designates the use of inorganic, non-asbestos mineral fibres as the material for mechanical couplings that transmit rotational motion. These fibres serve as reinforcing or structural components in coupling elements, providing durability and thermal resistance without relying on asbestos.
[F16D 2200/0069] being characterised by their size -- The CPC subgroup F16D 2200/0069 covers rotational couplings whose defining feature is their size, including specifications of overall dimensions, diameters, lengths or other dimensional parameters that distinguish the coupling, irrespective of its material or detailed design.
[F16D 2200/0073] having lubricating properties -- F16D 2200/0073 relates to couplings whose material itself provides lubrication, such as self-lubricating metals, polymers or composites, to minimise friction and wear during rotational transmission. The lubricating property is inherent to the coupling material rather than supplied by external grease or oil.
[F16D 2200/0078] laminated -- F16D 2200/0078 designates couplings that transmit rotation by means of a laminated construction, where two or more bonded layers of metal, plastic, composite or similar material form a flexible disc or element that can accommodate angular, radial or axial mis-alignment while transferring torque. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation, materials.
[F16D 2200/0082] Production methods therefor -- It covers manufacturing processes for mechanical couplings that transmit rotary motion, detailing the steps, equipment and materials used to produce these elements. The classification includes forming, machining, assembly and finishing techniques specific to rotation-transmitting couplings.
[F16D 2200/0086] Moulding materials together by application of heat and pressure -- This subclass covers techniques that join parts by heating them and applying pressure to mould the materials together, creating a solid coupling that can transmit rotational motion. It includes processes such as hot-pressing, thermoplastic bonding and metal extrusion used in mechanical engineering couplings.
[F16D 2200/0091] Impregnating a mat of fibres with a binder -- This subclass deals with the method of saturating a fibrous mat or fabric with a binding resin or adhesive to produce a composite material, typically for use in mechanical coupling components that transmit rotary motion. It includes steps of impregnation, curing and shaping of the reinforced material.
[F16D 2200/0095] Mixing an aqueous slurry of fibres with a binder, e.g. papermaking process -- F16D 2200/0095 concerns the mixing of a water-based fibre slurry with a binding agent, such as in papermaking or similar forming processes. It covers apparatus and methods that use rotating couplings to agitate and uniformly disperse the fibres and binder, ensuring consistent sheet formation. The classification is part of the mechanical engineering group for couplings that transmit rotational motion.
[F16D 2250/00] Manufacturing -- This subclass deals with manufacturing processes, methods and apparatus for producing rotational couplings, including machining, forming, assembling, heat-treating and surface-treatment steps applied to coupling elements that transmit torque between shafts. It includes techniques for producing shafts, hubs, sleeves, splines and other components of rotary couplings.
[F16D 2250/0007] Casting -- F16D 2250/0007 covers the casting of mechanical couplings used to transmit rotary motion. It pertains to manufacturing processes where the coupling elements are formed by pouring molten metal into molds, resulting in integral or assembled units that fit within the broader category of engineering elements and units for rotational transmission.
[F16D 2250/0015] around inserts -- F16D 2250/0015 designates couplings for transmitting rotation that incorporate around inserts, i.e., components placed around a shaft or other element to transmit torque while allowing axial movement or misalignment. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and manufacturing of couplings.
[F16D 2250/0023] Shaping by pressure -- F16D 2250/0023 covers methods for forming couplings that transmit rotation by applying pressure, such as forging, hydro-forming or other pressure-based shaping processes that shape the coupling's geometry while maintaining its mechanical integrity. It is used for producing transmission elements where precise dimensional control and material properties are required.
[F16D 2250/003] Chip removing -- F16D 2250/003 pertains to manufacturing processes that remove metal chips or shavings from couplings used to transmit rotation. It includes methods and equipment for clearing debris generated during machining, assembly or maintenance of rotating mechanical elements, ensuring proper fit, surface finish and performance of the coupling.
[F16D 2250/0038] Surface treatment -- F16D 2250/0038 covers surface treatment of rotational couplings, including hardening, coating, plating and other finishing techniques applied to the coupling's contact surfaces to enhance wear resistance, friction performance, corrosion protection or load-bearing capacity.
[F16D 2250/0046] Coating -- F16D 2250/0046 pertains to the application of protective or functional coatings on mechanical couplings used to transmit rotary motion. It includes methods and materials for surface treatment such as plating, painting, or polymeric layers, aimed at improving wear resistance, corrosion protection, or friction characteristics during manufacturing.
[F16D 2250/0053] Hardening -- The F16D 2250/0053 entry covers hardening treatments applied to rotational couplings, such as heat-treating, induction or case-hardening processes that raise surface hardness and improve wear resistance. It addresses manufacturing methods used to enhance the strength and durability of mechanical coupling components.
[F16D 2250/0061] Joining -- The CPC subclass F16D 2250/0061 refers to joining techniques or features used in manufacturing couplings that transmit rotary motion. It includes methods for permanently or temporarily connecting coupling parts such as shafts, hubs, or sleeves, and related apparatus for assembling these elements.
[F16D 2250/0069] Adhesive bonding -- It falls under Mechanical Engineering > Engineering Elements and Units > Couplings for Transmitting Rotation > Manufacturing. The code addresses the use of adhesive bonding processes to join components of rotary couplings.
[F16D 2250/0076] Welding, brazing -- F16D 2250/0076 covers welding and brazing techniques used to manufacture couplings that transmit rotational motion. It includes processes that join metal components of shafts, hubs or sleeves to form rigid or semi-rigid connections. The classification lies within the mechanical engineering group for engineering elements and units, specifically couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 2250/0084] Assembly or disassembly -- F16D 2250/0084 relates to the manufacturing processes for assembling or disassembling rotational couplings, describing the steps, tools and mechanisms used to join or separate components that transmit torque.
[F16D 2250/0092] Tools or machines for producing linings -- F16D 2250/0092 covers tools and machines used to produce linings for mechanical couplings that transmit rotation. These devices are employed in manufacturing to form protective or functional linings on coupling components, enhancing wear resistance and performance.
[F16D 23/00] Details of mechanically-actuated clutches not specific for one distinct type -- F16D 23/00 relates to mechanically-actuated clutches used for transmitting rotation, describing generic design features, operating principles and adjustment mechanisms without limiting the information to any specific clutch type.
[F16D 2300/00] Special features for couplings or clutches -- F16D 2300/00 designates special features for couplings or clutches, such as adjustable, overload-protective, damping, or self-locking mechanisms that enhance the transmission of rotational motion. It is a subcategory of mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 2300/02] Overheat protection, i.e. means for protection against overheating -- F16D 2300/02 covers devices built into rotational couplings that detect or limit temperature rise to avoid damage. It includes thermal sensors, fusible links, cooling passages or automatic disengagement mechanisms that activate when a preset temperature is exceeded, protecting the coupling and the connected machinery from overheating.
[F16D 2300/021] Cooling features not provided for in group -- F16D 2300/021 covers couplings or clutches that incorporate cooling provisions not otherwise listed in the standard groups, such as added fins, ducts, or liquid-cooling channels designed to dissipate heat generated during torque transmission. It is a subclass of mechanical engineering elements for transmitting rotation, focusing on special cooling features that improve thermal management of the coupling.
[F16D 2300/0212] Air cooling -- The code F16D 2300/0212 pertains to couplings or clutches that employ air cooling to manage heat generated during rotational transmission. It includes features such as fins, ventilated housings or forced-air ducts that remove heat from the mating elements, thereby maintaining performance and preventing overheating.
[F16D 2300/0214] Oil or fluid cooling -- The classification covers couplings or clutches that incorporate oil or fluid cooling systems to remove heat generated during rotation. It includes designs where coolant passages, external fins or heat-exchange elements are integral to the coupling unit, ensuring temperature control and extended service life. Such features are applied in mechanical engineering where rotational power is transmitted.
[F16D 2300/04] Heating means -- F16D 2300/04 covers heating means incorporated in couplings or clutches, such as electric heaters, fluid-based warmers or resistive elements, intended to raise or maintain the temperature of the engaged components for reliable torque transmission, reduced friction and prevention of condensation. These features are used where ambient conditions or operational cycles could otherwise impair performance.
[F16D 2300/06] Lubrication details not provided for in group -- F16D 2300/06 is a CPC subclass that covers couplings or clutches with special features when the patent does not specify any lubrication details. It is used for devices transmitting rotational motion whose lubrication method or material is not disclosed.
[F16D 2300/08] Details or arrangements of sealings not provided for in group -- This CPC subgroup covers any sealing features for rotating couplings or clutches that are not specified in the other groups, including custom or auxiliary sealing designs, seals for special operating conditions, and arrangements that protect the transmission from contaminants or leakage.
[F16D 2300/10] Surface characteristics -- This subclass concerns the surface properties and treatments of couplings or clutches used for transmitting rotation, including texture, roughness, coating, friction or wear-resistant features that affect engagement and torque transmission. It covers specifications, modifications, or manufacturing processes that alter surface characteristics to improve performance or durability.
[F16D 2300/12] Mounting or assembling -- F16D 2300/12 pertains to the mounting or assembly of couplings and clutches that transmit rotation, covering methods and structures for attaching, securing or aligning these components in mechanical systems. It falls under special features for couplings or clutches within the broader engineering elements and units category.
[F16D 2300/14] Clutches which are normally open, i.e. not engaged in released state -- F16D 2300/14 covers clutches that remain disengaged in their default condition, meaning they are normally open and only engage when activated. These devices are part of coupling mechanisms that transmit rotational motion and fall under the mechanical engineering subgroup of engineering elements and units.
[F16D 2300/18] Sensors -- F16D 2300/18 designates sensors associated with couplings or clutches that transmit rotation, including devices that monitor speed, torque, position, vibration or other operating parameters to provide feedback or control. The subclass covers any sensor integrated into or applied to such mechanical transmission elements.
[F16D 2300/20] Auxiliary indicators or alarms -- The CPC subclass F16D 2300/20 covers auxiliary indicators or alarm devices associated with couplings or clutches that transmit rotation. These features provide visual or audible signals to warn of conditions such as overload, mis-alignment, disengagement or other abnormal operating states, helping monitor and protect the coupling system.
[F16D 2300/22] Vibration damping -- The CPC class F16D 2300/22 covers couplings or clutches that incorporate vibration-damping features, aiming to reduce oscillations and noise transmitted between rotating shafts. It sits within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 2300/24] Concentric actuation rods, e.g. actuation rods extending concentrically through a shaft -- A concentric actuation rod is a rod that runs inside a shaft, aligned with its axis, to transmit motion or force while the shaft rotates. It is used as a special feature in couplings or clutches to actuate mechanisms without interfering with the rotating components.
[F16D 2300/26] Cover or bell housings -- The CPC code F16D 2300/26 falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation and special features for couplings or clutches. It designates cover or bell housings that enclose and protect the coupling mechanism, providing structural support, shielding from contaminants and a mounting interface for the assembled unit.
[F16D 23/02] Arrangements for synchronisation, also for power-operated clutches -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Shape or mounting of interengaging parts of clutch members to facilitate engagement -> F16D11/08
[F16D 23/025] Synchro rings -- F16D 23/025 covers synchro rings, which are intermediate, usually toothed, metal rings mounted between two rotating shafts or gears to equalise their speeds before engagement of a clutch. They transmit torque while allowing a smooth, controlled start-up and are used in automotive, aircraft and industrial power-train systems where precise synchronisation of rotation is required.
[F16D 23/04] with an additional friction clutch -- F16D 23/04 classifies couplings that incorporate an extra friction clutch to modulate or transmit rotation, combining a primary mechanical coupling with a secondary friction element. It applies to mechanically-actuated clutches whose design is not limited to a single specific type but includes an additional friction device for engagement or torque control.
[F16D 23/06] and a blocking mechanism preventing the engagement of the main clutch prior to synchronisation -- F16D 23/06 covers clutch assemblies that include a blocking device which stops the main clutch from engaging until the driven and driving elements are synchronised, typically using a mechanically-actuated mechanism. It falls under couplings for transmitting rotation and relates to general clutch designs without specifying a particular type.
[F16D 23/0606] the blocking mechanism comprising an axially-extending shouldered pin passing through a hole in a radial wall -- The entry covers a blocking arrangement used in mechanically-actuated clutches in which an axially-extending pin with a shoulder is inserted through a hole punched in a radial wall, the shoulder engaging the wall to lock or prevent relative rotation.
[F16D 23/0612] the blocking mechanism comprising a radial pin in an axial slot with at least one branch -- This CPC subclass covers blocking mechanisms in mechanically-actuated clutches that use a radial pin inserted into an axial slot featuring at least one branch, providing a lock-up function to prevent rotation. It pertains to general clutch designs rather than a specific type, and falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 23/08] with a blocking mechanism that only releases the clutching member on synchronisation -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - In combination with an additional friction clutch -> F16D23/06
[F16D 23/10] automatically producing the engagement of the clutch when the clutch members are moving at the same speed -- This subclass covers clutch arrangements that automatically produce engagement when the driving and driven members are already rotating at the same speed, using hydraulic, pneumatic, electromagnetic or purely mechanical means to apply the clutch without external control input. It includes devices that sense speed equality and trigger the clutch to lock the two shafts together.

The classification is for general sleeve mechanisms rather than designs specific to a particular clutch type, and falls under the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.

[F16D 23/144] With a disengaging thrust-ring distinct from the release bearing, and secured to the diaphragm -- This subclass refers to mechanically-actuated clutches that transmit rotation and feature a disengaging thrust-ring separate from the release bearing, with the thrust-ring secured to the clutch diaphragm. The arrangement distinguishes the clutch from designs where the thrust-ring is integrated with the bearing or other components.
[F16D 23/145] Arrangements for the connection between the thrust-ring and the diaphragm -- The subclass F16D 23/145 covers designs that link the thrust-ring to the diaphragm in mechanically-actuated clutches, detailing how the two components are joined to transmit axial forces while allowing rotational motion. It addresses generic connection methods rather than a single clutch type.

These arrangements are used in couplings that transmit rotation and are not limited to a single clutch type.
[F16D 23/147] bearing with rolling elements having at least one race or part fixed to the race blind axially, e.g. cup-shaped -- A bearing of this subclass has at least one race or a portion of a race that is blind-axially fixed, for example a cup-shaped element, while rolling elements engage with that fixed surface and the opposite race may shift axially. This arrangement provides a predetermined axial position for the rolling elements, typical in mechanically-actuated clutch assemblies.

[F16D 2500/50242] Cleaning of clutches, e.g. controlling the engine or the clutch to provoke vibrations eliminating particles from the clutch friction surfaces -- The entry covers methods that clean clutch friction surfaces by deliberately inducing vibrations, for example by controlling the engine or the clutch, to dislodge particles lodged on the surfaces. It relates to externally-controlled, electric or electronic clutch actuation for cleaning purposes.
[F16D 2500/50245] Calibration or recalibration of the clutch touch-point -- The code F16D 2500/50245 refers to methods and devices for adjusting the engagement point of a clutch that is controlled electronically, allowing the clutch's touch-point to be calibrated or re-calibrated. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 2500/50248] During assembly -- This CPC subgroup relates to the assembly phase of couplings that transmit rotation and are externally controlled by electric or electronic means, detailing procedures, tools or fixtures used to mount or integrate such electrically actuated clutches into mechanical systems.
[F16D 2500/50251] During operation -- This subclass covers electric or electronic control of a clutch while it is engaged, addressing how signals or power are applied to modify clutch behavior during normal operation, including sensors, actuators, and circuitry that adjust torque transmission without stopping the machine.
[F16D 2500/50254] Brake actuated -- A brake-actuated clutch uses an electric or electronic control signal to apply a brake that engages or disengages the clutch, allowing rotation to be transmitted only when the brake is released. This arrangement provides precise, automated control of power transfer in mechanical systems such as automotive transmissions or industrial drives.
[F16D 2500/50257] During a creep operation -- The entry covers couplings that transmit rotation and are externally controlled by electric or electronic signals when operating in a creep mode, meaning the clutch allows a slow, continuous slip under load. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 2500/5026] Gear engaged -- The F16D 2500/5026 classification defines gear-engaged couplings in which an electric or electronic control system operates a clutch to transmit rotational motion, using a gear to achieve engagement and disengagement of the drive. This type of coupling falls under mechanical engineering elements for transmitting rotation with external electronic control.
[F16D 2500/50263] During standing still -- The entry defines features for clutches that are controlled by electric or electronic means while the machine is stationary, covering idle-hold or stand-still operation of rotational couplings.
[F16D 2500/50266] Way of detection -- This CPC subclass concerns detection methods used in externally controlled clutches that are operated by electric or electronic signals. It covers ways of sensing clutch position, speed, torque, or slip to enable control systems to monitor and adjust engagement.
[F16D 2500/50269] Engine speed -- F16D 2500/50269 refers to devices that regulate engine speed by using electrically or electronically actuated clutches within the coupling system that transmits rotation. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and specifically couplings for transmitting rotation. It covers external control methods that adjust clutch engagement to vary engine output speed.
[F16D 2500/50272] Gearing speed -- The CPC code F16D 2500/50272 addresses gearing speed in couplings that transmit rotation and are externally controlled by electric or electronic means. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 2500/50275] Estimation of the displacement of the clutch touch-point due to the modification of relevant parameters, e.g. temperature, wear -- The code F16D 2500/50275 falls within the CPC class for mechanical engineering couplings that transmit rotation and are externally-controlled by electric or electronic means. It addresses methods for estimating the displacement of the clutch contact point when parameters such as temperature or wear are altered.
[F16D 2500/50278] Stalling -- This subclass covers situations where an electrically or electronically actuated clutch is intentionally prevented from transmitting torque, causing a stall condition. It is part of the mechanical engineering hierarchy for couplings that transmit rotation and deals with external control of clutches.
[F16D 2500/50281] Transmitted torque -- This subclass deals with the amount of torque that a clutch or coupling can transmit when its engagement is controlled by electric or electronic signals. It includes methods, devices and parameters for monitoring, regulating or limiting the transmitted torque in electrically actuated clutches.
[F16D 2500/50284] Control of secondary clutch in the driveline, i.e. not including clutches in automatic transmission, e.g. in the vicinity of rear axle or on parallel drive shaft -- This CPC subclass pertains to electrically or electronically actuated secondary clutches in a vehicle driveline, excluding those in automatic transmissions, such as clutches located near the rear axle or on a parallel drive shaft, and covers the control methods for engaging or disengaging these clutches.
[F16D 2500/50287] Torque control -- The entry F16D 2500/50287 pertains to torque control achieved by electrically or electronically actuated external devices that regulate clutch operation. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically couplings for transmitting rotation, and addresses how electronic signals are used to modulate the torque transferred through a clutch.
[F16D 2500/5029] Reducing drag torque -- The code F16D 2500/5029 relates to techniques for lowering drag torque in clutches that are controlled electrically or electronically, typically used in rotational couplings. It includes designs or methods that reduce resistance when the clutch is idle, improving efficiency and control.
[F16D 2500/50293] Reduction of vibrations -- The entry F16D 2500/50293 covers techniques for reducing vibrations in couplings that transmit rotation and are externally controlled by electric or electronic means. It pertains to mechanical engineering elements such as clutches where vibration damping is achieved through electronic control strategies.
[F16D 2500/50296] Limit clutch wear -- The entry pertains to electric or electronic control systems that limit wear in clutches used for transmitting rotation, for example by monitoring torque, slip or temperature and adjusting actuation to protect friction surfaces. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 2500/503] relating to the accumulator -- This subclass covers clutch mechanisms that incorporate an accumulator to store hydraulic, pneumatic or other energy for electronically-controlled engagement or disengagement. It includes devices where the accumulator supplies the pressure or fluid needed for the clutch actuation under external electric or electronic control.
[F16D 2500/5035] Filling level of an accumulator providing fluid for the engagement of the clutch -- This classification covers methods or devices that monitor or adjust the fluid level in an accumulator that supplies hydraulic pressure for clutch engagement, especially in systems where clutch operation is controlled electrically or electronically. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation, and external electronic control of clutches.

[F16D 2500/5043] Engine fuel consumption -- F16D 2500/5043 covers inventions that employ electric or electronic signals to control a clutch or other rotating coupling from outside the mechanical assembly, allowing precise regulation of torque transmission, often in vehicle powertrains to optimise engine fuel consumption.
[F16D 2500/5045] Control of engine at idle, i.e. controlling engine idle conditions, e.g. idling speed -- This subclass covers electronic or electric methods for controlling engine idle conditions, especially by regulating clutch engagement to maintain a set idling speed. It includes devices and control strategies that use signals to adjust the idle speed of an internal-combustion engine during operation.
[F16D 2500/5046] Preventing engine over-speed, e.g. by actuation of the main clutch -- A system that detects excessive engine speed and automatically engages the main clutch using electric or electronic control to limit over-speed. It integrates a speed sensor, a control unit and an actuated clutch, providing rapid, automated protection for engines by interrupting power transmission when predefined speed thresholds are exceeded.
[F16D 2500/5048] Stall prevention -- F16D 2500/5048 covers mechanisms that prevent a clutch or other rotational coupling from stalling by using electric or electronic control signals to modulate engagement. The technology monitors load or speed conditions and adjusts torque transmission to avoid lock-up. It is applied in automotive, industrial, and marine drive systems.

This technique is often controlled externally by electric or electronic means.

[F16D 25/02] with means for actuating or keeping engaged by a force derived at least partially from one of the shafts to be connected -- This CPC subclass defines clutches whose actuation or retention in the engaged position is achieved by a force taken at least partly from one of the shafts being coupled. [F16D 25/04] in which the fluid actuates an elastic clutching, member, e.g. a diaphragm or a pneumatic tube -- The F16D 25/04 subclass covers couplings in which a fluid-typically air, hydraulic oil or another pressurised medium-activates an elastic clutching element such as a diaphragm, membrane or pneumatic tube to transmit rotational motion. This non-mechanical actuation method enables smooth engagement and disengagement of shafts without direct mechanical linkage, often used in power-train and hydraulic control systems.
[F16D 25/042] the elastic actuating member rotating with the clutch -- F16D 25/042 concerns clutches that are engaged or disengaged by an elastic actuating element that rotates together with the clutch, providing non-mechanical actuation such as via a spring or elastomer. The design uses stored elastic energy to transmit the actuation force without a separate mechanical drive.
[F16D 25/044] and causing purely axial movement -- F16D 25/044 covers clutches that are actuated by non-mechanical means and that generate only axial movement of the driven member to engage or disengage the clutch. The actuation-such as hydraulic, pneumatic or electromagnetic pressure-pushes or pulls the clutch components along the shaft axis without any rotational or radial motion, enabling transmission of rotation while the control force remains purely axial.
[F16D 25/046] and causing purely radial movement -- This subclass covers clutches that are activated by non-mechanical means such as hydraulic, pneumatic, magnetic or electro-magnetic forces and that engage or disengage by moving purely in the radial direction relative to the shaft, without axial or tangential displacement. It falls under couplings for transmitting rotation within mechanical engineering.
[F16D 25/048] the elastic actuating member not rotating with a coupling part -- This CPC subclass F16D 25/048 covers clutch devices where the elastic actuating member is fixed relative to the clutch housing and does not rotate with the coupling part, providing non-mechanical actuation for torque transmission. It falls under couplings for transmitting rotation within mechanical engineering.
[F16D 25/06] in which the fluid actuates a piston incorporated in, the clutch -- F16D 25/06 covers clutches in which a fluid pressure moves a piston that is built into the clutch, using the fluid's force to engage or disengage the rotating elements without any mechanical linkage. This non-mechanical actuation typically employs hydraulic or pneumatic pressure to control the clutch's operating state.
[F16D 25/061] the clutch having interengaging clutch members -- F16D 25/061 covers clutches in which the clutch members interengage to transmit rotation and are actuated by non-mechanical means. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation, specifically clutches actuated non-mechanically. The entry applies to designs where engaging surfaces mesh directly to connect or disconnect the drive.
[F16D 25/062] the clutch having friction surfaces -- The CPC subclass F16D 25/062 covers clutches that transmit rotation by bringing friction surfaces into contact and are actuated by non-mechanical means such as hydraulic, pneumatic or electromagnetic control, allowing engagement without a direct mechanical linkage.
[F16D 25/063] with clutch members exclusively moving axially -- A non-mechanically actuated clutch in which the engaging members shift solely along the axis of rotation, typically by hydraulic, pneumatic, electromagnetic or thermal means, is classified under F16D 25/063, a subcategory of mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.

[F16D 25/0635] with flat friction surfaces, e.g. discs -- A non-mechanically actuated clutch that transmits rotation by means of flat friction surfaces, typically disc-type elements. This subclass falls under couplings for transmitting rotation in mechanical engineering.
[F16D 25/0638] with more than two discs, e.g. multiple lamellae -- This subclass relates to non-mechanically actuated clutches that use more than two friction discs arranged as multiple lamellae to transmit rotation. [F16D 25/064] the friction surface being grooved -- F16D 25/064 relates to non-mechanically actuated clutches in which the friction surface has grooves to improve engagement and torque transmission. It falls under mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.

[F16D 25/083] Actuators therefor  F16D25/085  -  F16D25/087  take precedence -- F16D 25/083 covers the actuators that drive clutches which are engaged or disengaged without direct mechanical linkage, such as hydraulic, pneumatic, electromagnetic, thermal or other energy-based devices. The subclass includes the components that convert the control signal into the force or motion needed to operate the clutch, while the more specific groups F16D 25/085 to F16D 25/087 define particular actuator types and therefore take precedence.
[F16D 25/085] the clutch actuation being of the pull type -- This subgroup of the mechanical-engineering couplings classification covers clutches whose engagement or disengagement is achieved by a pulling force applied to the actuation mechanism, such as cables, rods or hydraulic pistons that draw a component into position, rather than by direct mechanical linkage.
[F16D 25/086] the clutch being actuated by a push rod extending coaxially through the input or output shaft -- This subclass covers clutches that are engaged or disengaged by a push rod running coaxially through the input or output shaft, allowing axial actuation without external mechanical linkages. The rod transmits the actuation force along the shaft axis to operate the clutch surfaces.
[F16D 25/087] the clutch being actuated by the fluid-actuated member via a diaphragm spring or an equivalent array of levers  F16D25/085 ,  F16D25/086  take precedence -- This CPC subgroup concerns clutches whose engagement is controlled without direct mechanical input, specifically where a fluid-actuated element operates a diaphragm spring or a similar lever arrangement to transmit rotation. The design builds on the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation, and it is subordinated to the preceding subclasses F16D25/085 and F16D25/086.
[F16D 25/088] the line of action of the fluid-actuated members being distinctly separate from the axis of rotation -- F16D 25/088 classifies non-mechanically actuated clutches in which the line of action of the fluid-actuated members is offset from the axis of rotation, covering designs where hydraulic or pneumatic forces engage the clutch through a distinct, non-coaxial path.
[F16D 25/10] Clutch systems with a plurality of fluid-actuated clutches -- F16D 25/10 covers clutch assemblies that incorporate multiple fluid-actuated clutches, allowing the transmission of rotational motion to be controlled by hydraulic or pneumatic pressure rather than mechanical linkages. These systems are used where selective engagement of several clutches provides variable speed or torque characteristics.
[F16D 25/12] Details not specific to one of the before-mentioned types -- F16D 25/12 pertains to non-mechanically actuated clutches whose design details are not limited to a particular subtype, such as hydraulic, pneumatic or electromagnetic versions, and covers general features, control arrangements or adaptations applicable across various clutch types. It resides under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Couplings for Transmitting Rotation.
[F16D 25/123] in view of cooling and lubrication -- The F16D 25/123 subclass covers non-mechanically actuated clutches where cooling and lubrication are specifically addressed, such as designs that incorporate fluid, oil, or air cooling channels, lubricating passages, or heat-dissipating surfaces to manage temperature and friction during engagement and disengagement. These features are intended to improve reliability and performance of rotary couplings in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16D 25/126] adjustment for wear or play -- F16D 25/126 covers adjustments for wear or play in clutches that are actuated without mechanical force, specifying devices such as adjustable clearances, spring-loaded or hydraulic compensators that keep the clutch properly engaged as components wear.
[F16D 25/14] Fluid pressure control -- F16D 25/14 covers clutches that are engaged or released by fluid pressure rather than direct mechanical linkage. It includes hydraulic or pneumatic systems that control the transmission of rotational power, such as fluid-operated brakes or torque-converter mechanisms.
[F16D 27/00] Magnetically- actuated clutches -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Clutches with electro-rheological fluids -> F16D37/008 - Clutches with magnetisable particles -> F16D37/02 - Magnetic clutches e.g. electrostatic or Eddy current clutches -> H02K
[F16D 27/004] with permanent magnets combined with electromagnets -- F16D 27/004 classifies magnetically-actuated clutches that employ a combination of permanent magnets and electromagnets to engage or disengage rotational transmission. The design uses the constant magnetic field of a permanent magnet together with a controllable electromagnet to vary torque capacity or clutch speed, commonly in automotive or industrial drive systems.
[F16D 27/01] with permanent magnets -- F16D 27/01 designates couplings that transmit rotation by means of magnetically-actuated clutches, where the clutching force is generated with permanent magnets rather than electromagnets. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and specifically couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 27/02] with electromagnets incorporated in the clutch, i.e. with collecting rings -- F16D 27/02 covers clutches that use electromagnets or collecting rings built into the device to transmit rotational motion. The magnetic field is generated by the incorporated electromagnets, allowing the clutch to engage or disengage electrically rather than mechanically.
[F16D 27/025] and with a helical band or equivalent member co-operating with a cylindrical coupling surface -- This subclass covers magnetically-actuated clutches that transmit torque by using a helical band or similar element that engages a cylindrical coupling surface. It falls under mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 27/04] with axially-movable friction surfaces -- A magnetically-actuated clutch with axially-movable friction surfaces is a coupling that transmits rotary motion by using magnetic fields to engage and disengage friction plates that can slide along the shaft axis. The axial movement allows the friction surfaces to be brought into or out of contact, providing controlled torque transmission in mechanical systems. This design falls under the classification F16D 27/04 for couplings that incorporate such magnetic and axial-adjustable elements.
[F16D 27/06] with friction surfaces arranged within the flux -- F16D 27/06 covers magnetically-actuated clutches in which the friction surfaces are positioned inside the magnetic flux path, allowing the clutch to engage or disengage through magnetic force while the frictional contact occurs within the flux-generated field. This arrangement is used to create compact, efficient rotational couplings in mechanical systems.
[F16D 27/08] with friction surfaces arranged externally to the flux -- Magnetically-actuated clutches in which the friction surfaces that transmit torque are placed outside the magnetic flux path, so the magnetic field engages the clutch without directly contacting the friction area. This arrangement allows the magnetic circuit to be isolated from wear-prone surfaces while still providing controlled rotational coupling.
[F16D 27/09] and with interengaging jaws or gear-teeth -- Magnetically-actuated clutches that transmit rotation by means of interengaging jaws or gear-teeth are covered, detailing devices where magnetic fields drive the coupling of toothed or jawed elements to lock and unlock torque transmission.
[F16D 27/10] with an electromagnet not rotating with a clutching member, i.e. without collecting rings -- F16D 27/10 covers magnetically-actuated clutches in which the electromagnet is fixed and does not rotate with the clutching member, eliminating the need for collecting rings or similar moving contacts. The design uses a stationary magnetic field to engage or disengage the clutch, simplifying construction and reducing wear.
[F16D 27/102] with radially movable clutching members -- F16D 27/102 lies within mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation and covers magnetically-actuated clutches whose clutching members move radially to engage or disengage torque transmission. The classification applies to designs where a magnetic field causes the clutch components to shift outward or inward, effecting the clutching action.
[F16D 27/105] with a helical band or equivalent member co-operating with a cylindrical coupling surface -- F16D 27/105 pertains to couplings that transmit rotation via a helical band or similar member that engages a cylindrical coupling surface, often used in magnetically-actuated clutches. The helical element wraps around the shaft and transfers torque when the magnetic clutch is engaged, providing a compact, controllable drive connection.
[F16D 27/108] with axially movable clutching members -- F16D 27/108 covers couplings that transmit rotation via a clutch whose engaging elements move axially and are activated by magnetic forces, allowing the clutch to be engaged or released without direct mechanical actuation. This subclass falls under magnetically-actuated clutches within mechanical engineering coupling elements.
[F16D 27/11] with conical friction surfaces, e.g. cone clutches -- F16D 27/11 covers couplings that transmit rotation by means of conical friction surfaces, commonly called cone clutches. The design uses a cone-shaped friction pair that engages when a magnetic field or other actuation forces press the surfaces together, allowing controlled torque transmission and smooth disengagement.
[F16D 27/112] with flat friction surfaces, e.g. discs -- F16D 27/112 relates to magnetically-actuated clutches that transmit rotation using flat friction surfaces such as discs. The magnetic field engages or releases the disc surfaces to control torque transfer between rotating members.
[F16D 27/115] with more than two discs, e.g. multiple lamellae -- Magnetically-actuated clutches that incorporate more than two rotating discs, also known as multiple-lamella designs, belong to the coupling group for transmitting rotation. The classification covers devices where magnetic fields engage or release several interleaved friction plates to transmit torque between shafts.
[F16D 27/118] with interengaging jaws or gear teeth -- F16D 27/118 designates magnetically-actuated clutches that transmit rotation through interengaging jaws or gear teeth. The subclass includes mechanisms where a magnetic field engages or disengages toothed elements to couple or decouple rotating shafts, typically used in automotive or industrial power transmission.
[F16D 27/12] Clutch systems with a plurality of electro-magnetically-actuated clutches -- F16D 27/12 covers clutch assemblies that incorporate several electro-magnetically actuated clutches, allowing selective engagement of multiple drive paths within a single unit. These systems are used in mechanical engineering to provide precise control of torque transmission in applications such as automotive, industrial and aerospace gearboxes.
[F16D 27/14] Details -- F16D 27/14 in the Cooperative Patent Classification (EPO/USPTO) pertains to details of magnetically-actuated clutches, which are components used for transmitting rotation in mechanical engineering. This classification covers specific aspects and designs of these clutches.
[F16D 28/00] Electrically-actuated clutches -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Clutches actuated directly by means of an electromagnet -> F16D27/02 - F16D27/12 - Clutches with electro-rheological fluids -> F16D37/008 - Clutches with magnetisable particles -> F16D37/02 - Magnetic clutches e.g. electrostatic or Eddy current clutches -> H02K
[F16D 29/00] Clutches and systems of clutches involving both fluid and magnetic actuation -- This class covers clutches that integrate fluid power (hydraulic or pneumatic) with magnetic actuation to engage or disengage rotating shafts. It includes hybrid mechanisms where fluid pressure moves magnetic elements or magnetic forces regulate fluid flow, providing combined control for torque transmission in rotational couplings.
[F16D 29/005] with a fluid pressure piston driven by an electric motor -- A clutch system that uses fluid pressure generated by a piston that is driven by an electric motor, combining hydraulic actuation with magnetic (electric) power to transmit rotation. This hybrid approach integrates fluid-driven force with electrically powered control in a single coupling device.
[F16D 3/00] Yielding couplings, i.e. with means permitting movement between the connected parts during the drive -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Couplings disconnectable simply by axial movement -> F16D1/10 - Slip couplings -> F16D7/00
[F16D 3/005] incorporating leaf springs, flexible parts of reduced thickness or the like acting as pivots -- This CPC subclass defines yielding couplings that incorporate leaf springs, thin flexible elements or similar parts acting as pivots, allowing limited movement between the connected components while transmitting rotation. It is used for mechanical couplings that accommodate misalignment and absorb shock.
[F16D 3/02] adapted to specific functions -- F16D 3/02 designates yielding couplings that are specially designed or modified for particular functions such as overload protection, shock absorption, or torque limiting, where the coupling permits limited relative movement between connected shafts during operation.
[F16D 3/04] specially adapted to allow radial displacement, e.g. Oldham couplings -- F16D 3/04 covers couplings that are specifically designed to permit radial displacement of the connected shafts while transmitting torque, examples being Oldham couplings that use sliding plates to accommodate misalignment. These yielding couplings allow movement between parts during operation.
[F16D 3/06] specially adapted to allow axial displacement -- F16D 3/06 designates mechanical couplings that transmit rotation while permitting axial displacement of the connected parts, typically by using springs, sliding elements or other yielding features. These couplings allow the shafts to move longitudinally relative to each other during operation, helping to accommodate misalignment or thermal expansion.
[F16D 3/065] by means of rolling elements -- F16D 3/065 covers yielding couplings that transmit rotational motion while permitting relative movement between the connected shafts by employing rolling elements such as balls, rollers or needle-type bearings. These devices combine a slip or flex capability with low-friction rolling contact to accommodate misalignment or overload.
[F16D 3/08] Couplings for intersecting shafts, provided with intermediate bars bent in an angle corresponding with the angle of intersection -- F16D 3/08 covers yielding couplings that join intersecting shafts by using one or more intermediate bars bent to the same angle as the shaft intersection, allowing limited relative movement while transmitting rotation.
[F16D 3/10] Couplings with means for varying the angular relationship of two coaxial shafts during motion -- F16D 3/10 covers yielding couplings that incorporate mechanisms to vary the angular relationship between two coaxial shafts while they are rotating. These couplings allow controlled mis-alignment or angular movement during drive transmission, improving flexibility and reducing stress in mechanical systems.
[F16D 31/00] Couplings or clutches with a fluid or a semi-fluid as a power-transmitting means -- F16D 31/00 covers couplings and clutches that transmit rotational power by means of a fluid or semi-fluid, such as hydraulic or pneumatic fluid couplings, fluid-filled torque converters and similar devices. These mechanisms rely on fluid pressure or flow to engage and transmit torque between shafts without direct mechanical contact.
[F16D 31/02] using pumps with pistons or plungers working in cylinders -- F16D 31/02 designates couplings or clutches that transmit rotational power by means of a fluid or semi-fluid generated with piston or plunger pumps operating in cylinders. The classification covers systems where the fluid pressure produced by these pumps is the primary power-transmitting medium. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 31/04] using gear-pumps -- This subclass deals with couplings or clutches that transmit power by means of a fluid or semi-fluid when the fluid flow is generated by a gear-pump. It covers arrangements in which gear-pumps create the pressure or movement needed to transfer rotation between shafts.
[F16D 31/06] using pumps of types differing from those before-mentioned -- This subclass covers couplings or clutches that transmit rotation through a fluid or semi-fluid medium and specifies the use of pump types that differ from those previously listed for the power-transmitting unit. It addresses alternative pump designs within fluid-coupling systems.
[F16D 31/08] Control of slip -- F16D 31/08 covers methods and devices for controlling slip in fluid-or semi-fluid couplings and clutches that transmit rotation, such as adjusting fluid pressure, viscosity, flow rate or valve settings to limit relative movement between driving and driven components. It is used to improve torque transmission stability and prevent excessive slippage in hydraulic or pneumatic power-transmitting systems.
[F16D 3/12] specially adapted for accumulation of energy to absorb shocks or vibration -- This subclass covers couplings that are designed to store energy during operation, allowing them to absorb shocks or vibrations transmitted between rotating shafts. The devices use springs, elastomers or other flexible elements to permit relative movement while maintaining torque transmission. They are used where sudden loads must be damped to protect machinery.
[F16D 3/14] combined with a friction coupling for damping vibration or absorbing shock -- The F16D 3/14 subclass covers yielding couplings that incorporate a friction element to provide vibration damping or shock-absorption, allowing limited relative movement between connected rotating parts while reducing transmitted oscillations.
[F16D 3/16] Universal joints in which flexibility is produced by means of pivots or sliding or rolling connecting parts -- F16D 3/16 pertains to universal joints whose flexibility is obtained through pivots, sliding or rolling elements that link the shafts. These couplings transmit rotary motion while permitting relative angular or axial movement between connected parts, enabling alignment tolerance in mechanical power transmission.
[F16D 3/18] the coupling parts (1) having slidably-interengaging teeth -- The CPC subclass F16D 3/18 covers yielding couplings whose elements incorporate teeth that interlock by sliding relative to each other, allowing limited angular or axial movement while transmitting torque. These designs are used where shock absorption or misalignment tolerance is required in rotating machine assemblies.
[F16D 3/185] radial teeth connecting concentric inner and outer coupling parts -- Radial-teeth yielding couplings consist of concentric inner and outer members whose teeth are arranged radially, so that the inner part engages the outer part via interlocking teeth. The design transmits rotation while allowing limited axial, angular or longitudinal movement to accommodate misalignment or overload.
[F16D 3/20] one coupling part entering a sleeve of the other coupling part and connected thereto by sliding or rolling members -- A yielding coupling in which one coupling part is inserted into a sleeve of the other and is secured by sliding or rolling elements that allow relative movement during transmission of rotation. This arrangement enables limited motion between the connected parts while maintaining drive continuity.
[F16D 3/202] one coupling part having radially projecting pins, e.g. tripod joints -- F16D 3/202 covers couplings where a single part carries radially projecting pins, such as in tripod joints, enabling limited angular or axial movement while transmitting rotational power.
[F16D 3/205] the pins extending radially outwardly from the coupling part -- Pins that extend radially from a coupling component to engage a counterpart, providing a driven interface that allows limited axial or angular movement while transmitting torque in yielding couplings.
[F16D 3/2052] having two pins -- F16D 3/2052 covers yielding couplings that incorporate two pins as the means that permit relative movement between the connected parts during rotation. The two-pin arrangement allows limited axial or angular displacement while transmitting torque. Used in mechanical engineering applications where flexibility between shafts is required.
[F16D 3/2055] having three pins, i.e. true tripod joints -- The subclass defines yielding couplings that employ three pins to create a true tripod joint, permitting limited angular or axial movement between connected shafts while still transmitting torque. It is situated within the mechanical-engineering category for couplings used to transmit rotation.
[F16D 3/2057] having four or more pins, e.g. with compensation for relative pin movement -- F16D 3/2057 covers yielding couplings that incorporate four-or-more pins, optionally designed to allow relative movement among the pins during operation, such as compensating for misalignment or axial displacement.
[F16D 3/207] the pins extending radially inwardly from the coupling part -- F16D 3/207 relates to yielding couplings that incorporate pins projecting radially inward from the coupling part to engage a mating element and transmit torque while allowing limited relative movement, such as misalignment or axial play, during operation.
[F16D 3/22] the rolling members being balls, rollers, or the like, guided in grooves or sockets in both coupling parts -- F16D 3/22 covers yielding couplings that transmit rotation by using rolling members such as balls, rollers or similar elements, which are guided in complementary grooves or sockets formed in both connected parts, allowing controlled relative movement while maintaining torque transmission. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 3/221] the rolling members being located in sockets in one of the coupling parts -- F16D 3/221 covers rolling members that are seated in sockets of one of the coupling components in yielding couplings designed to transmit rotation while permitting relative movement between the parts.
[F16D 3/223] the rolling members being guided in grooves in both coupling parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Universal joints in which flexibility is produced by means of rolling connecting parts, one coupling part entering a sleeve of the other coupling part and connected thereto by rolling members whereby the rolling members are being guided in grooves in both coupling parts.
[F16D 3/2233] where the track is made up of two curves with a point of inflexion in between, i.e. S-track joints -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Universal joints where the rolling members are being guided in grooves on both coupling parts. The projection of the groove lines forming a S curve with an inflection point, see inner contour of part (12) and outer contour of part (16) in the figure.
[F16D 3/2237] where the grooves are composed of radii and adjoining straight lines, i.e. undercut free [UF] type joints -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Universal joints where the rolling members are being guided in grooves on both coupling parts. In the projection these grooves are composed of radii and straight lines, see groove line (11b) and (12b) in the figure.
[F16D 3/224] the groove centre-lines in each coupling part lying on a sphere -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Universal joints where the rolling members are being guided in grooves in both coupling parts, and the groove centre-lines of each coupling part are lying on a sphere with the same centre.
[F16D 3/2245] where the groove centres are offset from the joint centre -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Universal joints where the rolling members are being guided in grooves in both coupling parts, the groove centre-lines are lying on spheres and the centres of these spheres (D1, G1) are offset from the joint centre (E1).  Each complete groove centre-line lying on (a part of) a single sphere.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - In case the groove centre-lines are composed of combinations of parts of several spheres -> F16D3/223 .
[F16D 3/226] the groove centre-lines in each coupling part lying on a cylinder co-axial with the respective coupling part -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in F16D3/226 .
[F16D 3/2265] the joints being non-telescopic -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in F16D3/2265 .
[F16D 3/227] the joints being telescopic -- The subclass covers yielding couplings whose joints are telescopic, allowing the connected parts to slide axially while still transmitting rotation. It includes designs such as telescopic drive shafts, expandable couplings and other mechanisms that permit axial displacement during operation.
[F16D 3/229] Prismatic coupling parts having each groove centre-line lying on planes parallel to the axis of the respective coupling part -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in F16D3/229 .
[F16D 3/24] comprising balls, rollers, or the like between overlapping driving faces, e.g. cogs, on both coupling parts -- F16D 3/24 relates to rotational couplings that use balls, rollers or similar elements positioned between overlapping driving faces, such as cogs on both parts, to transmit motion while permitting limited relative movement.
[F16D 3/26] Hooke's joints or other joints with an equivalent intermediate member to which each coupling part is pivotally or slidably connected -- F16D 3/26 pertains to yielding couplings, specifically Hooke's joints or comparable joints that employ an intermediate member to which each coupling part is pivotally or slidably attached, allowing angular movement while transmitting rotational motion.
[F16D 3/265] in which one coupling part has a tongue received with the intermediate member(s) in a recess with a transverse axis in the other coupling part -- This subclass covers yielding couplings where one member carries a tongue that engages an intermediate component seated in a recess of the opposite member, the recess being oriented with a transverse axis. The arrangement permits relative movement between the parts while transmitting torque.
[F16D 3/28] in which the interconnecting pivots include elastic members -- This subclass covers yielding couplings in which the connecting pivots incorporate elastic elements such as springs or flexible rods that allow relative movement while transmitting torque. It pertains to mechanical engineering couplings that use elastic members to permit angular or axial flexibility during operation.
[F16D 3/30] in which the coupling is specially adapted to constant velocity-ratio -- This subclass covers yielding couplings that keep a constant velocity ratio between the connected shafts, such as constant-velocity joints or CV couplings that permit limited angular movement while transmitting rotation. It is used where smooth power transfer with minimal speed fluctuation is required.
[F16D 33/00] Rotary fluid couplings or clutches of the hydrokinetic type -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Retarders -> B60T - Torque converters -> F16H41/00
[F16D 33/02] controlled by changing the flow of the liquid in the working circuit, while maintaining a completely filled working circuit -- The class covers rotary fluid couplings or hydrokinetic clutches whose torque transmission is regulated by varying the liquid flow in a completely filled working circuit, allowing control without opening the circuit.
[F16D 33/04] by altering the position of blades -- F16D 33/04 covers rotary fluid couplings or hydrokinetic clutches whose torque transmission is varied by adjusting the position, angle or configuration of the internal blades. The classification applies to mechanisms that change the fluid flow path or blade geometry to control coupling performance in mechanical engineering systems.
[F16D 33/06] controlled by changing the amount of liquid in the working circuit -- F16D 33/06 covers rotary fluid couplings or hydrokinetic clutches whose speed or torque transmission is regulated by varying the quantity of liquid in the working circuit, such as adjusting fluid volume to change hydraulic resistance or pressure. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for transmitting rotation. It is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F16D 33/08] by devices incorporated in the fluid coupling, with or without remote control -- This subclass of F16D 33 covers rotary fluid couplings or hydrokinetic clutches that incorporate devices such as control valves, sensors or actuators, whether operated locally or by remote-control. It includes any mechanism built into the coupling to regulate fluid flow, torque transmission or engagement.
[F16D 33/10] consisting of controllable supply and discharge openings -- F16D 33/10 relates to rotary fluid couplings or hydro-kinetic clutches that incorporate adjustable supply and discharge openings, enabling the flow of fluid to be regulated during operation. By controlling these openings, the device can vary the timing and magnitude of torque transmission between rotating shafts.
[F16D 33/12] controlled automatically by self-actuated valves -- F16D 33/12 pertains to rotary fluid couplings or hydrokinetic clutches whose engagement and disengagement are automatically controlled by self-actuated valves, eliminating the need for external control signals. The valve action adjusts fluid flow to transmit or interrupt rotation as required.
[F16D 33/14] consisting of shiftable or adjustable scoops -- F16D 33/14 covers rotary fluid couplings or hydrokinetic clutches that incorporate shiftable or adjustable scoops. The scoops can be moved to vary the fluid flow between the rotating parts, providing a continuously or stepwise adjustable transmission of torque or speed.
[F16D 33/16] by means arranged externally of the coupling or clutch -- F16D 33/16 covers rotary fluid couplings or hydro-kinetic clutches in which the actuation or control mechanisms are situated outside the coupling body, such as external valves, pistons or hydraulic circuits that engage or disengage the rotation without being integral to the coupling itself.
[F16D 33/18] Details -- F16D 33/18 pertains to specific details and components of rotary fluid couplings or clutches of the hydrokinetic type, focusing on mechanical engineering elements and units. This classification covers aspects such as design, materials, and operational features that enhance performance and efficiency in these devices.
[F16D 3/32] by the provision of two intermediate members each having two relatively perpendicular trunnions or bearings -- F16D 3/32 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to yielding couplings that incorporate two intermediate members, each equipped with two trunnions or bearings positioned at right angles to one another, allowing for movement between connected parts during operation.
[F16D 33/20] Shape of wheels, blades, or channels with respect to function -- F16D 33/20 relates to the geometry of wheels, blades or channels in rotary fluid couplings or hydro-kinetic clutches, defining how their shape is tailored to achieve specific fluid-flow patterns and torque-transmission functions.
[F16D 3/33] with ball or roller bearings -- F16D 3/33 covers yielding couplings that incorporate ball or roller bearings to accommodate angular, axial or radial displacement while transmitting rotary motion. These bearings provide low friction and allow limited movement between the connected shafts, enhancing overload protection and alignment flexibility.
[F16D 3/34] parts being connected by ridges, pins, balls, or the like guided in grooves or between cogs -- F16D 3/34 covers couplings that join rotating parts by ridges, pins, balls or similar elements guided in complementary grooves or between cogs, allowing limited relative movement while transmitting torque. The design provides a yielding connection that can accommodate misalignment or shock loads.
[F16D 3/36] in which each pivot between the coupling parts and the intermediate member comprises a single ball -- The subclass F16D 3/36 covers yielding couplings that transmit rotation while allowing relative movement, where each pivot between the coupling parts and an intermediate member is formed by a single ball serving as the bearing element. This design provides a simple, ball-type pivot to accommodate angular or axial displacement during operation.
[F16D 3/38] with a single intermediate member with trunnions or bearings arranged on two axes perpendicular to one another -- This subclass covers yielding couplings that use a single intermediate component equipped with trunnions or bearings positioned on two mutually-perpendicular axes, allowing limited relative movement between the connected shafts during power transmission.
[F16D 3/382] constructional details of other than the intermediate member -- F16D 3/382 pertains to the structural details of the outer parts of flexible couplings that transmit rotation, such as hubs, sleeves, or locking elements, while excluding the intermediate elastic member. It addresses how these components are designed or assembled to permit relative movement yet convey torque.
[F16D 3/385] Bearing cup; Bearing construction; Bearing seal; Mounting of bearing on the intermediate member  mounting of bearing in fork  F16D3/382 -- F16D 3/385 pertains to specific aspects of yielding couplings, focusing on the design and installation of bearings within these components. This includes details such as bearing cups, construction, seals, and how bearings are mounted in forks or intermediate members.
[F16D 3/387] Fork construction; Mounting of fork on shaft; Adapting shaft for mounting of fork -- The subclass F16D 3/387 covers fork-type couplings, focusing on the design and installation of the fork on a rotating shaft and on any modifications made to the shaft to receive the fork, typically in yielding couplings that allow limited movement between connected parts during operation.
[F16D 3/40] with intermediate member provided with two pairs of outwardly-directed trunnions on intersecting axes -- F16D 3/40 covers yielding couplings that incorporate an intermediate member equipped with two pairs of outward-directed trunnions mounted on intersecting axes. The arrangement permits limited angular or axial movement between the connected shafts while transmitting rotary power, providing flexibility and shock absorption in mechanical drive systems.
[F16D 3/405] Apparatus for assembling or dismantling -- F16D 3/405 covers tools and equipment designed to assemble or dismantle yielding couplings, which transmit rotational motion while allowing relative movement between connected shafts. The classification includes devices such as removal tools, alignment fixtures and mounting aids used for flexible or elastic torque-transmission couplings.
[F16D 3/41] with ball or roller bearings -- F16D 3/41 covers yielding couplings that incorporate ball or roller bearings, allowing limited relative motion between connected shafts while transmitting rotational power.
[F16D 3/42] with ring-shaped intermediate member provided with bearings or inwardly-directed trunnions -- A yielding coupling that incorporates a circular intermediate component equipped with bearings or inward-directed trunnions, allowing the connected shafts to move relative to each other while transmitting rotational power.
[F16D 3/43] with ball or roller bearings -- This subclass covers yielding couplings that incorporate ball or roller bearings, allowing limited relative movement between connected shafts while transmitting rotational force. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 3/44] the intermediate member being connected to the coupling parts by ridges, pins, balls, or the like guided in grooves or between cogs -- F16D 3/44 covers yielding couplings in which an intermediate member links the two coupling parts by means such as ridges, pins, balls or similar elements that are guided in grooves or between cogs, allowing relative movement during power transmission.
[F16D 3/46] each coupling part embracing grooves or ridges on the intermediate member -- F16D 3/46 covers yielding couplings in which each of the two coupling parts grips an intermediate member by embracing grooves or ridges on that member. The arrangement allows relative movement between the connected shafts while still transmitting torque.
[F16D 3/48] one coupling part having pins arranged parallel to the axis and entering holes in the other coupling part -- The classification covers yielding couplings that use a single coupling element with pins aligned parallel to the rotational axis, which insert into corresponding holes in the mating part. This arrangement permits limited angular, axial or radial movement between the connected shafts while transmitting torque.
[F16D 3/50] with the coupling parts connected by one or more intermediate members -- Yielding couplings in which the two coupling elements are linked by one or more intermediate members that transmit torque while permitting relative movement are classified under F16D 3/50. This sub-group addresses designs that use shafts, pins, or flexible links to connect the parts, allowing angular, axial or torsional compliance during power transmission.
[F16D 35/00] Fluid clutches in which the clutching is predominantly obtained by fluid adhesion -- F16D 35/00 covers fluid clutches that achieve coupling mainly through the adhesion of a fluid film between rotating members, such as hydraulic or oil-based devices where torque transmission is generated by fluid viscosity and molecular attraction rather than mechanical interlocks.
[F16D 35/005] with multiple lamellae -- F16D 35/005 covers fluid clutches whose operating principle relies mainly on fluid adhesion and that incorporate multiple lamellae to transmit torque. The lamellae are typically stacked or arranged to increase contact area and improve clutch performance under varying speed and load conditions. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 35/02] with rotary working chambers and rotary reservoirs, e.g. in one coupling part -- Fluid clutches that achieve engagement mainly through fluid adhesion and incorporate rotating working chambers and rotating reservoirs, typically integrated within a single coupling component, belong to CPC class F16D 35/02. These designs allow continuous fluid circulation and pressure buildup as the chambers spin, providing smooth torque transmission and automatic disengagement when rotation stops.
[F16D 35/021] actuated by valves -- F16D 35/021 covers fluid clutches that rely mainly on fluid adhesion to transmit rotation and are controlled by valves. The clutch engages or disengages when valve actuation alters fluid pressure or flow, enabling precise, rapid torque transmission in mechanical systems. This falls under couplings for transmitting rotation in engineering elements.
[F16D 35/022] the valve being actuated by a bimetallic strip  F16D35/026  takes precedence -- F16D 35/022 covers fluid clutches that transmit rotation by fluid adhesion, focusing on designs where the clutch valve is operated by a bimetallic strip that bends with temperature changes. This subclass is subordinate to F16D 35/026, which has priority over it.
[F16D 35/023] the valve being actuated by a bimetallic coil  F16D35/026  takes precedence -- F16D 35/023 covers fluid clutches in which clutch engagement is controlled by a valve driven by a bimetallic coil, the coil's thermal expansion moving the valve to modulate fluid flow. The bimetallic coil provides temperature-dependent actuation, allowing precise control of the fluid coupling without external power.
[F16D 35/024] the valve being actuated electrically, e.g. by an electromagnet  F16D35/026  takes precedence -- F16D 35/024 covers fluid clutches that achieve engagement mainly through fluid adhesion and where the control valve is operated electrically, for example by an electromagnet. This subclass is subordinate to F16D 35/026, which has priority over it.
[F16D 35/025] the valve being actuated by inertia, e.g. using a flyweight or a centrifugal mass  F16D35/026  takes precedence -- This subclass concerns fluid clutches whose engagement is achieved by moving a valve through inertial action, such as a flyweight or centrifugal mass, to create fluid adhesion for torque transmission. It is located in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under couplings for transmitting rotation, and the more specific subclass F16D 35/026 takes precedence over it.
[F16D 35/026] actuated by a plurality of valves; the valves being actuated by a combination of mechanisms covered by more than one of groups  F16D35/022  -  F16D35/025 -- Fluid clutches that achieve engagement primarily through fluid adhesion and are actuated by several valves, where the valve actuation results from a combination of mechanisms covered by the groups F16D35/022 to F16D35/025, are classified under F16D 35/026.
[F16D 35/027] actuated by emptying and filling with viscous fluid from outside the coupling during operation -- The CPC subclass F16D 35/027 covers fluid-type clutches whose engagement is controlled by emptying or filling the coupling with viscous fluid from outside during operation, using fluid adhesion to transmit rotation. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 35/028] actuated electrically, e.g. by an electromagnet  valves actuated electrically  F16D35/024 -- F16D 35/028 pertains to fluid clutches where the engagement is primarily achieved through fluid adhesion and are actuated electrically, such as by electromagnets or electrically actuated valves. This classification is specific to components within mechanical engineering that control rotational transmission using electrical signals for operation.
[F16D 35/029] actuated by varying the volume of the reservoir chamber -- F16D 35/029 addresses fluid clutches that transmit rotation by changing the volume of a reservoir chamber, which varies the fluid pressure and adhesion between clutch elements. Adjusting the chamber volume allows the clutch to be engaged or disengaged, controlling power transmission in mechanical coupling applications.
[F16D 3/52] comprising a continuous strip, spring, or the like engaging the coupling parts at a number of places -- This subclass covers couplings that use a continuous strip, spring, or similar element contacting the mating parts at multiple points to allow limited movement while transmitting torque. It falls under yielding couplings for rotational transmission within mechanical engineering.
[F16D 3/54] Couplings comprising a chain or strip surrounding two wheels arranged side by side and provided with teeth or the equivalent -- F16D 3/54 designates yielding couplings that transmit rotation by a chain or strip wrapped around a pair of side-by-side wheels that carry teeth or equivalent engaging elements. The toothed chain engages both wheels simultaneously, allowing torque transfer while permitting relative movement of the connected parts.
[F16D 3/56] comprising elastic metal lamellae, elastic rods, or the like, e.g. arranged radially or parallel to the axis, the members being shear-loaded collectively by the total load -- A yielding coupling that transmits rotational motion by using elastic metal lamellae, rods or similar elements arranged radially or parallel to the shaft axis. The elastic members are collectively shear-loaded by the total torque, allowing limited relative movement between the connected parts while still transmitting power. This design falls under the CPC subclass F16D 3/56 for couplings with such shear-loaded elastic components.
