[F16D 3/58] the intermediate members being made of rubber or like material -- F16D 3/58 pertains to yielding couplings that transmit rotation using an intermediate element made of rubber or a similar elastomer, allowing relative movement between the connected parts to absorb shock, vibration or misalignment.
[F16D 3/60] comprising pushing or pulling links attached to both parts -- F16D 3/60 pertains to yielding couplings that employ pushing or pulling links attached to both connected parts, allowing relative movement while transmitting torque. The links transmit forces between the components, providing flexibility and compensation for misalignment during rotation.
[F16D 3/62] the links or their attachments being elastic -- F16D 3/62 covers yielding couplings that use elastic links or elastic attachments to permit relative movement between connected parts while transmitting rotational torque. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for couplings that transmit rotation, emphasizing the use of elasticity to absorb misalignment or shock.
[F16D 3/64] comprising elastic elements arranged between substantially-radial walls of both coupling parts -- F16D 3/64 denotes couplings for transmitting rotation that incorporate elastic elements placed between the substantially radial walls of the two coupled parts. The elastic members permit limited relative movement while torque is transmitted, providing a yielding or shock-absorbing function.
[F16D 3/66] the elements being metallic, e.g. in the form of coils -- The code refers to yielding couplings in which the functional parts are metal, such as coil springs or other metallic elements that permit relative movement while transmitting rotational torque.
[F16D 3/68] the elements being made of rubber or similar material -- F16D 3/68 covers yielding couplings used to transmit rotary motion that incorporate elements made of rubber or similar elastomeric materials, providing flexibility, vibration damping and shock-absorption while allowing limited movement between the connected shafts.
[F16D 3/70] comprising elastic elements arranged in holes in one coupling part and surrounding pins on the other coupling part -- The subclass F16D 3/70 covers yielding couplings that transmit rotation while incorporating elastic elements placed in holes of one coupling part and surrounding pins of the opposite part, allowing limited movement between the linked components during drive. It falls under mechanical-engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 37/00] Clutches in which the drive is transmitted through a medium consisting of small particles, e.g. centrifugally speed-responsive -- The CPC class F16D 37/00 covers clutches that transmit drive by means of a particulate medium such as granules or powders, which engage or disengage in response to centrifugal speed changes. These devices are a type of coupling for transmitting rotation within mechanical engineering elements.
[F16D 37/008] the particles being carried by a fluid, to vary viscosity when subjected to electric change, i.e. electro-rheological or smart fluids  composition of such fluids  C10M171/001 -- This subgroup covers clutches that transmit torque through a fluid containing fine particles whose viscosity can be altered by an electric field, i.e., electro-rheological or smart fluids. It also includes the formulation of such fluids for controlling clutch operation.
[F16D 37/02] the particles being magnetisable -- F16D 37/02 covers clutches that transmit rotation through a medium of small magnetisable particles, such as magnetic powder in a speed-responsive centrifugal clutch.
[F16D 3/72] with axially-spaced attachments to the coupling parts -- This subclass refers to yielding couplings that have attachment points spaced apart along the axis of the shaft, allowing torque transmission while permitting relative axial movement. They are used where flexibility between connected shafts is needed, such as in machinery with misalignment.
[F16D 3/725] with an intermediate member made of fibre-reinforced resin  made of rubber-like material  F16D3/74 ; shafts made of fibre-reinforced resin  F16C3/026 -- F16D 3/725 pertains to yielding couplings that incorporate an intermediate member made of fibre-reinforced resin with rubber-like characteristics, and may also involve shafts constructed from the same fibre-reinforced resin material. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering couplings designed to permit relative movement between connected parts during drive.
[F16D 3/74] the intermediate member or members being made of rubber or other flexible material -- This subclass covers yielding rotary couplings that incorporate an intermediate member-or multiple members-made of rubber or other flexible materials, allowing relative movement between the connected shafts while transmitting torque. The flexible element absorbs misalignment and vibration.
[F16D 3/76] shaped as an elastic ring centered on the axis, surrounding a portion of one coupling part and surrounded by a sleeve of the other coupling part -- A yielding coupling in which an elastic ring is positioned on the common axis, covering part of one coupling component and encircled by a sleeve of the other component. The elastic ring permits limited relative movement between the parts while transmitting rotational drive.
[F16D 3/77] the ring being metallic -- F16D 3/77 pertains to yielding couplings that use a metallic ring as the flexible element, allowing relative movement between connected shafts while transmitting torque. The metal ring accommodates axial, angular or radial misalignment, providing a durable, elastic connection within rotary power transmission systems.
[F16D 3/78] shaped as an elastic disc or flat ring, arranged perpendicular to the axis of the coupling parts, different sets of spots of the disc or ring being attached to each coupling part, e.g. Hardy couplings -- F16D 3/78 covers yielding couplings that use an elastic disc or flat ring positioned perpendicular to the coupling axis, with separate groups of attachment spots on the disc for each connected part. The disc provides flexibility while transmitting rotation and is exemplified by Hardy couplings.
[F16D 3/79] the disc or ring being metallic -- This subclass covers yielding couplings that transmit rotation using a metallic disc or ring as the flexible element, allowing limited angular, axial or radial movement between the connected shafts while maintaining power transfer.
[F16D 3/80] in which a fluid is used -- F16D 3/80 covers yielding couplings that use a fluid such as oil, water or gas to transmit rotary motion while allowing relative movement between the connected shafts. The fluid acts as a torsional buffer or damping medium, providing flexibility and shock absorption in the drive.
[F16D 3/82] with a coupling element in the form of a pneumatic tube -- F16D 3/82 covers yielding couplings where the coupling element is a pneumatic tube that transmits rotary motion while allowing relative movement between connected shafts. The design uses pressurised air within the tube to absorb shocks and accommodate angular or axial mis-alignment.
[F16D 3/84] Shrouds, e.g. casings, covers -- F16D 3/84 designates shrouds, casings or covers that encase yielding couplings used for transmitting rotation, providing protection, containment of lubricants and safety for the moving parts. These elements are part of mechanical engineering's coupling assemblies and enable controlled movement between connected shafts while shielding the coupling from external contamination.
[F16D 3/841] Open covers, e.g. guards for agricultural p.t.o. shafts -- The CPC code F16D 3/841 covers open protective covers such as guards for agricultural power-take-off shafts, which are regarded as yielding couplings that permit relative movement between connected parts during rotation. It is situated under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 3/843] enclosed covers -- The subclass F16D 3/843 concerns enclosed covers for yielding couplings that transmit rotation, specifying protective housings that shield the coupling's moving parts while permitting the permitted relative motion during drive. These covers are intended to guard the mechanism from dust, debris or fluid leakage and to contain lubrication within the coupling assembly.
[F16D 3/845] allowing relative movement of joint parts due to the flexing of the cover -- F16D 3/845 covers yielding couplings in which the cover flexes to permit relative movement of the connected parts during power transmission. The design allows the joint to accommodate slight misalignments or load variations while still transmitting rotation, improving durability and reducing stress on the coupling components.
[F16D 3/848] allowing relative movement of joint parts due to sliding between parts of the cover -- The CPC subclass F16D 3/848 covers couplings that achieve relative movement of their joint parts by allowing sliding between the components of the cover. Such yielding couplings enable limited displacement while still transmitting rotational motion, providing flexibility to accommodate misalignment, thermal expansion or shock loading.
[F16D 39/00] Combinations of couplings according to two or more of the groups -- This subclass covers devices that integrate two or more different types of rotational couplings from the surrounding groups, defining hybrid or composite coupling arrangements that combine the features, functions or mechanisms of the individual couplings to achieve specific performance characteristics.
[F16D 41/00] Freewheels or freewheel clutches -- This subclass covers devices that allow rotation in one-direction while preventing it in the opposite, commonly used in bicycle gears, automotive transmissions and machinery. They consist of mechanisms such as ratchets, sprags or overrunning clutches that engage to transmit torque only when driven in the forward direction.
[F16D 41/02] disengaged by contact of a part of or on the freewheel or freewheel clutch with a stationarily-mounted member -- This class covers freewheel or freewheel clutch mechanisms that are disengaged when a component of the device contacts a stationary part fixed to the housing or frame. The disengagement is achieved by direct contact rather than by springs, hydraulics or other actuators, separating the driving and driven members.
[F16D 41/04] combined with a clutch for locking the driving and driven members -- This subgroup covers freewheel or freewheel clutch mechanisms that incorporate a clutch capable of locking the driving and driven members together, enabling selective transmission of torque when engaged. It is applied in systems where rotation may be freely transmitted or deliberately restrained.
[F16D 41/06] with intermediate wedging coupling members between an inner and an outer surface -- F16D 41/06 covers freewheel or freewheel clutches that incorporate one or more intermediate wedging coupling members placed between an inner and an outer surface, permitting torque transmission in one direction while slipping in the opposite direction.
[F16D 41/061] the intermediate members wedging by movement having an axial component -- A freewheel or freewheel clutch in which an intermediate member engages by wedging as it moves with an axial component, allowing torque to be transmitted in one direction only. This design is classified under mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 41/063] the intermediate members wedging by moving along the inner and the outer surface without pivoting or rolling, e.g. sliding wedges -- F16D 41/063 covers intermediate members that wedge by sliding along both inner and outer surfaces of a freewheel or freewheel clutch, without pivoting or rolling. The design typically uses sliding wedges to transmit rotation while allowing disengagement.
[F16D 41/064] the intermediate members wedging by rolling and having a circular cross-section, e.g. balls -- It refers to freewheel clutch assemblies in which rolling elements such as balls serve as intermediate wedging members, transferring torque through rolling contact while allowing disengagement. These elements have a circular cross-section and provide a wedging action during rotation.
[F16D 41/066] all members having the same size and only one of the two surfaces being cylindrical -- This subclass pertains to freewheel or freewheel clutch mechanisms in which all rotating components share the same overall size, but only one of the two contacting surfaces is cylindrical, the other having a different profile. The design transmits rotation in one direction while allowing free motion the opposite way.
[F16D 41/067] and the members being distributed by a separate cage encircling the axis of rotation -- F16D 41/067 classifies freewheel or freewheel clutch assemblies in which the rotating members are arranged on a distinct cage that surrounds the shaft axis, separating the elements that engage and disengage the motion. This structure isolates the moving parts, allowing one-way drive while the cage maintains their spacing and alignment.
[F16D 41/069] the intermediate members wedging by pivoting or rocking, e.g. sprags -- F16D 41/069 covers freewheel or freewheel clutch mechanisms in which an intermediate component, such as a sprag, wedges by pivoting or rocking motion to transmit torque in one direction while allowing free rotation in the opposite direction. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation, focusing on the wedging action of pivoted elements.
[F16D 41/07] between two cylindrical surfaces -- The subclass F16D 41/07 covers freewheel or freewheel-clutch devices in which the rotating element is positioned between two cylindrical surfaces, typically a shaft and a surrounding hollow cylinder, allowing torque transmission in one direction while slipping in the opposite direction. This arrangement is used in mechanical engineering for coupling components that need to lock rotation in a single sense without additional springs or springs-like mechanisms.
[F16D 41/073] each member comprising at least two elements at different radii -- This subclass covers freewheel clutches whose individual member is built from at least two components positioned at different radii from the rotation axis, allowing differential engagement. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation, freewheels or freewheel clutches.
[F16D 41/076] the wedging coupling members being non-releasably joined to form a single annular piece, e.g. either the members being integral projections from the piece, or the piece being an elastic ring cast round the radial centres of the members -- This subclass covers wedging coupling members that are permanently joined to create a single annular component, for example by forming the members as integral projections of the ring or by casting an elastic ring around the radial centers of the members.
[F16D 41/08] with provision for altering the freewheeling action -- F16D 41/08 covers freewheel clutches that include a mechanism to modify or control the freewheeling function, allowing the degree or timing of disengagement to be adjusted during operation. Such devices are used in mechanical systems where variable torque transmission is required.
[F16D 41/082] the intermediate coupling members wedging by movement other than pivoting or rolling -- F16D 41/082 covers intermediate coupling components for freewheel or freewheel clutches where the wedging action is produced by a motion other than pivoting or rolling, such as linear or axial displacement. These members engage or disengage the transmission of rotation through a straight-line or similar movement.
[F16D 41/084] the intermediate coupling members wedging by pivoting or rocking -- The CPC class F16D 41/084 covers freewheel or freewheel clutch couplings in which an intermediate member wedges into the drive train by a pivoting or rocking motion. It addresses the geometry and operation of these wedging coupling elements that transmit rotation only in one direction.
[F16D 41/086] the intermediate members being of circular cross-section and wedging by rolling  F16D41/10  takes precedence -- This subclass covers freewheel or freewheel clutch mechanisms where the intermediate member has a circular cross-section and transmits torque by rolling wedge action. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 41/088] the intermediate members being of only one size and wedging by a movement not having an axial component, between inner and outer races, one of which is cylindrical -- F16D 41/088 covers freewheel or freewheel clutch designs where a single-size intermediate member wedges between the inner and outer races through a motion that lacks any axial component; typically one race is cylindrical and the wedging action occurs purely radially, enabling one-way torque transmission.
[F16D 41/10] with self-actuated reversing -- F16D 41/10 covers freewheel or freewheel clutch mechanisms that automatically reverse their engagement when the direction of rotation changes, without external control. These self-actuated reversing devices are used in gearboxes, drivetrains and other mechanical systems to permit one-way drive and to switch to the opposite direction when reverse torque is applied.
[F16D 41/105] the intermediate members being of circular cross-section, of only one size and wedging by rolling movement not having an axial component between inner and outer races, one of which is cylindrical -- Freewheel or freewheel clutch in which a single circular intermediate member of one size wedges by a pure rolling motion between an inner and an outer cylindrical race, transmitting rotation without any axial component of force.
[F16D 41/12] with hinged pawl co-operating with teeth, cogs, or the like -- This subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers freewheel clutches in which a hinged pawl engages with teeth, cogs or similar gear elements to lock or release rotational motion. The hinged design allows the pawl to swing into engagement when driven and to swing out when the driven side overtakes the driver, providing one-way torque transmission.
[F16D 41/125] the pawl movement having an axial component -- F16D 41/125 covers freewheel or freewheel clutch designs where the pawl that locks rotation moves with an axial component, typically sliding along the shaft as it rotates to engage or release the clutch. This axial motion enables controlled engagement, improves torque transmission, and prevents reverse rotation.
[F16D 41/14] the effective stroke of the pawl being adjustable -- The classification F16D 41/14 covers freewheel or freewheel clutch devices whose pawl travel can be varied. It pertains to mechanisms that transmit rotational motion while allowing adjustment of the effective stroke of the pawl to control the engagement point, improving performance in couplings and similar engineering elements.
[F16D 41/16] the action being reversible -- F16D 41/16 pertains to freewheel clutches whose operating principle allows the transmission to be reversed, meaning the device can engage to transmit torque in one direction and either disengage or transmit in the opposite direction when the input motion is reversed. This reversible action is used in mechanisms requiring bidirectional drive control while preventing back-driving.
[F16D 41/18] with non-hinged detent -- F16D 41/18 covers freewheels or freewheel clutches that incorporate a non-hinged detent - a fixed locking element that engages to hold the driven member when reverse rotation is prevented, rather than using a hinged or pivoted detent mechanism.
[F16D 41/185] the engaging movement having an axial component -- The subclass covers freewheel or freewheel clutches whose engaging movement includes an axial component, such as a sliding or helical motion that brings the clutch elements together to transmit rotation.
[F16D 41/20] with expandable or contractable clamping ring or band -- The CPC subclass F16D 41/20 refers to freewheel or freewheel clutch mechanisms that employ an expandable or contractable clamping ring or band to transmit rotation when engaged. The clamping element adjusts radially to lock or release the driven component, providing a simple means of one-way torque transmission in mechanical systems.
[F16D 41/203] having coils overlapping in a single radial plane, e.g. Archimedian spiral -- The subclass covers freewheel or freewheel clutch mechanisms in which the internal coils overlap within a single radial plane, typically formed as an Archimedean spiral. This coil arrangement enables one-way torque transmission while allowing slip in the opposite direction.
[F16D 41/206] having axially adjacent coils, e.g. helical wrap-springs -- This CPC subclass covers freewheel or freewheel clutch devices in which the friction or locking elements consist of axially adjacent coils, such as helical wrap-springs, that engage to transmit rotation when driven and disengage when reverse torque occurs. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 41/22] with clutching ring or disc axially shifted as a result of lost motion between actuating members -- This subgroup addresses freewheel or freewheel clutch mechanisms in which a clutching ring or disc is shifted axially as a result of lost motion between the actuating members, allowing the clutch to engage or disengage after the clearance is taken up.
[F16D 41/24] specially adapted for cycles -- F16D 41/24 covers freewheel or freewheel-clutch devices specially adapted for bicycles. These mechanisms allow the driven wheel to rotate while the pedal drive is disengaged, enabling coasting and often being integrated into chain or gear hubs for efficient cycling.
[F16D 41/26] with provision for altering the action -- F16D 41/26 refers to freewheel or freewheel clutches that incorporate a mechanism for altering the clutch action, such as adjustable slip, variable locking or selectable engagement modes. The subclass is situated under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → couplings for transmitting rotation, and it covers devices that can be tuned to change how they transmit rotation in one direction.
[F16D 41/28] with intermediate wedging coupling members -- F16D 41/28 covers freewheel or freewheel clutch devices that incorporate intermediate wedging coupling members to transmit rotation. The wedging elements engage or disengage by axial movement, allowing torque to be transmitted in one direction while permitting free rotation in the opposite direction. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 41/30] with hinged pawl co-operating with teeth, cogs, or the like -- F16D 41/30 covers freewheel or freewheel clutch mechanisms that employ a hinged pawl to engage the teeth of a gear, cog or similar member, transmitting torque in one direction while allowing free rotation in the opposite direction. The pawl pivots to lock with the gear teeth during forward drive and swings away when reverse torque is applied, providing a simple one-way drive coupling.
[F16D 41/32] with non-hinged detent -- A freewheel or freewheel clutch that includes a detent which is fixed rather than hinged, enabling the device to lock in a selected position when rotation stops. The non-hinged detent provides positive engagement without a movable pivot, improving reliability in transmission of rotational motion.
[F16D 41/34] with expandable or contractable clamping ring or band -- The CPC subclass F16D 41/34 covers freewheel or freewheel clutch mechanisms that incorporate an expandable or contractable clamping ring or band to engage or disengage rotation. This design provides a variable-pressure coupling element within the broader category of mechanical couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 41/36] with clutching ring or disc axially shifted as a result of lost motion between actuating members -- A freewheel or freewheel clutch in which the clutching ring or disc is displaced axially when there is lost motion between the actuating components, allowing the clutch to engage or disengage based on relative movement. This arrangement provides automatic transmission of rotation while preventing reverse torque.
[F16D 43/00] Automatic clutches -- Automatic clutches are mechanisms that automatically engage or disengage rotational power transmission in response to speed, torque, temperature or other operating conditions. They are used in automotive, industrial and aerospace systems to provide smooth start-up, load protection and precise control without manual intervention. This CPC entry falls under mechanical engineering, specifically couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 43/02] actuated entirely mechanically -- F16D 43/02 covers automatic clutches whose engagement and disengagement are achieved solely by mechanical means, such as springs, cams, or gear-driven linkages, without hydraulic, pneumatic or electrical actuation. These devices are used in transmitting rotation between shafts where a self-acting, speed-or torque-responsive clutch is required.
[F16D 43/04] controlled by angular speed -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Clutches in which the drive is transmitted through a medium consisting of small particles -> F16D37/00
[F16D 43/06] with centrifugal masses actuating axially a movable pressure ring or the like -- F16D 43/06 covers automatic clutches that use centrifugal masses to move a pressure ring axially, engaging or disengaging the clutch without manual input. As rotation speed increases, the outward-acting weights push the movable pressure ring, allowing torque to be transmitted automatically.
[F16D 43/08] the pressure ring actuating friction plates, cones or similar axially-movable friction surfaces -- This subclass deals with automatic clutch designs in which a pressure ring moves axially to engage friction plates, cones or comparable friction surfaces, thereby transmitting rotation. It focuses on the actuation mechanism that presses these friction elements together to control clutch engagement.
[F16D 43/09] in which the carrier of the centrifugal masses can be stopped -- This subclass concerns automatic centrifugal clutches that include a means for arresting the carrier holding the centrifugal masses, thereby stopping its rotation to disengage the clutch. It falls under mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation and specifies the stopping of the carrier as a control feature.
[F16D 43/10] the centrifugal masses acting directly on the pressure ring, no other actuating mechanism for the pressure ring being provided -- The subclass F16D 43/10 covers automatic clutches in which centrifugal masses act directly on the pressure ring, providing actuation without any additional mechanism for moving the pressure ring. It pertains to mechanical engineering couplings that transmit rotation, specifically those that rely solely on centrifugal force to engage the clutch.
[F16D 43/12] the centrifugal masses acting on, or forming a part of, an actuating mechanism by which the pressure ring can also be actuated independently of the masses -- This subclass covers automatic clutches in which centrifugal masses are used as part of the actuation mechanism and where the pressure ring can be moved separately from those masses. It relates to devices that transmit rotation by using centrifugal force to engage or disengage the clutch, with independent control of the pressure ring.
[F16D 43/14] with centrifugal masses actuating the clutching members directly in a direction which has at least a radial component -- F16D 43/14 covers automatic clutches in which centrifugal masses activate the clutching elements by moving them directly in a direction that includes a radial component, causing engagement or disengagement without intermediate linkages. These mechanisms are used where speed-dependent torque transmission is required, such as in wind-up toys, small machinery or vehicle accessories.
[F16D 43/16] with clutching members having interengaging parts -- F16D 43/16 covers automatic clutches whose clutching members incorporate interengaging components such as teeth, splines or keys that mesh to transmit torque. These devices enable rotation to be engaged or disengaged without manual control in various mechanical systems.
[F16D 43/18] with friction clutching members -- This subclass pertains to automatic clutches that transmit torque by means of friction-engaged members such as plates, discs or wedges. The friction elements provide the clutching action without hydraulic, electromagnetic or other actuation and are commonly used in gearboxes, vehicle drivetrains and industrial power transmission.
[F16D 43/20] controlled by torque, e.g. overload-release clutches, slip-clutches with means by which torque varies the clutching pressure -- F16D 43/20 covers automatic clutches whose engagement pressure is varied according to the transmitted torque, such as overload-release or slip-clutches that reduce or release coupling when torque exceeds a set value. These devices use torque-dependent mechanisms to adjust clutch pressure for protection or speed control.
[F16D 43/202] of the ratchet type -- F16D 43/202 pertains to ratchet-type automatic clutches, a subclass of coupling devices that automatically engage to transmit rotational motion through a ratchet mechanism. These clutches are used where a one-way transmission of torque is required without manual control.
[F16D 43/2022] with at least one part moving axially between engagement and disengagement  F16D43/206  takes precedence -- A CPC subclass for automatic clutches whose design includes at least one part that moves axially to shift between engaged and disengaged positions, situated under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → couplings for transmitting rotation. The more specific subclass F16D 43/206 takes precedence over this entry.
[F16D 43/2024] the axially moving part being coaxial with the rotation, e.g. a gear with face teeth -- This subclass covers automatic clutches in which the part that moves axially is positioned on the same axis as the rotating member, for example a gear with face teeth that slides along the shaft to engage or disengage torque transmission. It is part of the mechanical-engineering group for couplings that transmit rotation.
[F16D 43/2026] with a plurality of axially moving parts -- The CPC subclass F16D 43/2026 refers to automatic clutches that incorporate several parts moving axially relative to each other to transmit rotary motion. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically couplings for transmitting rotation, and details designs where multiple axially shifting components cooperate to engage or disengage power.
[F16D 43/2028] with at least one part moving radially between engagement and disengagement  F16D43/208  takes precedence -- F16D 43/2028 covers automatic clutches in which at least one component shifts radially to engage or disengage the drive. The radial movement provides the clutching action within couplings that transmit rotary power, and the subclass is superseded by the more specific F16D 43/208. This falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 43/204] with intermediate balls or rollers -- F16D 43/204 covers automatic clutches that transmit rotation through one or more intermediate balls or rollers positioned between the driving and driven members. These devices use the balls or rollers to engage or disengage the drive as speed or load changes, providing smooth torque transmission without manual intervention.
[F16D 43/206] moving axially between engagement and disengagement -- F16D 43/206 covers self-acting automatic clutches whose friction or coupling elements shift axially to engage or disengage the transmission of rotation. The design lets the clutch move from a disengaged to an engaged position without manual actuation, typically using springs, hydraulic pressure or centrifugal forces.
[F16D 43/208] moving radially between engagement and disengagement -- F16D 43/208 relates to automatic clutches that shift radially to engage or disengage a rotating drive, providing torque transmission control without manual operation. The radial motion moves the clutch elements between engaged and disengaged positions, allowing automatic response to speed or load changes.
[F16D 43/21] with friction members -- The CPC subclass F16D 43/21 covers automatic clutches that transmit rotary motion by means of friction members. These devices engage or disengage power without manual control, using friction surfaces to transfer torque within mechanical engineering couplings.
[F16D 43/211] with radially applied torque-limiting friction surfaces -- The subclass F16D 43/211 covers automatic clutches that limit torque by using friction surfaces arranged radially on the rotating member. These devices transmit rotation but slip when the applied torque exceeds a preset value, protecting downstream components, and belong to the mechanical-engineering group for couplings that transmit rotation.
[F16D 43/213] with axially applied torque-limiting friction surfaces -- An automatic clutch designed to limit torque by using friction surfaces that engage when torque is applied along the axle. The friction elements are arranged to slip axially, protecting the drive train from overload while maintaining rotational transmission. This falls under mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 43/215] with flat friction surfaces, e.g. discs -- F16D 43/215 covers automatic clutches that use flat friction surfaces such as discs to transmit rotational motion between coupled shafts. The design enables smooth engagement and disengagement without manual intervention, typically employing spring or hydraulic actuation. These devices are used in automotive and industrial power transmissions.
[F16D 43/216] with multiple lamellae -- F16D 43/216 relates to automatic clutch mechanisms that use multiple lamellae-separate friction plates or discs arranged to engage and transmit torque without manual actuation. The multiple-lamella design enables staged engagement, improving torque control and wear distribution in rotating couplings.
[F16D 43/218] with conical friction surfaces -- This subclass covers automatic clutch mechanisms that employ conical friction surfaces to engage and disengage rotational power. The conical design provides smooth torque transmission and self-adjusting contact pressure, used in various mechanical drive systems.
[F16D 43/22] controlled by both speed and torque -- F16D 43/22 covers automatic clutches whose operation is regulated by a combination of rotational speed and transmitted torque. These devices adjust engagement or disengagement based on both parameters to protect machinery, control power flow, or modify performance in rotating systems.
[F16D 43/24] controlled by acceleration or deceleration of angular speed -- F16D 43/24 designates automatic clutches that engage or disengage based on the acceleration or deceleration of the rotating shaft, using the rate of change of angular speed to control torque transmission. These devices self-adjust without external commands, providing speed-dependent coupling in mechanical power-train applications.
[F16D 43/25] controlled by thermo-responsive elements -- F16D 43/25 covers automatic clutches that are actuated by thermo-responsive elements, using temperature-sensitive materials or devices to engage or disengage torque transmission without external control signals. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 43/26] acting at definite angular position or disengaging after definite number of rotations -- The F16D 43/26 subclass concerns automatic clutches that engage or release at a predetermined angular position or after a specified number of revolutions. It includes devices that monitor rotation count or angular displacement to control the transmission of rotary motion within mechanical systems.
[F16D 43/28] actuated by fluid pressure -- F16D 43/28 designates automatic clutches whose engagement or disengagement is controlled by fluid pressure. Such clutches use hydraulic, pneumatic, or other fluid-driven actuators to transmit rotational motion without manual intervention.
[F16D 43/284] controlled by angular speed -- Automatic clutches that engage or disengage based on the rotational speed of the driven shaft. These devices modulate torque transmission without external control signals, using speed-dependent mechanisms such as centrifugal weights or hydraulic valves. They are used in machinery to protect against overload and to optimize performance.
[F16D 43/286] controlled by torque -- F16D 43/286 designates automatic clutches that are controlled by the torque transmitted through the coupling. These devices sense the applied torque and engage or disengage without external actuation, providing automatic load protection or speed regulation in mechanical power transmission systems.
[F16D 43/30] Systems of a plurality of automatic clutches -- F16D 43/30 covers arrangements that incorporate two or more automatic clutches within a single drivetrain or transmission to selectively engage and disengage power paths without operator input, often using hydraulic, pneumatic or electronic control. These systems coordinate clutch operation to allow smooth gear changes, torque distribution or redundancy in machinery such as vehicles, conveyors or industrial equipment.
[F16D 45/00] Freewheels or freewheel clutches combined with automatic clutches -- The CPC subclass F16D 45/00 covers mechanisms that integrate a freewheel or freewheel clutch with an automatic clutch, allowing torque to be transmitted in one direction while automatically disengaging in the opposite direction, commonly employed in gearboxes and transmission systems.
[F16D 47/00] Systems of clutches, or clutches and couplings, comprising devices of types grouped under at least two of the following sets of groups: -- F16D 47/00 covers systems of clutches or couplings in which the arrangement incorporates devices belonging to at least two of the defined groups of clutch or coupling types. These hybrid assemblies combine different operating principles to transmit rotation or control motion. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 47/02] of which at least one is a coupling -- The F16D 47/02 subclass covers systems that integrate two or more clutch types, where at least one of the elements is a coupling. It pertains to mechanical engineering arrangements that combine distinct clutch groups to transmit rotation or control motion.
[F16D 47/04] of which at least one is a freewheel -- F16D 47/04 covers systems of clutches or couplings that combine devices from at least two different groups, where one of the devices functions as a freewheel (a one-way clutch). It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for transmitting rotation. The classification is used to identify hybrid coupling arrangements that include a freewheel element.
[F16D 47/06] of which at least one is a clutch with a fluid or a semifluid as power-transmitting means -- F16D 47/06 covers systems of clutches or couplings that incorporate at least one clutch employing a fluid or semifluid as the power-transmitting medium, typically for torque modulation or smooth engagement in rotating machinery.
[F16D 48/00] External control of clutches -- It covers clutch mechanisms whose engagement or disengagement is operated by an external device such as hydraulic, pneumatic, electric or electronic actuators rather than directly by the rotating shafts. These systems are used to control power transmission in gearboxes, automotive drivetrains and industrial machinery. The classification groups designs that separate the control and clutch elements.
[F16D 48/02] Control by fluid pressure -- F16D 48/02 relates to clutch mechanisms that are engaged or released by fluid pressure, using hydraulic or pneumatic actuators, valves and control circuits to manage the transmission of rotational motion.
[F16D 48/0206] in a system with a plurality of fluid-actuated clutches -- F16D 48/0206 covers arrangements where a plurality of fluid-actuated clutches are controlled by an external mechanism rather than by the fluid pressure generated within each clutch. It includes hydraulic or pneumatic control circuits that command engagement or release of multiple clutches in a transmission or power-train system.
[F16D 48/04] providing power assistance -- This subclass covers mechanisms that externally control a clutch to supply additional power, such as hydraulic, pneumatic or electric assist systems that augment torque transmission in rotating couplings. It includes devices that modulate clutch engagement using an external power source to improve performance or reduce operator effort.
[F16D 48/06] Control by electric or electronic means, e.g. of fluid pressure -- The F16D 48/06 class covers clutches that are governed by electric or electronic devices, typically using signals to adjust hydraulic or other fluid pressure that engages or releases the coupling. It addresses external electronic control of the clutch's torque-transmission function within mechanical engineering.
[F16D 48/062] of a clutch system with a plurality of fluid actuated clutches -- This CPC subclass covers clutch systems that incorporate several fluid-actuated clutches whose operation is governed from outside the gearbox. It relates to arrangements where hydraulic or pneumatic pressure is used to engage or disengage multiple clutch elements, allowing selective transmission of torque.
[F16D 48/064] Control of electrically or electromagnetically actuated clutches  F16D48/062 ,  F16D48/068  take precedence -- F16D 48/064 covers the external control of clutches that are actuated by electrical or electromagnetic means, including the design of control circuits, power-supply arrangements, signal processing and sensor integration used to engage or disengage the clutch. The subclass addresses control strategies and hardware that manage the actuation, while related subclasses F16D48/062 and F16D48/068 deal with specific electrical or magnetic actuation mechanisms.
[F16D 48/066] Control of fluid pressure, e.g. using an accumulator  F16D48/062 ,  F16D48/068  take precedence -- This subgroup covers mechanisms that regulate hydraulic or pneumatic pressure in clutch systems by means of an accumulator or similar pressure-storage device, enabling precise external control of clutch engagement or disengagement. It is a subcategory of couplings for transmitting rotation in mechanical engineering.
[F16D 48/068] using signals from a manually actuated gearshift linkage -- F16D 48/068 covers clutch-control arrangements that derive actuation signals from a manually operated gear-shift linkage, allowing the driver's gear-selection motion to command clutch engagement or release. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering pathway of engineering elements and units, specifically couplings for transmitting rotation and the external control of clutches.
[F16D 48/08] Regulating clutch take-up on starting -- F16D 48/08 covers mechanisms that regulate the take-up of a clutch during the start-up phase, typically by adjusting the engagement force or position to ensure smooth transmission of rotation. It falls under mechanical engineering couplings and addresses external control methods for starter clutches.
[F16D 48/10] Preventing unintentional or unsafe engagement -- This classification covers features and mechanisms that stop a clutch from engaging accidentally or in hazardous conditions, such as safety interlocks, sensor-based controls, or mechanical blockers that require a deliberate signal before transmission of rotation is allowed. It applies to couplings where external control is used to manage engagement safety.
[F16D 49/00] Brakes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  External drum brakes.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Bicycle brakes engaging the tyre surface -> B62L1/04
[F16D 49/02] shaped as a helical band or coil with more than one turn, with or without intensification of the braking force by the tension of the band or contracting member -- F16D 49/02 covers brake devices formed as helical bands or multi-turn coils, where the band may be tensioned or contracted to increase the braking force.
[F16D 49/04] mechanically actuated -- F16D 49/04 covers brakes that are set into operation by a purely mechanical device such as a lever, cam, gear or spring, without reliance on hydraulic, pneumatic or electrical power.
[F16D 49/06] fluid actuated -- F16D 49/06 pertains to brake couplings that are operated by hydraulic or pneumatic fluid pressure to transmit or stop rotational motion. These devices employ fluid actuation to engage or release the braking mechanism in mechanical transmission systems.
[F16D 49/08] shaped as an encircling band extending over approximately 360 degrees -- F16D 49/08 concerns brakes formed as a continuous band that wraps around a rotating element for about a full circle, providing frictional resistance to motion. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation, and the brake subgroup. These band-type brakes are used where an encircling belt or strap applies pressure uniformly around the shaft.
[F16D 49/10] mechanically actuated -- F16D 49/10 covers brakes that are set in motion by a purely mechanical action, such as a lever, cam, gear or linkage, rather than by hydraulic, pneumatic or electrical means. The subclass includes devices for stopping or holding rotation in couplings where the braking force is applied directly through mechanical components. This category is used for describing manually or mechanically driven braking mechanisms in rotating transmission systems.
[F16D 49/12] fluid actuated -- F16D 49/12 falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation, specifically brakes. It covers fluid-actuated brake couplings that use hydraulic or pneumatic pressure to transmit rotational force and to engage or release a brake. Such devices are employed where fluid power controls braking action in rotating machinery.
[F16D 49/14] shaped as a fluid-filled flexible member actuated by variation of the fluid pressure -- F16D 49/14 pertains to brake systems where the braking force is applied through a flexible, fluid-filled component that changes shape in response to variations in fluid pressure. This method allows for precise control and application of braking forces in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16D 49/16] Brakes with two brake-blocks -- F16D 49/16 covers braking devices that employ a pair of brake blocks to engage a rotating shaft or drum, providing stopping torque by friction on both sides of the drive element. The class is situated within mechanical engineering under couplings for transmitting rotation, specifically the brake subcategory.
[F16D 49/18] Brakes with three or more brake-blocks -- F16D 49/18 covers braking systems that use three or more brake blocks to apply friction and stop rotation, such as multi-pad disc brakes, drum brakes with several shoes, or other devices where several blocks engage a rotating component simultaneously.
[F16D 49/20] Self-tightening brakes -- Self-tightening brakes are coupling devices that automatically increase braking force as rotation continues, using mechanisms that tighten the brake shoe or pad without external adjustment. They are employed where continuous, reliable stopping is required, such as in safety-critical machinery.
[F16D 49/22] with an auxiliary friction member initiating or increasing the action of the brake -- F16D 49/22 refers to brake mechanisms that incorporate an auxiliary friction element which is used to start or amplify the braking effect. The additional member may be a secondary pad, shoe or similar component that engages alongside the primary brake to increase torque-reduction. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 5/00] Impulse couplings, i.e. couplings that alternately accelerate and decelerate the driven member -- Impulse couplings are mechanical devices that transmit rotation by periodically accelerating and decelerating the driven member, providing intermittent power transfer, shock absorption or overload protection in rotating systems.
[F16D 51/00] Brakes with outwardly-movable braking members co-operating with the inner surface of a drum or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Internal drum brakes
[F16D 51/02] shaped as one or more circumferential band -- F16D 51/02 covers brakes whose outwardly-movable braking members are formed as one or more circumferential bands that engage the inner surface of a rotating drum or similar member. The band-shaped elements expand radially to create friction and stop rotation, typical of drum-type braking systems in vehicles and machinery.
[F16D 51/04] mechanically actuated -- F16D 51/04 classifies mechanically actuated brakes in which outward-moving braking members engage the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating part. These devices fall under couplings for transmitting rotation within mechanical engineering. The actuation is achieved by a mechanical linkage rather than hydraulic or electromagnetic means.
[F16D 51/06] fluid actuated -- A fluid-actuated brake of this group uses hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid pressure to drive outwardly moving brake components that press against the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating element, providing controlled stopping torque. This design falls under couplings for transmitting rotation within mechanical engineering, specifically brakes with outwardly movable members.
[F16D 51/08] shaped as an expansible fluid-filled flexible member -- This subclass covers brakes whose outward moving braking elements are formed as a flexible member that can be inflated with fluid to expand against the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating component. The fluid-filled membrane provides a reversible, conforming contact to transmit braking torque, and is used where a compliant, self-adjusting brake shoe is desired.
[F16D 51/10] shaped as exclusively radially-movable brake-shoes -- F16D 51/10 covers brakes whose shoes are designed to move exclusively in the radial direction, pressing outward against the inner surface of a rotating drum or similar component. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, specifically couplings for transmitting rotation, and pertains to outwardly-movable braking members.
[F16D 51/12] mechanically actuated -- F16D 51/12 covers mechanically actuated brakes that use outwardly-movable braking members to engage the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating component. It is situated within mechanical engineering, under engineering elements and units, and specifically couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 51/14] fluid actuated -- F16D 51/14 covers fluid-actuated brakes in which the braking members move outward to press against the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating component. The actuation is provided by hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid pressure, enabling precise control of braking force in mechanical coupling assemblies. This subclass falls within the broader category of couplings for transmitting rotation in mechanical engineering.
[F16D 51/16] shaped as brake-shoes pivoted on a fixed or nearly-fixed axis -- The subclass defines brakes whose outward-moving members are shoe-shaped elements that pivot on a fixed or nearly fixed axis to press against the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating part. This arrangement allows the shoes to transmit stopping torque while remaining mounted on a stationary pivot point.
[F16D 51/18] with two brake-shoes -- F16D 51/18 relates to drum brakes in which two outward-moving brake-shoes are mounted on a coupling that transmits rotation, pressing against the inner surface of the drum to create friction and stop motion.
[F16D 51/20] extending in opposite directions from their pivots -- F16D 51/20 relates to drum brakes in which the braking members are hinged on pivots and swing outward in opposite directions to engage the inner surface of the drum, delivering the braking force.
[F16D 51/22] mechanically actuated -- F16D 51/22 pertains to brakes featuring outwardly-movable braking components that engage the inside of a drum, specifically those activated by mechanical means. This classification is part of the broader category of couplings for transmitting rotation within the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F16D 51/24] fluid actuated -- F16D 51/24 refers to fluid-actuated brake devices in which the braking elements move outwardly to press against the inner surface of a drum or similar member, using hydraulic or pneumatic pressure to produce a braking torque.
[F16D 51/26] both extending in the same direction from their pivots -- F16D 51/26 classifies brakes in which two outward-moving braking members are mounted on separate pivots and extend in the same direction to engage the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating element, providing a coupled braking action within the broader category of mechanical couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 51/28] mechanically actuated -- F16D 51/28 covers mechanically actuated brakes where the braking elements move outward to engage the inner surface of a drum, providing rotational resistance. These devices are part of coupling systems that transmit rotation and are used in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16D 51/30] fluid actuated -- F16D 51/30 covers fluid-actuated brakes whose outwardly movable braking members engage the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating part. These devices belong to the mechanical-engineering group for couplings that transmit rotation and are a subset of brake systems with outwardly moving components.
[F16D 51/32] with three or more brake shoes -- The subclass F16D 51/32 relates to drum brakes in which outwardly-movable brake shoes press against the inner surface of a drum and the configuration includes three or more shoes. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 51/34] extending in opposite directions from their pivots -- The F16D 51/34 subclass deals with mechanical brake couplings in which the braking elements are mounted on pivots and extend outward in opposite directions to engage the inner surface of a rotating drum or similar component, providing a symmetrical outward-moving brake action.
[F16D 51/36] mechanically actuated -- F16D 51/36 designates mechanically actuated brakes in which outwardly-movable braking members, such as shoes or pads, are driven by a cam, lever or other mechanical linkage to press against the inner surface of a drum or similar rotary component, providing the clamping force needed to stop or retard rotation. This falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 51/38] fluid actuated -- F16D 51/38 designates fluid-actuated brakes whose braking members move outwardly to engage the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating element, forming part of the coupling systems used to transmit rotation in mechanical engineering. This subclass covers devices where hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid power drives the braking action, distinguishing them from manually or spring-operated counterparts.
[F16D 51/40] all extending in the same direction from their pivots -- F16D 51/40 covers brake devices in which several outwardly movable braking members are hinged on pivots and extend outward in the same direction to engage the inner surface of a drum or similar component. These configurations are used to transmit rotational braking force and belong to the coupling for transmitting rotation subgroup of mechanical engineering.
[F16D 51/42] mechanically actuated -- The code denotes mechanically-actuated brakes whose braking elements move outward to press against the inner surface of a drum or similar component. Activation is achieved by a direct mechanical input such as a lever, cam, or gear train, rather than by hydraulic, pneumatic, or electronic means.
[F16D 51/44] fluid actuated -- Fluid-actuated brakes in which a fluid pressure source moves outward-projecting braking members so they press against the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating element. The classification covers hydraulic or pneumatic mechanisms that convert fluid energy into the outward movement required for braking action.
[F16D 51/46] Self-tightening brakes with pivoted brake shoes, -- F16D 51/46 classifies self-tightening drum brakes in which the brake shoes are pivoted so that they move outwardly and press against the inner surface of the drum, tightening the braking action as rotation continues.
[F16D 51/48] with two linked or directly-interacting brake shoes -- F16D 51/48 covers brakes in which two brake shoes are linked or directly interact with each other while moving outwardly to engage the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating member. The configuration enables the shoes to act together as a unit, improving braking force distribution and simplifying actuation. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 51/50] mechanically actuated -- F16D 51/50 designates mechanically actuated brakes in which outwardly movable braking members engage the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating element. These devices belong to the couplings for transmitting rotation group within mechanical engineering and are used to convert mechanical input into a braking action.
[F16D 51/52] fluid actuated -- F16D 51/52 covers fluid-actuated brakes that use hydraulic or pneumatic pressure to move outwardly-movable braking members against the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating body. The system provides stopping torque by forcing shoes or pads outward to engage the drum. This class is part of mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 51/54] with three or more brake-shoes, at least two of them being linked or directly interacting -- This CPC subclass covers drum brakes that have three or more shoes, with at least two of the shoes linked or directly interacting. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation, specifically brakes with outwardly-movable braking members cooperating with the inner surface of a drum.
[F16D 51/56] mechanically actuated -- F16D 51/56 covers mechanically actuated brakes where outwardly-movable braking members press against the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating component. The design delivers braking force through a direct mechanical linkage rather than hydraulic or pneumatic means, offering a simple and reliable solution for torque transmission.
[F16D 51/58] fluid actuated -- Fluid-actuated brakes in this subclass use hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid pressure to move outwardly-displaceable braking members against the inner surface of a drum or similar rotator. The fluid system expands or contracts to apply the braking force, providing controlled engagement and release of the brake shoes.
[F16D 51/60] with wedging action of a brake-shoe, e.g. the shoe entering as a wedge between the brake-drum and a stationary part -- F16D 51/60 covers drum-type brakes in which the brake shoe moves outward and is forced into a wedge between the rotating drum and a fixed element, using this wedging action to generate friction and stop rotation. The design is typical for couplings that transmit torque where rapid, self-acting braking is required.
[F16D 51/62] mechanically actuated -- F16D 51/62 covers mechanically actuated brakes where outwardly-movable braking members engage the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating component, forming part of coupling elements used to transmit rotation.
[F16D 51/64] fluid actuated -- This subclass defines drum brakes whose outwardly-movable braking members are driven by fluid pressure, such as hydraulic or pneumatic actuation, to engage the inner surface of the drum and provide the braking force.
[F16D 51/66] an actuated brake-shoe being carried along and thereby engaging a member for actuating another brake-shoe -- This subclass concerns brake systems in which a driven brake-shoe moves along a surface and, by contacting a member, initiates the actuation of a second brake-shoe. The motion of the first shoe is used to transfer force to the second, providing coordinated braking in drums or similar devices.
[F16D 51/68] mechanically actuated -- F16D 51/68 covers brakes that are mechanically actuated and use outwardly-movable braking members which engage the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating part, providing a coupling for transmitting rotation within mechanical engineering.
[F16D 51/70] fluid actuated -- F16D 51/70 covers brakes in which fluid pressure drives outwardly-movable braking elements that press against the inner surface of a drum or similar rotating member. The fluid-actuated mechanism expands the shoes or pads to create friction and stop rotation.
[F16D 53/00] Brakes with braking members co-operating with both the periphery and the inner surface of a drum, wheel-rim, or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Brakes with a drum that is engaged both radially internally and externally.
[F16D 55/00] Brakes with substantially-radial braking surfaces pressed together in axial direction, e.g. disc brakes -- F16D 55/00 covers brakes that use substantially radial braking surfaces pressed together in the axial direction, such as disc brakes. These devices transmit rotational force by clamping disc-like surfaces against each other to create friction and halt motion, fitting within the broader class of mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 55/02] with axially-movable discs or pads pressed against axially-located rotating members -- A brake arrangement in which the disc or pad is designed to move axially so it can be pressed directly onto a rotating member that carries a substantially-radial braking surface, such as in typical disc-brake configurations.
[F16D 55/025] with two or more rotating discs at least one of them being located axially -- F16D 55/025 covers braking systems that employ two or more rotating discs, where at least one disc is positioned axially relative to the other. The discs have substantially radial braking surfaces that are pressed together in the axial direction, typical of certain disc-brake designs.
[F16D 55/04] by moving discs or pads away from one another against radial walls of drums or cylinders -- This subgroup defines brake arrangements where the rotating disc or pad is separated from a counterpart by moving it against a radial wall of a drum or cylinder, such as in axial-actuated disc-type brakes that press braking surfaces together in the axial direction. It classifies mechanisms that create the separating motion rather than the clamping action.
[F16D 55/06] without self-tightening action -- F16D 55/06 refers to disc-type brakes that use substantially radial braking surfaces pressed together in the axial direction but lack any self-tightening feature, meaning the braking force does not increase automatically as the surfaces wear. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 55/08] Mechanically-actuated brakes -- F16D 55/08 designates mechanically-actuated brakes that use a mechanical linkage to press radial braking surfaces together in the axial direction, as found in disc-type braking systems. The class applies to couplings that transmit rotation and convert the mechanical motion of the actuator into a braking force.
[F16D 55/10] Brakes actuated by a fluid-pressure device arranged in or on the brake -- This subclass covers braking systems where hydraulic or pneumatic pressure is applied directly to the brake assembly that is integrated with or mounted on a disc-type brake having radial friction surfaces pressed together axially. It includes mechanisms that use fluid-pressure actuators to engage or release the brake.
[F16D 55/12] comprising an expansible fluid-filled flexible member coaxial with the brake -- A brake having substantially radial disc surfaces pressed together axially that incorporates an expansible fluid-filled flexible member positioned coaxially with the disc, providing additional coupling or damping within the braking assembly.
[F16D 55/14] with self-tightening action, e.g. by means of coacting helical surfaces or balls and inclined surfaces -- F16D 55/14 covers disc-type brakes whose radial friction surfaces are pressed together axially and incorporate a self-tightening mechanism, such as coacting helical threads, balls or inclined surfaces, that automatically increase clamping force as rotation continues. This sub-class falls under mechanical couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 55/15] initiated by means of brake-bands or brake-shoes -- F16D 55/15 covers mechanically operated disc brakes where substantially radial braking surfaces are pressed together in the axial direction, and the braking action is started by brake-bands or brake-shoes that engage the rotating elements.
[F16D 55/16] Mechanically-actuated brakes -- F16D 55/16 designates mechanically-actuated brakes that press substantially radial braking surfaces together in the axial direction, typified by disc-type brakes used to stop or retard rotating shafts.
[F16D 55/18] Brakes actuated by a fluid-pressure device arranged in or on the brake -- F16D 55/18 designates brake assemblies that use a fluid-pressure actuator-hydraulic, pneumatic or similar-installed within or mounted on the brake to apply disc-type brakes whose radial friction surfaces are pressed together axially. It falls under mechanical-engineering couplings for transmitting rotation, specifically brakes with substantially-radial braking surfaces.
[F16D 55/20] comprising an expansible fluid-filled flexible member coaxial with the brake -- The entry F16D 55/20 covers disc-type brakes that use an expansible, fluid-filled flexible member positioned coaxially with the rotor to press substantially radial braking surfaces together axially. The fluid-filled element expands under pressure, providing a uniform clamping force for efficient torque transmission and heat dissipation.
[F16D 55/22] by clamping an axially-located rotating disc between movable braking members, e.g. movable brake discs or brake pads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Standard disc brakes having an axially fixed disc.
[F16D 55/224] with a common actuating member for the braking members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The common actuating member (e.g. caliper) is movably mounted (sliding, pivoting) in order to apply the brake force evenly on both sides of the disc.
[F16D 55/2245] in which the common actuating member acts on two levers carrying the braking members, e.g. tong-type brakes  similar brakes for rail vehicles  B61H5/00 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Tong brakes, mainly for railway vehicles.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rim brakes for bicycles -> B62L1/06
[F16D 55/225] the braking members being brake pads -- F16D 55/225 covers disc-type brakes in which the braking members are brake pads that are pressed axially against substantially radial braking surfaces. The code is situated under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 55/2255] in which the common actuating member is pivoted -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. pivoting caliper disc brakes.
[F16D 55/226] in which the common actuating member is moved axially -- F16D 55/226 classifies disc-type brakes whose radial friction surfaces are pressed together by a single actuator that moves in the axial direction, such as a piston that drives the pads toward the rotor. The entry pertains to mechanical engineering coupling elements that transmit rotation and specifies the axial actuation of the common braking member.
[F16D 55/2262] the axial movement being guided by open sliding surfaces, e.g. grooves -- F16D 55/2262 relates to disc-type brakes whose substantially radial braking surfaces are pressed together axially and whose axial movement is guided by open sliding surfaces such as grooves, enabling controlled linear motion while maintaining alignment.
[F16D 55/2265] the axial movement being guided by one or more pins -- This subclass covers disc brake assemblies whose substantially radial braking surfaces are pressed together in the axial direction and whose axial movement is guided by one or more pins, ensuring controlled motion of the moving part. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering group for couplings that transmit rotation.
[F16D 55/22655] Constructional details of guide pins -- This subgroup pertains to the design features of guide pins used in disc brake systems where the braking surfaces are radial and pressed together along the axis, covering aspects such as shape, mounting, materials and lubrication that ensure reliable guidance and alignment of the rotating disc and caliper.
[F16D 55/227] by two pins -- F16D 55/227 designates disc brakes whose braking surfaces are pressed together axially by a pair of pins that transmit the clamping force. The pins act as the actuation elements, moving toward each other to engage the radial friction surfaces. This subclass falls within couplings for transmitting rotation under mechanical engineering.
[F16D 55/228] with a separate actuating member for each side -- **Definition:** This place covers:  so-called fixed caliper brakes.
[F16D 55/24] with a plurality of axially-movable discs, lamellae, or pads, pressed from one side towards an axially-located member -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The term "plurality of" relates to stator and rotor elements, i.e. brakes with one axially movable rotor and one axially movable stator (disc or pad) fall within the scope of this class.
[F16D 55/26] without self-tightening action -- F16D 55/26 covers mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation, specifically disc-type brakes whose substantially radial braking surfaces are pressed together in the axial direction, and that operate without any self-tightening action.
[F16D 55/28] Brakes with only one rotating disc -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Brakes with a single disc which is rotating and axially movable.
[F16D 55/30] mechanically actuated -- F16D 55/30 covers mechanically-actuated brakes whose substantially-radial braking surfaces are pressed together in the axial direction, as seen in disc-type brakes. The subclass is applied to systems where rotation is stopped by a mechanical linkage rather than hydraulic or electric actuation.
[F16D 55/31] by means of an intermediate leverage -- F16D 55/31 covers brake devices that use an intermediate leverage mechanism to apply axial pressure to substantially-radial braking surfaces, such as disc brakes, thereby transmitting rotational stopping force through a lever-type actuation. It falls under mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 55/32] actuated by a fluid-pressure device arranged in or on the brake -- F16D 55/32 designates disc brakes whose braking force is applied by a fluid-pressure actuator that is integrated in or mounted on the brake itself. The class is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for couplings that transmit rotation and includes brakes with substantially radial surfaces pressed together axially.
[F16D 55/33] by means of an intermediate leverage -- F16D 55/33 covers brake assemblies that use an intermediate lever or linkage to apply the clamping force between substantially radial braking surfaces that are pressed together in the axial direction, such as disc brakes. The lever amplifies the driver's input, enabling efficient transmission of rotation-stopping torque.
[F16D 55/34] comprising an expansible fluid-filled flexible member coaxial with the brake -- F16D 55/34 covers disc-type brakes that incorporate a fluid-filled, expandable flexible element positioned along the brake's axis. The member can inflate or contract to modulate contact pressure between the rotating and stationary discs, providing adaptive braking torque or compensation for wear.
[F16D 55/36] Brakes with a plurality of rotating discs all lying side by side -- **Definition:** This place covers:  so-called multi-disc or lamellae brakes.
[F16D 55/38] mechanically actuated -- F16D 55/38 pertains to mechanically actuated disc brakes that have substantially radial braking surfaces pressed together in the axial direction to transmit rotation.
[F16D 55/39] by means of an intermediate leverage -- A subclass of disc-type brakes in which the radial braking surfaces are pressed together axially by means of an intermediate lever that transmits the actuation force from the driver to the disc. This arrangement is used to increase mechanical advantage while maintaining the compact coupling of rotating components.
[F16D 55/40] actuated by a fluid-pressure device arranged in or one the brake -- F16D 55/40 covers disc-type brakes having substantially radial friction surfaces that are pressed together axially, where the braking force is applied by a fluid-pressure actuator positioned on or within the brake itself, enabling axial clamping of the rotating discs.
[F16D 55/41] by means of an intermediate leverage -- The F16D 55/41 subclass concerns disc-style brakes in which the rotating and stationary members with radial braking surfaces are pressed together axially by means of an intermediate lever mechanism, providing the braking force through a leverage arrangement. It is part of the coupling group for transmitting rotation within mechanical engineering.
[F16D 55/42] comprising an expansible fluid-filled flexible member coaxial with the brake -- It covers disc-brake assemblies that include a coaxial fluid-filled flexible bladder which expands to press the braking surfaces together. The expandable member provides adjustable axial force and can improve contact uniformity.
[F16D 55/44] with the rotating part consisting of both central plates and ring-shaped plates arranged concentrically around the central plates -- F16D 55/44 covers disc-type brakes where the rotating component includes a central plate together with one or more annular plates arranged concentrically around the centre, the braking surfaces being substantially radial and engaged by axial pressure.
[F16D 55/46] with self-tightening action -- F16D 55/46 covers brakes that use substantially-radial braking surfaces pressed together along the axle and incorporate a self-tightening mechanism, meaning the braking force increases automatically as the disc surfaces engage. This falls within mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 55/48] with discs or pads having a small free angular travel relative to their support, which produces the self-tightening action -- This subclass covers brake assemblies with radial friction surfaces, such as disc brakes, where the disc or pad is allowed a limited angular movement relative to its mounting. The small free travel creates a self-tightening effect during operation, enhancing clamping force and braking efficiency.
[F16D 55/50] with auxiliary friction members, which may be of different type, producing the self-tightening action -- F16D 55/50 covers disc-type brakes that use additional friction elements, which may differ in material or design, to create a self-tightening effect as the surfaces are pressed together axially. The auxiliary members increase clamping force during operation, enhancing braking performance.
[F16D 57/00] Liquid-resistance brakes; -- F16D 57/00 classifies brakes designed to retain friction performance when exposed to liquids such as oil, water or hydraulic fluid, typically by using sealed, water-resistant or oil-impermeable components. The entry covers mechanisms that prevent fluid ingress or that function reliably while submerged, and falls under couplings for transmitting rotation in mechanical engineering.
[F16D 57/002] comprising a medium with electrically or magnetically controlled internal friction, e.g. electrorheological fluid, magnetic powder -- F16D 57/002 covers liquid-resistance brakes that incorporate a controllable medium such as an electrorheological fluid or magnetic powder, whose internal friction can be varied electrically or magnetically. The adjustable viscosity provides torque transmission or braking in couplings, allowing rapid modulation of resistance without moving mechanical parts.
[F16D 57/005] Details of blades, e.g. shape -- F16D 57/005 pertains to liquid-resistance brakes and specifies the design details of the blades, including their shape, profile and geometry, which affect the braking performance and fluid dynamics.
[F16D 57/007] with variable brake geometry, e.g. axially movable rotor or stator -- F16D 57/007 denotes liquid-resistance brakes that incorporate variable brake geometry, such as an axially movable rotor or stator, to adjust the braking surface. These devices are used in mechanical-engineering couplings that transmit rotation while providing controllable braking.
[F16D 57/02] with blades or like members braked by the fluid -- F16D 57/02 pertains to liquid-resistance brakes that employ blades or similar members, using a fluid to generate friction and resist rotational motion while transmitting torque.
[F16D 57/04] with blades causing a directed flow, e.g. Föttinger type -- F16D 57/04 covers liquid-resistance brakes that employ one or more blades to create a directed fluid flow, enhancing the braking action by controlling the movement of the working liquid. A typical example is the Föttinger-type fluid coupling, where the blades generate a specific flow pattern to transmit and modulate rotational torque.
[F16D 57/06] comprising a pump circulating fluid, braking being effected by throttling of the circulation -- F16D 57/06 pertains to liquid-resistance brakes that use a pump to circulate fluid and create a braking effect by throttling the fluid's flow. These devices are a specialized form of couplings for transmitting rotation within mechanical engineering.
[F16D 59/00] Self-acting brakes, e.g. coming into operation at a predetermined speed -- F16D 59/00 covers brake systems that engage automatically when a rotating shaft reaches a set speed, without manual control. Such self-acting brakes may employ centrifugal weights, fluid pressure, or other mechanisms to sense speed and apply braking force, commonly used in machinery and vehicles.
[F16D 59/02] spring-loaded and adapted to be released by mechanical, fluid, or electromagnetic means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Spring-applied brakes, regardless of their type (drum / disc).
