[F16D 61/00] Brakes with means for making the energy absorbed available for use -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electrodynamic brake systems for vehicles -> B60L7/00 - Dynamo-electric brakes -> H02K49/00
[F16D 63/00] Brakes not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Brakes combining a plurality of brakes, each of these brakes is additionally classified in the relevant groups, e.g. a combined disc and drum brake is classified F16D63/00 and F16D51/00 and F16D55/00 .
[F16D 63/002] Brakes with direct electrical or electro-magnetic actuation -- F16D 63/002 covers brakes that are actuated directly by electrical or electromagnetic means rather than by hydraulic, pneumatic or mechanical linkages. These devices apply braking force through an electric motor, solenoid, or magnetic field to stop or retard rotating shafts.
[F16D 63/004] comprising a rotor engaged both axially and radially by braking members, e.g. combined drum and disc brakes -- This subclass defines brake units where the rotor is engaged by braking members in both axial and radial directions, such as hybrid drum-disc arrangements. The design allows friction elements to act on the rotating surface from the side and from the face, providing combined braking action.
[F16D 63/006] Positive locking brakes -- Positive locking brakes are mechanical devices that secure a rotating shaft by engaging a lock that prevents further movement, typically using friction or a mechanical wedge. They are classified under CPC F16D 63/006, a subset of couplings for transmitting rotation within engineering elements and units.
[F16D 63/008] Brakes acting on a linearly moving member -- F16D 63/008 covers brakes that act on components moving in a straight line rather than rotating, such as devices that stop or control the travel of pistons, sliders or other linear actuators. It falls under the mechanical-engineering group for couplings transmitting rotation, specifically the subclass for brakes not otherwise provided for.
[F16D 65/00] Parts or details -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Brake parts, details and accessories which cannot be classified in any of the other groups or in the subgroups of F16D65/00 .
[F16D 65/0006] Noise or vibration control -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for noise or vibration control in brakes.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Resilient means interposed between pads and supporting members (so-called anti-rattle springs) -> F16D65/097
[F16D 65/0012] Active vibration dampers -- Active vibration dampers are devices incorporated in rotational couplings to suppress unwanted oscillations by converting kinetic energy into electrical, hydraulic or pneumatic energy. They improve the stability and lifespan of rotating machinery by actively counteracting vibrational forces.
[F16D 65/0018] Dynamic vibration dampers, e.g. mass-spring systems -- F16D 65/0018 covers dynamic vibration dampers, such as mass-spring systems, that are incorporated into couplings designed to transmit rotation. These devices absorb and reduce oscillatory forces, improving the stability and lifespan of rotating machinery components.
[F16D 65/0025] Rust- or corrosion-preventing means -- This CPC subclass covers protective measures applied to couplings that transmit rotary motion to prevent rust or corrosion, including coatings, surface treatments, corrosion-inhibiting materials, sacrificial anodes and sealing designs that extend service life and maintain reliable torque transmission.
[F16D 65/0031] Devices for retaining friction material debris, e.g. dust collectors or filters -- The subclass F16D 65/0031 includes devices mounted on rotating couplings that capture and retain friction-material debris produced by wear, such as dust collectors, filters, or particle traps. These components are intended to prevent wear particles from contaminating surrounding equipment or the environment.
[F16D 65/0037] Devices for conditioning friction surfaces, e.g. cleaning or abrasive elements -- F16D 65/0037 refers to devices used to condition friction surfaces, such as cleaning tools or abrasive elements, within the field of mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation. These devices are designed to maintain or restore surface properties to ensure reliable torque transmission.
[F16D 65/0043] Brake maintenance and assembly, tools therefor -- The CPC subclass F16D 65/0043 covers tools and equipment used for the maintenance, assembly, and adjustment of brake systems that are part of rotating couplings. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units related to couplings for transmitting rotation, and pertains to parts or details.
[F16D 65/005] Components of axially engaging brakes not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Parts of axially engaging brakes, e.g. disc brakes, which are classified F16D55/00 .
[F16D 65/0056] Brake supports -- Brake supports are components that hold or connect brake mechanisms to rotating shafts or housings, ensuring proper alignment and transmission of braking forces while allowing rotational movement. They are classified under F16D 65/0056, a subgroup of couplings for transmitting rotation within mechanical engineering.
[F16D 65/0062] integral with vehicle suspension, e.g. with the steering knuckle -- F16D 65/0062 covers parts or details of mechanical couplings for transmitting rotation that are integrated into a vehicle's suspension system, such as a steering knuckle that combines suspension and steering functions into a single component.
[F16D 65/0068] Brake calipers -- Brake calipers are components of disc brake systems that clamp a rotating disc to create friction and slow a vehicle, usually by hydraulic pressure or mechanical actuation, and they function as couplings transmitting rotational forces from the wheel hub to the braking mechanism.
[F16D 65/0075] assembled from a plurality of parts -- This subclass refers to rotational couplings that are constructed by joining together several separate components rather than being formed as a single piece. It includes designs where the functional elements are assembled, such as modular or bolted couplings, and pertains to parts or details of transmission couplings.
[F16D 65/0081] Brake covers -- Brake covers are protective components that shield the rotating surfaces of a coupling's braking mechanism from contamination, wear and external damage. They are classified under mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation, specifically as parts or details that enhance the durability and safety of brake assemblies.
[F16D 65/0087] Brake housing guide members, e.g. caliper pins; Accessories therefor, e.g. dust boots -- The CPC subclass F16D 65/0087 relates to guide members that position or support brake housings, notably pins used in calipers, and includes associated accessories such as protective dust boots. These components are part of coupling mechanisms that transmit rotational motion in mechanical systems.
[F16D 65/02] Braking members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Members interacting with each other to produce the braking effect, including active braking members, e.g. brake pads, and passive members to be braked, e.g. brake discs and drums; Accessories for mounting, e.g. holding springs, abutments.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Friction linings as such (physical aspects, e.g. shape) -> F16D69/00 - Friction lining composition -> F16D69/02
[F16D 65/028] Rollers -- Rollers are cylindrical elements incorporated into mechanical couplings that transmit rotational motion by providing rolling contact between shafts or other rotating components. Classified under F16D 65/028, they fall within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 65/04] Bands, shoes or pads -- F16D 65/04 refers to bands, shoes or pads that are part of couplings used to transmit rotational motion. These elements are typically friction or pressure members that engage with a shaft or drum to transmit torque, often found in clutches, brakes or other rotating machinery.
[F16D 65/06] for externally-engaging brakes -- F16D 65/06 refers to parts or details of rotational couplings that incorporate externally-engaging brake mechanisms. The classification covers brake devices mounted on the outer surface of a coupling, using friction or other contact to limit or stop transmitted torque.
[F16D 65/062] engaging the tread of a railway wheel -- F16D 65/062 covers devices that engage the tread of a railway wheel as part of a coupling that transmits rotational motion. It relates to mechanical-engineering elements designed to grip or lock onto the wheel's surface for power transfer, braking or traction purposes. This classification falls under couplings for transmitting rotation within engineering elements and units.
[F16D 65/065] Brake bands -- F16D 65/065 covers brake bands, which are friction components attached to a drum or wheel to convert rotational motion into stopping force. The band wraps partially or fully around the rotating part and is actuated to create friction, commonly used in mechanical braking systems for vehicles, machinery, and elevators.
[F16D 65/067] with means for mounting, e.g. end connection members -- F16D 65/067 covers rotary couplings that include mounting features, such as end connection members, which allow the coupling to be attached directly to shafts, housings or other components. It pertains to parts or details of mechanical engineering couplings designed for transmitting rotation while providing integrated means of securing the device in place.
[F16D 65/08] for internally-engaging brakes -- F16D 65/08 covers parts or details of couplings that incorporate internally-engaging brakes, i.e., mechanisms where a brake surface is engaged from within the rotating shaft or hub to transmit or stop rotation. These devices are used in mechanical engineering applications requiring compact, self-contained braking within coupling assemblies.
[F16D 65/09] Pivots or supporting members therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Drum brake shoe pivots or abutments, e.g. anchor blocks.
[F16D 65/091] for axially holding the segments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. springs holding drum brake shoes in contact with the brake carrier.
[F16D 65/092] for axially-engaging brakes, e.g. disc brakes -- This CPC subclass covers couplings that incorporate axially-engaging brake mechanisms such as disc brakes, used to transmit rotational motion while providing braking action. It is placed under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation, parts or details.
[F16D 65/095] Pivots or supporting members therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. details of disc brake pad abutments.
[F16D 65/097] Resilient means interposed between pads and supporting members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  primarily so-called anti-rattle springs.
[F16D 65/0971] transmitting brake actuation force, e.g. elements interposed between brake piston and pad -- The class F16D 65/0971 covers components that convey the actuation force from a brake piston to the brake pad, such as intermediate elements that transmit, amplify or control the force. These devices are used in braking systems to ensure efficient force transfer and may include levers, push rods or hydraulic couplings.
[F16D 65/0972] transmitting brake reaction force, e.g. elements interposed between torque support plate and pad -- This sub-group of the cooperative patent classification covers devices that convey the reaction force generated by a brake, such as components placed between the torque-support plate and the brake pad, ensuring the force is transferred to the supporting structure.
[F16D 65/0973] not subjected to brake forces -- F16D 65/0973 relates to parts of rotating couplings that are designed so they are not subjected to brake forces, meaning they transmit torque without being used to stop motion and are isolated from braking loads. This subclass covers elements intended for continuous power transmission where braking stresses are avoided.
[F16D 65/0974] acting on or in the vicinity of the pad rim in a direction substantially transverse to the brake disc axis -- The F16D 65/0974 subclass covers devices or methods that act on or near the rim of a brake-pad in a direction essentially across the brake-disc axis, such as side-applied forces used to engage, adjust or release the pad during braking operations. It pertains to coupling elements that transmit rotation while providing transverse actuation at the pad edge.
[F16D 65/0975] Springs made from wire -- F16D 65/0975 covers springs that are fabricated from wire and employed as parts or details in mechanical couplings that transmit rotation. The classification includes helical, coil and other wire-formed springs used to compensate, transmit torque or provide flexibility within the coupling assembly.
[F16D 65/0976] acting on one pad only -- A subclass for couplings that transmit rotation by acting on a single friction pad, such as single-plate clutches or brakes where only one pad engages the rotating member. It covers designs where the torque-transmitting force is applied to one pad rather than multiple pads.
[F16D 65/0977] Springs made from sheet metal -- F16D 65/0977 covers springs that are formed from sheet metal and used as components in mechanical couplings that transmit rotation. These sheet-metal springs may be designed as leaf, wave, or other spring types and serve to provide flexibility, damping or torque-limiting functions within the coupling assembly.
[F16D 65/0978] acting on one pad only -- This subgroup covers coupling components where the actuation mechanism engages a single friction pad rather than multiple pads, such as one-pad clutches or braking devices. It applies to designs in which force is applied to only one of the rotating or stationary elements to transmit torque.
[F16D 65/0979] acting on the rear side of the pad or an element affixed thereto, e.g. spring clips securing the pad to the brake piston or caliper -- This CPC subclass (F16D 65/0979) deals with components that act on the rear side of a brake pad or any element attached to it, such as spring clips used to secure the pad to the brake piston or caliper, within mechanical engineering couplings that transmit rotation.
[F16D 65/10] Drums for externally- or internally-engaging brakes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cylindrical braked members, engaged on the radially inner and/or outer side. Contains mainly brake drums for standard drum brakes, i.e. with radially inner braking surface.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rotors having both a drum and a disc part -> F16D65/12
[F16D 65/12] Discs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Simple disc rotors and rotors having both a drum and a disc part.
[F16D 65/121] consisting of at least three circumferentially arranged segments -- F16D 65/121 designates a type of mechanical coupling used to transmit rotation that is built from at least three segments arranged around a common axis. The segments are positioned circumferentially to engage each other and transfer torque, typically forming a split or multi-piece housing that can be assembled around a shaft. This entry falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and specifically couplings for transmitting rotation, classified as a part or detail of such devices.
[F16D 65/122] adapted for mounting of friction pads -- F16D 65/122 pertains to couplings or related components that are specially adapted to carry friction pads, such as brake shoes or clutch plates, providing a secure mounting arrangement for efficient transmission of rotational forces.
[F16D 65/123] comprising an annular disc secured to a hub member; Discs characterised by means for mounting -- F16D 65/123 pertains to mechanical couplings that transmit rotation by means of an annular disc secured to a hub member, the disc being distinguished by its mounting features. The entry falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation, and covers parts or details of such couplings.
[F16D 65/124] adapted for mounting on the wheel of a railway vehicle -- F16D 65/124 covers mechanical couplings that are specifically designed to be mounted on the wheel of a railway vehicle in order to transmit rotational power or torque between the wheel and other components. The classification includes parts or details of such couplings that facilitate the transfer of motion while being integrated with the wheel assembly.
[F16D 65/125] characterised by the material used for the disc body -- F16D 65/125 covers disc-type flexible couplings whose design is distinguished principally by the material from which the disc body is made, such as rubber, elastomers, polymers, metal, or composite substances. The material choice affects the coupling's elasticity, vibration damping, temperature resistance and torque-transmission characteristics, and is a key factor in selecting the appropriate device for a given mechanical application.
[F16D 65/126] the material being of low mechanical strength, e.g. carbon, beryllium; Torque transmitting members therefor -- F16D 65/126 refers to parts or details of rotational couplings in which the component is made from low-strength materials such as carbon, beryllium or similar substances, focusing on the design and performance of torque-transmitting members that rely on these weaker-strength materials.
[F16D 65/127] characterised by properties of the disc surface; Discs lined with friction material -- This subclass covers rotational couplings that transmit torque using disks whose surfaces have defined properties or are lined with a friction material, aiming to control friction, wear, heat dissipation or torque capacity.
[F16D 65/128] characterised by means for cooling -- F16D 65/128 covers parts or details of rotational couplings that incorporate means for cooling, such as internal channels, jackets or heat-dissipating fins, to manage temperature rise during operation. The classification is used for devices where cooling is integral to the coupling's design, ensuring reliable power transmission under thermal stress.
[F16D 65/14] Actuating mechanisms for brakes -- In this group, it is desirable to add the indexing codes of groups F16D2121/00 - F16D2131/00 relating to actuators.
[F16D 65/16] arranged in or on the brake -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Actuating mechanisms which are integrated in the brake itself.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Actuation mechanisms arranged apart from the brake, e.g. connected to the brake by a rod. -> F16D65/12
[F16D 65/18] adapted for drawing members together -- **Definition:** This place covers:  in particular disc brake actuators.
[F16D 65/183] with force-transmitting members arranged side by side acting on a spot type force-applying member -- This subclass covers couplings that employ several force-transmitting members placed side by side, each acting on a localized spot-type force-applying element as part of the coupling's details.
[F16D 65/186] with full-face force-applying member, e.g. annular -- F16D 65/186 covers rotational couplings that transmit torque with a full-face force-applying member, such as an annular ring that engages the entire face of a shaft or hub. The entry pertains to mechanical elements designed to transfer rotation through complete-face contact.
[F16D 65/22] adapted for pressing members apart -- **Definition:** This place covers:  in particular drum brake actuators.
[F16D 65/28] arranged apart from the brake -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Independent actuator units, e.g. secondary cylinders acting on the brake members via a rod.
[F16D 65/38] Slack adjusters -- Slack adjusters are mechanical components used in brake systems to automatically compensate for wear and maintain proper clearance between brake shoes and drums. Positioned within the coupling elements that transmit rotation, they ensure consistent braking performance by adjusting the slack in the linkage.
[F16D 65/40] mechanical -- F16D 65/40 covers the mechanical components and details of couplings used to transmit rotational motion between shafts. It includes parts such as hubs, sleeves, keys and other elements that form or modify the coupling mechanism. The classification applies to designs that facilitate torque transfer while allowing alignment or flexibility.
[F16D 65/42] non-automatic -- The CPC subclass F16D 65/42 pertains to couplings for transmitting rotation that are non-automatic, meaning they require manual action to engage or disengage the connection. It covers the parts or details of such manually operated rotational couplings used in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16D 65/44] by means of direct linear adjustment -- F16D 65/44 covers rotary couplings that transmit torque while allowing direct linear adjustment of the connecting elements, enabling axial positioning without changing the angular alignment. These devices are used in mechanical systems where precise spacing or alignment of shafts must be varied while maintaining rotational continuity.
[F16D 65/46] with screw-thread and nut -- F16D 65/46 covers mechanical couplings used to transmit rotation that incorporate a screw-threaded element and a nut as the joining feature. It applies to parts or details of such couplings where the threaded connection provides the mechanical link between shafts.
[F16D 65/48] with eccentric or helical body -- F16D 65/48 denotes couplings for transmitting rotation that feature an eccentric or helical body, allowing flexible connection and compensation for misalignment between shafts. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and the broader class of couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 65/50] for angular adjustment of two concentric parts of the brake control system -- F16D 65/50 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to components designed for adjusting the angle between two concentric parts within a brake control system, ensuring precise alignment and function. This classification is specific to mechanical engineering elements and units, particularly couplings that transmit rotational force.
[F16D 65/52] self-acting in one direction for adjusting excessive play -- F16D 65/52 designates couplings that automatically adjust in one direction to eliminate excessive axial or radial play, using self-acting mechanisms. The class covers parts or details that compensate for looseness during operation, ensuring continuous torque transmission.
[F16D 65/54] by means of direct linear adjustment -- This CPC subgroup concerns mechanical couplings for transmitting rotary motion that are adjusted axially by direct linear movement of the coupling parts. It specifies devices where the spacing or alignment of the coupling elements is set simply by moving them linearly, without intermediate mechanisms.
[F16D 65/543] comprising a plastically-deformable member -- F16D 65/543 covers rotational couplings that include a plastically-deformable member as part of the coupling assembly. The deformable element is intended to yield under overload, providing torque limiting or shock-absorbing functions while maintaining transmission of rotation between shafts.
[F16D 65/546] for mounting within the confines of a drum brake -- F16D 65/546 covers couplings designed to be installed inside a drum-brake assembly, where the mounting must fit within the brake's confined space. The invention relates to mechanical elements that transmit rotational motion while being integrated directly into the drum-brake housing.
[F16D 65/56] with screw-thread and nut -- A coupling for transmitting rotary motion that is assembled or secured by a screw-thread and a nut, allowing the linked parts to be tightened, adjusted or locked together through threaded engagement. This design is used where precise alignment or removable connection of rotating components is required.
[F16D 65/561] for mounting within the confines of a drum brake -- F16D 65/561 covers couplings designed to be installed inside a drum brake assembly, providing a means to transmit rotational motion to the brake components. It pertains to parts or details of mechanical-engineering coupling elements that fit within the limited space of the drum.
[F16D 65/562] arranged between service brake actuator and braking member, and subjected to service brake force -- This subgroup covers couplings that are positioned between a service-brake actuator and the braking member, and that are designed to carry the forces generated during normal service braking. It pertains to parts that transmit rotational motion from the actuator to the brake component while withstanding the applied brake load.
[F16D 65/563] arranged adjacent to service brake actuator, e.g. on parking brake lever, and not subjected to service brake force -- The code F16D 65/563 designates rotating couplings or related parts that are positioned next to a service-brake actuator-such as on a parking-brake lever-and are deliberately isolated from the forces generated by the service brake. It covers these elements when they are not subjected to the braking load.
[F16D 65/565] arranged diametrically opposite to service brake actuator, and subjected to service brake force -- F16D 65/565 designates coupling components that are positioned diametrically opposite the service brake actuator and that bear the service brake force, i.e., the parts of a rotational transmission that receive braking torque from the actuator across the shaft.
[F16D 65/566] having a temperature-sensitive element preventing adjustment when brake is hot -- F16D 65/566 concerns rotational couplings that incorporate a temperature-sensitive element which locks out any adjustment when the brake is hot, preventing inadvertent changes caused by thermal expansion.
[F16D 65/567] for mounting on a disc brake -- F16D 65/567 covers rotational couplings designed to be mounted directly on a disc-brake assembly, enabling the transmission of torque from a driven component to the brake rotor or its supporting hub.
[F16D 65/568] for synchronous adjustment of actuators arranged in parallel -- This sub-group covers coupling components that allow two or more actuators mounted side by side to be adjusted so that they move in synchrony. It includes mechanisms such as adjustable pins, shims or variable-length links that equalise the relative motion of parallel drives.
[F16D 65/58] with eccentric or helical body -- This subclass covers couplings that transmit rotational motion using a body shaped eccentrically or following a helical profile, providing flexible alignment and the ability to absorb mis-alignment while transmitting torque in compact mechanical arrangements.
[F16D 65/60] for angular adjustment of two concentric parts of the brake control systems -- F16D 65/60 covers couplings that allow angular adjustment between two concentric components in brake control systems, enabling precise alignment while transmitting rotational motion. It belongs to the mechanical engineering subclass for engineering elements and units, specifically couplings for transmitting rotation, parts or details.
[F16D 65/62] self-acting in both directions for adjusting excessive and insufficient play -- F16D 65/62 covers self-acting rotational couplings that automatically adjust for both excessive and insufficient play, allowing bidirectional compensation of clearance in the transmission. These devices operate without external control, continuously maintaining optimal engagement between coupled shafts.
[F16D 65/64] by means of direct linear adjustment -- This subclass covers couplings whose positioning or gap can be set by moving parts linearly along the axis, using a straight adjustment mechanism rather than rotational or angular means. It applies to parts and details of mechanical couplings that achieve alignment or preload through direct linear displacement.
[F16D 65/66] with screw-thread and nut -- F16D 65/66 designates rotary couplings that incorporate a screw-thread and a nut to join shafts or other rotating components, allowing a secure, adjustable transmission of torque.
[F16D 65/68] with eccentric or helical body -- F16D 65/68 covers couplings for transmitting rotation that incorporate an eccentric or helical body shape. These parts are designed to accommodate angular or axial misalignment while maintaining torque transfer, often used in drives where smooth, flexible connection between shafts is required. The classification falls within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 65/70] for angular adjustment of two concentric parts of the brake control system -- F16D 65/70 pertains to couplings that enable angular adjustment between two concentric components of a brake control system, such as adjustable brackets or sleeves that align rotating parts while allowing a precise setting of the relative angle.
[F16D 65/72] hydraulic -- F16D 65/72 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the European and United States patent offices, refers to hydraulic components or systems specifically related to parts or details of couplings for transmitting rotation in mechanical engineering. This classification covers aspects such as fluid pressure actuation for engaging or disengaging the coupling.
[F16D 65/74] self-acting in one direction -- F16D 65/74 designates couplings that operate automatically only when torque is applied in one direction, allowing them to engage or lock without external control while remaining free-running in the opposite direction. Such self-acting one-way couplings are used where a drive must be transmitted in a single sense and released when the load reverses.
[F16D 65/76] self-acting in both directions -- F16D 65/76 covers couplings that automatically engage and transmit torque in both clockwise and counter-clockwise directions without external control, typically used where bidirectional power transmission is required. These self-acting couplings are classified as parts or details within mechanical engineering.
[F16D 65/78] Features relating to cooling -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Anything to do with cooling of brakes; e.g. modification of brakes to improve cooling, liquid cooling circuits.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Brake drums with cooling fins -> F16D65/10 and F16D2065/1332 - Internally ventilated brake discs -> F16D65/12 and F16D2065/1328 - Vehicle modifications to facilitate cooling of brakes -> B60T5/00
[F16D 65/80] for externally-engaging brakes -- F16D 65/80 classifies parts or details of couplings that incorporate externally-engaging brakes, i.e., brake mechanisms that act on the outside of a rotating shaft or hub to stop or slow motion. The subclass is used for devices where braking is integrated into a mechanical coupling.
[F16D 65/807] with open cooling system, e.g. cooled by air -- F16D 65/807 refers to couplings for transmitting rotation that include an open cooling system, typically employing airflow to remove heat from the coupling's components.
[F16D 65/813] with closed cooling system -- F16D 65/813 designates couplings that incorporate a closed cooling system, meaning the cooling fluid is contained within a sealed circuit that does not exchange with the external environment. These couplings are used to transmit rotational motion while managing heat buildup, typically in high-speed or high-torque applications.
[F16D 65/82] for internally-engaging brakes -- F16D 65/82 designates couplings that incorporate internally-engaging brakes, where the braking surfaces are activated from within the rotating hub to stop or hold the driven shaft. These brakes are part of mechanical engineering elements used for transmitting rotation and are classified under parts or details of coupling devices.
[F16D 65/827] with open cooling system, e.g. cooled by air -- F16D 65/827 designates couplings that incorporate an open cooling system, such as air-cooled fins or ventilation passages, to dissipate heat generated during torque transmission. The classification covers any coupling part or detail where cooling is achieved by exposure to ambient air rather than a closed liquid or oil circuit.
[F16D 65/833] with closed cooling system -- F16D 65/833 covers couplings for transmitting rotation that incorporate a closed cooling system, allowing the component to dissipate heat while remaining sealed from the external environment. These parts are classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and specifically the parts or details of rotational couplings.
[F16D 65/84] for disc brakes -- F16D 65/84 covers parts and details of disc-brake couplings that transmit rotational motion in mechanical systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation, and includes components such as caliper mounts, gear interfaces or other rotating disc-brake elements.
[F16D 65/847] with open cooling system, e.g. cooled by air -- This subclass refers to rotating couplings that include an open cooling system, for example air-cooled designs, allowing heat to be dissipated directly to the surrounding environment. It applies to parts or details of couplings used for transmitting rotary motion.
[F16D 65/853] with closed cooling system -- This subclass covers rotating couplings that incorporate a sealed cooling circuit to remove heat generated during power transmission. The cooling system is closed, containing fluid or gas that circulates within the coupling without external discharge. Such couplings are used where high torque and temperature require internal heat management.
[F16D 66/00] Arrangements for monitoring working conditions, e.g. wear, temperature -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for brake monitoring only.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Monitoring brake control circuits -> B60T17/18
[F16D 66/02] Apparatus for indicating wear -- F16D 66/02 is a CPC subclass that covers apparatus used to indicate wear in rotating couplings, such as sensors or displays that detect material loss, temperature rise or other condition changes. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering group for couplings that transmit rotation and focuses on monitoring devices that provide real-time wear information.
[F16D 66/021] using electrical detection or indication means -- This CPC subclass (F16D 66/021) covers mechanical couplings that incorporate electrical sensors or indication devices to monitor operating conditions such as wear, temperature or load. It pertains to arrangements for detecting such parameters and providing an electrical signal or display, enabling real-time condition monitoring of rotating transmission elements.
[F16D 66/022] indicating that a lining is worn to minimum allowable thickness -- This subclass covers devices that monitor the condition of couplings by indicating when a lining has worn down to its minimum allowable thickness, enabling maintenance of rotational transmission systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 66/023] directly sensing the position of braking members -- The class covers devices that directly detect the position of braking components within rotating couplings, providing real-time monitoring of wear, temperature or other operating conditions of the brake system. It includes sensors or sensing arrangements that are integrated with the brake mechanism to give immediate position feedback for control or safety purposes.
[F16D 66/024] Sensors mounted on braking members adapted to contact the brake disc or drum, e.g. wire loops severed on contact -- The code F16D 66/024 covers sensors that are fixed to brake shoes, pads, shoes or other braking members and make direct contact with a rotating brake disc or drum. These devices, such as wire loops that open when touched, monitor operating conditions like wear, temperature or friction during braking.
[F16D 66/025] sensing the position of parts of the brake system other than the braking members, e.g. limit switches mounted on primary cylinders -- A subclass for sensing the position of brake-system parts that are not the braking members, such as limit switches mounted on primary cylinders, used to monitor operating conditions like wear or temperature. This falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 66/026] indicating different degrees of lining wear -- The F16D 66/026 subclass covers arrangements that monitor and indicate varying degrees of wear in rotating-element liners or couplings, such as bearings or sealing surfaces. It includes sensors, visual or electronic displays, and signal-generation methods that convey wear level, temperature or related condition to operators for timely maintenance.
[F16D 66/027] Sensors therefor -- F16D 66/027 relates to sensors built into rotational couplings that monitor working conditions such as wear, temperature or load. The sensors provide real-time feedback on the coupling's performance, aiding safe operation and preventive maintenance.
[F16D 66/028] with non-electrical sensors or signal transmission, e.g. magnetic, optical -- This subclass covers couplings that incorporate non-electrical sensing means such as magnetic or optical sensors to monitor conditions like wear or temperature and transmit the information without using electrical components.
[F16D 67/00] Combinations of couplings and brakes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements combining clutches with brakes in one unit  Arrangements combining couplings with brakes in one unit  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Conjoint control of brake systems and driveline clutches in vehicles -> B60W10/02 , B60W10/18
[F16D 67/02] Clutch-brake combinations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Clutches with external or internal control combined with a brake.
[F16D 67/04] fluid actuated -- The CPC subclass F16D 67/04 covers couplings that transmit rotation by means of a fluid-actuated mechanism, such as hydraulic or pneumatic devices that engage or disengage the transmission. It includes designs where fluid pressure moves internal components to lock, unlock, or modulate the coupling, often combined with braking functions.
[F16D 67/06] electromagnetically actuated -- A device that integrates a rotational coupling with a braking mechanism, where the actuation of the brake is performed by electromagnetic means, enabling controlled transmission of torque. It belongs to the class of mechanical engineering elements that combine couplings and brakes for regulating motion.
[F16D 69/00] Friction linings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Friction elements characterised by their physical aspects (e.g. shape or surface structure).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Braking members, e.g. assembly of friction material and support structure, friction block with backing plate -> F16D65/02 - Friction lining composition -> F16D69/02
[F16D 69/02] Composition of linings -- **Definition:** This place covers: processes for making friction linings special shapes or arrangements of linings semi-metallic compositions e.g. metal-ceramic  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** This place does not cover: ceramic composites (e.g. C/SiC): F16D69/023 with Indexing Code for "ceramic composite" and C04B anti friction materials: F16C33/00
[F16D 69/021] containing asbestos -- The subclass pertains to rotational couplings that employ friction linings incorporating asbestos as a component of the lining material, aimed at improving wear resistance and heat dissipation. It falls under mechanical engineering elements for transmitting torque where asbestos is part of the friction surface.
[F16D 69/022] in the form of fibres -- F16D 69/022 covers friction linings that are made from fibrous materials, such as woven, knitted or non-woven fibers, for use in couplings that transmit rotation. The fibrous linings provide wear resistance, heat dissipation and damping in rotating assemblies.
[F16D 69/023] Composite materials containing carbon and carbon fibres or fibres made of carbonizable material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  C/C composites and ceramic composites : carbon/carbon composites infiltrated with Si, B or Metals.
[F16D 69/025] Compositions based on an organic binder -- F16D 69/025 covers friction linings for rotary couplings that are formed from composites in which an organic binder such as a resin, polymer or similar material holds the friction particles or fibres together, specifying the formulation, preparation and use of these binder-based materials.
[F16D 69/026] containing fibres -- F16D 69/026 relates to friction linings used in rotational couplings that incorporate fibrous material, such as carbon or glass fibers, within the lining. The fibers reinforce the lining, enhancing wear resistance, heat dissipation and load-carrying capacity of the coupling.
[F16D 69/027] Compositions based on metals or inorganic oxides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compositions containing :
[F16D 69/028] containing fibres -- The F16D 69/028 subclass covers couplings that transmit rotation and incorporate friction linings made with or containing fibres, such as fibre-reinforced brake or clutch plates. These fibre-based linings are used to enhance wear resistance, heat dissipation or strength of the friction surface.
[F16D 69/04] Attachment of linings -- F16D 69/04 covers devices and methods for securing friction linings to rotating couplings that transmit torque. It includes fittings, clamps, adhesives or mechanical fasteners used to attach wear-resistant materials to shafts, gears or other rotating elements, ensuring stable operation and easy replacement.
[F16D 69/0408] specially adapted for plane linings -- F16D 69/0408 designates friction linings that are specially adapted for plane (flat) surfaces in couplings that transmit rotational motion, such as clutches or brakes. These linings are engineered to provide uniform contact and wear resistance on planar faces, enhancing torque transmission efficiency.
[F16D 69/0416] specially adapted for curved linings -- F16D 69/0416 covers friction linings that are specially adapted for curved surfaces in rotating couplings. The classification includes linings shaped to match conical, cylindrical or other non-flat contact geometries, enabling efficient torque transmission and wear resistance in mechanical drive assemblies.
[F16D 7/00] Slip couplings, e.g. slipping on overload, for absorbing shock -- F16D 7/00 covers slip couplings that are designed to slip when an overload occurs, thereby absorbing shock and protecting rotating shafts and gear trains. These devices allow controlled relative movement between connected shafts, limiting transmitted torque and preventing damage in mechanical power transmission systems.
[F16D 7/002] the torque being transmitted and limited by yielding of an elastomeric race -- F16D 7/002 covers slip couplings that limit transmitted torque by the controlled yielding of an elastomeric race, allowing the coupling to absorb overload shocks while maintaining rotation transmission. The elastomer deforms under excess torque, providing a protective, self-adjusting limit that prevents damage to the drivetrain.
[F16D 7/005] the torque being transmitted and limited by rolling friction, e.g. ball bearings axially loaded -- F16D 7/005 covers slip couplings where the transmitted torque is limited by rolling-friction elements such as axially loaded ball bearings, allowing controlled overload protection by slipping when the torque exceeds the friction limit.
[F16D 7/007] the torque being transmitted and limited by rolling surfaces skidding, e.g. skew needle rollers -- A slip coupling that transmits torque by using rolling surfaces that can skid, such as skew needle rollers, thereby limiting the torque to protect the drive from overload shock.
[F16D 7/02] of the friction type -- F16D 7/02 designates slip couplings that rely on friction to transmit rotational motion while permitting limited slip, typically to absorb shock or overload. These friction-type couplings allow controlled relative movement between shafts, protecting driven components from sudden torque spikes.
[F16D 7/021] with radially applied torque-limiting friction surfaces  F16D7/022  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass covers slip couplings that absorb shock by using radially applied torque-limiting friction surfaces, a type of mechanical element for transmitting rotation. It falls under the hierarchy mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → couplings for transmitting rotation, and it is superseded by F16D 7/022.
[F16D 7/022] with a helical band or equivalent member co-operating with a cylindrical torque limiting coupling surface -- A slip coupling that uses a helical band or comparable member working together with a cylindrical torque-limiting surface to absorb overload shock while transmitting rotation. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering class of couplings for transmitting rotation, specifically to elements that slip on overload.
[F16D 7/024] with axially applied torque limiting friction surfaces -- F16D 7/024 covers slip couplings that limit torque by using friction surfaces engaged by axially-applied torque, allowing controlled slipping under overload to absorb shock.
[F16D 7/025] with flat clutching surfaces, e.g. discs -- F16D 7/025 covers slip couplings that use flat clutching surfaces such as discs to transmit rotational motion while allowing controlled slip under overload, thereby absorbing shock. The flat interfaces provide a simple, compact way to engage and disengage torque, often employed in automotive and industrial gearboxes.
[F16D 7/027] with multiple lamellae -- This subclass covers slip couplings that use several thin lamellae which slide relative to each other to absorb overload shock in rotating shafts. The multiple-lamella design provides staged slip for protecting gear or motor assemblies during transient torque spikes.
[F16D 7/028] with conical friction surfaces -- These are slip couplings that incorporate conical friction surfaces to permit controlled slipping under overload, thereby absorbing shock while still transmitting rotation. The conical geometry provides consistent friction characteristics across the contact area.
[F16D 7/04] of the ratchet type -- F16D 7/04 covers slip couplings of the ratchet type that permit rotational transmission to slip under overload, providing shock absorption. These devices protect drivetrains in mechanical systems by allowing controlled disengagement when torque exceeds a preset limit.
[F16D 7/042] with at least one part moving axially between engagement and disengagement  F16D7/08  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass F16D 7/042 deals with slip couplings in which at least one part moves axially between engagement and disengagement, providing overload protection by allowing axial motion. It narrows the broader subclass F16D 7/08 for couplings that transmit rotation while absorbing shock.
[F16D 7/044] the axially moving part being coaxial with the rotation, e.g. a gear with face teeth -- This subclass covers slip couplings that absorb shock by allowing an axially moving component to be coaxial with the rotating part, such as a gear with face teeth. It applies to mechanical engineering elements where the moving element slides along the axis while the rotation is transmitted.
[F16D 7/046] with a plurality of axially moving parts -- F16D 7/046 covers slip couplings that incorporate several parts moving axially relative to each other to absorb shock or overload. These devices belong to the broader group of mechanical couplings for transmitting rotation and are designed to permit controlled slip when torque exceeds a set limit.
[F16D 7/048] with parts moving radially between engagement and disengagement  F16D7/10  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass (F16D 7/048) covers slip couplings whose components move radially to shift between engagement and disengagement, providing overload protection and shock absorption. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 7/06] with intermediate balls or rollers -- F16D 7/06 covers slip couplings that incorporate intermediate balls or rollers between the driving and driven members. The balls or rollers allow controlled slippage under overload, providing shock-absorption while transmitting rotational motion. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 7/08] moving axially between engagement and disengagement -- F16D 7/08 relates to slip couplings that can shift axially as they move from an engaged to a disengaged state, allowing controlled slipping to absorb shock and protect against overload. The axial movement occurs during the transition between engagement and disengagement.
[F16D 7/10] moving radially between engagement and disengagement -- This CPC group covers slip couplings that allow radial movement between engaged and disengaged positions, used to absorb shock when overload occurs. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 71/00] Mechanisms for bringing members to rest in a predetermined position -- F16D 71/00 classifies devices that secure rotating or moving components in a chosen position, including brakes, clutches, detents and other locking mechanisms used in couplings for transmitting rotation. These mechanisms are designed to stop motion and maintain the parts at rest until release.
[F16D 71/02] comprising auxiliary means for producing the final movement -- F16D 71/02 covers couplings that incorporate auxiliary means to produce the final movement that locks rotating members in a predetermined position, such as detents, brakes, cams or other devices that bring the shaft to rest at a set angle.
[F16D 71/04] providing for selection between a plurality of positions -- The code F16D 71/04 covers mechanisms that enable a user to choose and lock a component in one of several predefined positions, typically by using selectors, detents, or indexed stops that bring the moving part to rest at the chosen location. This is applied to couplings that transmit rotation, allowing discrete positioning for precise control.
[F16D 9/00] Couplings with safety member for disconnecting, e.g. breaking or melting member -- F16D 9/00 covers couplings that incorporate a safety element designed to separate the driven parts when a fault occurs, typically by breaking or melting a dedicated member. The safety member activates automatically to prevent damage or hazardous transmission of torque.
[F16D 9/02] by thermal means, e.g. melting member -- A coupling that incorporates a safety device designed to separate the driven parts by applying heat, causing a designated member to melt and thus break the transmission of rotation. This thermal-actuated safety feature is used to protect equipment from overloads or hazardous conditions.
[F16D 9/04] by tensile breaking -- F16D 9/04 relates to mechanical couplings that incorporate a safety member which disconnects the drive by tensile-breaking, such as a breakable or meltable link that separates when a pulling force exceeds a limit. The design is intended to protect equipment by intentionally separating the shafts under overload.
[F16D 9/06] by breaking due to shear stress -- F16D 9/06 covers couplings that incorporate a safety member designed to separate the driven and driving shafts when the shear stress on the member exceeds a prescribed limit, causing the member to break. The breaking action provides an overload-protective disconnect, preventing damage to the transmission or downstream equipment. This subgroup falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and couplings for transmitting rotation.
[F16D 9/08] over a single area encircling the axis of rotation, e.g. shear necks on shafts -- F16D 9/08 covers safety couplings in which a shear member is positioned around the shaft in a single continuous zone encircling the axis of rotation. The shear element, such as a necked portion of the shaft, is intended to break or melt when the transmitted torque exceeds a predetermined limit, thereby disconnecting the drive and protecting the system.
[F16D 9/10] having a part movable after disconnection so as to provide reconnection, e.g. advanceable shear pins -- F16D 9/10 covers couplings with safety mechanisms that allow for automatic reconnection after disengagement, such as through the use of advanceable shear pins or similar components designed to move and reset after breaking or melting under overload conditions.
[F16F] SPRINGS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Springs, shock-absorbers or vibration-dampers;  Their arrangement in, or adaptation for, particular apparatus if not provided for in the subclasses covering said apparatus.  **Glossary:** - Steel or metal: Mention of “steel” or “metal” in groups F16F , unless specific mention is made otherwise, should be seen in the light of the title of group F16F1/00 , i.e. material having low internal friction. This normally includes composite materials such as fibre-reinforced plastics. - Rubber or plastics: Mention of “rubber” or “plastics” in group F16F , unless specific mention is made otherwise, should be seen in the light of the title of group F16F1/36 , i.e. material having high internal friction. This normally does NOT include composite materials such as fibre-reinforced plastics except in the case of groups F16F1/366 - F16F1/3686 and F16F15/305 .
[F16F 1/00] Springs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Springs and spring elements made of elastic material  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Springs working with fluid -> F16F5/00 , F16F9/00
[F16F 1/02] made of steel or other material having low internal friction -- F16F 1/02 relates to springs constructed from steel or other materials that exhibit low internal friction, enabling efficient energy storage and release. Such springs are employed in mechanical engineering where minimal energy loss and reliable elastic performance are essential.
[F16F 1/021] characterised by their composition, e.g. comprising materials providing for particular spring properties  composition and manufacture of clock or watch springs  G04B1/145 -- F16F 1/021 covers springs that are defined primarily by the material or alloy from which they are made, including the use of specific compositions to achieve particular elastic or damping properties and the manufacturing methods for clock or watch springs. The subclass links to G04B1/145, which deals with time-keeping spring designs.
[F16F 1/022] made of ceramic materials -- F16F 1/022 refers to springs or spring elements that are manufactured from ceramic materials. It includes any type of mechanical spring-compression, tension, torsion or constant-force-where the load-bearing components are made of ceramics, offering high temperature resistance, corrosion protection and low weight.
[F16F 1/024] Covers or coatings therefor  F16F1/24  takes precedence -- The code F16F 1/024 designates spring elements that are provided with protective covers, cladding or surface coatings. It includes any material applied to a spring to shield it from corrosion, wear, chemical attack or to impart insulating or decorative properties. The classification is used for springs whose protective treatment is an integral part of the device.
[F16F 1/025] characterised by having a particular shape  F16F1/04 ,  F16F1/14 ,  F16F1/18 ,  F16F1/32 ,  F16F1/34  take precedence -- A subclass of springs defined by the specific shape of the spring element, superseding the more general groups listed (F16F1/04, F16F1/14, F16F1/18, F16F1/32, F16F1/34). It resides within mechanical engineering, under engineering elements and units, and is part of the CPC system used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F16F 1/027] Planar, e.g. in sheet form; leaf springs -- The code F16F 1/027 designates planar or sheet-form springs, commonly called leaf springs, which are flat metal elements that flex to absorb loads and provide suspension or support in mechanical systems.
[F16F 1/028] cylindrical, with radial openings -- F16F 1/028 designates cylindrical springs that incorporate radial openings through the coil or body. The openings reduce mass, allow fluid passage, or provide mounting features while preserving the spring's load-deflection behavior.
[F16F 1/04] Wound springs -- F16F 1/04 covers wound springs, which are made by coiling a wire or strip into a helical shape to store mechanical energy. The subclass includes torsion, compression and extension springs formed by such winding, with variations in pitch, diameter and end treatment.
[F16F 1/041] with means for modifying the spring characteristics  F16F1/12 ,  F16F3/06  take precedence; fluid regulation of coil spring characteristics in vehicle suspensions  B60G17/0272 -- This subclass covers springs that include mechanisms for adjusting their characteristics, such as stiffness, preload or damping, often by using fluid pressure or flow to vary coil spring behavior in vehicle suspension systems, and takes precedence over the earlier subclasses F16F1/12 and F16F3/06.
[F16F 1/042] characterised by the cross-section of the wire -- The subclass F16F 1/042 groups spring inventions where the defining feature is the shape of the wire cross-section, such as round, square, rectangular or other profiles, influencing stiffness and performance. It applies to mechanical engineering spring elements and units.
[F16F 1/043] the cross-section varying with the wire length -- F16F 1/043 classifies springs whose wire cross-section is not uniform but varies along the length, such as tapered or otherwise shaped coil wires. The varying profile modifies the spring's stiffness and load distribution, and the subclass is part of the mechanical-engineering group covering springs.
[F16F 1/045] Canted-coil springs -- Canted-coil springs are helical springs whose coils are set at an angle to the spring axis, enabling combined axial and radial deflection under load. This geometry provides progressive stiffness and good vibration-damping characteristics, making them suited for applications such as pressure regulation, suspension systems, and load-support devices. The CPC entry F16F 1/045 classifies these inclined-coil devices within the broader group of mechanical springs.
[F16F 1/046] with partial nesting of inner and outer coils  F16F3/04  takes precedence -- F16F 1/046 defines spring configurations where the inner and outer coils are partially nested, meaning the coils overlap but are not fully interleaved. This arrangement is employed to adjust spring characteristics such as stiffness or space utilization. The subclass is superseded where the broader group F16F3/04 applies.
[F16F 1/047] characterised by varying pitch -- F16F 1/047 covers springs whose coil pitch varies along their length, such as conical or other variable-pitch designs, providing non-linear load-deflection characteristics or fitting particular space constraints.
[F16F 1/048] with undulations, e.g. wavy springs -- F16F 1/048 covers springs whose coils or elements are formed with periodic undulations, producing a wavy or sinusoidal shape rather than a simple helical or straight profile. These wavy springs are used to achieve specific force-deflection characteristics, damping, or space-saving configurations in mechanical systems.
[F16F 1/06] with turns lying in cylindrical surfaces -- This subclass covers springs whose individual turns are positioned on a cylindrical surface, such as helical compression or extension springs where the coil follows the contour of a cylinder. The definition applies to any design where the spring windings lie in a continuous cylindrical geometry.
[F16F 1/065] characterised by loading of the coils in a radial direction  canted-coil springs  F16F1/045 -- A subclass of springs in which the coils are loaded in a radial direction, known as canted-coil springs, designed to absorb or transmit forces applied outward from the central axis of the spring.
[F16F 1/08] with turns lying in mainly conical surfaces -- F16F 1/08 covers springs whose coils are formed on essentially conical surfaces, meaning the turns are arranged so the spring tapers in diameter from one end to the other. This subclass includes conical compression, tension or torsion springs where the geometry follows a mainly conical shape, distinguishing them from cylindrical or helical springs with uniform coil radii.
[F16F 1/10] Spiral springs with turns lying substantially in plane surfaces -- F16F 1/10 covers spiral springs whose coils are arranged essentially in a single plane, such as flat torsion or clock springs, where the turns lie substantially within the same surface. These devices store mechanical energy by twisting or bending within that planar configuration.
[F16F 1/12] Attachments or mountings -- F16F 1/12 covers devices and arrangements for fixing springs to other parts, including brackets, housings, clamps, and other mounting elements that secure a spring in position or transmit its force to a structure. It includes the design of attachment mechanisms that enable proper alignment, adjustment, or replacement of the spring within mechanical assemblies.
[F16F 1/121] adjustable, e.g. to modify spring characteristics -- F16F 1/121 classifies springs that can be adjusted to change their mechanical properties, such as stiffness, preload or damping, by mechanisms that vary coil spacing, leaf thickness, effective length or other parameters. The adjustment may be manual or automatic and is intended to adapt the spring's behavior to different loads or operating conditions.
[F16F 1/122] where coils, e.g. end coils, of the spring are rigidly clamped or similarly fixed -- F16F 1/122 relates to springs whose coils, including end coils, are rigidly clamped or otherwise fixed in place, preventing coil movement and ensuring stable force transmission. This feature is used to improve load holding and reduce deformation in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16F 1/123] characterised by the ends of the spring being specially adapted, e.g. to form an eye for engagement with a radial insert  F16F1/122 ,  F16F1/125  take precedence -- The F16F 1/123 subclass covers springs whose terminal sections are specially shaped, such as forming an eye, to engage a radial insert or similar component. This design detail distinguishes the spring ends for secure attachment or mechanical interfacing, and it supersedes the broader categories F16F 1/122 and F16F 1/125.
[F16F 1/125] where the end coils of the spring engage an axial insert  F16F1/126 ,  F16F1/128  take precedence -- This subclass covers springs whose end coils are designed to engage an axial insert, such as a rod or threaded sleeve, so that the spring's load is transferred directly to the insert. It includes configurations where the terminal turns wrap around or lock onto the insert, providing axial positioning and load transmission.
[F16F 1/126] comprising an element between the end coil of the spring and the support proper, e.g. an elastomeric annulus  F16F1/13  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass (F16F 1/126) covers springs that incorporate an intermediate element-such as an elastomeric annulus-between the spring's end coil and its support, providing damping, isolation or load-distribution functions. It is nested under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs, and follows the broader group F16F 1/13.
[F16F 1/127] allowing rotation about axis of spring -- F16F 1/127 covers spring designs that permit the spring to rotate around its own longitudinal axis during operation, such as torsion or helical springs with rotating ends or fittings that allow angular movement while maintaining elastic function. This subclass addresses mechanisms that facilitate axial rotation without compromising the spring's load-bearing capabilities.
[F16F 1/128] with motion-limiting means, e.g. with a full-length guide element or ball joint connections; with protective outer cover  F16F1/121  takes precedence -- F16F 1/128 classifies springs that incorporate motion-limiting means such as a full-length guide element or ball-joint connections and are provided with a protective outer cover. The subclass is applied to designs where the spring's travel is restrained and the device is shielded from environmental damage.
[F16F 1/13] comprising inserts and spacers between the windings for changing the mechanical or physical characteristics of the spring -- F16F 1/13 covers springs that include inserts or spacers positioned between the windings, allowing the mechanical or physical characteristics of the spring-such as stiffness, load response, or damping-to be altered.
[F16F 1/14] Torsion springs consisting of bars or tubes -- F16F 1/14 covers torsion springs made from solid bars or hollow tubes that store energy by twisting around an axis. The spring's ends are anchored and the shaft rotates when torque is applied, commonly used in clamp mechanisms, hinges, and load-bearing devices.
[F16F 1/145] with means for modifying the spring characteristics  fluid regulation of torsion spring characteristics in vehicle suspensions  B60G17/0277 -- F16F 1/145 covers springs that include devices for changing their behavior, specifically methods that regulate the torsional characteristics of a spring by using fluid pressure in vehicle suspension systems. This allows the spring stiffness or response to be adjusted dynamically.
[F16F 1/16] Attachments or mountings -- The F16F 1/16 subclass covers devices that connect or secure springs to other parts, including brackets, pins, collars, clips and other mounting elements. These attachments enable proper positioning, load transmission and retention of springs within mechanical assemblies.
[F16F 1/18] Leaf springs -- Leaf springs are one-piece or multi-leaf flexible members, usually steel or composite, that support loads and absorb shocks in vehicle suspensions and other mechanical systems. They consist of stacked, tapered plates fastened together to form a simple, compact spring element for automotive, railway and industrial applications.
[F16F 1/182] with inter-engaging portions between leaves or between leaves and mountings, e.g. ridges, notches, ripples -- The CPC code F16F 1/182 pertains to leaf springs that incorporate inter-engaging features such as ridges, notches or ripples on the leaf surfaces or on the interfaces with their mountings. These elements allow the leaves to lock together or to connect securely with brackets, enhancing structural stability and load transmission.
[F16F 1/185] characterised by shape or design of individual leaves  F16F1/22  takes precedence -- This classification entry, F16F 1/185, pertains to springs within the broader category of engineering elements and units. It specifically addresses springs characterized by the shape or design of their individual leaves, noting that F16F1/22 takes precedence in cases where it applies.
[F16F 1/187] shaped into an open profile, i.e. C- or U-shaped -- F16F 1/187 covers springs whose wire or material is formed into an open profile such as a C- or U-shape. The open section allows the spring to flex while providing space for insertion of other components or for easier manufacturing. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs.
[F16F 1/20] with layers, e.g. anti-friction layers, or with rollers between the leaves -- This subclass covers leaf springs that incorporate intermediate layers or rollers between the leaves, such as anti-friction coatings or separate rolling elements, to reduce friction, distribute loads, and enhance durability.
[F16F 1/22] with means for modifying the spring characteristic -- This subclass covers springs that incorporate devices or mechanisms allowing their elastic characteristics to be altered, such as adjustable preload, variable stiffness, or tunable response. It includes designs where the spring's force-deflection behavior can be modified during operation or by user adjustment, facilitating adaptable performance in mechanical systems.
[F16F 1/24] Lubrication -- F16F 1/24 covers lubrication of springs, including methods, apparatus and materials used to apply oil, grease or other lubricants to spring elements to reduce friction, wear and corrosion. It addresses techniques for delivering lubricant to coil, leaf or torsion springs during manufacturing or operation.
[F16F 1/26] Attachments or mountings -- F16F 1/26 covers devices that attach or mount springs to other parts, such as brackets, pins, clips, housings and other mounting assemblies designed to secure springs in place during operation. It includes means for fixing, supporting or positioning springs within mechanical systems.
[F16F 1/28] comprising cylindrical metal pins pivoted in close-fitting sleeves -- F16F 1/28 relates to spring assemblies that incorporate cylindrical metal pins mounted in close-fitting sleeves, allowing the pins to pivot and provide a resilient or flexible connection. This configuration is used in mechanical devices where controlled movement or energy storage is required.
[F16F 1/30] comprising intermediate pieces made of rubber or similar elastic material -- The CPC subclass F16F 1/30 pertains to springs that incorporate intermediate pieces of rubber or comparable elastic material, such as elastomeric inserts or dampers positioned within the coil. These elastomeric components provide additional flexibility, shock absorption or damping while retaining the basic function of the spring.
[F16F 13/00] Units comprising springs of the non-fluid type as well as vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers, or fluid springs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices comprising a combination of a plastic springs (e.g. elastomeric springs) and dampers using friction or fluid  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Using liquid springs -> F16F5/00 - Unit comprising a magnetic spring -> F16F6/00 - Device comprising a sponge rubber as pressure absorbing means -> F16F9/003
[F16F 13/002] comprising at least one fluid spring  F16F13/005 ,  F16F13/02 ,  F16F13/04  take precedence -- In CPC class F16F 13/002 the classification covers mechanical units that incorporate at least one fluid spring, such as hydraulic or pneumatic springs used for energy storage, vibration damping, or shock absorption. This subgroup is subordinate to, and takes precedence from, the more specific groups F16F13/005, F16F13/02 and F16F13/04.
[F16F 13/005] comprising both a wound spring and a damper, e.g. a friction damper -- This subclass covers devices that incorporate a wound coil spring together with a damping mechanism, such as a friction damper, to provide combined spring and shock-absorbing action. It applies to mechanical units where the spring and damper are integrated in a single assembly.
[F16F 13/007] the damper being a fluid damper -- F16F 13/007 pertains to devices whose damping element is a fluid damper, using a liquid or gas to absorb vibration or shock. It applies to springs, shock-absorbers and other vibration-dampening units within the mechanical-engineering class for springs.
[F16F 13/02] damping by frictional contact between the spring and braking means -- The code F16F 13/02 covers spring systems where damping is produced by frictional contact between the spring element and a braking component, such as friction plates or surfaces that absorb kinetic energy. This approach is used to control vibrations or shock by converting motion into heat through the interacting surfaces.
[F16F 13/04] comprising both a plastics spring and a damper, e.g. a friction damper -- F16F 13/04 covers units that integrate a plastic spring together with a damping element, such as a friction damper, within a single assembly. These devices belong to the class of non-fluid springs and vibration-absorbing components used in mechanical engineering.
[F16F 13/06] the damper being a fluid damper, e.g. the plastics spring not forming a part of the wall of the fluid chamber of the damper -- This subclass covers fluid dampers that employ a plastic spring which is not incorporated into the wall of the fluid chamber, separating them from designs where the spring forms part of the chamber. It is nested under mechanical engineering, springs and vibration-dampers.
[F16F 13/08] the plastics spring forming at least a part of the wall of the fluid chamber of the damper -- F16F 13/08 relates to springs made of plastic that form at least a portion of the wall of the fluid chamber in a damper, integrating the spring element with the sealing structure of the chamber. This design is used in vibration-dampers and shock-absorbers to provide spring action while containing the fluid.
[F16F 13/085] characterised by features of plastics springs; Attachment arrangements -- The CPC subclass F16F 13/085 pertains to springs that are formed from plastic materials and includes the specific features of those plastic springs, such as their design and performance characteristics, together with the methods and arrangements used to attach or mount the springs in a device or assembly.
[F16F 13/10] the wall being at least in part formed by a flexible membrane or the like -- This CPC subclass covers spring or fluid-type units whose wall is at least partly made from a flexible membrane or similar structure, describing designs where part of the housing can deform to accommodate pressure changes or movement, as used in vibration dampers, shock absorbers and fluid springs.
[F16F 13/101] characterised by buffering features or stoppers -- F16F 13/101 relates to mechanical units that include springs and provide buffering features or stoppers to limit motion, such as shock-absorbers or vibration-dampers equipped with built-in stops. This subclass falls under springs in the engineering elements and units hierarchy.
[F16F 13/102] characterised by features of flexible walls of equilibration chambers; decoupling or self-tuning means -- F16F 13/102 covers spring units of the non-fluid type that incorporate flexible walls forming equilibration chambers, where the chambers' elasticity is used to provide decoupling, self-tuning or vibration-damping functions, such as shock-absorbers or fluid-spring devices that adjust their response automatically. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs.
[F16F 13/103] characterised by method of assembly, production or treatment -- F16F 13/103 pertains to spring units-including mechanical springs, vibration dampers, shock-absorbers and fluid springs-where the invention is defined by the way the device is assembled, manufactured or subjected to a specific treatment, rather than by its functional characteristics.
[F16F 13/105] characterised by features of partitions between two working chambers -- F16F 13/105 covers spring-type units, including vibration dampers, shock-absorbers and fluid springs, that are distinguished by the design of the partition separating two working chambers. The subclass applies when the shape, material or movement of the partition is a defining functional feature of the device.
[F16F 13/106] Design of constituent elastomeric parts, e.g. decoupling valve elements, or of immediate abutments therefor, e.g. cages -- The F16F 13/106 subclass covers the design of elastomeric components that form part of non-fluid springs, vibration dampers or shock absorbers, such as the elastomeric elements used in decoupling valves. It also includes the design of the immediate supporting structures for those parts, for example the cages that hold the elastomeric elements in place.
[F16F 13/107] Passage design between working chambers -- F16F 13/107 concerns the design of passages that connect the working chambers of non-fluid springs, vibration dampers, shock absorbers or similar fluid springs, focusing on the geometry and flow paths that allow fluid or mechanical coupling between chambers.
[F16F 13/108] characterised by features of plastics springs, e.g. attachment arrangements  F16F13/18  takes precedence -- This classification entry pertains to mechanical engineering elements, specifically focusing on units that include non-fluid type springs and vibration dampers. It details a subcategory for springs made of plastic materials, highlighting characteristics such as attachment methods, with a note that another code takes precedence in certain contexts.
[F16F 13/14] Units of the bushing type -- F16F 13/14 designates bushing-type units that incorporate a spring element for vibration damping, shock absorption or similar functions. These devices belong to the mechanical-engineering group of non-fluid spring units used as engineering elements and units.
[F16F 13/1409] characterised by buffering features or stoppers -- F16F 13/1409 covers units that incorporate springs or similar vibration-dampening elements where the design includes buffering features or built-in stoppers to limit motion. These are non-fluid spring assemblies such as shock absorbers with stop mechanisms.
[F16F 13/1418] characterised by the location or shape of the equilibration chamber -- F16F 13/1418 pertains to spring units, such as vibration dampers, shock absorbers, or fluid springs, where the defining feature is the position or shape of the equilibration chamber within the device.
[F16F 13/1427] characterised by features of flexible walls of equilibration chambers; decoupling or self-tuning means -- The entry covers non-fluid springs, vibration dampers, shock absorbers or fluid springs whose equilibration chambers have flexible walls that can change shape or volume, allowing the device to decouple from external motions or to self-tune its stiffness or damping characteristics. This feature is used to improve vibration isolation and adaptive response in mechanical systems.
[F16F 13/1436] with free- or virtually free-floating members -- This subgroup covers spring units in which one or more components are not rigidly attached but can move freely or almost freely within the assembly, such as coils or dampers with floating elements that slide or rotate to accommodate motion or load changes. It includes designs where the spring's members are deliberately unconstrained to enhance flexibility, vibration isolation, or shock-absorption.
[F16F 13/1445] characterised by method of assembly, production or treatment -- F16F 13/1445 covers non-fluid spring units such as mechanical springs, vibration dampers and shock absorbers, where the patent focus is on the specific method of assembly, production or treatment applied to the component.
[F16F 13/1454] Sealing of units -- The code refers to inventions that provide ways to seal spring-type units such as vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or fluid springs, preventing the escape of fluid or ingress of contaminants while allowing the spring to function. It includes sealing arrangements, gaskets, membranes or pressurised chambers integrated into the unit.
