[F16F 13/1463] characterised by features of passages between working chambers -- This subclass covers spring units, including non-fluid springs, vibration dampers, shock absorbers and fluid springs, that are characterised by passages connecting their working chambers, which affect fluid communication or pressure distribution within the device.
[F16F 13/1472] Valve elements to cope with over-pressure, e.g. lips -- The subclass F16F 13/1472 covers valve elements incorporated in non-fluid spring units, vibration dampers, shock-absorbers or fluid springs that relieve excess pressure, such as flexible lips that open when pressure exceeds a set limit. These components act as over-pressure safety or pressure-control devices within spring-type mechanisms.
[F16F 13/1481] characterised by features of plastic springs, e.g. presence of cavities or stiffeners; characterised by features of flexible walls of equilibration chambers, i.e. membranes -- This CPC subclass relates to spring units that use plastic springs, distinguished by design elements such as cavities, stiffeners or flexible membranes forming equilibration chambers, and is applied to non-fluid springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or fluid springs.
[F16F 13/149] Multiple bushings connected together; Restraining links -- F16F 13/149 relates to units comprising non-fluid springs, such as vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or fluid springs, where several bushings are joined together to form a composite element. It also includes restraining links that limit movement of the connected bushings within the assembly.
[F16F 13/16] specially adapted for receiving axial loads -- F16F 13/16 covers non-fluid spring units that are specially designed to bear axial loads, such as helical or leaf springs configured to transmit force along their axis. These components may include integrated vibration dampers or shock-absorbers that function primarily in the axial direction.
[F16F 13/18] characterised by the location or the shape of the equilibration chamber, e.g. the equilibration chamber, surrounding the plastics spring or being annular -- The F16F 13/18 subgroup pertains to spring units - particularly plastic or fluid springs - where the defining feature is the position or geometry of an equilibration chamber. Examples include chambers that encircle the spring or are formed as annular spaces to balance pressure or temperature.
[F16F 1/32] Belleville-type springs -- Belleville-type springs are conical disc springs that provide a high load in a very short axial space by flexing like a washer. They are used where compact, high-force spring action is required, such as in clutches, valve actuators and vibration isolation. This entry falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → springs.
[F16F 13/20] characterised by comprising also a pneumatic spring -- F16F 13/20 pertains to mechanical units that combine a conventional solid spring with a pneumatic (air) spring, such as shock absorbers or vibration dampers that use both types of spring action.
[F16F 1/322] Snap-action springs -- Snap-action springs are mechanical devices that store energy and release it suddenly, causing a rapid transition between two stable positions when a load exceeds a set threshold. They are employed in switches, relays and other fast-acting mechanisms and are classified under CPC code F16F 1/322 within the springs subgroup of engineering elements and units.
[F16F 13/22] characterised by comprising also a dynamic damper -- F16F 13/22 denotes mechanical units that incorporate springs together with a dynamic damper, such as vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers, used to control oscillations in a system. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs.
[F16F 1/324] characterised by having tongues or arms directed in a generally radial direction, i.e. diaphragm-type springs -- F16F 1/324 covers diaphragm-type springs where the flexible elements are formed as tongues or arms that extend outward in a generally radial direction. These springs deform like a diaphragm to provide a restoring force and are used in applications requiring compact, low-profile elastic components.
[F16F 13/24] the central part of the unit being supported by one element and both extremities of the unit being supported by a single other element, i.e. double acting mounting -- It classifies spring units of the non-fluid type, such as vibration dampers or shock absorbers, where the central portion of the spring is mounted on one support while both ends are mounted on a single other support, i.e. a double-acting mounting arrangement. This arrangement is covered by CPC code F16F 13/24 within the mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F16F 1/326] with a spiral-like appearance -- F16F 1/326 classifies springs that possess a spiral-like shape, typically helical or coil forms where the spring element winds around a central axis. It covers designs where the spiral geometry defines the spring's elasticity and load-bearing behavior, such as compression, tension, torsion or wind-up springs used in mechanical assemblies.
[F16F 13/26] characterised by adjusting or regulating devices responsive to exterior conditions -- F16F 13/26 covers spring units, including vibration-dampers, shock absorbers or fluid springs, that incorporate adjusting or regulating devices which automatically modify the spring's behaviour in response to external conditions such as temperature, pressure, load or motion. These adaptive mechanisms enable the spring to maintain performance despite varying operating environments.
[F16F 13/262] changing geometry of passages between working and equilibration chambers, e.g. cross-sectional area or length  F16F13/28  takes precedence -- A subclass of mechanical-engineering springs that includes units such as fluid springs, dampers and shock absorbers in which the geometry of the passages connecting the working chamber to the equilibration chamber can be altered, for example by varying the cross-sectional area or length of the flow channel.
[F16F 13/264] comprising means for acting dynamically on the walls bounding a working chamber -- This subclass includes spring-based or other mechanical units that apply dynamic forces to the walls of a working chamber, such as pistons, diaphragms or elastic elements used to modulate pressure, vibration or fluid displacement during operation. These devices are typically employed in compressors, shock absorbers or hydraulic accumulators to provide controlled response to changing loads.
[F16F 13/266] comprising means for acting dynamically on the walls bounding a passage between working and equilibration chambers -- This subclass covers spring-type units or vibration-damping devices that incorporate elements which act dynamically on the walls of a passage separating a working chamber from an equilibration chamber, such as actuators, flexible membranes or movable seals that modulate pressure or flow between the two spaces.
[F16F 13/268] comprising means for acting dynamically on the walls bounding an equilibration chamber  F16F13/264  take precedence -- This classification entry pertains to mechanical engineering units that include non-fluid springs, vibration dampers, and shock absorbers. Specifically, it addresses components with dynamic action on the walls of an equilibration chamber, noting that certain subcategories take precedence in this context.
[F16F 1/328] with undulations, e.g. wavy springs -- F16F 1/328 covers springs that have a sinusoidal or wavy shape, where the coils or helical elements are formed with undulations along their length. These undulating springs are designed to provide specific load-deflection characteristics, often for vibration isolation or energy storage.
[F16F 13/28] specially adapted for units of the bushing type -- F16F 13/28 is a subclass in the Cooperative Patent Classification that covers spring or fluid-spring units specially adapted as bushings. It belongs to the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering - Engineering Elements and Units - Springs and concerns designs where a bushing functions as the spring or shock-absorbing element.
[F16F 13/30] comprising means for varying fluid viscosity, e.g. of magnetic or electrorheological fluids -- The CPC subclass F16F 13/30 covers devices that include mechanisms to vary the viscosity of fluids, such as magnetic or electrorheological fluids, used in springs, shock absorbers, or other vibration-damping units. It is located in the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs.
[F16F 13/305] magnetorheological -- F16F 13/305 covers spring-type or damping units that employ magnetorheological fluids whose viscosity changes under a magnetic field, providing adjustable stiffness or shock-absorption. These devices are used where variable resistance to motion is required, such as in adaptive suspension, vibration control, or fluid-based shock absorbers.
[F16F 1/34] Ring springs, i.e. annular bodies deformed radially due to axial load -- Ring springs are annular components that compress or expand radially when an axial load is applied, providing spring action in a circular form. They are used where space constraints or uniform circumferential force distribution are required, such as in sealing or suspension applications.
[F16F 1/36] made of rubber or other material having high internal friction, -- F16F 1/36 covers springs that are manufactured from rubber or other materials possessing high internal friction, providing elastic resistance while dissipating energy. These elements are used where vibration damping and shock absorption are required in mechanical systems.
[F16F 1/3605] characterised by their material  F16F1/362 ,  F16F1/364 ,  F16F1/366 ,  F16F1/37  take precedence; composition of macromolecular compounds in general  C08L -- F16F 1/3605 classifies springs that are defined primarily by the material from which they are fabricated, such as specific polymers, composites, or other substances, without regard to shape or function. When a spring is also defined by more detailed material or structural features covered in subclasses F16F1/362, 364, 366, or 37, those take precedence. The class falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs.
[F16F 1/361] comprising magneto-rheological elastomers [MR],  magneto-rheological fluid dampers  F16F9/535 -- This subclass covers springs that incorporate magneto-rheological elastomers or use magneto-rheological fluid dampers to provide variable stiffness or damping characteristics. The technology enables the spring's mechanical response to be adjusted in real time by applying a magnetic field, allowing adaptive performance in vibration control and shock-absorption applications.
[F16F 1/3615] with means for modifying the spring characteristic  F16F1/371  takes precedence -- The subclass F16F 1/3615 relates to springs that include elements for altering the spring's characteristic, such as stiffness, load-deflection curve or preload, during use. It covers devices with adjustable geometry, variable-force mechanisms or active control means that modify performance. The broader subclass F16F 1/371, which also deals with adjustable-characteristic springs, is taken as the preceding classification.
[F16F 1/362] made of steel wool, compressed hair, -- The F16F 1/362 subclass covers springs whose elastic element is formed from steel wool or compressed hair, detailing the use of these unusual fibrous or metallic filaments as the primary material for spring construction within mechanical engineering applications.
[F16F 1/364] made of cork, wood or like material -- The subclass F16F 1/364 covers springs whose body or components are formed from cork, wood, or other comparable organic or natural-material substitutes. It applies to any spring design-such as compression, tension, or torsion types-where these materials serve as the primary load-bearing element.
[F16F 1/366] made of fibre-reinforced plastics, -- Springs made of fibre-reinforced plastics are composite spring elements that use polymer matrices reinforced with fibers to achieve high strength, low weight, and excellent corrosion resistance, making them suitable for lightweight and durable applications.
[F16F 1/3665] Wound springs -- F16F 1/3665 covers wound springs, a type of mechanical spring formed by coiling wire or ribbon into a helical shape to store and release energy. These components are used in various engineering applications for tension, compression, or torsional loading. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, in the CPC system shared by EPO and USPTO.
[F16F 1/368] Leaf springs -- Leaf springs are slender, layered steel bars used as suspension components that flex to absorb shocks, typically mounted longitudinally between chassis and axle in vehicles and other machinery. They belong to the subclass of springs in mechanical engineering dealing with elastic elements designed to support loads and provide ride comfort. This CPC code covers designs, materials, and mounting arrangements specific to leaf springs.
[F16F 1/3683] Attachments or mountings therefor -- F16F 1/3683 covers attachments or mountings for springs, including devices that connect springs to other components and enable their installation, adjustment or removal in mechanical systems.
[F16F 1/3686] End mountings -- F16F 1/3686 - End mountings are the components fixed to the ends of a spring that secure it to a supporting structure or load, transmitting forces and maintaining proper alignment. They may be brackets, plates, sleeves or other hardware designed to protect the coils and distribute load efficiently.
[F16F 1/37] of foam-like material , e.g. sponge rubber -- F16F 1/37 classifies springs that are formed from or incorporate foam-like materials such as sponge rubber, where the elastic behaviour relies on the cellular or porous nature of the material rather than metal coils. It covers designs in which the spring action is achieved by compression or deformation of the foam element, including various shapes and configurations intended for mechanical engineering applications.
[F16F 1/371] characterised by inserts or auxiliary extension elements, e.g. for rigidification -- F16F 1/371 covers springs that incorporate additional inserts or auxiliary extension elements designed to increase stiffness or prevent deformation, such as solid rods, plates or other reinforcing components attached to the spring body. These features are used to rigidify the spring while maintaining its basic function.
[F16F 1/3713] with external elements passively influencing spring stiffness, e.g. rings or hoops -- A subclass of mechanical-engineering springs that are equipped with passive external components, such as rings, hoops or sleeves, which alter the spring's stiffness without active control. The external elements are fixed or surrounding parts that influence the spring's elastic characteristics.
[F16F 1/3716] External elements such as covers or envelopes, that are flexible -- This subclass covers flexible external components such as covers, envelopes, or protective sleeves that are fitted to springs to provide sealing, insulation, or aesthetic protection, often forming a flexible sheath around the spring element.
[F16F 1/373] characterised by having a particular shape -- F16F 1/373 pertains to springs whose defining feature is a specific geometric shape, such as helical, conical, or serpentine forms, where the shape itself determines the spring's function or operation. It groups patents that emphasize shape as the primary characteristic of the spring design.
[F16F 1/3732] having an annular or the like shape, e.g. grommet-type resilient mountings -- This subclass concerns springs shaped as a closed ring or similar annular form, such as grommet-type resilient mounts used to isolate vibrations or support components. The design offers radial compliance while maintaining axial stiffness, making it suitable for mounting applications in mechanical systems.
[F16F 1/3735] Multi-part grommet-type resilient mountings -- F16F 1/3735 covers multi-part grommet-type resilient mountings, which are spring assemblies formed from two or more interlocking components that create a grommet shape to provide vibration isolation, shock absorption, and positional stability for mechanical systems. These devices are typically used to mount equipment to structures while allowing limited movement and reducing transmission of forces.
[F16F 1/3737] Planar, e.g. in sheet form  vibration dampers comprising one or more constrained viscoelastic layers  F16F9/306 -- This CPC subclass (F16F 1/3737) falls under mechanical engineering - engineering elements and units - springs and covers planar vibration dampers, typically in sheet form, that incorporate one or more constrained viscoelastic layers to reduce oscillations. The classification groups devices that use thin, layered viscoelastic materials to provide damping in a flat configuration.
[F16F 1/374] having a spherical or the like shape -- F16F 1/374 covers springs whose active element is formed in a spherical or ball-like shape, such as ball springs or spherical compression springs. These springs provide axial or radial force while accommodating limited movement and are used in mechanisms where compact, isotropic load distribution is required.
[F16F 1/376] having projections, studs, serrations or the like on at least one surface -- The subclass F16F 1/376 covers springs that incorporate projections, studs, serrations or similar features on at least one of their surfaces. These surface elements are intended to provide improved engagement, locking, or load-distribution characteristics within the spring's operation.
[F16F 1/377] having holes or openings -- This subclass covers springs that incorporate one or more holes, slots or other openings in the spring material. The openings may be provided for weight reduction, fluid passage, attachment points or to modify stiffness, and apply to any type of mechanical spring within the broader F16F group.
[F16F 1/379] characterised by arrangements for controlling the spring temperature, e.g. by cooling -- The classification pertains to springs that incorporate means for managing their temperature, such as built-in cooling or heat-dissipating arrangements, to maintain performance and prevent overheating during operation. It covers designs where temperature control is an integral part of the spring's structure or supporting components.
[F16F 1/38] with a sleeve of elastic material between a rigid outer sleeve and a rigid inner sleeve or pin -- This subclass covers springs designed with a sleeve of elastic material placed between a rigid outer sleeve and a rigid inner sleeve or pin, providing damping or cushioning while preserving the spring's load-bearing function.
[F16F 1/3807] characterised by adaptations for particular modes of stressing -- The subclass groups springs that include design adaptations tailored to a specific mode of stressing, such as tension, compression, bending or torsion. These adaptations may involve altered geometry, material choice or supporting features that optimise the spring's performance under the intended stress condition.
[F16F 1/3814] characterised by adaptations to counter axial forces  F16F1/393  takes precedence -- This CPC subgroup covers springs that include design features specifically intended to counter axial forces along the spring axis, such as end fittings, pre-load devices or geometry that limit compression or tension. It is a subcategory of the springs group in mechanical engineering and is overridden by the more specific subgroup F16F1/393.
[F16F 1/3821] characterised by adaptations to counter torsional forces -- F16F 1/3821 covers springs that incorporate features to resist or mitigate torsional loads, such as twisted coils, helical tension springs with particular geometry, or composite elements that absorb twisting. These adaptations may include pre-stress, anti-torsion eyes, or special winding directions to improve performance in rotary applications.
[F16F 1/3828] End stop features or buffering  F16F1/3807  takes precedence -- The class F16F 1/3828 relates to spring designs that incorporate end-stop or buffering elements to limit travel, absorb shock or control motion. It covers built-in stops, damping devices, cushions or other features that protect the spring or adjoining components during operation and it is subordinate to the broader F16F 1/3807 category.
[F16F 1/3835] characterised by the sleeve of elastic material, e.g. having indentations or made of materials of different hardness  F16F1/3807 ,  F16F1/387  take precedence -- A spring whose surrounding sleeve is made of an elastic material, which may include indentations or zones of differing hardness, falls under CPC subclass F16F 1/3835. This subclass refines the broader spring category and takes precedence over the related subclasses F16F 1/3807 and F16F 1/387.
[F16F 1/3842] Method of assembly, production or treatment; Mounting thereof  supports for pipes, cables or protective tubing  F16L3/00 -- F16F 1/3842 covers methods for assembling, producing or treating supports that secure pipes, cables or protective tubing, including the mounting processes for such supports. It applies to mechanical engineering solutions that attach or position these conduits within larger systems.
[F16F 1/3849] Mounting brackets therefor, e.g. stamped steel brackets; Restraining links -- The code F16F 1/3849 covers devices that mount or secure springs, including stamped-steel brackets and restraining links used to attach springs to other parts of a mechanical assembly. These elements are intended to hold the spring in place and control its motion or position.
[F16F 1/3856] Vulcanisation or gluing of interface between rigid and elastic sleeves -- The code covers techniques for joining a rigid sleeve to an elastic sleeve in a spring, such as vulcanisation or adhesive bonding, to create a durable interface that transfers loads while preserving flexibility. These methods are used in composite spring designs where a hard core is surrounded by a rubber or elastomeric layer.
[F16F 1/3863] characterised by the rigid sleeves or pin, e.g. of non-circular cross-section  F16F1/3807 ,  F16F1/387  take precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F16F 1/3863 pertains to springs characterized by rigid sleeves or pins, often with non-circular cross-sections, under the broader categories of Mechanical Engineering and Engineering Elements and Units. This specific classification takes precedence over related codes F16F1/3807 and F16F1/387 when applicable.
[F16F 1/387] comprising means for modifying the rigidity in particular directions -- The subclass F16F 1/387 refers to springs that include features for altering their stiffness selectively in specific directions. It covers designs where the rigidity can be varied, such as adjustable or self-adjusting elements, enabling tailored load-deflection behavior for particular applications.
[F16F 1/3873] having holes or openings -- This subgroup covers springs that incorporate holes, slots, or other openings in their material or coil shape. Such perforations may be used to reduce weight, allow fluid passage, or modify spring stiffness and response, and apply to any type of mechanical spring defined in the preceding classes.
[F16F 1/3876] by means of inserts of more rigid material -- F16F 1/3876 designates springs that incorporate inserts of a more rigid material to reinforce the spring body, modify load-deflection characteristics, or provide localized support. The inserts may be placed within coils, plates or other spring elements to increase stiffness, reduce fatigue or enable hybrid constructions.
[F16F 1/393] with spherical or conical sleeves -- F16F 1/393 relates to springs that are enclosed in a sleeve whose inner surface is spherical or conical, the sleeve serving to guide the spring's motion, distribute loads or protect the coil. The classification covers any mechanical device where such a sleeve is integral to the spring's function, including both metal and non-metal constructions. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and springs.
[F16F 1/3935] Conical sleeves -- Conical sleeves are tapered cylindrical components used in conical springs to guide and support the coils while allowing a gradual change in coil diameter. They provide axial alignment, distribute load evenly along the spring, and facilitate compact, variable-stiffness spring designs.
[F16F 1/40] consisting of a stack of similar elements separated by non-elastic intermediate layers -- F16F 1/40 covers spring assemblies that are formed by stacking a series of like spring elements with rigid, non-elastic layers positioned between each element, the layers keeping the individual springs separate while allowing the stack to act as a single spring unit. This design is used where a compact, modular spring structure is required and the intermediate layers provide support, damping or isolation.
[F16F 1/403] characterised by the shape of the non-elastic interengaging parts between the elements -- F16F 1/403 classifies spring mechanisms whose defining feature is the shape of the rigid interengaging components that link the elastic elements, such as hooks, lugs, or shaped inserts. The classification applies to patents where these non-elastic parts determine the overall function or assembly of the spring system.
[F16F 1/406] characterised by the shape of the elastic elements -- This subclass covers spring devices whose defining feature is the particular geometric form of the elastic elements, such as helices, convolutions or other shapes that influence load-deflection behavior. It is located within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs.
[F16F 1/41] the spring consisting of generally conically arranged elements -- F16F 1/41 pertains to springs formed from elements that are arranged in a generally conical configuration, such as tapered coil or conical leaf springs, where the geometry varies along the axis to provide a progressive spring rate. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs.
[F16F 1/42] characterised by the mode of stressing -- F16F 1/42 classifies springs that are distinguished by the manner in which they are stressed, such as tension, compression, torsion or a combination of these loading types. The subclass groups patents that define or claim springs primarily on the basis of their stress mode rather than geometry or material.
[F16F 1/422] the stressing resulting in flexion of the spring -- F16F 1/422 covers the mechanical stresses that cause a spring to bend or flex, detailing how loading conditions induce flexion in the spring element. It is used to classify inventions that address the analysis, design, or mitigation of such flexural stresses in springs.
[F16F 1/424] of membrane-type springs -- Membrane-type springs are springs that obtain their restoring force mainly from the elastic deformation of a thin, flexible membrane rather than from coils or leaf elements; they are used where a compact, low-profile spring action or sealing function is needed, often in vibration-damping, pressure-equalizing or acoustic-filtering applications.
[F16F 1/426] Radial flexion of ring-type springs -- F16F 1/426 pertains to the radial flexion of ring-type springs, i.e., the outward or inward bending of a spring formed as a closed loop or ring. It covers designs, mechanisms and methods that control or exploit this radial deformation for load handling, vibration isolation or torque transmission in mechanical systems.
[F16F 1/428] of strip- or leg-type springs -- F16F 1/428 covers springs formed from flexible strips or legs, such as leaf, cantilever or leg-type springs, which provide elastic deflection for supporting loads or absorbing vibrations. The classification applies to designs where the spring element consists of one or more elongated members that bend under load.
[F16F 1/44] loaded mainly in compression -- F16F 1/44 refers to springs that are principally subjected to compressive loads, where the primary deformation is a reduction in length. This includes compression coils, leaf or plate springs designed to store energy when squeezed, and excludes springs mainly loaded in tension or torsion.
[F16F 1/445] the spring material being contained in a generally closed space  F16F1/393  takes precedence -- This classification entry, F16F 1/445, pertains to springs where the spring material is enclosed within a mostly sealed environment. It specifies that when the material is also under fluid pressure, it should be classified under F16F1/393 instead.
[F16F 1/46] loaded mainly in tension -- F16F 1/46 covers springs that are designed to be loaded principally in tension, meaning they resist pulling forces rather than compression. Typical examples include extension or tension springs such as helical coil tension springs used in mechanical systems to store and release energy when stretched. This subgroup falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and springs.
[F16F 1/48] loaded mainly in torsion -- The code F16F 1/48 designates springs that are principally loaded in torsion, meaning they store energy by being twisted rather than compressed or extended. This includes torsion bars, helical torsion springs and similar devices used in mechanical systems to provide rotational force or return motion.
[F16F 1/50] loaded mainly in shear -- F16F 1/50 refers to springs that are designed to bear loads chiefly in shear rather than in tension or compression. Typical examples include leaf-type or plate springs where the principal deformation occurs by sliding parallel layers, providing support or damping under transverse forces. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs.
[F16F 15/00] Suppression of vibrations in systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Suppression of vibrations in rotating as well non rotating systems; and means or arrangements for avoiding or reducing out-of-balance forces; some examples:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Damping of non-rotary systems using inertia effect -> F16F7/10 - Prevention or isolation of vibrations in machine tools -> B23Q11/0032 - Vehicle seat suspension devices -> B60N2/50 - Absorbing or balancing means for aircraft propellers -> B64C11/008 - Absorbing or balancing means for rotorcraft rotors -> B64C27/001 - Methods or devices for protecting against, or damping of, acoustic waves, e.g. sound -> G10K11/16
[F16F 15/002] characterised by the control method or circuitry  control of mechanical oscillations  per se   G05D19/00 -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16F 15/002 pertains to the suppression of vibrations in systems, specifically focusing on methods and circuitry for controlling mechanical oscillations. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with springs within engineering elements and units.
[F16F 15/005] using electro- or magnetostrictive actuation means  generating of mechanical vibrations operating with electrostriction  B06B1/06 , with magnetostriction  B06B1/08 ; vehicle suspension arrangements characterised by use of piezoelectric elements  B60G17/01941 ; piezoelectric, electrostrictive and magnetostrictive devices  per se   H10N30/00 -- F16F 15/005 pertains to the suppression of vibrations in mechanical systems using electro- or magnetostrictive actuation means, which generate mechanical vibrations through electrostriction or magnetostriction. This classification also covers specific applications such as vehicle suspension arrangements with piezoelectric elements and devices that operate on these principles.
[F16F 15/007] Piezoelectric elements being placed under pre-constraint, e.g. placed under compression -- F16F 15/007 covers the use of piezoelectric components that are pre-constrained, such as being placed under compression, to improve vibration suppression in spring-based mechanical systems. This approach leverages the electromechanical coupling of pre-strained piezoelectric elements to enhance damping performance.
[F16F 15/02] Suppression of vibrations of non-rotating, e.g. reciprocating systems -- F16F 15/02 covers devices and methods that reduce or eliminate vibrations in non-rotating mechanisms such as reciprocating pistons, sliders or oscillating parts, using springs or other damping means. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → springs → suppression of vibrations in systems.
[F16F 15/021] Decoupling of vibrations by means of point-of-contact supports, e.g. ball bearings -- This subclass covers methods and devices that isolate or reduce vibration transmission by using point-contact supports such as ball bearings. The supports provide a localized contact that allows relative motion, thereby decoupling vibrating components from the rest of the structure. It is used in mechanical engineering to improve durability and performance of machines.
[F16F 15/022] using dampers and springs in combination -- F16F 15/022 pertains to the combined use of dampers and springs to suppress unwanted vibrations in mechanical structures or devices. The subclass includes designs where the spring provides elasticity while the damper dissipates kinetic energy, improving stability in applications such as vehicle suspensions, machinery mounts, and vibration isolation systems.
[F16F 15/023] using fluid means -- F16F 15/023 relates to methods or devices that suppress vibrations in springs by employing fluid means such as hydraulic, pneumatic or oil-filled chambers to damp oscillations. The fluid may be directed through passages, reservoirs or valves to absorb kinetic energy and reduce resonant motion.
[F16F 15/0232] with at least one gas spring  F16F15/027  takes precedence -- This classification entry, F16F 15/0232, pertains to the suppression of vibrations in mechanical systems using springs, specifically detailing the use of at least one gas spring. It is part of a broader category that includes various engineering elements and units under mechanical engineering.
[F16F 15/0235] where a rotating member is in contact with fluid  rotary viscous dampers  per se   F16F9/12 ; suppression of vibrations in rotating systems containing a fluid  F16F15/16 -- This subclass deals with rotary viscous dampers where a rotating element is in direct contact with a fluid, using fluid friction to absorb kinetic energy. It includes designs for suppressing vibrations in rotating systems that contain fluid, such as shafts, turbines or gears, by converting motion into heat within the fluid medium.
[F16F 15/0237] involving squeeze-film damping -- This subcategory covers spring-based mechanical elements that employ squeeze-film damping to reduce unwanted vibrations. It includes designs where a thin layer of fluid trapped between moving surfaces provides viscous resistance, thereby dissipating kinetic energy and stabilising the system's dynamic response.
[F16F 15/027] comprising control arrangements -- F16F 15/027 pertains to springs that incorporate control arrangements for vibration suppression, such as sensors, actuators, or feedback mechanisms that adjust the spring's behavior in response to dynamic loads. The classification applies to designs where the spring itself contributes to regulating system vibrations.
[F16F 15/0275] Control of stiffness -- The subclass F16F 15/0275 covers devices and methods that adjust the stiffness of springs or other elastic components to control vibration in mechanical systems. It includes active or passive mechanisms that vary stiffness in response to operating conditions, enabling precise tuning of dynamic behavior for vibration suppression.
[F16F 15/03] using electromagnetic means -- The subclass F16F 15/03 covers methods and devices that employ electromagnetic means to suppress or control vibrations in spring-based mechanical systems, such as electromagnetic dampers, actuators or active control elements that counteract oscillations. This includes the integration of coils, magnets or electronic feedback systems to modulate spring motion and reduce unwanted vibration.
[F16F 15/035] by use of eddy or induced-current damping  dynamo-electric brakes of the eddy-current type  H02K49/04 -- F16F 15/035 concerns the reduction of unwanted vibrations in mechanical springs and related components by employing eddy-current or induced-current damping methods. It includes devices such as dynamo-electric brakes that generate magnetic fields to dissipate kinetic energy, providing a non-contact means of vibration control.
[F16F 15/04] using elastic means ; -- The subclass F16F 15/04 covers techniques that reduce or control unwanted vibrations by employing elastic components such as springs, elastomers or other flexible elements, which absorb and dampen oscillatory motion in mechanical systems. This includes designs where the elastic means are integral to the system's vibration-suppression strategy.
[F16F 15/043] acting on a cam follower -- F16F 15/043 covers devices or measures that act on a cam follower to control or damp its motion, typically by applying forces that reduce vibration or oscillation in the follower-driven mechanism. This includes spring-based, hydraulic or magnetic elements integrated with the cam-follower assembly to improve stability and performance.
[F16F 15/046] using combinations of springs of different kinds  F16F15/085  takes precedence -- F16F 15/046 is a Cooperative Patent Classification entry for mechanical engineering that deals with engineering elements and units, specifically springs used to suppress vibrations in systems. It addresses methods that employ combinations of springs of different kinds to achieve vibration damping, and it is superseded by the more specific entry F16F 15/085.
[F16F 1/505] Rotational shear -- Rotational shear covers springs that develop a resisting torque through shear deformation of a component, such as torsion bars, twisted leaf elements or other members that resist rotation about an axis. The classification applies to devices where shear stress generated by twisting provides the spring action.
[F16F 15/06] with metal springs -- This subclass covers devices that employ metal springs to suppress vibrations in mechanical systems, encompassing designs where the spring material is metallic and is used as the primary means of damping oscillatory motion.
[F16F 15/063] with bars or tubes used as torsional elements -- F16F 15/063 covers spring designs that employ bars or tubular components as torsional elements to absorb and dissipate vibrational energy. These torsion-type springs are used in mechanical systems where controlling oscillations improves performance, stability, or durability. It falls under the broader categories of engineering elements and vibration-suppression springs.
[F16F 15/067] using only wound springs -- F16F 15/067 pertains to mechanical arrangements that achieve vibration suppression solely with wound (coil) springs, relying on the spring's elastic energy to absorb and release oscillatory forces without additional dampers or absorbers. The classification is used for inventions that use only wound springs to control vibrations in structures or machinery.
[F16F 15/073] using only leaf springs -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16F 15/073 pertains to the suppression of vibrations in mechanical systems, specifically focusing on methods and devices that utilize only leaf springs for this purpose. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with engineering elements and units, particularly springs.
[F16F 15/08] with rubber springs -- The subclass F16F 15/08 covers devices that employ rubber springs to suppress or control vibrations in mechanical systems, such as mounts, isolators or dampers. It includes arrangements where the elastic element is made wholly or partly of rubber, either alone or combined with other spring types, to reduce oscillatory motion.
[F16F 15/085] Use of both rubber and metal springs -- F16F 15/085 covers hybrid spring assemblies that combine rubber elements with metal springs to provide both stiffness and damping for vibration suppression. The technology is used in mechanical systems where conventional metal springs alone are insufficient to control oscillations, leveraging the elastic properties of rubber to absorb energy while the metal component bears load.
[F16F 15/10] Suppression of vibrations in rotating systems by making use of members moving with the system -- This CPC subclass covers devices that reduce vibration in rotating machinery by employing auxiliary components that move in sync with the system, such as dynamically balanced masses, moving pads, or spring-mounted elements. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and focuses on vibration suppression techniques that use moving members attached to the rotor or shaft.
[F16F 15/12] using elastic members or friction-damping members, e.g. between a rotating shaft and a gyratory mass mounted thereon -- This subclass covers methods that employ springs or friction-damping members placed between a rotating shaft and an attached gyratory mass to reduce vibrations, using elastic elements or friction devices to absorb or dissipate oscillatory energy.
[F16F 15/1201] for damping of axial or radial, i.e. non-torsional vibrations  F16F15/13107  takes precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16F 15/1201 pertains to mechanical engineering, specifically addressing springs and the suppression of vibrations in systems. It focuses on damping axial or radial, non-torsional vibrations, with a note that classification F16F15/13107 takes precedence for more specific cases.
[F16F 15/1202] the damping action being at least partially controlled by centrifugal masses  F16F15/13128  takes precedence -- The entry covers spring designs where vibration damping is achieved in part by using rotating or centrifugal masses that adjust the damping force. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and suppression of vibrations. The code F16F 15/1202 supersedes F16F15/13128.
[F16F 15/1203] characterised by manufacturing, e.g. assembling or testing procedures for the damper units  F16F15/13142  takes precedence -- F16F 15/1203 designates inventions whose novelty lies in the manufacturing processes of damper units used to suppress vibrations, including specific assembly steps, integration techniques, or testing procedures. It falls under mechanical engineering, springs, and vibration suppression. The subclass is superseded by the more specific F16F15/13142.
[F16F 15/1204] with a kinematic mechanism or gear system  F16F15/1202 ,  F16F15/13157  take precedence -- F16F 15/1204 covers spring devices that incorporate a kinematic mechanism or gear system to suppress vibrations in mechanical systems. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and the subcategory of vibration suppression.
[F16F 15/1205] with a kinematic mechanism, i.e. linkages, levers -- This CPC subclass concerns systems that suppress vibrations using kinematic mechanisms such as linkages or levers. It is nested under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and addresses vibration damping in spring-based devices.
[F16F 15/1206] with a planetary gear system -- F16F 15/1206 covers spring devices that incorporate a planetary gear mechanism to adjust or control the spring action for vibration suppression in mechanical systems. The classification belongs to the mechanical engineering field, under engineering elements and units for springs and vibration mitigation.
[F16F 15/1207] characterised by the supporting arrangement of the damper unit  F16F15/1238 ,  F16F15/13164  take precedence -- F16F 15/1207 falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and suppression of vibrations in systems. It covers designs where the damper unit is supported by a specific arrangement, such as brackets or frames, to improve vibration isolation. The classification also notes that related arrangements F16F15/1238 and F16F15/13164 take precedence.
[F16F 15/1208] Bearing arrangements -- F16F 15/1208 covers bearing arrangements designed to suppress vibrations in mechanical systems. It includes configurations of springs, dampers and other elements integrated with bearings to reduce oscillations and improve stability in machines.
[F16F 15/1209] comprising sliding bearings -- F16F 15/1209 classifies devices that use sliding bearings as part of vibration-suppression systems for springs and other mechanical elements. It covers arrangements where relative motion is guided by low-friction surfaces to damp oscillations in machinery.
[F16F 15/121] using springs as elastic members, e.g. metallic springs -- F16F 15/121 covers the use of springs as elastic members, such as metallic springs, in mechanical systems to suppress vibration. It is situated within the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and suppression of vibrations in systems.
[F16F 15/1211] C-shaped springs -- C-shaped springs are a type of curved spring with a C-shaped geometry that provides flexible axial or lateral compliance while fitting within limited space. They are used in mechanical engineering to store energy, absorb shocks and help suppress vibrations in various systems, often as part of suspension or mounting assemblies.
[F16F 15/1212] disposed around axis of rotation -- This subclass concerns spring-based vibration-damping devices that are arranged radially around a rotating shaft or other axis of rotation to reduce oscillations in mechanical systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, specifically springs used for suppression of vibrations in systems.
[F16F 15/1213] Spiral springs, e.g. lying in one plane, around axis of rotation -- F16F 15/1213 covers spiral springs that lie in a single plane and are oriented around a rotational axis, used to store and release energy and to suppress vibrations in mechanical systems. These springs are an engineering element within the broader category of mechanical engineering and spring design.
[F16F 15/1214] Folded springs, i.e. made of band-like material folded in an enclosing space -- F16F 15/1214 refers to folded springs made from band-like material that is folded into an enclosing space to provide spring action. These components are used in mechanical engineering to store energy, absorb shocks, or suppress vibrations in systems. The classification covers their design, construction, and application.
[F16F 15/1215] Leaf springs, e.g. radially extending -- F16F 15/1215 pertains to leaf springs that are radially extending, used as vibration-suppression elements in mechanical engineering systems.
[F16F 15/1216] Torsional springs, e.g. torsion bar or torsionally-loaded coil springs -- The CPC code F16F 15/1216 designates torsional springs such as torsion bars or torsionally-loaded coil springs, used to store and release rotational energy and to control vibrations in mechanical systems. These devices belong to the spring subgroup focused on vibration suppression within mechanical engineering.
[F16F 15/1217] Motion-limiting means, e.g. means for locking the spring unit in pre-defined positions  F16F15/1202 ,  F16F15/1338  take precedence -- This subclass covers devices that restrict or lock the movement of a spring assembly to specific positions, such as latches, catches, or detents that prevent further travel. It is used to control the motion range of springs in mechanical systems, improving vibration suppression and positioning accuracy.
[F16F 15/1218] by means of spring-loaded radially arranged locking means -- F16F 15/1218 covers vibration-damping solutions that employ springs equipped with radially arranged locking elements that are themselves spring-loaded. The design secures parts while permitting controlled movement, allowing the spring to absorb and dissipate vibratory energy in mechanical assemblies.
[F16F 15/1219] by means of spring-loaded axially arranged locking means -- This subclass covers methods and apparatuses that suppress vibrations using spring-loaded locking devices arranged along the axis of a component. The lock engages or releases by axial movement of a spring, providing controllable restraint in mechanical systems such as gearboxes, actuators or suspension elements.
[F16F 15/123] Wound springs -- Wound springs are tightly coiled, helical spring elements used in mechanical engineering to store and release energy, provide controlled force, and absorb shocks, thereby helping to suppress vibrations in a system. They are classified under the CPC code F16F 15/123 within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and springs.
[F16F 15/12306] Radially mounted springs -- Radially mounted springs are springs whose axis lies in a radial direction from a rotating shaft or hub, allowing them to absorb shock and suppress vibrations in mechanical systems such as gearboxes, turbines, or automotive components. They are installed in a circular arrangement around the axis and can be helical, disc or wave-type designs, providing axial or radial load support while damping unwanted oscillations.
[F16F 15/12313] characterised by the dimension or shape of spring-containing windows -- This subclass includes devices in which the vibration-damping function of a spring is achieved or adjusted by the dimensions or shape of the window that encloses the spring. The geometry of the spring-containing aperture is used to control stiffness, resonance or motion limits, typically in mechanical systems requiring vibration suppression.
[F16F 15/1232] characterised by the spring mounting  F16F15/12306 ,  F16F15/12313  take precedence -- The subclass F16F 15/1232 concerns systems that suppress vibrations by the way springs are mounted, building on the features defined in the preceding groups 15/12306 and 15/12313. It falls within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and addresses vibration-control through spring mounting arrangements.
[F16F 15/12326] End-caps for springs -- End-caps for springs are fitted to the terminal coils to limit expansion, protect against corrosion and mechanical damage, and improve vibration damping by restricting free movement of the spring ends. Their use enhances system stability, reduces noise and prolongs the service life of spring-based components.
[F16F 15/12333] having internal abutment means -- This subclass deals with springs that incorporate internal-abutment elements to control or reduce vibration in mechanical systems. It covers designs where internal stops or contacts are built into the spring to limit motion, dampen oscillations, or provide positional stability.
[F16F 15/1234] Additional guiding means for springs, e.g. for support along the body of springs that extend circumferentially over a significant length -- F16F 15/1234 covers additional guiding structures for springs, such as support elements that run along the length of a spring and extend circumferentially, intended to improve alignment and reduce vibration. These measures are used in mechanical engineering to control spring motion and suppress vibrations in systems.
[F16F 15/12346] Set of springs, e.g. springs within springs -- The entry relates to configurations where multiple springs are combined, such as a spring placed inside another spring, to provide progressive or tailored stiffness and to help suppress vibrations in mechanical systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and vibration-suppression.
[F16F 15/12353] Combinations of dampers, e.g. with multiple plates, multiple spring sets, i.e. complex configurations -- This code belongs to mechanical engineering, under engineering elements and units, springs, and the suppression of vibrations in systems, and it covers complex configurations that combine dampers with multiple plates or multiple spring sets. It includes hybrid spring-damper assemblies such as multi-plate dampers designed to improve vibration attenuation.
[F16F 15/1236] resulting in a staged spring characteristic, e.g. with multiple intermediate plates -- The code F16F 15/1236 designates spring designs that produce a staged force-deflection behavior, often achieved by inserting several intermediate plates between the coil or leaf elements. This arrangement allows the spring to exhibit multiple stiffness levels within one travel, providing tailored vibration suppression in mechanical systems.
[F16F 15/12366] acting on multiple sets of springs -- It refers to mechanisms that simultaneously engage more than one spring group to control motion, typically for vibration isolation or energy storage. It covers devices that apply forces to several spring assemblies in a coordinated manner, such as multi-stage suspension or tuning systems.
[F16F 15/12373] the sets of springs being arranged at substantially the same radius -- This subclass covers designs in which a group of springs is positioned at essentially the same radius to provide balanced support and aid in suppressing vibrations within mechanical systems, particularly in engineering elements and units that require precise vibration control.
[F16F 15/1238] with pre-damper, i.e. additional set of springs between flange of main damper and hub -- F16F 15/1238 classifies spring assemblies that include a pre-damper, i.e. an additional set of springs placed between the flange of the main damper and the hub, to enhance vibration suppression in mechanical engineering systems.
[F16F 15/12386] Pre-damper cage construction -- F16F 15/12386 designates a pre-damper cage construction incorporated into spring assemblies to suppress vibrations in mechanical systems. The cage is placed ahead of the main spring element, providing damping that reduces resonant oscillations and enhances the stability and durability of the equipment.
[F16F 15/12393] pre-damper springs are of non-wound type, e.g. leaf springs -- The entry covers pre-damper springs that are not wound, such as leaf springs, used to suppress vibrations in mechanical systems. It falls under the CPC subclass for springs and vibration control within mechanical engineering.
[F16F 15/124] Elastomeric springs -- Elastomeric springs are flexible components made from rubber-like materials that store and release energy, providing compliant support and damping in mechanical systems. They are used to reduce vibration and shock, offering low friction and adjustable stiffness for a wide range of applications, from vehicle suspensions to precision equipment.
[F16F 15/1245] Elastic elements arranged between substantially-radial walls of two parts rotatable with respect to each other, e.g. between engaging teeth -- This classification covers elastic elements positioned between substantially-radial walls of two parts that rotate relative to each other, such as springs placed between engaging teeth to provide flexibility, damping or load-bearing capacity while allowing the parts to turn independently.
[F16F 15/126] consisting of at least one annular element surrounding the axis of rotation -- This subclass covers spring arrangements that include one or more ring-shaped elements positioned around the rotational axis to absorb and reduce vibrations in rotating machinery. The annular component acts as a damping element, improving stability and prolonging component life.
[F16F 15/127] using plastics springs combined with other types of springs -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16F 15/127 pertains to the suppression of vibrations in mechanical systems, specifically focusing on the use of plastic springs in combination with other spring types. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with engineering elements and units, particularly springs.
[F16F 15/129] characterised by friction-damping means -- This CPC subclass (F16F 15/129) pertains to springs that incorporate friction-damping means to suppress vibrations in mechanical systems. It covers spring designs where friction surfaces or elements are integrated to dissipate kinetic energy, providing combined load-bearing and vibration-reduction functions.
[F16F 15/1292] characterised by arrangements for axially clamping or positioning or otherwise influencing the frictional plates -- The subclass F16F 15/1292 relates to spring-based vibration-suppression systems in which friction plates are axially clamped, positioned or otherwise acted upon to influence their movement and control vibration within mechanical assemblies. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and the suppression of vibrations in systems.
[F16F 15/1295] characterised by means for interconnecting driven plates and retainer, cover plates -- The subclass F16F 15/1295 defines spring-type vibration-suppression devices that use interconnecting driven plates together with a retainer and cover plates to link the plates and control motion, providing a means to damp oscillations in mechanical systems.
[F16F 15/1297] Overload protection, i.e. means for limiting torque -- The F16F 15/1297 subclass covers overload-protection devices that limit torque in mechanical systems, often employing springs or related elements to detect excessive load and intervene before damage occurs. These solutions are used to safeguard gears, shafts or other rotating components by disengaging, slipping or otherwise reducing transmitted torque when a preset threshold is exceeded, thereby contributing to vibration suppression and system reliability.
[F16F 15/131] the rotating system comprising two or more gyratory masses -- A rotating system that incorporates two or more gyratory masses is designed to counteract or dampen unwanted vibration by using the inertial forces of the masses to balance dynamic loads. Such arrangements are employed in engines, turbines and other mechanical devices where precise vibration control improves performance and longevity.
[F16F 15/13107] for damping of axial or radial, i.e. non-torsional vibrations -- This subclass covers devices or methods that provide damping of axial or radial (non-torsional) vibrations in springs, aiming to suppress unwanted motion in mechanical systems. It includes arrangements that absorb or dissipate energy along the length or perpendicular to the axis of a spring element.
[F16F 15/13114] characterised by modifications for auxiliary purposes, e.g. provision of a timing mark -- The subclass F16F 15/13114 refers to spring arrangements designed to suppress vibrations that incorporate auxiliary modifications, such as a timing mark or other reference features, to aid operation, monitoring or control of the spring's performance. These added elements do not affect the primary vibration-damping function but provide secondary benefits for the system.
[F16F 15/13121] characterised by clutch arrangements, e.g. for activation; integrated with clutch members, e.g. pressure member -- F16F 15/13121 covers spring devices where vibration-suppression is achieved through clutch mechanisms, such as a clutch that activates the spring or is built into the spring assembly, often incorporating a pressure member as part of the clutch.
[F16F 15/13128] the damping action being at least partially controlled by centrifugal masses  flywheels characterised by means to vary the moment of inertia  F16F15/31 -- A device that suppresses vibration in a spring-based system by using centrifugal masses such as flywheels, where the damping effect is at least partly controlled by varying the flywheel's moment of inertia. This method falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and the sub-class for vibration suppression.
[F16F 15/13135] simple connection or disconnection of members at speed -- F16F 15/13135 refers to simple, fast-acting connections or disconnections of mechanical members, such as quick-release couplings and snap-fit devices, used in systems where rapid assembly, disassembly, or vibration suppression is required.
[F16F 15/13142] characterised by the method of assembly, production or treatment  F16F15/13114  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass F16F 15/13142, within mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → springs → suppression of vibrations in systems, designates springs whose distinguishing feature is the method of assembly, production or treatment, and it takes precedence over the broader subclass F16F 15/13114.
[F16F 15/1315] Multi-part primary or secondary masses, e.g. assembled from pieces of sheet steel -- The subclass deals with vibration-control devices that use a primary or secondary mass formed from several separate components, for example pieces of sheet steel assembled into a composite mass. Such multi-part masses are employed in springs or dampers to adjust resonance and improve vibration suppression in mechanical systems.
[F16F 15/13157] with a kinematic mechanism or gear system, e.g. planetary  F16F15/13128  takes precedence -- This subclass pertains to spring devices that incorporate a kinematic mechanism or gear system-such as planetary gears-to suppress vibrations in mechanical systems. [F16F 15/13178] comprising slide bearings -- Slide bearings are low-friction sliding elements that support moving components while providing damping of vibratory motion in mechanical systems, especially those involving springs. [F16F 15/13192] Thermal shielding -- This CPC subgroup covers thermal shielding applied to springs used for vibration suppression in mechanical systems. It includes designs, materials or configurations that protect the springs from heat or provide thermal insulation to preserve performance.
[F16F 15/133] using springs as elastic members, e.g. metallic springs -- F16F 15/133 covers the use of springs, typically metallic, as elastic members to dampen or suppress vibrations in mechanical systems. [F16F 15/1331] C-shaped springs -- C-shaped springs are a type of mechanical spring formed in a curved, C-like profile, used primarily to store and release energy while providing a compact, flexible element that can absorb and dampen vibrations in mechanical systems. They belong to the CPC subclass F16F 15/1331 under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs → Suppression of vibrations in systems.
[F16F 15/1332] disposed around axis of rotation -- The code F16F 15/1332 designates springs that are positioned concentrically around a rotational axis to provide torsional resistance and damp vibration in rotating mechanical systems.

[F16F 15/1335] Folded springs, i.e. made of band-like material folded in an enclosing space -- F16F 15/1335 relates to folded springs made from a band-like material that is arranged within an enclosed space to provide spring action.
