[F16F 15/1336] Leaf springs, e.g. radially extending -- The F16F 15/1336 subclass covers leaf springs that extend radially, a type of mechanical spring used to absorb shocks and suppress vibrations in vehicles or machinery. These springs consist of a series of thin, flexible plates arranged in a curved shape that flex outward from a central point, providing load support while allowing controlled radial deformation.
[F16F 15/1337] Torsional springs, e.g. torsion bar or torsionally-loaded coil springs -- Torsional springs are mechanical elements that store energy through twisting of a bar or coil, delivering restoring torque when the applied rotation is released. They include torsion bars and coil springs loaded in torsion, used to absorb shocks, maintain position, or provide constant force in automotive, machinery and other applications.
[F16F 15/1338] Motion-limiting means, e.g. means for locking the spring unit in pre-defined positions  F16F15/13128  takes precedence -- F16F 15/1338 covers devices that limit the motion of a spring, such as lock or detent mechanisms that hold the spring in predetermined positions to control vibration. It falls under mechanical engineering, springs, and vibration suppression, and is superseded by the more specific subclass F16F15/13128.
[F16F 15/134] Wound springs -- Wound springs are helically coiled metal elements designed to store and release energy, often used in mechanical systems to absorb shocks or counteract vibrations. In the CPC classification F16F 15/134 they are defined as springs that are pre-stressed or wound to provide specific damping characteristics for vibration suppression in machines and structures.
[F16F 15/13407] Radially mounted springs -- Radially mounted springs are elastic components arranged around a shaft or other cylindrical axis, providing radial force to support or bias rotating parts. In mechanical engineering they are used to control vibration, maintain alignment, and absorb shocks in systems such as turbines, gearboxes, and bearings. The classification covers designs where the spring's axis is perpendicular to the load direction and the spring is fixed to a radial housing.
[F16F 15/13415] characterised by the dimension or shape of spring-containing windows -- The CPC entry addresses mechanical-engineering solutions that reduce vibration by shaping or sizing the windows that house springs. It classifies devices where the dimensions or form of the spring-containing openings are deliberately designed to improve vibration suppression.
[F16F 15/13423] Disposition of material for damping or avoiding wear -- The subclass F16F 15/13423 covers ways of arranging or applying material within mechanical components to provide vibration damping or prevent wear, such as fillers, pads, coatings or inserts that absorb energy or reduce friction. It includes devices, methods and configurations for positioning the damping or wear-avoidance medium in springs or related elements.
[F16F 15/1343] characterised by the spring mounting  F16F15/13407 ,  F16F15/13415  take precedence -- The subclass F16F 15/1343 covers springs whose mounting configuration is defined to aid the suppression of vibrations in mechanical systems. It specifies that the particular mounting features of the spring take precedence over related sub-groups such as F16F 15/13407 and F16F 15/13415. This entry falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and springs.
[F16F 15/13438] End-caps for springs -- End-caps for springs are components attached to the terminal coils or ends of a spring to constrain its motion, provide mechanical support, and prevent over-extension. By securing the spring's ends, these caps help dampen unwanted oscillations and improve vibration suppression in mechanical systems.
[F16F 15/13446] having internal abutment means -- This subclass covers springs that incorporate internal abutment means, such as built-in stops or bearings, to limit deflection and provide controlled damping for vibration suppression in mechanical systems. It includes designs where the abutment is situated within the spring element to enhance stability and reduce resonant motion.
[F16F 15/13453] Additional guiding means for springs -- F16F 15/13453 covers devices that add guidance to springs in order to control their motion and reduce vibration in mechanical systems, such as rods, sleeves, or other guiding elements that keep the spring aligned during operation. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and suppression of vibrations in systems.
[F16F 15/13461] Set of springs, e.g. springs within springs -- F16F 15/13461 covers arrangements of multiple springs configured within one another to form a set that enhances vibration suppression in mechanical systems. The nested or concentric spring designs provide staged stiffness and damping, allowing compact integration in devices requiring precise control of oscillations.
[F16F 15/13469] Combinations of dampers, e.g. with multiple plates, multiple spring sets, i.e. complex configurations -- The CPC entry F16F 15/13469 describes complex vibration-control assemblies that combine dampers with multiple plates, spring sets or similar elements, enabling enhanced suppression of vibrations in mechanical systems. It focuses on integrated damping-spring configurations used in engineering applications where vibration reduction is critical.
[F16F 15/13476] resulting in a staged spring characteristic, e.g. with multiple intermediate plates -- The code designates springs engineered to deliver a staged force-deflection response, achieved by inserting one or more intermediate plates that alter the stiffness at specific load points. Such staged-characteristic springs are employed to fine-tune vibration suppression in mechanical systems.
[F16F 15/13484] acting on multiple sets of springs -- The CPC entry F16F 15/13484 concerns devices that act on several spring assemblies simultaneously to reduce or eliminate vibration in mechanical systems. It falls within the mechanical-engineering branch for engineering elements and units, specifically springs used for vibration suppression.
[F16F 15/13492] the sets of springs being arranged at substantially the same radius -- F16F 15/13492 covers configurations in which a group of springs is positioned so that each spring lies at substantially the same radial distance from a common centre. This arrangement is used to provide uniform stiffness and to help suppress vibrations in mechanical systems that employ spring assemblies.
[F16F 15/136] Plastics springs, e.g. made of rubber -- F16F 15/136 defines plastic springs, for example rubber springs, that provide flexible support and vibration damping in mechanical assemblies. They fall under the spring subgroup for vibration suppression within engineering elements and units of mechanical engineering.
[F16F 15/137] the elastic members consisting of two or more springs of different kinds, -- F16F 15/137 refers to elastic members that combine two or more springs of different kinds into a single assembly. Such composite spring arrangements are employed in mechanical engineering to tailor stiffness and damping characteristics for the suppression of vibrations in various systems.
[F16F 15/139] characterised by friction-damping means -- F16F 15/139 covers springs that include friction-damping devices to reduce vibration. The classification applies to mechanical elements where the spring itself incorporates frictional elements, such as pads or surfaces, to dissipate energy and stabilize motion.
[F16F 15/1392] characterised by arrangements for axially clamping or positioning or otherwise influencing the frictional plates -- This CPC subclass covers vibration-damping devices that use springs and friction plates, where the design includes features that clamp or position the plates along the axis or otherwise modify their interaction. Such arrangements control the normal load on the plates, allowing precise adjustment of friction-based energy dissipation in mechanical systems.
[F16F 15/1395] characterised by main friction means acting radially outside the circumferential lines of action of the elastic members -- This CPC subclass covers spring assemblies where friction components are positioned radially beyond the elastic elements' lines of action, providing damping to reduce vibration. It is applied in mechanical engineering for vibration suppression in systems.
[F16F 15/1397] Overload protection, i.e. means for limiting torque -- The entry covers devices that protect mechanical systems from overload by limiting the transmitted torque, often using spring-based mechanisms that absorb excess energy. Such overload protection helps prevent damage and contributes to vibration suppression in engines, gearboxes and other rotating equipment.
[F16F 15/14] using masses freely rotating with the system, -- The entry concerns vibration-suppression techniques that employ inertial masses mounted so they can rotate freely with respect to the main structure, providing dynamic balancing and energy dissipation. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and specifically addresses using freely rotating masses to reduce oscillations in systems.
[F16F 15/1407] the rotation being limited with respect to the driving means -- The entry covers devices or methods that restrict the rotation of a spring or similar element relative to its driving mechanism in order to control or suppress vibration within a system. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units, springs, and the sub-category of vibration suppression. This limitation of rotational movement helps improve stability and performance of driven machinery.
[F16F 15/1414] Masses driven by elastic elements  F16F15/145 ,  F16F15/1464  take precedence -- The class covers devices in which one or more masses are set into motion by elastic elements to absorb or counteract vibration energy within mechanical systems. It includes arrangements where the elastic element drives a mass to produce a dynamic reaction that reduces oscillations. Related more specific subclasses F16F15/145 and F16F15/1464 take precedence over this broader category.
[F16F 15/1421] Metallic springs, e.g. coil or spiral springs -- F16F 15/1421 covers metallic springs such as coil or spiral springs that provide elastic force in mechanical systems, particularly for suppressing vibrations. These springs are used in a wide range of applications to absorb shock, maintain positioning, and improve dynamic stability.
[F16F 15/1428] with a single mass -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16F 15/1428 pertains to the suppression of vibrations in mechanical systems using springs, specifically addressing configurations that employ a single mass. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with engineering elements and units within mechanical engineering.
[F16F 15/1435] Elastomeric springs, i.e. made of plastic or rubber -- Elastomeric springs are flexible elements made from rubber or other plastic materials that store and release energy through elastic deformation. They are used to absorb shocks, isolate vibrations, and provide biasing forces in mechanical systems, offering advantages such as low weight, corrosion resistance, and tunable stiffness.
[F16F 15/1442] with a single mass -- The CPC subclass F16F 15/1442 covers spring systems designed to suppress vibrations by employing a single attached mass. It falls under mechanical engineering elements for springs that provide vibration damping through the dynamic interaction of the spring with that lone mass. This approach is used to isolate or reduce oscillations in mechanical structures.
[F16F 15/145] Masses mounted with play with respect to driving means thus enabling free movement over a limited range -- F16F 15/145 covers arrangements where a mass is attached to a machine with intentional clearance so it can move freely within a limited range relative to the driving components. This play allows the mass to absorb or counteract vibrations, enhancing dynamic stability of the system. Such configurations are typical in vibration dampers and spring-based isolation devices.
[F16F 15/1457] Systems with a single mass -- F16F 15/1457 covers designs and configurations of vibration-control systems that contain only one oscillating mass, typically involving a single spring-mass-damper arrangement used to attenuate resonances in mechanical structures.
[F16F 15/1464] Masses connected to driveline by a kinematic mechanism or gear system  F16F15/145  takes precedence -- The code designates devices that attach balancing masses to a driveline through a kinematic mechanism such as a gear train, aiming to counteract torsional or other vibration in mechanical systems. It covers arrangements where the masses are linked to the power-transmission line rather than directly mounted, enabling vibration suppression.
[F16F 15/1471] with a kinematic mechanism, i.e. linkages, levers -- The subclass F16F 15/1471 concerns springs used to suppress vibrations in mechanical systems by employing kinematic mechanisms such as linkages or levers, allowing controlled motion and energy dissipation.
[F16F 15/1478] with a planetary gear system -- The code F16F 15/1478 covers spring arrangements that incorporate a planetary gear system to suppress vibrations in mechanical assemblies. It applies to designs where the gearing works with the spring to absorb or control vibratory motion in a device.
[F16F 15/1485] the rotation being unlimited with respect to driving means  with a fluid connection between inertia member and rotating driving means  F16F15/167 -- The F16F 15/1485 subclass covers mechanisms in which the driven inertia element can rotate without limit relative to the driving source, using a fluid connection to transmit torque between them. It relates to vibration-suppression systems where unrestricted rotational motion is achieved through hydraulic or pneumatic coupling.
[F16F 15/1492] with a dry-friction connection -- F16F 15/1492 classifies mechanical spring assemblies that damp vibrations by linking the spring to other parts through a dry-friction connection rather than using lubricated or bonded joints. It addresses designs where friction without lubrication provides the required transmission of forces while reducing oscillatory motion.
[F16F 15/16] using a fluid -- F16F 15/16 addresses the use of a fluid to dampen or suppress vibrations in springs or spring-based mechanical assemblies, encompassing devices and methods that employ liquids or gases as vibration-absorbing media.
[F16F 15/161] characterised by the fluid damping devices, e.g. passages, orifices  F16F15/162  takes precedence -- F16F 15/161 pertains to spring constructions that use fluid-damping components, such as channels or orifices, to absorb vibrational energy. These designs belong to the suppression-of-vibrations subgroup of mechanical engineering spring elements.
[F16F 15/162] with forced fluid circulation -- F16F 15/162 classifies spring systems designed to suppress vibrations by circulating a fluid under pressure through the spring or its surrounding structure. The forced fluid flow provides active damping, enhancing the spring's ability to absorb and dissipate vibratory energy. This sub-group falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and vibration suppression.
[F16F 15/163] fluid acting as a lubricant -- This CPC subclass covers systems in which a fluid is employed as a lubricant to dampen vibrations in springs or spring-based mechanisms. It includes methods and apparatus that use lubricating fluids to reduce oscillations, improve stability and extend the service life of spring elements.
[F16F 15/164] characterised by manufacturing, e.g. assembling or testing procedures -- F16F 15/164 covers the suppression of vibrations in mechanical systems where the distinguishing feature lies in the manufacturing stage, such as specific assembly methods, testing procedures, or other production techniques used to realise the vibration-damping function. It is applied to inventions that address how the damping device or component is built rather than the physical principle of vibration control itself.
[F16F 15/165] Sealing arrangements -- F16F 15/165 covers sealing arrangements for springs used in vibration-suppression systems, detailing designs that prevent fluid or gas leakage, protect against contamination and maintain spring performance under dynamic loads. The classification includes gaskets, labyrinth seals, and other methods that integrate sealing functions with the mechanical behaviour of spring assemblies.
[F16F 15/167] having an inertia member, e.g. ring -- The CPC subclass F16F 15/167 covers devices that suppress vibrations in mechanical systems by incorporating an inertia member, such as a ring, into a spring or similar element. The inertia member adds mass to counteract oscillatory motion, improving damping performance.
[F16F 15/173] provided within a closed housing -- The F16F 15/173 subclass covers springs that are incorporated inside a sealed or closed housing and used to damp or suppress vibrations in mechanical systems, typically as part of engineered vibration-control devices. This arrangement protects the spring from external contaminants while allowing it to absorb kinetic energy and reduce oscillations.
[F16F 15/18] using electric, means -- F16F 15/18 relates to devices that suppress vibrations in mechanical systems by using electric means such as actuators, sensors, or control circuits. The classification includes methods and apparatuses that employ electrical energy to actively dampen oscillations in springs or other elastic components.
[F16F 1/52] loaded in combined stresses -- F16F 1/52 classifies springs that are subjected to more than one type of load simultaneously, such as axial, torsional, radial or bending stresses combined in operation. It covers design, analysis and testing of springs where the interaction of these stresses influences performance, durability or safety. This includes devices that experience mixed loading conditions during use.
[F16F 15/20] Suppression of vibrations of rotating systems by favourable grouping or relative arrangements of the moving members of the system or systems -- F16F 15/20 addresses techniques for reducing vibration in rotating machinery by arranging the moving parts so that their motions counteract each other. It includes designs that group or phase rotating members to create favorable dynamic interactions that suppress undesired oscillations in the system.
[F16F 15/22] Compensation of inertia forces -- This class covers methods and devices that counteract inertial forces in vibrating mechanical systems, typically using springs or other elastic elements to balance dynamic loads. It includes designs that reduce or eliminate unwanted motion by compensating for mass-induced forces, often in precision machinery or vehicle suspensions.
[F16F 15/223] Use of systems involving rotary unbalanced masses where the phase-angle of masses mounted on counter-rotating shafts can be varied  generation of mechanical vibrations  per se  with such systems  B06B1/166 -- F16F 15/223 addresses devices that deliberately create mechanical vibrations by employing rotating unbalanced masses whose relative phase angles can be varied, often using counter-rotating shafts to fine-tune vibration amplitude and frequency for testing, balancing or active control.
[F16F 15/226] in star engine arrangements -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16F 15/226 pertains to the suppression of vibrations in mechanical systems, specifically addressing techniques and devices used in star engine arrangements. This classification is part of a broader category that includes various engineering elements and units related to springs and vibration control mechanisms.
[F16F 15/24] of crankshaft systems by particular disposition of cranks, pistons, or the like -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16F 15/24 pertains to methods for suppressing vibrations in crankshaft systems through strategic placement of components such as cranks and pistons. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with mechanical engineering elements and units, specifically focusing on springs and vibration suppression techniques.
[F16F 15/26] of crankshaft systems using solid masses, other than the ordinary pistons, moving with the system -- F16F 15/26 relates to methods of reducing vibration in crankshaft assemblies by attaching solid masses that move with the rotating system, rather than using conventional pistons. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the springs sub-category for vibration suppression.
[F16F 15/261] where masses move linearly -- F16F 15/261 covers devices that suppress vibrations in mechanical systems by using linearly moving masses, such as linear motion dampers or tuned mass absorbers that slide or translate to counteract oscillations. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and the subcategory of vibration suppression.
[F16F 15/262] Masses attached to pinions, camshafts or driving shafts for auxiliary equipment, e.g. for an oil pump -- The subclass F16F 15/262 covers the use of inertial masses mounted on pinions, camshafts or drive shafts to power auxiliary devices such as oil-pump, providing a simple mechanical means of transmitting torque and dampening vibration in the system.
[F16F 15/264] Rotating balancer shafts  F16F15/262  takes precedence -- Rotating balancer shafts are components fitted to rotating machinery to counteract unbalanced forces and reduce vibration by adding adjustable masses that generate opposing inertial forces. They fall under CPC code F16F 15/264, a subcategory of springs for suppression of vibrations in systems.
[F16F 15/265] Arrangement of two or more balancer shafts  F16F15/267  takes precedence -- F16F 15/265 is a CPC subclass that deals with the design and relative positioning of two or more balancer shafts used in rotating machinery to counteract vibration, covering aspects such as shaft geometry, coupling, and phasing. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs → Suppression of vibrations in systems, and is superseded by the more specific subclass F16F 15/267.
[F16F 15/267] characterised by bearing support of balancer shafts; Lubrication arrangements -- F16F 15/267 is a CPC subclass within mechanical engineering that deals with spring devices aimed at suppressing vibrations, specifically those that use bearing-supported balancer shafts and include dedicated lubrication arrangements for the bearings.
[F16F 15/268] Hollow shafts -- F16F 15/268 covers hollow shafts used as spring elements to absorb and dissipate vibrational energy in mechanical systems. The classification deals with design features such as internal cavities, material selection and geometry that enable the shaft to function as a spring while reducing weight and improving dynamic performance.
[F16F 15/28] Counterweights, -- Counterweights are mass elements that are attached to or integrated with spring-based systems to offset dynamic forces and reduce oscillations, thereby improving the stability and performance of mechanical equipment. In the CPC hierarchy they fall under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs → Suppression of vibrations in systems, code F16F 15/28.
[F16F 15/283] for engine crankshafts -- The code F16F 15/283 covers mechanisms that employ springs to dampen or suppress vibrations in engine crankshafts, typically through dynamic balancing or shock-absorbing features integrated into the crankshaft assembly. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and the broader topic of vibration suppression in rotating machinery.
[F16F 15/286] Adjustable weights -- F16F 15/286 covers adjustable weights employed in spring mechanisms to fine-tune or suppress vibration in mechanical systems. The invention provides movable masses that can be repositioned to alter the dynamic response of a spring, enabling precise control of vibration characteristics. This classification lies under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and the suppression of vibrations in systems.
[F16F 15/30] Flywheels -- F16F 15/30 refers to flywheels, rotating masses that store kinetic energy and help smooth out speed variations in mechanical systems, thereby suppressing vibrations. They are employed in engines, generators, and other devices where stabilising rotational speed is required.
[F16F 15/302] comprising arrangements for cooling or thermal insulation -- F16F 15/302 covers spring assemblies that integrate cooling or thermal-insulation features, allowing the spring to operate at controlled temperatures while mitigating vibration. The classification falls under Mechanical Engineering, Engineering Elements and Units, Springs, specifically addressing suppression of vibrations in systems. It includes designs where cooling channels, heat-sink elements, or insulating materials are built into the spring structure.
[F16F 15/305] made of plastics, e.g. fibre reinforced plastics [FRP] -- F16F 15/305 classifies springs and vibration-damping elements that are made from plastics, including fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) composites. It covers designs where the plastic material provides the elastic or damping function to suppress vibrations in mechanical systems.
[F16F 15/31] characterised by means for varying the moment of inertia -- The F16F 15/31 subclass includes spring- or vibration-suppression devices that incorporate mechanisms for varying their moment of inertia, enabling adjustment of dynamic behaviour to better control vibration. Such means may involve movable masses, adjustable geometry or reconfigurable components that change inertia during operation.
[F16F 15/315] characterised by their supporting arrangement, e.g. mountings, cages, securing inertia member to shaft -- F16F 15/315 pertains to vibration-suppression devices in mechanical engineering that are defined by how the inertia element is supported, for example by mountings, cages or other fixtures that secure the inertia member to a rotating shaft.
[F16F 15/3153] Securing inertia members to the shafts -- F16F 15/3153 pertains to methods and devices for attaching inertia members such as flywheels, balancers or dampers directly to rotating shafts, ensuring reliable transmission of torque and accurate positioning for vibration suppression. It includes clamping, keyed, splined or bonded solutions within the springs subclass.
[F16F 15/3156] Arrangement of the bearings -- F16F 15/3156 covers the specific configuration and placement of bearings in spring-based devices that dampen vibrations, detailing how bearing geometry and positioning contribute to vibration suppression.
[F16F 15/32] Correcting- or balancing-weights or equivalent means for balancing rotating bodies, e.g. vehicle wheels -- This class concerns devices such as correcting or balancing weights and equivalent means that are fitted to rotating bodies-particularly vehicle wheels-to counterbalance uneven mass distribution and diminish vibration, falling under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and vibration suppression.
[F16F 15/322] the rotating body being a shaft  F16F15/34 ,  F16F15/36  take precedence -- F16F 15/322 covers spring devices in which the rotating component is a shaft, used to suppress vibrations in mechanical systems. [F16F 15/324] the rotating body being a vehicle wheel  F16F15/36  takes precedence; tyre parts or constructions not otherwise provided for  B60C19/00 -- This classification code, F16F 15/324, pertains to systems that suppress vibrations in mechanical engineering, specifically focusing on springs used with rotating bodies such as vehicle wheels. It notes that when the rotating body is a vehicle wheel, the more specific code F16F15/36 takes precedence, and for tyre parts or constructions not covered elsewhere, B60C19/00 applies.
[F16F 15/326] specially adapted for attachment to spokes -- F16F 15/326 falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs and addresses springs specially adapted for attachment to spokes to suppress vibrations, typically used on wheels to damp oscillations and improve ride comfort.
[F16F 15/328] Multiple weights on adhesive strip -- F16F 15/328 covers devices that attach several masses to an adhesive strip to tune the dynamic response of a spring or vibration isolation system. This approach is used in mechanical engineering applications where compact, adjustable vibration control is required.

[F16F 15/36] operating automatically -- F16F 15/36 covers spring-based systems that suppress vibrations by operating automatically, using self-acting dampers, active control mechanisms or smart materials that respond to motion without external commands. It includes devices that detect vibration and adjust stiffness or damping in real time to maintain stability.
[F16F 15/363] using rolling bodies, e.g. balls free to move in a circumferential direction -- The code F16F 15/363 covers spring designs that incorporate rolling elements such as balls that can rotate around the spring's circumference to attenuate vibrations. This approach uses the kinetic motion of the rolling bodies to dissipate energy, improving vibration suppression in mechanical systems.
[F16F 15/366] using fluid or powder means, i.e. non-discrete material -- F16F 15/366 covers methods for damping vibrations in spring-based systems by employing fluid or powder media rather than solid, discrete components. The classification applies to designs where a liquid or particulate substance is introduced to absorb kinetic energy, thereby reducing oscillations and improving stability.
[F16F 1/54] loaded in compression and shear -- F16F 1/54 pertains to springs that are loaded simultaneously in compression and shear, describing elements designed to endure combined axial and transverse forces typical in mechanical systems requiring multidirectional resilience.

The classification groups these components under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and springs, linking them to automotive suspension applications such as those defined in B60G11/225.
[F16F 2222/00] Special physical effects, e.g. nature of damping effects -- F16F 2222/00 classifies springs whose performance is defined by special physical effects such as damping characteristics, including the way the spring dissipates energy or modifies vibration. It is used for patents describing materials, designs or mechanisms that produce specific damping behaviour in mechanical spring systems.
[F16F 2222/02] temperature-related -- This subclass addresses springs whose performance or design is affected by temperature, covering material expansion or contraction, thermal-dependent stiffness, and heat-resistant or thermally compensated configurations. It includes methods for managing damping or load-carry­ing changes that arise from heating or cooling.
[F16F 2222/025] Cooling -- The CPC subclass F16F 2222/025, titled Cooling, pertains to springs within mechanical engineering and addresses special physical effects such as the removal of heat or control of temperature to influence damping behavior. It includes designs and methods for dissipating thermal energy generated in springs during operation.
[F16F 2222/04] Friction -- F16F 2222/04 - Friction classifies patents that address the frictional behavior of springs, focusing on how surface contact, wear and energy dissipation affect spring performance and damping characteristics. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs → Special physical effects such as damping.
[F16F 2222/06] Magnetic or electromagnetic -- The subclass F16F 2222/06 deals with springs that employ magnetic or electromagnetic phenomena for actuation, sensing or damping, where magnetic forces supplement or replace the mechanical restoring force. It includes devices that use magnetic fields to control spring behaviour or to provide special physical effects such as magnetic damping, and is nested under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → springs → special physical effects.
[F16F 2222/08] Inertia -- The CPC class F16F 2222/08 covers spring designs that exploit or mitigate inertia, i.e., the effects of mass and acceleration on a spring's dynamic response. It includes mechanisms where inertial forces influence damping, vibration control, or energy storage in spring systems.
[F16F 2222/10] Adhesion -- F16F 2222/10 covers the adhesion properties of springs, relating to how springs stick to other surfaces or materials, affecting their performance and damping behavior. This subclass deals with special physical effects such as the nature of damping caused by adhesive forces within spring mechanisms.
[F16F 2222/12] Fluid damping -- F16F 2222/12 covers springs that incorporate fluid damping, where the motion of the spring is restrained by the flow of a liquid or gas through a restricted passage, providing resistance that depends on viscosity or pressure. This includes hydraulic or pneumatic dashpots integrated with springs to control oscillations and dissipate energy.
[F16F 2222/123] decreasing with increasing flow -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16F 2222/123 pertains to springs where the damping effect decreases as the flow increases, a specific characteristic within the broader category of special physical effects in mechanical engineering.
[F16F 2222/126] using gases -- The subclass F16F 2222/126 concerns spring devices in which gases are employed to produce damping or other special physical effects, such as pneumatic or gas-filled shock absorbers and gas-spring supports. It addresses the use of compressed, expanding or vented gases to control vibration, shock, or load-bearing characteristics of mechanical springs.
[F16F 2222/14] superconducting -- The subclass F16F 2222/14 deals with springs that incorporate superconducting materials or exploit superconductivity to produce special physical effects such as reduced energy loss, magnetic levitation or enhanced damping characteristics. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and the study of special damping effects.
[F16F 2224/00] Materials -- F16F 2224/00 covers the choice and treatment of materials used to make springs, including metal alloys, polymers, composites and other substances selected for specific elasticity, strength, fatigue-resistance or corrosion-resistance characteristics. It addresses how material properties affect spring performance and suitability for particular applications.
[F16F 2224/005] Combined materials of same basic nature but differing characteristics -- F16F 2224/005 covers springs made from two or more materials that share the same basic nature-such as different grades of steel, copper alloys or polymers-but have distinct mechanical or physical characteristics, allowing the component to exploit complementary properties within a single structure.
[F16F 2224/02] solids -- F16F 2224/02 classifies springs that are made from a solid piece of material rather than being wound or formed from separate elements. It includes springs produced by machining, casting, forging or similar processes where the spring element is a single, continuous solid.
[F16F 2224/0208] Alloys -- The CPC subclass F16F 2224/0208 pertains to the selection and use of alloy materials for springs, including the composition, heat-treatment and mechanical-property optimization of steel, non-ferrous and composite alloys that affect spring performance. It covers specifications for alloying elements, microstructure control and related manufacturing considerations within the broader category of mechanical-engineering spring elements.
[F16F 2224/0216] bimetallic -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F16F 2224/0216 pertains to bimetallic materials used in springs, falling under the broader categories of Mechanical Engineering, Engineering Elements and Units, and Springs. This classification is specific to the material composition of springs that utilize bimetallic properties for their function or performance.
[F16F 2224/0225] Cellular, e.g. microcellular foam -- The F16F 2224/0225 subclass concerns springs that are formed from cellular or microcellular foam materials, such as foamed polymers or metal foams, where the foam structure provides the elastic behavior. It includes designs that exploit the lightweight, compressible nature of foam cells to achieve spring action in mechanical applications.
[F16F 2224/0233] deforming plastically in operation -- F16F 2224/0233 covers springs that deform plastically while in service, meaning they permanently change shape under load rather than returning to their original form. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and relates to the material behavior of the spring during operation.
[F16F 2224/0241] Fibre-reinforced plastics [FRP] -- F16F 2224/0241 covers the use of fibre-reinforced plastics in spring elements, including design, manufacturing and performance of composite springs made from polymer matrices reinforced with glass, carbon or other fibers. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and specifies materials that combine high strength and elasticity for spring applications.
[F16F 2224/025] Elastomers -- F16F 2224/025 covers elastomeric materials employed in spring manufacture, including natural and synthetic rubbers or other polymeric compounds that provide flexibility, resilience and damping. The classification is nested under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and specifically addresses the material aspect of spring design.
[F16F 2224/0258] Shape-memory metals, e.g. Ni-Ti alloys -- The code F16F 2224/0258 covers springs made from shape-memory metals such as nickel-titanium alloys. It addresses the use of these materials to create spring elements that can recover their original shape after deformation, leveraging the alloys' temperature-induced phase transformation.
[F16F 2224/0266] porosity -- This subclass concerns the porosity of spring materials, describing how voids or pores influence properties such as stiffness, strength and fatigue life. It is applied in patents that address manufacturing processes, material selection and performance optimisation of springs.
[F16F 2224/0275] Ceramics -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16F 2224/0275, titled "Ceramics," is part of the broader category dealing with materials used in springs within mechanical engineering.
