[F16F 2224/0283] piezoelectric -- F16F 2224/0283 is a CPC subclass under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs → Materials and designates springs that use piezoelectric material, meaning they generate electric charge when mechanically deformed or change shape in response to an electric field.
[F16F 2224/0291] PTFE -- F16F 2224/0291 relates to springs that use polytetrafluoroethylene as a material. PTFE provides low friction, chemical inertness and temperature stability, making it suitable for spring components exposed to harsh environments. This code falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, materials.
[F16F 2224/04] Fluids -- F16F 2224/04 classifies technologies that involve fluids in connection with springs, such as lubricants, hydraulic or pneumatic media that modify spring behaviour, assist in manufacturing, or provide actuation.
[F16F 2224/041] Dilatant -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F16F 2224/041, under the category of Materials for springs, specifically refers to dilatant materials. These are substances that thicken or harden when subjected to stress or impact, enhancing their use in specialized spring applications.
[F16F 2224/043] electrorheological -- F16F 2224/043 relates to electrorheological materials used in springs. It covers substances whose rheological properties change under an electric field, enabling adjustable stiffness or damping in spring mechanisms. The code is part of the CPC group for spring materials within mechanical engineering.
[F16F 2224/045] magnetorheological -- This subclass covers springs made from magnetorheological materials whose rheological properties can be changed by applying a magnetic field, providing tunable stiffness and damping for adaptive vibration control and suspension systems.
[F16F 2224/046] pneumatic -- F16F 2224/046 designates pneumatic (air-filled) springs, a type of mechanical spring that uses compressed gas to store and release energy. The classification covers their construction, mounting, and operation in devices where variable stiffness or damping is required, such as vehicle suspensions and industrial shock absorbers.
[F16F 2224/048] High viscosity, semi-solid pastiness -- The CPC subclass F16F 2224/048 relates to springs formed from highly viscous or semi-solid paste materials, such as polymer gels or putties, where the material's flow resistance is a significant design factor. It addresses manufacturing, shaping, and performance aspects of such pasty-material springs.
[F16F 2226/00] Manufacturing -- The CPC subclass F16F 2226/00 pertains to the manufacturing of springs, covering processes such as forming, winding, heat-treating, surface finishing, and related apparatus used to produce mechanical spring elements. It includes both conventional and specialized techniques for shaping and treating spring steel or other elastic materials. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs.
[F16F 2226/02] Surface treatments -- F16F 2226/02 covers surface-treatments applied to manufactured springs, including coating, plating, anodizing and other modifications that enhance corrosion resistance, wear performance or fatigue life. It addresses processes used during spring production to improve functional durability.
[F16F 2226/023] by laser or similar treatment by rays -- F16F 2226/023 covers methods of manufacturing springs that employ laser or other ray-based treatments, such as laser cutting, welding, surface hardening, or material removal, to shape or modify the spring components. This subclass is used for processes where directed energy beams replace conventional mechanical or thermal steps in spring production.
[F16F 2226/026] low-friction -- F16F 2226/026 covers manufacturing techniques and design features that reduce friction in springs, including low-friction materials, surface treatments, lubrication methods and geometry that minimise contact resistance during operation. It is used to classify spring production processes aimed at improving efficiency and performance.
[F16F 2226/04] Assembly or fixing methods -- F16F 2226/04 covers methods for assembling or fixing springs to other components or structures, including clamping, welding, bonding and other mechanical fastening techniques used in spring manufacturing.
[F16F 2226/041] Clipping -- F16F 2226/041 covers clipping methods and devices used in the manufacture or assembly of springs, where a clip is applied to secure spring ends or to join springs to other components. The classification pertains to mechanical engineering elements that provide a simple, often reversible, fastening solution for spring systems.
[F16F 2226/042] Gluing -- F16F 2226/042 covers the use of adhesives to join springs or to attach springs to other components during manufacturing. It includes methods for applying, curing and securing glue on spring elements, as well as equipment specially designed for adhesive bonding of spring parts.
[F16F 2226/044] Snapping -- F16F 2226/044 covers snap-action mechanisms in which a spring or elastic element stores energy and releases it abruptly to produce a rapid movement or locking action. The classification includes devices that snap into place or out of position, such as snap-fasteners, latch springs, and quick-release couplings that rely on a sudden release of stored strain.
[F16F 2226/045] Press-fitting -- F16F 2226/045 covers the manufacturing and assembly of springs by press-fitting, where axial force is used to insert a spring into a housing, bore or other component to secure it without welding, adhesives or additional fasteners. The classification applies to processes, tools and equipment designed to achieve a tight interference fit for springs in mechanical-engineering applications.
[F16F 2226/047] Sheet-metal stamping -- F16F 2226/047 refers to sheet-metal stamping methods used in the production of springs, classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, manufacturing. It covers techniques for forming spring components from sheet metal through stamping operations, including die design, punch-blank interactions, and related tooling.
[F16F 2226/048] Welding -- F16F 2226/048 covers welding techniques used in the manufacturing of springs, including methods for joining coil, leaf or torsion springs and surface preparation to achieve reliable bonds. It addresses processes that preserve spring functionality and performance.
[F16F 2228/00] Functional characteristics, e.g. variability, frequency-dependence -- F16F 2228/00 relates to springs whose functional behavior changes with operating conditions, such as load, temperature, or excitation frequency, encompassing designs with variable stiffness, damping, or frequency-dependent response. The subclass covers features that allow the spring's performance to be tuned or to vary automatically during use.
[F16F 2228/001] Specific functional characteristics in numerical form or in the form of equations -- F16F 2228/001 relates to springs whose functional characteristics-such as variability or frequency-dependence-are expressed in numerical terms or through equations, defining performance analytically rather than qualitatively. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering elements that specify spring behavior in precise, mathematical form.
[F16F 2228/002] Temperature -- The class covers springs whose functional behaviour changes with temperature, such as variations in stiffness, damping or resonant frequency caused by heating or cooling. It includes designs and methods for compensating or exploiting these temperature-dependent characteristics in mechanical systems.
[F16F 2228/004] Force or pressure -- F16F 2228/004 relates to springs that are characterized principally by the force they produce or by the pressure they generate. It includes specifications of force-displacement curves, load-frequency dependence and other functional parameters that define how the spring behaves under varying loads.
[F16F 2228/005] Material properties, e.g. moduli -- F16F 2228/005 classifies patents concerning the material characteristics of springs, such as elastic, shear or tensile moduli, that influence their functional behaviour. It covers disclosures on how these properties vary with factors like temperature, frequency or loading conditions, affecting spring performance and design.
[F16F 2228/007] of solids, e.g. hardness -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16F 2228/007 pertains to the functional characteristics of springs, specifically focusing on properties of solids such as hardness. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office for indexing and searching patent documents related to mechanical engineering elements.
[F16F 2228/008] of semi-solids or fluids, e.g. viscosity -- The F16F 2228/008 subclass deals with springs whose functional characteristics depend on the viscosity of semi-solids or fluids incorporated in or surrounding the spring, such as fluid-filled, lubricated or gel-filled elements that affect stiffness, damping or frequency response.
[F16F 2228/04] Frequency effects -- F16F 2228/04 addresses the influence of frequency on spring behavior, covering how stiffness, damping and other functional characteristics change with vibration or oscillation rates. It is used to classify designs and analyses of springs that must perform reliably under dynamic or resonance conditions.
[F16F 2228/06] Stiffness -- F16F 2228/06 covers the stiffness of springs, focusing on how a spring's resistance to deformation varies with load, frequency, or other operating conditions. It includes specifications of variable stiffness, frequency-dependent behavior, and related functional characteristics.
[F16F 2228/063] Negative stiffness -- The F16F 2228/063 entry covers springs whose stiffness is effectively negative, meaning they expand when a compressive load is applied and contract under tension. It addresses functional characteristics such as variability and frequency-dependent behavior, used in mechanical systems that require adaptive or energy-absorbing performance.
[F16F 2228/066] Variable stiffness -- The F16F 2228/066 entry covers springs whose stiffness can be varied, either by intrinsic design features, material properties, or active control mechanisms, allowing their force-deflection behaviour to change with load, temperature, frequency or other operating conditions. It includes devices that adjust spring rigidity in real time or through preset adjustments, enabling adaptable performance in mechanical systems.
[F16F 2228/08] pre-stressed -- This subclass covers springs that are manufactured or treated to have an initial pre-stressed condition, meaning they are loaded in advance to modify stiffness or vibration behavior. Pre-stressed springs are employed to improve performance under varying loads or frequencies.
[F16F 2228/10] with threshold or dead zone -- The CPC subclass F16F 2228/10 pertains to springs that exhibit a threshold or dead-zone characteristic, where no restoring force is generated until deformation exceeds a specified limit. Such behavior is used to achieve controlled activation, vibration isolation or shock-absorbing functions by delaying force transmission until the threshold is reached.
[F16F 2228/12] degressive -- F16F 2228/12 classifies springs that exhibit a degressive force-deflection behavior, meaning the restoring force diminishes as the spring is extended or compressed. Such springs are employed where a decreasing stiffness is desired, for example in vibration isolation, load-compensating devices, or progressive-actuation mechanisms. The code falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, functional characteristics.
[F16F 2228/14] progressive -- The subclass F16F 2228/14 covers springs whose functional characteristics-such as stiffness, damping or resonant frequency-vary progressively with load, displacement, time or frequency, providing a deliberately changing response. It includes designs where the spring's properties are tailored along its length or through material or geometric variations to achieve adjustable performance.
[F16F 2230/00] Purpose -- F16F 2230/00 classifies the purpose or intended function of springs within mechanical engineering, covering how a spring is used to achieve a specific mechanical effect or performance goal. It applies to disclosures that define the role of a spring in a system, such as load support, vibration isolation, energy storage, or motion control.
[F16F 2230/0005] Attachment, e.g. to facilitate mounting onto confer adjustability -- F16F 2230/0005 covers devices that attach to springs to facilitate mounting and provide adjustability, such as brackets, clamps or fastening elements that allow the spring to be positioned, secured and its tension or position altered easily.
[F16F 2230/0011] Balancing, e.g. counterbalancing to produce static balance -- F16F 2230/0011 concerns methods and devices that balance or counterbalance a spring system to achieve static equilibrium, such as adding masses, auxiliary springs or other forces that offset the spring's own force. It covers designs that ensure the load remains steady without movement when the spring is at rest. This classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, specifically springs.
[F16F 2230/0017] Calibrating -- The F16F 2230/0017 subclass deals with procedures and devices used to calibrate springs, including measuring and adjusting their force-deflection behavior, verifying spring rate, preload and travel. It covers test rigs, sensors and control systems that ensure springs meet specified performance criteria.
[F16F 2230/0023] protective -- The F16F 2230/0023 subclass covers springs designed primarily to protect equipment or structures by absorbing shocks, vibrations or overloads. These protective springs are employed in mechanical systems to safeguard components from damage caused by transient forces.
[F16F 2230/0029] Location, co-location -- The F16F 2230/0029 subgroup covers patents that specify the position or arrangement of a spring relative to other parts of a device, including how multiple springs are co-located or integrated within a mechanical assembly. It addresses design aspects such as mounting points, spacing, and alignment that affect the spring's performance within the overall system.
[F16F 2230/0035] Gripping -- The F16F 2230/0035 subclass covers features or devices designed to grip, hold, or secure springs within mechanical assemblies. It includes mechanisms such as clamps, jaws, or fixtures that engage the spring's coil or ends to control its position or motion during operation.
[F16F 2230/0041] Locking -- F16F 2230/0041 classifies locking mechanisms applied to springs, covering designs that secure or retain spring elements within mechanical engineering. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs and pertains to features that prevent spring movement or release.
[F16F 2230/0047] Measuring, indicating -- F16F 2230/0047 covers devices or methods for measuring or indicating the performance of springs, such as load, deflection, or frequency. It includes sensors, gauges and display mechanisms integrated with spring assemblies for monitoring their behavior during operation.
[F16F 2230/0052] Physically guiding or influencing -- F16F 2230/0052 covers arrangements that physically guide or influence the behavior of springs, such as guide rods, sleeves, or supporting structures that control spring alignment, motion, or compression within mechanical assemblies.
[F16F 2230/0058] using inserts or exterior elements, e.g. to affect stiffness -- F16F 2230/0058 pertains to spring designs in which inserts or external components are incorporated to modify the spring's stiffness or mechanical response. It covers the use of supplemental elements attached to or placed within a spring to achieve desired performance characteristics.
[F16F 2230/0064] using a cam -- F16F 2230/0064 covers inventions that use a cam to act on a spring, for example to compress, tension, or release the spring as part of a mechanical system. The subclass belongs to the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs.
[F16F 2230/007] with, or used as an end stop or buffer -- A spring classified under F16F 2230/007 is designed or employed to act as an end stop or buffer, limiting motion and absorbing impacts in mechanical assemblies. It typically provides a controlled stopping point or cushioning effect for moving parts, preventing damage or excess travel.
[F16F 2230/0076] Pivoting -- F16F 2230/0076 covers springs that incorporate a pivoting feature, allowing the spring element to rotate about a fixed axis while delivering force or torque. This classification includes designs such as pivoting leaf springs, torsional coil springs, and similar mechanisms used in hinges, steering links, and other components where controlled angular motion is required.
[F16F 2230/0082] Dimensional tolerances, e.g. play between mechanical elements -- The CPC subclass F16F 2230/0082 deals with specifications of dimensional tolerances, such as allowable clearance or play between moving mechanical parts, especially in spring assemblies. It addresses how these tolerances are defined, measured and applied to ensure proper function and reliability of spring-based mechanisms.
[F16F 2230/0088] Timing -- The CPC entry F16F 2230/0088 covers timing aspects of spring-powered mechanisms, including methods and devices that control the release or actuation of a spring to achieve a desired sequence or interval. It pertains to the synchronization and regulation of spring action in mechanical systems such as clocks, engines, and dispensing devices.
[F16F 2230/0094] Magnifying a physical effect -- The CPC subclass F16F 2230/0094 covers spring-based mechanisms that amplify or otherwise magnify a physical effect, such as force, displacement, or energy, by means of the spring's deformation. It includes designs where the spring's stored energy is harnessed to increase the magnitude of the output compared with the input. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs.
[F16F 2230/02] Surface features, e.g. notches or protuberances -- The CPC subclass F16F 2230/02 covers springs whose surfaces are intentionally modified with features such as notches, grooves, bumps or other protuberances. These surface alterations are used to influence the spring's mechanical performance, including stiffness, fatigue resistance, damping or load-distribution characteristics.
[F16F 2230/04] Lubrication -- F16F 2230/04 relates to the lubrication of springs, covering methods, apparatuses or substances used to reduce friction, wear or corrosion and to improve the functional performance of spring elements. It groups inventions that involve oiling, greasing, coating or other lubricating treatments applied specifically to mechanical spring components.
[F16F 2230/06] Fluid filling or discharging -- The subclass F16F 2230/06 deals with springs that incorporate a means for introducing or removing fluid, such as hydraulic or pneumatic springs where fluid is filled into or discharged from the spring during operation. It includes designs, components and methods for fluid handling in spring systems, covering both the supply and release mechanisms that affect spring performance.
[F16F 2230/08] Sensor arrangement -- F16F 2230/08 defines patent subject-matter that integrates sensors with spring mechanisms to detect load, displacement, vibration or other mechanical parameters. The subclass covers arrangements where a transducer is coupled to a spring element for measuring the spring's response, enabling monitoring or control of the spring-based system. This falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs.
[F16F 2230/10] Enclosure elements, e.g. for protection -- The classification covers components that form a housing or protective casing for springs, such as shells, covers, or barriers that shield the spring from environmental factors, mechanical damage, or contamination. These elements may be integral or removable and are designed to maintain spring performance.
[F16F 2230/105] Flexible, e.g. bellows or bladder -- The code F16F 2230/105 refers to flexible spring elements such as bellows or bladders that provide elastic movement or sealing. These components are used where a compressible, deformable element is needed to absorb loads, accommodate thermal expansion, or create a pressure-tight barrier. They fall under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and springs.
[F16F 2230/12] Gas generation, e.g. by mixing of chemicals -- F16F 2230/12 relates to spring assemblies that generate gas by chemical reactions, such as mixing reactants to produce pressurised gas for actuation or force generation. The classification applies to mechanisms where gas generation is integrated with or driven by the spring element.
[F16F 2230/14] Ball joints -- F16F 2230/14 covers ball joints, which are spherical bearing elements that connect two parts and permit angular movement while transmitting loads. They are employed in steering, suspension and other mechanisms where a pivoting connection is required, typically comprising a stud, socket and sealing features.
[F16F 2230/16] used in a strut, basically rigid -- F16F 2230/16 classifies rigid or essentially non-elastic components employed in struts, treating the spring element as a fixed support rather than a deformable device. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → springs and is used to index patents where a spring is used in a strut configuration with little or no flexure.
[F16F 2230/18] Control arrangements -- F16F 2230/18 is a CPC subclass that deals with control arrangements for springs. It covers mechanisms that modulate spring force, displacement, or damping, including valves, regulators, and electronic or mechanical controllers integrated with spring systems.
[F16F 2230/183] fluid actuated -- F16F 2230/183 designates springs whose motion is generated or controlled by a fluid, typically hydraulic or pneumatic, rather than by mechanical loading alone. It includes devices where fluid pressure expands, compresses, or otherwise actuates the spring element for energy storage, damping or positioning functions.
[F16F 2230/186] with manual adjustments -- F16F 2230/186 covers springs that incorporate means for manual adjustment of their characteristics, such as preload, stiffness or travel, allowing the user to fine-tune performance without disassembly. The classification applies to mechanical-engineering components where the adjustment is performed by hand-operated mechanisms like screws, levers or knobs.
[F16F 2230/20] Location of equilibration chamber -- The subclass F16F 2230/20 covers designs that define where an equilibration chamber is positioned in relation to a spring assembly, such as within the coil, at an end, or in an adjacent housing, to control temperature, pressure or fluid balance during operation.
[F16F 2230/22] Pumps -- F16F 2230/22 designates pumps that are driven by or incorporated into spring mechanisms, covering spring-powered or spring-actuated devices for moving fluids. The subclass includes self-acting pumps that store energy in a spring and release it to generate flow, typically used where external power sources are unavailable. This falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of engineering elements and units, specifically springs.
[F16F 2230/24] Detecting or preventing malfunction, e.g. fail safe -- F16F 2230/24 pertains to devices or methods that detect or prevent malfunction of springs, including fail-safe mechanisms that monitor spring performance and intervene when a fault is identified.
[F16F 2230/26] Air gap -- F16F 2230/26 covers springs that incorporate an air gap, meaning a void or space intentionally left within or between coil turns to modify the spring's load-deflection behavior. The air gap can reduce friction, allow compression, or provide cushioning in mechanical systems.
[F16F 2230/28] Inclination of a suspension element -- This subclass covers springs whose suspension element is tilted relative to its supporting structure, including designs that permit installation or operation at an angle. It addresses configurations that adjust the inclination to influence load distribution or movement.
[F16F 2230/30] Sealing arrangements -- This subclass covers sealing arrangements for springs, describing methods and devices that protect spring coils from fluid ingress or egress, such as protective covers, gaskets or integrated seals. It applies to mechanical-engineering spring components where sealing is required to maintain performance or prevent contamination.
[F16F 2230/32] Modular design -- F16F 2230/32 concerns the modular design of springs, covering construction techniques that enable standardized, interchangeable spring units or components. It addresses modularity in spring engineering to simplify assembly, replacement, or customization within mechanical systems.
[F16F 2230/34] Flexural hinges -- Flexural hinges are thin, flexible sections in a component that allow rotational movement by bending rather than using a separate mechanical joint. They are used in mechanical engineering where space, weight, or simplicity is critical, often incorporated into springs or compliant mechanisms. The CPC code F16F 2230/34 classifies devices that employ such hinges for controlled motion.
[F16F 2230/36] Holes, slots or the like -- F16F 2230/36 covers springs that have holes, slots or similar openings incorporated into the spring element or its arms. The openings are used to reduce mass, allow fluid flow, provide mounting points or modify stiffness, and the feature is regarded as part of the spring's functional design.
[F16F 2230/38] Off-centre positioning -- The CPC subclass F16F 2230/38 relates to springs whose mounting or coil axis is deliberately offset from the central line, i.e., off-centre positioning, to achieve particular mechanical characteristics. It is situated within the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs and defines designs where the spring's position is intentionally displaced for functional purposes.
[F16F 2230/40] Multi-layer -- F16F 2230/40 designates multi-layer springs, a subclass of springs used in mechanical engineering where the spring element consists of two or more superimposed layers of material to achieve specific load-deflection characteristics, improve durability, or combine different mechanical properties in a single component.
[F16F 2230/42] Multiple pistons -- F16F 2230/42 covers spring devices that incorporate multiple pistons within a single unit, such as hydraulic or pneumatic springs where several pistons move to provide a combined spring action. The classification includes designs where the pistons are arranged in series or parallel to increase force, travel, or damping characteristics, often used in shock absorbers, compressors and similar mechanical systems.
[F16F 2230/46] Maintenance -- F16F 2230/46 covers maintenance activities for springs, including inspection, cleaning, lubrication, adjustment, repair or replacement to restore performance or extend service-life of the component. It applies to any type of mechanical spring used in engineering systems.
[F16F 2230/48] Thermal insulation -- F16F 2230/48 covers the application of thermal insulation to springs, including materials, designs, and methods that protect springs from temperature extremes to preserve their mechanical properties and prevent degradation. This subclass relates specifically to engineering elements and units within the broader mechanical engineering category.
[F16F 2232/00] Nature of movement -- F16F 2232/00 covers patents concerning the nature of movement of springs, distinguishing how the spring stores and releases energy through linear, rotary, oscillatory or other motion patterns. It is used to classify inventions where the spring's mode of deformation or actuation is a defining feature.
[F16F 2232/02] Rotary -- F16F 2232/02 covers rotary springs, which store energy by twisting around an axis and release it as rotational motion. These springs are used in clutches, torsion bars, and torque transmitters, providing controlled angular displacement.
[F16F 2232/04] Rotary-to-translation conversion -- F16F 2232/04 classifies spring-based mechanisms that change rotary motion into linear movement. It includes devices such as torsion-spring cams, helical-spring drives and coil-spring actuators that store energy during rotation and release it to produce a translational output.
[F16F 2232/06] Translation-to-rotary conversion -- F16F 2232/06 covers devices that convert linear motion into rotary motion through the action of a spring. It pertains to spring-based mechanisms where translation of a component is transformed into rotation, commonly used in engines, actuators and other mechanical systems.
[F16F 2232/08] Linear -- The CPC subclass F16F 2232/08 designates springs whose movement is linear, meaning they extend or compress along a straight line rather than rotating or bending. It includes coil, leaf and other spring types that provide straight-line actuation in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16F 2234/00] Shape -- F16F 2234/00 covers the shape of springs, including the overall geometry of coil springs, leaf springs, torsion springs and similar elements, specifying cross-sectional forms, winding patterns and profile modifications that affect mechanical performance.
[F16F 2234/02] cylindrical -- The CPC code F16F 2234/02 designates springs whose overall configuration is cylindrical, meaning the spring element is formed into a generally round, tube-like shape. It applies to devices where the spring's geometry is defined primarily by a circular cross-section and a uniform length along its axis.
[F16F 2234/04] conical -- A conical spring is a helical spring whose coil diameter varies along its length, forming a cone shape that provides a progressive rate of force. This geometry allows a larger initial deflection with increasing stiffness as the spring compresses, useful in applications requiring space-saving mounting or variable load characteristics. It falls under the mechanical-engineering subclass for spring shapes.
[F16F 2234/06] plane or flat -- F16F 2234/06 designates springs whose overall shape is plane or flat, meaning the coil or element lies essentially in a single plane rather than forming a three-dimensional helix. This subcategory covers flat spring strips, leaf-type coils and other spring forms that maintain a flat profile while providing elastic deflection.
[F16F 2234/08] spherical -- F16F 2234/08 designates spring elements whose shape is spherical, such as ball springs or spherical coil springs, used to provide compressive or cushioning forces in mechanical assemblies. It is grouped under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, shape.
[F16F 2236/00] Mode of stressing of basic spring or damper elements or devices incorporating such elements -- F16F 2236/00 covers the way basic spring or damper elements are loaded or pre-stressed, including tension, compression, torsion, bending or combined stresses, and the techniques used to apply those stresses in devices that incorporate such elements.
[F16F 2236/02] the stressing resulting in flexion of the spring -- F16F 2236/02 is a CPC subclass for mechanical engineering that deals with springs. It covers the mode of stressing basic spring or damper elements where the load causes the spring to flex, describing how flexional forces are applied to the spring.
[F16F 2236/022] of membrane-type springs -- F16F 2236/022 covers membrane-type springs, which use a flexible membrane that deforms to store and release energy, providing a spring action without traditional coil or leaf elements. These devices are classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and specifically address the mode of stressing basic spring or damper elements that incorporate such membranes.
[F16F 2236/025] radial flexion of ring-type springs -- It covers designs where a ring-type spring is deformed by bending forces acting radially, causing the coil to flex outward or inward. The entry classifies the mode of stressing such springs by radial flexion within mechanical engineering spring elements.
[F16F 2236/027] of strip- or leg-type springs -- The subclass F16F 2236/027 covers the ways in which strip- or leg-type springs are subjected to load, including tension, compression, bending, torsion or combined stresses. It classifies the specific modes of stressing these basic spring or damper elements within mechanical engineering.
[F16F 2236/04] Compression -- The F16F 2236/04 class covers devices or arrangements where a basic spring or damper is subjected to compressive stress. It includes mechanisms that generate, transmit, or control compression forces within mechanical systems, such as pistons, shock absorbers, or load-bearing structures. The classification applies to any application where the primary action is to compress the spring element.
[F16F 2236/045] the spring material being generally enclosed -- This subclass addresses springs whose material is largely enclosed within a surrounding housing or casing, limiting direct exposure and influencing the mode of stressing. It applies to designs where the encapsulation is integral to the spring's function, such as sealed damper elements or protected coil systems.
[F16F 2236/06] Tension -- This CPC subclass pertains to springs or dampers that are loaded in tension, i.e., subjected to pulling forces that elongate the element. It includes the design, operation and testing of devices where the basic spring or damper element is stressed by tensile loads.
[F16F 2236/08] Torsion -- In the Cooperative Patent Classification, code F16F 2236/08 covers the torsional mode of stressing basic spring or damper elements, where torque is applied to twist the component. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and addresses devices that incorporate torsion-loaded spring or damper mechanisms.
[F16F 2236/085] the spring being annular -- This subclass covers annular, or ring-shaped, springs and dampers, focusing on the way they are loaded or stressed such as by radial compression, torsion or axial force. It is used to classify devices where the spring forms a closed loop rather than a linear coil.
[F16F 2236/10] Shear -- F16F 2236/10 covers springs or damper elements that are loaded primarily in shear, describing the way the component is stressed when forces act parallel to its cross-section. It includes leaf springs, torsion bars and similar devices where shear deformation is the main operating principle.
[F16F 2236/103] linear -- F16F 2236/103 relates to springs or dampers that are stressed in a linear manner, meaning the load is applied along the spring's axis in a straight line such as axial compression or tension. The subclass covers devices and mechanisms where the basic spring element operates under purely linear load conditions.
[F16F 2236/106] rotational -- The CPC entry F16F 2236/106 classifies spring or damper elements that are subjected to a rotational mode of stressing. It covers devices such as torsion springs or dampers where the primary load is a torque applied around an axis, including mechanisms that convert rotary motion into spring-or-damper action.
[F16F 2236/12] loaded in combined stresses -- The code F16F 2236/12 covers springs or dampers that are subjected to more than one type of load at the same time, such as axial force combined with torsion, bending or shear. It relates to designs, analyses or applications where the element experiences these combined stresses, affecting its performance or durability.
[F16F 2236/123] loaded in compression and shear -- The subclass F16F 2236/123 classifies spring or damper elements that are loaded simultaneously in compression and shear, specifying the combined stress mode applied to these components during operation. It sits within mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → springs.
[F16F 2236/126] Neidhart-type rubber springs -- Neidhart-type rubber springs are elastomeric spring elements whose basic structure is subjected to a specific mode of stressing, typically compression or shear, to provide damping and load-bearing capabilities. The classification groups them among mechanical engineering spring devices that incorporate rubber as the primary elastic material and defines their performance based on how the rubber element is loaded.
[F16F 2238/00] Type of springs or dampers -- F16F 2238/00 classifies the various kinds of springs and dampers, covering their design forms, materials and functional principles such as coil, leaf, torsion, gas, hydraulic or elastomeric types. It is used to identify and group these components within the broader mechanical-engineering domain.
[F16F 2238/02] Springs -- F16F 2238/02 is a CPC subclass that groups patents relating to springs and dampers used in mechanical engineering, covering their design, construction and functional characteristics. It includes various spring types such as coil, leaf, torsion and specialized forms, as well as integrated damping mechanisms, and is nested under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs → Type of springs or dampers.
[F16F 2238/022] leaf-like, e.g. of thin, planar-like metal -- The F16F 2238/022 subclass covers leaf-type springs and dampers made from thin, planar metal elements that flex like leaves. These leaf-like components are used to support loads, absorb shocks, and provide controlled deflection in mechanical systems such as vehicle suspensions and machinery.
[F16F 2238/024] torsional -- F16F 2238/024 refers to torsional springs, a type of spring that stores energy through twisting of its material and releases it as rotational torque. These springs are used in mechanical systems where controlled angular motion or torque is required, such as in valves, clutches, and torsion bar suspensions.
[F16F 2238/026] wound- or coil-like -- This subclass covers springs whose load-bearing element is formed by winding material into a helix or similar coil. It includes helical compression, tension, torsion or leaf springs fabricated by rolling or winding wire, strip, or composite material into a spiral shape. The classification is used for patents describing the geometry and manufacturing of coil-like spring components.
[F16F 2238/028] Winding direction thereof -- It covers springs where the direction the coil is wound-clockwise or counter-clockwise-is a relevant design or functional feature. The classification applies to mechanical-engineering elements such as compression, extension, torsion springs and dampers whose winding orientation influences performance.
[F16F 2238/04] Damper -- F16F 2238/04 designates dampers, devices that absorb or dissipate kinetic energy to control vibration or motion in mechanical systems. The class includes hydraulic, pneumatic, elastomeric or friction-based dampers used as complementary components to springs for vibration isolation and motion regulation.
[F16F 2238/045] Lead shear damper -- The code F16F 2238/045 designates a lead shear damper, a device that uses the shear deformation of lead to provide damping in mechanical systems, often integrated with springs to control vibration and shock. It falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering, Engineering Elements and Units, Springs, Type of springs or dampers.
[F16F 3/00] Spring units consisting of several springs, e.g. for obtaining a desired spring characteristic -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Spring units comprising several springs made of elastic material, e.g. springs which are superposed upon each other or springs arranged in parallel  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Springs working with fluid or including fluid spring -> F16F5/00 , F16F9/00 , F16F13/00
[F16F 3/02] with springs made of steel or of other material having low internal friction -- F16F 3/02 relates to spring units that incorporate several individual springs, where the springs are manufactured from steel or other low-internal-friction materials to obtain a desired overall spring behavior. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and springs.
[F16F 3/023] composed only of leaf springs -- The subclass F16F 3/023 refers to spring units that consist exclusively of leaf springs, combined to provide a desired overall spring characteristic. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of engineering elements and units, specifically springs.
[F16F 3/026] to give a zero-spring rate characteristic -- F16F 3/026 concerns spring assemblies built from two or more springs that are arranged to produce a zero or near-zero spring rate, giving a flat load-deflection response. Such configurations are used to achieve characteristics such as vibration isolation or load buffering by cancelling individual spring forces. The subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering, Engineering Elements and Units, Springs, spring units consisting of several springs.
[F16F 3/04] composed only of wound springs -- F16F 3/04 covers spring assemblies that are made up exclusively of wound, usually helical, springs. It applies to mechanical units where multiple springs are combined to achieve a specific load-deflection characteristic without including any other types of spring or component.
[F16F 3/06] of which some are placed around others in such a way that they damp each other by mutual friction -- The CPC subclass F16F 3/06 covers spring assemblies in which individual springs are arranged concentrically or helically around one another so that their motion is restrained by mutual friction, providing damping while preserving the overall spring characteristics. These units combine multiple springs to achieve tailored stiffness or energy-absorption properties.
[F16F 3/08] with springs made of a material having high internal friction, e.g. rubber -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F16F 3/08 pertains to spring units composed of multiple springs designed to achieve specific performance characteristics, particularly those incorporating materials with high internal friction such as rubber, which enhances damping and vibration absorption.
[F16F 3/087] Units comprising several springs made of plastics or the like material -- This subclass covers composite spring assemblies where several individual springs are molded or assembled from plastics or similar non-metallic material to achieve a desired spring characteristic. Such units are used where corrosion resistance, light weight, or tailored stiffness is required.
[F16F 3/0873] of the same material or the material not being specified -- This subclass covers spring assemblies that comprise several springs of identical material or where the material is unspecified, used to achieve a desired combined spring characteristic. It applies to designs in which the material uniformity or indeterminacy is a defining feature of the spring unit.
[F16F 3/0876] and of the same shape -- The subclass F16F 3/0876 pertains to spring units formed from several individual springs that have the same shape, allowing the combination to provide a particular overall spring characteristic. It is situated within mechanical engineering, under engineering elements and units, and more specifically under springs consisting of multiple springs.
[F16F 3/093] the springs being of different materials, e.g. having different types of rubber -- F16F 3/093 covers spring assemblies that combine multiple springs made from different materials, such as mixing various types of rubber, to achieve a specific overall spring behavior. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and springs.
[F16F 3/0935] and being of the same shape -- F16F 3/0935 pertains to spring units made up of two or more springs that share the same geometric shape, arranged together to produce a specific overall spring behaviour. The classification is used for designs where identical-shaped springs are combined to achieve a desired stiffness, load-deflection curve, or other performance characteristic.
[F16F 3/10] combined with springs made of steel or other material having low internal friction -- F16F 3/10 covers spring units that combine several springs and are made from steel or other low-internal-friction materials to achieve a specific spring characteristic. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs → Spring units consisting of several springs. This class addresses designs that exploit the reduced damping of such materials for more efficient energy storage and release.
[F16F 3/12] the steel spring being in contact with the rubber spring -- F16F 3/12 covers spring assemblies that combine a steel spring with a rubber spring, forming a single unit where the two components are in direct contact. The arrangement is used to tailor overall spring behaviour, such as stiffness, damping or load-deflection characteristics, by integrating the properties of both materials.
[F16F 5/00] Liquid springs in which the liquid works as a spring by compression, e.g. combined with throttling action -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Spring devices in which the compressibility of the liquid (specifically not a gas) is a key feature  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Dampers with solid or semi solid material -> F16F9/30
[F16F 6/00] Magnetic springs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Spring device in which the spring effect is given by magnetic attraction or repulsion; the device may work with permanent magnets or electromagnets  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Magnetic spring arrangements for the suppression of vibration in systems -> F16F15/03
[F16F 6/005] using permanent magnets only -- F16F 6/005 covers magnetic springs that generate a restoring force solely with permanent magnets, without any external power source. The classification addresses devices that store mechanical energy through magnetic repulsion or attraction, fitting within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and springs.
[F16F 7/00] Vibration-dampers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Vibration dampers using fluid -> F16F5/00 , F16F9/00 - Vibration dampers specific for rotary systems -> F16F15/10
[F16F 7/003] One-shot shock absorbers  using plastic deformation of members, e.g. using sacrificial, fibre-reinforced composite members  F16F7/12 -- F16F 7/003 relates to one-shot shock absorbers that dissipate impact energy through plastic deformation of a sacrificial element, typically a fiber-reinforced composite member. These devices are employed in vibration-damping applications where a single use permanently absorbs a shock.
[F16F 7/006] using textile means  safety belts or body harnesses incorporating energy absorbing means  A62B35/04 -- The classification code F16F 7/006 pertains to vibration dampers that utilize textile means, such as safety belts or body harnesses with integrated energy-absorbing features. This entry is part of the broader categories of Mechanical Engineering and Engineering Elements and Units, specifically focusing on Springs and Vibration-dampers.
[F16F 7/01] using friction between loose particles, e.g. sand -- F16F 7/01 covers vibration-dampers that dissipate energy by generating friction between loose particles such as sand, gravel or beads. The system relies on relative movement of the particles within a cavity or container to convert kinetic energy into heat, providing a simple, adjustable means of reducing vibrations in mechanical structures.
[F16F 7/015] the particles being spherical, cylindrical or the like -- This subclass covers vibration-dampers that incorporate particulate elements whose shape is defined as spherical, cylindrical or similar geometries. The particles are used to absorb or dissipate vibratory energy within springs or other mechanical components.
[F16F 7/02] with relatively-rotatable friction surfaces that are pressed together -- F16F 7/02 classifies vibration-dampers that absorb energy by means of relatively rotatable friction surfaces pressed together, such as dampers where sliding contact between rotating parts dissipates vibrations. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs → Vibration-dampers in the CPC hierarchy.
[F16F 7/023] and characterised by damping force adjustment means -- F16F 7/023 pertains to vibration-dampers that incorporate means for adjusting the damping force, enabling the device to be tuned to different operating conditions. It is located in the hierarchy under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs.
[F16F 7/026] resulting in the damping effects being different according to direction of rotation -- The F16F 7/026 classification covers vibration-damping devices whose damping characteristics change depending on the direction of rotation, such as springs or dampers that provide greater resistance in one rotational sense than the opposite. It applies to mechanical engineering elements designed to produce asymmetric rotational damping for control of vibrations or motion.
[F16F 7/04] in the direction of the axis of rotation -- F16F 7/04 pertains to vibration-dampers that act in the direction of the axis of rotation. It includes devices such as torsional springs or dampers designed to absorb or mitigate rotational vibrations along the shaft axis, typically used in rotating machinery.
[F16F 7/06] in a direction perpendicular or inclined to the axis of rotation -- This subclass deals with vibration-damping devices applied to rotating shafts where the damping force is generated in a direction that is perpendicular or at an angle to the axis of rotation, such as radial or oblique dampers. It includes designs where springs or other elements transmit forces across the shaft to reduce torsional or whirl vibrations.
[F16F 7/065] where elements interengaging frictionally are in the shape of spiral bands -- F16F 7/065 covers vibration-damping devices where the frictionally interengaging parts are formed as spiral bands, providing controlled slip and energy dissipation. The spiral geometry creates progressive contact for springs and similar mechanical elements, enhancing damping performance.
[F16F 7/08] with friction surfaces rectilinearly movable along each other -- F16F 7/08 relates to vibration-dampers that employ friction surfaces moving rectilinearly against one another, such as sliding plates or pads, to dissipate kinetic energy as heat and control oscillations. It falls under springs in mechanical engineering, defining devices that reduce vibration by converting motion into frictional resistance.
[F16F 7/082] and characterised by damping force adjustment means -- F16F 7/082 covers vibration-damper devices that incorporate mechanisms for adjusting the damping force. The classification is applied to spring-based systems where the user can vary resistance to motion, allowing the damper's performance to be tuned to different operating conditions. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and vibration-dampers.
[F16F 7/085] resulting in the damping effects being different according to direction of movement -- This subclass covers vibration-damping devices whose resistance varies with the direction of motion, providing different damping forces when moved along different axes. The anisotropic behaviour can be achieved by varying material properties, geometry or internal mechanisms to tailor performance for specific load directions.
[F16F 7/087] Elastomeric surface effect dampers -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F16F 7/087 pertains to elastomeric surface effect dampers, a specific type of vibration-damper used in mechanical engineering. These devices utilize the properties of elastomeric materials to absorb and dissipate vibrational energy, enhancing stability and reducing noise.
[F16F 7/09] in dampers of the cylinder-and-piston type -- F16F 7/09 pertains to vibration-dampers of the cylinder-and-piston type, in which a piston slides within a fluid-filled cylinder to absorb and dissipate mechanical energy. These devices are used in automotive, aerospace and other mechanical systems to reduce shocks and vibrations.
[F16F 7/095] frictional elements brought into engagement by movement along a surface oblique to the axis of the cylinder, e.g. interaction of wedge-shaped elements -- The subclass F16F 7/095 covers mechanical arrangements in which frictional elements engage by sliding along a surface that is set at an angle to the cylinder's axis, such as the interaction of wedge-shaped components. These designs use the oblique motion to create controlled friction for functions like torque transmission, braking or vibration damping within spring-type devices.
[F16F 7/10] using inertia effect -- F16F 7/10 addresses vibration-dampening devices that employ the inertia effect, such as masses or pendulums attached to springs, to counteract oscillatory motion. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and specifically vibration-dampers.
[F16F 7/1005] characterised by active control of the mass -- This subclass includes vibration-dampers or spring devices that employ active control of the moving mass, using actuators, sensors or feedback loops to vary mass position, stiffness or damping in real time for vibration reduction. Typical examples are active tuned-mass dampers and magnetorheological or fluid-filled systems that adjust the effective mass during operation.
[F16F 7/1011] by electromagnetic means -- F16F 7/1011 designates vibration-dampers that operate by electromagnetic means, using magnetic fields or eddy currents to convert vibratory energy into heat or electricity. These non-contact devices provide damping for springs and related mechanical components.
[F16F 7/1017] by fluid means -- The CPC subclass F16F 7/1017 covers spring or vibration-damper devices that act by fluid means, such as hydraulic or pneumatic shock absorbers and fluid-filled spring systems that use liquid or gas pressure to provide elastic or damping action. These mechanisms employ the movement or compression of a fluid to absorb energy or return a component to its original position.
[F16F 7/1022] the linear oscillation movement being converted into a rotational movement of the inertia member, e.g. using a pivoted mass -- This subclass covers devices that transform a linear oscillating motion into a rotary motion of an inertial component, typically by means of a pivoted mass or similar mechanism. It is used in vibration-dampers and spring-based systems to convert kinetic energy for control or storage.
[F16F 7/1028] the inertia-producing means being a constituent part of the system which is to be damped -- The code F16F 7/1028 covers vibration-dampers in which the inertial mass is built into the very component being controlled, rather than being an external or added weight. It applies to systems where the inertia-producing element forms an integral part of the structure that must be damped, such as self-contained spring or shock-absorber designs that use the component's own mass to reduce vibration.
[F16F 7/1034] of movement of a liquid -- The CPC subclass F16F 7/1034 covers vibration-dampers or spring devices whose operation relies on the movement of a liquid, such as hydraulic shock absorbers, fluid-filled dampers, and other mechanisms that dissipate vibration or control motion by forcing a liquid through orifices, valves, or chambers. It includes designs where the liquid's flow characteristics provide the primary damping effect.
[F16F 7/104] the inertia member being resiliently mounted -- F16F 7/104 covers vibration-dampers where the inertial mass is attached to the structure through a resilient mounting such as a spring or elastomer. The resilient connection isolates the mass from external vibrations while allowing it to move relative to the base, improving vibration attenuation.
[F16F 7/108] on plastics springs -- F16F 7/108 refers to plastic springs employed as vibration-dampers. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for springs, covering polymer-based elastic elements that absorb or reduce vibratory motion in equipment.
[F16F 7/112] on fluid springs -- F16F 7/112 pertains to fluid springs employed as vibration-dampers in mechanical engineering. The class includes hydraulic or pneumatic devices that provide spring action to absorb and dissipate vibrations in structures or machinery. It falls under the hierarchy of springs and vibration-dampers within engineering elements and units.
[F16F 7/116] on metal springs -- Covers metal springs and their manufacture, especially those used as vibration-dampers, including various shapes, material treatments, and methods to achieve specific stiffness or damping characteristics.
[F16F 7/12] using plastic deformation of members -- The F16F 7/12 entry covers vibration-dampers that attenuate oscillations by deliberately causing plastic deformation of their structural members. Examples include devices that use yieldable metal elements or deformable plates to absorb kinetic energy and reduce vibration. This approach is classified under springs within mechanical engineering.
[F16F 7/121] the members having a cellular, e.g. honeycomb, structure -- This subgroup covers vibration dampers whose members are built with a cellular, for example honeycomb, structure, giving lightweight yet stiff components that absorb vibrations. Such devices are employed in mechanical engineering where space and weight savings are important.
[F16F 7/122] characterised by corrugations, e.g. of rolled corrugated material -- F16F 7/122 is a subclass of vibration-dampers within the springs group of mechanical engineering, covering devices characterised by corrugations, such as those made from rolled-corrugated material, which are used to provide damping or stiffness functions.
[F16F 7/123] Deformation involving a bending action, e.g. strap moving through multiple rollers, folding of members  F16F7/125 ,  F16F7/128  take precedence -- F16F 7/123 covers devices in which a component is deformed by a bending action, for example a strap passing through a series of rollers or the folding of structural members. The classification groups such vibration-dampers under mechanical engineering and gives precedence to the more specific sub-entries F16F 7/125 and F16F 7/128.
[F16F 7/124] characterised by their special construction from fibre-reinforced plastics -- F16F 7/124 covers vibration-dampers whose design incorporates fibre-reinforced plastic components, using the material's high strength-to-weight ratio to achieve compact, corrosion-resistant shock-absorbing structures. The classification specifies devices where the plastic composite forms the primary load-bearing or damping elements, often substituting metal springs or dampers in mechanical systems.
[F16F 7/125] Units with a telescopic-like action as one member moves into, or out of a second member  F16F7/124 ,  F16F7/127 ,  F16F7/128  take precedence -- F16F 7/125 covers spring or vibration-damping units that operate with a telescopic-like motion, where one component slides into or out of another component. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs → Vibration-dampers and is subordinate to the related groups F16F 7/124, F16F 7/127 and F16F 7/128, which take precedence.
[F16F 7/126] against the action of shear pins; one member having protuberances, e.g. dimples, ball bearings which cause the other member to deform -- F16F 7/126 covers vibration-dampers that counteract the action of shear pins by employing a member bearing protuberances such as dimples or ball bearings, which cause the opposing member to deform. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs → Vibration-dampers in the CPC hierarchy.
[F16F 7/127] by a blade element cutting or tearing into a quantity of material; Pultrusion of a filling material -- This subclass covers vibration-damping devices where a blade element cuts or tears into a quantity of material, typically created by pultruding a filling material to form the damper structure.
[F16F 7/128] characterised by the members, e.g. a flat strap, yielding through stretching, pulling apart -- F16F 7/128 falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs and vibration-dampers. It designates dampers whose members-typically a flat strap-absorb vibration by yielding, stretching or being pulled apart, thereby converting kinetic energy into deformation. This approach provides a simple, flexible means of vibration control in various mechanical systems.
[F16F 7/14] of cable support type, i.e. frictionally-engaged loop-forming cables -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F16F 7/14 pertains to vibration dampers of the cable support type, specifically those that use frictionally-engaged loop-forming cables for mechanical engineering applications. This classification is used by both the European Patent Office (EPO) and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO).
[F16F 9/00] Springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers, or similarly-constructed movement-dampers using a fluid or the equivalent as damping medium -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Movement-dampers using a fluid (i.e.: compressible or incompressible) as damping medium  Some examples:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Using liquid springs -> F16F5/00 - Connection of valves to inflatable elastic bodies -> B60C29/00 - Braking devices, stops or buffers for wing-operating appliances -> E05F3/00 , E05F5/00
[F16F 9/003] Dampers characterised by having pressure absorbing means other than gas, e.g. sponge rubber -- F16F 9/003 covers dampers that absorb pressure using solid or semi-solid media rather than gas, such as sponge rubber or similar elastomeric materials. These devices are classified under mechanical engineering springs and vibration-dampers that employ a fluid-like damping medium, providing shock-absorption and movement control without reliance on compressed gas.
[F16F 9/006] characterised by the nature of the damping medium, e.g. biodegradable  variable viscosity damping adjustment  F16F9/53 -- This classification entry pertains to springs and dampers that use a fluid or similar material as a damping medium, specifically focusing on those characterized by the nature of the damping medium, such as being biodegradable or having variable viscosity for adjustable damping.
[F16F 9/02] using gas only -- The subclass F16F 9/02 covers springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers and similar movement-damping devices that use only a gas as the damping medium, such as pneumatic springs and gas-filled shock absorbers, where the resistance to motion is provided solely by compressed or vented gas.

[F16F 9/0263] characterised by actuation means, e.g. manually-operated lever arrangement  F16F9/0254  takes precedence -- This classification entry pertains to springs and dampers that use a fluid for damping, specifically focusing on those with manual actuation mechanisms like levers, where the method of operation is a key characteristic. However, if the device primarily features an electric or electronic control system, it should be classified under a different code.
[F16F 9/0272] with control rod extending through the piston rod into the piston -- The F16F 9/0272 subclass covers springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers that incorporate a control rod that passes through the piston rod and extends into the piston itself, enabling direct regulation of the spring or damping action. This design is used to adjust stiffness or response characteristics while maintaining the fluid-based damping medium.

The entry covers specifics such as construction, material choice, dimensions, sealing, and performance parameters of these fluid-based dampers.
[F16F 9/029] electrical, e.g. connections or contacts -- F16F 9/029 pertains to springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar movement-damping devices that incorporate an electrical function, such as integrated connections, contacts or other conductive elements. It covers designs where the damping mechanism and the electrical component are combined within a single unit.
[F16F 9/04] in a chamber with a flexible wall -- This subclass covers fluid-damped springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers that operate in a sealed chamber with a flexible wall, allowing the wall to deform as the device moves to provide damping.
[F16F 9/0409] characterised by the wall structure -- F16F 9/0409 refers to springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or comparable movement-dampers whose design is distinguished by the specific construction of their walls, such as shape, thickness or material arrangement, influencing how the device stores and releases energy. It lies within the mechanical-engineering subclass for springs.
[F16F 9/0418] having a particular shape, e.g. annular, spherical, tube-like  F16F9/05  takes precedence -- This subclass covers springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar movement-dampening devices that are manufactured with a particular geometry, such as annular, spherical or tube-like shapes, distinguishing them from other spring types. [F16F 9/0427] toroidal -- This subclass pertains to toroidal fluid-damped devices, i.e. springs or shock absorbers that incorporate a doughnut-shaped chamber filled with a liquid or gas to provide damping. The toroidal geometry allows the medium to move radially or circumferentially, improving vibration isolation in mechanical systems.
[F16F 9/0436] characterised by being contained in a generally closed space -- F16F 9/0436 covers springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar movement-damper devices that are enclosed within a generally closed space, where the damping medium is a fluid or equivalent. These devices belong to mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs.
[F16F 9/0445] characterised by intermediate rings or other not embedded reinforcing elements  wall structure  F16F9/0409 -- This subgroup concerns springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers whose wall structure incorporates intermediate rings or other reinforcing elements that are not fully embedded in the surrounding material, providing added strength and stiffness to the component. The reinforcing parts remain distinct within the wall, allowing improved load-bearing or damping performance without being integral to the wall's bulk.
[F16F 9/0454] characterised by the assembling method or by the mounting arrangement, e.g. mounting of the membrane  F16F9/0409 ,  F16F9/0445  take precedence -- This subgroup covers springs, vibration-dampers, or shock-absorbers that use a fluid or equivalent damping medium and are distinguished by their assembly technique or the way the membrane is mounted, with earlier subclasses F16F9/0409 and F16F9/0445 taking precedence.
[F16F 9/0463] with separate crimping rings -- F16F 9/0463 covers springs, vibration-dampers or shock absorbers that incorporate separate crimping rings, which are used to secure the ends of the device or to adjust its tension and performance.
