[F16F 9/0472] characterised by comprising a damping device  with plastic deformation of members  F16F7/12 ; delay devices or arrangements  F15B21/10 -- The code F16F 9/0472 covers springs, vibration-dampers or shock absorbers that incorporate a damping device that dissipates energy by plastic deformation of its members, and may also include a delay device or arrangement as defined in related subclasses.
[F16F 9/0481] provided in an opening to the exterior atmosphere -- This subclass covers springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar fluid-damped movement-dampening units that are installed in an opening to the exterior atmosphere. It is applied to devices whose damping medium is exposed to outside air through such an opening.
[F16F 9/049] multi-chamber units  F16F9/0472 ,  F16F9/05  take precedence -- F16F 9/049 covers multi-chamber spring or damping units that incorporate two or more chambers, often with separate fluid compartments, to provide staged or variable stiffness, energy storage, or shock absorption. These devices may combine springs, shock-absorbers, or vibration dampers in a single assembly. The classification follows the preceding groups F16F 9/0472 and F16F 9/05.
[F16F 9/05] the flexible wall being of the rolling diaphragm type -- The entry F16F 9/05 covers flexible walls of the rolling-diaphragm type that form part of fluid-damped springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers, where the diaphragm rolls to accommodate movement while sealing the fluid chamber.
[F16F 9/052] characterised by the bumper -- F16F 9/052 pertains to springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers that incorporate a bumper as part of the damping assembly. The classification covers devices where the bumper modifies or limits movement, often by contacting a fluid-filled chamber or similar medium to absorb energy.
[F16F 9/055] having a double diaphragm construction -- F16F 9/055 covers springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar movement-damping devices that use a fluid or equivalent damping medium and employ a double-diaphragm construction, wherein two flexible membranes enclose the fluid to provide controlled damping performance. This design is used to enhance stability and adjustability of the damping characteristics.
[F16F 9/057] characterised by the piston -- F16F 9/057 relates to springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar movement-dampening devices that employ a fluid as the damping medium and are distinguished by the presence of a piston within the device.
[F16F 9/06] using both gas and liquid -- F16F 9/06 covers springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar devices that employ a mixed damping medium consisting of both a gas and a liquid. The dual-phase fluid provides combined compressibility and viscosity to control motion and reduce vibration. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs.
[F16F 9/061] Mono-tubular units -- Mono-tubular units are single-tube devices that integrate a spring element with a fluid-filled chamber to supply combined elastic and hydraulic damping, serving as shock absorbers or vibration-isolators in mechanical systems.
[F16F 9/062] Bi-tubular units -- Bi-tubular units are fluid-damped devices comprising two concentric tubes that form a sealed chamber where a fluid or equivalent medium provides resistance to motion, functioning as springs, vibration dampers, or shock absorbers.Bi-tubular units are fluid-damped devices comprising two concentric tubes that form a sealed chamber where a fluid or equivalent medium provides resistance to motion, functioning as springs, vibration dampers, or shock absorbers.
[F16F 9/063] comprising a hollow piston rod -- F16F 9/063 classifies springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers that incorporate a hollow piston rod, allowing fluid to circulate within the rod for damping the movement. This design is used in fluid-based spring systems to improve shock absorption and control vibrations.
[F16F 9/064] Units characterised by the location or shape of the expansion chamber  F16F9/068 ,  F16F9/08  take precedence -- The code F16F 9/064 denotes fluid-damped spring or shock-absorber units whose defining feature is the location or shape of the expansion chamber. It applies to designs where the chamber's geometry or placement governs the damping performance of the element.
[F16F 9/065] Expansion chamber provided on the upper or lower end of a damper, separately there from or laterally on the damper -- F16F 9/065 relates to dampers that include an expansion chamber positioned at the upper or lower end of the damper, or attached laterally, to alter fluid movement and enhance vibration-damping performance.
[F16F 9/066] Units characterised by the partition, baffle or like element  F16F9/068 ,  F16F9/08  take precedence -- F16F 9/066 covers fluid-damped springs, shock absorbers and similar movement-dampening units that incorporate an internal partition, baffle or comparable element to direct or restrict the flow of the damping fluid. The partition or baffle creates separate chambers or channels, allowing the device to achieve specific damping characteristics or to isolate portions of the fluid.
[F16F 9/067] Partitions of the piston type, e.g. sliding pistons -- F16F 9/067 relates to piston-type partitions, such as sliding pistons, employed in mechanical springs, vibration dampers and shock absorbers. These partitions separate chambers and move relative to each other to provide controlled damping, often using metal or fluid-filled constructions.
[F16F 9/068] where the throttling of a gas flow provides damping action -- This subclass includes spring-type or shock-absorbing devices where the damping effect is produced by restricting the flow of a gas, using the pressure drop across a throttling element to dissipate kinetic energy. The approach applies fluid resistance as the primary means of vibration or shock attenuation within mechanical engineering spring systems.
[F16F 9/08] in a chamber with a flexible wall -- The code refers to springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers that operate inside a sealed chamber whose wall is flexible, allowing the fluid or equivalent medium to move as the wall deforms and provide damping. Such constructions are used to absorb impacts or control oscillations in mechanical systems.
[F16F 9/081] being of the fluid displacement type, i.e. the piston not comprising damping arrangements  F16F9/096  takes precedence -- F16F 9/081 is a CPC subclass for springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers that operate by fluid displacement, where the piston does not include any damping mechanism. It belongs to Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs and is superseded by the more specific subclass F16F 9/096.
[F16F 9/082] characterised by the hydropneumatic accumulator -- F16F 9/082 covers spring or vibration-damper devices that use a hydropneumatic accumulator as the primary energy-storage or damping component, combining compressed gas and liquid to provide elastic and fluid damping characteristics. The subclass applies to hydraulic shock absorbers, fluid-filled springs and similar mechanisms where the accumulator is integral to the movement-damping system.
[F16F 9/084] comprising a gas spring contained within a flexible wall, the wall not being in contact with the damping fluid, i.e. mounted externally on the damper cylinder -- F16F 9/084 covers devices that integrate a gas spring inside a flexible wall which is kept separate from any damping fluid, the wall being positioned externally on the damper cylinder. This arrangement provides a sealed gas-spring element that works alongside a fluid-based vibration damper without direct fluid contact.
[F16F 9/088] comprising a gas spring with a flexible wall provided within the cylinder on the piston rod of a monotubular damper or within the inner tube of a bitubular damper -- This subclass covers devices where a gas spring includes a flexible membrane positioned either on the piston rod inside the cylinder of a single-tube damper or within the inner tube of a double-tube damper, providing combined spring and damping functions. The arrangement enables compact, integrated movement-control components for mechanical systems.
[F16F 9/092] comprising a gas spring with a flexible wall provided between the tubes of a bitubular damper -- F16F 9/092 is a subclass of mechanical-engineering springs that refers to devices using a gas spring whose flexible wall is placed between the two tubes of a bitubular damper, providing fluid-based vibration or shock-absorption.
[F16F 9/096] comprising a hydropneumatic accumulator of the membrane type provided on the upper or the lower end of a damper or separately from or laterally on the damper -- The classification covers springs or vibration-dampers that integrate a hydropneumatic membrane accumulator positioned at the upper or lower end of the damper, or mounted separately or laterally on it, using a fluid as the damping medium. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, springs, and fluid-based movement-dampening devices.
[F16F 9/10] using liquid only -- F16F 9/10 designates springs, vibration dampers or shock absorbers that rely exclusively on a liquid as the damping medium. It includes hydraulic shock absorbers, oil-filled mounts and any similar devices where no gas or solid filler is used for the damping action.
[F16F 9/103] Devices with one or more members moving linearly to and fro in chambers, any throttling effect being immaterial, i.e. damping by viscous shear effect only  F16F9/53  takes precedence -- F16F 9/103 covers devices in which one or more members move linearly back and forth within chambers and are damped only by viscous shear of a fluid, with any throttling effect considered irrelevant. The classification falls under springs, vibration-dampers and shock-absorbers that use fluid as the damping medium and follows the broader group F16F 9/53.
[F16F 9/106] Squeeze-tube devices -- Squeeze-tube devices are compact dampers that use a flexible tube deformed by external pressure, forcing fluid through narrow passages to absorb shock or vibration. They provide adjustable, fluid-based damping in automotive, aerospace and industrial systems, and are classified under CPC code F16F 9/106 for fluid-filled movement-dampers.
[F16F 9/12] Devices with one or more rotary vanes turning in the fluid any throttling effect being immaterial, -- F16F 9/12 covers fluid-damped spring or shock-absorbing devices that incorporate one or more rotary vanes immersed in a fluid, where the vanes rotate to dissipate energy and the throttling effect of the fluid is not a functional requirement. These devices use the fluid's resistance to vane motion to provide vibration, shock or movement damping.
[F16F 9/125] characterised by adjustment means -- F16F 9/125 covers springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers and similar fluid-damped movement-damping devices that incorporate means for adjusting their performance, such as screws, valves, interchangeable inserts or variable-volume chambers that allow the damping force or stiffness to be altered. This sub-group focuses on the design of these adjustment mechanisms within the overall damping unit.
[F16F 9/14] Devices with one or more members, e.g. pistons, vanes, moving to and fro in chambers and using throttling effect -- This subclass covers devices that employ one or more reciprocating or oscillating members such as pistons or vanes moving within chambers and creating a damping or spring effect by throttling fluid flow. It lies in the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engineering elements, springs, and fluid-based vibration-dampers.
[F16F 9/145] involving only rotary movement of the effective parts  wing closers or openers with fluid brakes of the rotary type  E05F3/14 -- F16F 9/145 classifies fluid-damped spring devices whose functional parts move only in rotation, such as wing-closers or openers that employ rotary fluid brakes and are cross-referenced to E05F 3/14. These are movement-dampers using a fluid as the damping medium and are designed for purely rotary operation.
[F16F 9/16] involving only straight-line movement of the effective parts -- F16F 9/16 covers springs, vibration dampers, shock absorbers or similar fluid-damped movement-damping devices where the effective components move solely in a straight line. It applies to designs that use a fluid or equivalent medium for damping.
[F16F 9/165] with two or more cylinders in line, i.e. in series connection  F16F9/26  takes precedence -- In CPC class F16F 9/165 the focus is on fluid-damped springs with two or more cylinders in line, i.e., series connection. Dampers that use such sequential cylinders allow the fluid to flow through them one after another, providing staged resistance and improving vibration control in devices such as shock absorbers.
[F16F 9/18] with a closed cylinder and a piston separating two or more working spaces therein -- This classification code pertains to mechanical engineering elements, specifically springs and dampers that use a fluid for vibration or shock absorption. It focuses on devices featuring a closed cylinder with a piston that divides the internal space into multiple working chambers.
[F16F 9/182] comprising a hollow piston rod -- This subclass includes fluid-damped springs, shock absorbers or vibration attenuators that incorporate a hollow piston rod as part of the damping mechanism. The hollow rod allows fluid to flow through or be contained, providing adjustable or enhanced damping characteristics. Used in hydraulic or pneumatic spring designs.
[F16F 9/185] Bitubular units  where compression of gas leads to a clear spring action  F16F9/062 -- F16F 9/185 refers to bitubular units that produce a clear spring action through the compression of gas, typically within a fluid-filled chamber. These devices function as springs, vibration dampers or shock absorbers by using the compressed gas to generate the required elastic response.
[F16F 9/187] with uni-directional flow of damping fluid through the valves -- F16F 9/187 covers springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers that employ a fluid as the damping medium and are designed so the fluid can flow only in one direction through the internal valves, enabling controlled, uni-directional energy dissipation.
[F16F 9/19] with a single cylinder -- This subclass falls under mechanical engineering elements and units, specifically springs that use a fluid or equivalent as the damping medium. It includes springs, vibration dampers, shock absorbers or similar devices that are constructed with a single cylinder.
[F16F 9/20] with the piston-rod extending through both ends of the cylinder -- This subclass covers fluid-damped springs, vibration dampers or shock absorbers whose piston is mounted on a rod extending through both ends of the cylinder, permitting forces to be applied on either side and enabling double-acting operation. Such devices are employed in suspension systems, hydraulic shock absorbers and vibration-control mechanisms where bidirectional actuation is required.
[F16F 9/22] with one or more cylinders each having a single working space closed by a piston or plunger -- F16F 9/22 covers fluid-damped springs, vibration or shock-absorbing devices that use one or more cylinders, each containing a single working space sealed by a piston or plunger, to provide controlled movement damping.
[F16F 9/26] with two cylinders in line and with the two pistons or plungers connected together -- This classification code pertains to specific types of shock absorbers or vibration dampers that utilize a fluid for damping, featuring a design with two cylinders aligned in series and their respective pistons or plungers interconnected.
[F16F 9/28] with two parallel cylinders and with the two pistons or plungers connected together -- F16F 9/28 covers fluid-damped spring or shock-absorber devices that employ two parallel cylinders whose pistons or plungers are mechanically linked, allowing coordinated movement for vibration or impact attenuation.
[F16F 9/285] by a rocker arm -- The code F16F 9/285 classifies fluid-damped springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers whose damping action is transmitted by a rocker arm. It is placed under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, specifically springs that employ a fluid or equivalent medium for movement damping.
[F16F 9/30] with solid or semi-solid material, e.g. pasty masses, as damping medium -- The subclass F16F 9/30 covers springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers that use a solid or semi-solid material-such as a pasty mass-as the damping medium rather than a fluid.
[F16F 9/303] the damper being of the telescopic type -- F16F 9/303 classifies telescopic dampers, which are movement-dampening devices built from sliding, telescoping sections and employ a fluid or equivalent medium to absorb shocks or vibrations. The code falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs.
[F16F 9/306] of the constrained layer type, i.e. comprising one or more constrained viscoelastic layers -- F16F 9/306 relates to constrained-layer dampers, which incorporate one or more viscoelastic layers bonded to a substrate so that shear deformation of the constrained material dissipates vibrational energy, providing vibration-damping or shock-absorbing performance.
[F16F 9/32] Details -- F16F 9/32 pertains to specific details of springs, vibration dampers, shock absorbers, or similar devices that use a fluid for damping. This classification covers the intricate components and mechanisms involved in these devices, focusing on their construction and operational aspects.
[F16F 9/3207] Constructional features  F16F9/34  -  F16F9/50  take precedence; assembly or repair  F16F9/3271 -- This classification code, F16F 9/3207, pertains to specific constructional features of springs and dampers that use a fluid for damping, with certain aspects taking precedence over others in the hierarchy. It covers details not specifically addressed by more narrow codes related to assembly or repair.
[F16F 9/3214] of pistons  F16F9/0227  and  F16F9/36  take precedence; throttling passages in or on piston body  F16F9/3405 -- This CPC subgroup covers pistons that incorporate internal or external throttling passages to control fluid flow, a feature used in spring-type shock absorbers, vibration dampers and similar hydraulic movement-damping devices.
[F16F 9/3221] of piston rods -- F16F 9/3221 relates to piston rods that are integral to fluid-damped springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers, where the rod transmits loads while the surrounding fluid provides the damping action.
[F16F 9/3228] of connections between pistons and piston rods -- F16F 9/3228 covers the means for joining pistons to their rods in fluid-damped spring or shock-absorber assemblies. It includes bolts, pins, splines, welding, adhesives and other mechanical or bonding techniques that secure the piston to the rod while allowing the required movement.
[F16F 9/3235] of cylinders  F16F9/483  takes precedence -- This classification entry pertains to springs and dampers that use a fluid for vibration or shock absorption, specifically addressing the construction involving cylinders. It indicates that when classifying inventions under this code, priority should be given to the more specific subclass F16F9/483 if applicable.
[F16F 9/3242] of cylinder ends, e.g. caps -- The code F16F 9/3242 covers fluid-damped springs, vibration dampers or shock absorbers in which the cylinder ends, such as caps, form part of the damping assembly. It relates to designs where the caps seal or shape the fluid chamber that provides the movement-damping function.
[F16F 9/325] for attachment of valve units -- This subclass relates to fluid-damped springs, vibration dampers or shock absorbers that incorporate means for attaching valve units. It covers arrangements where the damping element includes fittings, brackets or connections that allow a valve to be mounted directly on the device.
[F16F 9/3257] in twin-tube type devices -- F16F 9/3257 covers twin-tube devices in which two concentric tubes form a sealed chamber filled with a fluid that provides damping, such as fluid-damped springs, vibration-dampers or shock absorbers. The design uses the fluid's resistance to motion to control oscillations and absorb shocks.
[F16F 9/3264] Arrangements for indicating, e.g. fluid level; Arrangements for checking dampers  F16F9/3292  takes precedence; testing of vehicle damping  G01M17/04 -- This classification code pertains to specific arrangements in springs and shock absorbers that use a fluid as a damping medium, focusing on mechanisms for indicating fluid levels or checking the condition of dampers. It also covers testing methods for vehicle damping systems, though it is noted that certain codes take precedence in more specialized areas.
[F16F 9/3271] Assembly or repair -- This class includes techniques, devices and steps for constructing, joining and servicing fluid-damped springs, vibration-dampers and shock-absorbers. It covers component alignment, sealing, filling, adjusting fluid volumes and any repair actions needed to restore performance.
[F16F 9/3278] for lubrication  lubricating  per se   F16N
[F16F 9/3285] for filtering  filters  per se   B01D
[F16F 9/3292] Sensor arrangements -- The CPC entry F16F 9/3292 covers designs that incorporate sensors into fluid-damped springs, vibration dampers, shock absorbers or similar movement-damping devices. It addresses arrangements for detecting parameters such as displacement, pressure, acceleration or vibration within the damping system, enabling monitoring, control or feedback functions.
[F16F 9/34] Special valve constructions -- F16F 9/34 covers springs, vibration-dampers or shock absorbers that incorporate specially designed valve structures to regulate the flow of a fluid or equivalent damping medium, thereby controlling the rate of energy dissipation during movement. These constructions typically integrate valves that open, close or throttle to achieve tailored damping characteristics.
[F16F 9/3405] Throttling passages in or on piston body, e.g. slots  F16F9/344 ,  F16F9/3481  take precedence -- The subclass F16F 9/3405 covers pistons in fluid-damped springs, shock absorbers or vibration-dampers that incorporate throttling passages - for example slots or grooves - formed in or on the piston body to regulate fluid flow and thus control the damping characteristics. It resides under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for springs and fluid-based movement dampers.
[F16F 9/341] comprising noise-reducing or like features, e.g. screens  F16F9/3415  takes precedence -- F16F 9/341 covers springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar devices that use a fluid or equivalent medium for damping and include noise-reducing features such as screens. This subclass refines the broader category of fluid-damped movement dampers. The more specific subclass F16F9/3415 is considered prior art within this group.
[F16F 9/3415] characterised by comprising plastics, elastomeric or porous elements -- F16F 9/3415 relates to mechanical springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers and comparable movement-damping devices that are characterised by containing plastics, elastomeric or porous elements as part of their construction.
[F16F 9/342] Throttling passages operating with metering pins -- F16F 9/342 covers throttling passages that incorporate metering pins for controlling fluid flow in springs, vibration dampers, shock absorbers or similar devices that use a fluid as the damping medium. The metering pins regulate the rate of fluid passage, providing adjustable damping characteristics.
[F16F 9/344] Vortex flow passages -- F16F 9/344 covers springs, shock absorbers or vibration-dampers that employ specially shaped fluid passages to create vortex flow, using the resulting turbulence to dissipate kinetic energy and provide damping. It includes designs where the fluid's rotational movement inside the passage is the primary means of resisting motion.
[F16F 9/346] Throttling passages in the form of slots arranged in cylinder walls -- F16F 9/346 relates to throttling passages configured as slots cut into the walls of cylinders, used in springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers to regulate fluid flow and modify damping behaviour.
[F16F 9/3465] Slots having a variable section along their length -- F16F 9/3465 covers springs, vibration-dampers or shock absorbers that incorporate slots whose cross-section varies along their length, allowing the stiffness, travel or damping characteristics to be tuned by changing the slot geometry. The variable-section design is used to optimize performance in fluid-filled or otherwise damped movement-damping devices.
[F16F 9/348] Throttling passages in the form of annular discs , operating in opposite directions -- This subclass covers fluid-damped springs or shock absorbers that use annular disc throttling passages, where two discs rotate in opposite directions to limit fluid flow and provide vibration attenuation. The opposing disc motion creates a controlled restriction that converts kinetic energy into heat within the damping medium.
[F16F 9/3481] characterised by shape or construction of throttling passages in piston  F16F9/344  takes precedence -- F16F 9/3481 classifies springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar movement-dampers that use a fluid or equivalent as the damping medium, where the defining feature is the shape or construction of throttling passages in the piston. It is a sub-category of F16F 9/344, which provides the broader context for fluid-damped devices. This entry falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and springs.
[F16F 9/3482] the annular discs being incorporated within the valve or piston body  F16F9/3484 ,  F16F9/3485  take precedence -- This subclass relates to fluid-damped springs or shock absorbers in which one or more annular discs are built into the valve or piston body to provide damping. The discs form part of the movement-restricting element and are used as vibration-dampers. Subclasses F16F9/3484 and F16F9/3485 are considered more specific and therefore take precedence.
[F16F 9/3484] characterised by features of the annular discs  per se , singularly or in combination -- This classification entry pertains to springs and dampers that use a fluid for vibration or shock absorption, specifically focusing on designs characterized by the unique features of annular discs used either individually or in combination.
[F16F 9/3485] characterised by features of supporting elements intended to guide or limit the movement of the annular discs  F16F9/3488  takes precedence -- The code F16F 9/3485 covers spring or vibration-damper assemblies in which supporting elements are shaped to guide or limit the movement of annular discs, such as guide ribs, stops or housings that control disc travel. This subclass follows the broader groups for springs and fluid-damped devices and supersedes subclass F16F9/3488.
[F16F 9/3487] with spacers or spacing rings -- A spring, vibration-damper or shock-absorber that includes spacers or spacing rings to keep the coils or moving parts at a defined distance, improving stability and controlling the travel of the device. This arrangement is used in mechanical systems where precise spacing of the spring elements is required for reliable damping performance.
[F16F 9/3488] characterised by features intended to affect valve bias or pre-stress -- F16F 9/3488 covers springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers that incorporate design elements specifically aimed at adjusting valve bias or pre-stress, influencing how the damping fluid or equivalent medium is released or retained during operation. This subclass falls under the broader category of fluid-damped movement-dampers within mechanical engineering.
[F16F 9/36] Special sealings, including sealings or guides for piston-rods -- F16F 9/36 pertains to special sealing arrangements for piston-rods in fluid-damped springs, vibration dampers and shock absorbers, encompassing seals and guide elements that prevent leakage and regulate rod movement.
[F16F 9/361] Sealings of the bellows-type -- The F16F 9/361 classification covers sealing solutions for bellows-type components, detailing methods and structures that prevent fluid or gas leakage in flexible diaphragms used in mechanical systems. It addresses designs that integrate seals with the bellows to maintain pressure integrity while allowing movement.
[F16F 9/362] Combination of sealing and guide arrangements for piston rods  F16F9/361 ,  F16F9/365  take precedence -- F16F 9/362 relates to piston-rod assemblies in which the seal and the guide are combined into a single arrangement, providing both fluid tightness and axial guidance for the rod within a cylinder while reducing wear and leakage.
[F16F 9/363] the guide being mounted between the piston and the sealing, enabling lubrication of the guide -- F16F 9/363 relates to hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders where a guide is positioned between the piston and the sealing element to supply lubrication to the guide, enhancing smooth motion and reducing wear. The arrangement is used to improve reliability of spring-type or fluid-damped movement devices.
[F16F 9/364] of multi-tube dampers -- F16F 9/364 covers multi-tube fluid dampers, which consist of several parallel tubes filled with a damping fluid to absorb vibrations or shock. These devices are classified under springs and vibration-dampers in the mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F16F 9/365] the sealing arrangement having a pressurised chamber separated from the damping medium -- F16F 9/365 covers designs where a sealed chamber holds a pressurised gas or liquid that is isolated from the fluid used for damping in springs, shock-absorbers or vibration-dampers. The arrangement provides a barrier between the pressurised medium and the damping fluid, improving performance and preventing mixing.
[F16F 9/366] functioning as guide only, e.g. bushings -- F16F 9/366 relates to springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar movement-dampening devices that are employed solely as guides, for example bushings, providing positional control without bearing significant loads.
[F16F 9/367] allowing misalignment of the piston rod -- F16F 9/367 covers fluid-damped spring or shock-absorber assemblies in which the piston rod is permitted to be offset from the cylinder axis, allowing misalignment between rod and housing. Such designs typically employ flexible joints, swivelling bearings or other mechanisms that maintain sealing while accommodating angular or lateral deviations. This subclass refines the broader category of springs, vibration-dampers and shock-absorbers using fluid as the damping medium.
[F16F 9/368] Sealings in pistons -- F16F 9/368 classifies designs and features of sealings used in pistons of fluid-damped springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers. It includes the geometry, materials and installation methods of piston seals that prevent fluid leakage and control movement within such dampers.
[F16F 9/369] Sealings for elements other than pistons or piston rods, e.g. valves -- This CPC entry (F16F 9/369) covers sealings used on components other than pistons or piston rods, such as valves, within fluid-damped springs, vibration-dampers and shock-absorbers. It falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Springs.
[F16F 9/38] Covers for protection or appearance -- The subclass F16F 9/38 concerns protective or decorative covers that are applied to springs, vibration dampers, shock absorbers or similar movement-damping devices, shielding them from external influences or providing an aesthetic appearance. These covers are integral to the device's housing and may be made of metal, plastic or other materials suited to the operating environment.
[F16F 9/42] Cooling arrangements -- F16F 9/42 pertains to cooling arrangements for fluid-damped springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar movement-dampers that employ a fluid as the damping medium. The subclass includes designs that control fluid temperature through heat exchangers, cooling channels, external coolers or other thermal-management features integrated into the damping unit to preserve performance and reliability.
[F16F 9/43] Filling arrangements, e.g. for supply of gas -- The F16F 9/43 subclass covers devices and methods that provide a gas supply to fluid-filled springs, dampers or shock absorbers, including the arrangement of valves, channels or filling ports used to charge, replenish or regulate the gas within the damping element. It addresses the design of filling systems that ensure proper pressure and performance of the movement-damping component.
[F16F 9/432] via piston rod sealing or guiding means -- The F16F 9/432 subclass includes springs, vibration-dampers, shock absorbers, or comparable fluid-based movement dampers that incorporate a piston rod sealing or guiding mechanism to control fluid flow and ensure precise rod guidance during operation. This design improves sealing integrity and reduces friction in hydraulic or pneumatic damping systems.
[F16F 9/435] via opening in cylinder wall  F16F9/432  takes precedence -- This classification code pertains to springs and dampers that use a fluid for vibration or shock absorption, specifically those with an opening in the cylinder wall. However, it notes that a more specific subclass, F16F9/432, should be considered first if applicable.
[F16F 9/437] Drainage arrangements -- This subclass covers designs for draining fluid from springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar fluid-damped movement-damping devices. It includes drainage channels, ports, reservoirs, venting or other arrangements that allow the damping medium to be expelled or circulated to preserve performance. The code for these drainage arrangements is F16F 9/437 in the CPC.
[F16F 9/44] Means on or in the damper for manual or non-automatic adjustment -- This CPC class covers devices incorporated in fluid-damped springs, vibration dampers or shock absorbers that permit a user to manually adjust the damping level or set a non-automatic condition, such as adjustable valves, pistons or orifices within the damper. It is designated F16F 9/44.
[F16F 9/443] manually adjusted while the damper is fully retracted or extended in a non-operational mode by rotating mechanical means that have engaged between the piston and one end of the cylinder -- This subclass concerns fluid-based dampers whose damping force can be manually set while the piston is locked in either the fully retracted or fully extended position. Adjustment is achieved by rotating a mechanical element that engages between the piston and one cylinder end, allowing the user to change the damper setting in a non-operational mode.
[F16F 9/446] Adjustment of valve bias or pre-stress  F16F9/443  takes precedence -- F16F 9/446 covers springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar movement-damping devices that use a fluid or equivalent medium and includes methods for adjusting valve bias or applying pre-stress to control performance; the broader subclass F16F 9/443 is considered prior art for these features.
[F16F 9/46] allowing control from a distance -- This subclass covers fluid-damped springs, shock absorbers, or vibration-dampers that can be operated remotely, such as through wireless or wired signals, enabling adjustments of damping characteristics from a distance.
[F16F 9/461] characterised by actuation means -- The subclass F16F 9/461 covers springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar movement-dampers that incorporate an actuation means, such as a valve, piston, or electromagnetic device, to initiate or control the damping action of the fluid or equivalent medium. It specifically addresses designs where the fluid-based damping is triggered or modulated by a dedicated actuation mechanism.
[F16F 9/462] Rotary actuation means -- F16F 9/462 designates rotary actuation mechanisms used to operate or control springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or comparable fluid-damped movement-dampers. The class covers gears, cams, shafts or other rotating elements that convert rotary motion into the desired damping action.
[F16F 9/463] characterised by electrical connections -- F16F 9/463 refers to springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar fluid-damped movement-dampers that are characterised by having electrical connections incorporated, such as integrated wiring, terminals or contacts for monitoring, control or heating functions.
[F16F 9/464] Control of valve bias or pre-stress, e.g. electromagnetically  F16F9/465  takes precedence -- This classification code pertains to the control mechanisms for adjusting valve bias or pre-stress in fluid-based dampers, often using electromagnetic methods. It is part of a broader category dealing with springs and vibration-dampers that utilize fluids for damping effects.
[F16F 9/465] using servo control, the servo pressure being created by the flow of damping fluid, e.g. controlling pressure in a chamber downstream of a pilot passage  self-adjustment of damping  F16F9/50 -- F16F 9/465 covers fluid-based movement dampers that employ servo control, where the pressure generated by the damping fluid's flow is used to regulate a downstream chamber, enabling automatic self-adjustment of the damping force. The subclass includes mechanisms that modulate pressure via a pilot passage to achieve adaptive vibration-suppression.
[F16F 9/466] Throttling control, i.e. regulation of flow passage geometry  F16F9/464 ,  F16F9/465  take precedence -- F16F 9/466 covers throttling control devices that regulate the geometry of fluid flow passages in springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers or similar movement-dampers where a fluid provides the damping medium. It includes mechanisms such as variable-area valves, orifices or adjustable channels that modify flow resistance to adjust damping characteristics, and is subordinate to the broader categories F16F 9/464 and F16F 9/465.
[F16F 9/467] using rotary valves -- F16F 9/467 classifies fluid-damped springs, vibration or shock absorbers that employ rotary valves to control the flow of the damping medium. The rotary valve modulates fluid movement, adjusting damping characteristics during operation.
[F16F 9/468] controlling at least one bypass to main flow path -- F16F 9/468 refers to mechanisms that regulate at least one bypass channel to the primary flow path in fluid-damped springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers, allowing control of fluid circulation and damping characteristics.
[F16F 9/469] Valves incorporated in the piston  F16F9/467  takes precedence -- F16F 9/469 classifies pistons that incorporate one or more valves for controlling fluid flow within the piston body. Such integrated valves are used in hydraulic or pneumatic dampers, shock absorbers and similar movement-damping devices to adjust damping characteristics or to release trapped fluid. The subclass follows the broader category of fluid-damped springs and takes precedence over F16F 9/467.
[F16F 9/48] Arrangements for providing different damping effects at different parts of the stroke -- The class covers springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers that are designed to vary the damping force along the travel of the device, using mechanisms such as variable-orifice passages, multi-chamber arrangements or adaptive control to produce different resistance at different parts of the stroke. It includes fluid-based systems whose damping characteristics change as the piston moves.
[F16F 9/483] characterised by giving a particular shape to the cylinder, e.g. conical -- F16F 9/483 covers springs, vibration-dampers, shock-absorbers and similar devices in which the fluid-filled cylinder is given a particular profile, such as a conical shape, to alter the damping characteristics of the movement.
[F16F 9/486] comprising a pin or stem co-operating with an aperture, e.g. a cylinder-mounted stem co-operating with a hollow piston rod -- A spring or vibration-damper design that uses a pin or stem inserted into a matching aperture, for example a cylinder-mounted stem that engages a hollow piston rod, allowing controlled movement or shock absorption. This configuration is covered by CPC code F16F 9/486.
[F16F 9/49] Stops limiting fluid passage, e.g. hydraulic stops -- F16F 9/49 pertains to stops that limit or block the passage of fluid, commonly known as hydraulic stops. These devices are used in hydraulic or pneumatic systems to control fluid flow, pressure or movement, often as part of springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbing assemblies.
[F16F 9/50] Special means providing automatic damping adjustment, ; -- F16F 9/50 classifies springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers that include automatic means for adjusting the damping force. The adjustment may be effected by sensors, electronic controls, variable-orifice valves or fluid-property changes that respond to operating conditions.
[F16F 9/504] Inertia -sensitive means -- This subclass covers devices that detect or react to inertial forces, such as accelerometers or inertial switches, and are integrated with fluid-damped springs or shock-absorbers to adjust damping in response to motion.
[F16F 9/512] Means responsive to load action, on the damper or fluid pressure in the damper, -- The classification code F16F 9/512 pertains to components within springs and dampers that respond to changes in load or internal fluid pressure. These mechanisms are designed to adjust damping characteristics dynamically based on the forces acting upon them, enhancing control and performance in various mechanical systems.
[F16F 9/5123] responsive to the static or steady-state load on the damper -- F16F 9/5123 covers spring-type or fluid-damped movement absorbers that adjust their behavior based on a constant or slowly varying load. The class includes devices whose damping characteristics change in response to static or steady-state forces applied to the damper.
[F16F 9/5126] Piston, or piston-like valve elements  F16F9/504  takes precedence -- F16F 9/5126 pertains to piston or piston-like valve elements that act as springs, vibration dampers or shock absorbers using a fluid or an equivalent medium to provide damping. The subclass defines fluid-damped movement-control devices within the broader category of mechanical springs and engineering elements. It refines the earlier class F16F 9/504.
[F16F 9/516] resulting in the damping effects during contraction being different from the damping effects during extension -- A spring or fluid-based vibration damper or shock absorber whose resistance to motion changes between compression and extension falls under CPC F16F 9/516, a subclass of mechanical-engineering elements dealing with springs that use a fluid or equivalent damping medium and exhibit different damping effects during contraction versus extension.
[F16F 9/5165] by use of spherical valve elements or like free-moving bodies -- This subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification, F16F 9/5165, pertains to mechanical springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers that achieve damping by employing spherical valve components or other freely moving bodies within the fluid-filled or equivalent system. It falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and springs.
[F16F 9/52] in case of change of temperature -- F16F 9/52 covers springs, vibration dampers, shock absorbers or similar fluid-damped devices that incorporate features to compensate for temperature variations, ensuring consistent damping performance despite thermal changes.
[F16F 9/523] with coil or spiral of bimetallic elements being used to change flow cross-section -- This classification entry pertains to springs and dampers that use a fluid for vibration or shock absorption, specifically focusing on designs where the flow cross-section is altered by bimetallic coils or spirals.
[F16F 9/526] Self-adjustment of fluid springs -- The F16F 9/526 subclass concerns fluid springs that automatically modify their stiffness, preload or damping characteristics in response to changing load or motion. It includes designs that adjust fluid volume, pressure or flow through internal valves or chambers, allowing the spring to self-regulate without external actuation. The focus is on self-adjusting shock-absorbing or vibration-damping devices that use a fluid medium for movement control.
[F16F 9/53] Means for adjusting damping characteristics by varying fluid viscosity, e.g. electromagnetically -- This subclass covers devices that alter the damping performance of fluid-based springs or shock absorbers by changing the fluid's viscosity, for example through electromagnetic heating or field-controlled rheology. The adjustment provides variable resistance to motion in vibration-dampening systems.
[F16F 9/532] Electrorheological [ER] fluid dampers -- This subclass covers dampers that use an electrorheological fluid whose viscosity changes under an electric field to provide variable resistance to motion. The devices are used as springs, shock absorbers, or vibration control elements in mechanical systems. They fall under the broader category of fluid-based movement dampers in the CPC.
[F16F 9/535] Magnetorheological [MR] fluid dampers  springs comprising magnetorheological [MR] elastomers  F16F1/361 -- The class F16F 9/535 covers springs and vibration-dampers that use magnetorheological (MR) fluid or MR-elastomer as the damping medium. It includes devices where a magnetic field changes the fluid's viscosity to provide adjustable stiffness or shock absorption, such as MR-fluid dampers and springs made from MR-elastomer composites.
[F16F 9/537] specially adapted valves therefor -- F16F 9/537 covers valves that are specifically designed for use in fluid-damped devices such as springs, vibration dampers and shock absorbers. The entry includes pressure-relief, check or control valves integrated into the damping system to regulate fluid flow and thereby adjust the device's performance.
[F16F 9/54] Arrangements for attachment -- F16F 9/54 concerns the ways in which fluid-damped springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers are mounted to other parts. It covers brackets, clamps, fasteners, housings and other attachment structures that secure the damper, provide load transfer and maintain proper alignment within a machine.
[F16F 9/56] Means for adjusting the length of, or for locking, the spring or damper, e.g. at the end of the stroke -- This subclass covers devices that adjust or lock the displacement of fluid-damped springs, shock absorbers or comparable movement dampers, usually by a mechanism positioned at the extremes of travel. It includes length-adjusting screws, clamps, locking pins and similar features that set or retain the stroke length.
[F16F 9/58] Stroke limiting stops, e.g. arranged on the piston rod outside the cylinder -- F16F 9/58 covers stroke limiting stops placed on the piston rod outside the cylinder, which restrict piston travel in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders to protect the system and control motion.
[F16F 9/585] within the cylinder, in contact with working fluid -- This subgroup covers springs, vibration-dampers or shock-absorbers that are installed inside a cylinder and are in direct contact with the working fluid, such as hydraulic or pneumatic fluid, which serves as the damping or spring medium. It addresses designs where the fluid itself provides the movement-damping function within the confined cylindrical space.
[F16G] BELTS, CABLES, OR ROPES, PREDOMINANTLY USED FOR DRIVING PURPOSES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Belts, e.g. V-belts, cables, ropes or chains that are specially adapted for transferring power/force from an input point to an output point to move or drive at least one mechanical part.  The belts, cables, ropes or chains being either  formed into a loop that is intended to travel around an endless path and engage two or more pulleys, sprockets or similar rotating elements and transfer power/force while travelling along its path; or  an elongated element that is intended to travel backward or forward along a path to transfer power/force between one point along its length to another point along its length, e.g. from a drum to a load.  Chains, e.g. hauling- or hoisting-chains, that are not specially adapted for transferring power/force.  Fittings and attachments primarily intended to be connected to or used with the above types of belts, cables, ropes or chains. These include:  means for fastening ends of belts together to form closed loops, e.g. fastenings for conveyor belts or V-belts;  means for fastening cables or ropes to one another or to other objects;  caps or sleeves for fixing on cables or ropes;  means for coupling chains;  components of chains, e.g. shackles, swivels, chain joints, chain links or chain bushes;  hooks as integral parts of chains.
[F16G 1/00] Driving-belts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Belts adapted for transferring power/force from an input point to an output point to move or drive at least one mechanical part.  Belts being either formed into a loop that is intended to travel around an endless path and engage two or more pulleys, sprockets or similar rotating elements and transfer power/force while travelling along its path; or an elongated element that is intended to travel backward or forward along a path to transfer power/force between one point along its length to another point along its length, e.g. from a drum to a load.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Producing belts of plastics -> B29D29/00 - Endless conveyor belts -> B65G15/30 - Making driving belts of leather -> C14B9/00 - Testing of belts -> G01M13/023
[F16G 1/02] made of leather -- The CPC subclass F16G 1/02 covers driving-belts whose material is leather. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the group for belts, cables or ropes used for driving purposes, and defines leather as a permissible construction material for such power-transmission belts.

[F16G 11/025] Fastening means which engage a sleeve or the like fixed on the cable, e.g. caps  F16G11/143  and  F16G11/146  take precedence -- F16G 11/025 covers fastening devices that secure a sleeve or similar fitting fixed on a cable or rope, such as caps, to join cables or attach them to other components. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, belts, cables or ropes for driving purposes, and is superseded by the more specific groups F16G11/143 and F16G11/146.

[F16G 11/042] using solidifying liquid material forming a wedge -- The entry covers a fastening technique for cables or ropes in which a liquid material is placed in a gap and then solidifies, forming a wedge that locks the cable to the adjoining part or structure.
[F16G 11/044] friction clamps deforming the cable, wire, rope or cord -- F16G 11/044 covers friction clamps that secure cables, wires, ropes or cords by locally deforming the material to create a gripping frictional force. These devices provide a means of fastening the element to another part without additional hardware, often used in mechanical power-transmission systems where belts, cables or ropes drive machinery. The clamp's deformation ensures the cable is held firmly while allowing easy installation or removal.
[F16G 11/046] by bending the cable around a surface -- This subclass covers means for fastening cables or ropes by bending the cable around a surface, such as loops, wraps or hooks that secure the rope to another object.
[F16G 11/048] by moving a surface into the cable -- F16G 11/048 pertains to methods of securing cables or ropes by engaging them with a moving surface, typically for driving purposes in mechanical engineering. This technique involves the use of dynamic elements that press against or grip the cable to achieve a secure connection.

[F16G 11/101] deforming the cable by moving a part of the fastener -- F16G 11/101 covers fastening methods where a portion of the fastener is moved to deform a cable or rope, creating a secure hold without additional hardware. The deformation typically involves pressing, compressing, or shaping the cable to lock it in place.
[F16G 11/103] deforming the cable by bending it around or inserting it into the fastener -- F16G 11/103 covers fastening devices for cables, ropes or similar elements where the cable is deformed to secure it, typically by bending the strand around a fastener or inserting it into a holder. The classification applies to mechanisms that achieve a grip by shaping the cable rather than using separate clamps or knots, and is used for engineering solutions in mechanical drive systems.
[F16G 11/105] Clamps holding in one direction only -- Clamps that secure cables or ropes by applying force in a single direction, allowing a one-way hold on the line or on a supporting element, are used in drive-system assemblies to fasten and tension ropes or cables while preventing movement opposite the clamping direction.
[F16G 11/106] using a toothed surface -- F16G 11/106 concerns the use of a toothed surface in devices that fasten cables or ropes to one another or to other components, mainly within belt, cable or rope drive systems. The toothed profile engages with a matching element to secure the connection and transmit motion.
[F16G 11/108] using a ball or a cylinder -- The CPC entry F16G 11/108 covers devices that join cables or ropes by employing a spherical or cylindrical element as a coupling. The ball or cylinder acts as a connector, allowing the strands to be securely fastened together or to other components in belt or rope-driving systems.

[F16G 13/20] stiff -- F16G 13/20 relates to stiff chains used in power-transmission systems, where the links are designed to resist bending and maintain a relatively rigid shape. [F16G 9/04] made of rubber or plastics -- F16G 9/04 covers ropes or cables specially adapted for driving purposes that are made of rubber or plastic materials. These flexible members are intended to be driven by or to drive pulleys, gears or similar elements.
[F16H] GEARING -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings for conveying rotary motion:  Combination of gearings.  General details of gearings.  Control of gearings.  **Glossary:** - gearing: mechanical, hydraulic, electric or other means for transmitting mechanical motion or force - gearbox: housing of the gearing - toothed gearing: includes worm gearing and other gearing involving at least one wheel or sector provided with teeth or the equivalent, except gearing with chains or toothed belts, which is treated as friction gearing - conveying motion: includes transmitting energy, and means that the applied and resultant motions are of the same kind, though they may differ in, e.g. speed, direction or extent - rotary motion: implies that the motion may continue indefinitely - oscillating motion: moving about an axis to an extent which is limited by the construction of the gearing and which may exceed one revolution, the movement being alternately forwards and backwards during continued operation of the gearing - reciprocating motion: moving substantially in a straight line, the movement being alternately forwards and backwards during continued operation of the gearing - reversing or reversal: applied movement in one direction may produce a resultant movement in either of two opposed directions at will. Note: When reversing reciprocating motion, input rotary motion (which is defined as indefinitely continuous rotary motion) would cause an automatic reversal of the reciprocating motion. If the input rotational direction is changed in order to cause reversal of the reciprocating motion, the input motion is an oscillating motion (which is defined as alternately forward and backward rotary motion) - central gears: includes any gears whose axis is the main axis of the gearing, e.g. sun or ring gear - Ravigneaux set: a planetary gear set with at least three central gears, and conveying rotary motion between axially-spaced orbital gears. It comprises a long orbital gear consisting of two axially spaced orbital gears which are fixedly connected to each other. It comprises a further orbital gear which meshes with one of the orbital gears of the long orbital gear to form a pair of intermeshing orbital gears. All orbital gears are mounted on a common planet carrier and are considered as a single set of orbital gears. - creeping: the vehicle has come to a stop, the engine is at idle (i.e., there is no request by the operator for a higher engine speed/torque), but due to the rotation of various transmission components, the vehicle starts to move, and the vehicle operator has some control over movement with a brake - inching: the vehicle operator has some control, besides using a brake, over moving the vehicle by small degrees
[F16H 1/00] Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearing with fixed gear ratio using only gears with teeth.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Toothed gearing for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio of for reversing rotary motion -> F16H3/00
[F16H 1/003] Unidirectionally torque-transmitting toothed gearing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion in which torque may be transmitted from the input to the output in only one input rotation direction, e.g. a clockwise input rotation is possible whereas a counter-clockwise input rotation is blocked.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  Figure 1a illustrates an example in which clockwise rotation is possible.  1b.  Figure 1b illustrates an example in which counter clockwise rotation is blocked.  2a.  2b.  Figures 2a and 2b illustrate an example in which rotation is allowed in only one direction.
[F16H 1/006] the driving and driven axes being designed to assume variable positions relative to one another during operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a worm drive shaft (a) and driven shaft (d) which may assume variable positions to one another, i.e. worm drive shaft (a) may pivot around driven shaft (d).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a drive shaft (left outermost gear) and a driven shaft (right outermost gear) may assume variable positions to one another, i.e. gear train (252B) may pivot with respect to gear train (252A).
[F16H 1/02] without gears having orbital motion -- F16H 1/02 covers gear systems that transmit rotary motion without using conventional gears, relying instead on orbital motion mechanisms such as planetary or epicyclic arrangements. These devices achieve power transmission through rotating carriers or satellites rather than toothed gear pairs.
[F16H 1/04] involving only two intermeshing members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-orbital toothed gearing wherein no more than a total of two intermeshing members are used to convey rotary motion.
[F16H 1/06] with parallel axes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion, with fixed gear ratio, without gears having orbital motion, involving only two intermeshing members, with parallel axes.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a gearing having two parallel axes and involving only two intermeshing members (1) and (2). The latter are formed as spur gears.  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a gearing having two parallel axes and involving only two intermeshing members (1) and (2). The latter are formed as face gears.

[F16H 1/26] Special means compensating for misalignment of axes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Special means compensating for misalignment of axes of toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion, with fixed gear ratio, and without gears having orbital motion. [F16H 1/28] with gears having orbital motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion, with fixed gear ratio, with gears having orbital motion.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates an orbital or planetary gearing comprising sun gear (4), orbital or planet gears (5), ring or internal gear (6) and planet carrier (7). Input shaft (1) is connected to sun gear (4), output shaft (2) is connected to planet carrier (7), and ring gear (6) is fixed to housing (3). Planet gears (5) rotate around their own axis. In addition, due to rotation of the planet carrier (7), planet gears (5) also orbit relative to and around rotating sun gear (4), i.e. they have orbital motion.
[F16H 1/2809] with means for equalising the distribution of load on the planet gears -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion, with fixed gear ratio, with gears having orbital motion, with means for equalising the distribution of load on the planet gears.

[F16H 1/30] in which an orbital gear has an axis crossing the main axis of the gearing and has helical teeth or is a worm -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion, with fixed gear ratio, with gears having orbital motion, in which an orbital gear is a worm; or in which the orbital gear has helical teeth and an axis crossing the main axis of the gearing.  This area also includes gearing in which the orbital gear has helical teeth and where the axis of the orbital gear is set at an angle relative to the main axis. Figures 2a and 2b illustrate a planetary gearing comprising three orbital gears (16), each of which has helical teeth and an axis (17) set at an angle α relative to the main axis (18).
[F16H 13/00] Other friction gearing for conveying rotary motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearing where rotary motion is transferred with a fixed ratio by the friction of the surfaces of members in the gearing, where the surfaces are pressed to each other.
[F16H 13/02] without members having orbital motion -- F16H 13/02 covers friction gears that transmit rotary motion without any of the gear members moving in an orbital (planetary) path, such as spur, helical or bevel gears where the teeth engage directly and each member rotates around its own axis only. This subclass is part of the broader category of other friction gearing used for conveying rotational power.
[F16H 13/04] with balls or with rollers acting in a similar manner -- F16H 13/04 covers gearing devices that transmit rotary motion by means of balls or rollers that act like bearing elements, as opposed to toothed gears. It applies to friction gear arrangements where the rolling elements replace gear teeth to provide smooth power transfer. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing.
[F16H 13/06] with members having orbital motion -- A gear system in which the gear elements move on orbital paths rather than simply rotating about fixed axes, such as planetary or epicyclic arrangements where carriers or gears revolve around a central sun gear while transmitting rotary motion. This type of friction gearing is used to convey rotational power with compact size and variable speed ratios.
[F16H 13/08] with balls or with rollers acting in a similar manner -- This subclass covers gearing arrangements that transmit rotary motion by means of balls or rollers acting as friction elements, such as ball or roller drives and similar devices. It falls under other friction gearing within mechanical engineering.
[F16H 13/10] Means for influencing the pressure between the members -- A means for influencing the pressure between the gear members in other friction gearing that transmits rotary motion, such as devices that adjust contact force, apply loading springs, or use hydraulic or pneumatic pressure to control slip and efficiency. This falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, and the specific CPC subclass F16H 13/10.
[F16H 13/12] by magnetic forces -- The CPC subclass F16H 13/12 addresses gear arrangements that convey rotary motion by magnetic forces rather than direct tooth contact, fitting within the "other friction gearing for conveying rotary motion" group of mechanical-engineering elements. It captures magnetic-driven gearing concepts used to transmit torque without physical mesh.
[F16H 13/14] for automatically varying the pressure mechanically -- F16H 13/14 covers friction-gear arrangements that transmit rotary motion while automatically varying the pressure between the gear elements by purely mechanical means, such as springs, cams or hydraulic actuators, to adapt load or speed during operation.
[F16H 1/32] in which the central axis of the gearing lies inside the periphery of an orbital gear -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion, with fixed gear ratio, with gears having orbital motion, in which the central axis of the gearing lies inside the periphery of an orbital gear, e.g. eccentric gearing or cycloidal gearing.
[F16H 1/321] the orbital gear being nutating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a planetary gear set in which orbital gears (9) and (9') are nutating.  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a planetary gear set in which orbital gears (9) and (11) are nutating.
[F16H 1/34] involving gears essentially having intermeshing elements other than involute or cycloidal teeth -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion, with fixed gear ratio, with gears having orbital motion, involving gears essentially having intermeshing elements other than involute or cycloidal teeth.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a planetary transmission, with fixed gear ratio, having intermeshing elements, i.e. teeth (7), (8) and (9), which are neither involute nor cycloidal teeth.  2a.  Figure 2a illustrates an eccentric gearing, with fixed gear ratio, having intermeshing elements, i.e. teeth in form of pins (36), which are neither involute nor cycloidal teeth.  2b.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion, with fixed gear ratio, with gears having orbital motion, in which an orbital gear has an axis crossing the main axis of the gearing, either directly or in a projected plane, and has helical teeth or is a worm -> F16H1/30
[F16H 1/36] with two central gears coupled by intermeshing orbital gears -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion, with fixed gear ratio, with gears having orbital motion, with two central gears coupled by intermeshing orbital gears.
