[F16H 1/46] Systems consisting of a plurality of gear trains each with orbital gears, -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion, with fixed gear ratio, with gears having orbital motion, consisting of a plurality of gear trains each with orbital gears and having three or more central gears.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a planetary transmission, with fixed gear ratio, comprising two gear trains (1) and (2) having altogether four central gears, i.e. sun gears (S1) and (S2) and ring gears (R1) and (R2). First gear train (1) has a single orbital gear (P1). Second gear train (2) has a pair of intermeshing orbital gears (P2) and (P3).
[F16H 1/48] Special means compensating for misalignment of axes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion, with fixed gear ratio, with gears having orbital motion, e.g. eccentric gearing or cycloidal gearing, and with special means compensating for misalignment of axes, e.g. for equalising distribution of load on the face width of the teeth.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a planetary transmission, with a fixed gear ratio, comprising two gear trains (101) and (103), each respectively comprising one set of orbital gears (109) and (125). Figure 1a illustrates a misalignment of the main axes of gear trains (101) and (103). Sun gear (111), sun gear shaft (129) and planet carrier (123) of Figure 1a are replaced with sun gear (201), sun gear shaft (207) and planet carrier (213) in order to compensate for the misalignment which are illustrated in Figure 1b. This results in a more equally distributed load on the face width of the teeth of the sun gear (201), ring gear (105) and planet gears (109).
[F16H 15/00] Gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio, or for reversing rotary motion, by friction between rotary members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transmission layout of transmissions where rotary motion is transferred with a variable ratio by the friction of the surfaces of members where the surfaces are pressed to each other.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Control of change-speed or reversing-gearings conveying rotary motion -> F16H59/00 - F16H63/00
[F16H 15/01] characterised by the use of a magnetisable powder or liquid as friction medium between the rotary members -- F16H 15/01 covers gearings that transmit rotary motion with a variable ratio or reverse direction by friction between rotating members, where the friction medium is a magnetisable powder or liquid. The use of the magnetisable medium enables magnetic control of the torque transmission.
[F16H 15/02] without members having orbital motion -- F16H 15/02 covers friction gearings that transmit rotary motion with a variable ratio or reverse direction, where the contacting members slide against each other without any orbital motion of one part relative to the other. These gearings rely on direct surface contact rather than teeth or planetary mechanisms.
[F16H 15/04] Gearings providing a continuous range of gear ratios -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Only transmission layout of conveying rotary motion with a continuously variable ratio by friction between rotary members that are not using flexible endless members and where the rotary members are not in planetary motion, examples of this group are toroidal transmission or friction ring transmissions.
[F16H 15/06] in which a member A of uniform effective diameter mounted on a shaft may co-operate with different parts of a member B -- F16H 15/06 concerns gear mechanisms where a uniformly sized member A mounted on a shaft can engage different portions of a member B, allowing variable transmission ratios or reversal of rotary motion through friction between the components.
[F16H 15/08] in which the member B is a disc with a flat or approximately flat friction surface -- F16H 15/08 covers gearings that transmit rotary motion by friction where the driven member B is a disc with a flat or essentially flat friction surface, enabling variable gear ratios or reversal of rotation in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16H 15/10] in which the axes of the two members cross or intersect -- F16H 15/10 is a CPC subclass for gearings that transmit rotary motion by friction between two members whose axes cross or intersect, enabling variable speed ratios or reversal of rotation through intersecting shafts.
[F16H 15/12] in which one or each member is duplicated, e.g. for obtaining better transmission, for lessening the reaction forces on the bearings -- This subclass covers gear arrangements in which one or more gear members are duplicated to achieve a smoother power transmission or to reduce reaction forces on the bearings, improving overall efficiency and load distribution.
[F16H 15/14] in which the axes of the members are parallel or approximately parallel -- This CPC subclass covers gearings that transmit rotary motion by friction between members whose axes are parallel or approximately parallel, providing a variable gear ratio or the ability to reverse rotation. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → gearing.
[F16H 15/16] in which the member B has a conical friction surface -- The CPC subclass F16H 15/16 covers gearings that transmit rotary motion by friction where the driven member B features a conical friction surface, enabling variable gear ratios or reversal of rotation. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 15/18] externally -- F16H 15/18 pertains to gearings that convey rotary motion with a variable gear ratio or reverse it through friction between external rotary members. This classification is specific to mechanical engineering and focuses on the design and function of gearing systems where external surfaces interact to achieve the desired motion control.
[F16H 15/20] co-operating with the outer rim of the member A, which is perpendicular or nearly perpendicular to the friction surface of the member B -- F16H 15/20 covers friction-based gearings in which the outer rim of one rotating member (A) engages the friction surface of another member (B) at an angle that is perpendicular or almost perpendicular to that surface, enabling variable or reversing rotary motion transmission. This sub-class falls under the mechanical engineering hierarchy for gearing elements.
[F16H 15/22] the axes of the members being parallel or approximately parallel -- F16H 15/22 covers gearings that transmit rotary motion by friction between rotating parts, where the axes of the engaged members are parallel or nearly parallel. It applies to mechanisms that provide a variable gear ratio or reverse motion without meshing teeth, falling under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearings.
[F16H 15/24] internally -- F16H 15/24 covers gearings that convey rotary motion with a variable gear ratio or reverse it through friction between rotary members, specifically focusing on internal configurations.
[F16H 15/26] in which the member B has a spherical friction surface centered on its axis of revolution -- F16H 15/26 is a Cooperative Patent Classification entry for gearings that transmit rotary motion by friction between rotating parts, where the second member (B) features a spherical friction surface whose centre lies on its axis of rotation, allowing variable or reverse motion.
[F16H 15/28] with external friction surface -- F16H 15/28 designates gearings that transmit rotary motion using an external friction surface, enabling variable speed ratios or reversal of direction. Such devices typically employ belts, rollers or disks that press against an outer surface of a driven member to transfer power through friction.
[F16H 15/30] with internal friction surface -- F16H 15/30 covers gearings that transmit rotary motion by friction between rotating parts and include an internal friction surface. These devices provide a variable gear ratio or reverse motion without teeth, using contact between an internal component and a rotating member to control speed and direction.
[F16H 15/32] in which the member B has a curved friction surface formed as a surface of a body of revolution generated by a curve which is neither a circular arc centered on its axis of revolution nor a straight line -- This CPC subclass F16H 15/32 covers friction gearings in which the driven member B has a curved friction surface formed as a surface of revolution generated by a curve that is neither a circular arc centered on its axis of revolution nor a straight line. It falls under mechanical engineering - engineering elements and units - gearing.
[F16H 15/34] with convex friction surface -- F16H 15/34 refers to gearings that transmit rotary motion by friction between rotating members, specifically those whose friction surface is convex, and are used for varying or reversing gear ratios.
[F16H 15/36] with concave friction surface, e.g. a hollow toroid surface -- F16H 15/36 covers gearings that transmit rotary motion by friction using a concave friction surface, such as a hollow toroid shape. These devices are employed for conveying rotary motion with a variable gear ratio or for reversing motion.
[F16H 15/38] with two members B having hollow toroid surfaces opposite to each other, the member or members A being adjustably mounted between the surfaces -- F16H 15/38 pertains to friction gearings for variable speed or reversing rotary motion, specifically those featuring two members with opposing hollow toroid surfaces, where at least one member is adjustably positioned between these surfaces.
[F16H 15/40] in which two members co-operative by means of balls, or rollers of uniform effective diameter, not mounted on shafts -- This subclass covers gearings that transmit motion by rolling contact between two members using balls or rollers of uniform effective diameter that are not mounted on shafts, such as ball-or roller-drives and similar friction-free transmissions.
[F16H 15/42] in which two members co-operate by means of rings or by means of parts of endless flexible members pressed between the first mentioned members -- F16H 15/42 covers gearings in which two rotating elements are linked by rings or by sections of an endless flexible member that is pressed between the elements, providing variable or reversible transmission of motion. This arrangement is used in mechanisms where frictional contact between the members transmits power, such as in continuously variable transmissions or reversible drive units.
[F16H 15/44] in which two members of non-uniform effective diameter directly co-operate with one another -- F16H 15/44 covers gearings where two rotating members with non-uniform effective diameters mesh directly, transmitting motion by friction to obtain a variable gear ratio or reversal. Such arrangements are employed in compact, continuously variable speed drives such as conveyors and other power-transmission devices.
[F16H 15/46] Gearings providing a discontinuous or stepped range of gear ratios -- F16H 15/46 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers gear systems that offer a set of distinct, non-continuous gear ratio options for transmitting rotary motion, typically used for stepping through different speed settings.
[F16H 15/48] with members having orbital motion -- **Glossary:** - orbital or planet members: members having orbital motion, i.e. which rotate around their own axis and orbit relative to a central member
[F16H 15/50] Gearings providing a continuous range of gear ratios -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a continuously variable friction gearing including input shaft (124), output shaft (170), and orbital friction balls (160).
[F16H 15/503] in which two members co-operate by means of balls or rollers of uniform effective diameter, not mounted on shafts -- A gear arrangement in which two rotating members transmit motion by means of uniform-diameter balls or rollers that are not fixed to shafts, using frictional contact to achieve variable speed ratios or reverse rotation. This type of gearing relies on rolling elements rather than toothed wheels.
[F16H 15/506] in which two members of non-uniform effective diameter directly co-operate with one another -- F16H 15/506 covers gearings where two members with non-uniform effective diameters directly cooperate, using frictional contact to transmit rotary motion with a variable gear ratio or to reverse the motion.
[F16H 15/52] in which a member of uniform effective diameter mounted on a shaft may co-operate with different parts of another member -- F16H 15/52 concerns gearings that transmit rotary motion with a variable ratio or reversal by using a shaft-mounted member of uniform effective diameter that engages different zones of another member, typically via frictional contact, to change the transmission characteristics.
[F16H 15/54] in which two members co-operate by means of rings or by means of parts of endless flexible members pressed between the first-mentioned members -- This subclass covers gear mechanisms where two rotating members transmit motion by means of rings or sections of an endless flexible member that are pressed between them, allowing variable gear ratios or reversal of rotary motion.
[F16H 15/56] Gearings providing a discontinuous or stepped range of gear ratios -- Gearings that offer a series of distinct, non-continuous gear ratios, allowing the output speed to be changed in stepped increments. These mechanisms typically use selectable gear sets or clutching devices to shift between predefined ratios, providing reversible or variable transmission of rotary motion.
[F16H 19/00] Gearings comprising essentially only toothed gears or friction members and not capable of conveying indefinitely-continuing rotary motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings for converting limited rotary movement, e.g. oscillation, into another rotary movement or a limited rotary movement into reciprocating movement or vice versa, e.g. by using flexible friction means, or rack and pinion mechanisms.  Gearings for converting reciprocating movement into another reciprocating movement, e.g. by flexible means.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gearings with intermittently-driving members -> F16H27/00 - F16H31/00
[F16H 19/001] for conveying reciprocating or limited rotary motion -- F16H 19/001 concerns gearings made solely of toothed gears or friction members that are designed to transmit motion that is reciprocating or limited to a finite number of revolutions, rather than providing continuous rotary drive. This subclass captures mechanisms such as oscillating gear trains, rack-and-pinion arrangements for back-and-forth movement, and other gear-based devices where rotation is intentionally intermittent or constrained.
[F16H 19/003] comprising a flexible member -- F16H 19/003 covers gearings that include a flexible member such as a belt, rope, chain or other deformable element to transmit torque between shafts. These arrangements are used when continuous rotary motion is not required and the flexible component provides power transmission by tension or deformation.
[F16H 19/005] for conveying oscillating or limited rotary motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a friction gearing comprising rollers (8) and (9) attached to belts (10) and (11). The belts (10) and (11) allow the gearing to convey limited rotary motion, i.e. oscillating motion.
[F16H 19/006] for converting reciprocating motion into another reciprocating motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a friction gearing comprising input belt (6), output belt (3) and pulley (12). The gearing converts the reciprocating input motion of belt (6) into a reciprocating output motion of belt (3).
[F16H 19/02] for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion -- F16H 19/02 lies in the mechanical-engineering section under engineering elements and units, gearing, and specifically gearings made essentially of toothed gears or friction members that cannot transmit indefinitely continuous rotary motion. It covers arrangements that convert rotary motion into reciprocating motion or vice versa.
[F16H 19/025] comprising a friction shaft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a transmission comprising gears (11) and shaft (22), which frictionally engage. Rotation of shaft (22) results in an axial movement of the carrier (24).
[F16H 19/04] comprising a rack -- F16H 19/04 designates gearings that incorporate a rack, a straight toothed element that engages a rotating gear to produce linear motion. Such gearings consist essentially of toothed gears or friction members and cannot transmit continuous rotary motion.
[F16H 19/043] for converting reciprocating movement in a continuous rotary movement or  vice versa , e.g. by opposite racks engaging intermittently for a part of the stroke -- F16H 19/043 covers devices that transform a linear back-and-forth motion into a steady rotary movement, or the reverse, typically using a pair of racks that alternately engage the drive during part of the stroke.
[F16H 19/06] comprising endless flexible member -- F16H 19/06 designates gearings that transmit rotary motion by means of an endless flexible member such as a belt, chain or rope, rather than toothed gears. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → gearing and applies to systems where a continuous loop conveys power between shafts.
[F16H 19/0604] with means to double or half the stroke of the reciprocating member -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanisms including flexible members, where the output movement is half or double compared with the movement of the input.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a transmission including rotary motor (1) and output (6). Motor (1) drives frame (3) along a linear track (5). The frame is coupled to output (6) via belts (7), (7'). Movement of frame (3) along a distance (1/2) creates movement of output (6) along a distance (l).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a transmission including piston (2) and output (11). Piston (2) provides input to belt (9) by movement back and forth in cylinder (3). Belt (9) engages rollers (10) and is connected to output (11), such that movement of the piston (2) is doubled at output (11).
[F16H 19/0618] the flexible member, e.g. cable, being wound on a drum or thread for creating axial movement parallel to the drum or thread -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanisms where the on-winding and off-winding on a drum or thread create axial movement parallel to the drum or thread. Winding and unwinding of thread (29) converts the rotary motion of input (11) to axial movement of output (13).
[F16H 19/0622] for converting reciprocating movement into oscillating movement and  vice versa , the reciprocating movement is perpendicular to the axis of oscillation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanisms where oscillating movement is converted into reciprocating movement perpendicular to the axis of oscillation or vice versa, e.g. by on-winding and off-winding a flexible member on a drum or pulley. The axial movement of carriage (2) is perpendicular to the axis of the stepped motor (7).

[F16H 19/08] for interconverting rotary motion and oscillating motion -- F16H 19/08 classifies mechanisms that convert continuous rotary motion into oscillating or reciprocating motion, and the reverse, using gearings composed mainly of toothed gears or friction elements. It includes devices such as cranks, cams, levers and similar linkages that transmit motion without providing indefinite rotary transmission.
[F16H 2001/2872] comprising three central gears, i.e. ring or sun gear, engaged by at least one common orbital gear mounted on an idling carrier -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Planetary gearing having a fixed gear ratio and including three central gears which are all engaged with the same planet gear, and the planet gear is mounted on an idling carrier.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a planetary gearing with a fixed gear ratio and including three central gears, i.e. sun gear (2) and two ring gears (3) and (4), which are all engaged by a common orbital gear (6) mounted on carrier (5). Carrier (5) is not connected to any shaft and therefore idles. In other words, carrier (5) constitutes an "idling carrier".

[F16H 2003/0807] with gear ratios in which the power is transferred by axially coupling idle gears to each other -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates an eight-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion, comprising idle gears (5) and (6) which are axially couplable to each other by shift element (I), which changes the gear ratio.  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion, including idle gears (33) and (34) which may be axially coupled to each other by engaging clutch (38), in order to transfer power.  **Glossary:** - winding transmission: a transmission including a gear ratio that is established by using multiple gear pairs in the transmission. For example, a transmission including a 1st gear ratio achieved by using more than one of the other existing gear pairs. In this 1 st gear ratio, torque is wound through the transmission via the several gear pairs. Thereby, a separate gear plane for the 1st gear ratio is not necessary. This concept is often applied to the 1st gear and the reverse gear. These gears are usually used only during short periods, such that the reduced efficiency by using several gear pairs instead of only one gear pair can be neglected. - winding gear ratio: a speed, e.g. 1 st speed, which is achieved by winding torque through a winding transmission
[F16H 2003/0811] using unsynchronised clutches -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a four-speed transmission having an input shaft (1) and a coaxial output shaft (3). It uses unsynchronised clutches (9), (11) and (12).
[F16H 2003/0815] using torque sharing, i.e. engaging two gear ratios simultaneously to transfer large torque, e.g. using one slipping clutch -- F16H 2003/0815 covers power-transmission arrangements that share torque by engaging two gear ratios at the same time, often using a slipping clutch to distribute load and allow large torque transfer. It pertains to gearboxes or similar devices that deliberately superimpose gear engagements for torque-sharing purposes.
[F16H 2003/0818] comprising means for power-shifting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a multi-speed transmission comprising means for power-shifting, by using bypass clutches (51) and (52).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a multi-speed transmission comprising means for power-shifting, by using friction clutches (CT1) to (CT6).
[F16H 2003/0822] characterised by the arrangement of at least one reverse gear -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a six-speed-transmission comprising a reverse shaft (48), two reverse gears (42) and (46) and a reverse clutch (44).
[F16H 2003/0826] wherein at least one gear on the input shaft, or on a countershaft is used for two different forward gear ratios -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  1c.  1d.  Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 1d illustrate a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion. Gear (Lo) on the output shaft is used for the (1 st ) and (3 rd ) forward speeds and gear (2) on the input shaft is used for the (2 nd ) and (6 th ) forward speeds.
[F16H 2003/0931] each countershaft having an output gear meshing with a single common gear on the output shaft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears, that can be disengaged from their shafts, with two or more countershafts, each countershaft having an output gear meshing with a single common gear on the output shaft.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission comprises an input shaft (10), output shaft (11) and two countershafts (12) and (13). Countershafts (12) and (13) comprise output gears (26) and (33), respectively, meshing with a single common gear (27) on the output shaft.
[F16H 2003/0933] with coaxial countershafts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears, that can be disengaged from their shafts, with two or more countershafts, with coaxial countershafts.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a five-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission comprises an input shaft (1) and an output shaft (4) as well as two coaxial countershafts (2) and (5).
[F16H 2003/0935] with multiple countershafts comprising only one idle gear and one gear fixed to the countershaft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears, that can be disengaged from their shafts, with two or more countershafts, in which all or two or more of its countershafts comprising only one idle gear and one gear fixed to the respective countershaft.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission comprises four countershafts (28), (13), (17) and (22). Countershafts (28), (13) and (22) each include only one idle gear and one gear fixed to the respective countershaft. It is noted that reverse countershaft (R), which is used for reversal only, is not counted as a countershaft, per the Note in F16H3/087 .
[F16H 2003/0936] with multiple countershafts comprising only two idle gears and one gear fixed to the countershaft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears, that can be disengaged from their shafts, with two or more countershafts, in which all or two or more of its countershafts comprising only two idle gears and one gear fixed to the respective countershaft.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission comprises input shaft (15), output shaft (38), and five countershafts (16), (17), (18), (19) and (37), each of which comprises only two idle gears and one gear fixed to the respective countershaft. It is noted that reverse countershaft (39), which is used for reversal only, is not counted as a countershaft, per the Note in F16H3/087 .
[F16H 2003/0938] with multiple gears on the input shaft directly meshing with respective gears on the output shaft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears, that can be disengaged from their shafts, with two or more countershafts, with multiple gears on the input shaft directly meshing with respective gears on the output shaft.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission comprises an input shaft (Sm), an output shaft (Sc), and two countershafts (Ss1) and (Ss2). Gears (31), (48), and (50) on input shaft (Sm) are directly meshing with respective gears (32), (52), and (53) on output shaft (Sc). It is noted that differential casing (26) is not considered an output shaft.
[F16H 2003/123] using a brake -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts, with means for synchronisation not incorporated in the clutches, using a brake.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission comprises a brake (26) which is engaged during upshifts in order to reduce the speed of input shaft (2).
[F16H 2003/442] comprising two or more sets of orbital gears arranged in a single plane -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Variable or reverse gearing including two or more orbital gear sets arranged in or near a single plane, e.g. in a stacked formation radially outward from the gearing main axis.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a multi-speed transmission including orbital gear set (P2) which is stacked radially outward of orbital gear set (P1).
[F16H 2003/445] without permanent connection between the input and the set of orbital gears -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a two-speed transmission including orbital gears (7a) and (7b), i.e. gears having orbital motion. Due to clutches (12a) and (12b) there is no permanent connection to input shaft (2).
[F16H 2003/447] without permanent connection between the set of orbital gears and the output -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a two-speed transmission including orbital gears (7a) and (7b), i.e. gears having orbital motion. Due to clutches (12a) and (12b) there is no permanent connection to output shaft (14).
[F16H 2007/026] with belts running in a mist of oil -- The subclass covers drive or transmission arrangements in which power-transmitting belts are operated within a fine mist of oil for lubrication and cooling. It applies to belt-driven mechanisms where the belt runs continuously through an oil-mist environment, often to reduce wear and heat in high-speed or high-load equipment.
[F16H 2007/0802] Actuators for final output members -- F16H 2007/0802 classifies actuators that directly drive final output members such as valve stems, pistons or flaps, converting hydraulic, pneumatic, electric or mechanical energy into the movement required to open, close or modulate a flow-control device. It covers the design, power source and coupling of these end-effectors.
[F16H 2007/0804] Leaf springs -- Leaf springs are multi-layered, curved steel plates used in vehicle suspension to absorb shocks and support loads. They flex under weight, providing a simple, durable system for maintaining ride height and handling. In the CPC, the code F16H 2007/0804 designates inventions that specifically concern the construction and mounting of leaf springs.
[F16H 2007/0806] Compression coil springs -- F16H 2007/0806 designates compression coil springs, which are helical metal springs intended to resist axial loads and store mechanical energy when compressed. They are employed in a wide range of applications such as automotive suspensions, industrial machinery, and consumer products for cushioning, support or force-biasing.
[F16H 2007/0808] Extension coil springs -- F16H 2007/0808 in the Cooperative Patent Classification designates extension coil springs, which are helical springs designed to resist tension by extending under load. They are used in mechanisms where a pulling force is needed, such as suspension systems, valve actuators, or tensioning devices.
[F16H 2007/081] Torsion springs -- F16H 2007/081 pertains to torsion springs, which store mechanical energy by twisting around an axis and release it as rotational force. The subclass includes various designs such as helical, coil and leaf torsion springs, their manufacturing methods, mounting arrangements and typical applications in machinery, automotive and consumer devices.
[F16H 2007/0812] Fluid pressure -- F16H 2007/0812 covers inventions that generate, transmit, or control power by means of fluid pressure, such as hydraulic or pneumatic actuators, pumps, valves and related control mechanisms.
[F16H 2007/0814] with valves opening on surplus pressure -- F16H 2007/0814 classifies fluid-power devices, mainly pumps or compressors, that use a valve which opens automatically when the internal pressure exceeds a preset level. The surplus-pressure actuation allows excess fluid to be released or redirected, providing pressure regulation, safety relief or flow control in hydraulic and pneumatic systems.
[F16H 2007/0817] with means for venting unwanted gas -- F16H 2007/0817 covers pumps that incorporate a venting mechanism to release unwanted gas from the working chamber or fluid circuit. The venting means may be a valve, passage or porous element that allows gas to escape while retaining liquid, improving pump performance and preventing cavitation or pressure buildup.
[F16H 2007/0819] Rubber or other elastic materials -- The CPC entry F16H 2007/0819 deals with rubber or other elastic materials used as the main component of springs, shock absorbers, dampers, cushions and similar elastic elements. It encompasses inventions that employ these materials to provide flexibility, vibration isolation or resilient mounting in mechanical devices.
[F16H 2007/0821] working with gravity -- The CPC subclass F16H 2007/0821 covers machines and devices that exploit gravitational force to perform work, such as gravity-driven lifts, escalators, conveyors, or material-handling systems that move loads by falling, rolling or sliding under gravity. It includes arrangements where gravity is the primary source of motion rather than motors or external power.
[F16H 2007/0823] Electric actuators -- The CPC entry F16H 2007/0823 covers electric actuators, devices that transform electrical energy into linear or rotary motion for operating valves, pumps, fans or similar mechanisms. It includes motor-driven, electromagnetic, piezoelectric and other electrically powered actuating systems used to control fluid flow or mechanical motion. This subclass groups all patent documents relating to the design, construction and application of such electric actuation technologies.
[F16H 2007/0825] influenced by other actuators of output members -- The entry relates to mechanisms in which the motion or force of an output member is altered by the action of other actuators, such as linked gears, cams, hydraulic or pneumatic circuits, or electronic controls that coordinate multiple drives to achieve synchronized or conditional operation.
[F16H 2007/084] having vibration damping characteristics dependent on the moving direction of the tensioner -- Covers tensioning devices whose vibration-damping performance varies with the direction in which the tensioner moves, providing asymmetric damping to control belt or chain oscillations.
[F16H 2007/0842] Mounting or support of tensioner -- F16H 2007/0842 falls under CPC section F for pipes, hoses and related fittings, subclass 16H covering tensioning devices. The subgroup specifies designs that mount or support a tensioner, such as brackets, frames or housings that secure the tensioning mechanism in hydraulic, pneumatic or mechanical systems. It addresses the arrangement of the tensioner to maintain proper tension on cables, belts or flexible lines.
[F16H 2007/0844] Mounting elements essentially within boundaries of final output members -- The CPC subclass F16H 2007/0844 covers mounting elements whose principal parts are situated inside the outer limits of the finished product they serve, such as fasteners, brackets or fixtures that are integrated within the shape of the final output member rather than projecting outward. It applies to designs where the mounting hardware is essentially contained within the product's boundaries.
[F16H 2007/0846] comprising a mechanical stopper -- The CPC group F16H 2007/0846 covers hydraulic or pneumatic components that incorporate a mechanical stopper to limit the travel of a piston, valve element or similar part, thereby preventing over-travel and improving safety.
[F16H 2007/0851] Wedges -- F16H 2007/0851 covers wedges, simple mechanical elements that convert a force applied in one direction into a force in another direction, typically used in presses, jacks and clamping devices to lift, spread or secure workpieces.
[F16H 2007/0853] Ratchets -- F16H 2007/0853 covers ratchet mechanisms that permit movement in one direction while preventing reverse motion, typically using a toothed wheel and a pawl. The class includes designs for hand tools, drives and safety devices where a controlled, non-backward movement is required.
[F16H 2007/0855] comprising a clip member engaging with the rack teeth -- The code F16H 2007/0855 covers gear-rack assemblies that incorporate a clip element which engages the rack's teeth, providing a secure, positive connection between the rack and its driving or driven component. It is used in transmission or positioning mechanisms where the clip retains the rack while allowing relative motion.
[F16H 2007/0857] Screw mechanisms -- F16H 2007/0857 classifies screw mechanisms that convert rotary motion into linear motion via threaded components such as leadscrews, ball screws, nuts, bolts or similar assemblies. The subclass includes devices where the screw action is used for positioning, driving or fastening in machines, actuators and other mechanical systems. It covers the design, construction and functional aspects of these thread-based mechanisms.
[F16H 2007/0859] Check valves -- F16H 2007/0859 covers check valves, a type of fluid power valve that permits flow in one direction and automatically shuts when the pressure gradient reverses. These devices are used to prevent backflow in hydraulic, pneumatic, and water systems and may include swing, ball, diaphragm, or poppet designs.
[F16H 2007/0861] comprising means for sensing tensioner position -- F16H 2007/0861 designates hoisting or cable-tensioning devices that incorporate sensors to determine the position of the tensioner. The classification covers mechanisms where the tensioner's travel or setting is sensed for monitoring, control or safety purposes, typically in lifting, winch or cable-driving systems.
[F16H 2007/0863] Finally actuated members, e.g. constructional details thereof -- F16H 2007/0863 covers members that move to a final, locked or secured position after being actuated, such as bolts, latches, or lockable elements, and the structural details of those components. The classification includes the design, mounting, and construction features that enable the member to remain in its end position without continuous power or force. It is applied to mechanical fastening and safety devices where a one-time or single-stroke motion achieves a permanent state.
[F16H 2007/0865] Pulleys -- F16H 2007/0865 covers pulleys, the wheel or drum used to change direction or magnitude of a force in a belt or rope drive system. It includes designs, manufacturing, and applications of single and multiple pulley arrangements.
[F16H 2007/0868] comprising means for changing working diameter of pulley -- F16H 2007/0868 covers pulley devices that incorporate mechanisms for varying the effective working diameter of the pulley, such as adjustable, conical or continuously variable sheaves, cams or expanders, allowing the speed or torque transmitted to be altered during operation.
[F16H 2007/087] Sprockets -- F16H 2007/087 covers sprockets, the toothed wheels that engage chain links in chain-driven systems, including their geometry, mounting arrangements and related manufacturing features. It is part of CPC subclass F16H for gearing and transmission elements.
[F16H 2007/0872] Sliding members -- F16H 2007/0872 covers sliding members used in fluid-control devices, such as valves and gates that move linearly to open, close or regulate flow. It includes sliding blocks, plates, pistons and similar components that provide a sealing or throttling function by sliding against a fixed surface. This subclass groups designs where the motion is purely translational rather than rotary.
[F16H 2007/0874] Two or more finally actuated members -- This subclass covers fastening or securing arrangements in which two or more components are driven to their final positions by a single actuation, such as locking, clamping or coupling mechanisms that simultaneously engage multiple members once motion is completed.
[F16H 2007/0876] Control or adjustment of actuators -- F16H 2007/0876 covers mechanisms and systems that regulate or fine-tune the operation of actuators, including electronic, hydraulic or pneumatic control circuits that adjust position, speed or force. It is applied to patents involving feedback loops, controllers and adjustment devices that ensure precise actuator performance.
[F16H 2007/0878] Disabling during transport -- F16H 2007/0878 covers arrangements that temporarily lock or render a valve inoperable while it is being moved or shipped, preventing accidental opening or closing during transport. The classification includes devices such as transport locks, protective caps, or other disabling mechanisms designed to secure the valve until it reaches its installation site.
[F16H 2007/088] Manual adjustment -- F16H 2007/088 covers hydraulic or pneumatic systems that include manually adjustable components, such as hand-operated valves, pressure-relief devices, or flow-control mechanisms. The classification is used for devices where an operator can change settings without power, allowing on-site tuning of pressure, flow rate or position to suit varying operating conditions.
[F16H 2007/0882] the tension being a function of temperature -- The code covers hydraulic or pneumatic components in which the applied tension is deliberately linked to temperature, such as temperature-compensated tensioners, bimetallic springs or control systems that adjust force as temperature varies. It includes methods for monitoring and regulating this relationship.
[F16H 2007/0885] the tension being a function of engine running condition -- The entry covers devices in which the tension applied to a component-such as a spring, belt or valve-actuating element-is automatically varied according to the engine's running condition, including speed, load or temperature. It includes control mechanisms that sense engine parameters and adjust the tension to optimise performance, reduce wear or enhance safety.
[F16H 2007/0887] the tension being a function of load -- This CPC entry covers the functional relationship between the tension generated by a spring and the load applied to it, typically describing load-deflection or force-displacement curves used to characterize spring performance. It is used for patents that disclose how spring tension changes with varying loads.
[F16H 2007/0889] Path of movement of the finally actuated member -- The subclass classifies patents that disclose the trajectory followed by the element that finally moves to perform a function in a valve, switch or fluid-actuated device, describing whether the motion is linear, rotary, oscillating, or a combination of paths.
[F16H 2007/0891] Linear path -- F16H 2007/0891 (Linear path) designates hydraulic or pneumatic pump and actuator designs in which the working fluid moves along a straight line, typically employing linear pistons, cylinders or similar elements that translate fluid motion directly without rotary conversion. This subgroup groups patents that describe such linear-flow mechanisms.
[F16H 2007/0893] Circular path -- F16H 2007/0893 relates to moulding or shaping devices where the material or the mould moves along a circular path, such as rotating or revolving dies employed in metal or polymer forming operations.
[F16H 2007/0895] Internal to external direction -- The CPC subclass F16H 2007/0895 relates to mechanisms that convey motion or force from an internal source to an external direction, such as couplings, gearings or other devices that redirect power outward. It is applied to patents describing how internal rotational or linear movement is converted into external output motion.
[F16H 2007/0897] External to internal direction -- The CPC class F16H 2007/0897 covers devices that move a fluid or gas from outside a system into an internal cavity or chamber. It includes pumps, blowers, or intake arrangements where the flow direction is external to internal, such as suction ports, intake manifolds and inlet ducts. This classification is applied to patents concerning the design and operation of such intake mechanisms.
[F16H 2007/185] the guiding surface in contact with the belt, rope or chain having particular shapes, structures or materials -- The entry covers guiding surfaces that directly engage belts, ropes or chains, focusing on the particular shapes, structural features or material compositions of those surfaces. It includes designs of rollers, idlers, pulleys and similar elements where the contact geometry or material is tailored to improve guidance, wear resistance or performance.
[F16H 2009/163] Arrangements of two or more belt gearings mounted in parallel, e.g. for increasing transmittable torque -- Arrangements where two or more belt drives are positioned side by side to share load and raise the torque that can be transmitted. The subclass covers parallel belt gearings, including their supporting structures, tensioning or idler pulleys, and coupling methods that enable the belts to work together to increase overall torque capacity.
[F16H 2009/166] Arrangements of two or more belt gearings mounted in series, e.g. for increasing ratio coverage -- F16H 2009/166 covers mechanical configurations where two or more belt gearings are linked in series to provide a broader range of speed ratios. The classification includes designs that use successive belts or pulleys to multiply or adjust transmission ratios for improved performance or flexibility.
[F16H 2009/245] with idle wheels to assist ratio change -- F16H 2009/245 covers mechanisms that incorporate idle wheels-gears or sprockets that transmit motion without contributing torque-to assist in changing gear ratios. These idle wheels are used in gearboxes, transmissions or similar devices to alter the speed or direction of output shafts while simplifying gear meshing and reducing wear.
[F16H 2015/383] with two or more sets of toroid gearings arranged in parallel -- F16H 2015/383 covers machines or devices that incorporate two or more sets of toroid gearings arranged in parallel, where the toroidal gears are placed side by side to transmit torque or motion. This subclass groups drive units, gear trains or power-transmission arrangements that use such parallel toroid gearing for improved load distribution or compactness.
[F16H 2015/386] with two or more sets of toroid gearings arranged in series -- F16H 2015/386 covers power-transmission units that employ two or more toroidal gear sets connected in series, enabling smooth, high-torque transmission while maintaining compact axial dimensions. The classification applies to mechanisms where multiple toroid gears are arranged consecutively to achieve step-down or step-up speed ratios within a single assembly.
[F16H 2019/008] Facilitating the engagement or stopping of gear sections -- The CPC subclass F16H 2019/008 covers devices and methods that aid in the smooth engagement or disengagement of gear sections, such as synchronizers, shift collars, clutch plates and other mechanisms that reduce impact and wear during gear changes. It includes designs that control timing, speed matching or torque transmission to make gear meshing reliable and quiet.
[F16H 2019/046] Facilitating the engagement or stopping of racks -- This subgroup covers devices or mechanisms that assist in inserting, aligning or locking rack components, as well as features that stop or prevent unintended rack movement. It includes guides, latches, brakes or other elements that ensure secure engagement of the rack system.
[F16H 2019/0609] the reciprocating motion being created by at least one drum or pulley with different diameters, using a differential effect -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanisms including flexible members, where the differential effect by using at least one drum or pulley with different diameters is creating the reciprocating movement.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a transmission including rotary input (11) and reciprocating output (17b). Rotation of the belt (15) around pulleys (16a) and (16b) of different diameter create the reciprocating output movement of output (17b).

[F16H 2019/0668] with open loop, e.g. with the free ends of the flexible member fixed to the casing, e.g. when the drive means are arranged on the carriage -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16H 2019/0668 pertains to mechanical power transmission systems, specifically those involving flexible members like belts or chains in an open loop configuration where the ends are fixed to the casing and the drive mechanism is typically mounted on a movable carriage.
[F16H 2019/0677] characterised by the means for fixing the flexible member to a drum -- This CPC subgroup covers inventions where a flexible element such as a belt or rope is secured to a rotating drum using specific attachment mechanisms. It pertains to devices such as conveyors, pulleys or winding machines where the fixing means are integral to the drum design.

[F16H 2019/085] by using flexible members -- F16H 2019/085 covers inventions that accomplish a mechanical function by employing flexible components such as springs, elastic strips, or other deformable members to transmit force, provide movement, or absorb shock. It is used for devices ranging from valves and actuators to mounting and support systems that rely on the elasticity of these flexible parts.
[F16H 2025/063] the intermediate members being balls engaging on opposite cam discs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b. [F16H 2025/066] the intermediate members being rollers supported in a chain -- The CPC subclass F16H 2025/066 relates to chain drive mechanisms in which the intermediate members are rollers rather than pins, providing reduced friction and wear during power transmission. It covers designs such as roller chains, conveyor chains and other roller-supported links used in mechanical power-train applications.
[F16H 2025/127] using electric solenoids for generating the reciprocating motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a. This motion is converted into rotary motion of output shaft (12) see arrow (30) via lateral force (39). The reciprocation is along and parallel to the axis of rotation.  1b.  1c.
[F16H 2025/2012] using a spring member creating rotary torque for counter rotating the two nuts, e.g. a torsion bar -- F16H 2025/2012 covers mechanisms that employ a spring element, such as a torsion bar, to produce rotary torque that drives two nuts in opposite directions. The spring stores mechanical energy and releases it to generate the counter-rotational movement, typically used in tightening, clamping or fastening applications.
[F16H 2025/2028] using screw profiles with high efficiency for converting reciprocating motion into oscillating movement -- F16H 2025/2028 covers devices that use specially shaped screw threads to convert a straight reciprocating stroke into a periodic oscillating motion with minimal energy loss, often applied in pumps, compressors and other actuators requiring efficient motion conversion.
[F16H 2025/2031] Actuator casings -- F16H 2025/2031 covers the design and construction of housings that enclose hydraulic, pneumatic or other types of actuators, including protective shells, outer bodies, mounting brackets and sealing arrangements. The code addresses materials, manufacturing methods and structural features that support, protect and integrate the actuator within a system.
[F16H 2025/2034] Extruded frame casings -- The CPC subclass F16H 2025/2034 covers extruded frame casings, which are hollow metal sections produced by extrusion and used as structural or decorative frames for doors, windows, panels and similar assemblies. It includes designs, manufacturing methods and the material compositions of such casings.
[F16H 2025/2037] Actuator supports or means for fixing piston end, e.g. flanges -- This subclass covers components that hold or secure the end of a piston in hydraulic or pneumatic actuators, including flanged connections, brackets or other mounting structures that attach the piston to a housing or external frame. The features enable reliable fixation and alignment of the actuator during operation.
[F16H 2025/204] Axial sliding means, i.e. for rotary support and axial guiding of nut or screw shaft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a gearing comprising primarily only a screw-and-nut mechanism. Guide members (16) move along guide surface (15) of the housing (2) to support and guide the nut (4).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a gearing comprising primarily only a screw-and-nut mechanism having a nut (3) and a rotatable screw (53). Nut (3) comprises axial sliding means in the form of flat surfaces which slide on axial guide surfaces (86) and (87) of housing (81), thereby preventing rotation of nut (3) while at the same time allowing its axial movement.
[F16H 2025/2043] Screw mechanisms driving an oscillating lever, e.g. lever with perpendicular pivoting axis -- The CPC subclass F16H 2025/2043 concerns screw mechanisms that transmit rotary motion to an oscillating lever whose pivot axis is perpendicular to the lever's plane. It includes designs where a screw thread drives a lever back-and-forth, typical in valves, pumps and hand-operated tools.
[F16H 2025/2046] with gears arranged perpendicular to screw shaft axis, e.g. helical gears engaging tangentially the screw shaft -- F16H 2025/2046 covers mechanical arrangements in which gears are mounted perpendicular to the axis of a screw shaft. Typical examples are helical gears that engage the screw tangentially, providing power transmission from a transverse gear train to a rotating screw.
[F16H 2025/2053] Screws in parallel arrangement driven simultaneously with an output member moved by the screws -- F16H 2025/2053 covers mechanisms where two or more screws are arranged in parallel and are driven simultaneously by an output member, providing coordinated linear motion as used in advanced actuators, jacks, or positioning devices.
[F16H 2025/2059] Common movement by two screws or two nuts, e.g. two connected screws with opposite thread direction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a gearing comprising primarily only a screw-and-nut mechanism having screws (22) and (32) engaging nuts (24) and (34), respectively. Nuts (24) and (34) are connected to each other and have opposite thread directions. Common rotation of nuts (24) and (34) results in opposite linear movement of screws (22) and (32).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a gearing comprising primarily only a screw-and-nut mechanism having screws (31) and (32) engaging nuts (41) and (42) respectively. Screws (31) and (32) are connected to each other and have opposite thread directions. Common rotation of screws (31) and (32) results in opposite linear movement of nuts (41) and (42).
[F16H 2025/2062] Arrangements for driving the actuator -- The code covers designs that provide power or motion to operate a fluid-control actuator, encompassing hydraulic, pneumatic, electric, manual or hybrid drive systems and the mechanical arrangements that transmit force to the actuator. It includes gear trains, levers, motors, couplings and other linkage configurations used to drive valves, regulators or similar devices.
[F16H 2025/2065] Manual back-up means for overriding motor control, e.g. hand operation in case of failure -- This subclass covers devices that provide a manual backup for motor-driven systems, allowing an operator to engage the mechanism directly by hand when the automatic motor control fails. Typical examples include hand cranks, levers or other manual actuators attached to pumps, winches, conveyors or similar equipment to ensure continued operation despite power loss or control malfunction.
[F16H 2025/2068] Means for returning linear actuator to zero position, e.g. upon occurrence of failure by using a spring -- A device that uses a spring or similar biasing element to automatically drive a linear actuator back to its reference or zero position when power is lost or a fault occurs. The mechanism provides a fail-safe return without external control, ensuring the actuator resets to its starting point for safe restart or maintenance.
[F16H 2025/2071] Disconnecting drive source from the actuator, e.g. using clutches for release of drive connection during manual control -- F16H 2025/2071 covers devices that separate a power source from an actuator, typically using clutches or similar mechanisms to disengage the drive during manual operation. The classification applies to systems where the drive connection is released to allow manual control of the movement while the powered drive remains idle. This falls under the cooperative patent classification for hydraulic and mechanical power transmission.
[F16H 2025/2075] Coaxial drive motors -- F16H 2025/2075 refers to coaxial drive motors, where the motor's rotor and the driven element are arranged on the same axis, allowing compact and efficient transmission of torque. This class includes designs such as integrated gear-motor units and motor-pump assemblies used in robotics, automotive and aerospace applications.
[F16H 2025/2078] the rotor being integrated with the nut or screw body -- F16H 2025/2078 covers rotary screw or nut assemblies in which the rotating element is formed as an integral part of the nut or screw body rather than being a separate component. The classification applies to mechanisms that combine the rotor and fastener into a single unit for power transmission or actuation, eliminating the need for a distinct, attachable rotor.
[F16H 2025/2081] Parallel arrangement of drive motor to screw axis -- F16H 2025/2081 relates to screw-driven machines in which the power-transmitting motor is positioned so its axis runs parallel to the screw's axis, rather than perpendicular or at an angle. This arrangement is used to streamline the drive layout, shorten overall length and improve balance in devices such as power-screws, jacks and linear actuators.
[F16H 2025/2084] Perpendicular arrangement of drive motor to screw axis -- The subclass F16H 2025/2084 covers mechanisms in which the drive motor is mounted at a right angle to the screw axis, employing gear, belt or other transmission means to turn the screw perpendicularly. This arrangement enables compact layouts and can simplify power transfer in linear actuators and similar devices.
[F16H 2025/2087] using planetary gears -- The CPC code F16H 2025/2087 covers pump or compressor designs that incorporate planetary gear sets to transmit power to the moving elements, providing a compact layout with high-torque transmission and speed-reduction or increase capabilities.
[F16H 2025/209] using worm gears -- The CPC subclass F16H 2025/209 covers the use of worm gears in mechanical power transmission, including design, integration and applications of worm gear sets for speed reduction and torque conversion.
[F16H 2025/2093] using conical gears -- The CPC subclass F16H 2025/2093 covers inventions that involve the use of conical (bevel) gears in mechanical systems, detailing how these gears transmit power between intersecting shafts and are integrated into devices such as gearboxes, drives and other power-transmission mechanisms.
[F16H 2025/2096] using endless flexible members -- F16H 2025/2096 covers devices and methods that employ continuous loops of flexible material such as belts, chains, ropes or similar members to transmit motion or power between components. It includes arrangements where the endless flexible member forms part of the drive, timing or traction system, and the associated supporting or guiding structures.
[F16H 2025/2242] Thread profile of the screw or nut showing a pointed "gothic" arch in cross-section -- F16H 2025/2242 relates to the geometric shape of screw or nut threads where the thread cross-section forms a pointed gothic-arch profile. This design provides a sharp crest and a narrow root, improving load bearing and resistance to stripping. The classification is used for fasteners with such gothic-arch thread forms.
[F16H 2025/2257] with means for shifting planetary rollers axially, e.g. into central position -- F16H 2025/2257 covers fluid-power devices such as pumps or hydraulic motors that incorporate planetary rollers whose axial position can be shifted, for example moving them into a central position, to adjust clearances, balance forces or change the unit's displacement.
[F16H 2025/2271] with means for guiding circulating rollers -- F16H 2025/2271 designates valve arrangements that incorporate guiding means for circulating rollers, which serve to support and direct moving parts within the valve to improve alignment and reduce wear. The subclass is used for patents where roller guidance is integral to the valve's operation or maintenance.
[F16H 2025/2276] using roller spacers, i.e. spacers separating the rollers, e.g. by forming a complete chain -- This subclass of CPC F16H concerns power-transmission chains in which the rollers are kept apart by spacers, forming a complete chain. It covers designs where the spacers separate the rollers to maintain pitch and prevent direct roller contact, improving flexibility and load distribution.
[F16H 2025/228] Screw mechanisms having rollers being supported by the screw shaft and engaging the nut -- The subclass concerns screw drives in which rollers are mounted on and supported by the screw shaft and roll within the nut to transmit motion. This arrangement reduces friction and wear compared with direct thread contact and is typical of ball-type or roller-type screws used in precise positioning.
[F16H 2025/2436] Intermediate screw supports for reducing unsupported length of screw shaft -- F16H 2025/2436 covers intermediate devices that are installed along a screw, bolt or stud to limit the length of shaft that remains unsupported by a nut or other element. These supports may be collars, sleeves, brackets or similar members that hold the fastener at one or more points between its threaded ends, improving load distribution and reducing bending. The entry is applied to fasteners where reducing the unsupported portion enhances performance.
[F16H 2025/2445] Supports or other means for compensating misalignment or offset between screw and nut -- The F16H 2025/2445 entry covers devices that compensate for misalignment or offset between a screw and its mating nut, such as supports, guide sleeves or flexible couplings. These elements ensure proper load transmission and smooth operation in assemblies where axial or angular displacement may occur.
[F16H 2025/2463] using a wrap spring brake, i.e. a helical wind up spring for braking or locking -- F16H 2025/2463 refers to mechanisms that use a wrap-spring brake, a helical wound spring that provides a braking or locking function when engaged, typically employed in gearboxes, winches, safety devices or other mechanical systems to control motion or hold positions.
[F16H 2025/2481] Special features for facilitating the manufacturing of spindles, nuts, or sleeves of screw devices -- The subclass covers design elements and process aids that simplify the production of spindles, nuts or sleeves used in screw mechanisms, for example features that guide material flow, provide shaping or fastening assistance, or enable more efficient machining or assembly of these components.
[F16H 2025/249] Materials or coatings for screws or nuts -- F16H 2025/249 covers inventions that involve the selection, formulation, or application of materials and surface treatments specifically for screws and nuts. It includes metallic, polymeric, ceramic, composite, or hybrid substances and various coatings such as plating, anodizing, painting, or surface alloying that improve strength, corrosion resistance, wear resistance, or lubricity of the fasteners.

[F16H 2035/005] Gearings or mechanisms preventing back-driving -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a gearing with a no-back mechanism consisting of an axially movable friction member (10). Friction member (10) and helical gear (13) are fixed to shaft (9). Only when torque is applied from output shaft (24), i.e. backdriving of the gearing, shaft (9) is axially moved by the axial force of helical gear (13) towards brake area (11), thereby pressing friction member (10) against brake area (11) of housing wall (4). This causes braking of shaft (9) and prevents backdriving of output shaft (24).
[F16H 2035/006] Gearings or mechanisms for stopping or limiting movement, e.g. stopping a movement after a few turns -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a worm gearing consisting of a worm (11) and a worm wheel (15). The worm wheel (15) includes end stops (21) with noses (22), which stop movement after a few turns of worm (11).  2a.  2b.  2c.  Figures 2a, 2b and 2c illustrate a toothed gearing including three spur gears (15), (16) and (17). Spur gear (17) comprises an end stop (18) which stops movement after a few turns of spur gear (15).
[F16H 2035/103] with drive interruption by structural failure of overload preventing means, e.g. using shear pins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a pulley (12) includes a torque limiter (15) with breaking portions (24) and (25). If the torque exceeds a certain threshold, breaking portions (24) and (25) break such that torque transfer is interrupted and overload is prevented.
[F16H 2035/106] Monitoring of overload -- F16H 2035/106 covers devices or methods that detect and monitor overload conditions in hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid-power systems, providing signals or control actions to prevent damage when pressures, forces or flows exceed safe limits. This includes sensors, electronic processors and alarm or protective mechanisms designed specifically for overload surveillance.
[F16H 2037/023] the combined gearing being provided with at least two forward and one reverse ratio in a serially arranged sub-transmission -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combinations of mechanical gearings comprising essentially only toothed gearing combined with continuously variable friction gearing [CVT]. The combination of gearing includes a serially arranged sub-transmission with at least two forward ratios and one reverse ratio.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a combination of a combination of mechanical gearings including a belt-type CVT 2 and a toothed sub-transmission (29) in series. Transmission (29) includes a reverse clutch (25), a brake (26) for a low forward speed and a clutch (28) for a high forward speed. Thus, the transmission (29) provides two forward ratios and one reverse ratio.  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a combination of mechanical gearings including belt-type CVT (121) and a toothed sub-transmission (140) in series between input gear (120) and output gear (152). Transmission (140) includes a low forward speed, a high forward speed and a reverse speed. Thus, the transmission (140) provides two forward ratios and one reverse ratio.
[F16H 2037/025] having continuously variable friction gearing, i.e. CVT, in which the ratio coverage is used more than once to produce the overall transmission ratio coverage, e.g. by shift to end of range, then change ratio in sub-transmission and shift CVT through range once again -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combinations of mechanical gearings comprising essentially only toothed gearing combined with continuously variable friction gearing [CVT]. The CVT includes a ratio coverage that is used more than once to produce the overall transmission ratio coverage, e.g. by shift to end of range, then change ratio in sub-transmission and shift CVT through range once again.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  Figure 1a illustrates a transmission comprising a combination of mechanical gearings including a belt-type CVT (H) and toothed gearing (Z1), (Z2), (Z3), (Z4), (Z5) and (Z6). The transmission ratio of CVT (H) is (i S ), and the transmission ratio of the whole transmission (the combined gearing) is (i G ). The CVT ratio (i S ) is shifted through its range from 0.5 to 2, then the toothed gearing is shifted at whole transmission ratio (i G ) of 2. Thereafter, the CVT ratio (i S ) is shifted through its range in the other direction, i.e. from 2 to 0.5. In other words, the CVT (H) is shifted through its range, the toothed gearing is then shifted, and the CVT (H) is shifted through its range again.  1b.
[F16H 2037/026] Layouts with particular features of reversing gear, e.g. to achieve compact arrangement -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combinations of mechanical gearings comprising essentially only toothed gearing combined with continuously variable friction gearing [CVT]. The layout of the combined gearing includes particular features of a reversing gear, e.g. to achieve compact arrangement.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a combination of three mechanical gearings including a belt-type CVT 22, a toothed input gearing (11) and (12), and a forward-reverse output unit (40). Forward-reverse output unit (40) includes reversing gear (42).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a combination of mechanical gearings including a belt-type CVT (18) and a toothed gearing comprising a planetary gear train (16). Brake (B1) is used to provide a reverse speed in planetary gear train (16).
[F16H 2037/028] having two distinct forward drive ratios and one reverse drive ratio arranged in series with a continuously variable transmission unit -- The CPC class F16H 2037/028 covers power-transmission devices that combine a continuously variable transmission with additional gear stages providing two separate forward ratios and a single reverse ratio arranged in series. It relates to mechanisms where a CVT is integrated with fixed-ratio gear sets to expand the range of selectable speeds while retaining a reverse gear. This arrangement is typical in drivetrains that require both smooth variability and distinct stepped ratios.
[F16H 2037/044] comprising a separate gearing unit for shifting between forward or reverse -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combinations of toothed change gear transmissions in group arrangement without gears having orbital motion comprising a separate gearing unit for shifting between forward or reverse.
[F16H 2037/045] comprising a separate gearing unit for shifting between high and low ratio range -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Combinations of toothed change gear transmissions in group arrangement, without gears having orbital motion, comprising a separate gearing unit for shifting between high and low ratio range.
[F16H 2037/047] comprising one or more orbital gear sets coaxial with a first shaft and having more than one drive connection to a second shaft parallel to the first shaft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1. The planetary gear trains (P1) and (P3) are coaxial with shaft (2), and there are two drive connections via (Ü 1 ) and (Ü 2 ) to second shaft (3) parallel to shaft (2).
