[F16H 2061/6641] Control for modifying the ratio control characteristic -- F16H 2061/6641 relates to control mechanisms that adjust the ratio-control characteristic of hydraulic or fluid-power systems, such as variable-displacement pumps or motors. It covers devices that modify the relationship between input and output flow or pressure to achieve desired performance changes during operation.
[F16H 2061/6642] dependent on ambient conditions -- F16H 2061/6642 designates devices or control systems whose functioning is adjusted according to ambient conditions such as temperature, pressure, humidity or chemical composition of the surrounding medium, typically in hydraulic, pneumatic or fluid-handling equipment. The classification covers mechanisms that sense environmental parameters and modify operation to maintain performance or safety.
[F16H 2061/6643] dependent on driver's choice -- F16H 2061/6643 covers valves or control devices in vehicle braking systems whose operation varies according to a driver-selected setting, such as selectable braking modes or driver-chosen distribution of braking force. The classification applies to mechanisms that adjust fluid flow or pressure based on the driver's choice.
[F16H 2061/6644] dependent on control input parameters other than ambient conditions or driver's choice -- The CPC subclass F16H 2061/6644 covers devices in which the operation of a fluid-power or hydraulic system is governed by control input parameters that are not ambient conditions or the operator's manual selection, such as sensor-derived signals, preset program settings, or automated feedback loops. It pertains to systems that adjust performance based on internal or external data rather than temperature, pressure, or direct driver choice.
[F16H 2063/005] Preassembled gear shift units for mounting on gear case -- F16H 2063/005 refers to preassembled gear-shift units that are supplied as a complete, ready-to-mount assembly for insertion into a gear case. The entry covers shift levers, linkages and associated components provided together as a single package to simplify installation.
[F16H 2063/025] Final output mechanisms for double clutch transmissions -- The CPC subclass F16H 2063/025 pertains to final output mechanisms for double-clutch transmissions, covering devices that transmit torque from the transmission to the driven shaft, including dual-clutch packs, gear arrangements and associated actuators specifically designed for dual-clutch systems.
[F16H 2063/202] using cam plates for selection or shifting, e.g. shift plates with recesses or groves moved by a selector extension -- This group covers mechanisms that use cam plates to achieve selection or shifting actions, where the cam plate incorporates recesses or grooves that are engaged and moved by a selector extension to change positions or functions.
[F16H 2063/204] the gear shift lever being the immediate final actuating mechanism, e.g. the shift finger being a part of the gear shift lever -- This subgroup covers vehicle transmission arrangements where the gear shift lever itself directly acts as the final actuator that moves the shift fork or finger, meaning the shift finger is integrated with the lever and no separate intermediate lever is used. It applies to manual or automatic gearboxes where the lever's motion is transferred directly to the gear-selecting element.
[F16H 2063/208] using two or more selecting fingers -- The code F16H 2063/208 relates to valve mechanisms that employ two or more selector fingers to choose between fluid passages, allowing either simultaneous or sequential actuation of ports for precise flow control.
[F16H 2063/3006] moved by a non-mechanical force -- This CPC subclass covers devices in which a component is displaced or rotated by forces that are not transmitted through direct mechanical linkages, such as magnetic, electric, thermal, acoustic or chemical forces, typically used in fluid-power or control systems. It addresses the design and operation of mechanisms where the motion results from a non-mechanical force.
[F16H 2063/303] the friction member is actuated and released by applying pressure to different fluid chambers -- This CPC subclass defines devices where a friction element is engaged and released by applying pressure to separate fluid chambers, allowing hydraulic or pneumatic control of the friction member without direct mechanical linkage. It is used in clutches, brakes and locking mechanisms that rely on fluid-actuated friction engagement.
[F16H 2063/3033] the brake is actuated by springs and released by a fluid pressure -- This CPC group covers brake mechanisms in which a spring provides the actuating force to engage the brake and the braking action is released when fluid pressure-hydraulic or pneumatic-acts on the spring or a piston to counteract it, as found in many parking-brake and safety-brake designs.
[F16H 2063/3036] the clutch is actuated by springs and released by a fluid pressure -- F16H 2063/3036 covers clutch mechanisms in which a spring provides the actuation force and the engagement is terminated by applying fluid pressure, such as hydraulic or pneumatic release. These designs are used in transmissions and power-train systems to provide quick, controlled disengagement.
[F16H 2063/3046] using electromagnetic clutch for coupling gear wheel to shaft -- F16H 2063/3046 covers mechanisms that employ an electromagnetic clutch to connect a gear wheel to a shaft, enabling controlled engagement and disengagement of power transmission by means of a magnetic field, commonly used in automotive and industrial drive systems.
[F16H 2063/305] using electromagnetic solenoids -- F16H 2063/305 relates to devices that employ electromagnetic solenoids to generate or control motion, especially in hydraulic or pneumatic systems. The classification covers mechanisms where the solenoid acts as a valve, actuator or selector within fluid-power equipment.
[F16H 2063/3053] using linear motors -- This subgroup covers inventions that employ linear electric motors to generate direct linear motion for driving or assisting mechanisms such as pumps, compressors, actuators, or other machines, removing the need for rotary-to-linear conversion. It includes the integration of linear motors in fluid-power or electro-hydraulic systems to provide precise, high-speed movement.
[F16H 2063/3056] using cam or crank gearing -- This CPC entry pertains to hydraulic or pneumatic power-steering devices that employ a cam or crank gear arrangement to transform rotary input into the oscillating motion needed for steering assistance, including gear-driven configurations that provide variable assistance.
[F16H 2063/3059] using racks -- F16H 2063/3059 covers the use of racks as integral elements in devices or processes for supporting, storing, or handling loads, especially in industrial material-handling systems. It includes designs where rack structures hold, organise or transport items during manufacturing, processing, or conveyance. This subclass groups inventions that rely on rack-based arrangements to improve stability, accessibility or efficiency of load management.
[F16H 2063/3063] using screw devices -- The subclass F16H 2063/3063 covers the use of screw-type mechanisms-such as screw pumps, augers or screw conveyors-to move fluids, gases or particulate media within pipelines or other fluid-handling installations, including their operation, control and integration into the system. It also addresses methods of employing screws for feeding, conveying, or regulating flow in such equipment.
[F16H 2063/3066] using worm gears -- This CPC subclass deals with inventions that employ worm gears as a means of transmitting motion or power, typically for high reduction ratios, torque multiplication, or changing rotation direction in mechanical drive systems. It includes designs, mounting, and sealing aspects related to worm gear use.
[F16H 2063/3073] final output mechanisms mounted on a single shaft -- The CPC subclass F16H 2063/3073 covers final output mechanisms-such as gear sets, cams, clutches, brakes or other motion-transmitting devices-that are mounted on a single rotating shaft to deliver the usable power from a machine. It is a further refinement of F16H 2063/30, which addresses final output mechanisms in general.
[F16H 2063/3076] Selector shaft assembly, e.g. supporting, assembly or manufacturing of selector or shift shafts; Special details thereof -- The CPC class F16H 2063/3076 relates to selector-shaft assemblies, covering components and methods for supporting, assembling or producing selector or shift shafts in gearboxes and transmission systems, and includes any special design details or manufacturing features of those shafts.
[F16H 2063/3079] Shift rod assembly, e.g. supporting, assembly or manufacturing of shift rails or rods; Special details thereof -- The subclass covers devices and methods that support, assemble, or manufacture shift rods and shift rails used in gear-shifting mechanisms, detailing their construction, mounting and integration within transmissions. It includes specialized features such as adjustable guides, coupling arrangements, and other technical refinements that improve the performance or reliability of the shift-rod system.
[F16H 2063/3083] Shift finger arrangements, e.g. shape or attachment of shift fingers -- This CPC subgroup covers inventions concerning the configuration, geometry or means of attaching the shift fingers that engage gear elements in mechanical shifting mechanisms. It includes variations in shape, profile, mounting brackets or coupling features designed to improve engagement reliability or reduce wear.
[F16H 2063/3086] Shift head arrangements, e.g. forms or arrangements of shift heads for preselection or shifting -- F16H 2063/3086 covers the design and layout of shift-head mechanisms used for pre-selection or shifting in gearboxes, focusing on the forms, positions and interconnections of the shift heads. The classification includes various configurations such as multiple or modular heads that control gear engagement and selection pathways.
[F16H 2063/3089] Spring assisted shift, e.g. springs for accumulating energy of shift movement and release it when clutch teeth are aligned -- A spring-assisted shifting mechanism uses a spring to store the energy of a shift movement and then releases it when the clutch teeth become aligned, assisting gear engagement and reducing driver effort. This concept is covered under CPC code F16H 2063/3089.
[F16H 2063/3093] Final output elements, i.e. the final elements to establish gear ratio, e.g. coupling sleeves or other means establishing coupling to shaft -- This subclass covers the final output elements of a transmission, such as coupling sleeves or other devices that connect the gear set to a driven shaft to establish the gear ratio. It includes any means that directly transmit torque from the gear assembly to the output shaft.
[F16H 2063/3096] Sliding keys as final output elements; Details thereof -- This subclass pertains to devices whose final output element is a sliding key - a key that translates linearly relative to its housing to produce a signal - and addresses the specific design features, construction details and mechanical arrangements of such keys, as found in keyboards, control panels and other input devices.
[F16H 2063/321] characterised by the interface between fork body and shift rod, e.g. fixing means, bushes, cams or pins -- This subclass groups hydraulic or pneumatic valve or pump assemblies whose design is defined by the way the fork body connects to the shift rod, using fixing elements such as bushes, cams, pins, or similar hardware. The focus is on the interface geometry and the securing means that transmit motion between the two parts.
[F16H 2063/322] characterised by catches or notches for moving the fork -- This subclass covers fork-type handling devices that incorporate catches or notches to control the movement of the fork. The catches or notches engage during operation to lock, release or guide the fork, enabling precise positioning or stepwise advancement in material handling or lifting equipment.
[F16H 2063/324] characterised by slide shoes, or similar means to transfer shift force to sleeve -- F16H 2063/324 relates to mechanisms that use slide shoes or comparable devices to transmit shifting force from a control element to a transmission sleeve, enabling precise gear selection. It covers the design of the sliding contacts and their integration within manual or automated gearbox systems.
[F16H 2063/325] Rocker or swiveling forks, i.e. the forks are pivoted in the gear case when moving the sleeve -- The CPC subclass F16H 2063/325 covers hydraulic or pneumatic gear units that incorporate rocker or swivelling forks, meaning the fork elements are hinged inside the gear case and rotate when the driven sleeve moves, allowing angular change of motion or torque transmission within the gearbox.
[F16H 2063/327] essentially made of sheet metal -- F16H 2063/327 classifies inventions whose principal construction is formed from sheet-metal components, such as frames, housings, enclosures or other structural parts made essentially of sheet metal. It covers mechanical or industrial devices where sheet metal is the dominant material in the design.
[F16H 2063/328] essentially made of plastics, e.g. injection molded -- F16H 2063/328 classifies pneumatic or hydraulic components whose bodies are essentially made of plastics, for example injection-moulded housings, valve bodies or pistons. It is applied when the primary structural material of the device is a molded polymer rather than metal or other substances.
[F16H 2063/423] Range indicators for automatic transmissions, e.g. showing selected range or mode -- The CPC code F16H 2063/423 covers devices that provide visual or auditory range indicators for automatic transmissions, such as displays showing the currently selected gear range or operating mode. It includes mechanisms that inform the driver of gear position, sport or economy mode, and related status signals integrated into the vehicle's control panel.
[F16H 2063/426] with means for advising the driver for proper shift action, e.g. prompting the driver with allowable selection range of ratios -- The code F16H 2063/426 covers systems that provide the driver with advice for selecting the proper gear shift, such as displaying an allowable range of gear ratios or prompting the driver when a shift is optimal, thereby assisting manual or automated transmission operation.
[F16H 2063/504] for bringing engine into special condition by transmission control, e.g. by changing torque converter characteristic to modify engine set point to higher engine speed for better acceleration performance -- The subclass covers methods that use transmission control to place the engine in a special operating condition, for example by adjusting the torque-converter characteristic to raise the engine's set point and increase speed, thereby improving acceleration performance.
[F16H 2063/506] for engine torque resume after shift transition, e.g. a resume adapted to the driving style -- The code F16H 2063/506 pertains to mechanisms that regulate the re-application of engine torque after a gear-shift, adjusting the torque rise to match the driver's style or operating conditions. It is part of the power-transmission class dealing with torque-control features during shift transitions.
[F16H 2063/508] for limiting transmission input torque, e.g. to prevent damage of transmission parts -- The class covers mechanisms that restrict the torque applied to a transmission's input shaft in order to protect gears, shafts and other components from overload. It includes devices such as torque limiters, slip clutches, friction couplings and other systems that automatically reduce or cut off torque when a preset limit is exceeded.
[F16H 21/00] Gearing for conveying or converting motion by means of levers, links, cams or screw-and-nut mechanisms -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Wobble-plate gearings; Oblique-crank gearings -> F16H23/00
[F16H 21/02] the movements of two or more independently-moving members being combined into a single movement -- This CPC subclass covers mechanisms that combine the motions of two or more independently moving components into a single resultant movement, such as linkages that transmit and transform motion. It is part of the F16H group dealing with gearing for conveying or converting motion by levers, links, cams or screw-and-nut mechanisms.
[F16H 21/04] Guiding mechanisms, e.g. for straight-line guidance -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a gearing including input (4), links (7) and (8), and output (6). Links (7) and (8) guide element (6).
[F16H 21/06] which can be made ineffective when desired -- F16H 21/06 covers gear assemblies or related mechanisms that can be rendered ineffective on command, such as clutches, brakes, or disengagement devices that interrupt power transmission in lever, link, cam or screw-nut gear systems.
[F16H 21/08] by pushing a reciprocating rod out of its operative position -- F16H 21/08 covers gear mechanisms that transmit motion by displacing a reciprocating rod out of its operative position, typically using levers, links, cams or screw-nut arrangements to convert or convey motion.
[F16H 21/10] all movement being in, or parallel to, a single plane -- F16H 21/10 is a subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification that covers gear systems in which all movement occurs in, or is confined to, a single plane. It lies within the hierarchy of mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → gearing, and relates to mechanisms that convey or convert motion using levers, links, cams or screw-and-nut arrangements.
[F16H 21/12] for conveying rotary motion -- This subclass covers mechanisms that transmit rotary motion by means of levers, links, cams or screw-nut arrangements, such as chain, belt or rope drives, rather than conventional toothed gears.
[F16H 21/14] by means of cranks, eccentrics, or like members fixed to one rotary member and guided along tracks on the other -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a gearing comprising input member (185) and output member (187). Crank pin (104) is fixed on rotary member (185) and guided in a first long hole of member (202), crank pin (106) is fixed on rotary member (187) and guided in a second long hole of member (202). Counter-clockwise rotation of rotary input member (185) is conveyed to clockwise rotation of rotary output member (187) via reciprocating motion of member (202).
[F16H 21/16] for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion -- F16H 21/16 pertains to mechanisms that transform rotary motion into reciprocating motion or the reverse, including cranks, cam drives, levers, links and screw-nut assemblies used in gear-based power transmission systems.
[F16H 21/18] Crank gearings -- Crank gearings are mechanisms that convert rotational motion into reciprocating motion or vice versa using a crank and gear system, typically employed in engines, pumps, and machinery to transmit power through linked levers and rotating gears.
[F16H 21/20] with adjustment of throw -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings comprising primarily only links or levers, with or without slides, crank gearings or eccentric gearings, for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion, all movement being in, or parallel to, a single plane, with adjustment of throw.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a crank gearing comprising primarily only links and levers for converting rotary motion of the crank shaft (1) into reciprocating motion of the piston (13) which functions as a guided slide. Crankshaft (1) comprises an oblique crank pin (9). The throw, i.e. the length of the reciprocating motion of piston (13), is adjusted by moving crankshaft (1) in a different axial position. The axial movement of crankshaft (1) between a minimum and maximum throw is shown by arrow (7).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a crank gearing comprising primarily only links and levers for converting rotary motion of the crank shaft (18) into reciprocating motion of the piston (13) which functions as a guide slide. The throw, i.e. the length of the reciprocating motion of piston (13), is adjusted by moving pivot (12) to a different position.
[F16H 21/22] with one connecting-rod and one guided slide to each crank or eccentric -- F16H 21/22 covers gear mechanisms that employ a single connecting-rod and a guided slide attached to each crank or eccentric, converting rotary motion into a controlled reciprocating movement. This arrangement is used in engines and machines where precise linear motion is required, integrating levers or cams within broader mechanical-engineering gearing systems.
[F16H 21/24] without further links or guides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a crank gearing comprising primarily only links or levers for converting rotary motion of crank shaft (21) into reciprocating motion of piston (14) which functions as a guided slide via connecting rod (7). The crank gearing does not comprise any further links or guides in addition to the one connecting rod (7) and one guided slide (14).
[F16H 21/26] with toggle action -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Crank gearings or eccentric gearings comprising primarily only links or levers, for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion. All movement is in, or parallel to, a single plane, with one connecting-rod and one guided slide to each crank or eccentric. The gearing includes joints or levers which may toggle, or move, between two locations.
[F16H 21/28] with cams or additional guides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1. [F16H 21/30] with members having rolling contact -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a crank gearing for converting rotary motion of one crank shaft (4) into reciprocating motion of one piston (1) (guided slide) via one connecting rod (21) and by using gears (24) and (25) as members having rolling contact.  **Glossary:** - members having rolling contact: e.g. a pair of toothed gears or a toothed pinion gear meshing with a rack.
[F16H 21/32] with additional members comprising only pivoted links or arms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a crank gearing comprising primarily only links and levers for converting rotary motion of crank shaft (A) into reciprocating motion of piston (F) which functions as a guided slide. [F16H 21/34] with two or more connecting-rods to each crank or eccentric -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1. [F16H 21/36] without swinging connecting-rod, e.g. with epicyclic parallel motion, slot-and-crank motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate an eccentric gearing including a crankshaft (11), eccentric member (9), rod (6) and piston (13). The crank pin is running in a cam-like slot, which generates a slot-and-crank motion. The connecting rod does not swing.
[F16H 21/365] with orbital gearing having a ratio of 2:1 between central gear and orbital gear -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  Figure 1a illustrates an eccentric gearing including a crank (10), (12), (13) which rotatably carries orbital gear (19), and further including ring gear (14), rod (21) and piston (8). Rotary motion of crank (10), (12), (13) and orbital gear (19) is converted into reciprocating motion of piston (8) which functions as a guided slide. Rod (21) moves only linearly and does not swing. The orbital gearing includes a ratio of 2:1 between the ring gear (14) and the orbital gear (19).  1b.
[F16H 21/38] with means for temporary energy accumulation, e.g. to overcome dead-centre positions -- F16H 21/38 covers gearing arrangements that incorporate means for temporarily storing energy, such as springs or flywheels, to help the mechanism pass dead-centre positions or other points where motion would otherwise stop. It is a subcategory of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing that converts motion by levers, links, cams or screw-and-nut mechanisms.
[F16H 21/40] for interconverting rotary motion and oscillating motion -- This subclass covers devices that convert continuous rotation into reciprocating or oscillating motion, or vice versa, using mechanisms such as cams, levers, links or screw-nut arrangements. It falls under CPC group F16H 21/40 within the gearing category for motion conversion.
[F16H 21/42] with adjustable throw -- This subclass covers gear arrangements that convert motion using levers, links, cams or screw-nut mechanisms where the output displacement, or throw, can be varied by adjustment. It includes adjustable cams, linkages and screw-driven devices that allow the motion amplitude to be set to different positions for flexible transmission.
[F16H 21/44] for conveying or interconverting oscillating or reciprocating motions -- F16H 21/44 designates gear arrangements that convert oscillating or reciprocating motions into continuous motion, typically using levers, links, cams or screw-and-nut mechanisms. It belongs to the CPC hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Gearing → Gearing for conveying or converting motion by means of levers, links, cams or screw-and-nut mechanisms.
[F16H 21/46] with movements in three dimensions [3D] -- F16H 21/46 covers gear arrangements that produce or transmit motion in three mutually perpendicular directions, enabling combined translation and rotation in three-dimensional space. Such mechanisms are used in complex drive systems where motion must be distributed across all axes, for example in robotic manipulators or multi-axis machine tools.
[F16H 21/48] for conveying rotary motions -- The F16H 21/48 subclass covers devices that transmit rotary motion between components, such as gear trains, shafts, couplings or other mechanisms that convey or convert rotary power without using levers, links, cams or screw-and-nut arrangements. It addresses the means by which rotational energy is transferred from one rotating part to another in mechanical systems.
[F16H 21/50] for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion -- F16H 21/50 designates mechanisms that transform continuous rotary motion into reciprocating linear movement or the reverse, typically employing cranks, cams, levers or screw-nut arrangements. These converters are used in engines, pumps and other machines where back-and-forth motion is required.
[F16H 21/52] for interconverting rotary motion and oscillating motion -- F16H 21/52 covers devices that convert rotary motion into oscillating motion or the reverse, such as crank-shafts, cam mechanisms and similar linkage arrangements. It falls under the gearing subgroup of mechanical engineering elements that convey or convert motion through levers, links, cams or screw-nut systems.
[F16H 21/54] for conveying or interconverting oscillating or reciprocating motions -- F16H 21/54 classifies gearing arrangements that transmit or change oscillating or reciprocating motion into another form of motion, such as rotary or linear motion, using levers, links, cams, screw-and-nut or similar mechanisms. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and specifically the gearing subgroup for motion conversion.
[F16H 2200/00] Transmissions for multiple ratios -- F16H 2200/00 covers mechanical transmission systems that provide multiple gear ratios, enabling a single drive source to deliver variable speed or torque. It includes multi-speed gearboxes, continuously variable transmissions and other devices that can select among several ratios for efficient power transfer.
[F16H 2200/0004] comprising a power take off shaft -- This subclass covers gear transmission assemblies that incorporate a power take-off shaft to divert mechanical power from the main output to auxiliary devices, commonly used in multi-ratio gearboxes for agricultural or industrial machinery.
[F16H 2200/0008] specially adapted for front-wheel-driven vehicles -- F16H 2200/0008 is a CPC subclass within mechanical engineering that concerns gearing, specifically transmissions offering multiple ratios that are specially adapted for front-wheel-driven vehicles. It covers designs, modifications, and components of gear sets intended to optimise power delivery to front wheels in automotive applications.
[F16H 2200/0013] specially adapted for rear-wheel-driven vehicles -- The classification F16H 2200/0013 covers gear transmission arrangements that provide several speed ratios and are specifically designed for vehicles where power is delivered to the rear-wheel drive. It includes designs of gearboxes, transfer cases or differential setups that are tailored to rear-wheel-driven configurations, enabling efficient torque distribution across multiple gears.
[F16H 2200/0017] specially adapted for four-wheel-driven vehicles -- It covers gear transmissions providing multiple ratios that are specifically designed for four-wheel-driven vehicles, such as transfer cases or differentials that distribute torque to all axles. The classification falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → gearing → transmissions for multiple ratios and is identified by CPC code F16H 2200/0017.
[F16H 2200/0021] specially adapted for electric vehicles -- F16H 2200/0021 covers gear-based transmission systems that provide multiple speed ratios and are specially adapted for electric-vehicle propulsion. The classification addresses designs that optimise efficiency, torque delivery and integration with electric motor characteristics, distinguishing them from conventional internal-combustion-engine gearboxes.
[F16H 2200/0026] comprising at least one creep low gear, e.g. additional gear for extra low speed or creeping -- This CPC subclass covers transmissions that provide multiple gear ratios and include at least one extremely low gear for very slow or creeping speeds, such as an additional gear to achieve extra low-speed operation.
[F16H 2200/003] characterised by the number of forward speeds -- This subclass groups transmissions whose defining feature is the number of forward speeds they provide, such as four-speed or six-speed gearboxes. It covers any gearing arrangement where the count of forward ratios is a primary technical characteristic, regardless of the specific mechanism used.
[F16H 2200/0034] the gear ratios comprising two forward speeds -- This subclass identifies gear configurations that deliver exactly two forward speed ratios, typically realised through a single gear pair or a simple shifting mechanism that provides two distinct forward gears. It is applied to transmissions where limited forward ratios are sufficient, such as low-power vehicles, basic machinery or specialised equipment.
[F16H 2200/0039] the gear ratios comprising three forward speeds -- The F16H 2200/0039 subclass covers gear sets that provide three forward speeds within a transmission, detailing the specific gear ratios used to achieve three distinct forward drive ratios in mechanical gearing systems.
[F16H 2200/0043] the gear ratios comprising four forward speeds -- F16H 2200/0043 classifies gear ratios that provide four forward speeds in transmissions designed for multiple ratios, covering mechanical engineering solutions where a gear set delivers a four-speed forward range.
[F16H 2200/0047] the gear ratios comprising five forward speeds -- F16H 2200/0047 covers transmissions that provide five forward gear ratios, detailing the selection and arrangement of gear sets to achieve a five-speed forward range. It pertains to mechanical engineering elements, specifically gearing systems designed for multiple-ratio operation, such as automotive or industrial gearboxes that require a five-speed forward configuration.
[F16H 2200/0052] the gear ratios comprising six forward speeds -- The classification F16H 2200/0052 covers transmission designs that provide six distinct forward gear ratios, defining the arrangement and selection of gear sets to achieve six forward speeds in mechanical systems such as automotive or industrial gearboxes. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Gearing → Transmissions for multiple ratios.
[F16H 2200/0056] the gear ratios comprising seven forward speeds -- This CPC subclass defines gear ratios used in transmission systems that provide seven forward speeds. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing, and is used to classify patents describing multi-ratio transmissions with seven forward gears.
[F16H 2200/006] the gear ratios comprising eight forward speeds -- The classification code F16H 2200/006 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the EPO and USPTO, specifies transmissions with multiple ratios where the gear ratios include eight forward speeds. This entry is part of a broader category dealing with mechanical engineering elements, specifically gearing and transmissions.
[F16H 2200/0065] the gear ratios comprising nine forward speeds -- F16H 2200/0065 designates gear ratios that deliver nine forward speeds in transmissions offering multiple ratios. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, gearing, and transmissions for multiple ratios, and is used by the EPO and USPTO to classify related patent documents.
[F16H 2200/0069] the gear ratios comprising ten forward speeds -- F16H 2200/0069 classifies gear ratios that provide ten forward speeds in a transmission. It covers the arrangement and selection of gear sets that achieve a ten-speed forward range, typically used in automotive or industrial applications where multiple forward ratios are required.
[F16H 2200/0073] the gear ratios comprising eleven forward speeds -- This CPC subclass concerns transmissions that offer eleven forward gear ratios, detailing the specific arrangement of gear sets required to achieve eleven distinct forward speeds in a multiple-ratio gearbox. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing.
[F16H 2200/0078] the gear ratio comprising twelve or more forward speeds -- F16H 2200/0078 refers to gear systems that provide twelve or more forward gear ratios, covering transmissions designed for vehicles or machinery requiring many speed steps. It falls under the mechanical engineering category, specifically within engineering elements and units for gearing and multiple-ratio transmissions.
[F16H 2200/0082] characterised by the number of reverse speeds -- The subclass covers mechanical transmission units whose design is defined by the number of reverse gear ratios they provide. It applies to gearboxes and other transmission systems where the quantity of reverse speeds is a distinguishing feature.
[F16H 2200/0086] the gear ratios comprising two reverse speeds -- F16H 2200/0086 classifies gear transmissions that provide two distinct reverse gear ratios, meaning the reverse function can be selected in two different speed settings within a multi-ratio drivetrain.
[F16H 2200/0091] the gear ratios comprising three reverse speeds -- The CPC code F16H 2200/0091 classifies transmissions that incorporate three distinct reverse gear ratios, detailing the gear arrangements and mechanisms used to achieve multiple reverse speeds in mechanical drive systems. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Gearing → Transmissions for multiple ratios.
[F16H 2200/0095] the gear ratios comprising four reverse speeds -- F16H 2200/0095 classifies gear arrangements that provide four distinct reverse speeds within a transmission offering multiple forward ratios. It covers the design and selection of gear sets that enable four reverse gear ratios for vehicles or machinery.
[F16H 2200/20] Transmissions using gears with orbital motion -- F16H 2200/20 denotes gear-based transmission systems that achieve multiple gear ratios through orbital motion of the gears. These transmissions use rotating carriers or orbiting gear carriers to vary output speed and torque, falling under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing categories.
[F16H 2200/2002] characterised by the number of sets of orbital gears -- This CPC subclass defines transmissions that provide multiple gear ratios and are distinguished by the number of orbital-gear sets they contain. It covers gearboxes where the count of planetary gear trains or satellite gear groups determines the classification, aiding search for multi-ratio gear systems.
[F16H 2200/2005] with one sets of orbital gears -- F16H 2200/2005 classifies gear transmission systems that achieve multiple ratios using a single set of orbital gears, where the planetary gear carriers move in orbital paths around a central axis. This arrangement is used in mechanical engineering applications that require compact, variable-speed drives.
[F16H 2200/2007] with two sets of orbital gears -- The F16H 2200/2007 class defines transmission devices that employ two sets of orbital gears to achieve multiple gear ratios. These systems use orbital gear pairs arranged on one or more shafts to provide compact, efficient speed reduction or multiplication, and are applied in automotive, industrial and other mechanical power-train contexts.
[F16H 2200/201] with three sets of orbital gears -- F16H 2200/201 classifies transmission units that achieve multiple gear ratios by employing three sets of orbital gears, where each gear set rotates about a common axis and meshes with the others to provide stepped speed changes. This subgroup falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, and transmissions for multiple ratios.
[F16H 2200/2012] with four sets of orbital gears -- This CPC group deals with transmissions that achieve several speed ratios by employing four planetary-gear sets in combination. The arrangement links multiple orbital gears to provide a range of output torques and speeds within a single mechanical system.
[F16H 2200/2015] with five sets of orbital gears -- This subclass defines transmissions that use five sets of orbital gears, a gear arrangement where the gear axes rotate around a common centre to provide multiple speed ratios in a compact form. It applies to mechanical drive systems needing several ratio changes within a single gearbox.
[F16H 2200/2017] with six sets of orbital gears -- F16H 2200/2017 covers transmissions that achieve multiple gear ratios using six sets of orbital gears, which rotate around a common axis while meshing with other gears. The subclass resides in mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, and transmissions for multiple ratios.
[F16H 2200/202] characterised by the type of Ravigneaux set -- The F16H 2200/202 entry in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers gearing systems that provide multiple transmission ratios, specifically those that employ a Ravigneaux set-a planetary arrangement in which two gear clusters share a common carrier and sun gear. It falls under mechanical engineering, within engineering elements and units, and more precisely under gearing for transmissions with multiple ratios.
[F16H 2200/2023] using a Ravigneaux set with 4 connections -- The code F16H 2200/2023 covers transmission designs that employ a Ravigneaux planetary gear set configured with four distinct connections, enabling multiple gear ratios within a single gearbox. [F16H 2200/2033] with one engaging means -- F16H 2200/2033 classifies transmissions that offer multiple gear ratios but employ a single engaging means to shift between them. [F16H 2200/2066] using one freewheel mechanism -- This subclass covers gear transmissions that achieve multiple speed ratios by incorporating a single freewheel (over-running) clutch, allowing torque to be transmitted in one direction while permitting disengagement in the opposite direction to change ratios or prevent back-driving.
[F16H 2200/2069] using two freewheel mechanism -- This classification covers gear transmission arrangements that provide multiple-speed ratios by employing two freewheel devices. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, transmissions for multiple ratios.
[F16H 2200/2071] using three freewheel mechanism -- The code F16H 2200/2071 covers gear transmissions that obtain multiple speed ratios by using three freewheel (overrunning clutch) mechanisms. [F16H 2200/2074] using four freewheel mechanism -- This class covers gear transmissions that achieve multiple speed ratios by using four freewheel (one-way clutch) mechanisms, allowing torque to be transmitted in one direction while permitting free rotation in the opposite direction, thus providing stepped speed changes without gear disengagement.
[F16H 2200/2076] using at least five freewheel mechanism -- F16H 2200/2076 covers transmission arrangements that achieve multiple gear ratios by incorporating at least five freewheel (one-way clutch) mechanisms, enabling sequential engagement of gear stages without reverse torque transmission. [F16H 2200/2084] two freewheel mechanisms -- The entry F16H 2200/2084 defines two freewheel mechanisms, devices that provide two independent free-wheel functions within a transmission so that one or more driven shafts can continue rotating when the driving shaft stops or reverses. [F16H 2200/2087] three freewheel mechanisms -- The F16H 2200/2087 entry classifies three-freewheel mechanisms used in transmissions with multiple ratios. [F16H 2200/2089] four freewheel mechanisms -- F16H 2200/2089 designates a transmission configuration that incorporates four freewheel mechanisms. [F16H 2200/2092] at least five freewheel mechanisms -- The CPC subclass F16H 2200/2092 falls under mechanical engineering gear transmissions for multiple ratios and covers devices that incorporate at least five freewheel mechanisms. These freewheel mechanisms allow rotary motion to continue in one direction while preventing motion in the opposite direction, and the classification is used for patents describing complex multi-freewheel gear systems.
[F16H 2200/2094] using positive clutches, e.g. dog clutches -- The code F16H 2200/2094 covers transmission designs that achieve multiple gear ratios by means of positive clutches such as dog clutches. Positive clutches provide a firm, non-slipping engagement to lock gear sets together for direct power transfer.
[F16H 2200/2097] comprising an orbital gear set member permanently connected to the housing, e.g. a sun wheel permanently connected to the housing -- The class F16H 2200/2097 refers to gear transmissions in which an orbital gear element-such as a sun wheel-is permanently fixed to the housing, making the gear inseparable from the casing. This design integrates the orbital member into the housing structure, typically found in planetary gear sets that provide multiple speed ratios.
[F16H 23/00] Wobble-plate gearings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings using rotating wobble plates or discs for converting rotary movement into a reciprocating movement of a gear member.
[F16H 2300/00] Determining of new ratio -- F16H 2300/00 covers methods and devices for determining a new gear ratio, including calculations, simulations or optimization techniques used to select or adjust the speed relationship between meshing gears. It applies to mechanical engineering contexts where designers need to establish appropriate transmission ratios for performance, efficiency or space-saving objectives.
[F16H 2300/02] Computing a new ratio -- F16H 2300/02 covers methods and devices for calculating a new gear ratio, including the mathematical and computational procedures used to determine optimal tooth count, pitch diameter or speed relationship for a gear set. It applies to design and analysis of gearing systems where a revised ratio is required.
[F16H 2300/14] Selecting a state of operation, e.g. depending on two wheel or four wheel drive mode -- The entry covers control methods for choosing a drive state in a vehicle transmission, such as switching between two-wheel and four-wheel drive, by selecting appropriate gear ratios or clutch engagement. It relates to gear mechanisms that determine or adjust the transmission ratio according to operating conditions.
[F16H 2300/18] Determining the range -- The code F16H 2300/18 covers methods for establishing the permissible range of a new gear ratio in a gearing system, including analytical, computational or experimental procedures used to calculate or verify the ratio limits during design or optimization of mechanical transmissions.
[F16H 23/02] with adjustment of throw by changing the position of the wobble-member -- The CPC subclass F16H 23/02 covers wobble-plate gearings where the output stroke, or throw, can be varied by shifting the position of the wobble member. This adjustment alters the geometry of the gear set, allowing the displacement produced per drive cycle to be increased or decreased without changing other components. It is used in mechanisms that need a selectable range of motion.
[F16H 2302/00] Determining the way or trajectory to new ratio, e.g. by determining speed, torque or time parameters for shift transition -- The code F16H 2302/00 relates to methods for selecting or calculating the optimal gear ratio during a shift, using parameters such as speed, torque, or time to define the trajectory of the transition. It covers techniques that determine how the new ratio is reached in mechanical gearing systems.

It pertains to optimizing shift trajectories in gear systems to achieve desired performance.
[F16H 2302/04] Determining a modus for shifting -- F16H 2302/04 relates to methods for determining the shifting mode of a gearbox by calculating the optimal gear-ratio trajectory using speed, torque, time or other operating parameters. It covers the analysis and selection of shift points to achieve desired performance during gear transitions. This falls within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing.
[F16H 2302/06] Determining timing parameters of shifting, e.g. start of shifting -- The entry F16H 2302/06 covers methods for determining the timing parameters of gear shifts, such as the start of shifting, by calculating speed, torque or time values needed for a transition to a new ratio. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Gearing.
[F16H 23/04] with non-rotary wobble-members -- F16H 23/04 designates gearings that employ a non-rotary wobble member, such as a wobble plate that oscillates but does not spin, to transmit motion between shafts.
