[F16H 23/06] with sliding members hinged to reciprocating members -- This classification concerns wobble-plate gearings that incorporate sliding members attached by hinges to reciprocating members, allowing the sliding parts to move parallel to the gear axis while being driven by a back-and-forth motion. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, and specifically wobble-plate gearings.
[F16H 2306/00] Shifting -- F16H 2306/00 concerns gear-shifting devices that alter the engaged gear set to vary the transmission ratio, including shift forks, selectors, and control mechanisms that move gear elements into or out of mesh.
[F16H 2306/14] Skipping gear shift -- F16H 2306/14 covers skipping gear shift, a mechanism that lets a transmission select non-adjacent gears directly, bypassing intermediate steps to reduce shift time and improve performance in mechanical drivetrains.
[F16H 2306/18] Preparing coupling or engaging of future gear -- F16H 2306/18 relates to methods for preparing a gear so it can be coupled or engaged with another gear, covering steps such as aligning, positioning and surface preparation before meshing occurs. It includes techniques that ensure correct engagement geometry and timing in gear assemblies.
[F16H 2306/20] Timing of gear shifts -- F16H 2306/20 relates to the timing of gear shifts in mechanical engineering, covering methods and devices that control when a gear change occurs in a transmission. This classification is used for inventions that improve shift timing to enhance performance, efficiency, or smoothness of gear changes.
[F16H 2306/21] for auxiliary gear shifts -- F16H 2306/21 classifies auxiliary gear-shift mechanisms that supplement the primary shifting system in a transmission. It covers devices such as secondary shift levers, actuators or control units that provide additional gear-selection functions, often for improving shifting speed, precision or user convenience. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Gearing → Shifting in the CPC system.
[F16H 2306/22] Swap shifting -- F16H 2306/22 - Swap shifting covers gear-shifting mechanisms in which whole gear sets are exchanged or swapped to change transmission ratios rather than sliding or rotating individual gears. It includes devices that move entire gear clusters or modules to achieve a different gear engagement in mechanical transmissions.
[F16H 2306/24] Interruption of shift, e.g. if new shift is initiated during ongoing previous shift -- F16H 2306/24 concerns gear-shifting mechanisms that halt an ongoing shift when a new shift command is received, enabling the current shift to be interrupted and a different gear to be engaged. This functionality is used in transmissions that require rapid or sequential gear changes, improving control and responsiveness during varying operating conditions.
[F16H 2306/30] characterised by the way or trajectory to a new ratio, e.g. by performing shift according to a particular algorithm or function -- F16H 2306/30 relates to gear-shifting arrangements in which the transition to a new gear ratio follows a predetermined trajectory, such as a specific algorithm or mathematical function that dictates how the shift is performed.
[F16H 2306/32] Preparing the opening or release of the torque transmitting element -- This subclass concerns techniques for preparing a gear or other torque-transmitting component for disengagement, such as pre-loading, alignment, or unlocking actions that allow the element to be opened or released during a shift. It includes mechanisms that adjust gear teeth, pawls, or other engagement devices to facilitate smooth shifting in mechanical transmissions.
[F16H 2306/36] Filling the dead volume of actuators -- The code F16H 2306/36 covers methods and devices for eliminating or reducing the unused space inside hydraulic, pneumatic or other actuators, often by introducing fluid, gas or a flexible material that occupies the dead volume. This improves response speed, efficiency and control accuracy of the actuator.
[F16H 2306/40] Shifting activities -- F16H 2306/40 pertains to the actions involved in changing gear ratios within mechanical gear systems, including the control, timing and actuation of gear shifts. It describes methods and devices that manage shifting operations to achieve smooth and efficient power transmission.
[F16H 2306/42] Changing the input torque to the transmission -- F16H 2306/42 concerns methods or mechanisms that vary the torque applied to a transmission during gear shifting, including devices that adjust input torque to improve shifting performance, reduce wear, or adapt to operating conditions. The entry falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing and shifting.
[F16H 2306/44] Removing torque from current gears -- F16H 2306/44 covers arrangements and methods for removing or releasing torque from gears that are already engaged, such as free-wheel devices, torque-limiting clutches or braking systems that allow the gear to rotate without transmitting drive torque.
[F16H 2306/46] Uncoupling of current gear -- F16H 2306/46 concerns devices or processes that disengage the gear currently transmitting torque in a gear train. It includes mechanisms such as clutches, synchronizers or shifting controls that separate a meshed gear to allow selection of another gear without interrupting power flow.
[F16H 2306/48] Synchronising of new gear -- The CPC entry F16H 2306/48 pertains to mechanisms that synchronize a newly engaged gear with the gear already in motion, ensuring a smooth transition during shifting. It covers devices and methods that align speeds or positions of gears before load transfer, typically used in automotive and industrial transmissions.
[F16H 2306/50] Coupling of new gear -- F16H 2306/50 covers the coupling of new gears within shifting mechanisms, detailing how newly designed gear elements are joined to transmit motion or torque. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and specifically the gearing category, addressing innovative gear-to-gear connections.
[F16H 2306/52] Applying torque to new gears -- The subclass F16H 2306/52 concerns techniques for applying torque to newly fabricated gears, typically during installation, testing or adjustment. It includes devices or methods that transmit rotational force to engage or set the gear without damaging its teeth, and relates to gear-shifting operations within mechanical engineering.
[F16H 2306/54] Synchronising engine speed to transmission input speed -- F16H 2306/54 covers mechanisms that synchronize the speed of an engine with the input speed of a transmission, typically using gear or clutch arrangements to match output torque and prevent speed mismatch. It belongs to the gearing subcategory of engineering elements and units within mechanical engineering.
[F16H 23/08] connected to reciprocating members by connecting-rods -- F16H 23/08 covers wobble-plate gearings that are linked to reciprocating members through connecting rods, enabling conversion of rotary motion into a back-and-forth movement for mechanisms such as piston engines.
[F16H 23/10] with rotary wobble-plates with plane surfaces -- The F16H 23/10 subclass covers gearings that employ rotary wobble-plates with flat, planar surfaces to transmit motion, a design often used in variable-speed mechanisms. These devices convert rotational input into oscillating motion through the wobbling action of the plates, providing precise speed control in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16H 2312/00] Driving activities -- F16H 2312/00 covers the driving actions in gear systems, including devices and methods that initiate, transmit, or modify rotational motion between gear elements, such as starters, couplings, clutches, and other mechanisms that convey power from a source to a gear train. It pertains to the functional aspects of imparting motion rather than the gear geometry itself.
[F16H 2312/02] Driving off -- F16H 2312/02 classifies devices and arrangements that provide a driving-off function, meaning they transmit motion from a driving component to another part that is disengaged or released. It includes mechanisms such as gear-driven clutches, couplings, and other elements that enable controlled separation or disengagement of driven members in mechanical systems.
[F16H 2312/022] Preparing to drive off -- F16H 2312/022 covers methods and mechanisms that ready a gear system for forward motion, including preparatory actions such as clutch or torque-converter engagement, pre-selection of gears, synchronization of rotating elements, and any control steps taken before the vehicle actually begins to drive off.
[F16H 2312/04] Holding or hillholding -- F16H 2312/04 relates to devices that secure or retain gears, shafts, or other rotating components in a fixed position during operation, commonly known as gear or shaft holders, including mechanisms that prevent axial or radial movement. The class covers designs such as clamps, plates, and brackets used to lock components to a machine frame or housing.
[F16H 2312/06] Creeping -- F16H 2312/06 (Creeping) relates to the phenomenon where gear teeth rotate relative to each other with a very small angular displacement under load, often called creeping or slip. The classification covers methods, designs or devices that manage, reduce or exploit this creeping motion in gear driving mechanisms.
[F16H 2312/08] Rocking -- F16H 2312/08 covers gear elements in which the driving member performs a rocking or oscillatory motion relative to the driven member. The class includes devices such as rocking pinions, oscillating gears and mechanisms that convert continuous rotation into a limited back-and-forth motion.
[F16H 2312/09] Switching between forward and reverse -- The CPC entry F16H 2312/09 relates to mechanisms that enable a drive unit to change the direction of rotation, allowing forward and reverse motion. It covers gear arrangements, clutches, and shifting devices that switch power flow in mechanical transmission systems, typically found in gearboxes, motor-driven machines, and vehicular drivetrains.
[F16H 2312/10] Inching -- F16H 2312/10 covers inching, which is the controlled, intermittent movement of a gear-driven mechanism to achieve very small incremental advances, typically used for precise positioning in machines such as printers, feed devices, or testing equipment. The classification applies to gear arrangements that provide start-stop motion without continuous rotation.
[F16H 2312/12] Parking -- F16H 2312/12 pertains to gearing arrangements and drive mechanisms specifically designed for vehicle parking functions, such as parking brakes, automatic parking gearboxes, and other devices that engage or disengage drive to hold a vehicle stationary. It covers technical solutions that control the transition between normal propulsion and a locked or restrained state for safe parking.
[F16H 2312/14] Going to, or coming from standby operation, e.g. for engine start-stop operation at traffic lights -- This CPC entry covers devices and methods that manage the transition of a vehicle's power unit between standby and active states, such as the automatic start-stop function used at traffic lights. It relates to gear systems that control how the engine is engaged or disengaged from the drivetrain during these brief interruptions.
[F16H 2312/16] Coming to a halt -- The code F16H 2312/16 covers devices or methods that stop the motion of a gear-driven mechanism, such as braking, deceleration or locking arrangements that bring the system to a halt. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy dealing with gearing and driving activities.
[F16H 2312/18] Strong or emergency braking -- F16H 2312/18 covers brake actuators designed to deliver strong or emergency braking in mechanical drive systems, typically hydraulic or pneumatic devices that provide rapid, high-force deceleration for safety or performance purposes. It includes fail-safe mechanisms, quick-release features and control arrangements that ensure reliable operation under sudden or extreme braking demands.
[F16H 2312/20] Start-up or shut-down -- The code F16H 2312/20 covers gear drives related to the start-up or shut-down of a transmission, describing mechanisms that engage, disengage or control power flow during initiation or stopping. It falls under mechanical engineering gear elements in the driving activities subclass, detailing methods or devices for starting and halting gear operation.
[F16H 2342/00] Calibrating -- F16H 2342/00 covers techniques and apparatus for calibrating gears and gear-trains. It includes methods for measuring tooth geometry, pitch, lead and checking conformity to specifications, as well as devices that adjust or verify gear dimensions during production or maintenance.
[F16H 2342/02] Calibrating shift or range movements -- F16H 2342/02 relates to the calibration of shift or range movements in gearing systems, covering methods and devices that adjust the position or travel limits of gear-shift mechanisms. It ensures accurate movement and proper range control for gearboxes and transmission components.
[F16H 2342/04] Calibrating engagement of friction elements -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16H 2342/04 pertains to the calibrating engagement of friction elements within the broader category of Gearing under Mechanical Engineering. This classification specifically addresses methods and devices for ensuring proper contact and operation of friction components in mechanical systems.
[F16H 2342/042] Point of engagement -- F16H 2342/042 refers to the point of engagement in gear systems, the specific location where gear teeth first make contact during mesh. It addresses the geometry and positioning of this contact point, which is crucial for calibrating gear performance, controlling noise, wear, and transmission efficiency.
[F16H 2342/044] Torque transmitting capability -- F16H 2342/044 covers provisions for assessing the torque transmitting capability of gears and other mechanical power transmission elements. The classification addresses methods, devices and criteria used to determine how much torque a gear can safely convey under operating conditions.
[F16H 2342/06] Determining which part to calibrate or timing of calibrations -- F16H 2342/06 covers methods for deciding which gear-related component should be calibrated and when the calibration should occur, including criteria for prioritising parts and scheduling timing to maintain optimal performance and reliability of mechanical transmission systems.
[F16H 2342/10] Calibrating valves -- F16H 2342/10 covers calibrating valves used in gear assemblies, including devices and methods that adjust valve settings to achieve precise control of fluid or lubrication flow within gearing systems. It encompasses mechanisms for fine-tuning valve positions to ensure accurate operation of mechanical gear units.
[F16H 25/00] Gearings comprising primarily only cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F16H 25/02] the movements of two or more independently moving members being combined into a single movement -- F16H 25/02 covers mechanisms that link the motions of two or more independently moving parts to create a single combined movement. It is applied to gearings that mainly consist of cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut arrangements, enabling coordinated motion without extra transmission components.
[F16H 25/04] for conveying rotary motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings comprising primarily only cams and cam followers for conveying rotary motion.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a cam gearing comprising only sphere (1) with a cam groove and disc (5) with cam follower pin (6). Rotary motion (4) of sphere (1) causes rotary motion (7) of disc (5).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a screw-and-nut gearing (10) comprising only nut (12) and circular screw (14). Rotary motion (R1) of nut (12) causes rotary motion (R2) of circular screw (14).
[F16H 25/06] with intermediate members guided along tracks on both rotary members -- A gear system where the primary power transmission elements are cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut mechanisms, rather than teeth, and that includes one or more intermediate members moving along tracks fixed on both rotating components. This arrangement is used for complex motion control within mechanical engineering gearings.
[F16H 25/08] for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings comprising primarily only cams and cam followers for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion.  Note: When reversing reciprocating motion, input rotary motion (which is defined as indefinitely continuous rotary motion) would cause an automatic reversal of the reciprocating motion. If the input rotational direction is changed in order to cause reversal of the reciprocating motion, the input motion is an oscillating motion (which is defined as alternately forward and backward rotary motion).  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a gearing comprising only a cam (7) and a cam follower (12). Rotary motion of shaft (5) is converted into reciprocating motion of rod (14) see arrows (15) via the cam (7) and cam follower (12).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Wobble-plate gearings; Oblique-crank gearings -> F16H23/00
[F16H 25/10] with adjustable throw -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings comprising primarily only cams and cam followers for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion, with adjustable throw.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  1c.  Figures 1a, 1b and 1c illustrate a gearing comprising only a cam (38) and a cam follower (42). Rotary motion of cam (38) is converted into reciprocating motion of cam follower (42). The throw (i.e. the length of the reciprocating motion), is adjusted by axially moving axially tapered cam (38).
[F16H 25/12] with reciprocation along the axis of rotation, e.g. gearings with helical grooves and automatic reversal -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings comprising primarily only cams and cam followers for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion, with reciprocation occurring in a direction along the axis of rotation. It is noted that "reciprocation along the axis of rotation" can be reciprocating movement either on or parallel to the axis of rotation.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  Figure 1a illustrates a gearing comprising only a cam shaft (4) and a cam follower (12). Rotary motion of the input shaft (see arrow 8) is converted into reciprocating motion of output shaft (14) (see arrow F, s). The reciprocation is along and coaxial to the axis of rotation (10). The gearing comprises wave-type profiles (22) and (24) for the cam.  1b.  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a gearing comprising only a cam (14) and a cam follower (9). Rotary motion of shaft (17) is converted into reciprocating motion of sledge (6) (see arrow 7). The reciprocation is along and parallel to the axis of rotation. The gearing includes helical projection (14).
[F16H 25/122] Gearings with helical grooves and automatic reversal -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a gearing comprising only a cam shaft (3) and a cam follower (1). Rotary motion of cam shaft (3) is converted into reciprocating motion of output (6). The reciprocation is along and parallel to the axis of rotation. Cam shaft (3) has an endless helical groove (4) which provides an automatic reversal of output (6) via cam follower (1).
[F16H 25/125] having the cam on an end surface of the rotating element -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a gearing comprising only a cam shaft (4) and a cam follower (5). Rotary motion of shaft (2) is converted into reciprocating motion of shaft (3). The reciprocation is along and coaxial to the axis of rotation. Cam shaft (4) includes a cam provided on an end surface of rotary cam shaft (4).
[F16H 25/14] with reciprocation perpendicular to the axis of rotation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings comprising primarily only cams and cam followers for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion, with reciprocation occurring in a direction perpendicular to the axis of rotation.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a gearing comprising only a cam (22) and a cam follower (20). Rotary motion of cam (22) is converted into reciprocating motion of cam follower (20). Reciprocation is perpendicular to the axis of rotation of the cam (22).  2a.  2b  Figures 2a and 2b illustrate a gearing comprising only a cam (38) and a cam follower (36). Rotary motion of cam shaft (32) is converted into reciprocating motion of piston (122). Reciprocation is perpendicular to the axis of rotation of cam shaft (32).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Crank or eccentric gearings comprising primarily only links or levers without swinging connecting-rod -> F16H21/36
[F16H 25/16] for interconverting rotary motion and oscillating motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings comprising primarily only cams and cam followers for interconverting rotary motion and oscillating motion.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a gearing comprising primarily only a cam (h) and a cam follower (i). Rotary motion of shaft (d) is converted into oscillating motion of lever (a) and output shaft (b).  It is noted that the gearing comprises a single lever (a). However, the primary functioning of the gearing consists of a cam and cam follower, and the presence of a single lever is not sufficient to consider the gearing as more than primarily cams and cam followers.
[F16H 25/18] for conveying or interconverting oscillating or reciprocating motions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings comprising primarily only cams and cam followers, for conveying or interconverting oscillating or reciprocating motions.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a gearing comprising only a cam (12) and a cam follower (23). Oscillating motion of cam (12) is converted into reciprocating motion (31) and (33) of shaft (22).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a gearing comprising primarily only a cam (11) and a cam follower (16). Oscillating motion of shaft (7) is conveyed into oscillating motion of lever (4).  It is noted that the gearing comprises a single lever (4). However, the primary functioning of the gearing consists of a cam and cam follower, and the presence of a single lever is not sufficient to consider the gearing more than primarily cams and cam followers.
[F16H 25/183] conveying only reciprocating motion, e.g. wedges -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a gearing comprising cams in the form of wedges. A horizontal reciprocating movement of the lower wedge is conveyed into a vertical reciprocating movement of the upper wedge.  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a gearing comprising cams in the form of wedges (10), (12) and (14). A horizontal reciprocating movement of wedges (10) and (12) in opposite directions is conveyed into a vertical reciprocating movement of wedge (14).  3.  Figure 3 illustrates a gearing comprising cams in the form of wedges (9), (7) and (8). A reciprocating horizontal movement of wedge (9) is conveyed into reciprocating horizontal movement of wedge (8) via ball (1) and wedge (7).
[F16H 25/186] with reciprocation along the axis of oscillation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a gearing comprising only cam shaft (4a) and cam follower (5a), which convert oscillating motion of input (11) to reciprocating motion of output (2). Reciprocating motion of output (2) is along the axis of oscillation of input (11).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a gearing comprising only cam (2) and cam follower (3), which convert reciprocating motion of input (1) to oscillating motion of output (4). Reciprocating motion of output (4) is along the axis of oscillation of input (1).
[F16H 25/20] Screw mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a gearing comprising primarily only a screw-and-nut mechanism. Rotation of screw (6) results in linear movement of nut (8).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a gearing comprising primarily only a screw-and-nut mechanism. Rotation of nut (23) results in linear movement of screw (40). It is noted that the gearing comprises a simple set of toothed gears (20). However, the primary functioning of the gearing consists of the screw and nut, and the presence of a simple set of gears is not sufficient to consider the gearing more than primarily a screw-and-nut mechanism.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gearings comprising primarily only cams and cam followers for interconverting rotary motion and reciprocating motion with reciprocation along the axis of rotation -> F16H25/12
[F16H 25/2003] with arrangements for taking up backlash  F16H25/2209  takes precedence -- F16H 25/2003 is a CPC subclass for gearings that consist mainly of cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut mechanisms, focusing on arrangements that take up backlash. This subclass is part of the mechanical engineering hierarchy under MECHANICAL ENGINEERING → ENGINEERING ELEMENTS AND UNITS → GEARING and is succeeded by F16H 25/2209.
[F16H 25/2006] with more than one nut or with nuts consisting of more than one bearing part -- This subclass covers gearings that employ screw-and-nut mechanisms in which more than one nut is used or each nut is made up of multiple bearing parts, often together with cams or cam-followers. It falls under F16H 25/20 for gearings primarily comprising cams, cam-followers and screw-nut arrangements.
[F16H 25/2009] with radial preloading -- F16H 25/2009 refers to gearings that consist mainly of cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut mechanisms which are preloaded in the radial direction to eliminate backlash and improve stiffness, typically used in precision drives and positioning systems where controlled axial forces are required.
[F16H 25/2015] Means specially adapted for stopping actuators in the end position; Position sensing means -- The code F16H 25/2015 covers devices that lock an actuator in its final stroke and provide feedback on its position, used in gearings that rely mainly on cams, cam-followers and screw-nut mechanisms. These stopping and position-sensing means ensure precise end-position control in mechanical systems.
[F16H 25/2018] with both screw and nut being driven, i.e. screw and nut are both rotating -- This subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification defines gearings made chiefly of cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut mechanisms in which both the screw and the nut are driven and rotate together. It is nested under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 25/2021] with means for avoiding overloading -- F16H 25/2021 refers to gearings that consist mainly of cams, cam-followers and screw-nut arrangements and that incorporate devices to prevent overload. Such means may include slip clutches, spring-loaded disengagements, torque limiters or other protective features that safeguard the mechanism when excessive forces occur.
[F16H 25/2025] with means to disengage the nut or screw from their counterpart; Means for connecting screw and nut for stopping reciprocating movement  F16H25/2015  takes precedence -- F16H 25/2025 pertains to gearings that consist mainly of cams, cam-followers and screw-nut mechanisms and include features for disengaging the nut or screw from its counterpart, thereby providing a means to halt reciprocating movement. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 25/205] comprising alternate power paths, e.g. for fail safe back-up -- The subclass F16H 25/205 covers gearings that rely chiefly on cams, cam-followers and screw-nut mechanisms and incorporate alternate power paths to provide fail-safe or backup transmission. Such arrangements create redundant routes so that motion can continue if the main drive fails.
[F16H 25/2056] Telescopic screws with at least three screw members in coaxial arrangement -- F16H 25/2056 designates telescopic screw devices that incorporate at least three coaxial screw members, enabling linear extension or retraction of a shaft, and is classified under gearings primarily composed of cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut mechanisms within mechanical engineering.
[F16H 25/22] with balls, rollers, or similar members between the co-operating parts -- F16H 25/22 covers gearings that use balls, rollers or comparable rolling members placed between the cooperating cam, cam-follower or screw-nut parts to transmit motion. The rolling elements act as intermediaries that reduce friction and wear while allowing smooth relative movement. This subclass is applied to mechanisms where only cams, followers or screw-nut pairs are present and the power transfer is aided by rolling components.
[F16H 25/2204] with balls -- F16H 25/2204 classifies gearings that consist mainly of cams, cam-followers and screw-nut arrangements where the motion is transmitted or guided by balls, such as ball-bearing cam mechanisms. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, and specifically gearings composed primarily of cams, cam-followers and screw-nut mechanisms.
[F16H 25/2209] with arrangements for taking up backlash -- F16H 25/2209 classifies gearings made principally of cams, cam-followers and screw-nut mechanisms that incorporate designs to take up backlash, i.e., to minimise play between mating parts. Such arrangements are used in precision mechanical systems where accurate motion transmission is required.
[F16H 25/2214] with elements for guiding the circulating balls -- F16H 25/2214 covers gearings that incorporate primarily cams, cam-followers or screw-and-nut elements and include components that guide circulating balls, such as ball-bearing guides or tracks integrated within the gearing to control ball movement during operation.
[F16H 25/2219] Axially mounted end-deflectors -- Axially mounted end-deflectors are components placed on the shaft axis at the ends of gearings that use cams, cam-followers or screw-nut mechanisms to redirect motion or limit travel. They guide interacting elements and protect the assembly by deflecting forces axially.
[F16H 25/2223] Cross over deflectors between adjacent thread turns, e.g. S-form deflectors connecting neighbouring threads -- F16H 25/2223 covers devices that provide crossover deflectors linking adjacent turns of a threaded gear, such as S-shaped elements that bridge neighboring threads to guide motion or transmit forces. These components are used in gearings that rely chiefly on cams, cam-followers or screw-nut mechanisms.
[F16H 25/2228] the device for circulation forming a part of the screw member -- F16H 25/2228 relates to devices that provide circulation of fluid or material as an integral part of a screw element, typically used in mechanisms where the screw itself incorporates a channel for flow. This subcategory falls under gearing that primarily involves cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut mechanisms and addresses integrated circulation features within the screw member.
[F16H 25/2233] with cages or means to hold the balls in position -- This subclass concerns gearings built mainly from cams, cam-followers or screw-nut mechanisms that incorporate a cage or other device to retain the balls in their designated positions, thereby integrating ball-bearing functionality within the gear assembly.
[F16H 25/2238] using ball spacers, i.e. spacers separating the balls, e.g. by forming a chain supporting the balls -- F16H 25/2238 covers gear assemblies that primarily use cams, cam-followers or screw-nut mechanisms and include ball spacers to separate the balls, such as by forming a chain that supports the balls within the gearing. This subclass deals with the specific use of spacers that keep the balls apart to improve load distribution and motion control.
[F16H 25/2247] with rollers -- F16H 25/2247 is a CPC subclass for gearings whose primary components are cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut mechanisms that include rollers, falling under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Gearing.
[F16H 25/2252] Planetary rollers between nut and screw -- F16H 25/2252 concerns gearings that use planetary rollers placed between a nut and a screw to transmit torque or axial force, a type of mechanism that relies mainly on cams, cam-followers and screw-nut components. It belongs to the mechanical engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 25/2261] arranged substantially perpendicular to the screw shaft axis -- This subclass covers gearings that use cams, cam-followers and screw-nut mechanisms arranged essentially at a right angle to the screw shaft, so the cam or follower is positioned substantially perpendicular to the axis of the screw. Such arrangements are employed to transmit motion or force between orthogonal directions within mechanical systems.
[F16H 25/2266] arranged substantially in parallel to the screw shaft axis  planetary rollers  F16H25/2252 -- F16H 25/2266 belongs to the mechanical-engineering group for gearings that mainly use cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut mechanisms. It covers planetary roller arrangements that are positioned substantially parallel to the axis of a screw shaft, detailing how these rollers engage and transmit motion in such oriented gear sets.
[F16H 25/2285] with rings engaging the screw shaft with the inner perimeter, e.g. using inner rings of a ball bearing -- F16H 25/2285 refers to gearings that are composed chiefly of cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut mechanisms, where the screw shaft is engaged by rings on its inner perimeter, such as the inner rings of a ball bearing. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Gearing.
[F16H 25/229] Eccentric rings with their axis arranged substantially parallel to the screw shaft axis -- This subclass covers gearings that use eccentric rings as cam elements whose axis is positioned essentially parallel to the screw shaft axis, enabling the conversion of rotary motion to axial displacement within mechanisms primarily composed of cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut arrangements.
[F16H 25/2295] Rings which are inclined or can pivot around an axis perpendicular to the screw shaft axis -- F16H 25/2295 designates rings that are inclined or can pivot around an axis perpendicular to the screw shaft axis, used in gearings comprising primarily cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut mechanisms.
[F16H 25/24] Elements essential to such mechanisms, e.g. screws, nuts -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Screw mechanisms with balls, rollers or similar members between the co-operating parts; Elements essential to the use of such members -> F16H25/22
[F16H 25/2409] one of the threads being replaced by elements specially formed for engaging the screw or nut, e.g. pins, racks, toothed belts -- The subclass F16H 25/2409 refers to gearings in which a conventional screw thread is replaced by specially formed engaging elements-such as pins, racks or toothed belts-that mate directly with a nut, commonly found in mechanisms that rely mainly on cams, cam-followers or screw-and-nut actions.
[F16H 25/2418] Screw seals, wipers, scrapers or the like -- F16H 25/2418 classifies screw-type sealing and cleaning components such as screw seals, wipers and scrapers that are integrated into mechanisms formed mainly by cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut arrangements. These elements serve to prevent fluid leakage, remove contaminants or maintain clear interfaces within the gear assembly. The code falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 25/2427] one of the threads being replaced by a wire or stripmetal, e.g. spring -- A subclass of mechanical-engineering gearing that deals with gearings composed mainly of cams, cam-followers and screw-nut mechanisms in which one of the usual threads is replaced by a wire or strip-metal element such as a spring. This arrangement modifies the transmission of motion or force while retaining the overall cam-or screw-based architecture.
[F16H 25/2454] Brakes; Rotational locks -- F16H 25/2454 pertains to braking and rotational locking devices that employ cam, cam-follower or screw-nut mechanisms as the principal means of stopping or holding rotational motion. These systems are used where a gear-like arrangement provides a controlled lock or brake without conventional friction pads.
[F16H 25/2472] Safety nuts -- Safety nuts are threaded fasteners designed to resist unintentional loosening in gear assemblies, using cam or deformed-thread features that lock onto mating threads. They fall under mechanical engineering gearings primarily composed of cams, cam-followers and screw-nut mechanisms.
[F16H 27/00] Gearings with intermittently-driving member -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanisms which include movement in a stepwise, or step-by-step, manner such that an output member is alternately moving and at rest during continuous motion of an input member.
[F16H 2700/00] Transmission housings and mounting of transmission components therein -- The F16H 2700/00 subclass relates to transmission housings and the means of mounting transmission components within them. It covers the structural design of cases, shells, brackets and other enclosures that contain gears, shafts, bearings and associated parts, including the attachment features that secure those components to the housing.
[F16H 2700/02] Transmissions, specially for working vehicles -- F16H 2700/02 covers transmissions designed for working vehicles, focusing on the housing and mounting of transmission components. It includes mechanical arrangements that integrate the gearbox within the vehicle's structure, such as housings, brackets and support systems that secure and protect the transmission during operation.
[F16H 2700/04] Starting devices or devices to start turning of shafts -- F16H 2700/04 covers devices that provide the initial motion to rotate a shaft, such as starter motors, hand-cranks, hydraulic or pneumatic actuators, and other mechanisms that engage a transmission housing to begin operation. The classification is used for gear-related systems where the starting device is integrated or mounted with the transmission components.
[F16H 2700/06] Protections for shifting mechanical transmissions -- The CPC class F16H 2700/06 covers protective devices for shifting mechanisms in mechanical transmissions, including guards, shields, housings or other elements that prevent damage, contamination or accidental engagement of gear selectors, synchronizer rings, shift forks and related components. It encompasses designs that isolate the shifting parts from external influences while allowing normal operation of the transmission.
[F16H 27/02] with at least one reciprocating or oscillating transmission member -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a step-by-step mechanism having a reciprocating rod (14) as an input member. Reciprocating movement of rod (14) and its attached pin (13) creates a step-by-step rotary movement of output member (1). During reciprocating movement of rod (14), output member (1) is at rest until the pin (13) arrives at groove surface (10b), as shown in illustration (A) and (B). When pin (13) impacts groove surface (10b) it forces output member (1) to rotate, as shown in illustration (C). Rotation of output member (1) stops when pin (13) arrives at groove surface (10c). Thereafter, during the continued reciprocating movement of rod (14) and its pin (13), output member (1) is again at rest. Continuous reciprocation of rod (14) results in a stepwise rotation of output member (1).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a gearing including a rotary input gear (2) with interrupted toothing (8a) and (8b) and a reciprocating rack (3) as an output member. Continuous rotation of input gear (2) results in a stepwise linear movement of rack (3).
[F16H 2702/00] Combinations of two or more transmissions -- F16H 2702/00 covers gear arrangements that integrate two or more separate transmission units within a single power train. The classification includes combined gearboxes, multi-stage gear trains, and hybrid systems that blend different transmission principles to achieve specific speed, torque or efficiency goals.
[F16H 2702/02] Mechanical transmissions with planetary gearing combined with one or more other mechanical transmissions -- The class comprises transmission systems that incorporate a planetary gear set together with one or more additional gear mechanisms, such as simple, helical, bevel or worm gears, to achieve combined speed reduction, torque multiplication or direction change. These hybrid arrangements are used in automotive, aerospace and industrial machinery where compact, high-ratio power transfer is required.
[F16H 2702/04] Combinations of a speed-change mechanism without planetary gearing with a differential for driving a vehicle drive axle -- This subclass covers vehicle powertrains that integrate a speed-change mechanism, such as a conventional gear set, but without planetary gearing, together with a differential to transmit the output to a drive axle. The combination provides variable speed and torque distribution for the axle.
[F16H 2702/06] Combinations of transmissions with parallel force splitting paths having same output -- F16H 2702/06 classifies gear arrangements that link two or more transmission stages in parallel so the input force is split among separate paths and then reconverges to a single output, with all paths delivering the same speed and torque. It applies to mechanical systems where parallel force-splitting paths are used to achieve combined transmission ratios or load sharing while maintaining identical output characteristics.
[F16H 27/04] for converting continuous rotation into a step-by-step rotary movement -- F16H 27/04 covers devices that transform a continuously rotating input into a rotary output that moves in discrete steps rather than smoothly. It includes mechanisms such as intermittent gears, Geneva wheels, cam-driven gears and other arrangements that produce step-by-step motion for precise positioning or timing.
[F16H 2704/00] Control mechanisms and elements applying a mechanical movement -- F16H 2704/00 covers control mechanisms and elements that transmit or modify a mechanical movement, such as governors, speed regulators and shifting devices, used in gearing and other mechanical systems. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 2704/02] Speed-change devices wherein the control lever actuates directly sliding gears pivoting around two non-parallel axis -- F16H 2704/02 covers mechanisms that change transmission speed by a control lever that directly moves sliding gears whose pivots lie on two axes that are not parallel. The lever's motion forces the gears to slide and engage different gear ratios, providing variable speed without intermediate linkages.
[F16H 2704/04] Speed-change devices with an intermediary mechanism placed between control member and actuator -- Speed-change devices that incorporate an intermediary mechanism between the control member and the actuator are designed to modify the rate of motion transmitted from a manual or automatic input to the driven component. Typical examples include gear reducers, variable-ratio transmissions and other intermediate gearing arrangements that allow precise adjustment of speed while maintaining mechanical control.
[F16H 27/045] Mechanism comprising a member with partially helical tracks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a gearing including a rotary input member (30) with a helical rib (38) having tracks (39) and (40), and a rotary output member (35) with cam followers (36) which interact with tracks (39) and (40). Continuous rotation of input member (30) is converted into a step-by-step rotation of output member (35). When one of the followers (36) engages with a straight section of helical rib (38), output member (35) is at rest. When one of the followers (36) engages with an oblique section of helical rib (38), the helical rib (38) drives follower (36) such that output member (35) is rotated about an angle of 45°. Thus, the continuous rotation of input member (30) results in a stepwise rotation of output member (35).
[F16H 27/06] Mechanisms with driving pins in driven slots, e.g. Geneva drives -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a gearing including rotary input shaft (Δ1) and rotary output shaft (Δ2). Continuous rotation of input shaft (Δ1) is converted into a step-by-step rotation of output shaft (Δ2). When driving pin (13) is within one of driven slots (1210), (1220), (1230) or (1240), output shaft (Δ2) is rotated about an angle of 90°. When driving pin (13) is outside of any driven slot, output shaft (Δ2) is at rest. Thus, the continuous rotation of input shaft (Δ1) results in a stepwise rotation of output shaft (Δ2). This type of gearing is considered a Geneva drive.
[F16H 2706/00] Rotary transmissions with mechanical energy accumulation and recovery without means for automatic selfregulation essentially based on spring action or inertia -- F16H 2706/00 covers rotary transmission systems that store mechanical energy and later release it, relying on spring action or inertial devices rather than automatic self-regulating mechanisms. It includes gear arrangements or other rotary components designed to accumulate kinetic or elastic energy during operation and recover it for subsequent use, without employing active control loops.
[F16H 27/08] with driving toothed gears with interrupted toothing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a gearing including a rotary input gear (2) with interrupted toothing (8) and a rotary output gear (3). Continuous rotation of input gear (2) results in a stepwise rotation of output gear (3).
[F16H 2708/00] Control devices for speed-changing geared mechanisms, e.g. specially adapted couplings for synchronising devices, devices to simplify control, control of auxiliary gearboxes -- F16H 2708/00 covers control devices for speed-changing geared mechanisms, including specially adapted couplings for synchronising devices, devices that simplify control, and control of auxiliary gearboxes. It is situated within mechanical engineering, under engineering elements and units, and specifically the gearing subgroup.
[F16H 2708/02] only the toothed wheels remain engaged -- The classification covers devices that keep the gear teeth in mesh while other parts are disengaged, such as specially adapted couplings used to synchronize gearboxes or simplify control of auxiliary gearboxes. These mechanisms allow speed-changing gear arrangements to maintain continuous tooth contact for smooth power transmission.
[F16H 2708/04] the control being mechanical -- A mechanical control device for speed-changing gear arrangements, including specially adapted couplings that enable synchronization, simplify operation, or manage auxiliary gearboxes. [F16H 2708/06] the control being hydraulic or pneumatic -- F16H 2708/06 refers to hydraulic or pneumatic control of speed-changing gear mechanisms, including specially adapted couplings for synchronising devices, devices that simplify control, and the regulation of auxiliary gearboxes within mechanical engineering's gearing sub-category.
[F16H 2708/08] the control being electric -- F16H 2708/08 relates to electric control devices for speed-changing gear mechanisms, including specially adapted couplings that synchronise gears, simplify operation, or manage auxiliary gearboxes.
