[F16H 2708/18] the control being mechanical -- F16H 2708/18 covers mechanical control methods for speed-changing gear mechanisms, including specially adapted couplings that synchronize gear operation, devices that simplify control, and systems that manage auxiliary gearboxes. It addresses how mechanical components are used to regulate and coordinate gear ratios in engineering applications.
[F16H 2708/20] the control being hydraulic or pneumatic -- F16H 2708/20 concerns control devices for speed-changing gear arrangements in which the control action is supplied by hydraulic or pneumatic means, such as fluid-powered couplings, synchronising devices, simplified control mechanisms, or the regulation of auxiliary gearboxes.
[F16H 2708/22] the control being electric -- F16H 2708/22 relates to electric control of speed-changing geared mechanisms, including specially adapted couplings for synchronising devices, devices that simplify control, and control of auxiliary gearboxes. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → gearing, and covers electrically actuated systems that manage gear operation.
[F16H 2708/24] with a preselection system, mainly semi-automatic, e.g. with automatic preselection, but controlled at the intended moment, with force amplification -- This subclass covers devices that manage gear shifting using a preselection mechanism, typically semi-automatic, where the gear is selected in advance and engaged at the chosen instant, often incorporating force amplification to aid the operator. It applies to couplings, synchronizers and auxiliary gearbox controls that simplify speed-changing operations.
[F16H 2708/26] only the toothed wheels remain engaged -- F16H 2708/26 covers control devices for speed-changing gear systems in which the toothed wheels stay meshed while other components are disengaged, such as specialised couplings that synchronise or simplify the control of auxiliary gearboxes.
[F16H 2708/28] only the toothed wheels may be disengaged -- F16H 2708/28 covers control devices for speed-changing gear mechanisms in which disengagement is achieved by releasing only the toothed wheels, such as specially adapted couplings that synchronize shafts, simplify gear shifting, or manage auxiliary gearboxes.
[F16H 27/10] obtained by means of disengageable transmission members, combined or not combined with mechanisms according to group -- Gearings with intermittently-driving member obtained by means of disengageable transmission members, optionally combined with mechanisms of the same group, transmit motion only during selected intervals using clutches, synchronisers or other devices that can connect and disconnect the driving and driven gears.
[F16H 2710/00] Control devices for speed-change mechanisms, the speed change control is dependent on function parameters of the gearing -- F16H 2710/00 covers control devices that adjust the speed of gearing mechanisms based on functional parameters such as load, torque or rotational speed. These devices monitor gear operation and modify the transmission ratio to achieve the desired performance.
[F16H 2710/02] Control dependent on speed and torque, wherein only the toothed wheels remain engaged, control being mechanical -- This CPC subclass covers mechanical control devices that adjust speed and torque of gear trains while the toothed wheels stay engaged, using only mechanical means. It falls under F16H 2710/02 in the hierarchy of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, and addresses speed-change control dependent on functional parameters.
[F16H 2710/04] Control dependent on speed -- The subclass F16H 2710/04 covers control devices for speed-change mechanisms in gearing where the regulation depends on functional parameters such as speed, torque, load or other operating conditions. It pertains to mechanical engineering elements that adjust gear performance based on these real-time inputs.
[F16H 2710/06] only the toothed wheels remain engaged -- The class covers gear-change mechanisms in which, during a speed transition, only the teeth of the engaged wheels stay meshed while the remaining gear teeth are disengaged. This approach controls the speed change by monitoring functional parameters of the gearing, improving efficiency and reducing wear.
[F16H 2710/08] the control being mechanical -- F16H 2710/08 covers mechanical control devices that regulate speed-change mechanisms such as gearboxes, where the control action depends on functional parameters of the gearing (e.g., load, torque or speed). It pertains to mechanical solutions-cams, levers, governors or similar components-used to adjust or maintain the desired gear performance. This falls under the broader groups of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing.
[F16H 2710/10] the control being hydraulic or pneumatic -- F16H 2710/10 covers hydraulic or pneumatic control devices for speed-change mechanisms in gear systems, where the control action depends on functional parameters of the gearing. It falls under the mechanical engineering class for engineering elements and units related to gearing.
[F16H 2710/12] the control being electric -- This subclass covers electric control devices that regulate the speed-change mechanisms of gears, where the control action varies according to functional parameters of the gearing such as load, torque or rotational speed. It applies to electrically driven governors, converters and similar units.
[F16H 2710/14] Control dependent on speed, wherein only the toothed wheels may be disengaged, control being mechanical -- A mechanical device that controls the speed of a gear train by disengaging only the toothed wheels, with the control action based on functional parameters of the gearing such as load or speed, falls under CPC class F16H 2710/14. This subclass covers speed-change mechanisms whose regulation depends on the gear's operating conditions and where disengagement is limited to the gear teeth rather than the entire transmission.
[F16H 2710/16] the gearing is not described or not essential -- This CPC subclass lies within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing and covers control devices for speed-change mechanisms whose operation depends on functional parameters of the gearing, but where the specific gear design is either not described or not essential to the invention.
[F16H 2710/18] the control being mechanical -- F16H 2710/18 relates to mechanical control devices that regulate the operation of speed-change mechanisms in gearing, with the control function depending on parameters such as load, torque or rotational speed. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing.
[F16H 2710/20] the control being hydraulic or pneumatic -- The subclass F16H 2710/20 covers hydraulic or pneumatic control devices that adjust the speed of gear sets by responding to functional parameters such as load, torque or rotational speed. It is used in speed-change mechanisms where the control action depends on the gearing's operating conditions.
[F16H 2710/22] the control being electric -- This CPC subclass covers electric control devices that regulate speed-change gear mechanisms by monitoring functional parameters such as load, torque, or rotational speed. It includes sensors, controllers and electric actuators used to adjust gear shifting or continuously variable transmissions in response to real-time operating conditions.
[F16H 2710/24] Control dependent on torque -- This subclass covers devices that regulate the speed of gear transmissions by monitoring or responding to the torque transmitted through the gears, adjusting gear ratios or engagement based on those torque measurements to achieve the desired performance.
[F16H 2710/26] wherein only the toothed wheels remain engaged, the control being mechanical -- This subclass of F16H 2710/26 refers to mechanical devices that control the speed of gear transmissions by varying the engagement of toothed wheels while keeping only the gears themselves engaged. The control is based on functional parameters of the gearing and does not involve additional clutch or brake elements.
[F16H 2712/00] Mechanisms for changing direction -- F16H 2712/00 covers mechanisms that change the direction of motion or force within gear systems. It includes devices such as bevel gears, worm gears, and other arrangements that redirect rotational or linear motion to a different axis. These mechanisms are used in transmissions, machine tools and power-transfer equipment to achieve angular or axial redirection.
[F16H 2712/02] Automatic control, e.g. for an alternating movement -- F16H 2712/02 covers automatic control systems that manage alternating or reciprocating motions in gear-driven mechanisms, such as cams, cranks or linkages. It includes devices that adjust speed, timing or phase without manual intervention, enabling precise, self-regulating directional changes in mechanical transmissions.
[F16H 2712/04] the control being hydraulic or pneumatic -- F16H 2712/04 covers gear mechanisms that change the direction of power transmission using hydraulic or pneumatic control, such as fluid-actuated shift devices, clutches or selectors that modify the gear path.
[F16H 2712/06] only with toothed wheels or friction wheels -- F16H 2712/06 covers mechanisms for changing motion direction that employ solely toothed or friction wheels, without reliance on other transmission elements. It is classified under mechanical engineering, specifically within engineering elements and units for gearing, describing gear arrangements limited to these wheel types.
[F16H 2712/08] only the toothed wheels may be disengaged -- The code F16H 2712/08 classifies gear mechanisms in which disengagement is achieved solely by separating the toothed wheels, without moving other components. It applies to devices that change direction of motion where only the gear teeth are released, such as selective gear disengagement in planetary or rack-and-pinion systems.
[F16H 2712/10] with a combination of engaged and disengageable toothed wheels -- F16H 2712/10 covers gear mechanisms that combine continuously engaged toothed wheels with wheels that can be disengaged, enabling a change in the direction of rotation while allowing selective engagement of gear elements. It is used in mechanical engineering designs where variable transmission paths are needed.
[F16H 2714/00] Different types speed-changing mechanisms for toothed gearing -- F16H 2714/00 covers mechanisms that alter the speed between meshing toothed gears, such as compound gear sets, epicyclic gear trains, variable-diameter gear arrangements, and other devices that provide ratio changes within a gear system. These speed-modifying designs are used in mechanical engineering applications to achieve desired torque and rotational speed outputs.
[F16H 2714/02] only with toothed wheels remaining engaged -- This class pertains to gear systems in which the toothed wheels stay continually engaged while the speed-changing ratio varies. It includes mechanisms such as continuously meshing planetary, epicyclic, or compound gear sets that provide speed variation without disengagement of any gear teeth.
[F16H 2714/04] with specially adapted devices -- The subclass F16H 2714/04 refers to speed-changing mechanisms for toothed gears that incorporate specially adapted devices, such as adjustable tooth profiles, synchronizing elements or variable-pitch components, to modify gear ratios dynamically. It covers gear systems where these adaptations are integral to the speed-changing function.
[F16H 2716/00] Control devices for speed-change mechanisms of planetary gearings, with toothed wheels remaining engaged, e.g. also for devices to simplify the control or for synchronising devices combined with control devices -- A control device for a planetary gear speed-change mechanism that keeps the toothed wheels continuously engaged, often incorporating features to simplify operation or to synchronize multiple gear elements. The classification also covers combined control and synchronising devices used to manage gear ratio changes without disengagement.
[F16H 2716/02] the control being mechanical -- A mechanical control device used to manage speed-change mechanisms in planetary gearings while the toothed wheels stay engaged. It includes arrangements that simplify control, provide synchronising functions, or combine synchronising and control actions, all implemented through purely mechanical means.
[F16H 2716/04] the control being hydraulic or pneumatic -- The subclass F16H 2716/04 falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → gearing and covers control devices for planetary gear speed-change mechanisms that keep the toothed wheels engaged, where the control action is provided hydraulically or pneumatically, including devices that simplify control or add synchronisation.
[F16H 2716/06] Circuits thereof -- It covers electrical or electronic circuits that are part of control mechanisms for planetary gear sets where the gears stay meshed, such as circuits used to simplify control, achieve synchronization, or integrate control functions. These circuits may include sensors, actuators, signal processing units and power electronics that manage speed change in engaged planetary gearings.
[F16H 2716/08] the control being electric -- The F16H 2716/08 subclass covers planetary gearing speed-change mechanisms whose toothed wheels remain continuously engaged, where the speed-change is governed by an electric control system. It includes devices that simplify control, synchronise operation, or combine synchronising functions with electric actuation.
[F16H 2716/10] only the toothed wheels may be disengaged, the control being mechanical -- This subclass deals with mechanical control mechanisms for planetary gearings in which only the toothed wheels may be disengaged while the other gears remain meshed. The devices are used to vary speed, simplify operation or provide synchronising functions.
[F16H 2716/12] with preselection system, mainly semi-automatic, e.g. with automatic preselection, but controlled at the intended moment, with force amplification -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16H 2716/12 pertains to control devices for speed-change mechanisms in planetary gearings that remain engaged, specifically focusing on systems with preselection capabilities, often semi-automatic, which allow for automatic preselection but require manual activation at the intended moment, typically featuring force amplification.
[F16H 2716/14] only with toothed wheels remaining engaged -- F16H 2716/14 covers control devices for planetary speed-change gear sets in which the toothed wheels stay continuously engaged, including mechanisms that simplify control or provide synchronization combined with control functions.
[F16H 2718/00] Mechanisms for speed-change of planetary gearing, the speed change control being dependent on function parameters of the gearing -- F16H 2718/00 pertains to mechanisms that modify the speed of planetary gear arrangements, with the speed-change control governed by functional parameters of the gearing such as load, torque, or rotational speed. These devices are used in mechanical engineering to adjust output ratios within transmissions and drive systems.
[F16H 2718/02] Control dependent on speed and torque, wherein only the toothed wheels remain engaged -- The subclass F16H 2718/02 relates to planetary gearing where the speed-ratio is controlled by the actual speed and torque of the system, and only the toothed wheels stay engaged while the control is effected. It is part of mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for gearing, covering speed-change mechanisms that use torque- and speed-dependent control without additional clutches or brakes.
[F16H 2718/04] the control being mechanical -- This CPC subclass F16H 2718/04 covers planetary gear speed-change mechanisms where the speed ratio is adjusted by a purely mechanical control linked to functional parameters of the gearing. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 2718/06] the control being hydraulic or pneumatic -- This CPC subclass covers planetary gear speed-change mechanisms whose adjustment is governed by hydraulic or pneumatic parameters. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 2718/08] Control dependent on speed -- This class covers planetary gear systems whose speed-change ratio is regulated by the gear's own operating parameters, such as input speed or load, rather than by external control. The mechanism adjusts gear engagement or ratio automatically in response to the speed condition.
[F16H 2718/10] only the toothed wheels remain engaged -- F16H 2718/10 covers planetary gear mechanisms that alter speed while all toothed wheels stay continuously engaged, the speed change being governed by functional parameters such as load, torque or control signals. It applies to designs where gear engagement is never interrupted during operation.
[F16H 2718/12] the control being mechanical -- F16H 2718/12 covers planetary gear sets whose speed ratio is altered by a mechanical control that responds to gear function parameters such as torque, load or position. The classification applies to devices where gears shift automatically or by user input using mechanical linkages, cams or governors rather than hydraulic or electronic means.
[F16H 2718/14] the control being hydraulic or pneumatic -- This CPC subclass describes mechanisms that change the speed of planetary gearing using hydraulic or pneumatic control, where the speed-change is governed by functional parameters of the gear set.
[F16H 2718/16] the control being electric -- This CPC subclass (F16H 2718/16) relates to planetary gear mechanisms whose speed-ratio adjustment is governed by an electric control that responds to functional parameters of the gear set, falling under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → gearing.
[F16H 2718/18] Control dependent on torque -- The CPC subclass F16H 2718/18 covers planetary gear arrangements whose speed-change operation is regulated by functional parameters, specifically torque. It includes mechanisms that adjust gear ratios or engage/disengage elements in response to the torque transmitted through the gearing.
[F16H 2718/20] only the toothed wheels remain engaged -- This subclass covers planetary gear arrangements that achieve speed-variation by altering geometric or functional parameters of the gear set rather than by using auxiliary clutches or brakes. The transmission keeps the teeth of the wheels continuously meshed, so only the toothed wheels remain engaged throughout operation.
[F16H 2718/22] the control being mechanical -- A mechanical control system that adjusts the speed-change of a planetary gear set based on the gear's functional parameters, using purely mechanical means rather than electronic or hydraulic devices. The mechanism varies transmission ratios by altering gear position or engagement in response to load or speed changes.
[F16H 2718/24] the control being hydraulic or pneumatic -- The CPC entry F16H 2718/24 covers planetary gear systems whose speed-change ratio is regulated by hydraulic or pneumatic control that responds to functional parameters of the gearing. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing categories.
[F16H 2718/26] the control being electric -- This CPC subclass F16H 2718/26 pertains to planetary gear speed-change mechanisms whose speed ratio is regulated by an electric control that responds to functional parameters of the gear set, such as load, torque or speed. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 2720/00] Different types of speed-change gear mechanisms -- The F16H 2720/00 class groups gear mechanisms that alter output speed relative to input, including variable-ratio gears, epicyclic gear sets, continuously variable transmissions and shiftable gear trains. It covers the design, arrangement and operation of such speed-change devices used in mechanical power transmission.
[F16H 2720/02] Gears with a non-circular rolling curve or gears with special teeth -- F16H 2720/02 covers gear sets whose teeth or rolling curve are not circular, such as elliptical, oval or other specially shaped profiles designed to alter speed ratios during a rotation. These non-standard gears are used where variable transmission or timing functions are required, often in indexing, cam or non-uniform motion applications.
[F16H 2720/04] Combining a planetary speed-change gearing with a motor vehicle drive axle differential -- This classification covers gear arrangements that combine a planetary speed-change gear set with the differential of a vehicle's drive axle, allowing variable gear ratios within the axle assembly.
[F16H 29/00] Gearings for conveying rotary motion with intermittently-driving members, e.g. with freewheel action -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings for conveying continuous rotary motion into a rotary output motion by using intermittently-driving members, e.g. with freewheel action:
[F16H 29/02] between one of the shafts and an oscillating or reciprocating intermediate member, not rotating with either of the shafts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings for conveying rotary motion from an input shaft to rotary motion of an output shaft, and including intermittently driving members, e.g. members with freewheel action. The intermittently driving members are located between one of the input and output shafts and an oscillating or reciprocating intermediate member. The intermittently driving members do not rotate with either of the input or output shafts.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gearings for conveying rotary motion with intermittently driving members, e.g. with freewheel action, the intermittently acting members being shaped as worms, screws or racks -> F16H29/20 - Gearings for conveying rotary motion with intermittently driving members, e.g. with freewheel action, with automatic speed change -> F16H29/22
[F16H 29/04] in which the transmission ratio is changed by adjustment of a crank, an eccentric, a wobble-plate, or a cam, on one of the shafts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a crank CVT including a rotary driving shaft (4), a rotary driven shaft (5), an adjustable eccentric (6), several cranks (8) and a freewheel clutch (7) between each of the cranks (8) and the driven shaft (5). Each crank (8) is considered an oscillating intermediate member, which does not rotate with either the drive shaft (4) or driven shaft (5). Each freewheel clutch (7) is regarded as an intermittently-driving member between each of the cranks (8) and driven shaft (5).
[F16H 29/06] with concentric shafts, an annular intermediate member moving around and being supported on an adjustable crank or eccentric -- The classification code F16H 29/06 pertains to gearings designed for conveying rotary motion using intermittently-driving members, specifically configurations where the shafts are concentric and feature an annular intermediate member that moves around and is supported by an adjustable crank or eccentric. This setup allows for precise control over the timing and nature of the intermittent motion.
[F16H 29/08] in which the transmission ratio is changed by adjustment of the path of movement, the location of the pivot, or the effective length, of an oscillating connecting member -- F16H 29/08 covers gearings that vary the transmission ratio by adjusting the movement path, the pivot location, or the effective length of an oscillating connecting member, typically used in mechanisms with intermittent rotary drive.
[F16H 29/10] in which the transmission ratio is changed by directly acting on the intermittently driving members -- F16H 29/10 covers gear arrangements that convey rotary motion using intermittently engaging members, such as ratchets or freewheels, where the transmission ratio is varied by directly influencing those intermittent members. The change in ratio is achieved through mechanisms that act on the engaging elements themselves rather than on the main gear set.
[F16H 29/12] between rotary driving and driven members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a gearing including levers (13) arranged between rotary driving shaft (1) and rotary driven shaft (5). Each lever (13) drives the driven shaft (5) only if it is the lever closest to teeth (7). Thus, each lever drives only intermittently and is considered as an intermittently-driving member between driving shaft (1) and driven shaft (5).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gearings for conveying rotary motion with intermittently driving members, e.g. with freewheel action, the intermittently acting members being shaped as worms, screws or racks -> F16H29/20 - Gearings for conveying rotary motion with intermittently driving members, e.g. with freewheel action, with automatic speed change -> F16H29/22
[F16H 29/14] in which the transmission ratio is changed by adjustment of an otherwise stationary guide member for the intermittently-driving members -- The subclass F16H 29/14 defines gearings that convey rotary motion intermittently, such as freewheel-type drives, where the transmission ratio is varied by adjusting a stationary guide that controls the motion of the intermittently-driving members.
[F16H 29/16] in which the transmission ratio is changed by adjustment of the distance between the axes of the rotary members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figure 1a illustrates a gearing including a driving shaft (20), a driven shaft (24) and levers (25). Each lever (25) drives the driven shaft (24) only if it is the lever closest to teeth (21). Thus, each lever drives only intermittently and is considered as an intermittently driving member between driving shaft (20) and driven shaft (24). The transmission ratio is changed by adjustment of the distance (eccentricity) between driving shaft (20) and driven shaft (24). Figure 1b illustrates a transmission ratio different from 1:1, while Figure 1a illustrates a transmission ratio of 1:1.
[F16H 29/18] in which the intermittently-driving members slide along approximately radial guides while rotating with one of the rotary members -- F16H 29/18 covers gear arrangements where the intermittently-driven members slide along essentially radial guides while rotating with a driving member, typical of freewheel or slip-gear mechanisms used to transmit rotary motion intermittently.
[F16H 29/20] the intermittently-acting members being shaped as worms, screws, or racks -- The F16H 29/20 subclass concerns gearings that convey rotary motion with intermittent action using members shaped as worms, screws or racks. These elements provide a free-wheel type transmission within the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing.
[F16H 29/22] with automatic speed change -- F16H 29/22 relates to gearings that transmit rotary motion using intermittently-driving members and incorporate an automatic speed-change mechanism. It is classified beneath mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing in the CPC scheme.
[F16H 3/00] Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings with variable gear ratio or reversing motion using only gears with teeth.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Speed-changing or reversing mechanisms -> F16H59/00 - F16H63/00
[F16H 3/001] convertible for varying the gear ratio, e.g. for selecting one of several shafts as the input shaft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings which are convertible when not being operated such that, after the conversion, a different gear ratio is provided. In other words, convertible does not mean shiftable during operation.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a gearing having an input shaft (21) and an output shaft (22). The gear ratio can be varied by converting the gearing outside of operation. This conversion is done by mounting output shaft (22) to a different one of gears (11-17). Thereby, different output gears are selected which result in different gear ratios.  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a gearing having an input shaft (5) and an output shaft (9). The gear ratio is varied from forward to reverse by converting the gearing as follows: the distance ring (13) is removed from its position between the casing body (1) and the lower lid (14) and is interposed between the casing body (1) and the upper lid (15). Thereby output shaft (9) is axially displaced so that input bevel gear (6) now meshes with lower output bevel gear (12) instead of upper output bevel gear (7) and the sense of rotation of the output shaft (8) being thereby reversed.  3.  Figure 3 illustrates a gearing including gear pair (56 and 58) which transfers torque during operation. Two replacement gear pairs (70 and 72) and (74 and 76) are stored in a chamber. When not in use, the gearing may be converted by substituting gear pair (56 and 58) with one of the two replacement gear pairs (70 and 72) and (74 and 76). This conversion varies the gear ratio.  **Glossary:** - convertible: an adaptation of the gearing when it is not in use. Convertible does not mean shiftable during operation of the gearing.
[F16H 3/002] using gears having teeth movable out of mesh   F16H3/16 ,  F16H3/20  and  F16H3/42  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  Figure 1a illustrates a gearing with a variable gear ratio including conical ring gear (6) meshing with gears (24), (25), (26), (27) and (28). Each of gears (24), (25), (26), (27) and (28) has teeth (29) which are radially moveable in and out of mesh with the teeth of conical ring gear (6).  1b.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Toothed gearing without orbital motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing, essentially with both gears that can be put out of gear and continuously-meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts -> F16H3/16 - Toothed gearing without orbital motion with variable gear ration or for reversing, exclusively or essentially using gears that can be moved out of gear -> F16H3/20 - Toothed gearing without orbital motion with variable gear ration or for reversing, with gears having teeth formed or arranged for obtaining multiple gear ratios, e.g. nearly infinitely variable -> F16H3/42
[F16H 3/003] the gear ratio being changed by inversion of torque direction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings in which the gear ratio is changed by inversion of torque, such that regardless of whether an input shaft changes rotation between clockwise and counter-clockwise, the output shaft always rotates in the same direction. For example, toothed gearing in which clockwise rotation of an input shaft results in clockwise rotation of an output shaft and a gear ratio of 1, and counter-clockwise rotation of the input shaft results in clockwise rotation of the output shaft and a gear ratio of -1.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a gearing with a variable gear ratio including an input shaft (2000) and an output shaft (3000). When input shaft (2000) rotates clockwise, output shaft (3000) rotates clockwise. When input shaft (2000) rotates counter-clockwise and output shaft (3000) rotates clockwise.  2a.  2b.  Figures 2a and 2b illustrate a gearing with a variable gear ratio including an input shaft (131) and an output shaft (133). Due to freewheels (151) and (153) the flow path and, thus the gear ratio, is changed when the direction of rotation of input shaft (131) is changed. The direction of rotation of output shaft (133) remains the same.
[F16H 3/005] for gearings using gears having orbital motion -- It covers gear mechanisms in which the gears follow an orbital motion, enabling a continuously varying gear ratio or reversal of rotary motion. Such gearings are employed in continuously variable transmissions and some planetary gear arrangements.
[F16H 3/006] power being selectively transmitted by parallel flow paths, e.g. dual clutch transmissions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Parallel selectable power or torque flow paths between the input and the output of the gearing, e.g. dual clutch transmissions.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a toothed gearing including parallel torque flow paths selectively created between input (10a) and output (15a) by clutches (K1a) and (K2a).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a toothed gearing including parallel torque flow paths selectively created between input (AN) and output (AB) by clutches (K1) and (K2).
[F16H 3/02] without gears having orbital motion -- This subclass covers transmission devices that provide a variable gear ratio or reverse rotation without using conventional toothed gears, relying instead on orbital motion of components. It includes mechanisms such as planetary, epicyclic, or other orbital arrangements that achieve speed change or reversal through rotating carriers or cams.
[F16H 3/04] with internally-toothed gears -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a multi-speed transmission, without gears having orbital motion, with internally toothed gears (6).
[F16H 3/06] with worm and worm-wheel or gears essentially having helical or herring-bone teeth -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a transmission without gears having orbital motion, comprising three worms (4a), (4b) and (4c) with different pitches, which may be engaged with gear (2) by being axially moved on input shaft (3). Thereby two forward speeds and one reverse speed are provided for output shaft (1).
[F16H 3/08] exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears, that can be disengaged from their shafts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a transmission without gears having orbital motion with only continuously meshing gears, e.g. (G06) and (G16) always meshing. The gears of the transmission can be disengaged from their shafts, e.g. gear (G16) can be disengaged from shaft (PIS) via coupling (PS2).
[F16H 3/083] with radially acting and axially controlled clutching members, e.g. sliding keys -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a three-speed transmission including balls (2) as radially acting clutching members. By axially moving sliding key (1), the three balls (2) are selectively radially moved into engagement with their respective idle gear.
[F16H 3/085] with more than one output shaft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts, e.g. gears (I), (II) and (III). The transmission comprises two output shafts (7) and (12). It is noted that differential casing (9) is not considered an output shaft.
[F16H 3/087] characterised by the disposition of the gears -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts, with radially acting and axially controlled clutching members, e.g. sliding keys -> F16H3/083 - Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts, with more than one output shaft -> F16H3/085
[F16H 3/089] all of the meshing gears being supported by a pair of parallel shafts, one being the input shaft and the other the output shaft, there being no countershaft involved -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears, that can be disengaged from their shafts, all of the meshing gears being supported by a pair of parallel shafts, one being the input shaft and the other the output shaft, there being no countershaft involved.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission comprises an input shaft (10) and an output shaft (20) which are parallel to each other. The transmission does not include a countershaft. It is noted that reverse countershaft (30), which is used for reversal only, is not counted as a countershaft, per the Note in F16H3/087 . It is further noted that differential casing (71) is not considered an output shaft, since the differential is not part of the multi-speed transmission.
[F16H 3/091] including a single countershaft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears, that can be disengaged from their shafts, including a single countershaft.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a six-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission comprises an input shaft (19) and an output shaft (22), which are parallel to each other, as well as a single countershaft (24). It is noted that reverse countershaft (40), which is used for reversal only, is not counted as a countershaft, per the Note in F16H3/087 . It is further noted that differential casing (15) is not considered an output shaft, since the differential is not part of the multi-speed transmission.
[F16H 3/0915] with coaxial input and output shafts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears, that can be disengaged from their shafts, including a single countershaft, with coaxial input and output shafts.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a four-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission comprises an input shaft (15) and output shaft (16), which are coaxial to each other, as well as a single countershaft (17).
[F16H 3/093] with two or more countershafts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears, that can be disengaged from their shafts, with two or more countershafts.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a six-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission comprises an input shaft (10), an output shaft (50) and two countershafts (20) and (30). It is noted that the differential housing (GFN) is not considered an output shaft, since the differential is not part of the multi-speed transmission.
[F16H 3/095] with means for ensuring an even distribution of torque between the countershafts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. Torque is simultaneously transmitted via both countershafts (14) and (16), i.e. torque is evenly distributed from the input shaft (32) to both countershafts (14) and (16).
[F16H 3/097] the input and output shafts being aligned on the same axis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears, that can be disengaged from their shafts, with two or more countershafts, the input and output shafts being aligned on the same axis.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission comprises two countershafts (4) and (6). It further comprises an input shaft (3) and an output shaft (1) which are coaxial.
[F16H 3/10] with one or more one-way clutches as an essential feature -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a three-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission comprises three one-way clutches (5a), (6a), and (7a) in addition to three friction clutches (5b), (6b) and (7b). During a shift of the transmission, the one-way clutches (5a), (6a), and (7a) allow an off-going friction clutch to remain engaged for a brief time while an on-coming friction clutch engages.
[F16H 31/00] Other gearings with freewheeling members or other intermittently driving members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings with freewheeling members or other intermittently-driving members for converting oscillating, i.e. non-continuous rotary input, or reciprocating movement into another movement, e.g. a step-by-step mechanism including a freewheel member.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gearings comprising primarily only links or levers -> F16H21/00 - Wobble plate or oblique crank gearings -> F16H23/00 - Gearings comprising primarily only cams, cam-followers and screw-and-nut mechanisms -> F16H25/00
[F16H 31/001] Mechanisms with freewheeling members -- F16H 31/001 covers gearings that include freewheeling or intermittently driven components, such as overrunning clutches, sprag devices or ratchet mechanisms, which allow rotation in one direction while preventing motion in the opposite direction. The classification applies to mechanical-engineering gear systems where certain members disengage automatically to transmit power only under selected conditions.
[F16H 31/002] Hand-driven ratchets -- A hand-driven ratchet is a mechanical gearing device that allows rotation in one direction while preventing motion in the opposite direction, using a pawl that engages teeth on a gear wheel. It provides intermittent, free-wheeling drive for manual tools and mechanisms.
[F16H 31/003] Step-by-step mechanisms for rotary motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanisms converting oscillating or reciprocating motion to rotary motion (or vice versa), which include movement in a stepwise, or step-by-step, manner such that an output member is alternately moving and at rest during continuous motion of an input member.
[F16H 31/004] with pawls driven by a rotary cam -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Step-by-step mechanisms for rotary motion including an intermittently driving member, wherein the intermittently driving member includes pawls driven by a rotary cam.
[F16H 31/005] with pawls driven by a reciprocating or oscillating transmission member  F16H31/002 ,  F16H31/004  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Step-by-step mechanisms for rotary motion including an intermittently driving member, wherein the intermittently driving member includes pawls driven by a reciprocating or oscillating transmission member.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Hand-driven ratchets -> F16H31/002 - Step-by-step mechanisms for rotary motion with pawls driven by a rotary cam -> F16H31/004
[F16H 31/006] with friction means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Step-by-step mechanisms for rotary motion including an intermittently driving member, wherein the intermittently driving member includes friction means.
[F16H 31/007] Step-by-step mechanisms for linear motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanisms for linear motion which include movement in a stepwise, or step-by-step, manner such that an output member is alternately moving and at rest during continuous motion of an input member.
[F16H 31/008] with friction means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Step-by-step mechanisms for linear motion including an intermittently driving member, wherein the intermittently driving member includes friction means.
[F16H 3/12] with means for synchronisation not incorporated in the clutches -- F16H 3/12 covers toothed gearings that provide a variable or reverse rotary motion and include synchronization devices that are separate from any clutch mechanisms, ensuring coordinated gear engagement without the clutch being part of the synchronising system.
[F16H 3/126] using an electric drive -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially with continuously meshing gears, that can be disengaged from their shafts, with means for synchronisation not incorporated in the clutches, using an electric drive.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a six-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission uses electric motor (29) as means for synchronisation.
[F16H 3/14] Gearings for reversal only -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a transmission without gears having orbital motion and including continuously meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts. The transmission includes one forward speed via meshing gears (3) and (4), and one reverse speed via meshing gears (7), (8) and (10).
[F16H 3/145] with a pair of coaxial bevel gears, rotatable in opposite directions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a transmission without gears having orbital motion. The transmission includes bevel gears (2) and (3) which are continuously meshing with gear (6). This allows bevel gears (2) and (3) to rotate in opposite directions. Bevel gears (2) and (3) can be disengaged from shafts (12a) and (12b), respectively, as shaft assembly (7) is moved axially.
[F16H 3/16] essentially with both gears that can be put out of gear and continuously-meshing gears that can be disengaged from their shafts -- In this group, gears which can be put out of mesh are not taken into consideration if they are used for reversal only.
[F16H 3/18] Gearings for reversal only -- F16H 3/18 covers gearings designed solely to reverse the direction of rotary motion, such as reverse gears in transmissions, without providing a variable ratio. These mechanisms typically consist of a pair of meshing gears that change the rotation sense when engaged.
[F16H 3/20] exclusively or essentially using gears that can be moved out of gear -- In this group, gears which can be put out of mesh are not taken into consideration if they are used for reversal only.
[F16H 3/22] with gears shiftable only axially -- F16H 3/22 covers toothed gearings that achieve a variable gear ratio or reverse rotation by moving the gears only along the axis of rotation, allowing axial shifting of the gear elements to change meshing. This arrangement is used in mechanical systems where compact, continuously adjustable transmission is required.
[F16H 3/24] with driving and driven shafts coaxial -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially using gears that can be moved out of gear, with gears shiftable only axially, with driving and driven shafts coaxial.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion. The transmission includes an input shaft (2) and an output shaft (3), which is coaxial to input shaft (2). Each of gears (19), (20) and (21) is axially shiftable such that they can be moved in and out of gear.
[F16H 3/26] and two or more additional shafts -- F16H 3/26 covers gear trains that include two or more auxiliary shafts in addition to the main drive and driven shafts, enabling variable transmission ratios or reversal of rotation. These configurations are used in mechanical systems where multiple shafts are needed to achieve complex motion control.
[F16H 3/28] an additional shaft being coaxial with the main shafts -- F16H 3/28 concerns gear assemblies that include an additional shaft positioned coaxially with the main shafts, allowing the transmission to vary the gear ratio or reverse the direction of rotary motion.
[F16H 3/30] with driving and driven shafts not coaxial -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Toothed gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion, without gears having orbital motion, exclusively or essentially using gears that can be moved out of gear, with gears shiftable only axially, with driving and driven shafts not coaxial.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion. The transmission includes an input shaft (1) and an output shaft (2), which is not coaxial to input shaft (1). Each of gears (3), (4), (5), (6), (17), (18), (19) and (20) is axially shiftable such that they can be moved in and out of gear.
[F16H 33/00] Gearings based on repeated accumulation and delivery of energy -- This subclass covers gearings that accumulate energy over time and release it in a repeated, controlled manner, such as mechanisms using flywheels, elastic elements or hydraulic accumulators to store and deliver power repeatedly to the driven gear train.
[F16H 33/02] Rotary transmissions with mechanical accumulators, e.g. weights, springs, intermittently-connected flywheels -- F16H 33/02 covers rotary transmission systems that incorporate mechanical energy accumulators such as weighted masses, springs, or flywheels which are coupled to the drive intermittently to store and release energy. These devices are used to smooth power delivery, provide surge capacity, or enable periodic motion in mechanical gear arrangements.
[F16H 33/04] Gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable velocity ratio, in which self-regulation is sought -- F16H 33/04 classifies gearings that transmit rotary motion while providing a variable velocity ratio, wherein the mechanism self-regulates the speed conversion without external control, typically to maintain a desired output despite changing load or speed conditions.
[F16H 33/06] based essentially on spring action -- F16H 33/06 covers gearings that obtain their motion primarily from the stored energy in a spring, using the spring's expansion or contraction to transmit power through a gear train. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering elements concerned with gearing mechanisms that repeatedly accumulate and deliver energy.
[F16H 33/08] based essentially on inertia -- This subclass covers gear mechanisms that store kinetic energy in rotating masses and release it repeatedly, such as flywheel-driven gearings where inertia is the primary means of energy transfer.
[F16H 33/10] with gyroscopic action, e.g. comprising wobble-plates, oblique cranks -- The F16H 33/10 subclass covers gear mechanisms that incorporate gyroscopic effects, such as those using wobble plates or oblique cranks to transfer and accumulate energy repetitively. These devices exploit the orientation-dependent inertia of rotating masses to achieve controlled motion within mechanical engineering gear systems.
[F16H 33/12] with a driving member connected differentially with both a driven member and an oscillatory member with large resistance to movement, e.g. Constantinesco gearing -- This subclass covers gearings where the driving member is connected differentially to both a driven member and an oscillatory member that presents a large resistance to movement, such as Constantinesco gearing used for repeated accumulation and delivery of energy.
[F16H 33/14] having orbital members influenced by regulating masses -- The subclass concerns gear arrangements in which orbital gear members have their motion modified or stabilized by regulating masses, such as weights, pendulums or counter-balancing elements that influence the speed or torque transmission. These devices use the masses to control energy accumulation and release within the gear set.
[F16H 33/16] which have their own free motion, or consist of fluid -- F16H 33/16 classifies gearings whose motion is generated internally, either by parts that move freely without external drive or by the flow of fluid through the mechanism. It includes devices such as fluid couplings, hydraulic gearings, and other self-acting transmission systems that accumulate and deliver energy without the need for a separate power source.
[F16H 33/18] of which the motion is constrained -- F16H 33/18 covers gearings that deliver energy by repeated accumulation but restrict the resulting motion, such as mechanisms that limit rotation through cams, ratchets, brakes or other constraints on the driven element. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → gearing → gearings based on repeated accumulation and delivery of energy.
[F16H 33/185] the masses being fixed to the orbital members -- F16H 33/185 concerns gear arrangements in which masses are permanently attached to the rotating members, such as wheels or gear teeth, to modify inertia, balance, or energy-storage characteristics. The fixed masses influence the dynamics of the gear set, aiding in smooth operation, vibration reduction, or controlled energy release during rotation.
[F16H 3/32] and an additional shaft -- F16H 3/32 refers to toothed gearings that incorporate an additional shaft to achieve a variable gear ratio or to reverse rotary motion. This subclass is used for gear trains where an extra shaft provides intermediate speed changes or direction reversal.
[F16H 33/20] for interconversion, based essentially on inertia, of rotary motion and reciprocating or oscillating motion -- The entry F16H 33/20 covers mechanisms that transform rotary motion into reciprocating or oscillating motion, or vice-versa, using the inertia of moving masses such as flywheels, pendulums or other stored-energy elements. It focuses on interconversion that relies primarily on the kinetic energy already accumulated in the system.
[F16H 3/34] with gears shiftable otherwise than only axially -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion. The transmission includes an input shaft (3) and an output shaft (5). Each of gears (9) and (10) is shiftable in a circumferential direction such that they can be moved in and out of gear.
[F16H 3/36] with a single gear meshable with any of a set of coaxial gears of different diameters -- F16H 3/36 pertains to gearing systems where a single gear can engage with multiple coaxial gears of varying sizes, facilitating changes in gear ratio or direction. This classification is part of the broader category of toothed gearings designed for conveying rotary motion with variable ratios or reversing capabilities.
[F16H 3/363] the teeth of the set of coaxial gears being arranged on a surface of generally conical shape -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1. The transmission includes coaxial gears (16a-16k) have conical teeth and are arranged on a surface of generally conical shape.  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion. The transmission includes coaxial gears (18), which do not have conical teeth, but are arranged on a surface of generally conical shape.
[F16H 3/366] the teeth of the set of coaxial gears being arranged on a generally flat, e.g. disc-type, surface -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion. The transmission includes three coaxial gears arranged on flat disc-type surface (12) which engage with the gear on (16).
[F16H 3/38] with synchro-meshing -- F16H 3/38 covers toothed gear sets designed to transmit rotary motion with a variable gear ratio or to reverse rotation, and that incorporate synchro-meshing to ensure smooth, simultaneous engagement of the gear teeth.
[F16H 3/385] with braking means -- F16H 3/385 covers toothed gearings that transmit rotary motion with a variable gear ratio or that reverse motion and that include an integrated braking device to control, stop, or hold the output rotation. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing.
[F16H 3/40] Gearings for reversal only -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b. Forward gear  1c. Reverse gear  Figures 1a-1c illustrate a multi-speed transmission without gears having orbital motion. The transmission includes one forward gear (Figure 1b) and one reverse gear (Figure 1c). Gears (21) and (22) may be moved in and out of mesh with output gear (12) when pivotable lever (23) is pivoted around the axis of input shaft (9).

[F16H 3/426] the teeth being arranged on a generally flat, e.g. disc-type surface -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a transmission without gears having orbital motion and providing a continuously variable gear ratio. The teeth of gear 1 are arranged on a flat disc-type surface.
[F16H 3/44] using gears having orbital motion -- F16H 3/44 pertains to mechanisms that employ gears whose axes describe an orbital path during operation, such as planetary or epicyclic gear sets used to vary speed or reverse rotation. The classification covers designs where the gear teeth engage while the gear carrier rotates around a central axis, enabling compact variable-ratio drives.
[F16H 3/46] Gearings having only two central gears, connected by orbital gears -- F16H 3/46 pertains to gearings that consist of only two central gears linked together by orbital gears, enabling a variable or reversing transmission of rotary motion. These mechanisms fall under the mechanical-engineering category of gearing, specifically within engineering elements and units for conveying motion with a variable gear ratio.
[F16H 3/48] with single orbital gears or pairs of rigidly-connected orbital gears -- F16H 3/48 covers gear trains that use a single orbital gear or a pair of orbital gears that are rigidly connected, enabling the transmission of rotary motion with a variable ratio or reversal. In these arrangements the gear elements orbit around a central axis while remaining meshed with other gears, providing compact variable-ratio transmission solutions.
[F16H 3/50] comprising orbital conical gears -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a two-speed planetary transmission including two central gears, i.e. left bevel gear (165) and right bevel gear (160), connected by orbital conical gears (155) and (170).
[F16H 35/00] Gearings or mechanisms with other special functional features -- F16H 35/00 covers gearings or mechanisms that incorporate special functional features beyond basic transmission, such as variable speed control, self-locking, shock-absorbing elements, or integrated braking. These designs are classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing, and relate to inventive solutions that modify standard gear operation for enhanced performance or safety.
[F16H 35/008] for variation of rotational phase relationship, e.g. angular relationship between input and output shaft -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings or mechanisms that include means to vary the rotational phase relationship, e.g. gearings that include means to vary the angular relationship between the input and the output shaft.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a toothed gearing (28) including input shaft (30), output shafts (32) and (34), and planetary gearing (38). The rotational phase relationship between output shafts (32) and (34) is adjusted by rotating ring gear (40) via worm (48) about an angle which corresponds to the desired phase relationship between shafts (32) and (34).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a chain-type gearing including input sprocket (30), output sprocket (62), tensioner sprocket (64) and sprocket (40). The rotational phase relationship between input sprocket (30) and output sprocket (62) is adjusted by varying the position of sprocket (40).
[F16H 35/02] for conveying rotary motion with cyclically varying velocity ratio -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a toothed gearing including input shaft (2) and output gear (3). Rotary motion of input shaft (2) is conveyed via elliptic gears (11a) and (11b), thereby varying the velocity ratio during any single revolution of elliptic gears (11a) and (11b), such that the velocity ratio is varied cyclically.  2a.  2b.  Figures 2a and 2b illustrate a toothed gearing including input shaft (10) and output shaft (20). Rotary motion of input shaft (10) is conveyed via gears (11) and (21), thereby varying the velocity ratio during any single revolution of gears (11) and (21) such that the velocity ratio is varied cyclically.
[F16H 35/06] Gearings designed to allow relative movement between supports thereof without ill effects -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Special means compensating for misalignment of axes -> F16H1/26 - Special means compensating for misalignment of axes for gears having orbital motion -> F16H1/48
[F16H 35/08] for adjustment of members on moving parts from a stationary place -- F16H 35/08 classifies gearings or mechanisms that incorporate means to adjust components on moving parts while the adjustment point remains stationary. It covers devices that allow the position or engagement of gears, cams, or other transmission elements to be varied without moving the adjustment tool with the driven element.
[F16H 35/10] Arrangements or devices for absorbing overload or preventing damage by overload -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate a torque limiter comprising a planetary gear train having a sun gear (5), planetary gears (6), a planetary carrier (8) and a brake (11). Brake (11) includes four brake lamellae (18, 18', 19, 19'), which are biased by spring (14) against brake lamellae (15, 16, 17) which are fixed to housing (1). If the torque exceeds a certain threshold, brake (11) starts to slip such that torque transfer from sun gear (5) to planet carrier (8) is limited and overload is prevented.  2a.  2b.  Figures 2a and 2b illustrate a torque interrupter comprising a pair of toothed gears (52) and (54). If the torque exceeds a certain threshold, gear (52) is radially moved out of mesh against the force of biasing spring (76) such that torque transfer is interrupted and overload is prevented.
[F16H 35/12] Transmitting mechanisms with delayed effect -- F16H 35/12 covers gearings or transmission systems that incorporate a built-in delay between input motion and output motion, such as mechanisms using cams, springs, hydraulic accumulators or other elastic or fluid elements to postpone power delivery, allowing controlled timing or shock absorption in mechanical drives.
[F16H 35/14] Mechanisms with only two stable positions, e.g. acting at definite angular positions -- The F16H 35/14 class covers gearing mechanisms that lock into only two stable angular positions, such as devices that snap between predefined angles and remain fixed until an external force moves them. These mechanisms are used where a binary, on-off rotational state is required, often in safety locks, indexing couplings, and other precision positioning systems.
[F16H 35/16] Mechanisms for movements or movement relations conforming to mathematical formulae -- F16H 35/16 covers mechanisms that generate or control motion according to specified mathematical relationships, such as cams, linkages or variable transmissions whose motion parameters are defined by formulae. These devices are used to achieve precise, predictable movement patterns in mechanical systems.
[F16H 35/18] Turning devices for rotatable members, e.g. shafts -- F16H 35/18 covers turning devices that drive or rotate shafts and other rotatable members, typically used in gear mechanisms with special functional features. The classification includes cranks, cam shafts and similar mechanisms that convert reciprocating motion into rotational motion for power transmission.
[F16H 3/52] comprising orbital spur gears -- F16H 3/52 covers gear trains that include orbital spur gears, a type of spur gear whose axis moves on an orbital path to provide variable speed ratios or reverse motion. It is placed in the hierarchy under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 3/54] one of the central gears being internally toothed and the other externally toothed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.
