[F16H 49/005] Magnetic gearings with physical contact between gears -- F16H 49/005 covers magnetic gearings where the rotating components are in direct physical contact, typically using ferromagnetic or conductive teeth that interact with a magnetic field to transmit torque. Unlike non-contact magnetic couplings, these devices combine magnetic flux transmission with mechanical engagement.
[F16H 51/00] Details of gearing or mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Levers for gearing mechanisms.
[F16H 51/02] adjustable -- F16H 51/02 covers gears or gear mechanisms that incorporate adjustability, allowing the gear position, spacing or mesh to be varied during operation or assembly. The subclass includes devices such as adjustable crown wheels, shiftable gear sets and mechanisms for fine-tuning transmission ratios. It belongs to mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 53/00] Cams or cam-followers, e.g. rollers for gearing mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cams, camshafts or cam followers.
[F16H 53/02] Single-track cams for single-revolution cycles -- F16H 53/02 covers single-track cam profiles that complete one functional cycle in a single revolution of the cam shaft. It applies to cam or cam-follower elements-such as rollers-used in gearing mechanisms where the cam's motion is confined to one uninterrupted lap per turn.
[F16H 53/025] characterised by their construction, e.g. assembling or manufacturing features -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F16H 53/04] Adjustable cams -- F16H 53/04 relates to adjustable cam mechanisms in which the cam profile, position, or geometry can be varied to modify the motion transferred to a follower, encompassing interchangeable cam plates, adjustable lobes, and cams with movable elements that permit fine-tuning of timing, lift or dwell characteristics.
[F16H 53/06] Cam-followers -- F16H 53/06 defines cam-followers, components that engage with a cam profile to transmit motion, often rollers or other elements within gear mechanisms. These devices convert the cam's angular displacement into a prescribed follower movement, typically used in mechanical timing or control systems.
[F16H 53/08] Multi-track cams, e.g. for cycles consisting of several revolutions -- F16H 53/08 classifies multi-track cam assemblies, such as those used in cycling gear mechanisms that require several successive revolutions to complete a motion cycle. These cams feature multiple parallel cam surfaces that engage followers in a coordinated sequence, enabling complex timing or variable transmission functions.
[F16H 55/00] Elements with teeth or friction surfaces for conveying motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Of screw-and-nut gearing -> F16H25/00 , F16H25/20
[F16H 55/02] Toothed members -- F16H 55/02 covers toothed members such as gears, sprockets and similar components that transmit motion by interlocking teeth. It includes elements with teeth or friction surfaces used for conveying rotation or linear motion in mechanical transmission systems.
[F16H 55/06] Use of materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F16H 55/08] Profiling -- Profiling refers to the processes and methods used to form or modify the tooth geometry of gears and other toothed components, shaping the surfaces that engage to transmit motion. It encompasses machining, grinding, hobbing, and finishing operations that determine the precise profile required for efficient power transmission. This class covers technologies for creating and adjusting gear tooth forms in mechanical engineering.
[F16H 55/0806] Involute profile -- The F16H 55/0806 entry covers gears that use an involute tooth profile, a standard shape where the tooth flank follows the curve traced by a line unwinding from a base circle. This geometry provides smooth meshing and constant velocity transmission between gear elements. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and the gearing subgroup.
[F16H 55/0813] Intersecting-shaft arrangement of the toothed members -- This subclass deals with gear arrangements in which the shafts bearing the toothed members intersect, enabling power transmission between intersecting axes in mechanical systems.
[F16H 55/082] Skewed-shaft arrangement of the toothed members, i.e. non-intersecting shafts -- F16H 55/082 covers gear arrangements where the shafts bearing the toothed members are skewed rather than intersecting, enabling power transmission between axes that are neither parallel nor coplanar. This configuration is used to transmit motion while keeping the shafts non-intersecting.
[F16H 55/0826] Novikov-Wildhaber profile -- F16H 55/0826, the Novikov-Wildhaber profile, designates a particular gear tooth shape used in mechanical transmissions to optimise load distribution and noise reduction. It belongs to the Gearing subclass of Mechanical Engineering, covering elements with teeth or friction surfaces that convey motion. This profile specifies the contour and dimensions of the gear tooth.
[F16H 55/0833] Flexible toothed member, e.g. harmonic drive -- F16H 55/0833 classifies flexible toothed members used in gear assemblies, most notably harmonic drives. These components consist of a deformable spline that meshes with a rigid circular spline to transmit motion and achieve high reduction ratios in compact, precision mechanisms.
[F16H 55/084] Non-circular rigid toothed member, e.g. elliptic gear -- F16H 55/084 covers rigid gear elements whose teeth are cut on a non-circular shape such as an ellipse, used to produce variable speed or displacement in gear trains. The classification applies to gears that maintain a fixed tooth profile while the overall pitch contour deviates from a circle.
[F16H 55/0846] Intersecting-shaft arrangement of the toothed members  F16H55/0813 ,  F16H55/0826 ,  F16H55/0833 ,  F16H55/084  take precedence -- F16H 55/0846 covers gear assemblies in which the shafts carrying the toothed elements intersect, defining the spatial relationship of intersecting shafts and their gear teeth. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, and elements with teeth or friction surfaces for conveying motion, and is subordinate to the broader groups F16H55/0813, F16H55/0826, F16H55/0833 and F16H55/084.
[F16H 55/0853] Skewed-shaft arrangement of the toothed members  F16H55/082 ,  F16H55/0826 ,  F16H55/0833 ,  F16H55/084  take precedence -- F16H 55/0853 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers skewed-shaft arrangements of toothed members for conveying motion, where the axes of the gears are not parallel. This classification is specific to designs that prioritize the orientation and interaction of gear teeth on non-parallel shafts, with related but more specific codes taking precedence.
[F16H 55/0873] for improving axial engagement, e.g. a chamfer at the end of the tooth flank -- This CPC subclass F16H 55/0873 covers modifications to gear tooth flanks that improve axial engagement, such as adding a chamfer at the end of the tooth. The chamfer eases meshing, reduces impact forces and enhances load distribution along the gear axis.
[F16H 55/088] with corrections on tip or foot of the teeth, e.g. addendum relief for better approach contact -- F16H 55/088 pertains to gears whose tooth tip or foot is deliberately altered, for example by adding addendum relief, to improve the approach contact between teeth and enhance meshing performance.
[F16H 55/0886] with corrections along the width, e.g. flank width crowning for better load distribution -- F16H 55/0886 refers to gear elements whose tooth surfaces are altered across the gear width, for example by flank-width crowning, to obtain a more even distribution of load during operation.
[F16H 55/10] Constructively simple tooth shapes, e.g. shaped as pins, as balls -- F16H 55/10 concerns gear elements whose teeth are formed by simple geometries such as pins or balls rather than conventional profiled teeth. These constructively simple tooth shapes are used to transmit motion by friction or impact between the pin or ball and a mating surface. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 55/12] with body or rim assembled out of detachable parts -- F16H 55/12 covers gear or toothed wheel designs whose body or rim is constructed from separate, detachable components that can be assembled together. This includes modular gears where the rim and hub are separate pieces joined by bolts, screws, or other detachable connections, facilitating maintenance or customization.
[F16H 55/14] Construction providing resilience or vibration-damping -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Toothed gear wheels using particular materials for providing resilience or vibration damping -> F16H55/06
[F16H 55/16] relating to teeth only -- The CPC subclass F16H 55/16 deals with gear elements that concern only the teeth themselves, such as their shape, profile, geometry or surface characteristics, without reference to the gear body, hub or assembly. It is applied to inventions that modify or improve tooth design, meshing or contact behaviour.
[F16H 55/17] Toothed wheels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Worm wheels -> F16H55/22 - Chain wheels or sprockets -> F16H55/30
[F16H 55/171] Toothed belt pulleys -- F16H 55/171 covers pulleys that are adapted for toothed or synchronous belts, featuring teeth or ribbed surfaces that mesh directly with the belt's teeth to transmit motion. These pulleys form part of gearing elements that convey power without slip.
[F16H 55/18] Special devices for taking up backlash -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arrangements for adjusting or for taking-up backlash for planetary gearings conveying rotary motion -> F16H1/2863
[F16H 55/20] for bevel gears -- F16H 55/20 covers bevel gears, which are conical gears that transmit rotary motion between intersecting shafts at a fixed angle, commonly used to change speed or direction in gearboxes and other mechanical systems.
[F16H 55/22] for transmissions with crossing shafts, especially worms, worm-gears -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F16H 55/24] Special devices for taking up backlash -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Worm gears or worm wheels with means for taking up backlash.
[F16H 55/26] Racks -- F16H 55/26 covers racks, which are linear toothed elements that engage with pinions to convert rotary motion into linear motion or vice versa. Racks are used in gear drives, steering systems, and machinery where precise linear displacement is required.
[F16H 55/28] Special devices for taking up backlash -- F16H 55/28 designates devices that eliminate or compensate for backlash in gear systems, such as spring-loaded adjusters, preloaded couplings, or other mechanisms that keep meshing teeth in constant contact. These special take-up devices are used to improve precision and reduce wear in mechanical transmissions.
[F16H 55/283] using pressure yokes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pressure yokes for biasing racks against pinion gears in order to take up backlash.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a rack and pinion gearing including a rack (16a) which is biased against a pinion gear (20a) using a pressure yoke (100) and a spring (102).
[F16H 55/285] with rollers or balls to reduce friction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pressure yokes with rollers or ball to reduce friction, for biasing racks against pinion gears in order to take up backlash.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.  Figures 1a and 1b illustrate an example of a rack and pinion gearing including a rack (2) which is biased against pinion (6) using a pressure yoke (8) and spring (25). Balls (19) reduce friction on rack (2).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a rack and pinion gearing including a rack (4) which is biased against a pinion gear (2) by a pressure yoke (5) and a spring (7). Roller bearings (9) reduce friction on rack (4).
[F16H 55/286] with asymmetric layout of the yoke -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pressure yokes with asymmetric layout of the yoke, for biasing racks against pinion gears in order to take up backlash.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a rack and pinion gearing including a rack (1') which is biased against a pinion gear (3') using an asymmetric yoke (7') and a spring (9').  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a rack and pinion gearing including a rack (29) which is biased against a pinion gear (28) using an asymmetric yoke (31) and a spring (33).
[F16H 55/288] comprising two or more pressure yokes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Two or more pressure yokes, for biasing racks against pinion gears in order to take up backlash.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a rack and pinion gearing including a rack (120) which is biased against a pinion gear (111) using two yokes (170) and respective springs (160).  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a rack and pinion gearing including a rack (41) which is biased against a pinion gear using two yokes (25) and respective springs (39).
[F16H 55/30] Chain-wheels -- Chain-wheels are circular sprockets with external teeth that mesh with a chain to transmit rotary motion and power in mechanical assemblies such as bicycles, conveyors and drive systems. They belong to the class of gearing elements where teeth or friction surfaces convey motion.
[F16H 55/303] for round linked chains, i.e. hoisting chains with identical links -- F16H 55/303 designates round linked chains, such as hoisting chains with identical links, classified under mechanical engineering gear elements that convey motion via teeth or friction surfaces. These chains are used for lifting loads and belong to the broader category of gearing elements.
[F16H 55/32] Friction members -- F16H 55/32 in the CPC designates friction members used in gearing systems, covering components that transmit motion by surface friction rather than interlocking teeth, such as belts, chains, friction wheels and similar elements with contact surfaces.
[F16H 55/34] Non-adjustable friction discs -- Non-adjustable friction discs are fixed-position friction elements used in clutches or brakes to transmit torque without any means for axial or radial adjustment. They belong to the gearing group of mechanical engineering components that convey motion through friction surfaces, and are classified under CPC code F16H 55/34.
[F16H 55/36] Pulleys -- Pulleys are rotating wheels or drums with grooved or smooth surfaces used to transmit or change the direction of motion by means of a belt, rope, chain or cable. In the CPC they belong to the mechanical engineering group for elements with teeth or friction surfaces that convey motion, under gearing. The code F16H 55/36 specifically covers pulleys and related components.
[F16H 55/38] Means or measures for increasing adhesion -- The subclass F16H 55/38 concerns devices, surface treatments, or design features that enhance the grip between toothed or frictional gear elements, raising adhesion to prevent slip during power transmission. It includes coatings, texturing, clamps or other measures that increase friction between moving parts.
[F16H 55/40] with spokes -- F16H 55/40 covers gearing elements that incorporate spokes, where a toothed rim is supported by a spoke framework instead of a solid disc, enabling motion transmission with reduced mass and improved cooling.
[F16H 55/42] Laminated pulleys -- F16H 55/42 designates laminated pulleys, which are pulley elements built from layered (laminated) materials to form a friction or toothed drive component for transmitting motion in mechanical gear systems.
[F16H 55/44] Sheet-metal pulleys -- Sheet-metal pulleys are flat or slightly curved components fabricated from rolled or stamped metal that transmit rotational motion by friction or engagement with a belt, rope or cable. Classified under gearing elements with teeth or friction surfaces, they are used in conveyors, machines and other power-transfer systems.
[F16H 55/46] Split pulleys -- F16H 55/46 in the CPC covers split pulleys, which are toothed wheels mounted on a common shaft that can be axially separated or engaged to vary the effective gear ratio in a transmission. They are used in mechanical engineering applications where continuous, stepless speed change is required, such as in automotive or industrial drives.
[F16H 55/48] manufactured exclusively or in part of non-metallic material, e.g. plastics -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Means or measures for increasing adhesion of pulleys -> F16H55/38 - Laminated pulleys -> F16H55/42 - Split pulleys -> F16H55/46
[F16H 55/49] Features essential to V-belts pulleys -- F16H 55/49 covers the functional and structural characteristics of pulleys designed specifically for V-belts, such as groove geometry, rib profile, material selection, tensioning and mounting features that are essential for reliable power transmission.
[F16H 55/50] Features essential to rope pulleys -- This subclass relates to the essential design features of rope pulleys, such as groove geometry, bearing arrangements, flanges and other structural elements that allow a rope to run smoothly and transmit loads effectively. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy covering gearing and elements with teeth or friction surfaces.
[F16H 55/52] Pulleys or friction discs of adjustable construction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pulleys or friction discs where the diameter of the pulley or friction disc can be modified in order to adjust the placement of the flexible member.
[F16H 55/54] of which the bearing parts are radially adjustable -- F16H 55/54 covers gear elements whose bearing parts are radially adjustable, enabling precise positioning of the gear on its shaft. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, specifically elements with teeth or friction surfaces for conveying motion.
[F16H 55/56] of which the bearing parts are relatively axially adjustable -- F16H 55/56 covers gearing elements whose bearing parts can be moved relative to each other along the axis, allowing axial adjustment of the teeth or friction surfaces. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 55/563] actuated by centrifugal masses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pulleys or friction discs with axially adjustable bearing parts and centrifugal masses for automatically changing the active diameter of the pulley or friction disc dependent on speed.
[F16H 55/566] only adjustable when pulley is stationary -- F16H 55/566 concerns gearing elements that can be adjusted only when the pulley is stationary, requiring the device to be set while the wheel is not rotating. It classifies adjustable sheave or gear arrangements whose adjustment is performed in a fixed position.
[F16H 57/00] General details of gearing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - General details of screw-and-nut gearing -> F16H25/00 - General details of fluid gearing -> F16H39/00 - F16H43/00
[F16H 57/0006] Vibration-damping or noise reducing means specially adapted for gearings  devices for varying tension of belts, ropes or chains with damping means  F16H7/0829 ; toothed members with construction providing vibration damping  F16H55/14 ; reducing vibrations or noise of the gearbox casing  F16H57/028 ; suppression of vibrations or noise of gear selectors  F16H59/0208 ; control of hydrostatic fluid gearing preventing or reducing vibrations or noise  F16H61/4183 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Belt tension means with vibration damping means -> F16H7/0829 - Construction of toothed members providing resilience or vibration-damping -> F16H55/14 - Gearboxes characterised by means for reducing vibration or noise -> F16H57/028 - Selector apparatus with means for suppression of vibrations or reduction of noise -> F16H59/0208 - Preventing or reducing vibrations or noise, e.g. avoiding cavitations, in control of hydrostatic gearing -> F16H61/4183
[F16H 57/0018] Shaft assemblies for gearings  camshafts with single track cams  F16H53/02 -- The CPC entry F16H 57/0018 covers shaft assemblies that incorporate camshafts with a single track of cams used in gearing systems, focusing on the design and arrangement of shafts that drive or coordinate gear motion where cam profiles are mounted on one continuous track for timing or actuation.
[F16H 57/0025] with gearing elements rigidly connected to a shaft, e.g. securing gears or pulleys by specially adapted splines, keys or methods -- F16H 57/0025 covers gearing components that are permanently attached to a shaft, such as gears or pulleys secured by specially designed splines, keys or similar fastening methods, ensuring a rigid, non-rotational connection. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing.
[F16H 57/0031] with gearing elements rotatable supported on the shaft  F16H57/021  takes precedence -- F16H 57/0031 refers to gear arrangements where the gearing elements are rotatable and are supported directly on the same shaft, describing the mounting configuration of gears. This subclass falls under the general details of gearing in mechanical engineering, but is superseded by the more specific subclass F16H 57/021.
[F16H 57/0037] Special features of coaxial shafts, e.g. relative support thereof -- This subclass covers innovations related to coaxial shafts within gear assemblies, focusing on features such as how the shafts are mutually supported, positioned or constrained relative to each other. It includes designs that improve alignment, load sharing or rotational stability of the shafts that share a common axis.
[F16H 57/01] Monitoring wear or stress of gearing elements, e.g. for triggering maintenance -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices attached to the gearbox or gearing for:
[F16H 57/02] Gearboxes -- F16H 57/02 covers gearboxes, the assemblies that transmit power between rotating shafts via gear sets. It includes the design, construction, and arrangement of gear trains within a housing, together with related components such as shafts, bearings and sealing elements.
[F16H 57/02004] the gears being positioned relative to one another by rolling members or by specially adapted surfaces on the gears, e.g. by a rolling surface with the diameter of the pitch circle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gears mounted in the gearbox by positioning them relative to one another by rolling members or by specially adapted surfaces on the gears, e.g. by a rolling surface with the diameter of the pitch circle.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates a pair of toothed spur gears (1) and (2) positioned relative to each other using left and right thrust collars attached to the surface of gear (2). The thrust collars are regarded as comprising specially adapted surfaces on the gears by which the two spur gears are positioned relative to one another.  2.  Figure 2 illustrates a pair of toothed spur gears (1) and (2) positioned relative to each other. Spur gear (1) has projections (5) between its teeth which interact with grooves 6 between the teeth of spur gear (2). Projections (5) and grooves (6) are regarded as comprising specially adapted surfaces on the gears by which the two spur gears are positioned relative to one another.  3.  Figure 3 illustrates a pair of toothed spur gears (62) and (72) positioned relative to one another using rolling members (64) and (74). Due to bearing (66), rolling members (64) and (74) do not transfer any torque and maintain alignment of the gears.
[F16H 57/021] Shaft support structures, e.g. partition walls, bearing eyes, casing walls or covers with bearings -- F16H 57/021 relates to shaft support structures used in gearing systems, covering elements such as partition walls, bearing eyes, casing walls or covers that incorporate bearings to locate, guide and support rotating shafts. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing.
[F16H 57/022] Adjustment of gear shafts or bearings -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - For compensating misalignment of axes of toothed gearings without orbital motion -> F16H1/26 - For compensating misalignment of axes of planetary gears -> F16H1/48
[F16H 57/023] Mounting or installation of gears or shafts in the gearboxes, e.g. methods or means for assembly -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and procedures for mounting or installing gearing elements or shafts in gearboxes.  Methods for mounting or installing gearing elements by using tools, e.g. guide members, jigs or the like.
[F16H 57/025] Support of gearboxes, e.g. torque arms, or attachment to other devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for supporting gearboxes or attaching them to other devices in order to prevent the casing from being moved due to reaction forces or torques produced by the gearing, e.g. torque arms.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F16H 57/027] characterised by means for venting gearboxes, e.g. air breathers -- F16H 57/027 falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → gearing and relates to gearboxes that incorporate ventilation features. The subclass covers means for venting gearboxes, such as air breathers, which relieve internal pressure and allow removal of contaminants.
[F16H 57/028] characterised by means for reducing vibration or noise -- This CPC subgroup (F16H 57/028) covers gears that incorporate measures to reduce vibration or noise, such as dampening elements, special tooth profiles, isolation mounts, or lubrication techniques designed to minimise acoustic emission and dynamic disturbances during operation.
[F16H 57/029] characterised by means for sealing the gearboxes, e.g. to improve airtightness -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearboxes characterised by means for preventing escape of liquids (e.g. lubrication oil) or gases.  Gearboxes characterised by means for preventing ingress of liquids, gases, dust or the like.
[F16H 57/03] characterised by means for reinforcing gearboxes, e.g. ribs -- The subclass F16H 57/03 covers gearboxes that are strengthened by structural additions such as ribs, gussets or other reinforcements attached to the housing or casing. These features increase the rigidity of the gearbox, improve load-bearing capacity and reduce deformation under torque, thereby enhancing durability and performance.
[F16H 57/031] characterised by covers or lids for gearboxes -- F16H 57/031 covers inventions where a gearbox is provided with a protective cover or lid. The classification includes designs and mechanisms for sealing, fastening or mounting lids that protect the gear train from contaminants and aid in maintenance. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing and general details of gearing.
[F16H 57/032] characterised by the materials used -- F16H 57/032 is a CPC subclass under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Gearing that groups patents whose defining feature is the material or material combination employed for gears, shafts, bearings or other gearing components, covering inventions that exploit specific metals, alloys, polymers, composites or treated surfaces to achieve particular performance or durability.
[F16H 57/033] Series gearboxes, e.g. gearboxes based on the same design being available in different sizes or gearboxes using a combination of several standardised units -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A group of similar gearboxes based on the same design, e.g. being available in different sizes.  A group of gearboxes which comprises a combination of several standardised units and which can meet various requirements by altering the combination of such units.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F16H 57/035] Gearboxes for gearing with endless flexible members -- This subclass covers gearboxes in which power is transmitted by endless flexible members such as belts or chains rather than by meshing gears. It includes the design, arrangement and housing of belt-drive or chain-drive gearboxes and related components that convert rotational motion through flexible transmission elements.
[F16H 57/037] Gearboxes for accommodating differential gearings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Housing components of gearboxes for accommodating differential gearings, which housing components are usually not intended to revolve with the differential gearings, e.g. housing (1) in the illustration below. [F16H 57/039] Gearboxes for accommodating worm gears -- F16H 57/039 relates to gearboxes specifically intended to contain worm-gear sets, addressing the arrangement of shafts, bearings and housing that support the worm and its mating gear for power transmission.
[F16H 57/04] Features relating to lubrication or cooling  -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubrication or cooling of gearings.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Control of lubrication or cooling in hydrostatic gearing -> F16H61/4165
[F16H 57/0401] using different fluids, e.g. a traction fluid for traction gearing and a lubricant for bearings or reduction gears -- The CPC subclass F16H 57/0401 covers gear systems that employ distinct fluids for separate functions, such as using a traction fluid in the teeth of traction gears while lubricating bearings or reduction gears with a conventional oil. It addresses the selection and application of multiple lubrication media to optimise performance and wear protection within the same gearing assembly.
[F16H 57/0402] Cleaning of lubricants, e.g. filters or magnets -- The F16H 57/0402 class covers techniques and apparatus for cleaning lubricants used in gear assemblies, including filtration systems, magnetic separators and other methods that remove particles and contaminants to maintain gear performance and longevity.
[F16H 57/0404] Lubricant filters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Filter devices.  Magnets for collecting metallic debris.
[F16H 57/0405] Monitoring quality of lubricant or hydraulic fluids -- This group covers techniques and apparatus for checking the condition of lubricants and hydraulic fluids used in gear units, such as sensors, sampling devices, and analytical methods that detect contamination, viscosity changes, or wear particles. The aim is to ensure reliable gear operation and prevent failure by providing real-time or periodic quality assessment.
[F16H 57/0406] Absorption elements for lubricants, e.g. oil felts -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F16H 57/0408] Exchange, draining or filling of transmission lubricant -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F16H 57/0409] characterised by increasing efficiency, e.g. by reducing splash losses -- This CPC subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, and covers gearing designs that aim to improve efficiency, particularly by minimizing fluid splash losses in gear meshing. The classification is applied to patents that disclose features such as surface treatments, lubricants or geometry changes that reduce splash and thereby boost power transmission efficiency.
[F16H 57/041] Coatings or solid lubricants, e.g. anti-seize layers or pastes -- The subclass covers the application of protective or solid-lubricant coatings to gear components, such as anti-seize layers, pastes or films applied to teeth, shafts or housings to reduce friction, wear and seizing. It includes the materials used and the methods for applying these solid lubricants.
[F16H 57/0412] Cooling or heating; Control of temperature -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cooling or heating of the transmission, its elements, e.g. gears, casings or of the lubricant.
[F16H 57/0413] Controlled cooling or heating of lubricant; Temperature control therefor -- F16H 57/0413 pertains to systems and methods for regulating the temperature of lubricants in gear mechanisms, including devices that actively cool or heat the lubricant to maintain optimal operating conditions.
[F16H 57/0415] Air cooling or ventilation; Heat exchangers; Thermal insulations -- F16H 57/0415 covers the integration of air-cooling, ventilation, heat-exchanger and thermal-insulation measures for gear systems, focusing on how temperature management is achieved in mechanical transmission units. It addresses design features, materials and arrangements that protect gears from overheating and improve efficiency through controlled heat dissipation.
[F16H 57/0416] Air cooling or ventilation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The term "air cooling or ventilation" shall include transmission elements and/or lubricants cooled by heat exchange with ambient air, e.g. ventilated air cooling of CVT belts, guidance of lubricant through air cooled tubes or air cooled parts of the casing, cooling fins inside or outside the casing.
[F16H 57/0417] Heat exchangers adapted or integrated in the gearing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The "heat exchangers" shall include lubricant cooled or heated by heat exchange with another fluid, e.g. with water, oil or exhaust gas.
[F16H 57/0419] Thermal insulations -- F16H 57/0419 covers the use of thermal insulation on gear-related components, such as housings, shafts or gear casings, to control heat buildup, limit temperature gradients and improve efficiency and durability of gearing assemblies. It includes insulating materials, designs and methods applied specifically to mechanical gear systems.
[F16H 57/042] Guidance of lubricant -- The code F16H 57/042 covers features that direct lubricating fluid to gear elements, such as ducts, grooves, channels or other guiding structures that supply oil or grease to gear teeth, meshes or bearing surfaces during operation. It includes arrangements for distributing lubricant within gear housings or between moving parts to reduce wear and improve efficiency.
[F16H 57/0421] on or within the casing, e.g. shields or baffles for collecting lubricant, tubes, pipes, grooves, channels or the like -- F16H 57/0421 covers features located on or inside a gear casing, such as shields, baffles, or other elements that collect or direct lubricant, together with integrated tubes, pipes, grooves, channels or similar passages. It pertains to internal arrangements that manage fluid flow, protect components and facilitate lubrication within mechanical gear assemblies.
[F16H 57/0423] Lubricant guiding means mounted or supported on the casing, e.g. shields or baffles for collecting lubricant, tubes or pipes -- The CPC entry F16H 57/0423 covers devices that guide or collect lubricant on a gear housing, including shields, baffles, tubes or pipes mounted on or supported by the casing. These elements direct oil flow, prevent leakage and protect gear teeth.
[F16H 57/0424] Lubricant guiding means in the wall of or integrated with the casing, e.g. grooves, channels, holes -- F16H 57/0424 belongs to the CPC gear group and specifies features that guide lubricants within the gear housing, such as molded grooves, channels or drilled holes in the casing wall. These integral passages direct oil to the gear teeth and bearings, improving lubrication distribution and reducing wear.
[F16H 57/0426] Means for guiding lubricant into an axial channel of a shaft -- This subclass covers devices that direct lubricating fluid into an axial channel of a rotating shaft, using features such as ducts, wicks or guides to ensure continuous supply within gear assemblies. It falls under gearing in mechanical engineering and addresses lubrication delivery systems integrated with shafts.
[F16H 57/0427] on rotary parts, e.g. using baffles for collecting lubricant by centrifugal force -- F16H 57/0427 covers gear systems where rotary components such as baffles are employed to collect and direct lubricant using centrifugal force. The feature improves lubrication distribution within the gear housing, reducing wear and enhancing efficiency.
[F16H 57/0428] Grooves with pumping effect for supplying lubricants -- F16H 57/0428 covers gearing designs that incorporate specially shaped grooves which create a pumping action to deliver lubricant to gear teeth or contact surfaces. The grooves are engineered to generate fluid flow as the gear rotates, ensuring continuous lubrication and reducing wear without external oil-feeding mechanisms. This improves gear efficiency and lifespan in mechanical systems.
[F16H 57/043] within rotary parts, e.g. axial channels or radial openings in shafts -- The F16H 57/043 subclass covers features incorporated into rotating components, specifically axial channels or radial openings formed in shafts. These openings are typically used for fluid passage, ventilation, or to accommodate other functional elements within the rotating part. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing.
[F16H 57/0431] Means for guiding lubricant directly onto a tooth surface or to foot areas of a gear, e.g. by holes or grooves in a tooth flank -- F16H 57/0431 covers devices that guide lubricant straight onto a gear tooth surface or foot area, usually by means of holes or grooves cut into the tooth flank. It is a sub-group of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, dealing specifically with direct lubrication of gear teeth.
[F16H 57/0432] Lubricant guiding means on or inside shift rods or shift forks -- This classification addresses features that direct or contain lubricating fluid on or within the shift rods or shift forks of a gear mechanism, such as ducts, channels, reservoirs or guiding surfaces integrated with the components to ensure proper lubrication during gear changes.
[F16H 57/0434] relating to lubrication supply, e.g. pumps; Pressure control -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All kinds of lubrication pumps for gearing or special means to produce flow of lubrication in gearing. Also, lubrication control units or valves to supply the transmission with lubricant.
[F16H 57/0435] Pressure control for supplying lubricant; Circuits or valves therefor -- F16H 57/0435 covers devices that regulate the pressure of lubricating oil supplied to gear assemblies, including pressure-control circuits and the valves used in those systems. The classification applies to mechanical engineering gear arrangements where maintaining proper lubrication pressure is essential for performance and wear reduction.
[F16H 57/0436] Pumps -- F16H 57/0436 pertains to pumps that are incorporated in gear mechanisms or used to circulate lubricating fluid within gear assemblies. It covers the design, operation and integration of gear-driven and gear-associated pumps for lubrication or hydraulic functions in gearboxes.
[F16H 57/0438] Pumps of jet type, e.g. jet pumps with means to inject high pressure fluid to the suction area thereby supercharging the pump or means reducing cavitations -- The CPC subclass F16H 57/0438 pertains to jet-type pumps that incorporate means for injecting high-pressure fluid into the suction area to supercharge the pump or to reduce cavitation. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → gearing, covering technologies that enhance pump performance by using jet injection for flow boost or cavitation mitigation.
[F16H 57/0439] using multiple pumps with different power sources or a single pump with different power sources, e.g. one and the same pump may selectively be driven by either the engine or an electric motor -- F16H 57/0439 covers gear arrangements that permit a pump to be driven by more than one power source, such as switching between an engine and an electric motor or using several pumps each powered separately. The classification applies to mechanical systems enabling selective power-source connection for fluid-moving devices.
[F16H 57/0441] Arrangements of pumps -- F16H 57/0441 covers configurations that integrate pumps with gear mechanisms, describing how pumps are coupled to, driven by, or incorporated within gear trains and related arrangements. It includes designs where the gearing provides power transmission, speed control or positioning for the pumping unit.
[F16H 57/0442] for supply in case of failure, i.e. auxiliary supply -- The F16H 57/0442 subclass covers gearing arrangements that include an auxiliary power source to keep the gear train running when the main supply fails, such as backup drives or secondary drive units that automatically engage to maintain operation.
[F16H 57/0443] for supply of lubricant during tilt or high acceleration, e.g. problems related to the tilt or extreme acceleration of the transmission casing and the supply of lubricant under these conditions -- The CPC subclass F16H 57/0443 covers mechanisms that provide lubricant to gears when the transmission casing is tilted or subjected to extreme acceleration, addressing lubrication challenges under such dynamic conditions. It pertains to gear systems requiring reliable oil supply despite positional or inertial stresses.
[F16H 57/0445] for supply of different gearbox casings or sections -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The term "gearbox section" means a section containing gearing elements like gear, belts, chains or clutches. A section comprising only shaft support bearings is not considered as a different gearbox section.
[F16H 57/0446] the supply forming part of the transmission control unit, e.g. for automatic transmissions -- F16H 57/0446 concerns the power supply-electrical, hydraulic or otherwise-that is incorporated into the transmission control unit of an automatic gearbox, providing the energy needed to regulate gear changes. It is classified under mechanical engineering elements pertaining to gearing systems.
[F16H 57/0447] Control of lubricant levels, e.g. lubricant level control dependent on temperature -- F16H 57/0447 covers devices or methods that regulate the amount of lubricant in gearing assemblies, adjusting the lubricant level in response to operating conditions such as temperature changes. It addresses control mechanisms that maintain optimal lubrication to ensure reliable gear performance.
[F16H 57/0449] Sensors or indicators for controlling the fluid level -- This subclass covers devices that detect or show the amount of fluid in a gearbox or other power-transmission unit, providing information for manual or automatic regulation of lubrication levels.
[F16H 57/045] Lubricant storage reservoirs, e.g. reservoirs in addition to a gear sump for collecting lubricant in the upper part of a gear case -- The code F16H 57/045 covers gear housings that incorporate dedicated lubricant storage reservoirs, such as additional tanks placed above the gear case, to collect and retain oil separate from the main gear sump. This feature aids in maintaining proper lubrication of the gearing assembly.
[F16H 57/0452] Oil pans -- Oil pans are receptacles that collect and retain lubricating oil within gearboxes, typically mounted to the housing and equipped with openings for filling, venting and draining. They may incorporate baffles, seals and mounting features to ensure proper oil circulation and prevent leaks, supporting the reliable operation of the gearing system.
[F16H 57/0453] Section walls to divide a gear sump -- F16H 57/0453 covers internal partitions or section walls built into a gear housing that separate the gear sump into multiple chambers. These walls help isolate lubricating oil, prevent cross-contamination, improve cooling and allow different gear sets to run under distinct conditions within the same gearbox.
[F16H 57/0454] Sealings between different partitions of a gearing or to a reservoir  means for sealing gearboxes  F16H57/029 -- This CPC subclass concerns sealing arrangements that separate the various partitions of a gearbox or isolate it from a reservoir, covering methods and devices that prevent fluid leakage between gear stages or between the gear housing and external reservoirs. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units for gearing.
[F16H 57/0456] Lubrication by injection; Injection nozzles or tubes therefor  oil mist or spray lubrication  F16H57/0458 -- F16H 57/0456 concerns methods and apparatus for lubricating gears by injecting oil mist or spray directly into the gear mesh. It includes injection nozzles, tubes and related devices that deliver lubricant under pressure to reduce wear and heat.
[F16H 57/0457] Splash lubrication -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Splash lubrication, e.g. by gearing elements plunging into an oil bath.
[F16H 57/0458] Oil-mist or spray lubrication; Means to reduce foam formation -- F16H 57/0458 covers methods and apparatus for lubricating gears with oil mist or spray, including systems that introduce the lubricant in a fine aerosol and devices that limit or eliminate foam generation during operation. The classification addresses how such spray lubrication improves gear performance and reliability while preventing foam-related losses.
[F16H 57/046] Oil-mist or spray lubrication -- F16H 57/046 pertains to the lubrication of gears by means of oil mist or sprayed oil, describing systems that generate a fine oil aerosol and distribute it onto gear teeth and bearings to reduce friction, wear and heat during operation.
[F16H 57/0461] Means to reduce foam formation -- F16H 57/0461 covers devices incorporated in gear lubrication or cooling systems that prevent or minimise foam in the lubricating fluid, thereby maintaining effective oil flow, reducing cavitation and improving transmission efficiency and component life. It relates to means for controlling foam-formation during gear operation.
[F16H 57/0463] Grease lubrication; Drop-feed lubrication -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F16H 57/0464] Grease lubrication -- F16H 57/0464 relates to the use of grease as a lubricant for gears, covering the selection, composition and application methods of greases that reduce friction and wear in gear sets. It includes provisions for greasing techniques, placement devices and performance criteria specific to gearing mechanisms.
[F16H 57/0465] Drop-feed lubrication -- F16H 57/0465 covers drop-feed lubrication for gear assemblies, describing systems where a controlled stream of lubricant is applied directly onto gear teeth or mesh areas during operation. This method ensures precise oil delivery, reduces splash loss, and improves wear protection in mechanical engineering gearing applications.
[F16H 57/0467] Elements of gearings to be lubricated, cooled or heated -- This subclass covers components of gear systems that incorporate fluid management for lubrication, temperature control or cooling, such as oil passages, spray nozzles, internal channels and heating elements integrated into gears, bearings or housings to maintain optimal operating conditions.
[F16H 57/0468] Shift rods or shift forks -- The CPC subclass F16H 57/0468 covers shift-rods or shift-forks, components that translate rotational motion into axial movement for selecting gear positions in transmissions. These parts are used in manual or automated gearboxes to engage or disengage gear sets.
[F16H 57/0469] Bearings or seals -- F16H 57/0469 covers bearings or seals employed in gearing mechanisms, specifying how these components support rotating parts and prevent contamination. It lies within mechanical engineering, under engineering elements and units, and the gearing group for general details of gearing.
[F16H 57/0471] Bearing -- F16H 57/0471 covers bearings incorporated in gear assemblies, detailing devices that support rotating shafts or gear elements while minimizing friction and wear. It includes journal, roller, ball and spherical bearings used to maintain alignment and transmit loads within mechanical gearing systems.
[F16H 57/0472] Seals -- F16H 57/0472 concerns seals used in gear assemblies. It addresses sealing solutions that prevent lubricant loss or contamination in meshing gears, including seal types, placement and design considerations specific to gearing mechanisms.
[F16H 57/0473] Friction devices, e.g. clutches or brakes -- F16H 57/0473 covers friction devices that are integrated with gear systems, such as clutches or brakes, which transmit or dissipate torque by means of surface friction. The classification applies to designs where the friction element is part of the gearing mechanism, enabling controlled engagement, disengagement or speed regulation.
[F16H 57/0475] Engine and gearing, i.e. joint lubrication or cooling or heating thereof -- This subclass concerns systems where the engine and its gear train share a common lubrication circuit or integrated thermal management, covering methods and apparatus for jointly lubricating, cooling or heating the engine and gears. It includes designs that combine oil supply, coolant flow or heating devices to serve both components simultaneously.
[F16H 57/0476] Electric machines and gearing, i.e. joint lubrication or cooling or heating thereof -- F16H 57/0476 covers arrangements where electric machines are directly combined with gear drives and share lubrication, cooling or heating functions, such as joint oil circuits, integrated thermal management or combined heat exchangers that serve both the motor and the gearing.
[F16H 57/0478] Synchromesh devices -- F16H 57/0478 concerns synchromesh devices used in gear systems to match the rotational speeds of meshing gears, enabling smooth engagement without shock. These devices fall under the broader category of gearing within mechanical engineering and engineering elements and units.
[F16H 57/0479] Gears or bearings on planet carriers -- F16H 57/0479 concerns the mounting of gears or bearings on planet carriers in planetary gear arrangements, describing how these components transmit torque between the carrier and the rotating planets. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, and general details of gearing.
[F16H 57/048] Type of gearings to be lubricated, cooled or heated -- F16H 57/048 refers to gearings that are designed to be lubricated, cooled or heated, detailing the methods and arrangements for thermal management of gear sets. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → gearing.
[F16H 57/0482] Gearings with gears having orbital motion -- F16H 57/0482 covers gearings in which the gears themselves follow an orbital path while transmitting motion, as in planetary or epicyclic gear systems where the gear bodies rotate around a central carrier rather than about fixed shafts.
[F16H 57/0483] Axle or inter-axle differentials -- The code F16H 57/0483 pertains to axle or inter-axle differentials, devices that allow wheels on the same vehicle to rotate at different speeds while transmitting torque. It covers the design and arrangement of differential gear sets used in automotive and other mechanical drivetrains.
[F16H 57/0484] with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion -- F16H 57/0484 pertains to gear arrangements that allow the gear ratio to be varied continuously or stepwise, or that can reverse the direction of rotary motion, as seen in continuously variable transmissions, reversible gearboxes and similar mechanisms.
[F16H 57/0486] with fixed gear ratio   axle or inter-axle differentials  F16H57/0483 -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16H 57/0486 pertains to mechanical engineering, specifically detailing gearing systems with a fixed gear ratio that include axle or inter-axle differentials. This classification is used for inventions involving the specific design and function of differential gears in vehicles or machinery.
[F16H 57/0487] Friction gearings -- F16H 57/0487 covers friction gearings, which transmit motion and torque between shafts using contact forces rather than teeth. These gearings include smooth wheels, rollers or other surfaces that rely on friction for power transfer, often employed where noise reduction or precise speed control is needed.
[F16H 57/0489] with endless flexible members, e.g. belt CVTs -- The subclass F16H 57/0489 covers gearing arrangements that transmit motion by means of endless flexible members such as belts, including those used in continuously variable transmissions. It pertains to mechanical engineering gear elements where the drive and driven parts are linked by a belt or similar flexible loop rather than rigid gears. This classification falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing.
[F16H 57/049] of the toroid type -- The classification F16H 57/049 pertains to gears of the toroid type, which are torus-shaped power-transmission elements used in mechanical systems. [F16H 57/0491] of the cone ring type -- The CPC code F16H 57/0491 covers cone-ring type gearing, a gear arrangement where a conical gear meshes with a ring-shaped gear. This configuration provides angular velocity change and a compact layout, commonly used in automotive differentials and other power-transmission systems.

This classification is part of a detailed hierarchy under mechanical engineering, focusing on the specific design and application of gearing systems.
[F16H 57/0494] with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion -- This CPC subclass covers gear mechanisms that can change their gear ratio during operation or are designed to reverse the direction of rotation. It includes variable-ratio transmissions, continuously variable gearboxes, and reversible gear sets used in mechanical power transmission.
[F16H 57/0495] with fixed gear ratio -- F16H 57/0495 relates to gear assemblies whose gear ratio does not change during operation. It includes mechanisms such as fixed-ratio gear trains, planetary gear sets with constant meshing, and any arrangement where the speed relationship between the input and output shafts remains constant, excluding adjustable or continuously variable transmission systems.
[F16H 57/0497] Screw mechanisms -- F16H 57/0497 covers screw mechanisms used in gearing systems, including screws, bolts, and threaded components employed to transmit motion, adjust axial position, or convert rotary to linear movement within mechanical assemblies. It pertains to the design, operation and integration of such screw-based devices in gear structures.
[F16H 57/0498] Worm gearings -- Worm gearings are gear arrangements in which a screw-like worm meshes with a worm wheel, providing high reduction ratios and converting rotary motion to a perpendicular axis. They are used where compact size, self-locking capability, and smooth, silent operation are required, such as in conveyors, lifts, and steering mechanisms. This subclass details the geometry, contact patterns and technical features specific to worm gear sets.
[F16H 57/05] of chains -- F16H 57/05 pertains to the use of chains within gearing systems, covering chain-driven transmissions, chain sprockets, and related components that transfer motion or power. It includes specifications for chain dimensions, link design, tensioning methods, and the integration of chains with other mechanical elements in gear assemblies.
[F16H 57/08] of gearings with members having orbital motion -- F16H 57/08 covers gearings whose members move in orbital paths, meaning the gear elements revolve around a common center while also rotating about their own axes. This includes epicyclic or planetary gear sets and similar mechanisms where the teeth or components describe an orbital motion as part of power transmission.
[F16H 57/082] Planet carriers -- F16H 57/082 designates planet carriers, the components that hold and support the planet gears within a planetary gear set. The classification encompasses structures that transmit torque between the sun gear, planet gears and ring gear, including fixed, rotating or adjustable carriers and their integration in gear assemblies.
[F16H 57/10] Braking arrangements -- The F16H 57/10 subclass covers braking arrangements for gear systems, describing devices or methods that stop, control or limit the rotation of gears. It includes hydraulic, pneumatic, mechanical or other types of brakes applied to gear trains, couplings or power-transmission units to provide safe stopping or speed regulation. This subclass is part of the broader gearing group within mechanical engineering.
[F16H 57/12] Arrangements for adjusting or for taking-up backlash not provided for elsewhere -- F16H 57/12 covers gear arrangements that provide means for adjusting or eliminating backlash where other classifications do not apply, such as adjustable tooth spacing, spring-loaded or shimmed couplings, take-up devices and other mechanisms that fine-tune gear mesh to reduce play.
[F16H 59/00] Control of gearings conveying rotary motion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Glossary:** - mechanism: a kinematic chain consisting either of a single element or alternatively of a series of elements, the position of each point on the kinematic chain being derivable from the position of any other point on the chain, and therefore, for a given position of a point on one of the elements forming the kinematic chain there is only one position for each of the other points on the element or series of elements forming the kinematic chain - final output mechanism: the mechanism which includes the final output element, e.g. hydraulic or electromagnetic clutch, synchronizer clutch, ball and ramp clutch - final output element: the final element which is moved to establish a gear ratio, i.e. which achieves the linking or coupling between two power transmission means, e.g. reverse idler gear, gear cluster, coupling sleeve, apply piston of a hydraulic clutch - actuating mechanism: the mechanism, the movement of which causes the movement of another mechanism by being in mutual contact - final actuating mechanism: the mechanism actuating the final output mechanism, i.e. this mechanism actuates the final output mechanism which includes the final output element, e.g. coupling sleeve, shift fork, hydraulic circuit, electromagnetic solenoid, motor
[F16H 59/02] Selector apparatus -- A selector apparatus is a mechanism that chooses or shifts between gear sets in a transmission, allowing control of the direction or speed of rotary motion. It typically consists of shift forks, cams or electro-mechanical components that engage the desired gear to transmit power.
[F16H 59/0204] for automatic transmissions with means for range selection and manual shifting, e.g. range selector with tiptronic -- F16H 59/0204 refers to control systems for gearings that convey rotary motion, specifically automatic transmissions equipped with a range-selection mechanism that also allows manual gear changes, such as tiptronic or similar driver-selected shift functions. [F16H 59/0217] with electric switches or sensors not for gear or range selection, e.g. for controlling auxiliary devices -- F16H 59/0217 covers control of gearings that convey rotary motion by means of electric switches or sensors that are not used for gear or range selection, but instead manage auxiliary functions such as safety monitoring or activation of secondary devices. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for gearing and the control of gearings that convey rotary motion.
[F16H 59/04] Ratio selector apparatus -- The F16H 59/04 ratio selector apparatus is a device for selecting and changing gear ratios in a transmission, enabling control of speed and torque between rotating shafts. [F16H 59/042] comprising a final actuating mechanism -- F16H 59/042 covers gear transmissions that incorporate a final actuating mechanism, such as a motor, hydraulic cylinder or other power source, to directly drive the output shaft. [F16H 59/68] Inputs being a function of gearing status -- The class F16H 59/68 relates to gear systems in which the controlling inputs-such as torque commands, speed signals or power distribution-are varied according to the present status of the gearing, for example based on gear engagement, load or speed. [F16H 61/0028] using a single pump driven by different power sources -- The CPC subgroup F16H 61/0028 concerns arrangements that use a single pump driven by different power sources, allowing the pump's drive to be switched or selected among multiple energy supplies. It typically involves gearing or control mechanisms that change speed or direction to convey rotary motion from the chosen source.
[F16H 61/0031] using auxiliary pumps, e.g. pump driven by a different power source than the engine -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Auxiliary pumps provided in addition to a main pump for supplying hydraulic fluid to the transmission control circuit, e.g. an auxiliary pump supplying hydraulic fluid when the engine has stopped.
[F16H 61/0059] Braking of gear output shaft using simultaneous engagement of engaging means, e.g. clutches or brakes, applied for different gear ratios -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearings where braking is achieved by simultaneous engagement of two or more engaging means, e.g. clutches or brakes, which are applied for different gear ratios. This method of braking the transmission is sometimes used instead of a parking lock.
[F16H 61/02] characterised by the signals used -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gearshift control units using specific types of shift signals.  Methods for generating shift signals, including:
[F16H 61/0202] the signals being electric -- The subclass concerns electric signals used to control change-speed or reversing gear sets that convey rotary motion. It includes electronic sensors, actuators, wiring and control circuitry that manage gear engagement, speed variation or direction reversal.
[F16H 61/0204] for gearshift control, e.g. control functions for performing shifting or generation of shift signal -- This CPC subclass defines gearshift control functions used in change-speed or reversing gearings, detailing mechanisms that perform shifting operations or generate shift signals to manage the conveyance of rotary motion.
[F16H 61/0206] Layout of electro-hydraulic control circuits, e.g. arrangement of valves -- This subclass covers the design of electro-hydraulic control circuits, focusing on how valves are arranged to provide control functions such as speed change or reversal in gear mechanisms that transmit rotary motion.
[F16H 61/0211] characterised by low integration or small number of valves -- F16H 61/0211 pertains to gear arrangements that provide change-speed or reversing functions where the control is achieved with a low degree of integration or only a few valves. It is applied to mechanical transmission units that rely on minimal valve components to manage the flow of fluid or pressure in the gearing system.
[F16H 61/0213] characterised by the method for generating shift signals -- F16H 61/0213 falls under mechanical engineering, specifically gearing elements that provide control functions for change-speed or reversing gearboxes used to convey rotary motion. The subclass covers gearings that are characterised by the method used to generate shift signals, detailing how signal generation is integrated into the transmission's control system.
[F16H 61/0246] characterised by initiating reverse gearshift -- This classification applies to gear mechanisms that manage speed changes or reverse motion, specifically those designed to initiate a reverse gearshift, directing rotary motion in the opposite direction.
[F16H 61/0248] Control units where shifting is directly initiated by the driver, e.g. semi-automatic transmissions -- This classification covers control units that let the driver directly initiate gear changes, as found in semi-automatic transmissions. It pertains to mechanisms that manage shift commands without full manual clutch operation, facilitating change-speed or reversing gear functions.
[F16H 61/0251] Elements specially adapted for electric control units, e.g. valves for converting electrical signals to fluid signals -- F16H 61/0251 covers components designed for electric control units within gear systems, such as valves that turn electrical signals into hydraulic or pneumatic actuation. These elements are used to manage speed-change or reversal in mechanical transmissions by converting electronic commands into fluid power.
[F16H 61/0262] the signals being hydraulic -- This classification covers hydraulic signals employed to control speed-change or reversing functions in gear transmissions that convey rotary motion, situated within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of engineering elements and gear units.
[F16H 61/0265] for gearshift control, e.g. control functions for performing shifting or generation of shift signals -- The CPC subclass F16H 61/0265 deals with control functions in change-speed or reversing gearings that transmit rotary motion. It covers mechanisms that perform gear shifting or generate shift signals, such as gearshift control systems.
[F16H 61/0267] Layout of hydraulic control circuits, e.g. arrangement of valves -- The subclass F16H 61/0267 covers the design and arrangement of hydraulic control circuits that manage change-speed or reversing gearings, emphasizing how valves and related hydraulic components are laid out to control the transmission of rotary motion. It pertains to gearing control functions within mechanical engineering.
[F16H 61/0269] characterised by low integration or small number of valves -- F16H 61/0269 covers gearings with control functions for speed change or reversal that are distinguished by using only a few or low-integration valves, typically in hydraulic or pneumatic systems where valve count is minimized to simplify the transmission of rotary motion.
[F16H 61/0272] characterised by initiating reverse gearshift -- The F16H 61/0272 class defines devices that initiate a reverse gearshift in change-speed or reversing gearings used for conveying rotary motion. It covers control mechanisms that cause the gearbox to move from forward to reverse operation, typically by engaging a reverse gear through a dedicated actuator or control assembly.
[F16H 61/0274] Control units where shifting is directly initiated by the driver, e.g. semi-automatic transmissions -- F16H 61/0274 covers control units in gearboxes where the driver directly initiates gear changes, such as in semi-automatic transmissions. These units manage shift commands without intermediate electronic mediation, giving the driver immediate control over speed or reverse gear selection.
[F16H 61/0276] Elements specially adapted for hydraulic control units, e.g. valves -- The CPC subgroup F16H 61/0276 covers parts that are specially adapted for hydraulic control units, notably valves, used in gear arrangements that manage speed changes or reverse rotary motion. These elements integrate hydraulic actuation with gearing to enable precise control of power transmission.
[F16H 61/0283] Governor valves -- Governor valves are control devices that regulate hydraulic or pneumatic fluid flow within change-speed or reversing gearings, adjusting the transmitted rotary motion to maintain a set speed or limit torque. They function as protective and speed-governing elements in mechanical transmission systems.
[F16H 61/0286] Manual valves -- F16H 61/0286 covers manual valves that provide the operator-controlled function for changing speed or reversing the direction of rotary motion in mechanical gearings. These valves are part of the control subsystem that regulates fluid or hydraulic power to gear sets, allowing start-stop, speed selection or reversal without automatic actuation.
[F16H 61/029] Throttle valves -- F16H 61/029 pertains to throttle valves that regulate the flow of fluid in gear mechanisms designed for speed change or reversal, thereby controlling the transmission of rotary motion. The valves adjust pressure or flow rate to modulate gear engagement and output speed in such systems.
[F16H 61/0293] the signals being purely mechanical -- The entry covers control functions in change-speed or reversing gearings that use purely mechanical signals to manage the transmission of rotary motion. It pertains to mechanisms where the control information is conveyed by physical movement or force, without electrical or electronic means, to adjust speed or direction in gearing systems.
[F16H 61/0295] Automatic gear shift control, e.g. initiating shift by centrifugal forces -- F16H 61/0295 covers automatic gear-shift control mechanisms that initiate a shift by centrifugal forces, used in change-speed or reversing gearings to convey rotary motion. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing.
[F16H 61/0297] Gear shift control where shifting is directly initiated by the driver, e.g. semi-automatic transmissions -- F16H 61/0297 covers gear-shift mechanisms that are activated directly by the driver, such as those used in semi-automatic transmissions. The classification belongs to the mechanical engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, gearing, and specifically to control functions for change-speed or reversing gearings that convey rotary motion.
[F16H 61/04] Smoothing ratio shift -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Controlling the gearing in order to smooth the shift between ratios, e.g. to reduce shift shock.
[F16H 61/0403] Synchronisation before shifting -- Synchronisation before shifting refers to control functions that match the speeds of engaged gears in change-speed or reversing gearings prior to a shift, ensuring smooth engagement and reducing shock or wear during the transition of rotary motion. This applies to mechanisms that coordinate gear elements before power transfer is altered.
[F16H 61/0437] by using electrical signals  F16H61/0403  and  F16H61/061  take precedence -- F16H 61/0437 covers gear arrangements that change speed or reverse rotation by means of electrical signals, using electronic control functions to manage the motion transfer. It is a sub-category of control functions within variable-speed or reversing gearings, and it takes precedence over the related subclasses F16H 61/0403 and F16H 61/061.
[F16H 61/06] by controlling rate of change of fluid pressure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of fluid pressure for a releasing gear shift element or an engaging gear shift element for shift transition from a current ratio to a new target ratio.
[F16H 61/061] using electric control means -- A subclass for gear assemblies that alter speed or reverse rotary motion and are regulated by electrical control devices, encompassing electronic sensors, switches, drives and control circuitry that manage gear engagement, speed selection or direction change.
[F16H 61/065] using fluid control means -- It covers gear systems where fluid devices such as valves, pumps or hydraulic actuators are employed to regulate speed changes or reversal of rotary motion. The subclass includes arrangements that use hydraulic, pneumatic or liquid-based control means to manage gear engagement, slip or torque transmission.
[F16H 61/067] using an accumulator -- F16H 61/067 relates to gear assemblies that employ an accumulator to store kinetic energy and release it for controlled speed changes or reversal of rotary motion. The accumulator smooths torque transmission, reducing shock loads and improving efficiency during gear transitions.
[F16H 61/068] using an orifice control valve  F16H61/067  takes precedence -- This subclass defines gear arrangements that regulate speed or direction of rotary motion by means of an orifice control valve, typically a hydraulic or pneumatic valve that modulates fluid flow to control gear engagement. It is positioned under control functions for change-speed or reversing gearings within mechanical engineering.
[F16H 61/08] Timing control -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Timing control during shift transition.  Some timing control is always performed during shifting. Therefore, only documents showing special inventive features related to timing control are covered by this group.
[F16H 61/10] Controlling shift hysteresis -- F16H 61/10 covers control methods that manage shift hysteresis in change-speed or reversing gearings used for transmitting rotary motion. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, focusing on how control functions reduce or adjust the lag between gear commands and actual gear position.
[F16H 61/12] Detecting malfunction or potential malfunction, e.g. fail safe  -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Detecting malfunction or potential malfunction in control of exclusively hydrostatic gearing -> F16H61/4192
[F16H 61/14] Control of torque converter lock-up clutches -- The entry covers mechanisms that manage the lock-up clutch of a torque converter within change-speed or reversing gearings, allowing the converter to be coupled directly to the transmission for efficient power transfer. It addresses control strategies for engaging and disengaging the clutch to regulate torque transmission and vehicle performance.
[F16H 61/141] using means only actuated by centrifugal force -- This subclass relates to gear arrangements that change speed or reverse rotary motion and are controlled exclusively by devices actuated by centrifugal force, without external power or hydraulic inputs. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, and specific control functions for conveying rotary motion.
[F16H 61/142] the means being hydraulic valves -- A hydraulic valve used to control the change-speed or reversing function of a gearbox, providing the means to adjust or invert rotary motion within mechanical transmission systems. This element is classified under mechanical engineering, gearing, and control functions for conveying rotary motion.
[F16H 61/143] using electric control means -- F16H 61/143 refers to gear systems that incorporate electric control means to regulate speed changes or reversal of rotary motion. It includes electric actuators, sensors, or electronic circuits used to manage the operation of change-speed or reversing gearings within mechanical engineering.
[F16H 61/148] using mechanical control means -- F16H 61/148 covers the use of mechanical control means in gearings that change speed or reverse rotation, including devices such as clutches, brakes and shifting mechanisms that manage the transmission of rotary motion.
[F16H 61/16] Inhibiting shift during unfavourable conditions  -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preventing unintentional or unsafe shift -> F16H61/18
[F16H 61/18] Preventing unintentional or unsafe shift -- The entry covers devices or control methods that prevent accidental or hazardous gear changes in variable-speed or reverse gearings used to transmit rotary motion. It includes interlocks, shift-lock mechanisms, detection sensors and other safety features that ensure a gear shift occurs only under authorised conditions, protecting both the machine and operators.
[F16H 61/20] Preventing gear creeping -- F16H 61/20 covers control functions in change-speed or reversing gearings that prevent gear creeping, a phenomenon where a gear unintentionally advances under load. It includes designs, mechanisms and methods that maintain precise gear positioning during operation, ensuring reliable transmission of rotary motion.
[F16H 61/21] Providing engine brake control -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Reducing the engine speed by controlling the transmission.
[F16H 61/22] Locking  -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locking of a control device with the exception of final output mechanisms, e.g. locking of range lever in the 'Park' position.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Locking of the final output mechanisms -> F16H63/34
[F16H 61/24] Providing feel, e.g. to enable selection -- The subclass covers mechanisms that provide tactile feedback in change-speed or reversing gearings to let an operator feel the gear position or selection. It includes devices that create a perceptible resistance, click or vibration to indicate which gear is engaged.
[F16H 61/26] Generation or transmission of movements for final actuating mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:

[F16H 61/2815] with a control using only relays and switches -- This subclass covers gear arrangements that alter speed or reverse rotary motion and are managed only by electrical relays and mechanical switches, without electronic or programmable controllers. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, and specifically control functions for change-speed or reversing gearings.
[F16H 61/30] Hydraulic motors therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fluid motors or actuators for moving final actuating mechanisms, e.g. hydraulic or pneumatic servo actuators, and their related control means.

[F16H 61/34] comprising two mechanisms, one for the preselection movement, and one for the shifting movement -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - With at least one movement, e.g. for selecting or shifting, being transmitted by a cable -> F16H61/36
[F16H 61/36] with at least one movement being transmitted by a cable -- This classification concerns gear assemblies for speed-change or reversing functions that transmit at least one movement through a cable, focusing on control mechanisms that convey rotary motion via cable action. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing.
[F16H 61/38] Control of exclusively fluid gearing -- F16H 61/38 relates to the control of gearings that transmit motion solely by fluid power, such as hydraulic or pneumatic gear sets used to change speed or reverse rotation. The subclass covers mechanisms, valves and control strategies that regulate the fluid flow to achieve the desired gearing action.

[F16H 61/461] not involving a variation of the output capacity of the main pumps or motors -- This CPC subclass concerns control functions applied to speed-change or reversing gearboxes that convey rotary motion, specifically where the control does not vary the output capacity of the main pump or motor.
[F16H 61/462] for achieving a target speed ratio -- **Definition:** This place covers:
