[F16H 61/478] for preventing overload, e.g. high pressure limitation -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F16H 61/48] hydrodynamic -- F16H 61/48 covers hydrodynamic control functions employed in change-speed or reversing gearings that convey rotary motion. It includes devices such as fluid couplings, torque converters and hydrostatic drives that use fluid pressure or flow to regulate speed, torque or direction in mechanical power transmission systems.
[F16H 61/50] controlled by changing the flow, force, or reaction of the liquid in the working circuit, while maintaining a completely filled working circuit -- F16H 61/50 covers gearings where the speed or direction of a rotary output is regulated by altering the flow, pressure or reaction of a liquid in a fully filled hydraulic circuit. The control is achieved without introducing air or voids, ensuring continuous fluid fill while the fluid dynamics modify the gear operation.
[F16H 61/52] by altering the position of blades -- This CPC subgroup covers control functions in variable-speed or reversing gearings that transmit rotary motion by moving blades to change their position, thereby altering the engagement or transmission characteristics of the gearing. It relates to mechanisms that adjust blade placement to achieve speed change or reversal.
[F16H 61/54] by means of axially-shiftable blade runners -- F16H 61/54 covers mechanisms that adjust the transmission of rotary motion by moving blade-like elements along the axis, allowing the gear to change speed or reverse direction. The axially-shiftable runners act as controllable teeth or cams that engage or disengage with other gear components, providing smooth and adjustable power transmission in mechanical systems.
[F16H 61/56] to change the blade angle -- This subclass addresses mechanisms that adjust the pitch or angle of blades-such as in turbines, fans or propellers-within gear arrangements that change speed or reverse rotation to convey rotary motion. It covers devices where blade-angle alteration is used to control flow, torque or performance as part of a change-speed or reversing gearing system.
[F16H 61/58] by change of the mechanical connection of, or between, the runners -- F16H 61/58 covers control methods for variable-speed or reversing gearings that transmit rotary motion, where the control is effected by changing the mechanical connection of, or between, the runners. It lies within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 61/60] exclusively by the use of freewheel clutches -- This subclass covers mechanisms that control the transmission of rotary motion in variable-speed or reversing gearings by employing freewheel clutches. The freewheel clutch permits torque flow in one direction while preventing motion in the opposite direction, enabling gear changes or reversal without additional braking.
[F16H 61/62] involving use of a speed-changing gearing or of a clutch in the connection between runners -- F16H 61/62 concerns mechanisms that use a speed-changing gear or clutch to link moving components such as runners in gear trains that alter speed or reverse direction. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, focusing on control functions within change-speed or reversing gearings for transmitting rotary motion.
[F16H 61/64] controlled by changing the amount of liquid in the working circuit -- F16H 61/64 concerns gear transmissions whose speed or direction is regulated by varying the amount of liquid in the hydraulic circuit, such as hydraulic couplings or variable-displacement pumps that modulate torque flow to achieve change-speed or reversing functions.
[F16H 61/66] specially adapted for continuously variable gearings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Only control of mechanical continuously variable gearings, e.g. for gearings using endless flexible members or friction gearings.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Control of exclusively fluid gearing -> F16H61/38
[F16H 61/662] with endless flexible members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of continuously variable gearings using endless flexible members.  **Glossary:** - control of shifting: control of one or more transmission ratios
[F16H 61/66227] controlling shifting exclusively as a function of speed and torque -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transmission ratio control specially adapted to continuously variable gearings with endless flexible members dependent only on the combination of speed and torque parameters.
[F16H 61/66231] controlling shifting exclusively as a function of speed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transmission ratio control specially adapted to continuously variable gearings with endless flexible members dependent only on speed parameters.
[F16H 61/66236] using electrical or electronic sensing or control means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a belt-type CVT where the distance between pulley sheaves (9) and (12) is adjusted such that the gear ratio of the belt-type CVT is adjusted, i.e. controlled, by electrical control means in the form of two electric motors each driving two respective screw-nut mechanisms (8). A control unit controls the electric motors exclusively on data provided by a speed sensor measuring the speed of the output shaft.
[F16H 61/6624] using only hydraulical and mechanical sensing or control means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place.  The Figure illustrates a belt-type CVT comprising a pitot tube (42) as hydraulic sensing means, a hydraulic cylinder (68) as hydraulic control means, and a lever (60) as mechanical control means. The pitot tube (42) detects the flow speed and pressure of the fluid inside the fluid reservoir (40), which is indicative of the rotational velocity of the pulley half (24) and input shaft (12). The lever (60) is connected to the throttle (62) of the engine (16) by means of a cable (64). The lever (60) turns in response to a change of position of the accelerator pedal (66), which controls the throttle (62). Movement of the pedal (66) pivots the lever (60), as indicated by double-headed arrow (67), and in turn the shaft (56) and housing (46) through the bevel gears (52, 54) to reposition the tip opening (44) of the pitot tube (42) in the reservoir (40). The fluid inside the fluid reservoir (40) flows into or out of the tip opening (44) of the pitot tube (42) to continually adjust the internal pressure of the hydraulic cylinder (68), as dictated by the rotational velocity of the input shaft (12).
[F16H 61/66245] using purely mechanical sensing or control means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a belt-type CVT comprising centrifugal weights (23) as purely mechanical control means. The higher the speed, the more the centrifugal weights (23) move radially outwards, thereby adjusting the distance between pulley sheaves (18) and (20) and, thus, the transmission ratio of the belt-type CVT.
[F16H 61/6625] controlling shifting exclusively as a function of torque -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transmission ratio control specially adapted to continuously variable gearings with endless flexible members dependent only on torque parameters.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a belt-type CVT comprising cam roller pressure means which provide a pressure for adjusting the distance between pulley sheaves (20) and (22). Proportional to the applied torque, cam roller (32) is axially moved by cam (30) and presses pulley sheave (22) toward pulley sheave (20), thereby adjusting the transmission ratio of the belt-type CVT.
[F16H 61/66254] controlling of shifting being influenced by a signal derived from the engine and the main coupling -- The code F16H 61/66254 covers gearboxes in which the act of shifting is governed by a signal taken from the engine or the main coupling, enabling speed-change or reverse functions to be automatically adjusted. It pertains to control mechanisms that modify gear engagement based on engine-derived inputs within mechanical engineering gearing systems.
[F16H 61/66259] using electrical or electronical sensing or control means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a belt-type CVT comprising an input shaft speed sensor (62), an output shaft speed sensor (64) and an oil temperature sensor (52) as electronic sensing means, as well as electronic control means (60) and (80) to control the transmission ratio of the belt-type CVT. Electronic control means (60) and (80) perform shift control on the transmission ratio of the belt-type CVT such that the speed of the input shaft (38) becomes equal to a target speed. In other words, the shift control is controlled as a function of the input and output speeds as well as of the oil temperature.
[F16H 61/66263] using only hydraulical and mechanical sensing or control means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a belt-type CVT comprising a rod assembly (33) and (34) as mechanical control means connected to an accelerator pedal (36). It further comprises a hydraulic revolution sensor (21) as hydraulic sensing means, and a hydraulic control circuit as hydraulic control means. The hydraulic control circuit includes a pressure medium pump (13) provided on drive shaft (2) at the engine (1) and main coupling (41). Pump (13) removes pressure medium from a reservoir (14) and takes it via a conduit (15) to the control valve (12) from where, depending on the switch position of the control valve, it is then fed via conduits (16, 17) to the hydraulic tensioning means on both sides of the transmission. In other words, the shift control is controlled as a function of the input speed and the position of the accelerator pedal.  It is noted that no electrical sensing or control means are included in the shift control.
[F16H 61/66268] using purely mechanical sensing or control means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a belt-type CVT comprising a lever transmission (2,10) as mechanical control means connected to an accelerator pedal (1). The transmission ratio of the belt-type CVT is varied in response to an engine-driven spring-loaded centrifugal governor (3) and position of the accelerator pedal (1). In other words, the shift control is controlled as a function of the input speed and the position of the accelerator pedal.
[F16H 61/66272] characterised by means for controlling the torque transmitting capability of the gearing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of pinching or clamping force, e.g. by means of a pressure control, or using input from a torque sensor.  Control of belt slipping, which is inversely related to pinching force.  Control of compensation of centrifugal pressure, i.e. the "extra" secondary pressure that results from the centrifugal effect on the oil in the actuator cylinder used to exert the clamping force.
[F16H 61/664] Friction gearings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of continuously variable gearings with friction gearing.  **Glossary:** - control of shifting: control of one or more transmission ratios
[F16H 61/6645] controlling shifting exclusively as a function of speed and torque -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transmission ratio control specially adapted to continuously variable gearings with friction gearing dependent only on the combination of speed and torque parameters.
[F16H 61/6646] controlling shifting exclusively as a function of speed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a friction-type cone CVT comprising centrifugal weights (12) as mechanical control means. As the speed increases, the centrifugal weights (12) move radially outwards, thereby adjusting the transmission ratio of the friction-type cone CVT.
[F16H 61/6647] controlling shifting exclusively as a function of torque -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a friction-type ball CVT comprising an input shaft (2), frictionally engaging balls (8) and a member (10) with a screw-thread connection to an output shaft (3). When the input shaft (2) is rotated in a first direction, balls (8) roll on an internal periphery (1 a ) of a relatively fixed housing (1), and thereby move orbitally around the axis of the input shaft (2). As a result, a member (10) together with the output shaft (3) is rotated at a transmission ratio dependent upon the radial position of the balls. When the output shaft (3) is rotatable freely, the compression spring (12) will prevent the member (10) from being moved axially thereby providing a relatively high transmission ratio. However, with increasing load or torque on the output shaft (3), the screw-thread connection between the output shaft (3) and the member (10) will cause the member (10) to move axially, thereby moving the balls (8) radially inwards and reducing the transmission ratio as a function of the load or torque on the output shaft (3).
[F16H 61/6648] controlling of shifting being influenced by a signal derived from the engine and the main coupling -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a toroidal toroidal friction CVT comprising an engine speed (Ne) sensor (34), a torque converter turbine speed (Nt) sensor (32), a transmission output shaft speed (No) sensor (33), a vehicle speed (VSP) sensor (30) and an accelerator opening position (APO) sensor (31) as electronic sensing means, as well as electronic control means (16) and (20) to control the transmission ratio of the toroidal CVT. In other words, the shift control is controlled as a function of the input and output speeds as well as of the position of the accelerator pedal.
[F16H 61/6649] characterised by the means for controlling the torque transmitting capability of the gearing -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F16H 61/68] specially adapted for stepped gearings -- This subclass covers gearings that are specially adapted for stepped gear arrangements, providing multiple discrete speed ratios within a single gear set to control or reverse rotary motion. It is part of mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units, gearing, and the control functions used in change-speed or reversing gearings.
[F16H 61/682] with interruption of drive -- The code refers to gear units that convey rotary motion while allowing the drive to be periodically stopped, such as in speed-change or reversing gearboxes that incorporate a clutch, brake or similar device to interrupt power transmission. It is used for mechanisms where controlled interruption of motion is required, for example in indexing or cyclical drive applications.
[F16H 61/684] without interruption of drive -- The entry covers gear arrangements that alter speed or reverse the direction of rotary motion while keeping the power flow continuous, meaning the drive is never interrupted during the transition. It applies to mechanical engineering gear systems designed for seamless change-speed or reversing operations.
[F16H 61/686] with orbital gears -- F16H 61/686 covers the use of orbital or epicyclic gears as part of control functions in change-speed or reversing gearings that convey rotary motion. It relates to arrangements where gear carriers rotate around a central axis to provide speed variation or direction change, commonly employed in automotive transmissions and industrial drives.
[F16H 61/688] with two inputs, e.g. selection of one of two torque-flow paths by clutches -- This CPC subgroup (F16H 61/688) covers gear arrangements that have two input shafts and select one of two torque-flow paths by using clutches. It applies to change-speed or reversing gearings where the control function directs rotary motion through one of the two paths.
[F16H 61/70] specially adapted for change-speed gearing in group arrangement, i.e. with separate change-speed gear trains arranged in series, e.g. range or overdrive-type gearing arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of transmissions with different gear trains in series, e.g. a main gear combined with an auxiliary range gear
[F16H 61/702] using electric or electrohydraulic control means -- This subclass covers gear mechanisms whose speed or direction is changed by means of electric or electrohydraulic control devices. It includes systems that employ motors, solenoids or hydraulic actuators to select gears or alter transmission ratios in conveyors or other rotary-motion equipment.
[F16H 61/705] using hydraulic and mechanical control means -- The code covers gear systems that change speed or reverse rotation and are operated by hydraulic or mechanical control devices such as valves, pistons, levers or linkages. These control means regulate the transmission of rotary motion in machinery.
[F16H 61/707] using only mechanical control means -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16H 61/707 pertains to the use of purely mechanical control methods within change-speed or reversing gearings designed for transmitting rotary motion. This classification is specific to mechanisms that do not incorporate electronic or hydraulic controls, focusing instead on mechanical components and interactions.
[F16H 63/00] Control outputs to change-speed- or reversing-gearings for conveying rotary motion -- **Glossary:** - mechanism: a kinematic chain consisting either of a single element or alternatively of a series of elements, the position of each point on the kinematic chain being derivable from the position of any other point on the chain, and therefore, for a given position of a point on one of the elements forming the kinematic chain there is only one position for each of the other points on the element or series of elements forming the kinematic chain - final output mechanism: the mechanism which includes the final output element, e.g. hydraulic or electromagnetic clutch, synchronizer clutch, ball and ramp clutch - final output element: the final element which is moved to establish a gear ratio, i.e. which achieves the linking or coupling between two power transmission means, e.g. reverse idler gear, gear cluster, coupling sleeve or an apply piston of a hydraulic clutch - actuating mechanism: the mechanism, the movement of which causes the movement of another mechanism by being in mutual contact - final actuating mechanism: the mechanism actuating the final output mechanism, i.e. this mechanism actuates the final output mechanism which includes the final output element, e.g. coupling sleeve, shift fork, hydraulic circuit, electromagnetic solenoid or motor
[F16H 63/02] Final output mechanisms therefor -- This subclass covers the last stage of a speed-changing or reversing gearbox that delivers the finished rotary motion to the load. It includes final shafts, couplings, sprockets, and other output elements that receive power from the gear train and transmit it to the driven equipment.
[F16H 63/04] a single final output mechanism being moved by a single final actuating mechanism -- F16H 63/04 pertains to devices in which a single final output mechanism is driven directly by a single final actuating mechanism, providing control of speed or reversal of rotary motion within gear transmissions.
[F16H 63/06] the final output mechanism having an indefinite number of positions -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F16H 63/062] electric or electro-mechanical actuating means -- Electric or electro-mechanical actuating means are devices that use electrical power or combined electrical-mechanical components to control the shifting or reversal of gears, thereby adjusting the speed or direction of rotary motion in mechanical transmissions.
[F16H 63/065] hydraulic actuating means -- F16H 63/065 covers hydraulic actuating means that provide control outputs for changing speed or reversing gearings in systems that convey rotary motion. The classification relates to hydraulic devices that adjust gear positions or engage/disengage gears to vary torque and direction in mechanical power transmission.
[F16H 63/067] mechanical actuating means -- This group concerns mechanical actuating devices that serve as control outputs for variable-speed or reversing gear transmissions used to convey rotary motion. It includes mechanisms that adjust gear ratios or direction in gearboxes, typically by levers, cams, or hydraulic/pneumatic linkages.
[F16H 63/08] Multiple final output mechanisms being moved by a single common final actuating mechanism -- The class defines devices in which one common final actuating mechanism drives several final output mechanisms, such as multiple gears or shafts, to convey rotary motion and provide speed-change or reversing functions.
[F16H 63/10] the final actuating mechanism having a series of independent ways of movement, each way of movement being associated with only one final output mechanism -- A final actuating mechanism composed of several independent movement paths, each of which is linked to a single output device. The design allows each movement to control a distinct speed-changing or reversing gear, providing separate control outputs for conveying rotary motion. This arrangement is used in gear-changing systems where individual actuators drive individual gear selections.
[F16H 63/12] two or more ways of movement occurring simultaneously -- F16H 63/12 pertains to gear control devices that generate two or more simultaneous movements to vary speed or reverse the direction of rotary motion. It falls within the hierarchy mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, and control outputs for changing speed or reversing gearings that convey rotary motion.
[F16H 63/14] the final output mechanisms being successively actuated by repeated movement of the final actuating mechanism -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16H 63/14 pertains to mechanical engineering, specifically gearing systems where control outputs for changing speed or reversing gearings are activated through the repeated movement of a final actuating mechanism. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office to categorize inventions related to these specific types of gear mechanisms.
[F16H 63/16] the final output mechanisms being successively actuated by progressive movement of the final actuating mechanism -- The entry covers final output devices that are engaged in sequence by the progressive movement of the last actuating mechanism, typically used to alter speed or reverse the direction of rotary motion in gear transmissions.
[F16H 63/18] the final actuating mechanism comprising cams -- F16H 63/18 covers the final actuating mechanism of gearboxes that uses cams to control output speed or reverse direction. It includes cam-driven devices that adjust gear ratios or change the sense of rotary motion in mechanical power transmission systems.
[F16H 63/20] with preselection and subsequent movement of each final output mechanism by movement of the final actuating mechanism in two different ways, e.g. guided by a shift gate -- The subclass concerns gear transmission systems in which each final output shaft is preselected and then moved by the final actuating mechanism in two distinct ways, typically using a shift gate to choose the gear before the actual shift. It covers control devices that change speed or reverse rotary motion by this dual-movement, preselection arrangement.
[F16H 63/206] the final output mechanisms being mounted coaxially on a single shaft, e.g. mono rail shift mechanism -- This CPC subclass covers gear-changing arrangements where the final output devices are mounted coaxially on a single shaft, allowing speed or direction shifts on the same axis, exemplified by mono-rail shift mechanisms.
[F16H 63/22] the final output mechanisms being simultaneously moved by the final actuating mechanism -- This CPC subclass covers final output mechanisms that are moved simultaneously by a single actuating mechanism in speed-changing or reversing gearings that transmit rotary motion.
[F16H 63/24] each of the final output mechanisms being moved by only one of the various final actuating mechanisms -- This subclass concerns gearings where each final output shaft or mechanism is driven by a single actuating device, providing independent speed or direction control for each output without sharing actuators.
[F16H 63/26] some of the movements of the final output mechanisms being caused by another final output mechanism -- This subgroup deals with configurations where the motion of one final output mechanism drives the motion of another final output mechanism, such as linked final drives that transmit motion between them, often used in variable-speed or reversing gearsets.
[F16H 63/28] two or more final actuating mechanisms moving the same final output mechanism -- The code F16H 63/28 classifies systems where two or more separate final actuators work together to drive a single output mechanism, typically in gearings that alter speed or reverse rotation to convey rotary motion. This falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 63/285] with a first final actuating member applying a force to two or more final output members and a second final actuating member locking in position another final output member -- A mechanism in which a primary actuating member simultaneously applies force to two or more output members while a secondary actuating member locks another output member in position, enabling speed change or reversal of rotary motion in gearing assemblies. This falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing, and control outputs for speed-changing or reversing gearings.

[F16H 63/3016] Final output mechanisms varying the leverage or force ratio -- The class F16H 63/3016 covers final-output mechanisms that modify the leverage or force ratio of a gearing system to alter speed, torque or direction of rotary motion. [F16H 63/3416] Parking lock mechanisms or brakes in the transmission -- Parking lock mechanisms or brakes in a transmission are components that engage the output shaft to prevent rotation when a vehicle is stationary, typically using a pawl, brake drum or similar device to hold the gear set in place and lock the drivetrain.

[F16H 7/00] Gearing for conveying rotary motion by endless flexible members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Belts drives, chain drives or rope drives with a fixed ratio, and tensioning mechanisms and guiding means used in such systems. [F16H 7/0829] with vibration damping means -- The subclass F16H 7/0829 covers gear mechanisms that transmit rotary motion using continuous flexible elements-such as belts or chains-and incorporate features that reduce vibration. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and gearing for conveying rotary motion.
[F16H 7/0831] of the dry friction type -- This CPC subclass covers gears that transmit rotary motion by endless flexible members such as belts or chains where power is transferred through dry-friction contact rather than interlocking teeth. It includes mechanisms that rely on surface friction without lubrication to convey motion.
[F16H 7/0834] of the viscous friction type, e.g. viscous fluid -- F16H 7/0834 covers gear assemblies that transmit rotary motion by viscous friction, using a fluid-filled interface to provide smooth, slip-free power transfer. These devices, such as fluid couplings and viscous-type gear drives, generate resistance through fluid shear rather than direct tooth contact.
[F16H 7/0836] of the fluid and restriction type, e.g. dashpot -- The entry concerns fluid-based and restriction-type devices such as dashpots that are incorporated into gearing systems that transmit rotary motion through endless flexible members like belts or chains. These components provide damping, torque control or speed regulation by restricting the flow of a fluid within the mechanism.
[F16H 7/0838] of the dissipating material type, e.g. elastomeric spring -- F16H 7/0838 covers gearing that transmits rotary motion using endless flexible members made of dissipating materials, such as elastomeric springs. It pertains to mechanisms where the elastic element itself performs the gear function, providing both energy storage and motion transfer within the gear train.
[F16H 7/0848] with means for impeding reverse motion -- F16H 7/0848 covers gearing mechanisms that use endless flexible members to transmit rotary motion and include a device that prevents the driven member from rotating backward. Such anti-reversal features are common in belt drives for conveyors, machine tools and automotive power-take-off systems.
[F16H 7/10] by adjusting the axis of a pulley -- This subclass covers gearings that transmit rotary motion through continuous flexible members such as belts or chains, where the transmission ratio or path is altered by moving the pulley's axis. Adjusting the axis changes the effective pulley diameter or belt tension, allowing speed or torque to be varied.
[F16H 7/12] of an idle pulley -- F16H 7/12 concerns idle pulleys, which are non-driven wheels positioned in belt or chain drives to guide, tension or change the direction of the endless flexible member without transmitting torque, serving mainly to align or support the moving belt in mechanical transmission systems.
[F16H 7/1209] with vibration damping means -- This subclass covers gear mechanisms that transfer rotary motion using continuous flexible elements such as belts or chains and incorporate devices or features that reduce or absorb vibration. Examples include belts or chains with built-in dampers, elastic inserts, or auxiliary springs designed to smooth torque transmission. It falls under CPC group F16H 7/12 for flexible-member gearing.
[F16H 7/1218] of the dry friction type -- F16H 7/1218 refers to gearing that transmits rotary motion by endless flexible members, specifically of the dry friction type. This falls within mechanical engineering, under engineering elements and units for gearing.
[F16H 7/1227] of the viscous friction type, e.g. viscous fluid -- This subclass covers gear systems that transmit rotary motion with endless flexible members such as belts or bands, where torque is transferred by viscous friction created by a fluid layer. [F16H 7/1281] where the axis of the pulley moves along a substantially circular path -- F16H 7/1281 covers belt-type gearing in which the pulley's shaft follows a substantially circular trajectory, such as a belt drive where the pulley revolves around a fixed centre. [F16H 7/129] with means for impeding reverse motion -- A gear system that transmits rotary motion through an endless flexible member and incorporates a mechanism that blocks or resists motion in the opposite direction. [F16H 7/16] without adjusting the driving or driven shaft -- The F16H 7/16 subclass covers gear systems that transmit rotary motion by means of endless flexible members such as belts or chains, where neither the driving nor the driven shaft is adjustable, allowing fixed-ratio power transfer without shaft positioning changes.
[F16H 7/18] Means for guiding or supporting belts, ropes, or chains -- This subclass covers devices that guide or support belts, ropes or chains used in power transmission, such as pulleys, idlers, rollers, tensioners and similar supporting elements. It falls under mechanical engineering gear arrangements that convey rotary motion by endless flexible members.
[F16H 7/20] Mountings for rollers or pulleys -- F16H 7/20 refers to mounting arrangements for rollers or pulleys that support and secure these components in belt or chain drive systems used to convey rotary motion. It includes brackets, housings, bases and other structures that attach the rotating elements to machines or frames.
[F16H 7/22] Belt, rope, or chain shifters -- F16H 7/22 covers mechanisms that shift or adjust the position of belts, ropes, or chains used to transmit rotary motion, enabling selection of different speed ratios or torque outputs. These shifters may be manual or automatic and are employed in gear drives, conveyors, and power transmission systems.

Such devices include pulleys, sprockets, tensioners and other supporting structures that secure the flexible drive elements to shafts or housings. It falls under the mechanical engineering hierarchy of engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 9/00] Gearings for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio, or for reversing rotary motion, by endless flexible members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Only transmission layout of belt or chain or rope drives for conveying rotary motion with variable gear ratio or for reversing rotary motion.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Control of change-speed or reversing-gearings conveying rotary motion -> F16H59/00 - F16H63/00 - Actuators for shifting -> F16H63/06
[F16H 9/02] without members having orbital motion -- F16H 9/02 classifies gearings that transmit rotary motion or provide a variable or reversing gear ratio without employing any members that move in an orbital (circular) path, such as belts, chains or sprockets. The transmission relies on other mechanisms rather than flexible, orbiting elements.
[F16H 9/04] using belts, V-belts, or ropes -- F16H 9/04 covers gear mechanisms that transmit rotary motion using belts, V-belts or ropes, typically employed to vary speed ratios or reverse direction in mechanical power transmission.
[F16H 9/06] engaging a stepped pulley -- F16H 9/06 covers gearings that transfer rotary motion with endless flexible members such as belts or chains and that engage a stepped pulley to obtain a variable gear ratio or reverse motion. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, gearing.
[F16H 9/08] engaging a conical drum -- F16H 9/08 covers gearing arrangements that transmit rotary motion by engaging a conical drum with an endless flexible member such as a belt or chain. The conical shape allows the gear ratio to vary or the direction of rotation to be reversed as the flexible member moves along the drum's taper, providing adjustable speed or torque transmission in mechanical drive systems.
[F16H 9/10] engaging a pulley provided with radially-actuatable elements carrying the belt -- This subclass covers pulleys that have radially-actuatable elements such as jaws or pins which move outward to engage and drive a belt, providing variable or reversible rotary transmission in gearings.
[F16H 9/12] engaging a pulley built-up out of relatively axially-adjustable parts in which the belt engages the opposite flanges of the pulley directly without interposed belt-supporting members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transmissions where a belt is axially squeezed between two sheaves of at least one pulley.
[F16H 9/125] characterised by means for controlling the geometrical interrelationship of pulleys and the endless flexible member, e.g. belt alignment or position of the resulting axial pulley force in the plane perpendicular to the pulley axis -- F16H 9/125 classifies belt-drive arrangements that include devices for actively controlling the geometry between pulleys and the endless flexible member, such as mechanisms that align the belt or adjust the axial force on a pulley in a plane perpendicular to its axis, enabling precise motion transmission or reversal.
[F16H 9/14] using only one pulley built-up out of adjustable conical parts -- F16H 9/14 relates to gearings that convey rotary motion with a variable ratio or reverse motion by means of endless flexible members, using a single pulley made from adjustable conical parts. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → gearing and describes mechanisms where the gear ratio is changed by altering the cone positions of one built-up pulley.
[F16H 9/16] using two pulleys, both built-up out of adjustable conical parts -- F16H 9/16 covers gear arrangements that transmit rotary motion by means of an endless flexible member whose ratio is varied using two pulleys constructed from adjustable conical parts, allowing continuous change of speed or direction without discrete gears. This falls under mechanical engineering gearings for variable-ratio or reversing drives.
[F16H 9/18] only one flange of each pulley being adjustable -- F16H 9/18 pertains to gearing systems that use endless flexible members to convey rotary motion with variable gear ratios or for reversing motion, specifically focusing on designs where only one flange of each pulley is adjustable. This classification is part of the broader category of mechanical engineering and engineering elements, under the subcategory of gearing.
[F16H 9/20] both flanges of the pulleys being adjustable -- F16H 9/20 relates to gearings that transmit rotary motion by endless flexible members such as belts, in which both pulley flanges are adjustable to vary the effective gear ratio or to reverse direction. The adjustable flanges allow fine-tuning of speed and torque without replacing the gear components.
[F16H 9/22] specially adapted for ropes -- F16H 9/22 covers gearings that use endless flexible members, such as ropes, to transmit rotary motion with a variable gear ratio or to reverse direction. It includes designs where the gearing system is specially adapted to work with ropes rather than chains or belts, allowing smooth power transfer in applications that require flexible, rope-driven drives.
[F16H 9/24] using chains or toothed belts, belts in the form of links -- F16H 9/24 covers gearings that transmit rotary motion by endless flexible members in the form of chains or toothed belts composed of linked elements. These arrangements provide variable gear ratios or reverse rotation, typically used in power transmission systems.
[F16H 9/26] with members having orbital motion -- F16H 9/26 relates to gearings that transmit rotary motion with endless flexible members that follow an orbital path around a fixed centre, such as belts or chains moving in a circular trajectory. The arrangement allows variable gear ratios or reversal of motion while the members travel in this orbital motion.
[F16J] PISTONS -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F16J 1/00] Pistons -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pistons not specific to a particular use.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rotary pistons, e.g. for "Wankel" type engines -> F01C - Pistons for hydraulic engines -> F03C - Pumps -> F04B - Floats -> F16K33/00
[F16J 10/00] Engine or like cylinders -- F16J 10/00 covers the design, construction and features of engine or similar cylinders used with pistons, including aspects such as bore geometry, cooling passages, sealing arrangements and material selection, applicable to internal-combustion, piston-type and comparable power-generation units.
[F16J 1/001] One-piece pistons -- The F16J 1/001 subclass concerns pistons that are produced as a single integral component rather than assembled from separate parts, such as cast, forged or machined one-piece pistons. It addresses designs and manufacturing methods for these monolithic pistons used in engines or compressors.
[F16J 10/02] Cylinders designed to receive moving pistons or plungers -- Cylinders designed to receive moving pistons or plungers are components that house a reciprocating element, providing alignment, sealing and support for the piston's motion in hydraulic, pneumatic or mechanical systems. The classification includes various designs of the cylinder barrel, end caps and internal features that enable efficient power transmission and resistance to wear.
[F16J 1/003] with integral sealing lips -- The CPC subclass F16J 1/003 covers pistons that incorporate integral sealing lips, typically metal pistons for hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders where the sealing elements are formed as part of the piston body to provide a leak-tight seal against the cylinder wall.
[F16J 10/04] Running faces -- F16J 10/04 relates to the running faces of pistons and similar cylinders, meaning the surfaces that slide against the cylinder wall during operation. It includes designs of skirts, rings and surface treatments intended to reduce friction and wear in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16J 1/005] obtained by assembling several pieces -- This subgroup relates to pistons that are manufactured by joining multiple components rather than being cast or forged as a single piece. It covers designs where the piston body is built from separate elements such as ring seats, skirts, and heads that are assembled. The classification is used for patents describing modular piston construction.
[F16J 1/006] of different materials -- F16J 1/006 covers pistons that are made from two or more different materials, such as metal-plastic composites or layered constructions, to combine specific mechanical, thermal or wear-resistant properties. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, within engineering elements and units, specifically pistons.
[F16J 1/008] with sealing lips -- F16J 1/008 covers pistons that incorporate sealing lips on their surface to provide a tight seal against the cylinder wall during operation. The sealing lips may be made of elastomeric or metal material and are designed to reduce leakage in hydraulic, pneumatic or oil-filled cylinders. This feature is applied to pistons used in various mechanical engineering applications.
[F16J 1/01] characterised by the use of particular materials -- F16J 1/01 pertains to pistons whose defining feature is the use of specific materials, such as alloys, composites or ceramics, selected to influence strength, wear resistance or thermal behavior. It falls within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pistons.
[F16J 1/02] Bearing surfaces -- F16J 1/02 covers bearing surfaces of pistons, i.e., the surfaces that slide against cylinder walls or other components, including design features, materials, lubrication, and geometry to reduce friction and wear. This subclass falls under pistons in mechanical engineering.
[F16J 1/04] Resilient guiding parts, e.g. skirts, particularly for trunk pistons -- The F16J 1/04 subclass refers to resilient guiding parts, such as flexible skirts, that are employed to guide, align and seal trunk pistons in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders. These components provide wear resistance and limited movement while maintaining piston positioning.
[F16J 1/06] with separate expansion members -- F16J 1/06 classifies pistons that incorporate separate expansion members, such as auxiliary rings, sleeves or devices that expand independently of the main piston body to improve sealing or adjust clearance. These features are used in internal-combustion engines, compressors and hydraulic actuators to enhance performance and wear resistance.
[F16J 1/08] Constructional features providing for lubrication -- F16J 1/08 relates to piston designs that incorporate built-in lubrication features. It includes surfaces, channels, grooves or other structural elements that supply oil to the piston-cylinder interface to reduce friction and wear.
[F16J 1/09] with means for guiding fluids -- F16J 1/09 covers pistons that incorporate devices for directing fluids, such as passages, channels or ducts that guide lubricants, cooling liquids or gases to specific piston surfaces. These fluid-guiding means improve thermal management, reduce wear and enhance sealing performance within hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders.
[F16J 1/10] Connection to driving members -- The F16J 1/10 subclass concerns the means by which a piston is linked to a driving member such as a crankshaft, cam, or linear actuator. It covers devices, fittings, or mechanisms that transmit motion or force from the driving element to the piston, including couplings, levers, and flexible links designed for mechanical-engineered piston systems.
[F16J 1/12] with piston-rods, e.g. rigid connections -- F16J 1/12 refers to pistons that incorporate piston-rods, meaning the piston and its guiding rod form a rigid assembly rather than a separate, flexible connection. This subclass covers designs where the rod is integral to the piston, as found in many hydraulic and pneumatic actuators.
[F16J 1/14] with connecting-rods, i.e. pivotal connections -- F16J 1/14 covers pistons that are joined to connecting rods through pivotal or hinged connections, allowing the piston's reciprocating motion to be transferred to a rotating crankshaft. The classification includes designs where the piston-rod link is a rotating or swinging joint rather than a fixed attachment.
[F16J 1/16] with gudgeon-pin -- F16J 1/16 designates pistons that are joined to the connecting rod by a gudgeon (wrist) pin, a cylindrical shaft that allows the piston to pivot as it moves within the cylinder. The subgroup covers the shape, location and securing methods of such pins in internal-combustion and other reciprocating engines.
[F16J 1/18] Securing of gudgeon-pins -- F16J 1/18, within the mechanical engineering hierarchy under pistons, covers devices and methods for securing gudgeon-pins in piston assemblies. The code includes retainers, clips, locking mechanisms and related structures that prevent axial or rotational movement of the pin during engine operation.
[F16J 1/20] with rolling contact, other than in ball or roller bearings -- F16J 1/20 classifies pistons that employ rolling contact with the cylinder wall or supporting surfaces, but do not use conventional ball or roller bearings. This includes designs where a lubricating film, flexible pads, or other mechanisms create a smooth rolling interface to reduce friction and wear.
[F16J 12/00] Pressure vessels in general -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Covers for pressure vessels -> F16J13/00
[F16J 1/22] with universal joint, e.g. ball-joint -- F16J 1/22 is a CPC subclass in the mechanical-engineering section that deals with pistons equipped with a universal joint, such as a ball-joint, enabling angular or pivoting motion between the piston and its drive mechanism.
[F16J 1/24] designed to give the piston some rotary movement about its axis -- F16J 1/24 covers pistons that are designed to give the piston a rotary movement about its own axis, usually by means of helical grooves, cam surfaces or similar features that cause the piston to spin while it moves linearly in a cylinder.
[F16J 13/00] Covers or similar closure members for pressure vessels in general -- F16J 13/00 covers general designs for covers, lids or comparable closure members used to seal pressure vessels, describing their structure, mounting and sealing features. The class is relevant to pistons and other mechanical engineering elements that require reliable, leak-proof closure of high-pressure containers.
[F16J 13/02] Detachable closure members -- F16J 13/02 relates to detachable closure members, such as covers, used to seal or provide access to pressure vessels. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for pistons and other engineering elements, describing devices that can be removed and reattached to enclose a vessel.
[F16J 13/04] attached with a bridge member -- F16J 13/04 concerns pistons and comparable closure members for pressure vessels that are secured to the vessel by a bridge member. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pistons, and addresses designs where the closure is linked to the vessel with a bridging component.
[F16J 13/06] attached only by clamps along the circumference -- F16J 13/06 refers to closure members for pressure vessels that are secured solely by clamps placed around the circumference, rather than by welding or bolts. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pistons.
[F16J 13/065] the clamp comprising a ring encircling the flange -- The entry F16J 13/065 defines a clamp that uses a ring passing around a flange to secure and seal a pressure-vessel closure. The encircling ring distributes load on the flange, providing a reliable closure member for pistons and similar mechanical components.
[F16J 13/08] attached by one or more members actuated to project behind a part or parts of the frame -- F16J 13/08 pertains to closure members for pressure vessels that are mounted on the frame by one or more linkage elements and, when actuated, swing or extend behind part of the frame to provide a sealing closure.
[F16J 13/10] attached by means of a divided ring -- The CPC subclass F16J 13/10 covers closure members such as covers or similar devices for pressure vessels that are secured to the vessel by means of a divided ring, a split band that can be assembled around the vessel opening and fastened to hold the closure in place. This falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pistons.
[F16J 13/12] attached by wedging action by means of screw-thread, interrupted screw-thread, bayonet closure, or the like -- F16J 13/12 covers closure members for pressure vessels that are secured by wedging actions such as screw-threads, interrupted threads, bayonet fittings or similar mechanisms. These devices provide a seal by mechanical wedging rather than welding or bolting.
[F16J 13/14] attached exclusively by spring action or elastic action -- F16J 13/14 pertains to covers or similar closure members for pressure vessels that are attached solely by spring or elastic action. It falls under the piston group within mechanical engineering elements and units. This classification addresses devices that rely on inherent elasticity rather than screws, latches or other fastening methods.
[F16J 13/16] Pivoted closures -- The CPC entry F16J 13/16 relates to pivoted closures, which are closure members that rotate about a pivot to seal pressure vessels. It includes covers and similar devices that close or open pressure chambers by swinging on a hinge, typically used in mechanical engineering applications such as pistons and other engineering elements.
[F16J 13/18] pivoted directly on the frame -- F16J 13/18 concerns closure members for pressure vessels that are mounted so they pivot directly on the surrounding frame, without intermediate hinges or brackets. The subclass includes lids, doors or similar closures whose pivot pin is fixed to the vessel frame, allowing the member to swing open or closed about that point.
[F16J 13/20] mounted by mobile fastening on swinging arms -- This CPC subclass covers pistons that act as closure members for pressure vessels and are attached to swinging arms by a mobile fastening. The mounting allows the piston to move between open and closed positions while remaining securely fastened.
[F16J 13/22] with movement parallel to the plane of the opening -- F16J 13/22 designates closure members for pressure vessels that open and close by moving parallel to the plane of the opening, such as sliding or swing-type lids and covers. It falls under the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of pistons and related elements. This classification is used in both the EPO and USPTO patent databases.
[F16J 13/24] with safety devices, e.g. to prevent opening prior to pressure release -- This class covers piston-like closure members for pressure vessels that incorporate safety mechanisms to stop the device from being opened until the internal pressure has been safely released.
[F16J 15/00] Sealings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sealing means that acts at a joint or juncture of adjacent parts
[F16J 15/002] comprising at least two sealings in succession  F16J15/162 ,  F16J15/40  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Special parts or details relating to lubrication or cooling of the sealing itself -> F16J15/162 - Sealings by means of fluid -> F16J15/40
[F16J 15/004] forming of recuperation chamber for the leaking fluid -- This subclass addresses techniques for shaping a recuperation chamber that captures fluid leaking from piston sealings, providing a cavity or passage that collects the escaped fluid and redirects it back into the system to improve efficiency and reduce loss.
[F16J 15/006] with division of the pressure  F16J15/44  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Free-space packings -> F16J15/44
[F16J 15/008] with provision to put out of action at least one sealing; One sealing sealing only on standstill; Emergency or servicing sealings  F16J15/164  takes precedence -- F16J 15/008 refers to piston seal designs that incorporate a mechanism allowing at least one sealing element to be rendered inoperative, typically used when the piston is stationary. It covers emergency or service seals and configurations where only a single seal is employed during standstill. The broader group F16J15/164 supersedes this subclass.
[F16J 15/02] between relatively-stationary surfaces -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sealings with packing ring expanded or pressed into place by fluid pressure -> F16J15/46 - Sealings influenced by the pressure within the member to be sealed -> F16J15/48
[F16J 15/021] with elastic packing  F16J15/08  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sealings with exclusively metal packing -> F16J15/08
[F16J 15/022] characterised by structure or material -- F16J 15/022 designates piston sealings whose defining feature is the particular structure or material used, such as metal, polymer, composite or multi-layer constructions. The subclass groups seal designs where the choice of material or structural arrangement is the primary means of distinguishing the sealing solution.
[F16J 15/024] the packing being locally weakened in order to increase elasticity -- This subclass covers piston seal packings that include a deliberately weakened zone to increase their elasticity, enabling the seal to conform more readily to surface irregularities and pressure variations while preserving overall sealing integrity.
[F16J 15/025] and with at least one flexible lip -- In the cooperative patent classification F16J 15/025, the focus is on piston sealings that incorporate at least one flexible lip to maintain contact with the cylinder wall and accommodate pressure variations. The flexible lip design improves sealing efficiency, reduces wear, and allows for tolerance compensation in mechanical engineering applications. This sub-category falls under pistons within engineering elements and units.
[F16J 15/027] and with a hollow profile -- This subgroup covers pistons that incorporate a hollow internal profile, allowing passage of fluid, lubrication or pressure-balancing chambers within the piston body. The hollow design is integrated with the sealing arrangement to improve sealing performance or enable specific fluid flows in hydraulic or pneumatic actuators.
[F16J 15/028] the packing being mechanically expanded against the sealing surface -- This subclass covers piston sealing arrangements in which the packing material is mechanically expanded to press tightly against the sealing surface, creating a leak-tight joint. It includes methods and devices that deform or pressure-the packing during installation to ensure firm contact with the piston or cylinder wall.
[F16J 15/04] without packing between the surfaces, e.g. with ground surfaces, with cutting edge -- F16J 15/04 covers piston sealing arrangements that eliminate separate packing material by using precisely ground mating surfaces or cutting edges to form a seal. These designs are applied in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders to reduce friction and wear while maintaining pressure tightness.
[F16J 15/06] with solid packing compressed between sealing surfaces -- F16J 15/06 classifies pistons that employ solid packing material placed between sealing surfaces, where the packing is compressed to create a seal. It pertains to sealing arrangements within piston assemblies used in hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid-driven systems.
[F16J 15/061] with positioning means  F16J15/0831  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Flat gasket with mounting aids -> F16J15/0831
[F16J 15/062] characterised by the geometry of the seat -- F16J 15/062 covers piston sealing arrangements in which the geometry of the sealing seat is a defining feature; it specifies the shape, angle or contour of the seat that contacts the sealing ring to control leakage. This subclass falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pistons → Sealings and addresses design details that affect sealing performance and durability.
[F16J 15/064] the packing combining the sealing function with other functions -- This subclass covers sealing packs that also perform additional tasks such as lubrication, heat transfer, or structural support within piston assemblies. The patents classified here describe designs where the packing provides the primary seal while integrating secondary functions to improve efficiency or durability.
[F16J 15/065] fire resistant -- The CPC entry F16J 15/065 covers sealings for pistons that are designed to retain their sealing function when exposed to fire or extreme heat. It relates to fire-resistant sealing elements, materials or constructions used in piston assemblies of engines or compressors to maintain performance and prevent leakage under high-temperature conditions.
[F16J 15/067] Split packings -- A split packing is a sealing assembly for pistons that is divided into two complementary halves, allowing installation without dismantling the cylinder or removing the piston. It provides a leak-tight barrier in hydraulic or pneumatic systems while facilitating maintenance and replacement.
[F16J 15/068] the packing swelling under working conditions -- F16J 15/068 addresses the swelling of sealing packings in piston assemblies when they are subjected to operating pressures, temperatures or fluids. It describes the causes of this expansion and its effect on sealing performance and maintenance.
[F16J 15/08] with exclusively metal packing -- A sealing for a piston that is formed solely from metal packing, used to prevent leakage while allowing movement within the cylinder. This type of seal is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pistons, sealings, CPC code F16J 15/08.
[F16J 15/0806] characterised by material or surface treatment -- The subclass F16J 15/0806 relates to pistons and their sealings where the defining feature is the material composition or a surface treatment applied to the sealing surfaces, such as coatings, hardening, or plating. It covers solutions that improve wear resistance, friction control, corrosion protection or chemical compatibility through these material or surface modifications.
[F16J 15/0812] with a braided or knitted body -- This subclass covers piston seal arrangements where the sealing element is formed from a braided or knitted material, typically used to provide flexible, wear-resistant sealing in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders. It falls under CPC group F16J 15/08 for sealings and specifies the braid or knit construction.
[F16J 15/0818] Flat gaskets -- Flat gaskets are thin, planar sealing elements placed between mating surfaces of piston assemblies to prevent leakage of fluids or gases. They are typically made of compressible materials such as rubber, fiber, or metal composites and are designed to deform under pressure to fill surface irregularities. In the CPC they fall under F16J 15/0818 covering flat gaskets for pistons.
[F16J 15/0825] laminated -- The F16J 15/0825 subclass covers piston seals that are formed from laminated structures, i.e., multiple bonded layers of different materials such as metals, polymers or composites. The layered construction provides enhanced sealing performance, resistance to wear, temperature variations and chemical attack compared with monolithic seal designs.
[F16J 15/0831] with mounting aids -- F16J 15/0831 pertains to piston sealings that incorporate features to facilitate their installation, such as guides, clamps, brackets or other mounting aids that simplify positioning and securing of the seal within the piston assembly.
[F16J 15/0881] the sealing effect being obtained by plastic deformation of the packing -- F16J 15/0881 covers sealing arrangements that obtain their sealing effect by plastically deforming the packing material, creating a tight joint by filling gaps through compression. It is applied to pistons and similar mechanical elements where the packing is permanently shaped to achieve a leak-tight seal.
[F16J 15/0887] the sealing effect being obtained by elastic deformation of the packing -- This CPC subclass covers seal designs in pistons where the packing material is intentionally deformed elastically to create a tight seal, relying on the material's ability to recover its shape after compression. It includes any arrangement where the sealing action is achieved primarily through such elastic deformation of the packing.
[F16J 15/0893] the packing having a hollow profile -- F16J 15/0893 covers packings that have a hollow profile used in piston seals. The hollow cavity within the sealing material can retain lubricant or allow for thermal expansion, enhancing sealing performance in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders.
[F16J 15/10] with non-metallic packing -- F16J 15/10 pertains to pistons that are sealed with non-metallic packing materials. It includes sealing arrangements for piston assemblies in hydraulic or pneumatic systems where the packing consists of polymers, elastomers or other non-metal substances.
[F16J 15/102] characterised by material -- F16J 15/102 covers piston sealing arrangements that are defined primarily by the material from which the seal is made, such as metal, elastomer, polymer or composite substances. The classification distinguishes designs where the sealing performance, durability or compatibility with the piston and cylinder is determined by the chosen material rather than by shape or configuration. It is used for patents describing material-specific sealing solutions in piston systems.
[F16J 15/104] characterised by structure -- F16J 15/104 pertains to piston sealing arrangements defined by their structural characteristics, such as the shape, material composition and construction details of the seal. It includes seal configurations and geometry that distinguish the sealing solution from other piston-seal designs.
[F16J 15/106] homogeneous -- F16J 15/106 covers piston sealings that are formed from a single uniform material without discrete layers or inserts, providing a continuous sealing surface. These homogeneous seals are typically machined or molded from elastomers, metals, or composites and are used to prevent leakage between piston and cylinder walls.
[F16J 15/108] Special methods for making a non-metallic packing -- F16J 15/108 covers specialized manufacturing techniques for producing non-metallic packing used as seals in piston assemblies. The methods focus on forming, shaping or treating polymeric or composite materials to achieve the required sealing performance.
[F16J 15/12] with metal reinforcement or covering -- F16J 15/12 covers piston sealings that incorporate metal reinforcement or a metal covering, used to improve durability and resistance to high pressure or temperature. The metal element may be a sleeve, liner or embedded strip that supports the sealing material. This sub-category falls under the broader pistons and sealing groups within mechanical engineering.
[F16J 15/121] with metal reinforcement -- F16J 15/121 refers to piston sealing designs that incorporate metal reinforcement, typically a metal band or strip integrated into the seal to increase strength, wear resistance and ability to withstand high pressures, while maintaining a seal between the piston and cylinder wall. This subgroup falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pistons → Sealings.
[F16J 15/122] generally parallel to the surfaces -- F16J 15/122 covers sealing configurations in piston assemblies where the sealing surfaces are arranged essentially parallel to one another, typically to restrict fluid flow along the cylinder wall. The classification applies to designs that rely on this parallel geometry to achieve a constant sealing gap and uniform pressure distribution during piston motion.
[F16J 15/123] Details relating to the edges of the packing -- The F16J 15/123 subclass covers technical features that pertain to the peripheral portions of packing used for piston sealing, including the shape, profile, surface treatment or reinforcement of the packing edges. It addresses how these edge details improve sealing performance, wear resistance and ease of installation in piston-type hydraulic or pneumatic devices.
[F16J 15/125] generally perpendicular to the surfaces -- F16J 15/125 refers to pistons with sealing arrangements where the sealing elements are oriented essentially perpendicular to the adjoining surfaces, such as the cylinder wall or piston head. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pistons, sealings, and addresses designs that control fluid leakage by using face-type or end-face seals positioned at right angles to the mating surfaces.
[F16J 15/126] consisting of additions, e.g. metallic fibres, metallic powders, randomly dispersed in the packing -- F16J 15/126 covers piston sealings that incorporate additives such as metallic fibers or powders randomly dispersed within the packing material. These additions improve wear resistance, sealing performance, or heat dissipation.
[F16J 15/127] the reinforcement being a compression stopper -- F16J 15/127 covers piston sealing arrangements where a reinforcement element functions as a compression stopper, preventing excessive axial movement or deformation of the seal under load, typically used in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders to maintain seal integrity.
[F16J 15/128] with metal covering -- This subclass covers piston sealing arrangements that incorporate a metal covering, protecting the sealing element and providing reinforcement against wear, pressure and temperature. It includes metal-jacketed lip seals, metal-capped O-rings and similar constructions used in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders.
[F16J 15/14] by means of granular or plastic material, or fluid -- F16J 15/14 covers sealing arrangements for pistons that use a granular, plastic or fluid medium to create a leak-tight barrier. The material is compressed or displaced by the piston movement to fill gaps and maintain pressure differentials, offering adaptable sealing performance in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16J 15/16] between relatively-moving surfaces -- F16J 15/16 pertains to sealings placed between surfaces that move relative to one another, such as pistons, rods or sliding components, and is intended to prevent fluid leakage while allowing motion. It includes static or dynamic designs like lip, O-ring, gland or similar sealing configurations.
[F16J 15/162] Special parts or details relating to lubrication or cooling of the sealing itself  F16J15/324 ,  F16J15/3404 ,  F16J15/40  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Details relating to lubrication or cooling of the sealing itself -> F16J15/324 - Sealings and characterised by parts or details relating to lubrication, cooling or venting of the seal -> F16J15/3404 - Sealings by means of fluid -> F16J15/40
[F16J 15/164] the sealing action depending on movements; pressure difference, temperature or presence of leaking fluid -- This subgroup covers sealing functions that are activated or modulated by relative movement between components, by differential pressure, temperature changes, or by the detection of fluid leakage. It pertains to pistons and other mechanical elements where the seal adapts its behavior to these operating conditions.
[F16J 15/166] with means to prevent the extrusion of the packing -- F16J 15/166 covers piston sealing arrangements that incorporate devices to prevent the packing material from being extruded out of the clearance during operation. It includes features such as anti-extrusion rings, backup rings or lip seals that act as barriers to packing extrusion under pressure.
[F16J 15/168] which permits material to be continuously conveyed -- F16J 15/168 relates to piston seals that incorporate a passage or channel enabling continuous conveyance of material through the sealed area while preserving the sealing function. Such designs are used in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders where fluid or other media must be transferred along the piston without compromising pressure containment.
[F16J 15/18] with stuffing-boxes for elastic or plastic packings -- This subclass F16J 15/18 relates to piston sealing arrangements that employ stuffing-boxes filled with elastic or plastic packings to maintain a leak-tight seal between moving surfaces. The packing deforms to fill gaps and accommodate wear, providing a reliable seal in hydraulic or pneumatic pistons. It falls under Mechanical Engineering, Engineering Elements and Units, Pistons, Sealings.
[F16J 15/181] for plastic packings -- F16J 15/181 covers sealing solutions for pistons that employ plastic packings, focusing on designs where the sealing element is made primarily of polymeric material. The classification includes plastic rings, sheets or molded components intended to prevent fluid leakage in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders, and related methods of installation.
[F16J 15/182] with lubricating, cooling or draining means -- F16J 15/182 relates to piston seal designs that incorporate built-in lubrication, cooling or drainage mechanisms, allowing fluid or oil to be supplied, heat to be removed or condensate to be expelled directly at the seal interface. These features reduce friction, control temperature and extend seal life in hydraulic and pneumatic cylinders.
[F16J 15/183] using a lantern ring -- F16J 15/183 pertains to the use of a lantern ring as a sealing element for pistons in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders. The lantern ring provides a flexible, radially extending seal that accommodates piston movement while preventing fluid leakage and wear.
[F16J 15/184] Tightening mechanisms -- F16J 15/184 pertains to devices that apply a securing force to pistons or sealing components, typically using clamps, screws, wedges, or similar tightening arrangements to maintain a leak-free or pressure-tight connection. The classification covers mechanisms that adjust or lock the seal in place during operation.
[F16J 15/185] with continuous adjustment of the compression of the packing -- F16J 15/185 covers piston sealing arrangements where the compression of the packing can be continuously adjusted, allowing the seal to adapt to changes in pressure, temperature or wear during operation. These mechanisms typically use screw, hydraulic or spring-actuated devices to modify the packing preload without needing to disassemble the piston.
[F16J 15/186] using springs -- The F16J 15/186 subclass covers pistons that employ springs as part of their sealing arrangement, typically to maintain contact pressure between the piston and cylinder wall. Such spring-assisted sealings improve reliability by compensating for wear, thermal expansion, or pressure variations, ensuring an effective barrier against fluid leakage. This technology falls under mechanical engineering elements concerning pistons and sealing mechanisms.
[F16J 15/187] Self-aligning stuffing-boxes -- Self-aligning stuffing-boxes are sealing devices for pistons that automatically adjust to maintain a tight seal despite misalignment, wear or pressure variations, commonly used in hydraulic and pneumatic cylinders. They are classified under CPC F16J 15/187, a subcategory of piston sealings in mechanical engineering.
[F16J 15/188] Split assemblies -- F16J 15/188 covers split sealing assemblies used on pistons, such as split gaskets, rings or other segmented seals that can be installed or removed without dismantling the piston itself. The design enables sealing of the piston chamber while allowing partial disassembly for maintenance or adjustment.
[F16J 15/189] Means for facilitating the removal of the packing -- Means for facilitating the removal of the packing are devices or mechanisms incorporated in piston sealing assemblies that allow the sealing material to be detached, extracted or replaced more easily, often using levers, removable clips, sliding sleeves or release brackets that reduce labor and minimise damage during maintenance.
[F16J 15/20] Packing materials therefor -- F16J 15/20 relates to the sealing or packing components used with pistons, covering the materials that fill gaps to prevent fluid leakage in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders. It includes various types of packing rings, glands, and other sealing inserts made from rubber, polymer, metal-filled composites or other engineered materials.
[F16J 15/22] shaped as strands, ropes, threads, ribbons, or the like -- The entry F16J 15/22 refers to sealing arrangements for pistons that are formed as flexible strands, ropes, threads, ribbons, or similar elongated shapes. Such seal elements are used to provide a leak-tight interface between moving piston components and the surrounding chamber, allowing compliance with mechanical tolerances while maintaining sealing performance.
[F16J 15/24] with radially or tangentially compressed packing -- F16J 15/24 relates to piston seal arrangements in which the sealing element is compressed either radially outward or tangentially around the piston to maintain a pressure barrier. It includes packings that are pre-loaded by deformation to fill gaps between moving and stationary surfaces, commonly used in hydraulic and pneumatic cylinders.
[F16J 15/26] with stuffing-boxes for rigid sealing rings -- F16J 15/26 covers piston sealing arrangements that use a stuffing-box to retain rigid sealing rings, providing a fixed-gap seal between the moving piston and the cylinder wall. The stuffing-box holds the rings in place and allows the seal to accommodate pressure and wear while maintaining rigidity.
[F16J 15/28] with sealing rings made of metal -- F16J 15/28 covers piston seal designs that employ metal sealing rings to prevent fluid leakage. The metal rings provide durable, wear-resistant contact with the cylinder wall, improving sealing performance in reciprocating engines, hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders, and similar devices.
[F16J 15/30] with sealing rings made of carbon -- F16J 15/30 designates piston sealing arrangements that employ sealing rings made of carbon, providing high wear resistance and low friction for reliable containment of gases or liquids. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pistons, and sealings.
[F16J 15/32] with elastic sealings, e.g. O-rings -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sealings with slip-ring pressed against a more or less radial face on one member -> F16J15/34  **Glossary:** - Lip: any flexible tapered or rounded part contacting the surface to be sealed e.g. O-ring
[F16J 15/3204] with at least one lip -- F16J 15/3204 relates to piston seal designs that incorporate at least one protruding lip to improve sealing performance. The lip contacts the cylinder wall or piston surface to prevent fluid leakage under pressure and is commonly used in hydraulic or pneumatic actuators.
[F16J 15/3208] provided with tension elements, e.g. elastic rings -- This subgroup covers piston sealing arrangements that incorporate tension elements such as elastic rings to maintain the seal. The tension components apply radial pressure, ensuring a tight fit between the piston and cylinder wall during operation. It is a specific refinement within sealings for pistons.
[F16J 15/3212] with metal springs -- F16J 15/3212 classifies piston sealings that incorporate metal springs, covering designs where a spring element provides a resilient seal against cylinder walls or pistons. It pertains to mechanical engineering applications that use metal-spring-based sealing arrangements to maintain pressure integrity and compensate for wear or thermal expansion.
[F16J 15/3216] supported in a direction parallel to the surfaces -- F16J 15/3216 pertains to piston sealings where the sealing element is supported in a direction parallel to the mating surfaces, such as by ribs, springs or supporting plates that run along the seal face. This arrangement helps maintain seal integrity under axial loads and reduces wear.
[F16J 15/322] supported in a direction perpendicular to the surfaces -- F16J 15/322 covers seal configurations in pistons where the sealing element is supported in a direction perpendicular to the sealing surfaces, such as a backing plate or spring that presses the seal onto the piston face. This arrangement provides consistent contact pressure and prevents extrusion of the seal material.
[F16J 15/3224] capable of accommodating changes in distances or misalignment between the surfaces, e.g. able to compensate for defaults of eccentricity or angular deviations -- A sealing arrangement designed to adjust to variations in spacing or misalignment of the mating surfaces, allowing it to compensate for eccentricity or angular deviations during operation. This flexibility helps maintain a reliable seal despite changes in distance or orientation between components.
[F16J 15/3228] formed by deforming a flat ring -- The code F16J 15/3228 refers to piston rings or sealing members that are produced by plastically deforming an initially flat ring into a shape with sealing lips or profiles. This method creates a ring that fits into a cylinder groove and provides a seal by elastic or hydraulic pressure. It is used in internal-combustion engines, hydraulic cylinders and similar mechanical systems.
[F16J 15/3232] having two or more lips -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sealings provided with a spring-tension element -> F16J15/3208 - Sealings formed by deforming a flat annular ring -> F16J15/3228
[F16J 15/3236] with at least one lip for each surface, e.g. U-cup packings -- F16J 15/3236 covers piston sealing arrangements that incorporate at least one sealing lip on each contacting surface, such as U-cup packings, designed to provide reliable pressure containment in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16J 15/324] Arrangements for lubrication or cooling of the sealing itself -- F16J 15/324 covers designs that supply lubricant or cooling fluid directly to piston seals, such as internal passages, spray nozzles or reservoirs that target the sealing surface, reducing wear, friction and temperature rise.
[F16J 15/3244] with hydrodynamic pumping action -- F16J 15/3244 covers piston-seal designs that employ hydrodynamic pumping action, where fluid is drawn into the seal clearance and expelled to generate a pressure film that maintains sealing under motion. This approach reduces friction and wear while preserving pressure containment in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders.
