[F16J 15/3248] provided with casings or supports -- This CPC subclass covers pistons that incorporate sealings which are mounted within or attached to protective casings or supporting structures. It includes designs where the sealing element is housed in a sleeve, bracket or other support to improve durability and prevent leakage.
[F16J 15/3252] with rigid casings or supports -- F16J 15/3252 covers piston sealings that are supported by rigid casings or other solid supports, used in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders to keep the sealing element in place and prevent fluid leakage while allowing the piston to move.
[F16J 15/3256] comprising two casing or support elements, one attached to each surface, e.g. cartridge or cassette seals -- This subclass relates to sealing arrangements that use two separate casing or support elements, each attached to one of the mating surfaces, forming a seal such as those found in cartridge-type or cassette seals. The configuration enables the seal to be assembled or replaced as a unit while maintaining pressure containment.
[F16J 15/326] with means for detecting or measuring relative rotation of the two elements -- F16J 15/326 covers pistons equipped with sealings that incorporate devices for detecting or measuring the relative rotation between the piston and the adjoining component, such as a cylinder or another sealing element. This enables monitoring of angular displacement to ensure proper function and wear detection in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16J 15/3264] the elements being separable from each other -- F16J 15/3264 is a subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification for sealing pistons, covering designs where the sealing elements are provided as separate, removable components rather than integral parts of the piston. This allows individual elements to be replaced or exchanged without dismantling the whole piston assembly.
[F16J 15/3268] Mounting of sealing rings -- The classification F16J 15/3268 concerns methods and devices for installing sealing rings onto pistons or similar components, including positioning, fastening and retaining techniques that ensure proper sealing performance within mechanical systems.
[F16J 15/3272] the rings having a break or opening, e.g. to enable mounting on a shaft otherwise than from a shaft end -- This group covers split sealing rings for pistons that include a break or opening to permit side-mounting on a shaft without needing end-wise access. The design enables easier installation and replacement in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders and falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pistons, sealings.
[F16J 15/3276] with additional static sealing between the sealing, or its casing or support, and the surface on which it is mounted -- F16J 15/3276 refers to pistons that include an additional static seal positioned between the sealing element-or its casing or support-and the surface on which it is mounted, providing enhanced leakage protection. This sub-class falls under sealings within the engineering elements and units of pistons.
[F16J 15/328] Manufacturing methods specially adapted for elastic sealings -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Moulding -> B29C
[F16J 15/3284] characterised by their structure -- F16J 15/3284 designates sealing arrangements for pistons that are defined primarily by their structural features, such as the shape, configuration, or composition of the seal elements themselves. It covers piston seals whose functional attributes are directly linked to specific geometric or material constructions.
[F16J 15/3288] Filamentary structures, e.g. brush seals -- F16J 15/3288 covers sealings for pistons that employ filamentary structures such as brush-type seals. These brush seals use flexible strands or filaments to maintain a low-friction, leak-tight barrier between moving surfaces. The design is used where conventional rubber or metal seals may wear quickly or generate excessive heat.
[F16J 15/3292] Lamellar structures -- F16J 15/3292 relates to lamellar structures employed as sealing elements in pistons, typically comprising layered or plate-like materials that provide a sealing surface, reduce wear and improve resistance to leakage under pressure.
[F16J 15/3296] Arrangements for monitoring the condition or operation of elastic sealings -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sealings between relatively-moving surfaces with elastic sealings comprising two casing or support elements, one attached to each surface with means for detecting or measuring relative rotation of the two elements -> F16J15/326
[F16J 15/34] with slip-ring pressed against a more or less radial face on one member -- F16J 15/34 covers sealing arrangements for pistons in which a slip-ring element is forced against a generally radial surface of one component to provide a seal, typically used to prevent fluid leakage between moving parts of a piston assembly. This design relies on the ring's compression against the face to maintain contact during reciprocating motion.
[F16J 15/3404] and characterised by parts or details relating to lubrication, cooling or venting of the seal -- The subclass F16J 15/3404 covers piston seals that incorporate elements for lubrication, cooling or venting of the seal, such as passages, channels or reservoirs that manage fluid flow to reduce friction, temperature rise or pressure buildup. It is part of the broader group on pistons within mechanical engineering.
[F16J 15/3408] at least one ring having an uneven slipping surface -- The CPC entry F16J 15/3408 falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pistons and sealings, and designates pistons that incorporate at least one sealing ring whose slipping surface is purposely uneven to improve friction control, wear distribution or sealing performance.
[F16J 15/3412] with cavities  F16J15/3424  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sealings with microcavities -> F16J15/3424
[F16J 15/3416] with at least one continuous groove -- This subclass covers piston sealing arrangements that incorporate at least one continuous groove, used to retain lubricant, control pressure differentials, or accommodate thermal expansion, thereby enhancing the sealing effectiveness of the piston.
[F16J 15/342] with means for feeding fluid directly to the face -- This subclass of mechanical engineering covers piston sealing arrangements where fluid is supplied directly to the sealing face, such as through channels or jets that provide lubrication or cooling, helping to reduce wear in hydraulic cylinders and internal-combustion engines.
[F16J 15/3424] with microcavities -- F16J 15/3424 covers piston sealing arrangements that incorporate microcavities in the seal surface. The microcavities are used to store lubricant, accommodate gas, or provide pressure compensation, improving seal performance and wear resistance. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pistons, sealings.
[F16J 15/3428] with a wavy surface -- F16J 15/3428 designates sealing arrangements for pistons that incorporate a wavy surface on the sealing face. The corrugated profile enhances flexibility and contact, improving leak resistance under varying pressures while allowing smoother motion.
[F16J 15/3432] the geometry of the surface being able to vary during operation -- F16J 15/3432 covers pistons whose sealing surface geometry can change while the device is operating, allowing the seal to adapt to pressure, temperature, or wear variations. It includes mechanisms that actively or passively modify the shape or profile of the sealing interface during use to maintain effective sealing performance.
[F16J 15/3436] Pressing means -- Pressing means are components that apply axial force to a piston or sealing arrangement to create or maintain a seal during movement. They are used in mechanical engineering to ensure tight clearance between moving parts, often in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders.
[F16J 15/344] the pressing force being applied by means of an elastic ring supporting the slip-ring -- F16J 15/344 relates to piston sealing arrangements where an elastic ring supports a slip-ring to transmit the pressing force that maintains the seal. The elastic ring provides a resilient, uniform pressure despite wear or thermal changes.
[F16J 15/3444] by magnetic attraction -- A sealing arrangement for pistons that employs magnetic attraction to maintain contact between sealing elements and the moving surface, reducing leakage and wear. The magnetic force holds the seal in place during operation, allowing reliable sealing without mechanical pressure. This technique is used in hydraulic and pneumatic piston applications.
[F16J 15/3448] the pressing force resulting from fluid pressure -- F16J 15/3448 covers the pressing force that originates from fluid pressure in piston and sealing arrangements. It addresses how fluid pressure is converted into a mechanical thrust for applications such as hydraulic presses, actuators and sealing devices within the broader mechanical engineering domain.
[F16J 15/3452] the pressing force resulting from the action of a spring -- F16J 15/3452 covers piston sealing arrangements in which a spring provides the pressing force that urges the seal against the piston or cylinder wall, describing the use of coil, leaf or other springs to generate the axial load that maintains a leak-tight seal under varying operating conditions.
[F16J 15/3456] without external means for pressing the ring against the face, e.g. slip-ring with a resilient lip -- This subgroup deals with sealing devices that rely on the intrinsic elasticity of a ring rather than external actuators to maintain pressure against a mating face, exemplified by slip-rings equipped with a resilient lip that automatically presses the sealing element into place. It falls under F16J 15/3456 in the CPC hierarchy covering pistons and sealings.
[F16J 15/346] the pressing force varying during operation -- F16J 15/346 pertains to piston sealing arrangements where the pressing force applied to the seal varies during the stroke of the piston, typically to accommodate changing pressure or temperature conditions. The classification includes designs that adjust hydraulic or mechanical force in real-time to maintain sealing integrity.
[F16J 15/3464] Mounting of the seal -- F16J 15/3464 deals with the mounting of seals on pistons and related components. It includes designs, fixtures and methods for attaching sealing rings, gaskets or other sealing elements to piston surfaces to provide fluid-tight operation.
[F16J 15/3468] Means for controlling the deformations of the contacting faces -- F16J 15/3468 belongs to the CPC subclass dealing with pistons and their sealings. It covers any device, structure or method that controls the deformation of the contacting faces of a piston seal, such as adjustable rings, spring-loaded elements or fluid-filled diaphragms that keep the seal geometry stable under pressure or temperature changes.
[F16J 15/3472] Means for centering or aligning the contacting faces -- F16J 15/3472 covers devices that center or align the contacting faces of piston sealings to maintain proper alignment and prevent leakage. It includes alignment pins, guide sleeves, and other mechanical features that ensure the seal surfaces meet evenly during piston operation.
[F16J 15/3476] Means for minimising vibrations of the slip-ring -- F16J 15/3476 covers devices or arrangements that reduce or damp vibrations in slip-ring assemblies, such as resilient mounts, damping pads or balanced rotor designs that minimise oscillatory motion and improve signal stability. The classification applies to mechanical engineering solutions for vibration control within the broader context of pistons and sealings.
[F16J 15/348] Pre-assembled seals, e.g. cartridge seals -- Pre-assembled seals, such as cartridge seals, are ready-made sealing units designed for installation in piston assemblies without the need for separate components. They provide a reliable barrier against leakage of fluids or gases, simplify assembly processes, and are commonly employed in hydraulic, pneumatic and internal-combustion engines.
[F16J 15/3484] Tandem seals -- Tandem seals are a pair of sealing elements arranged in series within a piston or similar moving component to provide redundancy and enhanced leak protection, often used in hydraulic, pneumatic or combustion chambers. The design ensures that if one seal fails, the second maintains sealing integrity, improving safety and reliability.
[F16J 15/3488] Split-rings -- F16J 15/3488 describes split-rings, a type of sealing used on pistons in mechanical engineering. These flexible rings can be opened and reassembled around a shaft to provide sealing or retaining functions in piston assemblies.
[F16J 15/3492] with monitoring or measuring means associated with the seal -- F16J 15/3492 refers to pistons whose sealing arrangement includes monitoring or measuring devices that assess seal performance, detect leaks or track wear. The classification applies to mechanical-engineering elements where sensors or instrumentation are integrated with the seal for real-time condition monitoring.
[F16J 15/3496] use of special materials -- The subclass F16J 15/3496 covers the use of special materials-such as polymers, composites, ceramics or alloyed metals-in piston sealing elements to enhance properties like wear resistance, chemical compatibility, temperature tolerance or elasticity. It addresses material selection and application methods that improve the performance and durability of sealings in mechanical engineering.
[F16J 15/36] connected by a diaphragm to the other member -- The F16J 15/36 subclass covers sealing arrangements in pistons where the sealing element is joined to the adjoining component by a flexible diaphragm, allowing relative movement while maintaining a fluid-tight barrier. This design is used to connect the piston to another member without rigid links, improving sealing performance under variable pressures.
[F16J 15/363] the diaphragm or bellow being made of metal -- F16J 15/363 covers metal diaphragms or metal bellows used as sealing elements in piston assemblies. It falls within the mechanical engineering class for piston sealings, specifying that the flexible sealing component is constructed from metal rather than other materials.
[F16J 15/366] and comprising vibration-damping means -- F16J 15/366 covers pistons that incorporate sealing features together with vibration-damping elements. It applies to mechanical engineering designs where the piston's sealing function is combined with mechanisms such as springs, elastomers or dampers that reduce oscillations during operation. This subclass refines broader piston and sealing categories.
[F16J 15/38] sealed by a packing -- A piston sealing arrangement in which a packing material, such as a compressible gasket or O-ring, is placed around the piston rod or shoe to prevent fluid leakage between the moving piston and the cylinder wall. The packing may be of textile, rubber, or metal-filled composition and is compressed by a gland or seal housing to maintain a leak-tight interface during operation. This solution is used in hydraulic, pneumatic and combustion engines.
[F16J 15/40] by means of fluid -- F16J 15/40 designates piston seal arrangements that employ a fluid-such as oil, gas or water-to create or enhance the seal. It covers mechanisms where fluid pressure, lubrication or injection is used to prevent leakage around the piston. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pistons → Sealings.
[F16J 15/403] by changing the state of matter -- F16J 15/403 relates to piston sealings that function by altering the state of matter of a sealing material. The sealing element uses a phase-change process such as melting, solidification or vaporization to fill clearances and maintain pressure in hydraulic, pneumatic or combustion engines.
[F16J 15/406] by at least one pump -- F16J 15/406 covers sealing arrangements that are integrated with or operated by at least one pump in piston-type hydraulic or pneumatic systems. The classification pertains to mechanical engineering elements where the pump provides the sealing function or assists the seal performance. Typical applications include pump-driven oil seals for reciprocating compressors and pump-actuated gas barriers in hydraulic cylinders.
[F16J 15/42] kept in sealing position by centrifugal force -- This class covers piston sealing arrangements that remain engaged by the action of centrifugal force generated during rotation. The force pushes the sealing element outward, keeping it in contact with the cylinder wall without additional springs or pressure. It is applied in rotating pumps, compressors and similar machinery where reliable sealing is needed.
[F16J 15/43] kept in sealing position by magnetic force -- F16J 15/43 covers piston sealing arrangements that are retained in their sealing position by magnetic attraction rather than mechanical devices. The magnetic force holds the sealing element against the cylinder surface to maintain a fluid-tight seal during operation, often used in hydraulic or pneumatic actuators.
[F16J 15/44] Free-space packings -- F16J 15/44 covers free-space packings, which are sealing elements placed in an unrestricted gap rather than a confined cavity. These packings are used on pistons or similar moving parts to prevent leakage by providing a compressible barrier that adapts to pressure and movement, often employing materials such as rings, fibers or filler compounds.
[F16J 15/441] with floating ring -- The F16J 15/441 subclass covers piston sealings that incorporate a floating ring, a loosely fitting annular element that can move independently of the piston to maintain a seal under varying pressures and clearances. This arrangement improves sealing performance by allowing the ring to adapt to surface irregularities while remaining secured within the piston assembly.
[F16J 15/442] segmented -- F16J 15/442 is a CPC subclass that addresses segmented sealings used on pistons, comprising sealing elements divided into separate sections to accommodate thermal expansion, machining tolerances or variable pressure zones. It lies within the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pistons → Sealings.
[F16J 15/443] provided with discharge channels -- This subclass classifies pistons that incorporate one or more discharge channels to vent fluid or gas from the cylinder or working chamber, enhancing sealing performance and pressure control. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pistons, sealings.
[F16J 15/444] with facing materials having honeycomb-like structure -- F16J 15/444 covers piston sealing arrangements that use facing materials formed with a honeycomb-like cellular structure, improving sealing performance, reducing wear and providing lightweight support. These designs are employed in mechanical engineering to enhance durability and thermal management of pistons.
[F16J 15/445] with means for adjusting the clearance -- A piston sealing arrangement that incorporates a mechanism to vary the clearance between the piston and its housing, allowing precise control of leakage and wear. This adjustment feature is designed to maintain optimal sealing performance under changing operating conditions.
[F16J 15/447] Labyrinth packings -- Labyrinth packings are non-contact sealing devices that use a series of interlocking grooves, chambers or ridges to create a tortuous path, limiting fluid leakage while allowing relative motion of pistons. They rely on precise clearances and high-speed flow turbulence rather than a traditional sealing lip, offering wear-resistant, low-maintenance protection in hydraulic, pneumatic and rotating-shaft applications.
[F16J 15/4472] with axial path -- This CPC subclass covers pistons that incorporate an axial fluid flow path, allowing hydraulic or pneumatic fluid to travel straight through the piston along its axis. Such designs simplify sealing and improve efficiency in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders.
[F16J 15/4474] Pre-assembled packings -- Pre-assembled packings are ready-made sealing units designed for insertion into pistons or similar chambers to prevent fluid or gas escape. They are typically composed of a combination of compressible and non-compressible materials such as metal rings, graphite or polymer sheets, and are supplied as a complete, installable component.
[F16J 15/4476] with radial path -- F16J 15/4476 relates to piston seal arrangements in which the sealing element follows a radial path, moving outward or inward relative to the piston to maintain a fluid-tight connection. The subclass includes radial sliding and expanding seals used in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders to control leakage and pressure.
[F16J 15/4478] Pre-assembled packings -- F16J 15/4478 covers pre-assembled packings, which are ready-made sealing units placed around pistons or other moving parts to prevent leakage of fluids or gases. These packings are typically made from elastomeric, metallic or composite materials and are designed for quick installation and reliable sealing performance.
[F16J 15/453] characterised by the use of particular materials -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sealings with facing materials having honeycomb-like structure -> F16J15/444
[F16J 15/46] with packing ring expanded or pressed into place by fluid pressure, e.g. inflatable packings -- The code covers sealing devices in which a packing ring is positioned and then expanded or forced into place by fluid pressure, such as inflatable packings used around pistons or other moving members to create a fluid-tight seal.
[F16J 15/48] influenced by the pressure within the member to be sealed -- F16J 15/48 covers sealing devices whose operation or design is affected by the pressure inside the component they seal, such as pistons, cylinders or hydraulic members, where the internal pressure influences the sealing shape, material behavior or actuation. These seals rely on pressure-driven deformation or engagement to maintain a leak-tight barrier.
[F16J 15/50] between relatively-movable members, by means of a sealing without relatively-moving surfaces, e.g. fluid-tight sealings for transmitting motion through a wall -- This subclass covers sealing arrangements that allow motion to be transmitted between relatively-movable parts while the seal itself has no moving surfaces, such as fluid-tight seals that pass motion through a wall, often used in piston and hydraulic applications.
[F16J 15/52] by means of sealing bellows or diaphragms -- F16J 15/52 refers to piston sealing arrangements that employ flexible bellows or diaphragms to maintain a fluid-tight barrier while allowing linear motion. This technique is used in hydraulic and pneumatic cylinders where a compliant element provides a reliable seal despite pressure variations and piston travel.
[F16J 15/525] fixed to a part of a transmission performing a wobbling or a circular translatory movement -- F16J 15/525 covers sealing arrangements that are mounted on a transmission component that moves in a wobbling or circular translatory manner, such as pins, cams or wobble plates. The seal is secured to the moving part to prevent leakage while accommodating the specific oscillating or rotary-reciprocating motion.
[F16J 15/54] Other sealings for rotating shafts -- F16J 15/54 classifies sealings used on rotating shafts that do not fall under more specific seal types. It covers miscellaneous shaft sealing solutions such as gaskets, packing, magnetic or electro-static seals, and other unconventional methods employed to prevent leakage along the shaft's surface. These designs are used where standard lip, labyrinth or mechanical seals are unsuitable.
[F16J 15/545] submitted to unbalanced pressure in circumference; seals for oscillating actuator -- This subclass covers sealing arrangements for pistons or other components that experience uneven circumferential pressure, particularly in devices where the piston moves back and forth. It includes designs that maintain leakage control under oscillating motion and varying pressure distribution around the seal.
[F16J 15/56] Other sealings for reciprocating rods -- F16J 15/56 covers sealings used on reciprocating rods that are not covered by more specific subclasses, such as specialized packing, labyrinth, or lip-type seals. These sealings protect against fluid escape and contamination in pistons and other mechanical engineering elements where rod motion is linear.
[F16J 2015/0837] with an edge portion folded over a second plate or shim -- This subgroup covers gears whose tooth edge is formed by folding over a secondary plate or shim to create a reinforced portion, improving load capacity and reducing wear. It applies to manufacturing methods where the edge portion is bent onto a supporting element.
[F16J 2015/0843] with an edge portion folded over the plate itself -- The entry refers to plates or sheet members whose edge is folded back onto the plate itself to create a reinforced rim, which is used for securing, mounting or joining the part to other components. This folding provides additional strength and a convenient attachment feature.
[F16J 2015/085] without fold over -- F16J 2015/085 refers to fastening or clamping arrangements that secure a workpiece without using a fold-over element; the clamp applies pressure directly to the parts to be held, typically via a screw, lever or spring mechanism, rather than a movable tongue or flap that folds over the material.
[F16J 2015/0856] with a non-metallic coating or strip -- This CPC subgroup covers fluid-handling devices such as pumps or compressors that incorporate a non-metallic coating or a strip of non-metallic material on one or more functional parts. The coating or strip is applied to components like rotors, impellers or housings to improve wear resistance, corrosion protection or sealing performance, and is classified under the broader F16J class for fluid-equipment.
[F16J 2015/0862] with a bore ring -- F16J 2015/0862 covers hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders and related pipe or chamber assemblies that incorporate a bore ring, a ring-shaped element positioned within the bore to define, reinforce or seal the internal passage. The subclass includes designs where the bore ring improves alignment, wear resistance or fluid sealing.
[F16J 2015/0868] Aspects not related to the edges of the gasket -- F16J 2015/0868 covers gasket features that do not involve the gasket's peripheral edges, including the central sealing surface, material composition, surface texture, stiffness, and any internal or face-related modifications that affect sealing performance without altering the edge geometry.
[F16J 2015/0875] comprising welds -- F16J 2015/0875 refers to inventions in the field of mechanical engineering that incorporate welds as a defining feature of the structure or component. It covers devices, assemblies, or methods where the presence of welded joints is essential to the function or construction, such as welded frames, brackets, or pipe connections. The classification is used to index patents that specifically claim or rely on welding in their design.
[F16J 3/00] Diaphragms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Diaphragm, bellows and bellow pistons not specific to a particular use
[F16J 3/02] Diaphragms -- F16J 3/02 designates diaphragms that function as flexible piston elements in mechanical systems, especially for transmitting fluid pressure in hydraulic, pneumatic or pump arrangements. The classification covers structures where a membrane replaces or supplements a solid piston to convert pressure into linear motion.
[F16J 3/04] Bellows -- F16J 3/04 relates to bellows, flexible accordion-type components that accommodate axial movement while providing a fluid-tight seal in piston and diaphragm assemblies. They are commonly made of metal or elastomeric material and allow elastic deformation to compensate for pressure or dimensional changes.
[F16J 3/041] Non-metallic bellows -- F16J 3/041 covers bellows made from polymers, composites, ceramics or other non-metallic materials that provide flexible sealing or volume changes in pistons and diaphragms. These elastic devices replace metal bellows where corrosion resistance, lightweight, or cost are critical, and are used in fluid control, pressure compensation and vibration isolation.
[F16J 3/042] Fastening details -- F16J 3/042 relates to the fastening details of diaphragms used in piston assemblies. It covers the various means such as bolts, clips, welding, adhesives or sealing rings employed to secure the diaphragm to the piston housing or related components.
[F16J 3/043] with particular means for limiting wear -- F16J 3/043 covers piston diaphragms that incorporate specific features to limit wear, such as protective coatings, surface texturing, or the use of lubricating films or sacrificial layers that reduce friction and extend component life. It is a sub-category of mechanical engineering elements dealing with pistons, focusing on wear-reduction measures for diaphragm-type pistons.
[F16J 3/045] Split bellows -- F16J 3/045 designates split bellows, a type of flexible diaphragm consisting of two or more adjacent chambers joined at the ends, used in pistons and other mechanical elements to accommodate movement while maintaining a seal. They are employed in pumps, compressors and hydraulic cylinders to allow axial or radial displacement.
[F16J 3/046] Lubrication or venting arrangements -- The F16J 3/046 subclass covers devices that provide lubrication or venting for pistons and diaphragms in mechanical engineering. It includes systems that supply oil, grease or other lubricants to moving surfaces, as well as arrangements that allow gases or fluids to be expelled to prevent pressure buildup, ensuring smooth operation and reduced wear.
[F16J 3/047] Metallic bellows -- Metallic bellows are flexible, metal-walled diaphragms that expand and contract axially to accommodate movement, pressure changes, or thermal expansion in pumps, actuators and other mechanical systems. They are fabricated from thin sheets of corrosion-resistant alloys and may be formed by rolling, welding or hydroforming. The design provides a sealed, elastic element for precise displacement or compensation in engineering applications.
[F16J 3/048] with guiding or supporting means -- The class covers pistons or diaphragms that include guiding or supporting means, such as rods, guides, bearings or support frames, which keep the moving element aligned and stable during operation. It is a sub-category of engineering elements and units within mechanical engineering dealing with piston-type devices.
[F16J 3/06] Bellows pistons -- Bellows pistons are flexible, accordion-like devices that act as moving pistons in hydraulic or pneumatic systems, providing a sealed, expandable chamber that accommodates variable pressures and volumes. They are employed in pumps, compressors and other mechanical assemblies where a compliant displacement element is needed.
[F16J 7/00] Piston-rods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Piston-rods not specific to a particular use
[F16J 9/00] Piston-rings , seats therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Piston-rings or piston-ring expanders metallic or non-metallic; Seats therefore  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Ring sealing of similar construction in general also when used between pistons and cylinders -> F16J3/06 , F16J15/16
[F16J 9/02] L-section rings -- F16J 9/02 designates L-section rings employed as sealing or supporting elements in piston assemblies. These L-shaped cross-section rings are fitted into piston-ring seats to provide improved sealing, load distribution and wear resistance in internal combustion or hydraulic engines.
[F16J 9/04] Helical rings -- F16J 9/04 covers helical piston rings, which are spiral-shaped sealing elements placed in cylinder bores to maintain compression and control oil consumption. Their helical geometry allows flexible expansion and better conformity to cylinder walls, improving sealing performance in pistons and similar devices.
[F16J 9/06] using separate springs expanding the rings -- F16J 9/06 covers piston-ring expansion mechanisms that employ separate, individual springs to push the rings outward. The spring-driven design provides controlled ring pressure for improved sealing and wear compensation in pistons, commonly used in internal-combustion engines.
[F16J 9/061] using metallic coiled or blade springs  F16J9/145  takes precedence -- This subdivision covers piston-ring seats that employ metallic coiled or blade springs to provide resilience and maintain sealing pressure. The springs are used to keep the ring in constant contact with the cylinder wall, improving wear resistance and performance.
[F16J 9/062] Coiled spring along the entire circumference -- F16J 9/062 covers piston assemblies in which a continuous coiled spring is placed around the full perimeter of the piston-ring seat, providing uniform radial force to maintain seal integrity during operation. The spring encircles the entire circumference, improving wear distribution and sealing performance in mechanical engines.
[F16J 9/063] Strip or wire along the entire circumference -- A piston-ring design in which a single continuous strip or wire forms the ring around the entire circumference of the piston, providing a uniform sealing surface. This construction eliminates separate segment gaps and is used to improve gas-tightness and wear resistance in internal-combustion engines.
[F16J 9/064] Rings with a flat annular side rail -- Rings with a flat annular side rail are piston-ring components that incorporate a straight, ring-shaped protrusion along their outer edge to improve sealing and stability within a cylinder bore. The flat side rail provides a consistent contact surface and helps distribute pressure evenly during engine operation.
[F16J 9/065] Spring expander with massive cross-section -- A spring expander with a massive cross-section is a robust tool that generates a large radial force to expand or seat piston rings and their seats. Its oversized, solid spring design ensures uniform pressure and prevents deformation of delicate ring surfaces during installation or removal.
[F16J 9/066] Spring expander from sheet metal -- A spring expander made from sheet metal is a component that provides radial force to enlarge or support piston-ring seats in engines. It is formed by shaping a metal sheet into a spring-like structure that expands when installed, ensuring a tight seal and proper alignment of the piston rings.
[F16J 9/067] corrugated in the radial direction -- This subgroup covers piston rings or their seats that have radial corrugations-wavy or ribbed profiles extending outward from the cylinder axis-to improve sealing performance, flexibility and wear resistance.
[F16J 9/068] corrugated in the axial direction -- The entry covers piston-ring designs in which the ring wall is formed into axial corrugations-ridges running parallel to the piston's axis-to give the ring flexibility, enhance sealing and accommodate thermal expansion, typically for internal-combustion engines and similar mechanical applications.
[F16J 9/069] with a "C"-shaped cross section along the entire circumference -- The F16J 9/069 subclass covers piston-ring or seat designs whose entire circumference has a C-shaped cross-section, providing a specific profile that improves sealing and wear characteristics in mechanical engineering applications. This detail refines the broader category of pistons and their auxiliary components within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[F16J 9/08] with expansion obtained by pressure of the medium -- The subclass F16J 9/08 covers piston-ring seats that achieve expansion by using the pressure of the working medium itself. In these designs the fluid or gas acting on the piston forces the sealing element outward to maintain a tight seal, eliminating the need for external springs or mechanical actuators.
[F16J 9/10] Special members for adjusting the rings -- The F16J 9/10 class deals with special components used to adjust piston rings or their seats, such as adjustable or replaceable ring-guides, shims, or other mechanisms that fine-tune ring clearance and positioning within a piston assembly.
[F16J 9/12] Details -- F16J 9/12 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers specific details and features of piston rings and their seats, including design aspects that enhance functionality or durability. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office to organize patent applications related to mechanical engineering elements.
[F16J 9/14] Joint-closures -- F16J 9/14 covers joint-closures, which are combined sealing and fastening devices used to close the clearance between piston rings and their seats, ensuring a leak-tight connection while permitting the required movement of the piston assembly.
[F16J 9/145] of spring expanders -- F16J 9/145 covers spring expanders that are used to radially push piston-ring seats outward in hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders, improving sealing and wear resistance. The sub-class includes devices employing coil or helical springs to expand or retain the ring, and forms part of the broader group on piston-rings and their seating mechanisms.
[F16J 9/16] obtained by stacking of rings -- F16J 9/16 refers to piston-ring designs that are produced by stacking individual rings to create the sealing and seating elements for pistons, typically used in internal-combustion engines. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically piston components, and addresses manufacturing methods that achieve the final ring configuration through a layered approach.
[F16J 9/18] with separate bridge-elements -- F16J 9/18 covers piston-ring seats that incorporate separate bridge elements, meaning the bridge that supports the ring is a distinct component rather than integral with the cylinder wall. This design is used to improve sealing and wear characteristics in internal-combustion engines.
[F16J 9/20] Rings with special cross-section -- F16J 9/20 covers piston rings that have a special cross-section shape, designed to improve sealing, wear resistance, or lubrication performance in internal-combustion engines. These rings may incorporate grooves, profiles, or other geometric features that differ from standard round or rectangular sections.
[F16J 9/203] Oil-scraping rings -- The CPC class F16J 9/203 relates to oil-scraping rings fitted on pistons to remove excess oil from cylinder walls, thereby controlling lubrication, reducing oil consumption and limiting smoke. These rings are designed with specific profiles or surface treatments to efficiently scrape oil away during piston movement.
[F16J 9/206] One-piece oil-scraping rings -- The CPC subclass F16J 9/206 relates to one-piece oil-scraping rings used on pistons to remove excess oil from cylinder walls while providing sealing. These rings are formed as a single component, usually metal, and fit into piston grooves to control lubrication, reduce blow-by and improve engine efficiency.
[F16J 9/22] Rings for preventing wear of grooves or like seatings -- F16J 9/22 covers protective rings that are fitted to piston-ring assemblies to prevent wear of the grooves or seatings that retain the rings. These wear-resistant rings reduce abrasion and extend the service life of the sealing surfaces.
[F16J 9/24] Members preventing rotation of rings in grooves -- F16J 9/24 covers devices that keep piston rings from rotating within their circumferential grooves, such as pins, stops or other members that engage the ring or groove to maintain proper orientation and prevent slippage.
[F16J 9/26] characterised by the use of particular materials -- F16J 9/26 concerns piston-ring or piston-seat designs that are distinguished by the specific materials from which they are made, such as particular alloys, ceramics, polymers or composite substances, chosen to provide desired sealing, wear-resistance or lubrication properties.
[F16J 9/28] of non-metals -- F16J 9/28 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the European and United States patent offices, specifies piston rings or their seats made from non-metallic materials. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with mechanical engineering elements and units, specifically focusing on pistons and related components.
[F16K] VALVES -- F16K covers valves used to control the flow of liquids, gases or slurries in mechanical systems. It includes designs such as gate, ball, check and pressure-relief valves and relates to their construction, operation and sealing mechanisms. The class is part of the mechanical engineering section focusing on engineering elements and units.

[F16K 1/06] Special arrangements for improving the flow, e.g. special shape of passages or casings -- F16K 1/06 refers to valve designs that incorporate special geometries of passages or casings to improve fluid flow, such as streamlined channels, flow-optimising contours, or uniquely shaped housings.
[F16K 1/08] in which the spindle is perpendicular to the general direction of flow -- This subclass covers valve designs in which the spindle or actuating stem is positioned perpendicular to the main direction of fluid flow, such as lift or gate valves where the moving component crosses the flow channel to open or close it. It pertains to constructional types where the spindle motion intersects rather than runs parallel to the flow path.
[F16K 1/10] in which the spindle is inclined to the general direction of flow -- This subclass covers valves whose operating spindle is positioned at an angle to the main flow direction rather than being parallel, a design that can aid sealing, reduce flow resistance or simplify actuation in rotary or sliding-gate types. The inclined spindle aligns the motion with the fluid path to improve performance or accommodate specific installation constraints.

[F16K 11/0743] with both the supply and the discharge passages being on one side of the closure plates -- A multiple-way valve in which the supply and discharge passages are arranged on the same side of the valve's closure plates, allowing fluid to enter and exit from a single side of the valve body. This configuration simplifies piping, reduces space requirements and can improve sealing performance in compact hydraulic or pneumatic systems.

[F16K 11/0785] the movable closure member being pivotally supported at one point and being linked to the operating lever at only one other point -- The classification code F16K 11/0785 pertains to multiple-way valves, specifically those with a movable closure member that is pivotally supported at one point and connected to the operating lever at another single point. [F16K 11/0787] with both the supply and the discharge passages being on the same side of the closure members  F16K11/0785  takes precedence -- The subclass F16K 11/0787 in the Cooperative Patent Classification concerns multiple-way mixing valves whose supply and discharge passages are positioned on the same side of the closure members. It refines the broader group of multiple-way valves and is subordinate to F16K 11/0785, which defines the preceding arrangement.
[F16K 11/08] comprising only taps or cocks -- F16K 11/08 covers multiple-way mixing valves that are built only from taps or cocks, meaning the valve's flow-control elements are simple on-off or throttling devices rather than complex plug or rotary components. It classifies arrangements where these taps or cocks alone provide the required diversion or mixing of fluid streams.
[F16K 11/083] with tapered plug -- The F16K 11/083 subclass covers multiple-way mixing valves that use a tapered plug to regulate and direct fluid flow. The tapered plug provides progressive sealing and flow control, often employed in hydraulic or pneumatic systems to achieve precise mixing or distribution of streams.
[F16K 11/0833] having all the connecting conduits situated in a single plane perpendicular to the axis of the plug -- A sub-group of multiple-way (mixing) valves in which all inlet and outlet conduits are arranged in a single flat plane that is perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the valve plug, providing a planar layout for the connecting passages.
[F16K 11/0836] having all the connecting conduits situated in more than one plane perpendicular to the axis of the plug -- This subclass defines multiple-way mixing valves in which all connecting conduits are arranged in more than one plane that is perpendicular to the plug's longitudinal axis, allowing flow paths to intersect from different directions.
[F16K 11/085] with cylindrical plug -- This subclass covers multiple-way mixing valves that use a cylindrical plug as the moving element to direct fluid among several inlet and outlet ports. The plug slides, rotates or lifts within a cylindrical housing to open or close flow passages, allowing mixing or diversion of streams.
[F16K 11/0853] having all the connecting conduits situated in a single plane perpendicular to the axis of the plug -- This subclass covers multi-way mixing valves where the inlet and outlet passages are arranged in one flat plane orthogonal to the valve plug's axis, allowing the conduits to lie in the same geometric plane. This configuration simplifies the valve construction and fluid routing.
[F16K 11/0856] having all the connecting conduits situated in more than one plane perpendicular to the axis of the plug -- This subgroup covers multi-way mixing valves whose inlet and outlet channels are arranged in more than one plane that is perpendicular to the plug axis, meaning the connecting conduits are not co-planar but extend in separate orthogonal planes to allow complex flow routing.
[F16K 11/087] with spherical plug -- F16K 11/087 covers multiple-way mixing valves that use a spherical plug as the flow-controlling element. The spherical plug rotates or pivots to connect different ports, allowing compact, low-friction switching of fluid streams.
[F16K 11/0873] the plug being only rotatable around one spindle -- F16K 11/0873 covers multiple-way mixing valves whose internal plug rotates about a single spindle axis rather than multiple axes, allowing the flow paths to be changed by turning the plug on one shaft. This design is used to direct fluids between several ports with a compact, single-actuator mechanism.
[F16K 11/0876] one connecting conduit having the same axis as the spindle -- This subclass covers multiple-way mixing valves where the inlet or outlet conduit that links the valve chambers is arranged coaxially with the valve spindle. The conduit runs along the same axis as the spindle, allowing fluid to be introduced or withdrawn without offset. It is used where compact alignment and balanced flow are required.
[F16K 11/10] with two or more closure members not moving as a unit -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Multiple-way valves with either a common acutating means or separate actuating means for every valve member.
[F16K 11/105] Three-way check or safety valves with two or more closure members -- A three-way check or safety valve with two or more closure members is a multiple-way valve that controls flow among three ports, using at least two movable sealing elements to allow flow in one direction while preventing backflow or providing a safety shut-off. It is used in hydraulic, pneumatic and fluid-handling systems to protect equipment and regulate fluid paths.
[F16K 11/12] with one plug turning in another -- A multiple-way valve in which a rotating plug is housed inside another plug, allowing the inner plug to turn relative to the outer one to open, close, or mix fluid passages. This arrangement provides a compact, interchangeable flow-control mechanism often used in mixing or diverting applications.
[F16K 11/14] operated by one actuating member, e.g. a handle -- F16K 11/14 designates multiple-way valves that are operated by a single actuating member such as a handle, allowing selection among several flow paths. The classification covers mixing or diverting valves where one lever or knob controls the positioning of the valve elements.
[F16K 11/16] which only slides, or only turns, or only swings in one plane -- F16K 11/16 covers multiple-way mixing valves whose operative part moves only in a single plane, such as a sliding, rotating or swinging element that does not combine motions. These designs are used where a simple one-axis movement controls fluid direction.
[F16K 11/161] only slides -- This class covers multi-way mixing valves that are operated solely by sliding components. The slides move linearly to open or close flow passages, providing selection or blending of fluid streams without rotating or other types of moving parts. The definition is limited to valves in which the only actuating element is a slide.
[F16K 11/163] only turns -- This CPC subgroup (F16K 11/163) pertains to multiple-way or mixing valves whose function is achieved solely by rotating a valve element. The rotary motion redirects fluid among several ports without any linear movement of the sealing component, enabling compact and reliable flow control in mechanical-fluid systems.
[F16K 11/165] with the rotating spindles parallel to the closure members -- F16K 11/165 pertains to multiple-way valves, such as mixing valves, in which the rotating spindles that control flow are arranged parallel to the closure members, allowing coordinated opening and closing of several ports.
[F16K 11/166] with the rotating spindles at right angles to the closure members -- F16K 11/166 covers multiple-way mixing valves in which the driving spindles are arranged perpendicular to the valve closure members. The right-angle spindle orientation allows rotational motion to open, close, or modulate several flow paths simultaneously, facilitating precise mixing or distribution of fluids.
[F16K 11/168] only swings -- F16K 11/168 designates multiple-way mixing valves whose moving part swings only, meaning the valve element pivots about a fixed axis to direct fluid between ports. The classification applies to devices where the swing motion is the sole means of actuating the flow paths.
[F16K 11/18] with separate operating movements for separate closure members -- F16K 11/18 covers multiple-way valves, such as mixing valves, that use distinct operating movements for each separate closure member. Each port or flow path is controlled by its own actuator, allowing independent opening and closing within the same valve assembly.
[F16K 11/185] with swinging shafts -- F16K 11/185 covers multiple-way mixing valves that incorporate swinging shafts to control flow, allowing the shaft to rotate to connect different ports. [F16K 1/2035] the axis of rotation having only one bearing -- This subgroup covers valve designs where the rotating shaft is supported by a single bearing, limiting support points and affecting load distribution. It includes mechanisms where the axis of rotation is mounted on one bearing, typical for certain rotary valves or throttling devices.
[F16K 1/2042] Special features or arrangements of the sealing -- F16K 1/2042 relates to valves that incorporate special sealing features or arrangements, including additional sealing elements, variable-geometry seals or integrated sealing mechanisms intended to enhance leak-tightness, durability, or functional performance.
[F16K 1/205] the sealing being arranged on the valve member -- F16K 1/205 covers valves in which the sealing element is mounted directly on the moving or stationary valve member, such as a plug, disc, or stem, rather than being a separate external component. The arrangement includes integrated gaskets, O-rings, or other seal profiles that are part of the valve body to provide leak-tight closure.
[F16K 1/2057] the sealing being arranged on the valve seat -- F16K 1/2057 relates to valve designs in which the sealing component is mounted on the valve seat itself, forming a sealing arrangement that directly contacts the seat to prevent flow when closed. This subclass falls under constructional types of valves within mechanical engineering.
[F16K 1/2064] with a channel- or U-shaped seal covering a central body portion -- The F16K 1/2064 subclass covers valves whose sealing element is formed as a channel- or U-shaped seal that envelops the central portion of the valve body, providing enclosure and containment of the flow path within the valve structure.
[F16K 1/2071] and being forced into sealing contact with the valve member by a spring or a spring-like member -- F16K 1/2071 pertains to valves where the sealing is achieved through a spring or spring-like mechanism that forces the seal against the valve member, ensuring tight closure. This classification is part of the broader category of constructional types within the valves section of mechanical engineering.
[F16K 1/2078] Sealing means for the axis of rotation -- The code F16K 1/2078 covers sealing devices that protect the rotating shaft of a valve from leakage. It includes mechanical seals, packing arrangements and other means fitted around the axis to retain fluid while allowing rotation, typically used in pumps, compressors and similar rotating-shaft valve assemblies.
[F16K 1/2085] Movable sealing bodies -- F16K 1/2085 pertains to valves that incorporate movable sealing bodies, i.e., components that can slide, rotate or be withdrawn to open or close a passage. It includes designs such as gate, globe, plug and ball valves where the seal element moves relative to the valve seat to control flow.
[F16K 1/2092] the movement being caused by the flowing medium -- F16K 1/2092 covers valves that are actuated directly by the motion of the fluid flowing through them, including self-acting or pressure-balanced designs where the moving medium causes opening, closing or throttling without external power.
[F16K 1/22] with axis of rotation crossing the valve member, e.g. butterfly valves -- This subclass covers valves whose rotating shaft passes through the valve member itself, as exemplified by butterfly valves. The design lets the disc swing about a central axis intersecting the flow passage, providing a compact, quick-acting shutoff.
[F16K 1/221] specially adapted operating means therefor  operating means  per se   F16K31/00 -- F16K 1/221 classifies valves whose operating mechanisms are specially adapted, including dedicated actuators, gear arrangements, linkage systems or other devices that directly control valve movement. It covers designs where the operating means themselves are integral to the valve's function, rather than generic or standard actuation components. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, valves, constructional types.
[F16K 1/222] Shaping of the valve member -- F16K 1/222 concerns the manufacturing processes used to form the movable part of a valve, such as machining, forging, casting and other techniques that give the valve member its final shape.
[F16K 1/223] with a plurality of valve members -- F16K 1/223 belongs to the mechanical-engineering class for valves and designates constructions that incorporate a plurality of valve members, such as multiple discs, gates or seats operating together or in sequence. The subgroup covers arrangements where two or more moving parts provide the sealing or flow-control function within a single valve unit.
[F16K 1/224] Details of bearings for the axis of rotation -- F16K 1/224 covers the specific design features of bearings that support the rotating shaft in valve assemblies. It includes the arrangement, material selection, lubrication provisions and dimensional details that ensure smooth axial and radial movement while maintaining seal integrity. This sub-class is used to classify inventions dealing with bearing configurations that directly affect the performance and reliability of valve actuation mechanisms.
[F16K 1/225] the axis of rotation having only one bearing -- This CPC subclass refers to valves whose rotating shaft is supported by a single bearing. It covers designs where the axis of rotation relies on one bearing for stability and positioning, distinguishing them from multi-bearing or bearing-free configurations. The classification is used for mechanical engineering valve construction types.
[F16K 1/226] Shaping or arrangements of the sealing -- F16K 1/226 covers the design features that define how sealing elements in valves are shaped and positioned. It includes the geometry of seal faces, the arrangement of lips, beads or ribs, and any configurations that affect how the seal mates with the valve body to prevent leakage. This subclass focuses on the structural aspects of seal shaping rather than the material of the seal.
[F16K 1/2261] the sealing being arranged on the valve member -- F16K 1/2261 designates valves whose sealing component is mounted directly on the valve member itself, such as a disc, plug or piston, so the seal moves with the operating part. It covers constructional arrangements where the seal is integral to or attached on the moving element of the valve.
[F16K 1/2263] the sealing being arranged on the valve seat -- F16K 1/2263 designates valves whose sealing element is placed on the valve seat itself, so that shut-off is achieved by a seal that is integral with or directly contacts the seat surface. This constructional type is used in fluid power or flow-control systems to improve sealing reliability and reduce wear.
[F16K 1/2265] with a channel- or U-shaped seal covering a central body portion -- A valve subclass in which the sealing component is formed as a channel- or U-shaped element that surrounds and covers the central portion of the valve body, sealing around the flow passage. This design is used to provide a reliable seal in various mechanical engineering valve applications.
[F16K 1/2266] and being forced into sealing contact with the valve member by a spring or a spring-like member -- This subclass deals with valves whose sealing element is urged into contact with the valve member by a spring or spring-like component, ensuring a reliable seal when the valve is closed. It applies to constructional types where the spring provides the sealing force rather than external pressure or fluid forces.
[F16K 1/2268] Sealing means for the axis of rotation -- F16K 1/2268 covers sealing devices that prevent leakage along a rotating shaft in valves or other rotating equipment. It includes mechanical face seals, lip seals, labyrinth seals and similar means mounted on the axis of rotation to retain fluids while allowing motion. These solutions are employed in pumps, compressors and similar mechanical systems.

[F16K 1/26] Shape or arrangement of the sealing -- F16K 1/26 is a subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification for valves that deals specifically with the shape or arrangement of sealing elements. It covers designs of sealing surfaces, seats, gaskets and sealing lips, including their geometry, placement and interaction, intended to improve leak tightness and flow control in mechanical valve assemblies.
[F16K 1/28] Movable sealing bodies -- F16K 1/28 classifies movable sealing bodies used in valves, encompassing components such as plugs, disks, gates or other elements that shift to open or close a flow path. These parts are designed to create a seal against a seating surface and are integral to the constructional types of mechanical-engineering valve assemblies.

[F16K 13/00] Other constructional types of cut-off apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Valve arrangements which do not belong per se to any of the previous constructional classes or having a function which cannot be classified under the existing functional classes.
[F16K 1/301] only shut-off valves, i.e. valves without additional means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Valves used for opening/closing the flow path, without other functions (e.g. regulation) or other valves on the same valve body (e.g. safety valves).
[F16K 1/302] with valve member and actuator on the same side of the seat -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16K 13/02] with both sealing faces shaped as small segments of a cylinder and the moving member pivotally mounted -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16K 1/303] with a valve member, e.g. stem or shaft, passing through the seat -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16K 1/304] Shut-off valves with additional means -- **Glossary:** - Additional means: Valves or devices, used for other functional purposes than only opening/closing the valve (e.g. regulation, safety, monitoring).
[F16K 13/04] with a breakable closure member -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16K 1/305] with valve member and actuator on the same side of the seat -- F16K 1/305 covers valves whose moving part and the mechanism that drives it are positioned on the same side of the valve seat. This constructional arrangement is typical for compact designs such as pneumatic or hydraulic actuated shut-off or control valves where space constraints or simplified wiring are important.
[F16K 1/306] with a valve member, e.g. stem or shaft, passing through the seat -- The subgroup covers valves in which a valve member such as a stem or shaft passes through the valve seat, a construction found in certain rotary, sliding or poppet valves where the actuation element traverses the sealing surface.
[F16K 13/06] constructed to be ruptured by an explosion -- F16K 13/06 relates to valves or other shut-off devices that are intentionally designed to rupture when exposed to an explosion or sudden pressure surge, providing rapid isolation of a fluid system. These rupture-type safety devices are used in hydraulic, pneumatic or other pressure-bearing installations to protect equipment from over-pressure damage.
[F16K 1/307] Additional means used in combination with the main valve -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Flow regulation -> G05D  **Glossary:** - Additional means: Valves or devices, used for other functional purposes than only opening/closing the valve (e.g. regulation, safety, monitoring).
[F16K 1/308] Connecting means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of the (permanent) connection of the valve to the container.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Connectors per se (quick connectors with valves) -> F16L37/28
[F16K 13/08] Arrangements for cutting-off -- F16K 13/08 covers the design and layout of devices that interrupt fluid flow, such as shut-off valves, plugs, or other cut-off mechanisms. It addresses the spatial arrangement, mounting and integration of these components within a system, including the positioning of actuators and control elements. The class is used for patents describing how cut-off apparatuses are organized rather than their specific functional details.
[F16K 13/10] by means of liquid or granular medium -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16K 1/32] Details -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details (e.g. shape, materials, function) of valve members, seats, attaching members to spindles, preventing rotation, additional adjustment (with additional means) of the flow and other arrangements of the valve member for modifying the rate of flow (see sub-classes).
[F16K 1/34] Cutting-off parts, e.g. valve members, seats -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16K 1/36] Valve members -- F16K 1/36 relates to the individual components that make up a valve, such as bodies, stems, seats, plugs, disks, guides or other elements that together form a valve assembly. The subclass covers these parts regardless of the overall valve type, focusing on their construction and function within fluid-control mechanisms.
[F16K 1/38] of conical shape -- This subclass covers valves whose principal geometry is conical, meaning the body, seat, plug or other functional parts are shaped like a cone. It includes designs where the conical form is used to control flow, seal or direct fluids within a valve assembly.
[F16K 1/385] contacting in the closed position, over a substantial axial length, a seat surface having the same inclination -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. "needle" valves.
[F16K 1/40] of helical shape -- F16K 1/40 covers valves whose construction incorporates a helical shape, including helical valve bodies, springs or winding flow passages used to regulate fluid flow. It belongs to the mechanical engineering branch, under engineering elements and units, valves, constructional types, and is used in CPC listings for patents describing such helical-shaped valve designs.
[F16K 1/42] Valve seats -- F16K 1/42 covers valve seats, the surfaces or inserts within a valve that engage the moving closure element to create a seal when the valve is closed. They may be integral to the valve body or separate replaceable members, and are designed from metal, ceramic or elastomeric materials to withstand pressure, temperature and wear.
[F16K 1/422] attachable by a threaded connection to the housing -- F16K 1/422 designates valves that are mounted to their housing by means of a threaded connection. The classification covers any valve or valve element whose attachment to the surrounding structure uses screw threads, enabling secure fastening and easy removal or replacement. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, valves, constructional types.
[F16K 1/425] Attachment of the seat to the housing by plastical deformation, e.g. valve seat or housing being plastically deformed during mounting -- The class relates to mounting a valve seat to its housing by plastically deforming either the seat or the housing during assembly, creating an interference fit that secures the components without extra fasteners. Techniques may include pressing, swaging or other forming actions that permanently shape the part to lock it in place.
[F16K 1/427] Attachment of the seat to the housing by one or more additional fixing elements -- The code F16K 1/427 refers to valve designs in which the seat is attached to the housing using one or more additional fixing elements such as bolts, screws, clamps, welding or adhesive devices, beyond the primary seat-housing connection. This sub-class covers the specific means of securing the seat to improve sealing, stability or serviceability.
[F16K 1/44] Details of seats or valve members of double-seat valves -- F16K 1/44 concerns the design and construction details of the sealing seats and valve members used in double-seat valves. It includes specifications of seat shape, material, adjustment mechanisms and how the two opposing seats are arranged to provide balanced flow control. The code falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, valves, constructional types.

[F16K 1/482] with a collar on the spindle or a groove in the spindle, by which a fixing element is supported, the spindle reaching into the valve member -- F16K 1/482 covers valve designs in which the spindle carries a collar or a groove that supports a fixing element, allowing the spindle to extend into the valve body. The feature provides a secure attachment point for the fixing part while the spindle remains integral to the valve assembly.
[F16K 1/485] with a groove in the spindle -- F16K 1/485 covers valve designs in which the spindle is provided with a circumferential groove, typically to receive a seal, retainer or spring element that enhances sealing or actuation. This constructional type falls under valves within mechanical engineering.
[F16K 1/487] by a fixing element extending in the axial direction of the spindle, e.g. a screw -- F16K 1/487 refers to valves that are secured by a fixing element that runs in the axial direction of the spindle, for example a screw, which holds the valve parts together. [F16K 1/523] for limiting the maximum flow rate, using a stop -- F16K 1/523 refers to valve constructional types that limit the maximum flow rate by means of a stop mechanism. [F16K 1/526] for limiting the maximum flow rate, using a second valve -- A valve arrangement that uses a second valve to restrict the maximum flow rate, typically employed as a flow-limiting or safety device in hydraulic or pneumatic systems. The secondary valve may operate in series or parallel with the primary valve to control flow under specified conditions.

[F16K 17/00] Safety valves -- Groups F16K17/00 - F16K17/42 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group F16K15/03 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
