[F16K 7/068] by bending the hose -- A diaphragm cut-off device that stops flow by bending a flexible hose or similar member, which deforms without being displaced bodily to seal the passage, is classified under F16K 7/068 within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and valves.
[F16K 7/07] by means of fluid pressure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16K 7/075] a rigid body being located within the tubular diaphragm -- F16K 7/075 covers diaphragm cut-off valves where a rigid body is placed inside the tubular diaphragm to seal the passage. The rigid element deforms the diaphragm locally without moving as a whole, providing a stop for fluid flow.
[F16K 7/08] constrictable by twisting -- F16K 7/08 covers diaphragm cut-off valves whose closure is achieved by twisting the diaphragm, causing it to constrict and seal the passage without a bulk movement of the member. This sub-category falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, specifically valves that use a deformed but stationary diaphragm to stop flow.
[F16K 7/10] with inflatable member -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16K 7/12] with flat, dished, or bowl-shaped diaphragm -- F16K 7/12 covers valve arrangements that use a flat, dished or bowl-shaped diaphragm as the closing element. The diaphragm deforms to seal the passage without being displaced as a solid piece, providing a simple, reliable cut-off function in fluid control systems.
[F16K 7/123] the seat being formed on the bottom of the fluid line -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16K 7/126] the seat being formed on a rib perpendicular to the fluid line -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16K 7/14] arranged to be deformed against a flat seat -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16K 7/16] the diaphragm being mechanically actuated, e.g. by screw-spindle or cam -- F16K 7/16 covers diaphragm cut-off devices in which the diaphragm is moved to block a flow path by a mechanical actuation such as a screw-spindle or cam. The classification is used for valves that seal by deforming the diaphragm rather than translating a solid member.
[F16K 7/17] the diaphragm being actuated by fluid pressure -- F16K 7/17 covers valve arrangements where a flexible diaphragm forms the closing element and is deformed by fluid pressure to seal a passage, without the diaphragm moving as a rigid body. The actuation relies on pressure-driven deformation rather than mechanical linkage.
[F16K 7/18] with diaphragm secured at one side only, e.g. to be laid on the seat by rolling action -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16K 7/20] with a compressible solid closure member -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[F16K 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- F16K 99/00 is a catch-all subgroup in the Cooperative Patent Classification for valves, placed under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Valves. It covers any valve-related invention whose technical features do not fit the more specific groups defined in the subclass, allowing such patents to be indexed without forcing an inaccurate fit.
[F16K 99/0001] Microvalves  microdevices  B81B1/00 ; manufacture or treatment of devices or systems in or on a substrate  B81C1/00 ; microfluidic structures  B01L3/5027 ; micropumps  F04B19/006 -- F16K 99/0001 classifies microvalves and related microdevices, encompassing their manufacture or treatment on substrates, microfluidic structures, and micropumps. Positioned within mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → valves, it captures very small valve technologies not covered by other subclasses.
[F16K 99/0003] Constructional types of microvalves; Details of the cutting-off member -- F16K 99/0003 covers the structural designs of microvalves and the specific features of their cutting-off members, encompassing any unconventional or specialized configurations not addressed in other groups of the valve subclass.
[F16K 99/0005] Lift valves -- Lift valves are devices that raise or lower a valve seat or plug in a vertical motion to start or stop fluid flow, commonly used in water, steam or gas systems. They are classified under CPC group F16K 99/0005, covering valve types not otherwise listed in this subclass.
[F16K 99/0007] of cantilever type -- Cantilever-type valves use a flexible, beam-like element fixed at one end that deflects to open or close a flow passage, providing sealing with minimal friction and wear. They are employed in various fluid-control applications where compact, reliable actuation is required and are classified under the residual group for valve designs not covered elsewhere.
[F16K 99/0009] the valve element held by multiple arms -- F16K 99/0009 pertains to valves with a valve element supported by several arms, addressing designs not covered in more specific categories within the subclass. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office.
[F16K 99/0011] Gate valves or sliding valves -- F16K 99/0011 designates gate or sliding valves whose features are not covered by other groups within the valve subclass. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, valves, as a catch-all category for unconventional or miscellaneous gate-type valve designs.
[F16K 99/0013] Rotary valves -- F16K 99/0013 covers rotary valves, which are valve assemblies that control the admission or discharge of fluids by rotating a disc, cylinder or sleeve rather than using a reciprocating motion. They are employed in engines, compressors and other mechanical systems where space or flow-rate requirements favor a rotating sealing element.
[F16K 99/0015] Diaphragm or membrane valves -- A diaphragm or membrane valve employs a flexible diaphragm or membrane as the sealing element that moves to open or close the flow passage, providing tight isolation and precise throttling of liquids or gases while keeping the actuation mechanism separate from the media. Such valves are used where contamination must be avoided or a hermetic seal is required, and they are classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, valves.
[F16K 99/0017] Capillary or surface tension valves, e.g. using electro-wetting or electro-capillarity effects -- F16K 99/0017 relates to valves that control fluid flow by using capillary action or surface tension, frequently adjusted with electro-wetting or electro-capillarity effects. The technology falls under mechanical engineering valves where the operating principle is not otherwise classified.
[F16K 99/0019] Valves using a microdroplet or microbubble as the valve member -- A valve in which the moving element that controls flow is a microdroplet or a microbubble, rather than a solid or mechanical part. The device uses the surface tension and dynamics of the tiny fluid or gas pocket to open, close or throttle the passage.
[F16K 99/0021] No-moving-parts valves -- F16K 99/0021 covers no-moving-parts valves, which regulate fluid flow by fixed geometry rather than mechanical motion. These static flow-control devices are employed where high reliability, low maintenance, or resistance to wear is required.
[F16K 99/0023] with ball-shaped valve members -- F16K 99/0023 refers to valves that employ ball-shaped valve elements to start, stop or regulate fluid flow. The design includes a spherical or near-spherical member that rotates within a seat to open or close the passage. This class is used for valve types not covered by other more specific groups.
[F16K 99/0025] Valves using microporous membranes -- F16K 99/0025 covers valves that control fluid flow by means of microporous membranes, which permit selective passage of gases or liquids while blocking contaminants or larger particles. These valves are used in applications such as breathable barriers, fuel-cell ventilation and filtration-integrated flow control.
[F16K 99/0026] Valves using channel deformation -- This subclass covers valves that control fluid flow by deforming the passage itself rather than using discrete moving elements, such as flexible or elastomeric channels that change shape to open or close. It includes designs where the channel geometry is altered to regulate flow and is used for valve concepts not classified elsewhere in the valves hierarchy.
[F16K 99/0028] Valves having multiple inlets or outlets -- F16K 99/0028 covers valves that have more than one inlet or outlet, allowing flow to be directed through several ports simultaneously. These designs are used where multiple fluid streams need to be controlled by a single valve body, such as in complex hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F16K 99/003] Valves for single use only -- F16K 99/003 covers valves that are intended for single-use operation and are discarded after their service life. These disposable devices are usually made from inexpensive, often polymeric materials and lack features for cleaning, re-assembly or repeated actuation.
[F16K 99/0032] using phase transition or influencing viscosity -- F16K 99/0032 classifies valves that achieve flow control by inducing a phase change in the medium or by altering its viscosity, for example thermally-triggered shut-off devices, fluid-solidifying or gelling mechanisms, and other systems where a change of state or viscosity governs the opening or closing operation.
[F16K 99/0034] Operating means specially adapted for microvalves -- Operating means specially adapted for microvalves comprise miniature actuation devices, control mechanisms or integrated systems designed to open, close or regulate flow through very small-scale valves, providing precise or rapid movement suited to micro-fluidic applications. These arrangements may include piezoelectric, magnetic, thermal or hydraulic actuators, along with associated control electronics, that are tailored to the size and performance requirements of microvalve technology.
[F16K 99/0036] operated by temperature variations -- Valves that are triggered or controlled by temperature changes, employing mechanisms such as bimetallic strips, shape-memory alloys, thermal expansion of a fluid or a sealing element, to open, close or modulate flow without external power. These devices respond directly to the heat or cooling of the surrounding medium.
[F16K 99/0038] using shape memory alloys -- F16K 99/0038 covers valves that incorporate shape-memory alloy components to achieve actuation, sealing or positional change. It includes designs where the alloy's temperature-induced deformation provides the functional movement of the valve without external mechanical drive. The classification applies when the valve's operation relies primarily on the alloy's shape-memory effect.
[F16K 99/004] using radiation -- F16K 99/004 covers valve-related inventions that employ radiation for functions such as actuation, monitoring, cleaning or sterilisation, where the use of radiation is not covered by any other subgroup in the valve class. It groups designs that rely on ionising, ultraviolet, infrared or other radiation types to influence valve performance or operation. This catch-all category ensures that novel radiation-based valve concepts are classified even when they do not fit existing specific groups.
[F16K 99/0042] Electric operating means therefor -- The subgroup F16K 99/0042 covers electric devices used to operate valves when no other more specific CPC groups apply, including electric actuators, motors, solenoids and associated control electronics that provide the power and control needed to open, close or modulate a valve.
[F16K 99/0044] using thermo-electric means -- F16K 99/0044 covers valve arrangements that utilise thermoelectric devices, such as Peltier or Seebeck elements, to generate or manage temperature differentials for controlling valve movement, sealing, or flow regulation. It is applied when the thermoelectric principle is the primary means of actuation and the invention does not fit any more specific valve subgroup.
[F16K 99/0046] using magnets -- F16K 99/0046 covers valves that employ magnetic principles to operate, control, or seal fluid flow. This includes designs where magnets provide actuation, positioning, or locking of valve components, as well as magnetic seals that prevent leakage. The class is used for magnetic valve concepts that do not fit into other more specific F16K subclasses.
[F16K 99/0048] using piezoelectric means -- This group (F16K 99/0048) covers valves whose operation or control is achieved with piezoelectric devices. It includes any valve that uses a piezoelectric actuator, sensor or transducer to open, close, modulate flow or provide feedback, irrespective of the valve's type or application.
[F16K 99/0049] using an electroactive polymer [EAP] -- This subclass covers valve devices that employ electroactive polymers as the actuation medium. The polymers deform when electrically stimulated, providing motion to open, close, or modulate flow without conventional mechanical linkages. Applications include micro-valves, adaptive flow control and smart fluid systems.
[F16K 99/0051] using electrostatic means -- The CPC code F16K 99/0051 designates valve designs that employ electrostatic principles to effect operation, such as electrostatic actuators or spark-based mechanisms for opening or closing a flow path.
[F16K 99/0053] using magnetostrictive means -- This subgroup classifies valves that are operated, controlled, or assisted by magnetostrictive devices, where the deformation of a magnetostrictive material is used to move or adjust the valve element. The entry applies to any valve type that employs such magnetic-strain actuation for opening, closing, or modulating flow.
[F16K 99/0055] actuated by fluids -- This group includes valves whose moving parts are driven directly by liquid or gas pressure, such as hydraulic or pneumatic actuators, where the actuation method is not otherwise specified in the subclass. It covers devices that use fluid power to open, close or modulate flow without additional mechanical linkage.
[F16K 99/0057] the fluid being the circulating fluid itself, e.g. check valves -- A valve in which the circulating medium itself provides the operating force, such as a check valve that relies on the flow of the fluid to open or close. This group covers designs where no external actuator or spring is used, and the fluid pressure or velocity directly controls the valve movement.
[F16K 99/0059] actuated by a pilot fluid -- F16K 99/0059 belongs to the CPC subclass for valves that are not covered by more specific groups and specifies devices whose main valve element is moved or controlled by a pilot fluid, such as hydraulic or pneumatic pressure, rather than by direct mechanical action.
[F16K 99/0061] actuated by an expanding gas or liquid volume -- F16K 99/0061 covers valve actuators that are powered by the expansion of a gas or liquid volume, such as pneumatic or hydraulic actuators that move a valve element when the fluid expands. It is placed in the Mechanical Engineering - Engineering Elements and Units - Valves subclass for devices that do not fit into any other more specific valve groups.
[F16K 99/0063] using centrifugal forces -- F16K 99/0063 covers valve arrangements that employ centrifugal forces to influence fluid flow, such as rotating elements that generate outward pressure to open, close, or modulate the passage. The classification is used for designs where spin-induced forces are the primary mechanism for valve operation and is placed in the "subject matter not provided for in other groups" section of the mechanical-engineering valve subclass.
[F16K 99/0065] using chemical activation -- The code covers valves that are operated or enhanced by chemical activation, such as using a chemical reaction to change valve position or flow characteristics. It includes devices where a chemical trigger modifies the valve element, catalyst, or sealing surface to achieve opening, closing, or modulation.
[F16K 99/0067] actuated by a pyrotechnical charge -- F16K 99/0067 covers valves whose operation is triggered by a pyrotechnic charge, using the rapid energy release to open or close the flow path. Such devices are employed in safety and emergency systems where immediate actuation is required, for example in fire-protection or aerospace applications.
[F16L] PIPES -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F16L 1/00] Laying or reclaiming pipes -- F16L 1/00 pertains to techniques and equipment for installing, laying, or retrieving pipelines and related components. It includes methods for positioning, joining, supporting and recovering pipes in various environments such as underground, underwater or on land. The subclass covers both initial placement and the reuse or reclamation of pipe sections.
[F16L 1/024] Laying or reclaiming pipes on land, e.g. above the ground -- The class F16L 1/024 covers methods and apparatus for installing or retrieving pipes on land, including systems that place pipes above ground level. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units related to pipes, and addresses techniques for laying or reclaiming pipes in terrestrial environments.
[F16L 1/0243] above ground  F16L1/026  takes precedence -- F16L 1/0243 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to laying pipes above ground, with a note that classification F16L 1/026 takes precedence when applicable. This code is part of the broader categories of Mechanical Engineering and Pipes.
[F16L 1/0246] at a certain height off the ground -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pipes passing over streets or bodies of water
[F16L 1/026] in or on a frozen surface -- F16L 1/026 pertains to techniques for installing or recovering pipelines that lie on or within frozen ground, ice or permafrost. It includes methods for thawing, anchoring, insulating or otherwise managing pipes on frozen surfaces during laying or reclamation operations.
[F16L 1/028] in the ground -- F16L 1/028 covers methods and apparatus for installing pipes below the surface of the ground, including trenchless placement, burial and reclamation of underground pipelines.
[F16L 1/032] the pipes being continuous -- F16L 1/032 covers methods or arrangements for laying or reclaiming pipes in which the pipe is installed as a continuous length rather than in segments. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and the pipes subgroup. The classification applies to technologies that join pipe sections on-site to form an uninterrupted conduit.
[F16L 1/036] the pipes being composed of sections of short length -- F16L 1/036 covers pipes that are assembled from short-length sections, typically used in laying or reclaiming operations where the pipe must be built up or reclaimed in modular segments. This classification is part of the mechanical-engineering group for engineering elements and units relating to pipes.
[F16L 1/038] the pipes being made -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods of laying pipes wherein the material which forms the pipe later takes the pipe-shape at the building site, e.g. concrete.
[F16L 1/06] Accessories therefor, e.g. anchors -- The CPC entry F16L 1/06 falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes and concerns accessories used in the laying or reclaiming of pipes, such as anchors and related devices that secure or support pipe sections during installation or removal.
[F16L 1/065] fixed on or to vehicles -- The CPC subclass F16L 1/065 covers pipe-laying or pipe-reclaiming installations that are attached to vehicles. It includes equipment and systems mounted on trucks, trailers, or other mobile platforms for deploying, retrieving, or repairing pipelines while the vehicle moves. The focus is on the means of fixing the pipe-handling apparatus to the vehicle structure.
[F16L 1/09] for bringing two tubular members closer to each other -- F16L 1/09 covers devices or methods that draw two pipe sections together, typically used in the laying, installation or reclamation of pipelines to achieve a tight joint or alignment. The techniques may involve mechanical clamps, hydraulic pushers, or other mechanisms that reduce the gap between tubular members. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and the broader pipes category.
[F16L 1/10] for aligning -- F16L 1/10 refers to devices, tools or methods used to align pipes during their installation or retrieval. It covers apparatus that ensure correct positioning, orientation or straightness of pipelines while they are being laid, reclaimed or repositioned, typically in civil or industrial pipe-laying operations.
[F16L 11/00] Hoses, i.e. flexible pipes -- F16L 11/00 concerns hoses, which are flexible pipes used to convey fluids or gases under pressure. It covers their construction, materials, reinforcement and connection methods in mechanical engineering systems. The class includes design features for durability, pressure resistance and sealing in a variety of industrial and domestic applications.
[F16L 11/005] consisting completely or partially of material other than fibres, plastics or metal -- F16L 11/005 covers flexible pipes, or hoses, whose construction includes any material that is not fibre, plastic or metal, either for the whole hose or for part of its structure, distinguishing them from conventional fibre-reinforced, plastic or metal hoses.
[F16L 11/02] made of fibres or threads, e.g. of textile -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hoses, i.e. flexible pipes, made of fibres or threads, e.g. of textile, which may or may not be impregnated, or provided with an impermeable layer, e.g. fire-hoses.
[F16L 11/04] made of rubber or flexible plastics -- F16L 11/04 pertains to hoses and flexible pipes that are made from rubber or flexible plastics, covering their design, manufacture and application in mechanical-engineering systems for conveying fluids.
[F16L 11/042] formed by bending a sheet and connecting the edges -- This CPC subgroup covers hoses or flexible pipes that are produced by bending a sheet material into a tubular shape and joining the edges, for example by welding, brazing, riveting or adhesive bonding, to form a continuous conduit.
[F16L 11/045] with four or more layers without reinforcement -- F16L 11/045 covers flexible hoses that consist of four or more layers and lack any additional reinforcement, used in mechanical engineering for fluid transport where multilayer construction provides protection and durability without reinforced elements.
[F16L 11/06] with homogeneous wall -- F16L 11/06 covers flexible hoses whose walls are composed of a single uniform material throughout, rather than layered or composite structures. This subclass is applied to patents describing homogeneous-wall hoses used for fluid transport.
[F16L 11/08] with reinforcements embedded in the wall -- This sub-category refers to flexible hoses whose walls contain reinforcement elements-such as fibers, wires or fabric-integrally embedded in the pipe material to increase pressure resistance, durability and dimensional stability. The reinforcement is built into the hose wall rather than being applied externally.
[F16L 11/081] comprising one or more layers of a helically wound cord or wire  in combination with braided layers  F16L11/088 -- This subclass covers flexible hoses built from one or more helically wound cord or wire layers combined with braided reinforcement layers, providing strength and flexibility for fluid conveyance in hydraulic, pneumatic or other mechanical systems.
[F16L 11/082] two layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. hoses with two layers of helically wound cord or wire
[F16L 11/083] three or more layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. hoses with three or more layers of helically wound cord or wire
[F16L 11/085] comprising one or more braided layers  in combination with layers of a helically wound core or wire  F16L11/088 -- This CPC subclass covers flexible hoses that are built with one or more braided reinforcement layers together with a helically wound core or wire layer, a construction that provides combined strength and flexibility. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes and hoses.
[F16L 11/086] two layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. hoses with two braided layers
[F16L 11/087] three or more layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. hoses with three of more braided layers
[F16L 11/088] comprising a combination of one or more layers of a helically wound cord or wire with one or more braided layers -- F16L 11/088 covers flexible hoses built from a combination of one or more helically wound cord or wire layers together with one or more layers that are braided, defining a hybrid reinforcement structure for pipe-type conveyance.
[F16L 1/11] for the detection or protection of pipes in the ground -- The CPC subclass F16L 1/11 covers devices, methods or arrangements used to locate or protect underground pipelines, such as detection systems that identify pipe position or protective measures that shield pipes from external damage. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and the specific activity of laying or reclaiming pipes.
[F16L 11/10] with reinforcements not embedded in the wall -- F16L 11/10 refers to flexible hoses or pipes that incorporate reinforcement elements such as braids, cords or sleeves that are applied to the hose but remain separate from the wall material, rather than being embedded within it. This subclass covers designs where the strengthening components are attached externally or inserted without being integral to the hose structure.
[F16L 11/11] with corrugated wall -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Corrugated hoses made from elastic material.
[F16L 11/111] with homogeneous wall -- F16L 11/111 denotes flexible hoses-pipes made of a single, uniform material throughout the wall without layered reinforcement. It applies to hose designs where the wall is homogeneous, covering all types of such mechanically engineered fluid-conveyance elements.
[F16L 11/112] having reinforcements embedded in the wall -- F16L 11/112 relates to flexible hoses or pipes whose walls contain embedded reinforcements such as fibers, cords or metal strips, providing increased strength, pressure resistance or durability.
[F16L 11/115] having reinforcements not embedded in the wall -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F16L 11/118] having arrangements for particular purposes, e.g. electrically conducting -- The subclass F16L 11/118 refers to flexible pipes or hoses that incorporate special functional arrangements beyond simple fluid conveyance, such as embedded electrical conductors, heating elements, sensors or other purpose-specific features integrated into the hose structure.
[F16L 11/1185] electrically conducting -- F16L 11/1185 classifies flexible hoses, i.e., flexible pipes, that incorporate electrical conductivity for the transmission of power or signals. It covers hoses designed with conductive materials or coatings, enabling combined fluid and electrical functions in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16L 11/12] with arrangements for particular purposes, e.g. specially profiled, with protecting layer, heated, electrically conducting -- F16L 11/12 covers flexible hoses that incorporate specific arrangements for particular purposes, such as a specially profiled cross-section, a protective layer, integrated heating, or built-in electrical conductors to meet defined functional requirements.
[F16L 11/121] specially profiled cross sections -- F16L 11/121 designates flexible pipe elements whose cross-section is intentionally shaped beyond the standard round or rectangular form, such as corrugated, ribbed or other specially profiled profiles that improve flow, strength, or handling. It falls under the CPC group for hoses within mechanical engineering.
[F16L 11/122] Hoses provided with integrated fixing means, e.g. hooks -- F16L 11/122 covers hoses that incorporate built-in fixing devices such as hooks, allowing the hose to be attached directly to structures without separate clamps. The integrated fixings simplify installation and improve reliability in fluid conveyance systems.
[F16L 11/124] Distinguishing marks for hoses -- F16L 11/124 pertains to the application of distinguishing marks on flexible hoses, including color codes, labels, serial numbers, bar-codes or other identifier systems that indicate material type, dimensions, standards compliance or traceability. The class covers methods, devices and arrangements for affixing or integrating such markings to enable quick visual identification, maintenance tracking and anti-counterfeit measures.
[F16L 11/125] non-inflammable or heat-resistant hoses -- F16L 11/125 covers hoses that are designed to resist ignition or to withstand high temperatures. These flexible pipes are used in systems where fire safety or thermal durability is required, such as in hydraulic, pneumatic or chemical transport applications. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and hoses.
[F16L 11/127] electrically conducting -- F16L 11/127 covers flexible hoses that incorporate electrically conducting elements such as conductive cores, layers or coatings, allowing the transmission of electric current or discharge of static charge while retaining the hose's flexibility.
[F16L 11/133] buoyant -- The F16L 11/133 subclass concerns flexible pipes that incorporate buoyancy features to keep the hose afloat, such as built-in floatation members, gas-filled chambers or low-density materials, enabling the hose to remain at the water surface or resist sinking.
[F16L 11/14] made of rigid material, e.g. metal or hard plastics -- F16L 11/14 classifies hoses that are made of rigid material such as metal or hard plastics, distinguishing them from flexible pipe designs. The subclass applies to pipe elements that retain their shape and are used where stiffness or structural support is required.
[F16L 11/15] corrugated -- F16L 11/15 designates corrugated flexible pipes, commonly referred to as hoses, within the mechanical-engineering field. The code applies to pipe elements that incorporate a series of bends or ridges to provide flexibility and resistance to pressure, covering a range of industrial and hydraulic applications.
[F16L 11/16] wound from profiled strips or bands -- A hose or flexible pipe produced by winding profiled metal or composite strips or bands to form a reinforced tube. The method provides a strength-enhanced conduit for fluid or gas transport in mechanical systems.
[F16L 11/18] Articulated hoses, e.g. composed of a series of rings -- The subclass F16L 11/18 covers articulated hoses formed from a series of linked rings that provide flexibility while retaining strength and pressure resistance, typically employed in hydraulic, pneumatic or fluid-transfer systems where a rigid pipe would be impractical. It relates to flexible pipe elements designed to bend in a controlled manner through a modular, ring-based construction.
[F16L 1/12] Laying or reclaiming pipes on or under water -- F16L 1/12 covers methods and apparatus for installing or retrieving pipelines that are placed on or beneath bodies of water. It includes techniques for laying pipes on the seabed, in rivers or lakes, and for their reclamation after service, focusing on mechanical handling and positioning equipment.
[F16L 11/20] Double-walled hoses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Double-walled hoses, i.e. two concentric hoses.
[F16L 11/22] Multi-channel hoses -- Multi-channel hoses are flexible pipe assemblies that incorporate two or more separate conduits within a single outer sheath, allowing simultaneous transport of different fluids or gases while maintaining a compact, protected form. They are employed where space-saving, pressure containment, or segregation of media is required in mechanical systems.
[F16L 1/123] Devices for the protection of pipes under water -- **Definition:** This place covers:  protection against fishing, cortex induced vibrations
[F16L 11/24] wound from strips or bands -- The code F16L 11/24 covers flexible hoses that are formed by winding one or more strips or bands around a core to create a pipe-like conduit. Such wound-from-strips hoses are used for fluid or gas transport where bendability and compact construction are required.
[F16L 1/126] on or close to the surface -- F16L 1/126 pertains to techniques for laying or reclaiming pipelines that run on or close to the surface, such as shallow burial or surface placement, within the broader field of mechanical engineering pipe installation.
[F16L 11/26] made of sound-absorbing materials or with sound-absorbing structure -- F16L 11/26 pertains to flexible pipes or hoses that are constructed from or include sound-absorbing materials or structures to lessen noise transmission. It applies to mechanical-engineering components where acoustic control is required, such as HVAC, hydraulic or pneumatic hoses with built-in sound-damping features.
[F16L 1/14] between the surface and the bottom -- This subgroup covers methods for installing or retrieving pipes in the space that lies between the ground surface and the trench bottom, including shallow-bedding, covering or other techniques that place the pipe directly under the surface without deep excavation.
[F16L 1/15] vertically -- **Definition:** This place covers:  pipes that stay vertically, for example: riser pipes
[F16L 1/16] on the bottom -- F16L 1/16 covers methods or apparatus for laying or reclaiming pipes that act on the bottom of a trench, channel or other conduit, such as positioning, supporting or fixing the pipe to the underlying surface during installation or retrieval.
[F16L 1/161] the pipe being composed of sections of short length -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g.pipe laying methods wherein the pipe segments are joined together on the bottom of the body of water.
[F16L 1/163] by varying the apparent weight of the pipe during the laying operation -- This subclass covers methods for laying or reclaiming pipes where the pipe's effective weight is altered during installation, such as by adding buoyancy devices, using water or air pressure, or adjusting ballast to control sinking or floating, thereby facilitating precise placement.
[F16L 1/165] by towing the pipe on or near the bottom -- This subclass covers techniques for installing or retrieving pipelines by towing the pipe along the seabed or river bottom, typically using a tow bar or similar apparatus to pull the pipe while it rests on or near the ground.
[F16L 1/166] Reclaiming pipes -- F16L 1/166 concerns methods and apparatus for reclaiming pipes, i.e., retrieving, cleaning, repairing or re-using pipelines that have already been installed, typically in underground or subsea settings. It covers techniques for pulling out, restoring and repositioning sections of pipe to enable their subsequent use or disposal.
[F16L 1/168] under ice -- F16L 1/168 pertains to techniques for installing or retrieving pipelines beneath ice cover, including methods for cutting, melting or tunneling through ice to place or recover pipes in frozen water bodies such as lakes, rivers or seas. It addresses equipment and procedures for maintaining pipe integrity and managing ice-related challenges during the laying or reclaiming process.
[F16L 1/18] the pipes being S- or J-shaped and under tension during laying -- F16L 1/18 classifies the laying or reclaiming of pipes that are S- or J-shaped and kept under tension during the operation. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, specifically pipes and their installation.
[F16L 1/19] the pipes being J-shaped -- F16L 1/19 covers the installation of J-shaped pipes in systems for laying or reclaiming pipelines. It pertains to mechanical engineering elements where the pipe is bent into a J shape to facilitate navigation around obstacles or to connect sections at an angle. The code is used by the EPO and USPTO to classify such pipe configurations.
[F16L 1/20] Accessories therefor, e.g. floats or weights -- The subclass F16L 1/20 covers accessories used in laying or reclaiming pipes, such as floats, weights and other devices that facilitate positioning, sinking or stabilising the pipe during installation. These components assist in handling, alignment and retention of pipe sections while they are placed or recovered.
[F16L 1/201] Anchor rods -- F16L 1/201 covers anchor rods employed to secure or stabilize pipes during installation or reclamation. The class addresses devices that fasten pipe sections to surrounding structures, ground or supports, ensuring proper alignment and resistance to movement under load. It is part of the mechanical engineering hierarchy for engineering elements and units related to pipes.
[F16L 1/202] fixed on or to vessels -- F16L 1/202 covers techniques for securing pipes directly to vessels, including ships, barges or offshore platforms, during installation, maintenance or reclamation. It relates to methods of anchoring, clamping or welding pipes to the hull or deck to ensure stable attachment and proper operation.
[F16L 1/203] the pipes being wound spirally prior to laying -- This subclass covers the preparation of pipes for installation by winding them in a spiral shape before laying or reclaiming. It includes techniques for coiling pipe sections to improve handling, transport, and placement in trenches or on the seabed.
[F16L 1/205] Pipe-laying ships  F16L1/225 ,  F16L1/23  and  F16L1/235  take precedence -- F16L 1/205 covers vessels designed for laying or reclaiming pipelines on the seabed, including the equipment and methods used to transport, position, and install pipe sections during offshore construction. The class falls under mechanical engineering pipe elements and is subordinate to the broader entries F16L1/225, F16L1/23 and F16L1/235, which take precedence.
[F16L 1/206] Apparatus for forming or coating the pipes -- F16L 1/206 covers devices that shape or apply a coating to pipes during manufacturing or refurbishment. The apparatus may include extrusion heads, rollers, spray systems or other mechanisms that continuously form pipe walls or deposit protective layers, facilitating production or repair of pipelines.
[F16L 1/207] Pipe handling apparatus -- F16L 1/207 covers apparatus designed to handle pipes during installation or retrieval, such as devices that lift, move, position, or support pipes while they are being laid or reclaimed in pipelines or infrastructure projects. It includes machinery that facilitates controlled handling to reduce manual effort and improve alignment and safety.
[F16L 1/225] Stingers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F16L 1/23] Pipe tensioning apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  clamping devices holding the pipe during laying
[F16L 1/235] Apparatus for controlling the pipe during laying -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. checking welding seams or coatings just before laying of the pipe
[F16L 1/24] Floats -- The F16L 1/24 subclass covers floating devices used to support, keep afloat, or stabilize pipes during laying, reclaiming or maintenance operations. It includes buoys, foamed or inflatable elements, and other buoyant structures attached to or integrated with the pipe to control its position in water, trenches or on the seabed.
[F16L 1/26] Repairing or joining pipes on or under water -- F16L 1/26 addresses methods for repairing or joining pipes that are on or beneath water, including underwater welding, clamping, sealing and other techniques used during installation, maintenance or reclamation of submerged pipeline systems.
[F16L 1/265] Underwater vehicles moving on the bottom -- The classification F16L 1/265 covers underwater vehicles that travel along the seabed, employed for laying, inspecting, or recovering pipelines and other seafloor installations. It relates to mechanical-engineering methods for handling pipes on the ocean floor.
[F16L 13/00] Pipe joints -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-disconnectable joints between rigid pipes.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Non-disconnectable pipe joints to walls or other pipes, the joined pipe axis being perpendicular to the plane of the wall or to the axis of the other pipe -> F16L41/082 - Non-disconnectable joints for rigid pipes of plastics -> F16L47/00
[F16L 13/002] for pipes having a rectangular cross-section -- F16L 13/002 concerns pipe joints specifically adapted for pipes that have a rectangular cross-section. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and pipe joints, and addresses the connection of non-circular conduits in various applications.
[F16L 13/004] Shrunk pipe-joints -- Shrunk pipe-joints are connections in which a pipe or fitting is heated, then allowed to cool so that it contracts and grips the adjoining surface, creating a tight, leak-proof seal without the use of additional fasteners or adhesives. This method is used for joining sections of pipe in mechanical engineering applications where a permanent, high-strength joint is required.
[F16L 13/007] specially adapted for joining pipes of dissimilar materials -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Joints between metal and plastic pipes -> F16L47/24
[F16L 13/013] Accessories therefor -- F16L 13/013 designates accessories for pipe joints, such as clamps, sleeves, gaskets, seals and other auxiliary components that facilitate the connection, alignment, reinforcement or protection of pipelines. The code falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for pipes and their joints.
[F16L 13/02] Welded joints -- F16L 13/02 classifies pipe joints that are created by welding, encompassing the various welding techniques, joint designs and reinforcement methods used to fuse pipe sections together. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the engineering elements and units of pipes.
[F16L 13/0209] Male-female welded joints  F16L13/0245  and  F16L13/0254  take precedence -- This CPC subclass covers pipe joints formed by welding a male end into a female socket, providing a secure, leak-tight connection between pipe sections. It applies to welded male-female joints of various shapes and materials, and more specific sub-groups such as 13/0245 and 13/0254 take precedence when their criteria are met.
[F16L 13/0218] having an inner or outer ring  F16L13/0245  and  F16L13/0254  take precedence -- The subclass F16L 13/0218 covers pipe joints that incorporate an inner or outer sealing ring as part of the connection. This feature is further refined in the more specific subclasses F16L 13/0245 and F16L 13/0254, which take precedence over the broader ring-based definition.
[F16L 13/0227] having an inner ring -- F16L 13/0227 covers pipe joints that incorporate an inner ring within the connection. The inner ring is positioned inside the pipe to provide sealing, support, or reinforcement of the joint.
[F16L 13/0236] having an outer ring -- F16L 13/0236 covers pipe joints that incorporate an outer ring surrounding the connection. The outer ring typically provides sealing, reinforcement, or alignment functions, improving joint integrity under pressure or mechanical loads. This feature is used in mechanical engineering applications for piping systems.
[F16L 13/0245] with holes in the sleeve or spigot being filled with weld -- F16L 13/0245 pertains to pipe joints in which holes occurring in the sleeve or spigot are sealed by welding, a method that restores continuity and structural integrity of the joint.
[F16L 13/0254] the pipes having an internal or external coating -- F16L 13/0254 covers pipes that feature an internal or external coating, addressing the material or layer applied to the pipe surface for protection, corrosion resistance, or functional enhancement. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes → Pipe joints in the CPC system.
[F16L 13/0263] having an internal coating -- F16L 13/0263 covers pipe joints that incorporate an internal coating on the mating surfaces or bore of the joint. The coating is applied to improve resistance to wear, corrosion or chemical attack, and may also provide lubrication or sealing benefits within the joint. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes → Pipe joints.
[F16L 13/0272] having an external coating -- F16L 13/0272 classifies pipe joints that are provided with an external coating, such as paint, polymer or a corrosion-resistant layer, applied to the joint surface to protect against wear, corrosion or environmental damage.
[F16L 13/0281] cold welded -- F16L 13/0281 covers pipe joints that are assembled by cold welding, a process that joins metal sections without melting, using pressure or friction to create a solid-state bond. This method is used for creating leak-tight connections in various mechanical engineering applications.
[F16L 13/029] for concrete pipes -- F16L 13/029 covers pipe-joint arrangements specifically designed for concrete pipes, including devices, methods and fittings used to connect, seal or secure concrete pipe sections in civil-engineering and water-management applications.
[F16L 13/04] with arrangements preventing overstressing -- F16L 13/04 pertains to pipe joints that incorporate arrangements to prevent overstressing of the joint or the adjoining pipe during service. These arrangements may include flexible sleeves, springs, geometry that limits stress concentrations, or other mechanisms that protect the joint from excessive pressure, vibration or thermal loads, ensuring reliable operation.
[F16L 13/06] with tension-relief of the weld by means of detachable members, e.g. divided tensioning rings, bolts in flanges -- F16L 13/06 refers to pipe joint designs that incorporate tension-relief features for the welded joint, achieved by detachable components such as divided tensioning rings or bolts mounted in flanges. The element belongs to the mechanical engineering group, under engineering elements and units, specifically pipes.
[F16L 13/08] Soldered joints -- F16L 13/08 covers pipe joints that are formed by soldering, where a filler metal is melted to bond pipe sections together, typically used for water, gas or steam distribution. The classification pertains to mechanical engineering elements involving piping systems.
[F16L 13/10] Adhesive or cemented joints -- This subclass covers pipe joints that are formed by bonding the pipe sections with adhesives, cement, or similar binding agents rather than by welding, threading or mechanical fittings. It includes the materials, preparation steps and processes used to create a permanent seal between the pipes.
[F16L 13/103] Adhesive joints -- F16L 13/103, titled Adhesive joints, covers pipe joints that are created by bonding components together with adhesives rather than welding or mechanical fastening. It includes the types of adhesives, surface preparations, and curing processes used to unite sections of metal, plastic or composite pipes, as well as the design features that enable a reliable seal.
[F16L 13/106] Tools -- F16L 13/106 covers tools used for assembling, installing, maintaining, or disassembling pipe joints, such as wrenches, fitting devices, sealing implements, cutting tools, and protective equipment. These tools are designed for handling metallic or plastic piping systems in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16L 13/11] using materials which fill the space between parts of a joint before hardening -- F16L 13/11 relates to pipe joint assemblies in which a material is introduced into the space between the joint parts prior to curing, such as sealants, adhesives or filler compounds that harden to form a sealed, bonded connection.
[F16L 13/113] for concrete pipes -- This CPC subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, pipe joints, specifically for concrete pipes. It covers joints, connections, couplings and fittings used to join sections of concrete pipe, including sealing methods and reinforcement techniques.
[F16L 13/116] for socket pipes -- The F16L 13/116 subclass covers pipe-joint designs that join sections by inserting the pipe ends into a socket or sleeve, creating a secure, often sealed connection without additional fastening hardware. It includes methods, shapes and sealing features specific to socket-type pipe joints used in mechanical and fluid-transport systems.
[F16L 13/12] with a seal made of lead, caulked packing, or the like -- F16L 13/12 covers pipe joints that achieve sealing by using lead, caulked packing, or similar materials, rather than mechanical gaskets or welding. The classification applies to connections where the seal is formed by a pliable or malleable substance that fills gaps and prevents leakage in the joint.
[F16L 13/122] for male-female connections  F16L13/124  and  F16L13/126  take precedence -- F16L 13/122 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers pipe joints designed for male-female connections, where specific subcategories like F16L13/124 and F16L13/126 provide more detailed classifications and should be considered first when applicable.
[F16L 13/124] for concrete pipes -- F16L 13/124 relates to pipe joints specifically designed for concrete pipes, covering couplings, seals, gasket arrangements and methods of connecting segments to maintain structural integrity and prevent leakage. The classification addresses both precast and in-situ concrete pipe connections used in water, sewage and drainage systems.
[F16L 13/126] Attachments -- F16L 13/126 - Attachments covers devices that connect or secure pipe sections, such as clamps, brackets, fittings or other elements used to join pipes within mechanical engineering systems.
[F16L 13/128] Tools -- F16L 13/128 designates tools employed in the fabrication, assembly, repair or inspection of pipe joints, including wrenches, threading and cutting devices, joint-opening implements and other specialised equipment used to manipulate or test pipe connections.
[F16L 13/14] made by plastically deforming the material of the pipe, e.g. by flanging, rolling -- F16L 13/14 concerns pipe joints that are created by plastically deforming the pipe wall itself, for example by flanging or rolling, rather than by attaching separate fittings. The method shapes the material to form a permanent connection and is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 13/141] by crimping or rolling from the outside -- F16L 13/141 covers pipe joints that are formed by applying external pressure to crimp or roll the pipe ends together, creating a sealed connection without welding. This method is used for joining metallic or non-metallic pipes in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16L 13/142] with a sealing element inserted into the female part before crimping or rolling -- F16L 13/142, situated in the Mechanical Engineering > Engineering Elements and Units > Pipes hierarchy, pertains to pipe joints in which a sealing element is placed inside the female component prior to crimping or rolling the connection. This arrangement improves leak-tightness by ensuring the seal is positioned before the joint is mechanically deformed.
[F16L 13/143] with a sealing element placed around the male part before crimping or rolling -- F16L 13/143 refers to pipe joints in which a sealing element is positioned around the male component prior to crimping or rolling, providing a leak-tight connection. This subcategory falls under mechanical engineering's pipe and fitting elements, focusing on methods that enhance sealing performance during assembly.
[F16L 13/146] by an axially moveable sleeve -- F16L 13/146 relates to pipe joints that are formed with an axially moveable sleeve, a sliding component that can be positioned along the pipe axis to lock, seal or adjust the connection. The sleeve allows assembly, disassembly and compensation for length changes while maintaining a secure joint in mechanical-engineering piping systems.
[F16L 13/147] by radially expanding the inner part  F16L13/168  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Non-disconnectable pipe joints, made by plastically deforming the material of the pipe, the pipe joint consisting of overlapping extremities having mutually co-operating collars, for screw threaded pipes -> F16L13/168
[F16L 13/148] specially designed to ensure an intended leakage until correct deformation -- F16L 13/148 relates to pipe joints that are intentionally made to leak a controlled amount until the joint is deformed to its correct shape, serving as a visual or functional indicator that proper assembly or deformation has not yet been achieved. Such designs are used to signal incorrect installation or to allow pressure release until the joint reaches the intended configuration.
[F16L 13/16] the pipe joint consisting of overlapping extremities having mutually co-operating collars -- F16L 13/16 relates to pipe joints in which the ends of the pipes overlap and are secured by mutually cooperating collars that interlock to provide a sealed connection. This type of joint is used in mechanical-engineering applications for joining sections of pipe.
[F16L 13/161] the pipe or collar being deformed by crimping or rolling -- The code F16L 13/161 covers pipe joints in which the pipe or a collar is permanently deformed by crimping or rolling to achieve a seal or connection. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes, describing a forming method rather than a separate fitting.
[F16L 13/163] one collar being bent over the other -- F16L 13/163 covers pipe-joint designs in which a collar is deformed so that it bends over an adjacent collar, creating a sealed connection by overlapping the two parts. This method is used for joining cylindrical pipes without additional fasteners.
[F16L 13/165] the pipe or collar being deformed by an axially movable sleeve -- F16L 13/165 covers pipe-joint designs in which a pipe or collar is deformed by a sleeve that moves axially along the joint, applying compressive force to secure and seal the connection. The mechanism typically uses the sleeve's longitudinal motion to reshape the pipe or collar, creating a tight, leak-free joint without external fasteners.
[F16L 13/166] Deformed by radially expanding an inner part  F16L13/168  takes precedence -- F16L 13/166 covers pipe joints that are formed by radially expanding an inner part to deform the surrounding pipe material, creating a tight seal or reinforcing the connection. The method involves inserting a mandrel or similar device and applying outward pressure so the pipe wall yields around it. This technique is used where welding or external fittings are impractical.
[F16L 13/168] for screw threaded pipes -- The CPC subclass F16L 13/168 covers pipe joints designed for screw-threaded pipes, specifying connections that rely on external or internal threads to secure sections together. It pertains to mechanical engineering components used in piping systems for fluid transport and pressure containment.
[F16L 15/00] Screw-threaded joints -- F16L 15/00 covers pipe connections that are joined by screw threads, including the design, dimensions and sealing methods of male and female threaded fittings. It addresses the use of tapered or parallel threads, thread sealing compounds and the materials suited for pressure-tight, removable pipe joints. This subclass is part of the mechanical engineering hierarchy for engineering elements and units related to pipes.
[F16L 15/001] with conical threads -- F16L 15/001 classifies pipe screw-threaded joints that use conical (tapered) threads; the thread profile narrows along its length to create a tightening fit and sealing action when the male and female parts are rotated together. This subgroup falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.
[F16L 15/002] with more than one threaded section -- This subclass covers pipe joints that incorporate two or more separate threaded sections along the length of a pipe or fitting, providing multiple coupling points or incremental assembly. It includes designs where the threads are spaced apart or arranged in series to create distinct attachment zones.
[F16L 15/003] with sealing rings -- F16L 15/003 concerns screw-threaded pipe joints that incorporate sealing rings to provide a leak-tight connection. The classification covers designs where one or more rings are placed in the thread or joint to ensure sealing under pressure, including different ring materials, shapes and mounting methods used in hydraulic, pneumatic or fluid transport applications.
[F16L 15/004] with axial sealings having at least one plastically deformable sealing surface  with sealing rings  F16L15/003 -- The classification F16L 15/004 pertains to screw-threaded pipe joints that employ axial sealing elements, where at least one sealing surface is plastically deformable and works together with sealing rings to provide a leak-tight connection.
[F16L 15/005] for thin-walled pipes having at least their extremities deformed so as to have the shape of screw-threads -- The subclass F16L 15/005 covers devices that create screw-threaded connections on thin-walled pipes by deforming at least the pipe ends into a threaded profile. It pertains to methods and tools for forming such threads on pipes whose wall thickness is relatively small compared with their diameter.
[F16L 15/006] with straight threads -- This CPC subclass concerns pipe connections that employ screw threads with a constant, non-tapered profile. It covers joints where the threads run straight along the pipe axis, providing a uniform pitch and diameter for joining sections. Used in mechanical engineering for assembling straight-threaded pipe fittings.
[F16L 15/007] with more than one threaded section -- F16L 15/007 covers pipe or fitting designs that incorporate two or more separate threaded portions along the same element, enabling multiple screw-threaded connections on a single piece. These configurations are used in mechanical engineering piping to join sections at several locations without additional components.
[F16L 15/008] with sealing rings -- F16L 15/008 pertains to screw-threaded pipe joints that incorporate sealing rings to prevent leakage. It covers the design, construction and application of sealing-ring devices integrated with threaded connections in mechanical engineering pipe systems.
[F16L 15/009] with axial sealings having at least one plastically deformable sealing surface  with sealing rings  F16L15/008 -- F16L 15/009 refers to screw-threaded pipe joints that provide axial sealing by using at least one plastically deformable sealing surface, often with sealing rings that are pressed into place to create a leak-tight connection. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 15/02] allowing substantial longitudinal adjustment by the use of a long screw-threaded part -- This subgroup covers pipe connections that achieve a large axial movement through a single extended threaded component, enabling the pipe sections to be positioned or aligned over a considerable distance while maintaining a sealed joint. It applies to screw-threaded joints where the adjustment length is provided by a long screw part.
[F16L 15/04] with additional sealings -- F16L 15/04 covers screw-threaded pipe joints that incorporate extra sealing mechanisms to improve leak resistance. It applies to mechanical engineering components where the joint design includes additional sealing elements such as O-rings, gaskets or integrated seals.
[F16L 15/06] characterised by the shape of the screw-thread -- F16L 15/06 refers to pipe connections where the defining feature is the shape of the screw thread used in the joint. It includes designs that rely on particular thread profiles, such as trapezoidal, round, or custom shapes, to achieve sealing or mechanical strength. The classification is used for patents concerning pipe fittings, couplings, and similar components.
[F16L 15/08] with supplementary elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Screw-threaded connections with supplementary elements not forming a seal, e.g. additional locking elements.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Screw-threaded joints with additional sealings -> F16L15/04
[F16L 17/00] Joints with packing adapted to sealing by fluid pressure -- F16L 17/00 covers pipe joints that incorporate a packing element designed to seal the connection by means of fluid pressure applied to the packing. The arrangement typically uses a gasket, sleeve or similar material that expands or is forced against the mating surfaces when pressure is introduced, providing a leak-tight seal in mechanical-engineered piping systems.
[F16L 17/02] with sealing rings arranged between outer surface of pipe and inner surface of sleeve or socket -- The F16L 17/02 subclass covers pipe joints that achieve sealing by fluid pressure using sealing rings positioned between the pipe's outer surface and the inner surface of a surrounding sleeve or socket. This arrangement isolates the joint from leakage while allowing the pipe to transmit fluid under pressure.
[F16L 17/025] the sealing rings having radially directed ribs -- **Definition:** This place covers: Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place: 1a. 1b. In figures 1a and 1b, note radially directed ribs (46 and 48) of sealing ring (42).  **Glossary:** In this place, the following terms or expressions are used with the meaning indicated: The term "radially directed ribs" also includes ribs which are deformed in an axial direction upon connection.
[F16L 17/03] having annular axial lips -- The subclass F16L 17/03 covers pipe joints that employ annular axial lips as part of the packing arrangement to improve sealing under fluid pressure. The axial lips form a continuous ring around the joint axis, providing a compliant surface that presses against the packing material when pressure is applied.
[F16L 17/032] the sealing rings having only one lip -- The subclass F16L 17/032 pertains to sealing rings that have a single lip and are used in pipe joints where sealing is achieved by fluid pressure. These one-lip rings provide a simple, compact seal for mechanical engineering applications involving pipe connections.
[F16L 17/035] the sealing rings having two lips parallel to each other -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.
[F16L 17/04] with longitudinally split or divided sleeve -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  1a.  1b.
[F16L 17/06] with sealing rings arranged between the end surfaces of the pipes or flanges or arranged in recesses in the pipe ends or flanges -- F16L 17/06 classifies pipe or flange joints that rely on fluid pressure to activate packing for sealing. It specifically covers configurations where sealing rings are positioned between the end surfaces or placed in recesses of the pipe ends or flanges.
[F16L 17/063] forming a whole with the pipe or joint -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sealing rings which are an integral part of the pipe, flange or joint, i.e. forming a whole with the pipe, flange or joint.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  Sealing member (42) adapted to seal by fluid pressure is integral with joint (30).
[F16L 17/067] Plastics sealing rings -- F16L 17/067 pertains to plastic sealing rings employed in pipe joints whose packing is designed to be sealed by fluid pressure. The subclass covers the form, material and mounting features of such rings, which are used to prevent leakage in mechanical engineering pipe systems.
[F16L 17/073] the sealing rings having two lips parallel to each other -- This CPC sub-group relates to sealing rings that have two parallel lips and are used in pipe joints where the seal is achieved by fluid pressure. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and joints with packing adapted to sealing by fluid pressure.
[F16L 17/08] Metal sealing rings -- Metal sealing rings are components used in pipe joints where the packing is designed to achieve sealing through fluid pressure. They provide a reliable, pressure-responsive barrier that prevents leaks in mechanical-engineering applications such as pipelines, valves and hydraulic systems.
[F16L 17/10] the packing being sealed by the pressure of a fluid other than the fluid in or surrounding the pipe -- F16L 17/10 covers pipe joints whose sealing packing is activated by a fluid pressure that is separate from the fluid flowing through or surrounding the pipe. It applies to mechanical-engineering pipe connections where an external pressure source compresses the packing to achieve a leak-tight joint.
[F16L 19/00] Joints in which sealing surfaces are pressed together by means of a member, e.g. a swivel nut, screwed on, or into, one of the joint parts -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Joints with packing adapted to sealing by fluid pressure -> F16L17/00 - Flanged joints with sealing surfaces pressed together by bolts or equivalent connecting means -> F16L23/00 - Connecting arrangements or other fittings specially adapted to be made of plastics or to be used with pipes made of plastics -> F16L47/00 - Joints, specially adapted for pipes of brittle material, in which sealing surfaces are pressed together by means of a member, e.g. swivel nut, screwed on, or into, one of the joint parts -> F16L49/06
[F16L 19/005] comprising locking means for the threaded member -- This CPC subclass F16L 19/005 covers pipe joints where sealing surfaces are pressed together and the joint includes a locking mechanism for the threaded component, such as a swivel nut or screw that secures the connection. It belongs to the mechanical engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 19/02] Pipe ends provided with collars or flanges, integral with the pipe or not, pressed together by a screwed member -- F16L 19/02 covers pipe ends fitted with collars or flanges, whether integral with the pipe or attached separately, that are joined by a screwed member which presses the sealing surfaces together. The arrangement provides a mechanical joint such as a swivel nut or screwed nut to achieve a pressure-tight connection.
[F16L 19/0206] the collar not being integral with the pipe -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Joints with flanges not forming part of the pipe body. This includes welded or screwed on flanges which are not considered to be integral with the pipe
[F16L 19/0212] using specially adapted sealing means -- F16L 19/0212 covers pipe joints whose sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member such as a swivel nut or screw, focusing on the use of specially adapted sealing means to enhance the seal. It addresses design features and methods that modify or add seals, gaskets or sealing inserts for these pressure-pressed connections. This subclass falls within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units for pipes.
[F16L 19/0218] comprising only sealing rings -- This subgroup of the CPC covers pipe joints in which the sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member such as a swivel nut or screw, and the joint relies exclusively on sealing rings as the sealing element.
[F16L 19/0225] without sealing rings -- F16L 19/0225 refers to pipe joints where the sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member such as a swivel nut or screw, and the seal is achieved without any separate sealing rings, relying on metal-to-metal contact.
[F16L 19/0231] with specially adapted means for positioning the threaded member behind the collar -- F16L 19/0231 covers pipe joints whose sealing surfaces are pressed together and that incorporate specially adapted mechanisms for positioning the threaded member behind the collar, such as swivel nuts or screwed elements that lock the joint while allowing precise alignment and secure sealing.
[F16L 19/0237] specially adapted for use with attachments, e.g. reduction units, T-pieces, bends or the like -- F16L 19/0237 classifies pipe joints in which the sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member such as a swivel nut, and that are specially adapted to receive attachments like reduction units, T-pieces, bends or similar fittings.
[F16L 19/0243] specially adapted for use with coated pipes -- This subclass covers pipe joints in which the sealing surfaces are forced together by a member such as a swivel nut or screw, and the joint is specially designed for use with pipes that have protective coatings. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and the broader F16L 19/024 group.
[F16L 19/025] the pipe ends having integral collars or flanges -- F16L 19/025 covers pipe ends that are manufactured with built-in collars or flanges, allowing them to be joined directly to other components without separate fittings. These integral features facilitate sealed connections where the sealing surfaces are pressed together by a fastening member such as a swivel nut or screw, fitting within the broader category of pipe joints in mechanical engineering.
[F16L 19/028] the collars or flanges being obtained by deformation of the pipe wall -- This subclass covers pipe joints where the sealing surfaces are pressed together using a component such as a swivel nut, and specifically includes collars or flanges formed by deforming the pipe wall to create the sealing or attachment feature. It pertains to mechanical engineering pipe connections employing deformed pipe-wall elements as the sealing or fastening part.
[F16L 19/0283] and having a bell-mouthed shape -- F16L 19/0283 refers to pipe joints where sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member such as a swivel nut or screw, and the joint includes a bell-mouthed shape that guides or expands the connection.
[F16L 19/0286] and being formed as a flange -- This subgroup covers pipe joints whose sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member such as a swivel nut, screw or similar device, and where the joint incorporates a flange that is formed as part of the component. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 19/04] using additional rigid rings, sealing directly on at least one pipe end, which is flared either before or during the making of the connection -- This subclass covers pipe joints that achieve a metal-to-metal seal by adding one or more rigid rings that are placed directly on at least one flared pipe end, either before or during assembly of the connection, such as in swivel-nut or screwed joints.
[F16L 19/041] the ring being an insert  F16L19/043  takes precedence -- This subclass covers pipe joints where the sealing surfaces are compressed by a tightening member such as a swivel nut or a screw, and the seal is provided by a ring used as an insert. It applies to arrangements where the ring functions as the primary sealing element, but more specific rules in subclass F16L 19/043 override it.
[F16L 19/043] with additional sealing means -- This subgroup deals with pipe joints where the sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member such as a swivel nut or screw, and the joint includes extra sealing elements-such as O-rings, gaskets or secondary seals-to enhance leak-tightness.
[F16L 19/045] consisting of cutting edges on one of the connecting parts which penetrate into the wall of the pipe -- F16L 19/045 covers pipe joints in which one of the connecting components is provided with cutting edges that penetrate the pipe wall to achieve sealing by mechanical interlock. This design is used where the joint is assembled by pressurising the parts together, such as with swivel nuts or screwed members.
[F16L 19/046] consisting of a soft ring -- F16L 19/046 covers pipe joints that use a compressible soft ring as the sealing element between pressed-together surfaces. The soft ring, usually made of elastomer or similar material, deforms under tightening pressure from a member such as a swivel nut or screw to create an airtight seal.
[F16L 19/048] specially adapted for use with attachments, e.g. reduction units, T-pieces, bends or the like -- A pipe joint in which the sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member such as a swivel nut or screw, and which is specially adapted to accommodate attachments like reduction units, T-pieces, bends or similar components. This type of joint falls under the mechanical engineering category for pipe elements.
[F16L 19/05] with a rigid pressure ring between the screwed member and the exterior of the flared pipe end -- F16L 19/05 covers pipe joints where a rigid pressure ring is positioned between a screwed component such as a swivel nut and the exterior of a flared pipe end to provide a sealed, pressure-resistant connection. The design presses sealing surfaces together by the tension of the screwed member, improving leak-tightness in mechanical-engineering applications.
[F16L 19/055] the pressure ring being rotatably connected to the threaded member -- The entry F16L 19/055 defines a joint arrangement in pipe systems where the sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member such as a swivel nut, and a pressure ring is attached to the threaded component in a way that allows it to rotate relative to that component, improving sealing reliability.
[F16L 19/06] in which radial clamping is obtained by wedging action on non-deformed pipe ends -- This subclass covers pipe joints whose sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member that creates radial clamping through a wedging action on pipe ends that remain undeformed. The wedging element, such as a swivel nut or screw, forces the ends together to achieve a leak-tight connection.
[F16L 19/061] a pressure ring being arranged between the clamping ring and the threaded member or the connecting member -- **Definition:** This place covers:  connections having two rings
[F16L 19/062] specially adapted for use with attachments, e.g. reduction units, T-pieces, bends or the like -- This subclass covers pipe joints where the sealing surfaces are pressed together by a component such as a swivel nut or screw, and the joint is specially adapted to receive accessories like reduction units, T-pieces, bends or similar fittings. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 19/063] by means of conical threaded surfaces -- The code F16L 19/063 covers pipe joints whose sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member with conical threaded surfaces, such as a swivel nut that is screwed onto or into one part of the joint. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 19/065] the wedging action being effected by means of a ring -- **Definition:** This place covers:  connections having one ring
[F16L 19/0653] the ring being rotatably connected to one of the connecting parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  connections wherein the ring rotates with the nut during the fastening
[F16L 19/0656] integral with one of the connecting parts -- The code F16L 19/0656 pertains to pipe joints where the sealing member is formed as an integral part of one of the connecting elements. It includes arrangements in which the sealing surfaces are pressed together by a component such as a swivel nut or screwed member that is built into the pipe or fitting.
[F16L 19/07] adapted for use in socket or sleeve connections -- F16L 19/07 covers pipe joints where sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member such as a swivel nut, and the joint is specifically adapted for use in socket or sleeve connections.
[F16L 19/075] specially adapted for spigot-and-socket joints -- This CPC subclass covers pipe joints where the sealing surfaces are pressed together by a compressive member such as a swivel nut or screw, and the joint is specially adapted for spigot-and-socket connections.
[F16L 19/08] with metal rings which bite into the wall of the pipe -- The subclass F16L 19/08 covers pipe joints that achieve sealing by metal rings pressed into the pipe wall, such as in swivel nuts or screwed-on connections. These biting rings embed in the pipe material to create a reliable pressure-tight joint.
[F16L 19/083] the longitudinal cross-section of the ring not being modified during clamping -- **Definition:** This place covers:  connections wherein the ring being a split ring
[F16L 19/086] with additional sealing means -- This subclass covers pipe joints where the sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member such as a swivel nut or screw, and the joint includes additional sealing elements beyond the basic compression. It belongs to the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes.
[F16L 19/10] the profile of the ring being altered -- **Definition:** This place covers:  connection wherein the ring being no split ring
[F16L 19/103] with more than one ring per pipe end being used -- It falls within the mechanical engineering category for engineering elements and units, specifically pipes. The subclass addresses pipe joints whose sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member such as a swivel nut or screw, and where each pipe end uses more than one sealing ring to achieve the seal.
[F16L 19/106] the ring comprising a shoulder against which the pipe end abuts -- F16L 19/106 relates to pipe joints in which sealing surfaces are forced together by a member such as a swivel nut or screw, and specifically addresses a ring that incorporates a shoulder providing a stop against which the pipe end abuts.
[F16L 19/12] with additional sealing means -- F16L 19/12 covers pipe joints where the sealing surfaces are forced together by a fastening element such as a swivel nut or screw, and the joint includes one or more additional sealing devices to improve leak tightness. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.
[F16L 19/14] the rings being integral with one of the connecting parts -- F16L 19/14 covers pipe joints where sealing surfaces are pressed together and the sealing rings are formed as an integral part of one of the joint components, such as a swivel nut or screwed member. This subclass falls within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, concerning joints whose sealing rings are built into a connecting part.
[F16L 2011/047] with a diffusion barrier layer -- F16L 2011/047 refers to seals that incorporate a diffusion barrier layer. The layer is applied to prevent intermetallic diffusion or chemical migration between the sealing material and the adjoining surface, enhancing durability especially under high temperature or corrosive conditions.
