This subclass is used for patents describing such barrier-enhanced sealing arrangements.
[F16L 2013/145] Tools specially adapted therefor -- F16L 20/13 - Tools specially adapted therefor covers specialized instruments and accessories designed for the assembly, servicing, testing or adjustment of pump units. It includes cleaning brushes, inspection devices, repair kits and other equipment made specifically to work with the pump's components.
[F16L 21/00] Joints with sleeve or socket -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Non-disconnectable pipe-joints -> F16L13/00 - Screw-threaded joints -> F16L15/00 - Joints adapted to sealing by fluid pressure -> F16L17/00 - Joints in which sealing surfaces are pressed together by means of a member, e.g. a swivel nut -> F16L19/00 - Joints for pipes made of reinforced concrete -> F16L25/0027 - Connecting arrangements or other fittings specially adapted to be made of plastics or to be used with pipes made of plastics -> F16L47/00 - Joints specially adapted for pipes of brittle material -> F16L49/00

[F16L 21/007] clamped by a wedging action  F16L27/00 ,  F16L37/092 ,  F16L37/122 ,  F16L37/123 ,  F16L37/15 ,  F16L37/16  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Adjustable joints; Joints allowing movement -> F16L27/00 - Quick-acting type couplings combined with automatic locking by means of elements wedged between the pipe and the frusto-conical surface of the body of the connector -> F16L37/092 - Quick-acting type couplings using hooks tightened by a wedge section -> F16L37/122 - Quick-acting type couplings using a retaining member in the form of a wedge -> F16L37/123 - Quick-acting type couplings secured by inserting a wedge between mating surfaces -> F16L37/15 - Quick-acting type couplings tightened by the action of a wedge-shaped hinged hooks -> F16L37/16
[F16L 2101/00] Indexing scheme associated with groups -- F16L 2101/00 covers methods and systems for assigning an indexing scheme to groups of pipes, such as labeling, coding or numbering arrangements that identify and organise pipe sections within an installation or plant. The classification pertains to the documentation and management of pipe groupings for easier reference, maintenance and inventory control.
[F16L 2101/10] Treating the inside of pipes -- This classification covers methods for treating the interior surfaces of pipes, including cleaning, coating, corrosion protection or other modifications, applied to pipe elements in mechanical engineering.
[F16L 2101/12] Cleaning -- F16L 2101/12 concerns cleaning of pipes, covering techniques and devices for removing deposits, scale, or contaminants from the interior or exterior of pipe sections. It includes mechanical, hydraulic, chemical, or combined processes designed to restore flow or prevent fouling.
[F16L 2101/16] Coating by application of fluent materials, e.g. painting -- F16L 2101/16 covers the coating of pipes by applying fluent materials such as paints, varnishes or other liquid finishes. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for pipes, and addresses processes that protect or enhance pipe surfaces.
[F16L 2101/18] Lining other than coating -- F16L 2101/18 refers to pipe linings that are not simple surface coatings, covering internal treatments such as cement, polymer, ceramic or metallic linings applied to enhance corrosion resistance, wear protection or structural integrity. The code falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes in the CPC system used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F16L 2101/20] Expelling gases or fluids -- This group covers pipe elements and assemblies whose main function is to discharge gases or fluids from a system, such as venting, blow-out, pressure-relief or exhaust pipes and the associated mechanisms that control or direct the flow.
[F16L 2101/30] Inspecting, measuring or testing -- F16L 2101/30 covers techniques and devices for inspecting, measuring or testing pipe components and systems. It includes visual examination, ultrasonic, radiographic and pressure testing to assess integrity, dimensions or performance. The classification falls under Mechanical Engineering - Engineering Elements and Units - Pipes.
[F16L 2101/40] Separating transported fluids -- F16L 2101/40 designates pipe-related devices that separate transported fluids, such as separators, filters or phase-splitters incorporated in piping systems. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and then pipes, covering equipment that isolates different liquids, gases or mixtures during flow.
[F16L 2101/50] Pulling cables or the like -- F16L 2101/50 is a subclass of the Mechanical Engineering section covering pipe-related engineering elements. It specifically pertains to devices or methods for pulling cables, wires, or similar conductors through pipes or tube systems, facilitating installation or maintenance of cable runs within pipe networks.
[F16L 2101/60] Stopping leaks -- F16L 2101/60 covers devices and methods for stopping leaks in pipe systems, including seals, clamps, stop-valves and other mechanical means that isolate or repair a leak without dismantling the pipe. It applies to solutions that can be installed on or within existing pipelines to quickly restore pressure containment and prevent fluid loss.
[F16L 2101/70] Drill-well operations -- F16L 2101/70 covers the technical features and processes used in drill-well operations, including the design, arrangement and handling of drill-pipes, casing and related equipment for creating boreholes. It addresses the mechanical aspects of pipe conveyance, rotation, and pressure control during drilling, as well as methods for inserting and retrieving pipe sections. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes.
[F16L 21/02] with elastic sealing rings between pipe and sleeve or between pipe and socket, e.g. with rolling or other prefabricated profiled rings -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Joints with sleeve or socket with a divided sleeve or ring clamping around the pipe-ends -> F16L21/06 - Joints with sleeve or socket with additional locking means -> F16L21/08 - Adjustable joints -> F16L27/00
[F16L 21/022] used with sleeves or nipples for pipes of the same diameter, or with reduction pieces  F16L21/025  takes precedence -- F16L 21/022 refers to pipe joints that employ a sleeve or socket, used with sleeves or nipples for pipes of the same diameter or with reduction pieces, falling under mechanical engineering elements for pipes. This subclass is superseded by the more specific F16L 21/025.
[F16L 21/025] Rolling sealing rings -- F16L 21/025 covers rolling sealing rings employed in pipe joints that use a sleeve or socket to connect sections. The rings are designed to roll into place, creating a tight, leak-proof seal while allowing axial movement and thermal expansion. Applications include water, gas and oil pipelines.
[F16L 21/03] placed in the socket before connection -- F16L 21/03 relates to pipe connections in which a component is placed into a socket before the joint is completed, such as inserting a sleeve or other fitting into the socket to prepare the pipe for final assembly. This sub-category falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes, and specifically addresses the pre-connection insertion step for sleeve-type joints.
[F16L 21/035] placed around the spigot end before connection -- F16L 21/035 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to joints with a sleeve or socket that are specifically designed to be placed around the spigot end of a pipe before it is connected, ensuring a secure and sealed joint.
[F16L 21/04] in which sealing rings are compressed by axially-movable members -- The CPC subclass F16L 21/04 covers pipe joints that use a sleeve or socket design in which one or more sealing rings are compressed by components that move axially to create a leak-tight connection. It applies to mechanical engineering applications requiring secure, pressure-resistant pipe assemblies.
[F16L 21/045] the members passing through the sealing rings -- F16L 21/045 covers pipe joints in which the pipe members pass through sealing rings to form a fluid-tight connection, typically in sleeve or socket configurations. The subclass addresses the geometry and arrangement of the members that traverse the rings to achieve sealing and mechanical support.
[F16L 21/05] comprising a first ring being placed on a male part and a second ring in the sleeve or socket -- This F16L 21/05 classification covers pipe joints that use a two-ring arrangement: a first ring is slipped onto the external surface of a male pipe section and a second ring is positioned inside the corresponding sleeve or socket, providing sealing or reinforcement for the connection.

[F16L 21/065] tightened by tangentially-arranged threaded pins -- F16L 21/065 covers pipe joints that incorporate a sleeve or socket and are secured by threaded pins placed around the circumference, each pin engaging the joint tangentially to provide the tightening force. The arrangement allows uniform compression of the joint and is used in applications requiring reliable, leak-tight connections.
[F16L 21/08] with additional locking means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Joints with sleeve or socket with a divided sleeve or ring clamping around the pipe-ends -> F16L21/06 - Couplings of the quick-acting type -> F16L37/00
[F16L 2201/00] Indexing scheme associated with main groups -- F16L 2201/00 is a CPC entry for the indexing scheme used to organise the main groups within pipe-related inventions. It provides the systematic classification of pipe components, assemblies and related engineering elements under the broader mechanical engineering category. This code helps patent examiners and researchers locate specific pipe technologies in the mechanical engineering field.
[F16L 2201/10] Indicators for correct coupling -- F16L 2201/10 covers devices that provide a signal-visual, tactile, audible or electronic-to confirm that a pipe coupling has been assembled correctly. These indicators alert users to proper alignment, engagement or sealing of the joint, helping prevent leaks or mis-connections in pipe systems.
[F16L 2201/20] Safety or protective couplings -- F16L 2201/20 relates to safety or protective couplings for pipe systems, which are designed to prevent accidental disconnection, isolate sections, or shut off flow in response to over-pressure, impact or leakage. These devices protect downstream equipment and maintain system integrity by automatically engaging when fault conditions are detected.
[F16L 2201/30] Detecting leaks -- F16L 2201/30 falls under the mechanical engineering hierarchy covering engineering elements and units, specifically pipes. It designates inventions related to detecting leaks in pipe systems, including sensors, monitoring methods, and leak-location techniques. The entry is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F16L 2201/40] for special environments -- F16L 2201/40 relates to pipes designed for special environments, including extreme temperature, pressure, corrosive, radioactive or otherwise harsh conditions. It addresses the selection of materials, construction features and protective measures that allow the pipe to operate reliably where standard pipe designs would be unsuitable.
[F16L 2201/44] sterile -- F16L 2201/44 covers pipe or conduit elements designed to maintain sterility, such as sterilizable tubing, fittings or liners used in medical, pharmaceutical or food-processing systems. The classification applies to components that prevent contamination during transport or processing of sterile fluids, often featuring smooth internal surfaces, sealed joints or materials compatible with sterilisation methods. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.

[F16L 2201/80] Dust covers -- The CPC code F16L 2201/80 designates dust covers used on pipe systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for pipes, and addresses protective devices that prevent ingress of dust or contaminants into pipe interiors.

[F16L 23/02] the flanges being connected by members tensioned axially -- In the Cooperative Patent Classification F16L 23/02, flanged pipe connections are defined where the flanges are joined by members that are tensioned in the axial direction, such as bolts, rods or tie bars that pull the flanges together. This subcategory falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes and flanged joints.
[F16L 23/024] characterised by how the flanges are joined to, or form an extension of, the pipes -- F16L 23/024 covers flanged pipe joints in which the flanges are formed as extensions of the pipe or are attached to the pipe by welding, brazing, threading, clamping or other joining methods, rather than being separate components that are bolted together. The classification is used for designs that integrate the flange directly with the pipe wall to achieve a sealed connection.
[F16L 23/026] by welding -- F16L 23/026 refers to flanged pipe joints that are assembled by welding the flange faces together. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes → Flanged joints, and covers methods and configurations where welding provides the seal and mechanical strength of the joint.
[F16L 23/028] the flanges being held against a shoulder -- F16L 23/028 covers flanged pipe joints in which the flange is secured by pressing or fitting it against a shoulder on the adjoining pipe or fitting, rather than using bolts or clamps. The design relies on the shoulder to transmit axial loads and maintain sealing contact, often employed in compact or high-pressure applications where a simple, robust attachment is required. This subclass falls within the broader category of mechanical engineering pipe elements.
[F16L 23/0283] the collar being integral with the pipe -- F16L 23/0283 classifies flanged pipe joints in which the collar is formed as an integral part of the pipe, eliminating the need for a separate collar component. The entry addresses designs and manufacturing methods for such built-in collars within pipe flanged connections.

[F16L 23/032] characterised by the shape or composition of the flanges -- The CPC subclass F16L 23/032 covers flanged pipe joints whose defining feature is the shape or material of the flanges, describing design variations, profile forms and specific compositions of the flange components used to connect piping sections.
[F16L 23/036] characterised by the tensioning members, e.g. specially adapted bolts or C-clamps -- F16L 23/036 covers flanged pipe joints whose defining feature is the use of tensioning members such as specially adapted bolts or C-clamps to secure the flanges together. The classification addresses the design and arrangement of these securing elements to achieve the required joint strength and sealing performance. It falls within mechanical engineering, under engineering elements and units for pipes.
[F16L 23/04] the flanges being connected by members tensioned in the radial plane -- F16L 23/04 covers flanged pipe joints in which the flanges are joined by members that are stretched in the radial direction, such as bolts, rods, or tie-bars, to create a tensile preload across the joint. The classification applies to designs that rely on radial tension to maintain sealing and structural integrity of the connection.
[F16L 23/06] connected by toggle-action levers -- F16L 23/06 belongs to the CPC group for pipe flanged joints and covers joints in which the flanges are brought together and secured by toggle-action levers, using a lever mechanism that provides a quick opening and locking movement.
[F16L 23/08] connection by tangentially arranged pin and nut -- This subclass covers pipe flanged joints that are secured using a pin and nut positioned tangentially around the flange, allowing the fastening elements to be offset from the central axis of the joint. The arrangement provides a compact, easily assembled connection for mechanical engineering applications.
[F16L 23/10] with a pivoting or swinging pin -- F16L 23/10 classifies flanged pipe joints that incorporate a pivoting or swinging pin to permit angular adjustment or rotation of the connected sections, facilitating alignment and reducing stress in mechanical systems.
[F16L 23/12] specially adapted for particular pipes -- F16L 23/12 refers to flanged joints that are specially adapted for particular pipes, meaning the joint design, dimensions, sealing elements or attachment features are customized to suit the size, material, pressure rating or other specific characteristics of the pipe it will connect.
[F16L 23/125] with an internal or external coating -- F16L 23/125 covers flanged pipe joints that have an internal or external coating applied to protect against corrosion, wear or chemical attack. The coating may be polymeric, metallic or ceramic and is applied to the sealing surfaces of the flange.
[F16L 23/14] for rectangular pipes -- F16L 23/14 relates to flanged joints specially designed for rectangular pipes, covering the coupling, sealing and fastening arrangements suited to non-circular cross-sections. It includes configurations that enable secure connections and leak-tight performance for rectangular pipe profiles.
[F16L 23/16] characterised by the sealing means -- F16L 23/16 pertains to flanged pipe joints that are defined primarily by the type of sealing means used to achieve a leak-tight connection. It covers designs where gasket, O-ring, metal seal or other sealing elements are integral to the joint's function, distinguishing them from joints sealed solely by clamping or welding. The classification is used for mechanical engineering components involving pipe connections with specialized sealing mechanisms.
[F16L 23/162] the pipe ends abutting each other -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Joints where pipe ends are abutting each other with no extra sealing rings present, such that there is surface to surface contact between the flanges. [F16L 23/165] comprising a viscous mass, e.g. hardenable -- F16L 23/165 classifies flanged pipe joints where a viscous mass, such as a hardenable sealing compound, is placed in the joint gap to provide a leak-tight seal. The viscous material may cure or solidify after assembly, enhancing the joint's integrity.
[F16L 23/167] in connection with the appearance or detection of leaks -- F16L 23/167 covers inventions dealing with the appearance of or methods for detecting leaks in flanged pipe joints, including visual indicators, sensors, and design features that reveal or signal leakage. It is a subcategory of flanged joints within pipe engineering, focusing on leak-related monitoring and alarm functions.
[F16L 23/18] the sealing means being rings -- This subclass covers ring-type sealing elements used in flanged pipe joints to provide leak-tight connections, such as gasket rings, O-rings and similar devices. [F16L 23/20] made exclusively of metal -- F16L 23/20 covers pipe flanged joints that are made exclusively of metal. The subclass includes designs, manufacturing methods and applications of metal-only flanged connections in mechanical engineering, without any non-metal components.
[F16L 23/22] made exclusively of a material other than metal -- F16L 23/22 refers to flanged pipe joints that are constructed entirely from non-metallic materials such as polymers, ceramics or composite substances. [F16L 23/24] specially adapted for unequal expansion of the parts of the joint -- F16L 23/24 covers flanged pipe joints that incorporate features to allow uneven expansion of the connected parts, such as flexible gaskets, compensating sleeves or specially shaped flanges, ensuring a sealed connection despite differential temperature or pressure changes.
[F16L 25/00] Construction or details of pipe joints not provided for in, or of interest apart from, groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of pipe joints not provided for in, or of interest apart from, groups F16L13/00 - F16L23/00 , e.g. electrically conducting or insulating means.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Adjustable joints -> F16L27/00 - Joints with fluid cut-off means -> F16L29/00 - Couplings of the quick-acting type -> F16L37/00 - Joints or fittings for double-walled or multi-channel pipes or pipe assemblies -> F16L39/00 - Specially adapted to be made of plastics or to be used with pipes made of plastics -> F16L47/00 - Specially adapted for pipes of brittle material -> F16L49/00
[F16L 25/0009] Joints for pipes with a square or rectangular cross-section -- A pipe joint designed for non-circular conduits with a square or rectangular cross-section, covering construction features or details that are not allocated to other specific pipe-joint groups. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and the broader pipes category.
[F16L 25/0018] Abutment joints -- F16L 25/0018 covers abutment joints, a type of pipe joint where the ends of two pipe sections are pressed together without the use of additional sealing or locking elements. These joints are typically employed in temporary or low-pressure applications where simple alignment and load transfer are required. The classification addresses design features, materials, and methods for creating such connections.
[F16L 25/0027] Joints for pipes made of reinforced concrete -- F16L 25/0027 deals with the design, construction and detailing of joints used in reinforced-concrete pipes, covering connections, couplings and sealing methods that are not addressed in other pipe-joint groups. The entry applies to mechanical-engineering contexts where concrete pipe systems require specialised joint solutions.
[F16L 25/0036] Joints for corrugated pipes -- The CPC entry F16L 25/0036 covers fittings and coupling devices specifically designed to join corrugated pipes. It includes joint types such as flexible connectors, clamps, mechanical couplings, and sealing elements that accommodate the ribbed profile of corrugated tubing, used in fluid transport, drainage and protective conduit systems.
[F16L 25/0045] of the quick-acting type -- F16L 25/0045 covers pipe joints that operate by a quick-acting mechanism, such as springs, pistons or fast-acting clamps, enabling rapid connection or disconnection of pipe sections. These joints are used where fast assembly or disassembly is required and are not classified in more specific pipe-joint groups.
[F16L 25/0054] with specially shaped sealing rings -- F16L 25/0054 covers pipe joints where the sealing is achieved by sealing rings that have a non-cylindrical or otherwise specially shaped cross-section, intended to enhance sealing under pressure or misalignment. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and concerns construction details of joints not covered by other groups.
[F16L 25/0063] with two corrugated pipes being directly connected to each other -- F16L 25/0063 pertains to pipe joints in which two corrugated pipes are directly coupled to each other, covering the design, construction and sealing details of such direct connections without intermediate fittings.

[F16L 25/0081] Pipe joints comprising a liquid or fusible seal -- This subclass covers pipe joints that achieve sealing by using a liquid sealant that hardens or a fusible material that melts and solidifies to form a leak-tight connection. The design may involve injecting, pouring or surrounding the joint with the sealing medium for applications where conventional metal-to-metal or gasket seals are not specified.
[F16L 25/009] Combination of a quick-acting type coupling and a conventional one -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F16L 25/01] specially adapted for realising electrical conduction between the two pipe ends of the joint or between parts thereof -- F16L 25/01 covers pipe joints or joint parts that are specially designed to allow electrical conduction between the two pipe ends, such as for grounding, signal transmission or electro-static discharge. It applies to any construction or detail of a joint where the primary function includes providing a conductive path alongside the mechanical connection. This falls within the mechanical-engineering class for pipes and their assemblies.
[F16L 25/02] specially adapted for electrically insulating the two pipe ends of the joint from each other -- F16L 25/02 refers to pipe-joint constructions that are specially adapted to electrically insulate the two pipe ends from each other, preventing electrical continuity across the joint. It is classified under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → pipes.
[F16L 25/021] for screw-threaded joints -- F16L 25/021 relates to pipe joints that are assembled using screw-threaded connections, addressing the construction and design details of such joints not covered in other groups. It concerns the form, dimensions and securing features of threaded pipe fittings used in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16L 25/023] for joints in which sealing surfaces are pressed together by means of a member, e.g. a swivel nut, screwed on or into one of the joint parts -- This subclass deals with pipe joints whose sealing surfaces are pressed together by a separate member, such as a swivel nut that is screwed onto or into one of the joint parts, creating a pressure-tight connection without additional sealing material. It covers designs where the compressive action of the member ensures leak-free sealing.
[F16L 25/025] for joints with sleeve or socket -- F16L 25/025 relates to pipe joint constructions that employ a sleeve or socket to connect pipe sections. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and specifically covers details of pipe joints not addressed in other groups. The code applies to designs where a cylindrical sleeve or socket provides a sealed or removable connection between pipes.
[F16L 25/026] for flanged joints -- F16L 25/026 classifies pipe joint constructions that use flanged connections, addressing the design, sealing and assembly features of flanged joints for pipe systems. It is relevant for high-pressure or corrosion-resistant applications where a flange provides a detachable, bolted connection between pipe sections.
[F16L 25/028] for branching pipes, for joining pipes to walls -- This subclass covers pipe-branching and wall-attachment arrangements, such as fittings, brackets, clamps or other structures that join a pipe to a wall or create a branch from a main line. It includes construction details of pipe joints not classified in other groups.
[F16L 25/03] in non-disconnectable pipe joints -- The F16L 25/03 subclass deals with the design and construction of pipe joints that are not intended to be disconnected, covering details not listed elsewhere in the mechanical engineering hierarchy. It includes permanent or welded connections where the joint cannot be taken apart for maintenance or re-assembly.
[F16L 25/04] comprising a collar or ring having a threaded pin rigid with the pipe-encircling member -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F16L 25/06] comprising radial locking means -- F16L 25/06 relates to pipe joints that incorporate radial locking means, which are outward-directed components that engage around the pipe circumference to secure sections together and prevent axial separation. The classification applies to mechanical-engineering pipe connections where such radial locking elements are used, either as integral parts of the joint or as added devices.
[F16L 25/065] the locking means being actuated by radial screws -- F16L 25/065 concerns pipe joint constructions where the locking mechanism is driven by screws that act radially outward, pushing a locking element into place to secure the connection. This design enables the joint to be tightened and retained without additional external clamps or fasteners.
[F16L 25/08] in the form of screws, nails or the like -- F16L 25/08 covers pipe-joining elements that are applied as fasteners such as screws, nails or similar devices. It pertains to constructions or details of pipe joints that are not otherwise classified, describing components used to secure pipe sections where a specific joint type is not provided for in other groups.
[F16L 25/10] Sleeveless joints between two pipes, one being introduced into the other -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F16L 25/12] Joints for pipes being spaced apart axially -- F16L 25/12 relates to pipe joints that are intentionally spaced apart along the pipe axis, including couplings, expansion joints or supports that keep the pipes aligned while permitting an axial gap for movement, thermal expansion or installation tolerances.
[F16L 25/14] Joints for pipes of different diameters or cross-section -- F16L 25/14 relates to pipe joints that connect members of different diameters or cross-sectional shapes, providing a secure transition between mismatched pipe sizes or profiles. It includes various mechanical designs such as reducers, expanders and special couplings used in pipe systems where dimensions or shapes change.
[F16L 27/00] Adjustable joints -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Couplings of the quick-acting type -> F16L37/50 - For double-walled or multi-channel pipes or pipe assemblies -> F16L39/04 - Adjustable joints specially adapted to be made of plastics or to be used with pipes made of plastics; Joints allowing movement specially adapted to be made of plastics or to be used with pipes made of plastics -> F16L47/18 - Adjustable joints specially adapted to be used with pipes of brittle material; Joints allowing movement specially adapted to be used with pipes of brittle material -> F16L49/08
[F16L 27/02] Universal joints, i.e. with mechanical connection allowing angular movement or adjustment of the axes of the parts in any direction -- F16L 27/02 classifies universal joints, also called Cardan joints, which provide a mechanical connection that permits the axes of connected parts to rotate relative to each other in any direction. These joints are used in drive shafts, machinery, and other applications requiring angular displacement or alignment adjustment while transmitting torque.
[F16L 27/023] Universal and rotating joints -- F16L 27/023 covers universal and rotating joints that connect pipe sections while allowing angular or rotary misalignment. These joints transmit torque or fluid under varying angles, often featuring splined, ball-bearing or flexible elements to accommodate movement in mechanical systems.
[F16L 27/026] Universal and axially displaceable joints -- F16L 27/026 covers universal and axially displaceable joints used in pipe systems, allowing angular adjustment and axial movement while maintaining a sealed connection. These joints are designed to accommodate misalignment, thermal expansion, or vibration in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16L 27/04] with partly-spherical engaging surfaces -- F16L 27/04 denotes adjustable pipe joints that incorporate partly-spherical engaging surfaces, enabling limited angular movement and self-centering alignment between connected pipe sections. These surfaces provide a hybrid of spherical and flat contact, offering both flexibility and precise positioning in mechanical engineering piping systems.
[F16L 27/042] comprising two pipes normally at right angles to each other -- F16L 27/042 relates to adjustable pipe joints in which two pipes are arranged at right angles to each other, enabling angular or positional changes while maintaining a sealed connection. The design is used in mechanical engineering for flexible routing of fluid or gas lines and often includes mechanisms for tightening, loosening, or pivoting the joint.
[F16L 27/044] specially adapted for tubing between vehicles -- F16L 27/044 pertains to adjustable pipe joints that are specially adapted for connecting tubing between vehicles, providing a flexible yet secure coupling that can accommodate movement and vibration during operation.
[F16L 27/047] held in place by a screwed member having an internal spherical surface -- **Definition:** This place covers:  joints held together with a swivel nut
[F16L 27/053] held in place by bolts passing through flanges -- This CPC subgroup defines adjustable pipe joints that are secured by bolts passing through the mating flanges, permitting angular or axial adjustment while keeping the connection sealed. It applies to mechanical-engineering pipe systems where precise alignment or compensation for movement is required.
[F16L 27/06] with special sealing means between the engaging surfaces -- F16L 27/06 covers adjustable pipe joints that include special sealing means positioned between the engaging surfaces to prevent leakage when the joint is set or moved. These joints are used in mechanical engineering for pipe systems where precise alignment and reliable sealing are required.
[F16L 27/067] the sealing means being actuated by the medium pressure -- F16L 27/067 relates to sealing means in adjustable pipe joints that are actuated by the medium pressure, enabling the seal to engage or release automatically in response to changes in the fluid pressure within the system.
[F16L 27/073] one of the cooperating surfaces forming the sealing means -- The CPC subclass F16L 27/073 concerns an individual surface that mates with another to create a seal in an adjustable pipe joint. It covers the design, geometry, and material of that sealing face, which may be flat, conical, or otherwise shaped to ensure tight, leak-proof connections in pipe systems. This element is part of the broader category of mechanical engineering pipe components.
[F16L 27/08] allowing adjustment or movement only about the axis of one pipe -- F16L 27/08 covers pipe joints that permit adjustment or movement solely around the longitudinal axis of a single pipe, enabling axial rotation or alignment while the pipe remains fixed in other directions.
[F16L 27/0804] the fluid passing axially from one joint element to another -- F16L 27/0804 relates to adjustable pipe joints in which fluid flows axially from one joint element into the next. The classification covers designs that allow longitudinal movement while maintaining a sealed passage for the fluid, typical in modular piping systems.
[F16L 27/0808] the joint elements extending coaxially for some distance from their point of separation -- F16L 27/0808 covers adjustable pipe joints in which the joining elements extend coaxially beyond the point where the two pipe sections separate, allowing fine-tuning of alignment and sealing over a short distance.
[F16L 27/0812] with slide bearings -- F16L 27/0812 relates to adjustable pipe joints that incorporate slide bearings, enabling linear movement of the pipe sections while maintaining a seal. Such joints are used where precise alignment or positional adjustment of piping is required.
[F16L 27/0816] having radial sealing -- F16L 27/0816 covers adjustable pipe joints that incorporate a sealing mechanism acting radially around the joint to prevent fluid escape. The design provides a circumferential seal that can be tightened or released to accommodate alignment or thermal changes. It falls under the mechanical engineering category for pipes and adjustable joints.
[F16L 27/082] having axial sealing -- Adjustable pipe joints that incorporate an axial sealing arrangement, where the sealing surfaces are oriented along the joint's axis to prevent fluid leakage while permitting axial movement of the connected pipe sections. This class covers designs that combine adjustability with a sealing mechanism aligned with the pipe's longitudinal direction.
[F16L 27/0824] with ball or roller bearings -- F16L 27/0824 covers adjustable pipe joints that incorporate ball or roller bearings to facilitate rotation or angular movement while maintaining a sealed connection, falling under the mechanical-engineering class for pipe elements and units.
[F16L 27/0828] having radial bearings -- F16L 27/0828 designates adjustable pipe joints that incorporate radial bearings, allowing rotation and axial movement while keeping the pipe sections aligned. These joints are used in mechanical engineering to connect and finely position pipe elements.
[F16L 27/0832] having axial bearings -- F16L 27/0832 refers to adjustable pipe joints that incorporate axial bearings, allowing longitudinal movement while keeping the joint aligned. The axial bearings support axial loads and accommodate thermal expansion or vibration damping in mechanical-engineering piping systems.
[F16L 27/0837] the joint elements being bends -- F16L 27/0837 covers pipe adjustable joints where the joint elements are formed as bends. It relates to mechanical-engineering components that allow angular movement while maintaining a sealed connection, typically used in plumbing, fluid-transfer or hydraulic systems.
[F16L 27/0841] forming an angle of less than 90 degrees -- F16L 27/0841 refers to adjustable pipe joints that are designed to form an acute angle, i.e., an angle of less than 90 degrees, between connected pipe sections. These fittings enable precise directional changes in piping systems while allowing for alignment adjustments, and are classed under mechanical engineering pipe elements.
[F16L 27/0845] forming an angle of 90 degrees -- F16L 27/0845 designates adjustable pipe joints that form a 90-degree angle. This subclass covers mechanisms enabling the angle between pipe sections to be set or locked at a right angle, often using pivoting or sliding components, and is used in mechanical engineering for routing fluids, gases or cables.
[F16L 27/0849] the fluid being turned through an angle when passing from one joint element to another -- The classification covers pipe adjustable joints in which the conveyed fluid is turned through a defined angle as it passes from one joint element to the next. It applies to designs that change the flow direction within a joint, typically using hinged, ball-type or swivel mechanisms to accommodate angular displacement while maintaining a sealed passage.
[F16L 27/0853] with spherical hinge -- This class covers pipe joints that can be adjusted and incorporate a spherical hinge, allowing angular movement in multiple planes while maintaining a seal. It applies to mechanical engineering elements used in piping systems where flexible alignment is required.
[F16L 27/0857] with hinge and bellows sealing -- F16L 27/0857 covers adjustable pipe joints that incorporate a hinge and a bellows to provide a flexible, leak-tight seal. These joints are used in mechanical engineering systems where pipe alignment must be varied while maintaining pressure resistance.

[F16L 27/0865] between vehicles -- A pipe joint designed to connect sections of tubing mounted on separate vehicles, allowing relative angular, longitudinal or transverse movement while maintaining a sealed fluid path. Such adjustable joints accommodate vehicle dynamics, vibration and misalignment, commonly used for fuel, hydraulic or pneumatic lines linking a tractor and its trailer.
[F16L 27/087] Joints with radial fluid passages -- F16L 27/087 pertains to pipe joints that incorporate radial passages for the flow of fluids, enabling the transfer of liquids or gases across the joint while allowing adjustment or movement. Such joints are used in adjustable pipe systems where a sealed, configurable connection with side-ward fluid channels is required.
[F16L 27/093] of the "banjo" type, i.e. pivoting right-angle couplings -- F16L 27/093 covers adjustable pipe joints of the "banjo" type, i.e., pivoting right-angle couplings that connect a pipe at a 90° angle while allowing rotation to align or compensate for misalignment.
[F16L 27/10] comprising a flexible connection only -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[F16L 27/1004] introduced in exhaust pipes for hot gases -- F16L 27/1004 relates to adjustable joints incorporated into exhaust pipes that carry hot gases, providing thermal-expansion compensation and facilitating disassembly for maintenance. These joints create a secure yet movable connection within high-temperature flow channels.
[F16L 27/1008] comprising a swivel nut or collar engaging the pipe -- This subclass covers pipe connections that use a swivel nut or collar to engage the pipe, providing an adjustable joint that can rotate while maintaining a sealed connection. It applies to mechanical-engineering pipe systems where angular adjustment of the joint is required.
[F16L 27/1012] Flanged joints -- F16L 27/1012 covers flanged joints, a type of adjustable pipe connection where two pipe ends are fitted with matching flanges that are bolted or clamped together to provide alignment, pressure resistance and easy disassembly. These joints are used in mechanical engineering systems to join pipelines, valves or equipment, allowing secure sealing while permitting maintenance or re-configuration.
[F16L 27/1017] Joints with sleeve or socket -- F16L 27/1017 covers pipe joints that employ a sleeve or socket to connect adjustable sections. The sleeve or socket encircles the pipe ends, allowing rotation, angular displacement or length variation while maintaining a seal. Such joints are used in mechanical systems where flexible alignment of piping is required.
[F16L 27/1021] comprising an intermediate resilient element, e.g. a ring -- A pipe joint that can be adjusted and includes an intermediate resilient element, such as a ring, positioned between the connecting parts. The resilient component absorbs movement, maintains sealing, and provides flexibility to accommodate thermal expansion or misalignment in piping systems.
[F16L 27/1025] Abutment joints -- F16L 27/1025 covers abutment joints, a type of adjustable pipe joint that connects sections while allowing for axial load transfer and compensation of misalignment. These joints typically feature movable elements-such as bolts, wedges or sliding plates-to fine-tune the fit and maintain sealing under varying pressures. They are used in mechanical-engineering piping systems where precise positioning and load-bearing capability are required.
[F16L 27/103] in which a flexible element, e.g. a rubber-metal laminate, which undergoes constraints consisting of shear and flexure, is sandwiched between partly curved surfaces -- F16L 27/103 pertains to adjustable pipe joints where a flexible element, such as a rubber-metal laminate, is sandwiched between partially curved surfaces and is subjected to shear and flexure constraints.
[F16L 27/107] the ends of the pipe being interconnected by a flexible sleeve -- This subclass covers pipe connections in which the ends of the pipes are joined by a flexible sleeve that allows for adjustment, misalignment or movement. It is situated under adjustable joints within the broader category of engineering elements and units for pipes.
[F16L 27/108] the sleeve having the form of a bellows with only one corrugation -- F16L 27/108 pertains to sleeves shaped as bellows that contain just one corrugation, used as adjustable joints in pipe systems. The single-wave design provides axial flexibility and sealing while accommodating misalignment or thermal expansion in mechanical engineering installations.
[F16L 27/1085] the bellows being externally or internally reinforced -- This subclass covers adjustable pipe joints that use a bellows whose strength is increased by reinforcement applied to its outer surface or its interior. The external or internal reinforcement improves the bellows' pressure resistance and fatigue life in mechanical-engineering pipe systems.
[F16L 27/11] the sleeve having the form of a bellows with multiple corrugations -- A bellows-type sleeve with multiple corrugations is used as an adjustable joint in pipe systems, allowing axial, angular or radial displacement while maintaining a sealed connection. Its corrugated shape provides flexibility and can absorb thermal expansion, vibration or misalignment between pipe sections. This element falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, specifically pipes, and is classified as F16L 27/11.
[F16L 27/111] the bellows being reinforced -- F16L 27/111 covers adjustable pipe joints where the bellows component is reinforced, typically by external sleeves, ribs, or embedded fabric, to increase stiffness, pressure rating, or fatigue life while retaining flexibility for alignment or thermal expansion.
[F16L 27/113] the ends of the pipe being interconnected by a rigid sleeve -- F16L 27/113 covers pipe connections where the pipe ends are joined using a rigid sleeve that locks the sections together, typically forming an adjustable joint. The sleeve provides a solid, non-flexible link that aligns and secures the pipes, allowing for precise positioning while maintaining mechanical integrity.
[F16L 27/1133] the sleeve being longitudinally divided -- The subclass F16L 27/1133 covers pipe joints in which the connecting sleeve is cut lengthwise so that it can be opened, adjusted and then re-closed to secure the joint. The longitudinal split allows the sleeve to expand or rotate for alignment, tightening, or disassembly of the pipe connection.
[F16L 27/1136] the sleeve comprising a screwed member -- A sleeve that incorporates a threaded (screwed) component for connecting or adjusting pipe sections, providing a means to fine-tune alignment or length while maintaining a secure joint. This element is used in adjustable pipe joints to achieve precise positioning and sealing within mechanical-engineering applications.
[F16L 27/12] allowing substantial longitudinal adjustment or movement -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Joint allowing movement by use of screw-threads -> F16L15/02
[F16L 27/125] having longitudinal and rotary movement -- The CPC code F16L 27/125 designates pipe-joint assemblies that permit simultaneous longitudinal (axial) and rotary (angular) movement. These adjustable joints accommodate misalignment, thermal expansion or vibration while maintaining a sealed connection in mechanical-engineering pipe systems.
[F16L 27/127] with means for locking the longitudinal adjustment or movement in the final mounted position -- The subclass F16L 27/127 pertains to pipe joints that incorporate a mechanism to lock the longitudinal adjustment or movement once the joint is installed in its final position. It includes devices such as clamps, screws, locking pins or similar means that secure the joint against further sliding or shifting.
[F16L 27/1273] by quick-acting means -- F16L 27/1273 defines adjustable pipe joints that are operated by quick-acting means, such as pneumatic, hydraulic, electric or spring mechanisms, allowing rapid connection or release. The subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and adjustable joints, and addresses fittings where the adjustment is achieved without manual turning.
[F16L 27/1274] by means of a swivel nut -- Adjustable pipe joints that are secured or released by rotating a swivel nut, which allows angular or positional adjustment of the connected pipe sections while maintaining a sealed connection. The swivel nut provides a compact, reusable means of locking the joint in the desired orientation without requiring separate fastening elements. This solution is used in mechanical-engineering pipe systems where precise alignment and easy re-adjustment are required.
[F16L 27/1275] by means of at least an external threaded bolt -- The CPC subclass F16L 27/1275 covers adjustable pipe joints whose positioning or alignment is achieved by means of at least one external threaded bolt. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the engineering elements and units for pipes, and pertains to joints that can be tightened or loosened through the external bolt to modify pipe geometry or pressure resistance.
[F16L 27/12751] the threaded bolt extending longitudinally -- F16L 27/12751 classifies the longitudinally extending threaded bolt used in adjustable pipe joints. The element provides a means to fine-tune pipe alignment and length by turning the bolt, allowing controlled expansion or contraction of the joint. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, adjustable joints.
[F16L 29/00] Joints with fluid cut-off means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The fluid cut off means must be a part of, or functionally associated with the connection, not just a valve in a pipe, otherwise its classified only with the valves F16K  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Couplings of the quick-acting type with cut-off means -> F16L37/28
[F16L 29/002] joints with taps -- A joint with taps is a pipe connection that includes one or more threaded or welded openings, allowing additional fittings, valves, or instruments to be attached while maintaining the ability to isolate flow. It provides a fluid cut-off point and branching capability within a piping system.
[F16L 29/005] joints with cut-off devices which can be perforated -- These are pipe joints equipped with cut-off devices that can be deliberately perforated to allow fluid passage or sampling, such as perforated plugs, valves or sealing elements that are pierced after installation. The design integrates the shut-off function with a controlled opening in the joint.
[F16L 29/007] Joints with cut-off devices controlled separately -- F16L 29/007 classifies pipe joints that include cut-off devices which are operated independently of the fluid flow, such as valves or shut-off mechanisms that can be actuated separately from the joint itself.
[F16L 29/02] with a cut-off device in one of the two pipe ends, the cut-off device being automatically opened when the coupling is applied -- A pipe joint that incorporates a cut-off device on one end, which remains closed to stop fluid flow until the coupling is engaged, at which point the device automatically opens to allow passage.
[F16L 29/04] with a cut-off device in each of the two pipe ends, the cut-off devices being automatically opened when the coupling is applied -- This CPC subclass covers pipe couplings that have a shut-off valve at each end, which remain closed until the two pipe sections are joined, at which point the valves open automatically to allow fluid flow. It is intended for applications where automatic isolation of the pipeline is required during installation or maintenance.
[F16L 3/00] Supports for pipes, cables or protective tubing, e.g. hangers, holders, clamps, cleats, clips, brackets -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Anchors for holding pipes on or under the ground -> F16L1/06 - Noise absorbers in the form of specially adapted hangers or supports -> F16L55/035 - Supports for insulated bodies -> F16L59/12

[F16L 3/1236] the member being of a material other than metal -- F16L 3/1236 covers pipe-supporting elements such as hangers, brackets, clamps or clips whose main body is made from a non-metallic material, for example plastic, composite, wood or ceramic, rather than metal.

[F16L 3/13] and engaging it by snap action -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Supports comprising a member substantially surrounding the pipe, cable or protective tubing, with a pair of arms moved automatically to closed position by overcenter spring -> F16L3/1203
[F16L 3/133] and hanging from a pendant -- The subclass F16L 3/133 covers devices that support or secure pipes, cables or protective tubing by hanging them from a pendant. It includes hangers, brackets, clamps, cleats and similar holders that attach to a pendant point for vertical suspension.
[F16L 3/137] and consisting of a flexible band -- The subclass F16L 3/137 covers pipe, cable or protective-tubing supports that are made from a flexible band. These supports wrap around the conduit and are fastened by tightening the band, providing an adjustable and often removable means of holding the pipe in place.
[F16L 3/14] Hangers in the form of bands or chains -- F16L 3/14 designates pipe support devices that are hangers shaped as bands or chains, used to suspend or secure pipes, cables or protective tubing to structures. These flexible or looped elements attach to anchors or brackets, providing adjustable support and spacing.
[F16L 3/16] with special provision allowing movement of the pipe -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Supports for pipe, with special provision allowing the pipe to move locally, e.g. within the support.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Supports for supporting or guiding pipes, cables or protective tubing, between relatively movable points -> F16L3/01 - Supporting pipes or cables inside other pipes or sleeves -> F16L7/00
[F16L 3/18] allowing movement in axial direction -- The CPC subclass F16L 3/18 pertains to pipe, cable or protective-tube supports-such as brackets, clamps, clips, hangers and cleats-that are designed to allow axial movement of the supported element, accommodating expansion, contraction or vibration while maintaining positioning.
[F16L 3/20] allowing movement in transverse direction -- This subgroup of F16L covers pipe, cable or protective-tube supports such as brackets, clamps, hangers and clips that are designed to allow side-to-side or transverse movement of the line, accommodating thermal expansion, vibration, or alignment changes.
[F16L 3/202] the transverse movement being converted to a rotational movement -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Movement of the pipes being hydraulically or electrically controlled -> F16L3/215
[F16L 3/205] having supporting springs -- F16L 3/205 defines pipe-support devices that employ supporting springs, covering hangers, clamps, brackets or other holders for pipes, cables or protective tubing where spring elements provide flexible or resilient support. The entry resides in the mechanical-engineering branch under engineering elements and units, specifically within the pipes subgroup.
[F16L 3/2053] the axis of each spring being parallel with the direction of the movement of the pipe -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F16L 3/2056] the axis of at least one spring being oblique or perpendicular to the direction of the movement of the pipe -- F16L 3/2056 concerns pipe-supporting devices that incorporate at least one spring whose axis is positioned obliquely or perpendicular to the pipe's direction of movement, such as hangers or brackets that allow flexible or damping action while maintaining pipe alignment. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and specifically the subcategory of supports for pipes, cables or protective tubing.
[F16L 3/21] providing constant supporting spring force -- F16L 3/21 covers pipe support devices that use a spring to apply a constant supporting force, compensating for thermal expansion, vibration or load changes. Such spring-loaded hangers, brackets or clamps maintain steady pressure on pipes, cables or protective tubing, ensuring reliable positioning while allowing movement.
[F16L 3/215] the movement being hydraulically or electrically controlled -- This subclass includes pipe, cable or protective-tubing supports-such as hangers, clamps, brackets or clips-whose positioning or movement is regulated by hydraulic or electric actuation, enabling controlled adjustment, alignment or compensation.
[F16L 3/217] hydraulically -- F16L 3/217 covers pipe support devices that are actuated or adjusted by hydraulic means, such as hangers, clamps, brackets or other holders that use fluid pressure to maintain or alter pipe position. These hydraulic supports are used to compensate for thermal expansion, vibration, or load changes in piping systems.
[F16L 3/22] specially adapted for supporting a number of parallel pipes at intervals -- F16L 3/22 covers pipe-supporting devices that are specifically designed to hold several parallel pipes at regular intervals. These devices include specialised brackets, clamps or cleats that maintain consistent spacing and alignment of multiple pipes or cable bundles.
[F16L 3/221] having brackets connected together by means of a common support -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Support for supporting a number of parallel pipes at intervals, wherein the brackets and support are originally two different parts.
