[F16L 3/23] for a bundle of pipes or a plurality of pipes placed side by side in contact with each other -- F16L 3/23 covers supports designed for a bundle of pipes or a plurality of pipes placed side by side in contact, such as hangers, brackets, clamps or similar elements that hold the group together.
[F16L 3/233] by means of a flexible band -- F16L 3/233 covers pipe-support devices that secure a pipe, cable or protective tubing by means of a flexible band, such as straps, bands or similar elastic elements that wrap around the pipe and are fastened to a holder, clamp, bracket or other supporting structure.
[F16L 3/2332] having a single plastic locking barb -- A pipe support made of plastic that secures a pipe, cable or protective tubing by means of a single locking barb which engages the member to prevent movement.
[F16L 3/2334] the barb having a plurality of serrations -- F16L 3/2334 covers pipe or cable supports that incorporate a barb equipped with several serrations to grip the conveyed element. The serrated barb provides a secure, self-locking attachment, typically used in clamps, brackets or hangers to prevent slipping.
[F16L 3/2336] having two or more locking barbs  F16L3/2338  takes precedence -- The subclass covers pipe-support elements such as hangers, clamps or brackets that retain a pipe by means of two or more locking barbs. It is a refinement of the "supports for pipes, cables or protective tubing" group and is superseded by the related subclass F16L 3/2338.
[F16L 3/2338] having at least one metal locking barb -- F16L 3/2338 covers pipe, cable or protective-tube supports that include at least one metal locking barb to grip and retain the conveyed element. The barb provides a positive, non-slipping connection, typically used in hangers, clamps, brackets or similar devices for mechanical-engineering applications.
[F16L 3/237] for two pipes -- F16L 3/237 covers devices that simultaneously support two pipes, such as brackets, clamps, or holders designed to secure the pipes in a fixed position or to maintain spacing between them. The apparatus may be mounted on a wall, frame, or other structure.
[F16L 3/24] with special member for attachment to profiled girders -- This subclass covers supports for pipes, cables or protective tubing that include a special member designed to fasten the support directly to profiled girders, such as brackets, clamps or clips.
[F16L 3/243] the special member being inserted in the profiled girder -- This subclass covers a special member that is inserted into a profiled girder to provide a mounting point for pipes, cables or protective tubing. The member functions as a built-in hanger, bracket or clip integrated within the girder profile, allowing secure attachment without separate external fixtures. It is used in mechanical engineering applications where space or weight constraints favor internal supports.
[F16L 3/2431] the special member being inserted and subsequently rotated to a limited extent -- The subclass covers pipe-support devices in which a special component is fitted into the holder and then turned only a short distance to lock or adjust the pipe, such as a rotating clamp, sleeve or insert that secures the conduit after limited rotation.
[F16L 3/245] the special member embracing the entire profiled girder -- F16L 3/245 relates to a special structural member that encloses an entire profiled girder, acting as a support element for pipes, cables or protective tubing. The member integrates functions such as holding, clamping or bracketing the girder to provide continuous, uniform support along its length. This solution is used where a single component can replace multiple conventional clamps or brackets.
[F16L 3/26] specially adapted for supporting the pipes all along their length, e.g. pipe channels or ducts -- F16L 3/26 relates to pipe-support devices that are adapted to hold a pipe, cable or protective tubing continuously along its length, such as pipe channels, ducts or elongated brackets. These fittings distribute the load over the full span of the pipe rather than at isolated points.
[F16L 33/00] Arrangements for connecting hoses to rigid members -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Connecting arrangements or other fittings specially adapted to be made of plastics or to be used with pipes made of plastics -> F16L47/00
[F16L 33/003] comprising elements arranged in the hose walls -- F16L 33/003 covers hoses whose walls incorporate reinforcing or functional elements such as cords, fibers, metal strips or ribs that are arranged within the hose material itself. These embedded components give the hose added strength, flexibility or sealing capability and are used where hoses must be securely joined to rigid fittings.
[F16L 33/006] for hoses of plastics other than artificial rubber -- F16L 33/006 classifies arrangements that join hoses to rigid components when the hoses are made of plastic materials other than artificial rubber, falling under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → pipes. It covers designs and fittings specifically intended for non-rubber plastic hoses.
[F16L 33/01] specially adapted for hoses having a multi-layer wall -- This subclass covers pipe fittings and coupling arrangements that are specifically designed to attach to hoses whose walls consist of multiple layers, such as reinforcement and inner fluid-tight layers. The designs address sealing, support and pressure handling for the composite structure of the hose.
[F16L 33/02] Hose-clips -- F16L 33/02 pertains to hose-clips, devices that fasten a flexible hose to a rigid component. These fittings clamp, lock or otherwise secure the hose at connection points in hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid-handling systems, ensuring a reliable seal and preventing disconnection.
[F16L 33/021] with the ends bent around each other -- F16L 33/021 covers hose-to-rigid-member connections in which the hose ends are bent around each other to form the joint. This arrangement is used to secure flexible hoses to fixed pipework or other solid components, providing a mechanically interlocked seal in pipe systems.
[F16L 33/023] fixed by bending one end of the strap -- The F16L 33/023 arrangement secures a hose to a rigid pipe by bending one end of a metal strap around the pipe, creating a fixed clamp that holds the hose without extra fasteners. This simple method provides a reliable, leak-free connection commonly used in hydraulic and pneumatic lines.
[F16L 33/025] tightened by deforming radially extending loops or folds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. Oetiker ears
[F16L 33/03] Self-locking elastic clips -- F16L 33/03 covers self-locking elastic clips designed for securing hoses to rigid pipe fittings, providing a simple, reusable means of connection without additional fasteners. These clips deform elastically to grip the hose and lock in place, facilitating quick assembly and reliable sealing in pipe systems.
[F16L 33/035] fixed by means of teeth or hooks -- The subclass F16L 33/035 covers pipe-to-rigid-member connections in which the hose is secured by interlocking teeth or hook elements. These devices rely on a toothed or hooked geometry to lock the hose in place, providing a firm, releasable fix without additional fasteners.
[F16L 33/04] tightened by tangentially-arranged threaded pin and nut -- F16L 33/04 covers pipe-type hose connections where the hose is secured to a rigid component using a threaded pin and nut that are arranged tangentially around the joint, providing a tightened, leak-free seal. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and specifically the pipes category for arrangements linking hoses to fixed members.
[F16L 33/06] in which the threaded pin is rigid with the hose-encircling member -- The CPC subclass F16L 33/06 covers arrangements where a rigid threaded pin is used to connect a hose-encircling member to a fixed component. It relates to mechanical engineering pipe systems in which the threaded pin, fixed in the hose-encircling part, provides a secure, non-flexible attachment to the hose.
[F16L 33/08] in which a worm coacts with a part of the hose-encircling member that is toothed like a worm-wheel -- F16L 33/08 covers hose-to-rigid-member connections where a worm gear engages a toothed portion of the hose-encircling member, acting like a worm wheel to secure or adjust the hose. This arrangement provides a precise, self-locking attachment that can be tightened or released by rotating the worm element.
[F16L 33/085] with a scroll-type screw -- F16L 33/085 pertains to pipe-fitting arrangements that join flexible hoses to rigid members by means of a scroll-type screw, a fastener with a spiral groove that provides secure, adjustable attachment and sealing of the hose to the fixed component.
[F16L 33/10] with a substantially-radial tightening member -- F16L 33/10 covers arrangements for joining flexible hoses to fixed pipes or other rigid components where the tightening element extends substantially radially outward from the connection zone, such as radial clamps, rings or similar devices that secure the hose by pressing outward around its circumference.
[F16L 33/12] with a pivoted or swinging tightening or securing member, e.g. toggle lever -- F16L 33/12 classifies pipe-or-hose connection arrangements that secure the hose to a rigid member using a pivoted or swinging tightening element, such as a toggle lever, to provide adjustable clamping and easy release.
[F16L 33/14] with a taping-bolt, i.e. winding up the end of the hose-encircling member -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. fastening means working like a sardine-can opener
[F16L 33/16] with sealing or securing means using fluid pressure -- F16L 33/16 pertains to pipe-hose connections in which fluid pressure is employed to create a seal or to lock the hose onto a rigid fitting. The arrangement typically uses the pressure of the conveyed fluid to expand a sealing element or to activate a clamping mechanism, ensuring a leak-tight and secure joint.
[F16L 33/18] characterised by the use of additional sealing means -- F16L 33/18 classifies hose-to-rigid-member fittings that incorporate extra sealing means such as O-rings, gaskets, or secondary seals to enhance leak-tightness. The code applies to arrangements where the connection is made by a coupling, adapter or similar device that adds these sealing elements beyond the basic joint. It is used for mechanical-engineering pipe systems requiring reliable, sealed hose connections.
[F16L 33/20] Undivided rings, sleeves, or like members contracted on the hose or expanded inside the hose by means of tools -- F16L 33/20 pertains to undivided rings, sleeves or similar members that are either contracted onto a hose or expanded inside it by using tooling, serving as fittings for joining hoses to rigid components. These elements are applied without splitting the hose and are commonly used in fluid or pneumatic systems.
[F16L 33/207] only a sleeve being contracted on the hose -- F16L 33/207 covers the use of a sleeve that is contracted, typically by crimping, onto a hose to provide a secure connection between the flexible hose and a rigid member. The sleeve acts as an intermediate element in pipe and hose assemblies, ensuring tight sealing and mechanical strength.
[F16L 33/2071] the sleeve being a separate connecting member -- This CPC subclass covers pipe-to-hose connections that use a separate sleeve as the joining element. The sleeve is an independent component that fits over the hose and the rigid member to secure the connection, rather than being formed as part of either part.
[F16L 33/2073] directly connected to the rigid member -- The subclass F16L 33/2073 pertains to hose-to-rigid-member connections that are made without intermediate fitting, i.e., the hose is attached directly to a fixed pipe, arm, or other solid component. It includes the means of securing, sealing or anchoring the hose directly onto the rigid part, such as integral clamps, bonding or embossing methods, and covers the associated design features that enable a direct, leak-tight attachment.
[F16L 33/2075] by quick acting -- F16L 33/2075 covers quick-acting arrangements for joining hoses to rigid pipe members, such as snap-fit, cam-lock, or push-pull couplings that enable rapid connection and disconnection without tools. These devices are used in mechanical-engineering piping systems to simplify maintenance and reduce downtime.
[F16L 33/2076] by plastic deformation -- F16L 33/2076 covers pipe-and-hose connections in which the hose is joined to a rigid member by plastically deforming one or both parts to create a secure joint, such as crimping, swaging or heat-forming, without using separate fasteners. This method relies on permanent shape change to achieve a leak-tight, mechanically robust attachment.
[F16L 33/2078] connected to the rigid member via an intermediate element -- The code covers hose-to-rigid-member connections that employ an intermediate element, such as a coupling, reducer, sleeve or similar fitting, to join the flexible hose to a solid pipe or component, providing isolation, pressure adaptation or alignment between the two parts.
[F16L 33/213] only a sleeve being expanded inside the hose -- The subclass covers connections in which a sleeve is placed inside a hose and then expanded-by mechanical, thermal or hydraulic means-to secure the hose to a rigid member, forming a fluid-tight joint without separate clamps or fittings.
[F16L 33/22] with means not mentioned in the preceding groups for gripping the hose between inner and outer parts -- F16L 33/22 covers hose-to-rigid-member connections that use a gripping mechanism not listed in earlier groups, where the hose is clamped between an inner and an outer component. The subclass includes novel clamp designs that secure the hose without additional fittings.
[F16L 33/221] the external piece comprising segments hingedly connected to an interior part -- This subclass pertains to the outer element of a hose-to-rigid-member connector that consists of several segments linked by hinges to an interior part, allowing flexible attachment and movement while securing the hose to a fixed component.
[F16L 33/222] the external piece comprising segments pressed against the hose by wedge shaped elements -- F16L 33/222 covers a coupling component whose outer piece is formed of several segments pressed against the hose by wedge-shaped elements, forming a secure link between a flexible hose and a rigid pipe or fitting. This arrangement is employed in mechanical engineering to provide reliable hose-to-rigid-member connections.
[F16L 33/223] the sealing surfaces being pressed together by means of a member, e.g. a swivel nut, screwed on or into one of the joint parts -- F16L 33/223 classifies connections of hoses to rigid members where the sealing surfaces are forced together by a component such as a swivel nut that is screwed onto or into one of the joint parts. It belongs to the mechanical engineering group under engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 33/224] a clamping ring being arranged between the threaded member and the connecting member -- F16L 33/224 pertains to a specific type of hose connection where a clamping ring is positioned between a threaded component and a connecting part, ensuring a secure and leak-proof attachment in mechanical systems. This classification is used for detailed design solutions within the broader category of pipes and connections in engineering.
[F16L 33/225] a sleeve being movable axially -- F16L 33/225 classifies pipe and hose connections that use an axially movable sleeve to join a flexible hose to a rigid component. The sleeve slides along the hose or pipe to provide sealing, alignment or decoupling while allowing axial motion, typical in hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F16L 33/226] the sleeve being screwed over the hose -- The CPC subclass F16L 33/226 covers arrangements where a hose is joined to a rigid component by means of a sleeve that is threaded onto the hose and then screwed onto the adjoining part. It applies to the securing of flexible tubing to pipes, fittings or other fixed members using a screwed-on sleeve.
[F16L 33/227] the hose being introduced into or onto the connecting member and automatically locked  F16L37/084  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass F16L 33/227 relates to pipe systems where a hose is fitted onto a rigid connecting member and is secured by an automatic locking mechanism, ensuring quick and reliable attachment without manual fastening. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes, and is superseded by F16L 37/084.
[F16L 33/228] a flexible wire being coiled upon the hose -- F16L 33/228 covers hose-to-rigid-member connections in which a flexible wire is helically coiled around the hose to provide reinforcement, sealing or a secure attachment point. The wire coil captures the hose and enables it to be clamped or tied to a rigid fitting, improving strength and preventing kinking.
[F16L 33/23] the outer parts being segmented, the segments being pressed against the hose by tangentially arranged members -- This subclass covers hose-to-rigid-member couplings where the outer coupling is made of separate segments that are pressed against the hose by members arranged tangentially, creating a segmented, compressive sealing arrangement.
[F16L 33/24] with parts screwed directly on or into the hose -- This CPC subclass covers pipe coupling arrangements where the hose is attached directly by screwing parts onto or into the hose, without intermediate fittings. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 33/245] the inner or outer part being moulded  in situ -- The entry concerns pipe fittings that join hoses to rigid components where the inner or outer part of the connection is created by moulding it directly in place, forming a seamless joint during installation. This in-situ moulded element provides a customized, leak-tight interface for fluid-transfer systems.
[F16L 33/26] specially adapted for hoses made of metal -- **Definition:** This place covers:  corrugated hoses
[F16L 33/28] for hoses with one end terminating in a radial flange or collar -- This subclass covers pipe fittings that join flexible hoses to fixed components, specifically where the hose end terminates in a radial flange or collar that mates with a corresponding connector.
[F16L 33/30] comprising parts inside the hoses only -- The F16L 33/30 entry covers hose-to-rigid-member connections that rely solely on components placed inside the hose, such as internal seals, liners or reinforcement, without any external fittings. These arrangements provide a sealed joint by embedding the functional parts within the hose material, ensuring a leak-tight connection to a rigid pipe or equipment.
[F16L 33/32] comprising parts outside the hoses only -- This subclass covers hose-to-rigid-member connections in which the defined elements are limited to the parts mounted on the outside of the hose, such as clamps, fittings, brackets or other fixtures that secure the hose to a rigid component, while the hose itself is not included in the claimed structure.
[F16L 33/34] with bonding obtained by vulcanisation, gluing, melting, or the like -- F16L 33/34 relates to pipe systems where flexible hoses are joined to rigid members by a bonded union, such as vulcanisation, gluing, melting or similar methods, rather than by mechanical clamps. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → pipes → arrangements for connecting hoses to rigid members.
[F16L 35/00] Special arrangements used in connection with end fittings of hoses, e.g. safety or protecting devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  safety housings; devices against unauthorized use, anti-kink devices for pipe ends; pressure relief devices
[F16L 35/005] Nozzles -- The CPC entry F16L 35/005 covers nozzles that form special arrangements attached to the ends of hoses, such as safety or protective devices for pipe fittings, within the field of mechanical engineering pipe systems.
[F16L 37/00] Couplings of the quick-acting type -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Joints wherein the connection is established by one or two simple quick movements.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for use where pipes pass through walls by means of a joint of the quick acting type -> F16L5/027 - Radially binding sleeves -> F16L17/04 - Connecting hoses to rigid members -> F16L33/00
[F16L 37/002] which can be controlled at a distance -- The subclass F16L 37/002 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that can be operated or released from a remote location, typically by pneumatic, hydraulic, electric or mechanical signals. These devices are used in fluid transport systems to enable rapid, safe connection or disconnection without manual intervention.
[F16L 37/004] using magnets -- This CPC subclass concerns quick-acting pipe couplings that employ magnetic forces to engage, retain or release the connection. It covers designs where magnets replace or supplement traditional latching or hydraulic mechanisms, enabling rapid coupling and decoupling in fluid-transfer systems.
[F16L 37/006] plug-cocks -- F16L 37/006 refers to plug-cocks, a type of quick-acting coupling used in pipe systems to start or stop flow rapidly. They are mechanical devices that provide a quick, reliable shutoff within the broader class of pipe couplings.
[F16L 37/008] for branching pipes; for joining pipes to walls -- F16L 37/008 covers quick-acting pipe couplings designed to connect branching pipes or attach pipes to walls. These fittings allow fast, tool-free installation and are used in mechanical engineering systems where rapid assembly or disassembly is required.
[F16L 37/02] in which the connection is maintained only by friction of the parts being joined -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Couplings that can be separated by force, e.g. pulling strongly  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Connections maintained by means of balls, rollers or helical springs under radial pressure between the parts -> F16L37/22
[F16L 37/025] with an inner elastic part pressed against an outer part by reason of its elasticity -- A quick-acting pipe coupling that uses an inner elastic element pressed against an outer part by the element's own elasticity to create a seal or connection. This design belongs to the mechanical-engineering group for pipe couplings and is classified under CPC code F16L 37/025.
[F16L 37/04] with an elastic outer part pressing against an inner part by reason of its elasticity -- F16L 37/04 designates quick-acting pipe couplings that incorporate an elastic outer part which, by virtue of its elasticity, presses against an inner component to create a sealing or retaining action. The classification applies to devices where the elastic element supplies the coupling force without additional mechanical actuators.
[F16L 37/05] tightened by the pressure of a mechanical organ -- F16L 37/05 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that achieve a sealed connection by the pressure exerted by a mechanical element such as a spring, diaphragm or pressure plate. The tightening is generated automatically when the coupling parts are brought together, eliminating the need for external tools or manual force. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering elements for pipes and specifically addresses rapid-acting, pressure-based sealing mechanisms.
[F16L 37/06] tightened by fluid pressure -- The subgroup F16L 37/06 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that are tightened by the application of fluid pressure, such as hydraulic or pneumatic actuation, which rapidly draws the coupling halves together to form a sealed connection. This design is used where fast, reliable joining of pipelines is required, especially in systems that can supply the necessary pressure to effect the lock.
[F16L 37/08] in which the connection between abutting or axially overlapping ends is maintained by locking members -- F16L 37/08 pertains to quick-acting pipe couplings in which the joint between abutting or axially overlapping pipe ends is held together by locking members. The locking mechanism secures the connection without permanent joining methods, enabling fast assembly and disassembly in mechanical engineering pipe systems.
[F16L 37/082] using an element which is hinged on one end of the pipe-ends and which is maintained in locked position by a screw tightened against the other pipe-end -- This subclass covers quick-acting pipe couplings where a hinge element attached to one pipe end is swung into place and then secured by a screw that presses against the opposite pipe end, locking the joint. It is used for rapid assembly and disassembly while maintaining a sealed connection.
[F16L 37/084] combined with automatic locking -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Joints wherein the connection is established by one simple quick movement: simply push the two pipe ends together: "click"
[F16L 37/0841] by means of a transversally slidable locking member surrounding the tube -- This subclass covers quick-acting pipe couplings that are secured by a transverse sliding member that surrounds the tube, moving sideways to lock or release the connection. The arrangement enables rapid assembly and disassembly while maintaining a reliable seal.
[F16L 37/0842] by means of a ring which is split into a plurality of component parts which are held in place by means of a resilient ring member -- Quick-acting pipe couplings in this subgroup use a split ring formed from several separate pieces that are secured together by an elastic retaining ring. The design enables rapid assembly and disassembly while maintaining a reliable seal.
[F16L 37/0844] by means of a ring pivoting so as to lie against the tube -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F16L 37/0845] by means of retaining members associated with the packing member -- Quick-acting pipe couplings that secure the sealing or packing element with retaining members such as clips, brackets or clamps are covered by this classification. The retaining parts hold the packing in place, allowing fast connection and disconnection while maintaining a leak-tight joint. This solution is commonly used in hydraulic, pneumatic or fluid-transport systems where rapid assembly is required.
[F16L 37/0847] by means of hooks  F16L37/096 ,  F16L37/098  take precedence -- F16L 37/0847 pertains to quick-acting pipe couplings that use hooks for connection, though specific designs with features detailed in codes F16L37/096 and F16L37/098 are given priority.
[F16L 37/0848] rocking freely -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Couplings with rigid hook members which can pivot.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F16L 37/086] by means of latching members pushed radially by spring-like elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Radially moved latching members forming a ring -> F16L37/0841
[F16L 37/088] by means of a split elastic ring -- F16L 37/088 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that are engaged or released by means of a split elastic ring, such as an O-ring or similar element that expands to lock the joint and contracts to allow separation. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.
[F16L 37/0885] with access to the split elastic ring from a radial or tangential opening in the coupling -- F16L 37/0885 designates quick-acting pipe couplings that provide access to a split elastic sealing ring through a radial or tangential opening in the coupling, enabling ring inspection or replacement without fully dismantling the assembly.
[F16L 37/0887] with an axially movable separate member for releasing the coupling -- This CPC subclass F16L 37/0887 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that include a separate member moving axially to release the coupling. The design allows disengagement without rotating the entire coupling, improving service speed and safety in fluid conveyance systems.
[F16L 37/091] by means of a ring provided with teeth or fingers -- A quick-acting pipe coupling that connects two pipe sections by means of a ring equipped with teeth or fingers, which engage corresponding features on the pipe ends to achieve a fast, secure assembly without the need for bolts or welding.
[F16L 37/0915] with a separate member for releasing the coupling -- F16L 37/0915 pertains to quick-acting pipe couplings that incorporate an independent releasing element, such as a lever or button, separate from the main clamping parts. The separate member allows the coupling to be disengaged without dismantling the primary connector.
[F16L 37/092] by means of elements wedged between the pipe and the frusto-conical surface of the body of the connector -- A quick-acting pipe coupling in which sealing is obtained by inserting wedging elements between the pipe end and the frusto-conical surface of the connector body, allowing rapid connection and disconnection while maintaining a leak-tight joint.
[F16L 37/0925] with rings which bite into the wall of the pipe -- This CPC subclass (F16L 37/0925) falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and quick-acting couplings, and it specifically concerns couplings that use rings which bite into the pipe wall to provide a secure, non-leaking connection.
[F16L 37/0926] with an inner support sleeve arranged within the pipe -- This subclass covers quick-acting pipe couplings that incorporate an inner support sleeve positioned inside the pipe to bear loads and maintain alignment during rapid connection or disconnection. The sleeve reinforces the pipe wall, reduces stress concentrations and enhances sealing performance.
[F16L 37/0927] the wedge element being axially displaceable for releasing the coupling -- The class F16L 37/0927 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that use an axially displaceable wedge element to unlock or release the joint, enabling rapid disconnection of pipe sections. It is situated in the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering > Engineering Elements and Units > Pipes > Couplings of the quick-acting type.

[F16L 37/0982] with a separate member for releasing the coupling -- A quick-acting pipe coupling that includes an independent release member, allowing the coupling to be disengaged without rotating the main pipe sections. The separate component operates the uncoupling mechanism, providing rapid and convenient disconnection in mechanical pipe systems.
[F16L 37/0985] the flexible hook extending radially inwardly from an outer part and engaging a bead, recess or the like on an inner part  F16L37/0982  takes precedence -- F16L 37/0985 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to quick-acting pipe couplings where a flexible hook extends radially inward from an outer part, engaging with a bead or recess on an inner part. This specific design ensures secure and rapid connection between pipe sections.
[F16L 37/0987] the flexible hook being progressively compressed by axial tensile loads acting on the coupling -- F16L 37/0987 covers coupling designs where a flexible hook element is gradually compressed under axial tensile forces, allowing the connection to tighten as the load increases. This mechanism provides progressive securing of pipe or hose sections.
[F16L 37/10] using a rotary external sleeve or ring on one part -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Joints wherein the connection is established by two simple quick movements: Pushing the two pipe ends together plus an additional second action; an action to achieve the locking
[F16L 37/101] in which the coupling is coaxial with the pipe -- F16L 37/101 classifies quick-acting pipe couplings whose coupling is aligned coaxially with the pipe, allowing rapid connection and disconnection while maintaining a straight flow path. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for pipes, and covers designs that integrate the coupling directly on the pipe axis.
[F16L 37/103] the connection being maintained by the eccentricity of the two parts of the joint -- F16L 37/103 concerns quick-acting pipe couplings where the connection is kept secure by an eccentric relationship between the two mating parts, so that their offset maintains the joint without extra locking mechanisms.
[F16L 37/105] the rotating sleeve having on its inner surface several axially spaced and circumferentially discontinuous threads which engage with the threads on the male part which are also spaced axially and circumferentially discontinuous -- This subclass covers quick-acting pipe couplings in which a rotating sleeve carries inner threads that are axially spaced and circumferentially discontinuous, matching similarly spaced, discontinuous threads on the male part, enabling rapid engagement and release of the connected sections.
[F16L 37/107] Bayonet-type couplings -- F16L 37/107 covers bayonet-type couplings, a quick-acting mechanical device used to connect pipes by rotating and locking components together. These couplings provide rapid, secure connections in fluid-transport systems and are classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.
[F16L 37/113] the male part having lugs on its periphery penetrating into the corresponding slots provided in the female part -- F16L 37/113 covers quick-acting pipe couplings in which the male part bears lugs around its periphery that engage matching slots in the female part, allowing rapid and secure connection or release of the pipe sections.
[F16L 37/12] using hooks, pawls, or other movable or insertable locking members -- F16L 37/12 classifies quick-acting pipe couplings that are secured by hooks, pawls or other movable or insertable locking members, describing devices where a hooking or pawl mechanism provides rapid engagement and release for connecting or disconnecting pipe sections.

[F16L 37/121] using freely rocking hooks  F16L37/1215  takes precedence -- The code F16L 37/121 designates quick-acting pipe couplings that employ freely rocking hooks to secure and release pipe sections rapidly without rotating the entire fitting. Such hooks pivot freely, allowing fast engagement while maintaining alignment, and the more specific subclass F16L 37/1215 supersedes it for particular hook configurations.
[F16L 37/1215] using hooks provided with a screw-thread adapted to engage and mesh with an appropriate corresponding part -- This subclass covers quick-acting pipe couplings that use hooks fitted with screw threads designed to interlock with a corresponding part. The threaded hooks create a secure, reusable connection that enables rapid assembly and disassembly of piping systems.
[F16L 37/122] using hooks tightened by a wedge section -- This subclass covers quick-acting pipe couplings that employ a hook mechanism locked in place by a wedge shaped section, allowing rapid connection and release of pipe sections. The design typically provides a simple manual tightening action without separate fasteners, suitable for fluid conveyance systems where fast assembly is required.
[F16L 37/1225] using a retaining member the extremities of which, e.g. in the form of a U, engage behind a shoulder of both parts -- This subclass covers quick-acting pipe couplings that lock by means of a retaining member, often U-shaped, which engages behind a shoulder on each of the two parts, providing a secure, rapid connection.
[F16L 37/123] using a retaining member in the form of a wedge -- F16L 37/123 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that secure the connection by means of a wedge-shaped retaining member. This solution is used in mechanical engineering where fast, reliable coupling of fluid conduits is required.
[F16L 37/1235] the connection taking place from inside the pipes -- This CPC subclass concerns quick-acting pipe couplings whose joining is carried out from inside the pipe, using internal mechanisms to lock, seal or connect the conduit without external access. It includes designs where the coupling device is introduced and actuated from within the pipe for rapid assembly or maintenance.
[F16L 37/124] using bolts, fixed to a flange, which are able to tilt in slots of another flange, and being maintained there by the tightening of nuts -- F16L 37/124 relates to quick-acting pipe couplings where bolts are anchored to one flange and can tilt within slots of the mating flange, the connection being secured by tightening nuts. This arrangement allows rapid assembly and disassembly while maintaining a reliable seal. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for pipes.
[F16L 37/127] using hooks hinged about an axis -- F16L 37/127 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that employ hooks hinged about an axis. This design is used where fast assembly and disassembly of fluid-transport lines are required.
[F16L 37/133] using flexible hooks -- F16L 37/133 concerns quick-acting pipe couplings that incorporate flexible hooks. [F16L 37/138] using an axially movable sleeve -- F16L 37/138 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that achieve connection or release by means of an axially movable sleeve. The sleeve slides along the pipe axis to engage sealing or locking elements, allowing rapid assembly or disassembly without tools. This approach is used for fluid transmission lines where fast, reliable coupling is required.
[F16L 37/14] Joints secured by inserting between mating surfaces an element, e.g. a piece of wire, a pin, a chain -- F16L 37/14 relates to quick-acting pipe couplings in which the joint is locked by inserting a separate element, such as a wire, pin or chain, between the mating surfaces. This element holds the parts together, providing a rapid and removable means of securing the connection.
[F16L 37/142] where the securing element is inserted tangentially -- F16L 37/142 covers quick-acting pipe couplings in which the securing element-such as a lock ring, wedge or bolt-is introduced tangentially into the coupling body, allowing rapid assembly and disassembly while maintaining a sealed connection. This design is used for fast, reliable pipe joining in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16L 37/144] the securing element being U-shaped -- F16L 37/144 covers U-shaped securing elements employed in quick-acting pipe couplings. The component provides a latch or hold-down function that locks the coupling halves together and can be released rapidly for maintenance or disassembly. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 37/146] the securing element being a rigid pin, screw or the like -- F16L 37/146 covers quick-acting pipe couplings whose locking or securing part is a rigid element such as a pin, screw, or similar device. It falls within the mechanical-engineering group for pipe connections, specifying how the coupling is held together by a solid, non-elastic fastener.

[F16L 37/20] Joints tightened by toggle-action levers -- A quick-acting pipe coupling in which the joint is secured by a toggle-action lever, providing rapid connection and firm sealing of the pipe ends. The lever mechanism multiplies force to tighten the joint and can be released easily for disassembly, commonly used in hydraulic and fluid-transport systems. This falls under the mechanical-engineering class for pipe couplings tightened by toggle-type levers.
[F16L 37/22] in which the connection is maintained by means of balls, rollers or helical springs under radial pressure between the parts -- F16L 37/22 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that keep the joint together by using balls, rollers or helical springs that apply radial pressure between the connected parts. [F16L 37/23] by means of balls -- F16L 37/23 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that are engaged or released using balls. [F16L 37/24] in which the connection is made by inserting one member axially into the other and rotating it to a limited extent, e.g. with bayonet-action -- F16L 37/24 covers quick-acting pipe couplings in which one component is inserted axially into the other and then rotated a small amount to lock, a mechanism exemplified by bayonet-type connections. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.
[F16L 37/242] in which the rotation takes place between the eccentric parts -- This subclass covers quick-acting pipe couplings in which the driving and driven members rotate relative to each other via eccentric elements, allowing rapid connection or disconnection. The eccentric mechanism provides angular displacement while maintaining sealing surfaces. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 37/244] the coupling being co-axial with the pipe -- F16L 37/244 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that are coaxial with the pipe, meaning the coupling and pipe share the same longitudinal axis for a seamless connection. These devices allow rapid joining or release of pipe sections without misalignment, facilitating maintenance and assembly in mechanical-engineering pipe systems.
[F16L 37/2445] in which a male cylindrical element is introduced into a female cylindrical element, each element containing several threads axially spaced and circumferentially discontinuous which engage with each other as a result of the rotation of one of the elements -- This CPC subclass (F16L 37/2445) pertains to quick-acting pipe couplings in which a male cylindrical part is inserted into a female cylindrical part, each component carrying several axially spaced, circumferentially discontinuous threads that mesh as one element is rotated. [F16L 37/256] the coupling not being coaxial with the pipe -- F16L 37/256 covers couplings of the quick-acting type where the coupling is installed at a non-coaxial angle relative to the pipe, meaning the coupling's axis diverges from the pipe axis. This includes designs that permit connection despite misalignment, often using flexible or swivel arrangements in mechanical engineering.
[F16L 37/26] in which the connection is made by transversely moving the parts together, with or without their subsequent rotation -- F16L 37/26 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that are joined by moving the components sideways into alignment, after which they may be rotated to lock. The transverse motion brings the parts together, enabling rapid assembly or disassembly without the need for axial insertion. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering pipe-connection elements.
[F16L 37/28] with fluid cut-off means -- F16L 37/28 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that incorporate a fluid-cut-off mechanism, such as a valve, valve seat or similar device that stops flow when the coupling is disconnected or opened, providing a sealed, safe shutdown of the conveyed fluid. This sub-class belongs to the mechanical engineering group for pipe coupling elements.
[F16L 37/30] with fluid cut-off means in each of two pipe-end fittings -- This subclass defines quick-acting pipe couplings that include a fluid cut-off device in each of the two pipe-end fittings, enabling rapid connection while simultaneously isolating the flow in both ends. It applies to mechanical-engineering pipe systems where swift mating and shut-off are required.
[F16L 37/32] at least one of two lift valves being opened automatically when the coupling is applied -- The CPC code F16L 37/32 falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes and covers quick-acting pipe couplings that automatically open at least one of two lift valves when the coupling is engaged. This automatic valve actuation ensures rapid sealing and fluid flow without manual intervention.
[F16L 37/33] the lift valves being of the ball type -- F16L 37/33 classifies quick-acting pipe couplings that integrate lift valves of the ball type, where a spherical valve element moves axially to open or close the flow path. The entry covers designs that use this ball lift mechanism to provide rapid, reliable connection and shut-off in hydraulic or pneumatic pipe systems.
[F16L 37/34] at least one of the lift valves being of the sleeve type, i.e. a sleeve being telescoped over an inner cylindrical wall -- F16L 37/34 covers quick-acting pipe couplings in which at least one lift valve is a sleeve type, meaning a sleeve slides over an inner cylindrical wall to open or close the flow.
[F16L 37/35] at least one of the valves having an axial bore communicating with lateral apertures -- The code F16L 37/35 covers quick-acting pipe couplings in which at least one valve incorporates an axial bore that is linked to side apertures, allowing fluid to be directed through the main passage or diverted laterally. This design enables rapid connection or disconnection while maintaining flow control within the coupling.
[F16L 37/36] with two lift valves being actuated to initiate the flow through the coupling after the two coupling parts are locked against withdrawal -- F16L 37/36 refers to quick-acting pipe couplings that use a pair of lift valves which stay closed until the two coupling halves are firmly locked together, after which the valves open simultaneously to allow fluid to flow.
[F16L 37/367] with two gate valves or sliding valves -- F16L 37/367 classifies quick-acting pipe couplings that integrate two gate valves or sliding valves, allowing rapid connection and simultaneous isolation of a pipeline. This design is used in mechanical engineering for efficient control of fluid flow.
[F16L 37/373] with two taps or cocks -- A quick-acting pipe coupling equipped with two built-in taps or cocks, allowing separate control of flow at each end of the connection. This type of coupling belongs to the mechanical engineering group for pipes and provides rapid engagement while offering dual valve functions.
[F16L 37/38] with fluid cut-off means in only one of two pipe-end fittings -- F16L 37/38 pertains to quick-acting pipe couplings that include a fluid cut-off mechanism on only one of the two pipe-end fittings, allowing rapid connection while isolating flow through the opposite fitting. This design is used in hydraulic and pneumatic systems where selective shut-off is required.
[F16L 37/40] with a lift valve being opened automatically when the coupling is applied -- F16L 37/40 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that automatically open a lift valve when the two halves are joined, allowing rapid fluid connection without manual valve operation. The design is used in mechanical and hydraulic systems where fast, leak-free assembly is required.
[F16L 37/407] the lift valve being of the ball type -- A ball-type lift valve is a quick-acting pipe coupling component in which a spherical valve element moves axially to open or close the flow passage. The valve lifts the ball to permit flow and lowers it to shut off, providing rapid and reliable control in mechanical piping systems.
[F16L 37/413] the lift valve being of the sleeve type, i.e. a sleeve being telescoped over an inner cylindrical wall -- This CPC subclass concerns lift valves of the sleeve type where a sleeve telescopes over an inner cylindrical wall to provide a quick-acting coupling. The arrangement allows rapid opening and closing of the valve, commonly used in pipe systems that require fast actuation.
[F16L 37/42] the valve having an axial bore communicating with lateral apertures -- F16L 37/42 classifies quick-acting pipe couplings that include a valve with an axial bore linked to side openings, enabling flow to be diverted or shut off through lateral apertures. The design is used for rapid connection and disconnection of pipelines while maintaining control of the fluid stream.
[F16L 37/44] with one lift valve being actuated to initiate the flow through the coupling after the two coupling parts are locked against withdrawal -- This class refers to quick-acting pipe couplings that start flow only after the two coupling parts are locked together, using a single lift valve that is actuated to initiate the fluid passage while preventing premature discharge.
[F16L 37/46] with a gate valve or sliding valve -- The code F16L 37/46 designates quick-acting pipe couplings that integrate a gate valve or a sliding valve, allowing rapid connection and shut-off in fluid-transport systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically within engineering elements and units for pipes.
[F16L 37/47] with a tap or cock -- F16L 37/47 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that incorporate an integrated tap or cock, allowing rapid connection and immediate flow control within hydraulic or fluid-transport systems. The design combines a fast-release coupling mechanism with a valve element to start or stop the fluid stream without disconnecting the pipe. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.
[F16L 37/48] for fastening a pipe on the end of a tap -- F16L 37/48 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the EPO and USPTO, specifies couplings of the quick-acting type designed for fastening a pipe to the end of a tap. This classification is part of the broader category of mechanical engineering, focusing on specific components for plumbing connections.
[F16L 37/50] adjustable -- F16L 37/50 relates to adjustable quick-acting pipe couplings, devices that enable rapid connection and disconnection of pipes while allowing the coupling size or sealing pressure to be varied to suit different pipe dimensions or operating conditions.
[F16L 37/505] allowing substantial longitudinal adjustment or movement -- Quick-acting pipe couplings designed to permit substantial longitudinal adjustment or movement, enabling the connected pipes to shift axially while retaining a sealed joint. The feature facilitates alignment and compensation for thermal expansion or vibration in piping systems.
[F16L 37/52] Universal joints, i.e. with a mechanical connection allowing angular movement or adjustment of the axes of the parts in any direction -- F16L 37/52 covers universal joints used as quick-acting pipe couplings that provide a mechanical connection capable of transmitting motion while permitting the axes of connected parts to rotate or tilt in any direction. These joints enable angular adjustment and flexible alignment in fluid or power transmission systems.
[F16L 37/53] allowing adjustment or movement only about the axis of one pipe -- The F16L 37/53 subclass covers quick-acting pipe couplings that permit adjustment or movement solely about the longitudinal axis of one pipe. Such couplings enable axial rotation or alignment of a single pipe while maintaining a sealed connection, often using snap-or latch-type mechanisms.
[F16L 37/54] for pipes under pressure which are supported only on one side -- F16L 37/54 designates quick-acting pipe couplings that allow a pressurised pipe to be secured while being supported on only one side. The couplings typically incorporate hinged, pivoted or swinging elements that maintain a seal despite pipe movement or angular deflection.
[F16L 37/56] for double-walled or multi-channel pipes -- This subclass covers quick-acting couplings designed for double-walled or multi-channel pipes, providing rapid connection and sealing of composite conduit systems. It applies to devices that join such pipes in fluid-transport, fire-fighting or hydraulic installations.
[F16L 37/565] Concentric pipes -- Concentric pipes refer to a set of tubes arranged with a common central axis, used in quick-acting couplings to transmit fluids or pressures while maintaining a compact, sealed connection. This configuration allows relative axial movement of the inner and outer pipes for rapid coupling and decoupling in hydraulic, pneumatic or fluid power systems.
[F16L 37/58] the extremities of the two halves of the joint being pressed against each other without being locked in position -- This subgroup concerns quick-acting pipe couplings in which the two half-sections are forced together so that their ends press against each other and remain joined solely by the applied pressure, without any separate locking element. Such designs enable rapid connection and disconnection of piping systems while maintaining a sealed joint.
[F16L 37/60] with plug and fixed wall housing -- F16L 37/60 covers quick-acting pipe couplings that include a plug and a fixed wall housing to lock the connection in place. The arrangement enables rapid installation and removal while keeping a sealed joint, and is used for fluid or gas transport. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 37/62] pneumatically or hydraulically actuated -- F16L 37/62 covers quick-acting couplings that are actuated by pneumatic or hydraulic pressure, used to connect pipes for rapid joining and release. These devices employ fluid power to move internal elements, enabling fast, sealed connections in mechanical engineering applications.

[F16L 41/008] for connecting a measuring instrument -- F16L 41/008 designates branching pipe fittings that provide a connection point for a measuring instrument, such as a pressure or flow sensor, to a pipe system. [F16L 41/02] Branch units, e.g. made in one piece, welded, riveted -- Branch units are components that create a branch off a main pipe, allowing flow to be diverted or tapped. They can be produced as a single piece or formed by welding or riveting separate parts together, providing a robust, leak-tight connection in fluid-transport systems.
[F16L 41/021] T- or cross-pieces  F16L41/025 ,  F16L41/026 ,  F16L41/028  take precedence -- T- or cross-pieces are pipe fittings that join a main pipe with one or more branch pipes at right angles, forming a "T" shape or a four-way intersection. They provide a junction point for fluid or gas flow to be diverted or combined, and are typically welded, threaded or clamped to the adjoining pipe sections. This class covers their design, manufacture and application in mechanical engineering piping systems.
[F16L 41/023] Y- pieces  F16L41/025 ,  F16L41/026 ,  F16L41/028  take precedence -- F16L 41/023 covers Y-shaped pipe fittings used to join three pipe sections at angles, allowing flow division or convergence in fluid-handling systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, branching pipes. The more specific subclasses F16L 41/025, 41/026 and 41/028 supersede this entry for detailed designs.
[F16L 41/025] with rectangular cross-section -- A subclass for branching pipe fittings whose passage has a rectangular cross-section, covering ducts, manifolds and transition pieces that split fluid flow in a rectangular shape. It applies to mechanical-engineering components where rectangular pipes are branched or joined.
[F16L 41/026] with a layer protecting against erosion -- This subclass concerns branching pipe components that include a protective surface layer or lining intended to prevent erosion of the pipe material during fluid or solid conveyance. The erosion-resistant coating may be a cladding, metallurgical layer, polymer film or similar barrier applied to the interior or exterior of the branch.
[F16L 41/028] of concrete, cement or asbestos-cement -- F16L 41/028 covers branching pipe sections that are fabricated from concrete, cement or asbestos-cement. It includes the designs, joining methods and typical applications of these rigid, non-metallic pipe branches in water, sewage or drainage systems.

[F16L 41/04] Tapping pipe walls, i.e. making connections through the walls of pipes while they are carrying fluids -- F16L 41/04 relates to techniques and apparatus for tapping the walls of pipes that are operating under pressure, enabling the creation of branches, fittings or valves without interrupting fluid flow. It addresses in-service connections through pipe walls in mechanical-engineering piping systems.
[F16L 41/045] without removal of material  F16L41/065  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Tapping pipe walls without removal of material, e.g. the tapping means piercing the wall.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Tapping pipe walls making use of attaching means embracing the pipe without removal of material -> F16L41/065
[F16L 41/06] making use of attaching means embracing the pipe -- The subclass concerns methods of joining branch pipes by employing attaching means that surround or embrace the pipe, such as clamps, collars, sleeves or similar devices that enclose the pipe to secure it to a main line, typically avoiding welding or permanent deformation. It covers the use of these encircling fittings to create a reliable mechanical connection.
[F16L 41/065] without removal of material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. the tapping means piercing the wall by using guidance on attaching means embracing the pipe
[F16L 41/08] Joining pipes to walls or pipes, the joined pipe axis being perpendicular to the plane of a wall or to the axis of another pipe -- The CPC class F16L 41/08 covers fittings, brackets, clamps and other devices used to join a pipe to a wall or to another pipe when the pipe axis must be perpendicular to the wall plane or to the other pipe's axis. It relates to the mechanical-engineering sector of pipe-connection elements that create right-angle joints.
[F16L 41/082] Non-disconnectable joints, e.g. soldered, adhesive or caulked joints -- The F16L 41/082 subclass refers to permanent, non-disconnectable joints used in branching pipe systems, where the connection is made by methods such as soldering, adhesive bonding or caulking, providing a fixed seal without the possibility of later disassembly.
[F16L 41/084] Soldered joints -- F16L 41/084 pertains to the joining of branching pipe sections by soldering, covering the design, preparation, and execution of soldered joints used to connect branch lines to a main conduit. It includes methods, materials, and configurations specific to creating reliable, leak-tight soldered connections in mechanical pipe systems.
[F16L 41/086] fixed with screws -- F16L 41/086 covers branching pipe connections that are secured to the main pipe using screws. The classification applies to pipe fittings where the branch is attached by screwing onto the pipe body, commonly used in mechanical engineering for fluid transport.
[F16L 41/088] fixed using an elastic grommet between the extremity of the tube and the wall -- The CPC subclass F16L 41/088 deals with branching pipes that are secured to a surrounding wall by placing an elastic grommet between the tube's extremity and the wall, providing flexible fixing and sealing. It is used where a rubber-like insert cushions the joint and maintains a tight connection.
[F16L 41/10] the extremity of the pipe being screwed into the wall -- F16L 41/10 relates to the extremity of a pipe that is screwed into a wall or similar structure, detailing the threaded connection that secures the pipe. It is applied to branching pipe systems within mechanical engineering.
[F16L 41/12] using attaching means embracing the pipe -- F16L 41/12 relates to branching pipe arrangements where the branch is secured to the main line by an attaching means that embraces the pipe, such as clamps, sleeves or banded fittings. The embracing device holds the pipe together and provides a connection without permanent welding, allowing removable or adjustable branch connections.
[F16L 41/14] by screwing an intermediate part against the inside or outside of the wall -- F16L 41/14 relates to branching pipe connections where an intermediate fitting is secured by screwing it against the inner or outer surface of the pipe wall, creating a threaded joint that diverts flow to a branch.
[F16L 41/16] the branch pipe comprising fluid cut-off means -- F16L 41/16 pertains to branch pipes that incorporate built-in fluid cut-off means, such as valves or shut-off devices, enabling the flow through the branch to be stopped or regulated. It is used in mechanical engineering contexts for piping systems where the branch itself provides the means to interrupt fluid passage.
[F16L 41/18] the branch pipe being movable -- This classification concerns branch pipes that can be moved relative to the main line, such as hinged, swivel or telescopic connections allowing adjustment of direction or length. It covers mechanisms enabling the branch to be repositioned while maintaining flow.
[F16L 43/00] Bends -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Bends with cleaning apertures -> F16L45/00
[F16L 43/001] made of metal -- F16L 43/001 designates pipe bends that are fabricated from metal. It falls under the Mechanical Engineering section, within Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes → Bends. The code is used by the EPC and USPTO to classify metal bending methods and components.
[F16L 43/002] and formed from sheet having a circular passage -- F16L 43/002 pertains to pipe bends that are produced by shaping a sheet of material so that it incorporates a circular passage, essentially a curved conduit formed from a single sheet. This subclass addresses the manufacture of such bends where the circular opening is integral to the formed sheet.
[F16L 43/003] having a rectangular cross-section -- F16L 43/003 covers pipe bends whose cross-section is rectangular, falling under the mechanical engineering group for engineering elements and units, specifically pipes. The classification addresses design and manufacturing features of rectangular-section bends, including their geometry, fabrication methods and applications in fluid or gas transport systems.
[F16L 43/005] Return bends -- The CPC entry F16L 43/005 covers return bends, which are pipe bends that reverse the flow direction to bring a line back toward its original course. These components are used in mechanical-engineering piping systems to fit space constraints and reconnect to preceding sections, and they fall under the hierarchy of Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes → Bends.
[F16L 43/006] telescopic -- The F16L 43/06 subclass defines telescopic pipe bends, where one pipe section slides within another to vary the overall length while preserving a curved shape. These adjustable bends are used in mechanical-engineering installations such as fluid-transport, ventilation or hydraulic systems to accommodate changing distances or alignment requirements.
[F16L 43/007] made of concrete, cement or asbestos-cement -- F16L 43/007 covers bends for pipes that are made from concrete, cement or asbestos-cement, specifying the shape and construction of such curved pipe sections used in civil and industrial installations. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, bends.
[F16L 43/008] made from plastic material -- F16L 43/008 is a CPC subclass for pipe bends that are made from plastic material, classifying inventions concerning the design, manufacture or use of plastic pipe elbows and curvatures. It resides under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes → Bends, and is used to identify patents dealing with plastic-based pipe bending solutions.
