[F16L 45/00] Pipe units with cleaning aperture and closure therefor -- F16L 45/00 refers to pipe units that incorporate a cleaning aperture-an opening that allows access for inspection or flushing of the interior-and a built-in closure or plug to seal the aperture when not in use. These fittings are designed to facilitate maintenance while preserving the integrity of the pipe system.
[F16L 47/00] Connecting arrangements or other fittings specially adapted to be made of plastics or to be used with pipes made of plastics -- F16L 47/00 covers fittings and coupling systems that are designed specifically for plastic piping, including couplings, elbows, tees and other connectors made from or intended for use with plastic pipes. The classification addresses the geometry, sealing methods and material considerations required to join or adapt plastic pipe sections in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16L 47/005] the first pipe being joined to the ends of two other pipes placed one inside the other, e.g. gas pipe with protective sheath -- The CPC subclass F16L 47/005 covers joining a primary pipe to the ends of two nested pipes, where the outer pipe encloses the inner one, for example a gas conduit fitted with a protective sheath. It pertains to plastic fittings designed for such double-pipe assemblies.
[F16L 47/02] Welded joints -- F16L 47/02 pertains to welded joints used in connecting arrangements or fittings that are specially designed for plastic pipes or for use with plastic pipe systems, covering the methods and structures that create permanent, seamless connections between such components.
[F16L 47/03] Welded joints with an electrical resistance incorporated in the joint -- F16L 47/03 pertains to welded connections for plastic pipe systems in which an electrical resistance element is built into the joint to provide heat for resistance welding. The subclass covers fittings and joining arrangements specially adapted for plastics that incorporate this heating feature.
[F16L 47/04] with a swivel nut or collar engaging the pipe -- F16L 47/04 covers fittings for plastic pipes that incorporate a swivel nut or collar to engage the pipe, providing a rotatable and securely attached connection.
[F16L 47/041] the plastic pipe end being flared either before or during the making of the connection -- F16L 47/041 covers methods where the end of a plastic pipe is expanded into a flared shape either before the fitting is applied or during the joining process, enabling a secure connection with compatible plastic fittings. This technique is used in plumbing and fluid transport systems.
[F16L 47/06] with sleeve or socket formed by or in the pipe end -- F16L 47/06 designates pipe connections in which a sleeve or socket is formed as part of the pipe end, typically for plastic piping systems. The feature provides an integral joining element without separate external fittings.
[F16L 47/065] with sealing rings arranged between outer surface of pipe and inner surface of sleeve or socket, the sealing rings being placed previously on the male part -- F16L 47/065 covers plastic pipe connections in which sealing rings are pre-installed on the male portion before it is inserted into a sleeve or socket, creating a seal between the pipe's outer surface and the inner surface of the female fitting. This arrangement is used for joining plastic pipes or fittings with integrated sealing elements.
[F16L 47/08] with sealing rings arranged between the outer surface of one pipe end and the inner surface of the sleeve or socket, the sealing rings being placed previously in the sleeve or socket -- A fitting for plastic pipes in which sealing rings are pre-installed in the sleeve or socket and positioned between the outer surface of the pipe end and the inner surface of the sleeve, providing a sealed joint without additional ring insertion during assembly.
[F16L 47/10] the sealing rings being maintained in place by additional means -- F16L 47/10 pertains to plastic pipe connections in which the sealing rings are secured by an additional retaining device such as a clip, spring, cage or similar means, rather than relying solely on compression. The classification covers designs that keep the sealing element in place to maintain leak-proofness in fittings specially adapted for plastic pipes.
[F16L 47/12] with additional locking means -- F16L 47/12 covers plastic pipe fittings or connecting arrangements that include additional locking mechanisms to secure the joint. The subclass pertains to fittings specially adapted for use with plastic pipes, providing an extra means of locking beyond standard snap or friction fits.
[F16L 47/14] Flanged joints -- F16L 47/14 covers flanged joints designed for plastic pipe systems or for use with plastic components. These connections join pipe sections by aligning matching flanges and securing them with bolts, gaskets or other fastening methods, providing a sealed, detachable interface. The subclass is part of the broader group for pipe fittings adapted to plastic materials.
[F16L 47/145] for rectangular pipes -- The subclass F16L 47/145 covers fittings and connection methods designed specifically for rectangular plastic pipes. It includes any joining, sealing or coupling arrangements adapted to the non-circular shape of such pipes, ensuring secure and leak-free assemblies in mechanical systems.
[F16L 47/16] Screw-threaded joints -- F16L 47/16 refers to screw-threaded joints that connect plastic pipes or fittings, or are intended for use with plastic pipe systems. The classification addresses devices such as threaded couplings, unions or adapters designed specifically for plastic-material piping.
[F16L 47/18] Adjustable joints -- F16L 47/18 covers adjustable joints used as connecting arrangements or fittings that are specially adapted for plastic pipes or for use with plastic pipe systems. The classification includes joints that can be varied in angle, length or position to accommodate changes in alignment or movement while maintaining a seal.
[F16L 47/20] based principally on specific properties of plastics -- F16L 47/20 covers pipe-fittings and other connecting arrangements that are specially designed to be made from, or used with, plastic pipes, where the design relies on the distinctive properties of plastics such as flexibility, chemical resistance or thermal behaviour. It includes couplings, elbows, tees and similar components whose functionality is based principally on these material characteristics.
[F16L 47/22] using shrink-down material -- F16L 47/22 covers fittings or joining devices for plastic pipe systems that are installed by applying a shrink-down material, which contracts when heated to securely grip the pipe. The method is used for connectors specially adapted for plastic pipes and includes various shrinkable sleeves or couplings that form a tight seal after shrinking.
[F16L 47/24] for joints between metal and plastics pipes -- F16L 47/24 covers fittings and other joining arrangements that are specially designed to connect metal pipes with plastic pipes. It includes transition couplings, adapters, and sealing devices that enable a reliable mechanical and leak-proof joint between dissimilar pipe materials. This subclass falls under the CPC hierarchy of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.
[F16L 47/26] for branching pipes -- This CPC entry concerns fittings and connecting arrangements specially adapted for plastic piping that create branch connections, such as tees, wyes and similar branch devices made of or compatible with plastic pipes. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → pipes.
[F16L 47/265] Reduction units -- Reduction units are plastic fittings that join pipe sections of differing diameters, providing a step-down transition in a plumbing or fluid-handling system. They are classified under CPC F16L 47/265 within the mechanical-engineering group for pipe connections specially adapted for plastics.
[F16L 47/28] Joining pipes to walls or to other pipes, the axis of the joined pipe being perpendicular to the wall or to the axis of the other pipe -- F16L 47/28 covers fittings and methods for attaching plastic pipes to walls or to other pipes where the joined pipe meets the surface or other pipe at a right angle, emphasizing perpendicular connections.
[F16L 47/285] with fluid cut-off means in the branching pipe -- Plastic pipe or fitting designed to include a fluid shut-off device in a branch line, allowing flow to be stopped or regulated directly at the connection. The feature is integrated into the fitting rather than added separately.
[F16L 47/30] using attaching means embracing the pipe -- F16L 47/30 covers connecting arrangements or fittings for plastic pipes in which the attaching means embraces, wraps or encircles the pipe to secure it, rather than inserting or clamping separate parts. It pertains to mechanical-engineering pipe components specially adapted for use with plastic tubing.
[F16L 47/32] Branch units, e.g. made in one piece, welded, riveted -- F16L 47/32 pertains to branch units for pipe systems, such as tees or elbows, that are made as a single piece or joined by welding or riveting and are intended for plastic pipe connections. These fittings are designed to link branching lines to a main pipe and are specially adapted for use with plastic piping.
[F16L 47/34] Tapping pipes, i.e. making connections through walls of pipes while carrying fluids -- F16L 47/34 in the Cooperative Patent Classification addresses methods and devices for tapping into plastic pipes to create connections without interrupting fluid flow, specifically focusing on techniques that penetrate the pipe wall.
[F16L 47/345] making use of attaching means embracing the pipe -- The F16L 47/345 entry concerns methods that employ the pipe itself as part of the attachment mechanism, specifically for plastic pipes or fittings designed for plastic piping. It covers joining techniques where the pipe is integrated into the fastening means, facilitating secure connections without separate auxiliary components.
[F16L 49/00] Connecting arrangements, e.g. joints, specially adapted for pipes of brittle material, e.g. glass, earthenware -- This subclass covers devices and methods for joining pipes made of brittle materials such as glass or earthenware, including specially designed joints, fittings and coupling arrangements that accommodate the fragility of the pipe walls.
[F16L 49/02] Joints with a sleeve or socket -- This subclass covers pipe joints that employ a sleeve or socket to connect brittle materials such as glass or earthenware, providing a secure, stress-relieving arrangement that links pipe sections while protecting the fragile walls.
[F16L 49/04] Flanged joints -- F16L 49/04 covers flanged joints used to connect pipes made of brittle materials such as glass or earthenware. The classification addresses the design of flange faces, bolts, gaskets and related features that provide a secure, leak-tight connection while accommodating the fragility of the pipe material. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 49/06] Joints in which sealing surfaces are pressed together by means of a member, e.g. swivel nut, screwed on, or into, one of the joint parts -- This CPC subclass covers pipe joints for brittle materials where the sealing surfaces are forced together by a fastening member such as a swivel nut or a screw that is applied to one of the joint components. It includes arrangements where the pressurizing element is threaded onto or into the joint to achieve a seal.
[F16L 49/08] Adjustable joints -- F16L 49/08 covers adjustable joints designed for connecting brittle pipes such as glass or earthenware. The class deals with mechanisms that allow length or angle adjustment while maintaining a seal, facilitating installation and maintenance of fragile pipe systems. These joints may include threaded, clamping or sliding components that can be repositioned without breaking the pipe.
[F16L 5/00] Devices for use where pipes, cables or protective tubing pass through walls or partitions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Holders for pipes passing through walls or partitions, the pipe passing from one side of the wall to the other side.
[F16L 5/02] Sealing -- This class covers sealing devices for pipe, cable, or tubing penetrations through walls or partitions, including gaskets, sealing rings and other means to prevent leakage or ingress. It is part of the mechanical engineering group, under engineering elements and units for pipes, and is identified by CPC code F16L 5/02.
[F16L 5/022] by welding -- F16L 5/022 covers devices that allow pipes, cables or protective tubing to pass through walls or partitions and are installed by welding, where the fitting, sleeve or seal is permanently joined to the pipe or surrounding structure using welding methods.
[F16L 5/025] the pipe being movable  F16L5/10  takes precedence -- F16L 5/025 covers devices that allow a pipe, cable or protective tubing passing through a wall, partition or similar barrier to be moved, such as swivel, pivot or flexible fittings that accommodate angular or positional changes. It falls under the broader category of pipe-through-wall devices and is subordinate to F16L 5/10, which governs the general arrangement of such penetrations.
[F16L 5/027] by means of a joint of the quick-acting type -- F16L 5/027 covers devices for pipe, cable or tubing penetrations that are sealed by a quick-acting joint. The quick-acting joint enables rapid opening and resealing of the penetration, allowing quick maintenance or emergency access while preserving the seal. This entry falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 5/04] to form a firebreak device -- F16L 5/04 covers devices designed to form a firebreak where pipes, cables or protective tubing penetrate walls or partitions, preventing the spread of fire through those openings. It includes fire-stopping fittings, sleeves, collars or seals that maintain the integrity of the barrier while allowing the service to pass.
[F16L 5/06] by means of a swivel nut compressing a ring or sleeve -- This subclass covers devices that secure pipes, cables or protective tubing passing through walls or partitions by using a swivel nut that compresses a surrounding ring or sleeve, creating a sealed, rotatable joint. It is classified under mechanical engineering pipe fittings.
[F16L 5/08] by means of axial screws compressing a ring or sleeve -- A device that lets pipes, cables or protective tubing pass through walls or partitions by tightening axial screws that compress a sealing ring or sleeve, providing a secure, leak-tight joint. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering group for pipe-through-wall fittings.
[F16L 5/10] by using sealing rings or sleeves only -- F16L 5/10 covers devices that allow pipes, cables or protective tubing to pass through walls, partitions or other barriers where the seal is achieved solely with sealing rings or sleeves. The design relies on these simple ring or sleeve components to prevent leakage without additional gaskets, clamps or flanges.
[F16L 51/00] Expansion-compensation arrangements for pipe-lines -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Telescopic pipes -> F16L27/12
[F16L 51/005] for concrete pipe-lines -- F16L 51/005 covers devices and methods that provide expansion-compensation for concrete water or sewage pipelines. It includes joints, fittings or other arrangements that absorb thermal or pressure-induced length changes, preventing cracking or deformation of the concrete pipe while maintaining flow continuity.
[F16L 51/02] making use of a bellows or an expansible folded or corrugated tube -- F16L 51/02 covers pipe-line expansion-compensation designs that incorporate a bellows or an expansible folded or corrugated tube to absorb length changes caused by temperature or pressure variations, providing a flexible segment that maintains flow continuity while allowing extension or contraction of the pipeline.
[F16L 51/021] having a rectangular cross-section -- The code F16L 51/021 pertains to expansion-compensation arrangements for pipelines in which the compensating element is shaped with a rectangular cross-section. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes, specifying that the compensator's geometry is rectangular rather than circular or other forms. This classification is used to index devices that accommodate thermal or pressure-induced length changes in pipe systems.
[F16L 51/022] with a single corrugation -- F16L 51/022 designates expansion-compensation arrangements for pipelines that employ a single corrugation in the pipe wall or connecting element. The single corrugated section provides flexibility to absorb thermal or pressure-induced elongation while maintaining seal integrity, and falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.

[F16L 51/035] for cancelling the axial loading resulting from fluid pressure -- F16L 51/035 covers expansion-compensation arrangements for pipelines that cancel the axial loading produced by fluid pressure, using devices such as expansion joints or compensators that permit longitudinal movement while preserving seal integrity.
[F16L 51/04] making use of bends, e.g. lyre-shaped -- F16L 51/04 pertains to expansion-compensation arrangements for pipelines that use bends-often lyre-shaped-to absorb length changes while preserving fluid flow, integrating the bends as functional elements in the pipe system.
[F16L 5/12] the pipe being cut in two pieces -- F16L 5/12 covers arrangements in which a pipe is cut into two sections to allow it to pass through a wall, partition or other barrier, often involving sealing or fastening of the separated ends. The classification addresses the pipe-cutting approach as a means of facilitating installation or maintenance of pipe, cable or protective tubing systems.

These fittings ensure a sealed passage for pipes, cables or protective tubing while maintaining isolation between the inner and outer conduits and fall under the mechanical engineering group for pipe elements.
[F16L 53/00] Heating of pipes or pipe systems -- F16L 53/00 pertains to methods and apparatus for heating pipes or entire pipe systems, including external heaters, internal flow heating, and temperature-control devices used to prevent freezing, maintain process temperatures, or improve fluid flow characteristics.
[F16L 53/30] Heating of pipes or pipe systems -- F16L 53/30 relates to technologies for heating pipes or pipe networks, including systems that provide thermal energy to maintain fluid temperature, such as trace heating, steam tracing, electric heating cables, and control mechanisms. It covers both the installation of heating elements on pipes and the methods for regulating heat output to prevent freezing or to achieve process temperatures.
[F16L 53/32] using hot fluids -- The CPC subclass F16L 53/32 covers heating of pipes or pipe systems by circulating hot fluids. It includes methods and devices for delivering heated liquids or gases to raise pipe temperature for purposes such as preventing freezing, improving flow, or pre-heating components.
[F16L 53/34] using electric, magnetic or electromagnetic fields, e.g. induction, dielectric or microwave heating -- F16L 53/34 covers methods of heating pipes or pipe networks by applying electric, magnetic or electromagnetic fields, such as induction, dielectric or microwave heating. These techniques generate heat directly in the pipe material or the fluid it carries, improving energy efficiency and allowing rapid temperature control.
[F16L 53/35] Ohmic-resistance heating -- F16L 53/35 covers devices that heat pipes or pipe systems by passing electric current directly through the pipe material or a surrounding conductor, using the pipe's own electrical resistance to generate heat. It belongs to the Mechanical Engineering section under Engineering Elements and Units - Pipes - Heating of pipes or pipe systems.
[F16L 53/37] the heating current flowing directly through the pipe to be heated -- This subclass covers pipe-heating techniques in which an electric current is passed directly through the pipe wall itself, using the pipe as a resistive element to generate heat and raise the temperature of the conveyed fluid. It is part of the broader group on heating of pipes or pipe systems.
[F16L 53/38] using elongate electric heating elements, e.g. wires or ribbons -- F16L 53/38 covers heating of pipes or pipe systems by means of elongated electric heating elements such as wires or ribbons. The classification applies to devices in which the heating element is integrated along the length of the pipe to provide uniform temperature control. It is used for mechanical engineering solutions involving pipe heating.
[F16L 53/70] Cooling of pipes or pipe systems -- F16L 53/70 pertains to the cooling of pipes or pipe systems, covering methods, devices and arrangements that remove heat from pipework or its contents. It includes heat-exchanger designs, coolant circulation, and temperature-control techniques used to lower pipe temperatures in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16L 53/75] using cooling fins -- F16L 53/75 relates to pipe-heating arrangements that employ cooling fins to manage temperature. The fins are attached to the pipe or its housing to dissipate heat from the surface, enabling more efficient control of the pipe's temperature during heating operations.
[F16L 55/00] Devices or appurtenances for use in, or in connection with, pipes or pipe systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for use in, or in connection with, pipes or pipe systems that are not covered by groups F16L1/00 – F16L53/00 , F16L57/00 or F16L59/00 , e.g. damping devices, means for stopping flow, devices for covering leaks, pigs or moles, or preventing accumulation of dirt.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Laying or reclaiming pipes; Repairing or joining pipes on or under water -> F16L1/00 - Repairing or joining pipes on or under water -> F16L1/26 - Supports for pipes, cables or protective tubing -> F16L3/00 - Devices for use where pipes, cables or protective tubing pass through walls or partitions -> F16L5/00 - Supporting pipes or cables inside other pipes or sleeves -> F16L7/00 - Pipes -> F16L9/00 - F16L11/00 - Pipe joints; Hose nipples -> F16L13/00 - F16L49/00 - Expansion-compensation arrangements for pipe-lines -> F16L51/00 - Heating of pipes or pipe systems; Cooling of pipes or pipe systems -> F16L53/00 - Protection of pipes or objects of similar shape against external or internal damage or wear -> F16L57/00 - Thermal insulation in general -> F16L59/00 - Cleaning of pipes -> B08B9/02 - Removal of blockages in pipes -> B08B9/027
[F16L 55/005] Devices restraining ruptured tubes from whipping -- Devices that secure a pipe or tube after it has ruptured to stop it from flailing or whipping, thereby protecting surrounding equipment and personnel. They are typically mounted on or near the pipe system and may include clamps, restraints, braces or split-ring assemblies designed to absorb the kinetic energy of a burst line.
[F16L 55/02] Energy absorbers -- F16L 55/02 covers energy absorbers installed in or connected to pipe systems, such as shock absorbers, dampers or protective cushions designed to mitigate pressure surges, vibrations or impact forces within the piping.
[F16L 55/027] Throttle passages -- Throttle passages are pipe-mounted components that regulate the flow rate or pressure of fluids within a piping system, typically by narrowing or modulating the conduit's cross-section. They are used in various mechanical engineering applications to control fluid dynamics and ensure system performance.
[F16L 55/02709] in the form of perforated plates -- The classification covers pipe-related devices that consist of perforated plates, used to distribute, filter, or regulate fluid flow within a pipe system. Such plates may serve as flow distributors, strainers or pressure-relief elements and are designed to be installed in or alongside pipes.
[F16L 55/02718] placed transversely -- F16L 55/02718 relates to devices or appurtenances that are positioned transversely across pipes or pipe systems, such as supports, fittings or clamps installed at right angles to the pipe axis. It falls within the mechanical-engineering subclass for pipe elements and connections.
[F16L 55/02727] placed parallel to the axis of the pipe -- In the CPC, entry F16L 55/02727 covers devices or appurtenances installed parallel to the longitudinal axis of a pipe, such as supports, guides or fittings that run alongside the pipe rather than across it. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units for pipes, and deals with components used in pipe systems.

[F16L 55/02745] by passing through a mass of particles or a porous member -- F16L 55/02745 covers pipe devices that convey fluid by forcing it through a bed of particles or a porous element, such as filters, sintered-metal screens, or other particulate media used to separate or clean the flow within a pipe system.
[F16L 55/02754] using a central core throttling the passage -- A device for pipe systems that controls fluid flow by placing a central core within the pipe to restrict or modulate the passage. The core acts as a throttling element, adjusting pressure or rate without requiring external valves. This approach is used to achieve precise flow regulation in mechanical-engineering applications.
[F16L 55/02763] using an element with multiple tubes -- The code F16L 55/02763 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers devices or appurtenances for pipe systems that employ a component containing several tubes, such as multi-tube heat exchangers or manifold assemblies.
[F16L 55/02772] using spirally or helically shaped channels -- A device or appurtenance for pipe or pipe-system use that incorporates spirally or helically shaped channels, typically to promote fluid movement, mixing, or pressure control. [F16L 55/02781] The regulating element being provided with radial outputs -- F16L 55/02781 pertains to regulating elements equipped with radial outlets, such as valves or flow-control devices that direct fluid outward from a central axis. [F16L 55/0279] The fluid flowing two or more times longitudinally in opposite directions, e.g. using parallel or concentric tubes -- This subclass covers pipe devices that cause a fluid to flow longitudinally more than once in opposite directions, typically by employing parallel or concentric tubes within a single pipe assembly. Such arrangements are used to enhance heat-transfer, mixing or separation processes in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16L 55/033] Noise absorbers -- This class covers devices attached to pipe systems that reduce acoustic noise, such as silencers, acoustic linings, or mufflers designed to dampen vibrations and sound transmission in fluid conveyance. It includes components integrated in pipe walls or fittings for noise control.
[F16L 55/0331] by inserting an elongated element in the pipe -- In the Cooperative Patent Classification, code F16L 55/0331 refers to pipe accessories that are installed by inserting an elongated element into a pipe, such as rods, pins or sleeves used for reinforcement, sealing, alignment or similar functions. It is grouped under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → pipes, covering devices or appurtenances for use in or with pipe systems.
[F16L 55/0332] by inserting a body of compressible material in the pipe -- F16L 55/0332 designates devices that create a seal or cushioning by placing a compressible material inside a pipe, typically to accommodate movement, reduce vibration, or provide insulation within pipe systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.
[F16L 55/0333] by means of an active system -- F16L 55/0333 designates devices or appurtenances for pipes that function by means of an active system, meaning they incorporate powered or otherwise controlled mechanisms to move, clean, regulate or otherwise manipulate fluid flow within pipe networks.
[F16L 55/0335] by means of external rings -- F16L 55/0335 designates pipe devices or appurtenances that are installed or secured by means of external rings, such as clamps or banding systems, which encircle the pipe to provide fixation, sealing or reinforcement within a pipe system.
[F16L 55/0336] by means of sound-absorbing materials -- F16L 55/0336 classifies devices or appurtenances for pipe systems that employ sound-absorbing materials to attenuate noise generated by fluid flow, vibration or pressure surges, such as acoustic liners, mufflers or insulated pipe sections. This subcategory falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes, focusing on noise-control solutions integrated with or attached to piping infrastructure.
[F16L 55/0337] by means of a flexible connection -- F16L 55/0337 designates devices or appurtenances for pipe systems that join sections by means of a flexible connection such as a hose, bellows or pliable joint, allowing relative movement, vibration or thermal expansion between the pipes. It is part of the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 55/0338] by means of a membrane -- F16L 55/0338 covers pipe-related devices that operate by means of a membrane, such as filtration, separation or barrier elements integrated into pipe systems. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes, and applies to any apparatus where a membrane provides the functional action within the pipe or its connections.
[F16L 55/035] in the form of specially adapted hangers or supports -- F16L 55/035 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to pipe-related devices that function as specially adapted hangers or supports, used to attach, suspend or stabilize piping systems and their components.
[F16L 55/04] Devices damping pulsations or vibrations in fluids -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Energy absorbers; Noise absorbers -> F16L55/02
[F16L 55/041] specially adapted for preventing vibrations  flexible pipe connections  F16L27/10 -- F16L 55/041 pertains to devices specifically designed for use in pipes or pipe systems to prevent vibrations, focusing on flexible pipe connections as detailed in F16L27/10.
[F16L 55/043] specially adapted for protecting instruments from water hammer or vibrations -- F16L 55/043 covers devices or appurtenances that are specifically designed to protect instruments from the effects of water hammer or vibration in pipe systems. It falls under the mechanical-engineering subclass for pipes and related equipment.
[F16L 55/045] specially adapted to prevent or minimise the effects of water hammer -- F16L 55/045 covers pipe components that are specifically designed to reduce or eliminate water-hammer effects, such as surge tanks, air chambers, or dampening devices installed in piping systems.
[F16L 55/05] Buffers therefor -- F16L 55/05 refers to buffers installed in pipe systems to absorb shocks, limit motion, or damp vibrations. These components serve as protective devices attached to or integrated with pipes, reducing mechanical stress and preventing damage during operation or maintenance.
[F16L 55/052] Pneumatic reservoirs -- F16L 55/052 refers to pneumatic reservoirs, devices that store compressed air or other gases to smooth pressure fluctuations and provide a ready supply within pipe or pipe-system installations. These reservoirs serve as ancillary components in pneumatic networks, helping maintain system performance and energy efficiency.
[F16L 55/053] the gas in the reservoir being separated from the fluid in the pipe -- **Definition:** This place covers:  pneumatic reservoirs having a membrane
[F16L 55/054] the reservoir being placed in or around the pipe from which it is separated by a sleeve-shaped membrane -- The F16L 55/054 entry covers pipe-system components in which a reservoir is positioned inside or around a pipe and isolated from the pipe's interior by a sleeve-shaped membrane. This arrangement allows fluid exchange or storage without direct contact between the pipe contents and the reservoir.
[F16L 55/055] Valves therefor -- F16L 55/055 covers valves that are specifically intended for installation in or connection with pipe networks, including devices that control, regulate or shut flow within plumbing or industrial piping. It falls under the mechanical engineering subclass for pipe elements, focusing on components that manage fluid movement.
[F16L 55/07] Arrangement or mounting of devices, e.g. valves, for venting or aerating or draining -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus for draining -> F16T
[F16L 55/09] Air-conditioning, e.g. de-watering, in pneumatic systems -- The F16L 55/09 subclass pertains to air-conditioning equipment integrated with pneumatic pipe systems, including de-watering and temperature regulation devices that keep the conveyed air or gas dry and at a suitable temperature for efficient operation.
[F16L 55/10] Means for stopping flow in pipes or hoses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for stopping flow from or in pipes or hoses.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Joints with fluid cut-off means -> F16L29/00 - Quick-acting joints with cut-off means -> F16L37/28 - Valves -> F16K
[F16L 55/1003] by introduction of paste, powder, particles, or the like -- F16L 55/1003 classifies devices or appurtenances for pipe systems that introduce a paste, powder, particles or similar material into the flow. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → pipes, covering insertion or dispensing mechanisms integrated with or attached to piping.
[F16L 55/1007] Couplings closed automatically when broken -- F16L 55/1007 covers pipe couplings that automatically shut off flow when the pipe or the coupling itself is broken, providing an immediate seal to prevent leakage or pressure loss. These safety devices are used in fluid transport systems where rapid isolation of a damaged section is required.
[F16L 55/1011] Soluble closing devices -- F16L 55/1011 concerns soluble closing devices used in pipe systems. These are components that can be dissolved or removed by chemical means to provide temporary sealing or to facilitate cleaning, typically installed on pipes or fittings. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.
[F16L 55/1015] Couplings closed automatically when disengaging force exceeds preselected value  F16L55/1007  takes precedence -- F16L 55/1015 covers couplings that automatically shut when the disengagement force exceeds a preset limit, used in pipe systems to prevent leaks. It falls under mechanical engineering - engineering elements and units - pipes, and supersedes the broader F16L55/1007 class.
[F16L 55/1018] Pivoting closing devices -- Pivoting closing devices are mechanisms that rotate about a hinge or axle to regulate flow in a pipe system, typically comprising a movable disc, flap or gate that seals against a seat when closed. They are used as valves, blinds or shutters to start, stop or throttle liquids, gases or slurries, and may be operated manually, hydraulically, pneumatically or electrically.
[F16L 55/1022] Fluid cut-off devices automatically actuated -- F16L 55/1022 classifies devices that automatically stop the flow of fluid in a pipe or pipe system, such as valves or shut-off mechanisms triggered by pressure, temperature, leakage detection, or other process signals without manual intervention. These are considered pipe-related appurtenances within the mechanical engineering domain.
[F16L 55/1026] Fire protection devices -- F16L 55/1026 denotes fire-protection devices for pipe systems, covering apparatus such as fire-resistant valves, dampers, shutters or other components that prevent the spread of fire through pipelines or are mounted on pipelines to provide fire safety.
[F16L 55/103] by temporarily freezing liquid sections in the pipe -- F16L 55/103 pertains to methods and devices used to create a temporary blockage in a pipe by freezing the liquid inside, facilitating maintenance or repair work without draining the entire system. This technique is particularly useful in plumbing and industrial applications where water or other fluids need to be temporarily stopped.
[F16L 55/105] Closing devices introduced radially into the pipe or hose -- F16L 55/105 classifies closing devices that are inserted radially into a pipe or hose, such as side-introduced valves, plugs or seals, used to shut off or control flow within pipe systems.
[F16L 55/11] Plugs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Closing means which can be fixed inside the pipe end.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - In situ expandable members introduced axially into pipes or hoses -> F16L55/128
[F16L 55/1108] fixed by screwing or by means of a screw-threaded ring -- This subclass covers pipe fittings or accessories that are attached to a pipe or pipe system by turning a threaded component or by using a screw-threaded ring to secure the part in place, providing a permanent or semi-permanent fixation.
[F16L 55/1116] glued or welded -- F16L 55/1116 relates to devices, fittings or appurtenances that are attached to pipes or pipe systems by means of glue or welding. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → pipes, and covers components bonded or welded to pipe structures.
[F16L 55/1125] fixed by rotating a limited amplitude -- This subclass covers pipe-related devices or appurtenances that are secured by applying a limited-amplitude rotation, such as clamps, couplings or fittings that lock in place when turned only a small angle. The classification is used for mechanisms that achieve fixing through this restricted rotational motion.
[F16L 55/1133] fixed by means of balls -- F16L 55/1133 covers devices where a pipe or pipe-system component is secured in a fixed position by using ball elements. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and refers to devices or appurtenances for use in or with pipe systems.
[F16L 55/1141] the plug being made of elastic material -- Elastic plugs intended for installation in pipe or pipe-system connections to seal or block flow, characterised by being made of an elastomeric material and used as a device or appurtenance for pipes.
[F16L 55/115] Caps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Closing means which can be fixed on the outside of the pipe end.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - In situ expandable caps introduced axially into pipes or hoses -> F16L55/1286
[F16L 55/1152] fixed by screwing or by means of a screw-threaded ring -- This subclass covers pipe components or accessories that are secured to a pipe or pipe system by screwing or using a threaded ring, including couplings, fittings, clamps and similar devices that rely on screw-threaded connections for fixation.
[F16L 55/1155] fixed by rotating a limited amplitude -- F16L 55/1155 relates to pipe components or fittings that are secured by a limited-amplitude rotation, such as clamps, couplings or brackets that lock with a small turn of the part. The mechanism provides a fixed connection in pipe systems without requiring full unthreading, enabling quick and repeatable fastening.
[F16L 55/1157] using hooks, pawls, or other movable or insertable locking members -- This subclass addresses pipe or pipe-system devices that employ hooks, pawls or other movable or insertable locking members to secure, retain or release connections, providing quick-acting or reversible fastening within the piping arrangement. It is situated under the broader F16L category for pipe-related engineering elements.
[F16L 55/12] by introducing into the pipe a member expandable -- F16L 55/12 covers devices that are introduced into a pipe and then expanded to form a seal, support or connection, such as expandable members or sleeves that lock in place after insertion. This sub-category falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes and pertains to appurtenances used with pipe systems.
[F16L 55/124] introduced radially into the pipe or hose -- F16L 55/124 pertains to devices or fittings that are introduced radially into a pipe or hose, extending from the pipe wall outward for purposes such as clamping, coupling, sealing or supporting the conduit. These elements are installed perpendicular to the pipe axis and are used to connect, reinforce or modify pipe- or hose-based systems.
[F16L 55/128] introduced axially into the pipe or hose -- The CPC code F16L 55/128 covers devices or appurtenances that are introduced axially into a pipe or hose, such as seals, couplings, liners, sensors or other components inserted along the pipe's axis to perform a function within the pipe system. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering subclass for pipe elements and units.
[F16L 55/1283] Plugging pig -- F16L 55/1283 covers plugging pigs, which are devices inserted into a pipe to block, seal or clean a section during testing or maintenance. They are propelled through the pipe to perform sealing, cleaning or pressure-testing functions and the code falls under Mechanical Engineering - Engineering Elements and Units - Pipes.
[F16L 55/1286] The closing device being a cap -- F16L 55/1286 concerns caps that serve as closing devices for pipes or pipe systems, sealing the open end to prevent leakage, contamination, or pressure loss. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes, and covers caps that may be removable, fixed, or equipped with sealing features.
[F16L 55/13] the closure device being a plug fixed by plastic deformation -- This CPC subclass addresses closure devices for pipe systems in which the plug is fixed by plastic deformation of the material, creating a permanent seal. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 55/132] the closure device being a plug fixed by radially deforming the packing -- F16L 55/132 covers pipe closure devices in which a plug is secured by radially deforming the surrounding packing to create a seal. The deformation locks the plug in place, providing a reliable shut-off for pipe or conduit systems. This subtype falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and devices/appurtenances for pipe systems.
[F16L 55/134] by means of an inflatable packing -- F16L 55/134 covers devices or accessories for pipe systems that achieve sealing, support or spacing by using an inflatable packing element. The inflatable pack expands within a pipe or around a conduit to create a tight, adjustable seal or to hold components in place.
[F16L 55/136] the closure device being a plug fixed by radially expanding or deforming a split ring, hooks or the like -- F16L 55/136 covers closure devices for pipe systems in which a plug is secured by radially expanding or deforming a split ring, hooks or similar mechanisms. The design provides a removable seal that locks in place by outward pressure on the pipe wall.
[F16L 55/16] Devices for covering leaks in pipes or hoses, e.g. hose-menders -- This subclass covers devices used to seal or patch leaks in pipe or hose systems, including temporary or permanent hose-menders. It falls within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, pipes, and addresses apparatus that protect or repair pipe connections.
[F16L 55/1604] by means of a by-pass conduit -- F16L 55/1604 pertains to pipe-related devices that incorporate a by-pass conduit, allowing fluid to be diverted around a section of a pipe or pipe system. This classification covers the design and arrangement of such bypass channels used for maintenance, flow control, or pressure regulation.
[F16L 55/1608] by replacement of the damaged part of the pipe -- The entry F16L 55/1608 designates pipe-related devices or appurtenances that are used by replacing a damaged portion of a pipe, covering the method of component substitution within a pipe system. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes.
[F16L 55/1612] by means of a plug -- F16L 55/1612 covers pipe-related devices that use a plug to block, seal or regulate fluid flow within a pipe or pipe system. The plug may be inserted, removable or fixed to provide a shut-off or isolation function.
[F16L 55/1616] the material forming the pipe or hose being self-sealing -- F16L 55/1616 covers pipe or hose constructions in which the material itself provides a seal, eliminating separate sealing devices. Such self-sealing materials are used in pipe systems to prevent leakage and simplify installation.
[F16L 55/162] from inside the pipe -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for covering leaks in pipes or hoses, e.g. hose-menders, by means of a plug -> F16L55/1612 - Devices for covering leaks in pipes or hoses, e.g. hose-menders, specially adapted for bends, branch units, branching pipes or the like -> F16L55/179
[F16L 55/163] a ring, a band or a sleeve being pressed against the inner surface of the pipe -- The code F16L 55/163 covers devices that are inserted into a pipe and pressed against its inner wall, such as rings, bands or sleeves that grip the interior surface to provide sealing, support or reinforcement. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.
[F16L 55/164] a sealing fluid being introduced in the pipe -- F16L 55/164 concerns devices or appurtenances that introduce a sealing fluid into a pipe to prevent leaks and enhance seal integrity. It is classified in the mechanical engineering section under engineering elements and units for pipes.
[F16L 55/1645] a sealing material being introduced inside the pipe by means of a tool moving in the pipe -- F16L 55/1645 covers devices or methods for placing a sealing material inside a pipe by means of a tool that travels through the pipe itself, allowing the sealant to be introduced directly into the pipe's interior during operation. This falls under the pipe-related subclass dealing with appurtenances for pipe systems.
[F16L 55/16455] a part of the tool defining, together with the inner wall of the pipe, an enclosed space into which sealing material is injected -- F16L 55/16455 covers a component of a pipe-sealing tool that, together with the inner wall of the pipe, creates an enclosed cavity into which sealing material is injected, ensuring the material is confined to the intended joint or crack for effective sealing.
[F16L 55/165] a pipe being inserted in the damaged section -- The code F16L 55/165 relates to devices or appurtenances used to repair pipe systems by inserting a replacement pipe segment into a damaged section, allowing restoration of flow without dismantling the whole line. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → pipes.
[F16L 55/1651] the flexible liner being everted -- This subclass covers devices where a flexible tubular liner is installed by eversion, meaning the liner is turned inside out as it is fed into a pipe to form a seal or reinforcement. It applies to pipe rehabilitation, sealing, or lining systems where the liner is introduced and inverted to conform to the host pipe interior.
[F16L 55/1652] the flexible liner being pulled into the damaged section -- The entry F16L 55/1652 covers devices and methods for inserting a flexible liner into a damaged portion of a pipe, enabling repair without excavating or replacing the pipe. It relates to pipe-rehabilitation systems that pull the liner through the pipe to seal cracks or restore integrity.
[F16L 55/1653] and being pressed into contact with the pipe by a tool which moves inside along the pipe -- This subclass covers devices or methods in which a tool moving inside a pipe presses a component, such as a liner, seal or fitting, into contact with the pipe wall, thereby securing it to the pipe interior.
[F16L 55/1654] and being inflated -- F16L 55/1654 relates to pipe-system devices that are inflated, such as bladders, balloons or inflatable seals, used to form or restore a seal, expand a pipe, support it during installation or testing, or temporarily block flow. The classification covers the structure, operation and means of inflating these components within or on pipes.
[F16L 55/1655] a pipe being formed inside the old pipe by winding strip-material -- F16L 55/1655 covers techniques for forming a new pipe inside an existing conduit by winding strip material around the inner surface, creating a reinforced or lined pipe without removal of the old pipe. This method is used to repair, upgrade or protect pipe systems while maintaining continuous operation.
[F16L 55/1656] materials for flexible liners -- F16L 55/1656 pertains to the materials employed for flexible liners that line or coat pipe systems, such as polymers, composites and other sealing compounds. These substances provide protection, corrosion resistance and flexibility, improving the durability and performance of pipes in mechanical-engineered applications.
[F16L 55/1657] lengths of rigid pipe being inserted  F16L55/1658  takes precedence -- F16L 55/1657 covers devices or appurtenances related to pipe systems that specify the lengths of rigid pipe sections being inserted. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes, and is superseded by the more specific subclass F16L 55/1658.
[F16L 55/1658] the old pipe being ruptured prior to insertion of a new pipe -- This subclass covers methods in which an existing pipe is intentionally ruptured before a replacement pipe is inserted, often to ease removal or create space for the new section. It also includes devices designed to create the rupture and to manage the subsequent insertion of the new pipe.
[F16L 55/168] from outside the pipe -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for covering leaks in pipes or hoses, e.g. hose menders, specially adapted for bends, branch units, branching pipes or the like -> F16L55/179

The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, specifically pipes and their ancillary components.
[F16L 55/1686] by winding a tape -- F16L 55/1686 pertains to the method of reinforcing or protecting pipes or pipe systems by winding a tape around them, ensuring structural integrity and durability. This technique is used in mechanical engineering for various applications requiring enhanced pipe performance.
[F16L 55/17] by means of rings, bands or sleeves pressed against the outside surface of the pipe or hose -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rings, bands or sleeves, including two half sleeves, for covering leaks in pipes from outside the pipe.
[F16L 55/1705] with a substantially radial tightening member -- The CPC subclass F16L 55/1705 covers pipe-related devices or appurtenances that incorporate a substantially radial tightening member, such as clamps or bands that tighten around a pipe cross-section to secure connections, fittings or seals.
[F16L 55/171] the ring or the sleeve being tightened by a wedge section -- F16L 55/171 covers pipe-related devices in which a ring or sleeve is secured by a wedge section that expands or contracts to create a tight fit. It applies to fittings and accessories that use this wedge-tightening principle to join, support or seal pipe sections within mechanical engineering systems.
[F16L 55/1715] the ring or the sleeve being tightened by hooks, pawls, or other movable members -- This subclass covers pipe fittings where a sealing ring or sleeve is tightened onto the pipe by mechanisms such as hooks, pawls or other movable members. It relates to devices that clamp or lock the ring in place, often to prevent leakage or reinforce the pipe connection.
[F16L 55/172] the ring, band or sleeve being tightened by a tangentially arranged threaded pin and a nut -- F16L 55/172 concerns pipe accessories in which a ring, band or sleeve is secured around a pipe by a threaded pin placed tangentially and a nut that tightens the assembly. The arrangement provides a clamping or sealing function for pipe or pipe-system components.
[F16L 55/1725] in which the threaded pin is rigid with the hose encircling member -- This CPC subclass covers pipe fittings in which a rigid threaded pin is combined with a hose that encircles the pin, forming a secure connection between pipe sections or to other components. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes, specifically devices or appurtenances for use in, or in connection with, pipes.
[F16L 55/175] by using materials which fill a space around the pipe before hardening -- It covers pipe-related devices that use a material introduced around the pipe which then hardens to fill the surrounding space, providing sealing, support or protection.
[F16L 55/178] by clamping an outer gasket against a joint with sleeve or socket -- F16L 55/178 pertains to devices for use in pipe systems that secure connections through the method of clamping an outer gasket against a joint using a sleeve or socket, ensuring tight seals and preventing leaks.
[F16L 55/179] specially adapted for bends, branch units, branching pipes or the like -- F16L 55/179 covers devices or appurtenances specifically designed for pipe bends, branch units or branching pipe sections, enabling connections or changes in direction within pipe systems. These adaptations may include specially shaped fittings, elbows, tees or other components that facilitate efficient flow and structural integration in mechanical engineering pipe networks.
[F16L 55/18] Appliances for use in repairing pipes -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Means for stopping flow in pipes or hoses -> F16L55/10
[F16L 55/24] Preventing accumulation of dirt or other matter in pipes, e.g. by traps, by strainers -- F16L 55/24 covers solutions that keep pipes clean by removing or retaining dirt, debris, or other material, typically using traps, strainers, or similar filtering devices integrated into the pipe or its accessories. These measures aim to maintain flow and prevent blockages.
[F16L 55/26] Pigs or moles, i.e. devices movable in a pipe or conduit with or without self-contained propulsion means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General features of pigs or moles for use in pipes or pipe systems.
[F16L 55/265] specially adapted for work at or near a junction between a main and a lateral pipe -- F16L 55/265 designates devices or accessories that are specially adapted for use at or near the junction where a main pipe connects to a lateral pipe, facilitating installation, maintenance, or flow control at that point in a pipe system.
[F16L 55/28] Constructional aspects -- F16L 55/28 covers the constructional aspects of devices and appurtenances used with pipes or pipe systems, such as supports, couplings, joints, seals and fittings that influence their shape, material, mounting or assembly. The classification deals with how these components are built and integrated into pipe networks.
[F16L 55/30] of the propulsion means, e.g. towed by cables -- F16L 55/30 classifies devices or appurtenances for pipe systems that provide the propulsion means, such as mechanisms that move or tow the pipe by cables. It falls under mechanical engineering - engineering elements and units - pipes, covering components that generate or transmit motion to the pipe.
[F16L 55/32] being self-contained -- The CPC code F16L 55/32 designates devices or appurtenances that are self-contained for use in or with pipe systems, meaning the functional unit incorporates all necessary parts without external connections. Typical examples include integrated valves, pumps, regulators or measurement units that operate independently within the piping network.
[F16L 55/34] the pig or mole being moved step by step -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for moving a pipe-cleaning or inspection device, called a pig or mole, incrementally through a pipe system. It includes step-by-step techniques for pushing or pulling the device along the pipeline.
[F16L 55/36] jet driven -- F16L 55/36 relates to jet-driven devices used with pipe systems, where a high-velocity fluid jet provides propulsion, thrust, or suction for moving fluids, cleaning, or pumping. These devices are mounted on or integrated into pipes and may include nozzles, ejectors, or venturi-type arrangements that generate a jet to achieve the desired function.
[F16L 55/38] driven by fluid pressure -- F16L 55/38 designates devices or appurtenances for pipe systems that are driven by fluid pressure. It covers mechanisms in which the movement or operation of pipe components is powered directly by the pressure of the fluid flowing through the system.
[F16L 55/40] of the body -- F16L 55/40 pertains to devices or appurtenances for use in, or in connection with, pipes or pipe systems, specifically addressing components of the body. This classification includes parts that are integral to the structure and function of piping systems, such as valves, fittings, and supports.
[F16L 55/42] gelled or degradable -- The F16L 55/42 subclass covers pipe-related devices or appurtenances that are made from or incorporate gelled or degradable materials, such as temporary seals, liners, or cleaning agents that dissolve or break down after service, facilitating removal or environmental disposal.
[F16L 55/44] expandable -- F16L 55/44 covers expandable devices for pipe systems, such as couplings, seals or fittings that are inserted in a pipe and then expanded to engage the inner surface for joining, sealing or reinforcement. These elements are designed to adapt to the pipe diameter after installation, providing a secure connection without requiring external clamps or welding.
[F16L 55/46] Launching or retrieval of pigs or moles -- F16L 55/46 covers apparatus and methods for inserting, launching, or retrieving pipeline inspection devices known as pigs or moles within pipe systems. It includes equipment such as launchers, pullers, and associated control mechanisms designed to move these devices along the pipe for cleaning, inspection, or maintenance.
[F16L 55/48] Indicating the position of the pig or mole in the pipe or conduit -- F16L 55/48 covers devices that indicate the location of a pig or mole traveling within a pipe or conduit. It relates to pipe accessories that provide positional information for pipeline inspection or cleaning tools.
[F16L 57/00] Protection of pipes or objects of similar shape against external or internal damage or wear -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Protection under water -> F16L1/123 - Supporting of pipes inside other pipes or sleeves -> F16L7/00 - Used in connection with end fittings of hoses -> F16L35/00 - Protection of pipes or pipe fittings against corrosion or incrustation -> F16L58/00 - Protecting of pipes during transport -> B65D59/00
[F16L 57/005] specially adapted for the ends of pipes -- This subclass covers devices, fittings or methods that are specifically designed for the ends of pipes to protect them from external impact, internal wear or other damage. It includes seals, caps, protective sleeves and similar elements that secure pipe terminations and preserve the pipe's integrity.
[F16L 57/02] against cracking or buckling -- F16L 57/02 covers protective measures that prevent pipes or similarly shaped objects from cracking or buckling, typically by using reinforcement, support structures, or material treatments that enhance resistance to internal pressure, external loads, or thermal stresses. The subclass falls within the broader category of pipe protection against damage or wear in mechanical engineering.
[F16L 57/04] against fire or other external sources of extreme heat -- F16L 57/04 relates to protection of pipes or similarly shaped components from fire or other extreme external heat sources. [F16L 57/06] against wear -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Protection against fire or sources of extreme heat -> F16L57/04
[F16L 58/00] Protection of pipes or pipe fittings against corrosion or incrustation -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Compound tubes -> F16L9/14

[F16L 58/04] Coatings characterised by the materials used -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Coatings in the form of a bandage -> F16L58/16
[F16L 58/06] by cement, concrete, or the like -- The subclass F16L 58/06 concerns protection of pipes or pipe fittings against corrosion or incrustation by cement- or concrete-based materials, typically applied as coatings, linings or encasings to interior or exterior surfaces. It is part of the mechanical engineering hierarchy under engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 58/08] by metal -- F16L 58/08 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the European and United States patent offices, specifies methods for protecting pipes or pipe fittings against corrosion or incrustation using metal. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with mechanical engineering elements and units, specifically focusing on pipes.
[F16L 58/10] by rubber or plastics -- F16L 58/10 refers to protective measures for pipes or pipe fittings that employ rubber or plastic materials, such as coatings, linings, sleeves or encapsulation, to guard against corrosion, scaling or incrustation. This subclass falls under the broader category of mechanical engineering elements for pipe protection.
[F16L 58/1009] the coating being placed inside the pipe -- F16L 58/1009 concerns the application of a protective coating to the interior surface of a pipe or pipe fitting to prevent corrosion or incrustation. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units related to pipes, and addresses methods or materials for internally placed coatings.
[F16L 58/1018] the protective layer being fixed by means of anchoring devices -- This CPC subgroup covers protective layers on pipes or pipe fittings that are secured in place by anchoring devices such as clamps, brackets or welds to prevent corrosion or incrustation. It pertains to mechanical engineering solutions for pipe protection.
[F16L 58/1027] the coating being a sprayed layer -- F16L 58/1027 relates to protective coatings applied to pipes or pipe fittings by spraying, forming a layer that shields the metal from corrosion or incrustation. It covers the composition and application of sprayed barrier layers used in mechanical engineering pipe systems.
[F16L 58/1036] the coating being a preformed pipe  F16L58/1027  takes precedence -- This subclass concerns protective coverings for pipes in which the coating is supplied as a preformed pipe segment that is applied to the pipe body. The coating may serve to prevent corrosion or incrustation. The related subclass F16L58/1027, which deals with similar protective measures, is considered the primary reference.
[F16L 58/1045] the coating being an extruded or a fused layer -- F16L 58/1045 relates to protective coatings applied to pipes or pipe fittings in the form of an extruded or fused layer, intended to prevent corrosion or incrustation. It is situated within mechanical engineering, under engineering elements and units and the pipes subgroup.
[F16L 58/1054] the coating being placed outside the pipe -- The code F16L 58/1054 covers methods and materials for applying a protective layer to the external surface of a pipe or pipe fitting. It includes processes that deposit coatings such as paints, polymer films or metallic layers aimed at preventing corrosion or incrustation. The classification is used for patents concerning external pipe protection in mechanical engineering.
[F16L 58/1063] the coating being a sheet wrapped around the pipe -- The entry relates to mechanical-engineering pipe protection, specifically a sheet-like coating that is wrapped around a pipe to guard against corrosion or incrustation. It falls under CPC group F16L 58/1063, which addresses methods of protecting pipes or pipe fittings.
[F16L 58/1072] the coating being a sprayed layer -- F16L 58/1072 covers methods and materials for applying a sprayed coating to pipes or pipe fittings to protect them from corrosion or incrustation. The classification relates to mechanical engineering, specifically engineered pipe elements.
[F16L 58/1081] the coating being a preformed pipe -- The entry F16L 58/1081 relates to protecting a pipe or pipe fitting from corrosion or incrustation by using a pre-formed pipe as the coating. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → pipes → protection of pipes or pipe fittings against corrosion or scale buildup.
[F16L 58/109] the coating being an extruded layer -- F16L 58/109 covers protective treatments for pipes and pipe fittings where the protective coating is applied as an extruded layer, such as extruded polymer or metal linings, intended to prevent corrosion or incrustation. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes.
[F16L 58/12] by tar or bitumen -- F16L 58/12 covers methods of protecting pipes or pipe fittings from corrosion or incrustation using tar or bitumen. It falls within the mechanical engineering domain, under engineering elements and units, specifically pipes. The classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F16L 58/14] by ceramic or vitreous materials -- F16L 58/14 relates to protective treatments for pipes or pipe fittings that employ ceramic or other vitreous materials to guard against corrosion or incrustation. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes, focusing on non-metallic barrier solutions for pipe durability.
[F16L 58/16] the coating being in the form of a bandage -- F16L 58/16 covers protective treatments for pipes or pipe fittings where the corrosion-inhibiting coating is applied as a bandage-type layer, such as a strip, wrap or sleeve that adheres to the pipe surface. This falls within the mechanical engineering subclass for pipe protection.
[F16L 58/18] specially adapted for pipe fittings -- The F16L 58/18 subclass covers protective measures such as coatings, linings or treatments that are specially adapted for pipe fittings to prevent corrosion or incrustation. It falls under mechanical engineering, within the broader categories of engineering elements and units and pipes, focusing on corrosion-protective solutions for fitting components.
[F16L 58/181] for non-disconnectable pipe joints -- F16L 58/181 concerns protective treatments for pipe joints that cannot be disconnected, including coatings, linings or other anti-corrosion or anti-incrustation measures applied to permanently joined sections of pipe or pipe fittings. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes.
[F16L 58/182] for screw-threaded joints -- F16L 58/182 covers protective measures for pipe fittings that use screw-threaded joints to guard against corrosion or incrustation. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for pipes, and includes coatings, linings or treatments designed to preserve threaded connections.
[F16L 58/184] for joints in which sealing surfaces are pressed together by means of a member, e.g. a swivel nut, screwed on or into one of the joint parts -- F16L 58/184 pertains to pipe joints whose sealing surfaces are pressed together by a member such as a swivel nut that is screwed onto or into one of the joint parts, providing corrosion or incrustation protection for the pipe or fitting.
[F16L 58/185] for joints with sleeve or socket -- F16L 58/185 falls under the CPC group for protecting pipes or pipe fittings against corrosion or incrustation. It specifically relates to protective solutions for pipe joints that employ a sleeve or socket to isolate the joint from the surrounding environment.
[F16L 58/187] for flanged joints -- F16L 58/187 covers protective measures applied to flanged pipe joints to prevent corrosion or incrustation. It pertains to engineering elements and units within the mechanical engineering field, specifically addressing how pipe fittings and flanges are treated or coated to extend service life and maintain integrity.
