[F16L 59/00] Thermal insulation in general -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Thermal insulation for pipes or hoses or pipe cover elements, or in general, e.g. when no other special application of the insulation is given.
[F16L 59/02] Shape or form of insulating materials, with or without coverings integral with the insulating materials -- F16L 59/02 covers the geometric shape or configuration of thermal-insulating materials for pipes, including any integral covering that is part of the insulation. It addresses how the insulation is formed or molded to fit pipe systems, whether or not the covering is built into the material.
[F16L 59/021] comprising a single piece or sleeve, e.g. split sleeves; consisting of two half sleeves; comprising more than two segments -- This CPC subclass F16L 59/021, under mechanical engineering - engineering elements and units - pipes - thermal insulation in general, relates to pipe insulation formed as a single piece or sleeve, split sleeves, two half sleeves, or assemblies of more than two segments.
[F16L 59/022] with a single slit -- F16L 59/022 covers pipe or conduit insulation that incorporates a single slit, usually a longitudinal opening that facilitates wrapping, fitting, expansion or inspection of the thermal cover. The slit enables the insulating material to be placed or removed without cutting the pipe, while still providing a continuous barrier around most of the circumference. This subclass falls under thermal insulation for pipes within the broader mechanical-engineering element hierarchy.
[F16L 59/023] with a hinge opposite the slit -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[F16L 59/024] consisting of two half sleeves -- F16L 59/024 covers pipe thermal insulation arrangements that use two half sleeves to encircle a pipe. The two half sleeves are positioned opposite each other and joined to form a complete insulating sleeve, providing protection against heat loss while allowing easy installation and maintenance.
[F16L 59/025] comprising more than two segments -- This CPC subclass covers pipe thermal-insulation constructions that are made up of more than two separate segments. It applies to systems where the insulation is divided into multiple sections to adapt to complex piping geometries, improve installation flexibility, or enhance heat-retention performance.
[F16L 59/026] Mattresses, mats, blankets or the like -- F16L 59/026 covers flexible thermal insulation products such as mattresses, mats, blankets and similar items used to protect pipes and related mechanical engineering components from heat loss or gain. These flexible insulating items are designed to wrap around or line pipe surfaces, providing thermal retention or shielding. The classification includes their design, material composition and methods of installation.
[F16L 59/027] Bands, cords, strips or the like for helically winding around a cylindrical object -- The F16L 59/027 class covers bands, cords, strips or similar flexible elements that are applied in a helical fashion around a cylindrical body, typically to provide reinforcement, protection, or thermal insulation for pipes or other rotating shafts. These components are designed to conform to the shape of the cylinder and may be used to improve strength, limit vibration, or retain heat.
[F16L 59/028] Compositions for or methods of fixing a thermally insulating material -- This subclass covers chemical compositions and processes used to attach or secure thermal insulation to pipe systems, including adhesives, binders and curing methods for fixing the insulating material.
[F16L 59/029] layered -- F16L 59/029 relates to pipes that employ thermal insulation formed by two or more distinct layers, such as combinations of metal, polymer, ceramic or other materials, arranged to enhance heat retention, protection or resistance to temperature variations.
[F16L 59/04] Arrangements using dry fillers, e.g. using slag wool -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements using dry fillers, e.g. slag wool, which is added to the object to be insulated by pouring, spreading, spraying or the like.
[F16L 59/06] Arrangements using an air layer or vacuum -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements using an air layer or vacuum, air meaning any type of gas
[F16L 59/065] using vacuum -- F16L 59/065 covers pipe insulation methods that employ a vacuum to reduce heat transfer, using the low-pressure space as the principal insulating medium. It falls within mechanical engineering, specifically the engineering elements and units for pipes and general thermal insulation.
[F16L 59/07] the air layer being enclosed by one or more layers of insulation -- F16L 59/07 covers pipe thermal insulation that incorporates an air space sealed between one or more layers of insulating material, focusing on designs that use an enclosed air layer to reduce heat loss in pipelines.
[F16L 59/075] the air layer or the vacuum being delimited by longitudinal channels distributed around the circumference of a tube -- This subclass covers pipe insulation where an air or vacuum layer is formed by a series of longitudinal channels that run around the tube's circumference, separating the pipe from the surrounding environment. The channels create a sealed gap that reduces heat transfer.
[F16L 59/08] Means for preventing radiation, e.g. with metal foil -- F16L 59/08 covers devices incorporated in pipe thermal insulation that shield heat-radiating surfaces, typically using metal foil or similar reflective layers to reduce radiative heat loss. The technology is applied to improve the efficiency of insulated piping systems by limiting thermal radiation from the pipe to its surroundings.
[F16L 59/10] Bandages or covers for the protection of the insulation, e.g. against the influence of the environment or against mechanical damage -- The code F16L 59/10 relates to bandages or covers used to protect thermal insulation on pipes and similar engineering elements. These accessories shield the insulation from environmental influences such as moisture, temperature changes, or mechanical damage.
[F16L 59/103] Rigid covers for tee pieces -- F16L 59/103 covers rigid protective sleeves fitted onto tee joints in pipe systems to provide thermal insulation, reduce heat loss and shield the connection from mechanical damage. The classification groups these covers with pipe engineering elements and broader thermal-insulation solutions in mechanical engineering.
[F16L 59/106] Flexible covers for flanges, junctions, valves or the like -- F16L 59/106 covers flexible protective or sealing covers designed to fit over flanges, pipe junctions, valves and similar fittings, providing insulation, protection against heat loss or environmental exposure, and facilitating easy removal and replacement. These covers are typically made of elastic or pliable materials that conform to the shape of the component.
[F16L 59/11] Rigid covers for elbows -- F16L 59/11 concerns rigid covers that are fitted over pipe elbows to provide thermal insulation, typically made of solid materials such as metal or polymer shells that enclose the elbow and reduce heat loss while protecting the pipe from external damage.
[F16L 59/12] Arrangements for supporting insulation from the wall or body insulated, e.g. by means of spacers between pipe and heat-insulating material -- F16L 59/12 addresses pipe installations where the thermal insulation is kept away from the pipe or surrounding wall by supports such as spacers, brackets or other retaining structures. The arrangement prevents direct contact between the pipe surface and the insulating material, improving heat management and reducing condensation or mechanical stress. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and general thermal insulation.
[F16L 59/121] for pipes passing through walls or partitions -- F16L 59/121 covers thermal insulation applied to pipes that penetrate walls or partitions, including methods and materials for reducing heat-loss at such junctions. The classification is part of the mechanical engineering section dealing with engineering elements, specifically pipe systems.
[F16L 59/123] Anchoring devices; Fixing arrangements for preventing the relative longitudinal displacement of an inner pipe with respect to an outer pipe, e.g. stress cones -- The CPC subclass F16L 59/123 covers anchoring devices and fixing arrangements that prevent an inner pipe from moving longitudinally relative to an outer pipe, such as stress cones and similar components used in pipe assemblies and thermal-insulation systems.
[F16L 59/125] Helical spacers -- F16L 59/125 pertains to helical spacers used for thermal insulation in pipes, falling under the broader categories of mechanical engineering and engineering elements and units. These spacers are designed to maintain spacing and support while providing insulation properties.
[F16L 59/13] Resilient supports -- Resilient supports are pipe-mounted mounting elements that absorb vibrations, thermal expansions and shocks while maintaining the pipe's position. They are typically made from elastomeric or spring-loaded materials and are used to protect piping systems and attached thermal-insulation components from mechanical stress.
[F16L 59/135] Hangers or supports specially adapted for insulated pipes -- F16L 59/135 covers hangers and supports that are specially designed for insulated pipes, providing mounting solutions that accommodate the additional bulk and thermal properties of pipe insulation. These devices belong to the mechanical engineering subclass dealing with pipe elements and are intended to secure insulated pipelines in a variety of installations.
[F16L 59/14] Arrangements for the insulation of pipes or pipe systems -- The F16L 59/14 subclass covers designs and methods for thermally insulating individual pipes or entire pipe networks, including the placement of insulating materials, jackets, sleeves, or protective housings. It addresses how the insulation is arranged around the pipe, mounted, sealed or supported to enhance heat-retention or temperature control.
[F16L 59/141] in which the temperature of the medium is below that of the ambient temperature -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Insulation for pipes conveying fluids below ambient temperature, e.g. cryogenic fluids.
[F16L 59/143] Pre-insulated pipes -- F16L 59/143 covers pre-insulated pipes, which are pipe sections whose thermal insulation is permanently bonded or integral to the pipe wall. It includes designs where insulation is applied during manufacture, forming a single component for transporting heated or cooled fluids in heating, cooling or industrial processes.
[F16L 59/145] providing fire-resistance -- F16L 59/145 covers methods and arrangements for giving pipes fire-resistance, typically by applying protective coatings, insulating layers, or incorporating fire-retardant materials that maintain pipe integrity during a fire. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and thermal insulation, addressing how to protect piping systems from high-temperature exposure.
[F16L 59/147] the insulation being located inwardly of the outer surface of the pipe -- The CPC subclass F16L 59/147 concerns pipes whose thermal insulation is applied on the inner side of the pipe wall, i.e., beneath the outer surface of the pipe. It covers methods, materials and arrangements for providing such internal insulation to reduce heat loss or protect the pipe.
[F16L 59/15] for underground pipes -- F16L 59/15 pertains to thermal insulation specifically designed for underground pipes, covering materials, methods or arrangements that protect buried pipelines from heat loss or gain. This subclass falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes → Thermal insulation in general, and is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification used by the EPO and USPTO.
[F16L 59/153] for flexible pipes -- F16L 59/153 pertains to thermal insulation methods and materials specifically designed for flexible pipes, addressing the unique challenges of maintaining thermal efficiency while accommodating the movement and flexibility of these conduits.
[F16L 59/16] Arrangements specially adapted to local requirements at flanges, junctions, valves or the like -- F16L 59/16 covers pipe thermal-insulation arrangements that are specially adapted to local requirements at components such as flanges, junctions, valves or similar fittings. It includes custom sleeves, seals or other devices designed to maintain heat retention while accommodating the geometry or service conditions of those connections.
[F16L 59/161] Housings for valves, tee pieces, or the like -- F16L 59/161 pertains to housings that enclose valves, tee pieces or similar pipe components, designed to provide thermal insulation and protect them from temperature extremes. These housings are classified within mechanical engineering under engineering elements and units for pipes, focusing on general thermal insulation solutions.
[F16L 59/163] Branch units; Insulation forming a whole with branches -- F16L 59/163 covers pipe branch fittings in which the insulation is incorporated as a single, integral unit together with the branching sections. The classification applies to components where the thermal-protective material forms a continuous layer over the entire branch assembly, rather than being applied separately. This enables a seamless insulated system for piping networks.
[F16L 59/165] Repairing insulated pipes -- F16L 59/165 covers methods and devices for repairing insulated pipes, focusing on restoring thermal insulation after damage. It includes techniques for re-applying or replacing insulation materials without dismantling the entire pipe system.

[F16L 59/18] adapted for joints -- F16L 59/18 refers to pipe systems where thermal insulation is incorporated specifically for joints. [F16L 59/181] Joints in which sealing surfaces are pressed together by means of a member, e.g. a swivel nut, screwed on or into one of the joint parts -- F16L 59/181 refers to pipe joints in which the sealing surfaces are pressed together by a separate member such as a swivel nut, screw or similar device, creating a pressure-tight connection often employed in thermal-insulated piping systems.
[F16L 59/182] Joints with sleeve or socket -- F16L 59/182 covers pipe joints created by inserting a sleeve or socket over the adjoining ends, securing the connection and often providing a cavity for thermal insulation. The classification applies to mechanical-engineering pipe assemblies where the sleeve or socket forms the primary joining element, facilitating leak-tight seals and protection against heat loss.
[F16L 59/184] Flanged joints -- Flanged joints are pipe connections that use a flat ring with bolt holes to join two pipe ends, allowing assembly, disassembly, and a sealed interface with a gasket. They are employed in mechanical engineering piping systems and can be combined with thermal insulation to reduce heat loss.
[F16L 59/185] Adjustable joints; Joints allowing movement -- F16L 59/185 covers pipe fittings that can be adjusted to accommodate angular, axial or rotational displacement, allowing movement while maintaining a seal and often providing thermal insulation. These adjustable joints are used in mechanical systems where pipe alignment varies or expansion occurs.
[F16L 59/187] Arrangements for connecting hoses to one another, to flexible sleeves or to rigid members -- F16L 59/187 covers designs and devices that join hoses together or attach hoses to flexible sleeves or rigid components, focusing on the mechanical arrangement of such connections. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically the pipe and thermal insulation categories, and relates to how fluid conduits are linked in equipment.
[F16L 59/188] Couplings of the quick-acting type -- The subclass F16L 59/188 covers quick-acting couplings intended for joining pipe sections and associated thermal-insulation components, enabling rapid assembly and disassembly without tools. These couplings are used where fast, reliable connections of insulated piping are required.
[F16L 59/20] for non-disconnectable joints -- F16L 59/20 pertains to thermal insulation applied to pipe joints that are permanently fixed and cannot be taken apart. The classification includes methods, materials or structures for protecting non-disconnectable joints from heat loss or temperature extremes.
[F16L 59/21] adapted for expansion-compensation devices -- F16L 59/21 refers to pipe thermal-insulation solutions that incorporate or are specially designed for expansion-compensation devices, allowing the insulation to accommodate pipe movement caused by temperature changes without loss of performance. This sub-class falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and general thermal insulation.
[F16L 59/22] adapted for bends -- The F16L 59/22 subclass covers pipe thermal insulation that is adapted for bends, i.e., insulation systems specifically designed to fit around curved pipe sections while maintaining effective thermal protection, often using flexible, segmented or specially shaped material.
[F16L 7/00] Supporting pipes or cables inside other pipes or sleeves, e.g. for enabling pipes or cables to be inserted or withdrawn from under roads or railways without interruption of traffic -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sleeves for supporting pipes, cables or protective tubing, between relatively movable points -> F16L3/01
[F16L 7/02] and sealing the pipes or cables inside the other pipes, cables or sleeves -- F16L 7/02 relates to methods and devices for sealing pipes or cables that are placed inside other pipes, cables or sleeves, typically used to protect and support the inner lines while allowing them to be inserted or withdrawn without disrupting traffic. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and the pipes subgroup.
[F16L 9/00] Pipes -- F16L 9/00 groups inventions relating to pipes, covering their design, manufacture, assembly, connection and supporting structures. It includes pipe shapes, materials, sealing and joining methods, as well as arrangements that guide fluids or gases within mechanical systems. The subclass is used for patents on pipe-based engineering solutions.
[F16L 9/003] with a rectangular cross-section  ducting arrangements in air-conditioning or ventilation  F24F13/02 -- F16L 9/003 covers pipe-like components that have a rectangular cross-section and are used as ducting in air-conditioning or ventilation systems, corresponding to the related subclass F24F13/02. It falls under the hierarchy Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes. This classification pertains to rectangular ducts for distributing conditioned air.
[F16L 9/006] specially profiled  F16L9/003  takes precedence -- F16L 9/006 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to pipes that are specially profiled, with a note indicating that F16L 9/003 takes precedence when applicable. This classification is used for detailed patent documentation and search purposes within mechanical engineering.
[F16L 9/01] of wood -- F16L 9/01 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the European and United States patent offices, specifies pipes made of wood within the broader category of mechanical engineering elements and units. This classification helps in organizing and searching for patents related to wooden pipes.
[F16L 9/02] of metal -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rigid pipes wound from sheets or strips, with or without reinforcement -> F16L9/16 - Rigid pipes obtained by bending a sheet longitudinally and connecting the edges -> F16L9/17 - Double-walled pipes; Multi-channel pipes or pipe assemblies -> F16L9/18 - Rigid pipes made of sound-absorbing materials or with sound-absorbing structure -> F16L9/21 - Rigid pipes composed of a plurality of segments -> F16L9/22
[F16L 9/04] Reinforced pipes -- F16L 9/04 covers reinforced pipes, which are pipe sections strengthened by external rings, ribs, sleeves or internal liners to resist higher pressures, mechanical stresses or external loads. The reinforcement can be metal, composite or other materials and is applied to improve durability and structural integrity in piping systems.
[F16L 9/042] the reinforcement comprising one or more layers of a helically wound cord, wire or strip  F16L9/047  takes precedence -- F16L 9/042 classifies pipe reinforcement that uses one or more helically wound layers of cord, wire or strip to strengthen the pipe wall. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes and is subordinate to the more specific subclass F16L 9/047, which takes precedence.
[F16L 9/045] using profiled strips -- This subgroup deals with pipe constructions that incorporate profiled metal strips as structural elements. The strips may be wrapped, fitted or assembled to form or reinforce the pipe wall, providing shape, stiffness or additional strength to the finished product.
[F16L 9/047] comprising reinforcement rings -- F16L 9/047 covers pipes that incorporate reinforcement rings, which are circumferential members fitted around the pipe wall to increase strength, stiffness and resistance to deformation under pressure or external loads. The rings can be welded, bolted or otherwise secured and are used in a range of mechanical engineering applications where enhanced pipe integrity is required.
[F16L 9/06] Corrugated pipes -- F16L 9/06 covers corrugated pipes, which are hollow tubular members with regularly spaced transverse or longitudinal ridges that increase stiffness and flexibility. These pipes are employed in drainage, irrigation, and protective covering applications, and the class sits within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.
[F16L 9/08] of concrete, cement, or asbestos cement, with or without reinforcement -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Rigid pipes wound from sheets or strips, with or without reinforcement -> F16L9/16 - Rigid pipes obtained by bending a sheet longitudinally and connecting the edges -> F16L9/17 - Double-walled pipes; Multi-channel pipes or pipe assemblies -> F16L9/18 - Rigid pipes made of sound-absorbing materials or with sound-absorbing structure -> F16L9/21 - Rigid pipes composed of a plurality of segments -> F16L9/22
[F16L 9/085] Reinforced pipes -- F16L 9/085 covers reinforced pipes, i.e., pipe sections that incorporate additional strengthening elements such as ribs, external sleeves, cords, or jackets to improve pressure, structural or impact resistance. The reinforcement may be applied to metallic, polymeric or composite pipes and is intended for applications where higher load-bearing capacity or durability is required.
[F16L 9/10] of glass or ceramics, e.g. clay, clay tile, porcelain -- F16L 9/10 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system covers pipes made from glass or ceramic materials, including clay, clay tiles, and porcelain, detailing their specific engineering aspects and applications.
[F16L 9/105] of glass -- F16L 9/105 pertains to pipes made specifically of glass, falling under the broader categories of mechanical engineering and engineering elements and units. This classification is used in the Cooperative Patent Classification system by the European Patent Office (EPO) and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO).
[F16L 9/12] of plastics with or without reinforcement -- F16L 9/12 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system refers to pipes made of plastics, which may include reinforced materials. This classification is used for inventions involving plastic piping that can be strengthened with various types of reinforcements to enhance structural integrity and performance.
[F16L 9/121] with three layers -- F16L 9/121 designates pipes that are constructed with three distinct layers - an inner conduit for fluid flow, a middle reinforcement layer providing mechanical strength, and an outer protective layer shielding against environmental damage. Such multi-layered pipe structures are used in mechanical-engineering applications where durability, pressure resistance, and corrosion protection are required.
[F16L 9/123] with four layers -- F16L 9/123 covers pipes that are constructed from four distinct layers, typically an inner liner, a reinforcement layer, a barrier layer and an outer protective layer, forming a multilayer structure for enhanced strength, corrosion resistance and pressure containment. This classification is used for pipe systems where the four-layer construction is a defining technical feature.
[F16L 9/125] electrically conducting -- The CPC entry F16L 9/125 pertains to pipes that are electrically conducting, including metallic, alloyed or otherwise coated conduits designed to carry electric current for grounding, electro-static discharge, power transmission or signalling within mechanical systems. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Pipes.
[F16L 9/127] the walls consisting of a single layer -- F16L 9/127 covers pipes whose walls are formed from a single, uninterrupted layer of material rather than a composite or multi-layer structure. It applies to mechanical-engineering components where the pipe wall consists of one homogeneous thickness, typically used for simple pressure-carrying or fluid-conveying applications.
[F16L 9/128] Reinforced pipes -- Reinforced pipes are tubular components whose structural capacity is increased by applying a strengthening element such as external ribs, sleeves, or a surrounding layer of metal or composite material. The reinforcement enables the pipe to withstand higher internal pressures, external loads, or corrosive environments, and is commonly used in hydraulic, fire-suppression, and high-temperature systems.
[F16L 9/133] the walls consisting of two layers -- F16L 9/133 covers pipe walls that are formed from a two-layer construction, typically a protective inner layer and a structural outer layer, used to improve strength, corrosion resistance or thermal insulation. The classification applies to any pipe where the separate layers are integral or bonded together as part of the pipe's construction.
[F16L 9/14] Compound tubes, i.e. made of materials not wholly covered by any one of the preceding groups -- F16L 9/14 covers compound tubes, which are pipe-like structures formed from two or more different materials that are not fully addressed by the other pipe categories. These tubes combine the properties of the constituent materials to achieve functions such as enhanced strength, corrosion resistance, or thermal insulation.
[F16L 9/147] comprising only layers of metal and plastics with or without reinforcement -- F16L 9/147 covers pipe constructions that consist exclusively of layered metal and plastic materials, optionally incorporating reinforcement. The classification applies to designs where the pipe wall is formed by alternating or combined metal and polymer layers, without the use of additional material types. This entry falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and pipes.
[F16L 9/153] comprising only layers of metal and concrete with or without reinforcement -- F16L 9/153 concerns pipes that are made only of metal and concrete layers, optionally incorporating reinforcement, and are used as mechanical-engineering elements.
[F16L 9/16] wound from sheets or strips, with or without reinforcement -- F16L 9/16 covers pipes that are formed by winding sheets or strips, optionally reinforced, to create a tubular structure. Such wound pipes are used where flexibility, strength or corrosion resistance is required, often in hydraulic or fluid-transport applications.
[F16L 9/165] of metal -- F16L 9/165 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system, used by the European and United States patent offices, specifies pipes made of metal. This classification is part of a broader category that includes mechanical engineering elements and units, specifically focusing on pipes within this technical area.
[F16L 9/17] obtained by bending a sheet longitudinally and connecting the edges -- F16L 9/17 covers pipe manufacture where a flat sheet is bent along its length to form a cylindrical shape and the adjoining edges are joined, typically by welding, brazing or other bonding methods, creating a seamless or welded pipe from a single longitudinally rolled sheet.
[F16L 9/18] Double-walled pipes -- F16L 9/18 covers double-walled pipes, which consist of an inner pipe surrounded by a second protective outer pipe, often with a sealing space between them. The design provides enhanced resistance to corrosion, pressure, or leakage and is used in water, gas, or chemical transport systems.
[F16L 9/19] Multi-channel pipes or pipe assemblies -- F16L 9/19 relates to multi-channel pipes or pipe assemblies, meaning pipes that incorporate two or more separate flow channels within a single outer sheath or combine several individual pipes into one unit to provide multiple fluid paths. It is applied in mechanical engineering for transporting liquids, gases or slurries through parallel conduits.
[F16L 9/20] Pipe assemblies -- F16L 9/20 covers pipe assemblies, i.e., the combination of pipe sections, fittings, couplings, supports and related components that form a complete piping system. The subclass addresses how these elements are arranged, connected and integrated within mechanical engineering applications.
[F16L 9/21] made of sound-absorbing materials or with sound-absorbing structure -- The classification covers pipes that are made from or contain sound-absorbing materials, or that incorporate a structure designed to dampen acoustic vibrations. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, pipes, and is indexed as CPC F16L 9/21.
[F16L 9/22] Pipes composed of a plurality of segments -- **Definition:** This place covers: Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  **Glossary:** In this place, the following terms or expressions are used with the meaning indicated: The term "segments" means radial segments, not axial segments.
[F16M] FRAMES, CASINGS OR BEDS OF ENGINES, MACHINES OR APPARATUS, NOT SPECIFIC TO ENGINES, MACHINES OR APPARATUS PROVIDED FOR ELSEWHERE -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Frames, casings, beds for machines or engines not otherwise provided for in the classification. The machines or engines are attached to the frames, casings or beds in order to provide stability to the machines or engines, and also to make easier the use, the handling and the transportation of the machines or engines. This subclass also covers stands or support for one or more articles not otherwise provided for in the classification. The stands or supports can lie on the floor, can be attached to another object, can be attached to a human body, or can be attached to the wall or the ceiling. The object attached is heldi or resting steady on the support or stand and can also possibly move relatively to the surface on which the support is attached or lies thanks to the different degrees of freedom that the support of stand allows.
[F16M 1/00] Frames or casings of engines, machines or apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This group concerns frames or casings for machines or engines or the like, in order to make easier handling, transportation, fixation or use of the machines or engines or the like.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: DE102009052748
[F16M 1/02] for reciprocating engines or similar machines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Crankcase or casings for reciprocating engines in general. If the crankcase is used in relation with a special type of engine (e.g. internal combustion engine) then it has to be classified in the specific domain and not in F16M1/02 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Casings for internal combustion engines -> F02F7/00
[F16M 1/021] for housing crankshafts -- The F16M 1/021 subclass covers frames, casings or housings specifically designed to contain the crankshaft of an engine, machine or apparatus. It pertains to structural elements that support and protect the crankshaft while integrating with the overall engine or machine framework. This falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and frames or casings of engines, machines or apparatus.
[F16M 1/022] of tunnel type, i.e. wherein the crankshaft can only be introduced axially -- F16M 1/022 covers tunnel-type frames or casings for engines, machines or apparatus in which the crankshaft can be inserted solely along its axis. The design limits crankshaft entry to an axial direction, distinguishing it from other frame configurations.
[F16M 1/023] specially adapted for engines or machines with star-shaped cylinder arrangement -- F16M 1/023 covers frames or casings that are specially designed to accommodate engines or machines with a star-shaped cylinder arrangement, providing support for the angular layout of the cylinders and integration with surrounding components. It falls under mechanical engineering elements for frames, casings or beds not specific to any particular engine or apparatus.
[F16M 1/024] facilitating assembly of power-transmitting parts of engines or machines, e.g. of connecting-rods -- This subgroup deals with design features that aid the assembly of power-transmitting parts such as connecting rods within frames, casings or beds of engines and machines. It is part of CPC class F16M 1, which covers frames or casings not specific to a particular engine, machine or apparatus.
[F16M 1/025] Assembling bearings in casings, e.g. having anchor bolts -- This subclass concerns the installation of bearings within the housings or frames of engines, machines or other apparatus, where the bearing is secured by anchor bolts or similar fastening features. It covers the particular arrangement and method of assembling the bearing into the casing.
[F16M 1/026] for housing movable engine or machine parts other than crankshafts, e.g. valve-gear housings -- F16M 1/26 refers to frames or casings designed to contain movable engine or machine components other than crankshafts, such as housings for valve gear. It falls under the mechanical engineering subclass for general frames, casings or beds of engines, machines or apparatus.
[F16M 1/04] for rotary engines or similar machines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Crankcase or casings for rotary engines in general. If the crankcase is used in relation with a special type of engine, then it has to be classified in the specific domain and not in F16M1/02 .
[F16M 1/08] characterised by being built-up of sheet material or welded parts -- The subclass F16M 1/08 refers to frames, casings or beds of engines, machines or apparatus that are constructed from sheet material or assembled from welded parts, rather than being cast or forged as a single piece. It is used for mechanical-engineering elements where a fabricated, sheet-metal or welded structure forms the primary support.
[F16M 11/00] Stands or trestles as supports for apparatus or articles placed thereon  -- **Definition:** This place covers:  F16M11/00 and F16M13/00 and the depending lower groups are used to classify the features in relation:
[F16M 11/02] Heads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for attachment of an apparatus and when existing, the first cinematically independent joint assembly directly attached to the latter.  Particular case: If the support only consist of the connection and no movement of the object to be supported is allowed by the support once attached, then the head will be the attachment and the rest of the support is an undercarriage.
[F16M 11/04] Means for attachment of apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The supports which do not allow any movement of the apparatus to be supported and which allow a permanent connection of the apparatus e.g. by screws, this connection is considered as being the head. Then the undercarriage (monolithic bloc not allowing any movements) should be classified in F16M11/22 and eventually in F16M2200/08 in case of other special features of the foot or base stand.  Illustrative example: JP2010175998

[F16M 11/043] Allowing translations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  F16M11/043 should be used when the translation possible is not clearly defined with respect to the direction and that it is possible that the supports be positioned such that translation in all three directions is possible. If the support can only be used such that the translation is always a translation as defined in one of the dependent groups then this group should be given.  Illustrative example: EP2288960

[F16M 11/125] for tilting and rolling  F16M11/14  takes precedence -- F16M 11/125 designates stands or trestles that support apparatus or articles and include mechanisms for tilting or rolling the load, allowing repositioning, maintenance or transport. It applies to generic frames, casings or beds not tied to a specific engine or machine, falling under mechanical-engineering support elements.
[F16M 11/126] for tilting and panning  F16M11/14  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass F16M 11/126 designates stands or trestles used as supports for apparatus or articles that are capable of tilting and panning. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering group for frames, casings or beds of machines and is a narrower child of the broader F16M 11/14 category, which takes precedence.
[F16M 11/128] for panning and rolling  F16M11/14  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass (F16M 11/128) concerns stands or trestles that support apparatus or articles while panning and rolling, a specific type of support structure within the broader group for frames, casings or beds of engines, machines or apparatus. It is a narrower subdivision of F16M 11/14, which covers generic support stands.
[F16M 11/14] with ball-joint -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: us2011020055
[F16M 11/16] Details concerning attachment of head-supporting legs, with or without actuation of locking members thereof -- This subclass deals with the design and mechanisms for linking the legs that support the head of a stand or trestle, including any devices that lock or release the leg positions. It covers both permanent and adjustable attachments and how the legs are secured to the frame.
[F16M 11/18] with mechanism for moving the apparatus relatively to the stand -- F16M 11/18 covers stands or trestles used as support structures for equipment or items placed on them, where the stand includes a mechanism that moves the apparatus relative to the support, such as slides, lifts or rotating devices. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering elements for frames, casings or beds that are not specific to particular machines.
[F16M 11/20] Undercarriages with or without wheels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Definition of undercarriage:  everything making up the stand under the head but not including this one (see definition of head in F16M11/02 ).

[F16M 11/2021] around a horizontal axis  F16M11/2035  takes precedence -- F16M 11/2021 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers stands or trestles that rotate around a horizontal axis, with specific emphasis on those where F16M11/2035 takes precedence. This classification is used for mechanical supports designed to hold apparatus or articles and allows for precise categorization of patent applications related to these structures.
[F16M 11/2028] for rolling, i.e. for creating a landscape-portrait rotation -- F16M 11/2028 refers to stands or trestles that support apparatus or articles while they are rotated to change orientation, such as converting a portrait layout to a landscape layout or the reverse. The classification applies to mechanical-engineering frames used specifically for rolling or rotating loads during positioning or processing.
[F16M 11/2035] in more than one direction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Contains the undercarriages constituted of several independent joints and/or allowing tilting, panning and rolling.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: US2007164176
[F16M 11/2042] constituted of several dependent joints -- F16M 11/2042 is a CPC subclass describing stands or trestles that support apparatus or articles, forming part of frames, casings or beds not tied to a specific engine or machine. The entry specifies constructions that are assembled from several dependent joints, indicating a modular or multi-joint support structure.
[F16M 11/205] the axis of rotation intersecting in a single point, e.g. gimbals -- F16M 11/205 relates to frames, casings or beds that serve as stands or trestles supporting apparatus whose axis of rotation meets at a single point, such as in gimbal arrangements. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically under engineering elements and units for non-engine-specific frames and supports.

These members may be ribs, pads, serrations or other surface features that increase friction with the floor or base.

[F16M 13/00] Other supports for positioning apparatus or articles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In F16M13/00 , the objects to be supported are hand-held apparatus (e.g. pda, tablet, gps, small portable screens, radio) and these objects are lying or resting on a support. The supports need also to be hand held support. A heavy tripod for a camera (which is a handheld apparatus) is not classified in F16M13/00 and depending lower groups as this support is not handheld. A support classified in F16M13/00 can also be classified in F16M13/022 , e.g. a support provided with suction cup for a GPS apparatus.  This group only contains support separated from the object to be supported contrary to F16M13/005 and only support for handheld apparatus.  Illustrative example: US2009230161
[F16M 13/005] integral with the apparatus or articles to be supported -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The support is integrated in the object.  For support integrated in the object see the illustrative example: US2009314915  and: US2010133414
[F16M 13/02] for supporting on, or attaching to, an object, e.g. tree, gate, window-frame, cycle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The supports are attached to a wall or at another object like e.g. a post.  Support attached to the wall, see the illustrative example: WO2011003115
[F16M 13/022] repositionable -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Supports easily repositionable using e.g. clamps; see the illustrative example: US2010264281  or suction cups: US2011/0024587
[F16M 13/025] Corner supports -- F16M 13/025 covers corner supports, which are elements affixed to the corners of frames, casings or beds to locate and hold machinery or other articles in position. These devices provide stability and alignment for equipment where the structure is not otherwise specially designed for the engine, machine or apparatus.
[F16M 13/027] Ceiling supports -- **Definition:** This place covers:  See the illustrative example: EP2288960
[F16M 13/04] for supporting on, or holding steady relative to, a person, e.g. by chains -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: US2010254696
[F16M 13/06] also serviceable for other purposes, e.g. to be used as spade, chair, ski-stick -- F16M 13/06 covers generic support structures that are primarily intended to position apparatus or articles but are also designed to be repurposed for secondary functions such as a spade, a chair, or a ski-stick. It falls under mechanical engineering, within the subgroup for frames, casings or beds of engines and machines, and specifically addresses other supports for positioning that have additional serviceable uses.
[F16M 13/08] for use as a walking-cane -- F16M 13/08 designates supports used to position apparatus or objects that are shaped as walking canes. These devices combine a handle or frame for personal assistance with a function for stabilising or locating items, and are classified under mechanical-engineering frames and casings.
[F16M 2200/00] Details of stands or supports -- F16M 2200/00 covers the design and construction of stands or supports that form part of frames, casings or beds used in machines, engines or apparatuses. It includes details such as mounting points, load-bearing structures and adjustment mechanisms that secure equipment without being integral to the primary functional components. This subclass falls under the broader group of mechanical engineering elements for frames and housings.
[F16M 2200/02] Locking means -- F16M 2200/02 pertains to locking means employed in stands or supports that constitute frames, casings or beds for engines, machines or apparatus. The classification covers devices that secure or retain components in a fixed position, such as pins, clamps, latches or other fastening mechanisms integrated into supporting structures.
[F16M 2200/021] for rotational movement -- This subclass covers frames, casings or beds that act as stands or supports designed to permit rotational movement of the supported equipment, including turntables, rotating platforms, swivel mounts and other rotating support structures for machines and apparatus.
[F16M 2200/022] by friction -- The F16M 2200/022 subclass covers stands, supports or similar frame elements whose holding or adjustment is achieved by friction, such as friction pads, clutches, or friction-based locks. It applies to mechanical structures where friction is the primary means of securing the component.
[F16M 2200/024] by positive interaction, e.g. male-female connections -- The code F16M 2200/024 covers details of stands or supports in frames, casings or beds of machines where the components are joined by a positive interaction such as male-female connections, enabling secure attachment without additional fasteners. It falls under the Mechanical Engineering hierarchy for engineering elements and units.
[F16M 2200/025] for translational movement -- This CPC subclass covers frames, casings or beds that provide translational movement of a supported element, describing details of stands or supports such as guides, slides, linear bearings and adjustment mechanisms that enable straight-line motion.
[F16M 2200/027] by friction -- F16M 2200/027 classifies stands or supports that rely on friction to hold or position components. It falls under mechanical engineering for frames, casings or beds of engines and machines, covering friction-based methods for stabilising elements without specifying a particular engine or apparatus.
[F16M 2200/028] by positive interaction, e.g. male-female connections -- This subclass covers designs of frames, casings or beds where components are joined by positive interaction such as male-female connections, providing a mechanical interlock without adhesives or friction. It pertains to details of stands or supports within the broader mechanical engineering hierarchy.
[F16M 2200/04] Balancing means -- F16M 2200/04 covers balancing means incorporated into frames, casings or beds of machines and apparatus. These devices are designed to counteract uneven mass distribution or dynamic forces, reducing vibration and improving stability of the supported structure. The classification applies to any stand or support that includes such balancing mechanisms.
[F16M 2200/041] for balancing rotational movement of the head -- F16M 2200/041 covers features of stands or supports that are configured to balance the rotational movement of a head, such as a cutting or tool head, within a frame or housing of machinery. It is part of the mechanical engineering class for frames, casings or beds of engines, machines or apparatus.
[F16M 2200/042] for panning movement -- The subclass F16M 2200/042 covers designs of stands or supports that provide a panning movement-such as swinging or rotating motion-to frames, casings or beds used with engines, machines or other apparatus. It includes details of the supporting structure that enables this lateral or arcuate motion.
[F16M 2200/044] for balancing rotational movement of the undercarriage -- F16M 2200/044 is a subclass of the CPC that deals with frames, casings or beds incorporating means to balance the rotational movement of an undercarriage. It covers supports or stands designed to counteract uneven forces and improve stability of rotating undercarriage assemblies in machines.
[F16M 2200/045] for panning movement -- This CPC subclass pertains to mechanical frames, casings or beds that include stands or supports designed to allow a panning movement. It covers devices where the support structure enables rotation about a vertical axis for positioning or sweeping actions. Used for machinery where the whole assembly or a mounted component pans.
[F16M 2200/047] for balancing translational movement of the head -- The code F16M 2200/047 covers features of frames, casings or supports that provide a means for balancing the translational movement of a head, such as a tool or work-piece holder, within a mechanical assembly. It applies to designs that counteract linear displacement to improve stability and precision of the head during operation.
[F16M 2200/048] for balancing translational movement of the undercarriage -- The F16M 2200/048 subclass covers stands or support structures designed to balance the translational movement of an undercarriage within a frame, casing or bed of a machine or apparatus. It pertains to mechanical engineering solutions that stabilize or counteract linear motion of the under-carriage during operation.
[F16M 2200/06] Arms -- F16M 2200/06 - Arms covers structural arms that form part of frames, casings or supports for machines and apparatus, describing how such arms are shaped, joined or articulated to provide bearing or mounting functions. It is used for patents dealing with supporting or positioning elements rather than weapon arms.
[F16M 2200/061] Scissors arms -- F16M 2200/061 classifies scissors-type arms that form part of stands, supports or similar structures. The code covers pivoting, hinged or foldable arms used to adjust height, angle or reach of a frame, such as in collapsible workbenches, adjustable scaffolding or portable mounting brackets. These elements belong to the broader class of mechanical-engineering frames and casings that are not specific to a particular machine.
[F16M 2200/063] Parallelogram arms -- F16M 2200/063, titled Parallelogram arms, is a subclass of mechanical engineering that deals with frames, casings or beds of machines and the details of stands or supports. It covers the design and construction of arms forming a parallelogram linkage, used to keep a load or platform level while it moves in a translating motion.
[F16M 2200/065] with a special structure, e.g. reinforced or adapted for space reduction -- The class covers frames, casings or beds of machines or apparatus that incorporate a special structure such as reinforcement, folding, nested sections or other design features intended to increase strength while minimizing overall size or facilitating compact storage. It includes stand or support designs where the geometry is adapted for space-saving or load-bearing efficiency.
[F16M 2200/066] being part of the head -- F16M 2200/066 covers features of stands or supports that are incorporated into the head of frames, casings or beds for engines, machines or apparatus. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Frames, Casings or Beds, focusing on head-related details.
[F16M 2200/068] being part of the undercarriage -- F16M 2200/068 covers components that form part of the undercarriage of a machine, such as stands, supports or frames that provide structural support below the main body. It falls under mechanical engineering elements and units dealing with frames, casings or beds that are not specific to a particular engine, machine or apparatus.
[F16M 2200/08] Foot or support base -- F16M 2200/08 relates to the foot or support base of a frame, casing or bed that provides a standing or supporting function for machines or apparatus. It covers the design, shape, mounting and load-bearing features of such bases used to stabilize equipment.
[F16M 3/00] Portable or wheeled frames or beds, e.g. for emergency power-supply aggregates, compressor sets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: EP0976967

[F16N] LUBRICATING -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Processes for applying liquids or other fluent materials to a surface, to obtain an anti-friction surface -> B05D5/08 - Lubricating compositions; Selection of particular substances as lubricants -> C10M  **Glossary:** - lubricant: any fluid (e.g. water, oil), fluent material (e.g. particulate graphite), semi-fluid material (e.g. oil with thickener), or semisolid material (e.g. grease), or any mixture of such types of substance, which is used for the specific purpose of lubrication. - lubrication: apparatus, arrangements or methods for introducing or applying a thin layer of a discrete lubricant between two or more relatively-moving adjacent solid surfaces of a device or apparatus, for the specific purpose of reducing friction, heat or wear between the adjacent surfaces.
[F16N 1/00] Lubrication devices or arrangements for oil or grease -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Modifying portions of a work device for the supply or transfer of lubricant, or altering the moving portions of a work device to facilitate reducing friction that could respectively not be covered by a specific application for lubrication (see list of references relevant to classification mentioned above such as. B62J31/00 : Lubrication of cycles). This group is seldom used.
[F16N 11/00] Arrangements for supplying grease from a stationary reservoir or the equivalent in or on the machine or member to be lubricated -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements for supplying grease from a stationary reservoir or the equivalent in or on the machine or member to be lubricated such as e.g. grease cups, hand-actuated grease cups, stauffer cups, spring-loaded devices, weight-loaded devices, or by pressure of another fluid (the scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).This group cover some structure to facilitate the higher (for lubricating-pumps see F16N13/00 )
[F16N 11/02] Hand-actuated grease cups, e.g. Stauffer cups -- The entry covers devices that supply grease from a stationary reservoir by manual operation, such as hand-operated grease cups often called Stauffer cups. These arrangements allow an operator to press or squeeze a cup to transfer grease directly to the part or mechanism being lubricated.
[F16N 11/04] Spring-loaded devices -- F16N 11/04 classifies spring-loaded devices used in grease-supply arrangements that draw lubricant from a fixed reservoir to a machine component. The category includes mechanisms where a spring provides the force to dispense grease, such as spring-actuated greasing fittings, dispensers and automatic lubricators.
[F16N 11/06] Weight-loaded devices -- F16N 11/06 covers weight-loaded devices, i.e., mechanisms that employ a load or gravity-acting weight to drive the supply of grease from a stationary reservoir to a machine or component, such as weight-actuated grease pumps or distributors within the lubricating arrangements of mechanical engineering.
[F16N 11/08] with mechanical drive, other than directly by springs or weights -- F16N 11/08 relates to lubricating arrangements that supply grease from a stationary reservoir to a machine or component using a mechanical drive that is not a spring or weight, such as a gear-driven or motor-powered pump, to provide continuous lubrication of moving parts.
[F16N 11/10] by pressure of another fluid -- This subclass covers arrangements that deliver grease from a stationary reservoir to a machine or component by using the pressure of a second fluid, such as oil, air or another liquid, to force the grease through the supply system. It falls under lubricating methods within engineering elements and units of mechanical engineering.
[F16N 11/12] by centrifugal action -- F16N 11/12 covers lubricating systems that deliver grease from a fixed reservoir to a machine or component using centrifugal force. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, lubricating, specifically arrangements for supplying grease from a stationary source.
[F16N 13/00] Lubricating-pumps -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubricating-pumps such as e.g. with a reciprocating piston, with an adjustable reciprocating pumps, an actuation of lubricating-pumps by hand or foot, a mechanical drive, or fluid drive, with relative movement of pump parts being produced by inertia of one of the parts or of a driving member, with a rotary pumps, or with distributing equipment (the scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 13/02] with reciprocating piston -- F16N 13/02 designates lubricating-pump designs that employ a reciprocating piston to move fluid. It falls under the broader class of mechanical engineering elements for lubrication, specifically focusing on piston-driven pump mechanisms used to supply oil or other lubricants.
[F16N 13/04] Adjustable reciprocating pumps -- F16N 13/04 covers adjustable reciprocating pumps, a type of lubricating-pump where the piston or plunger moves back and forth to displace fluid and the pump's stroke or displacement can be varied by the operator. These pumps provide precise flow control for applications such as oil circulation, pressure testing and variable-rate lubrication. They belong to the mechanical-engineering subclass for lubricating-pump designs.
[F16N 13/06] Actuation of lubricating-pumps -- F16N 13/06 pertains to the actuation of lubricating-pumps, describing mechanisms that start, stop or regulate pump operation within mechanical systems. It includes various actuator types and control methods used to ensure proper lubrication flow in engineering components.
[F16N 13/08] by hand -- Hand-operated lubricating pumps are portable devices that manually deliver grease or oil to bearings and other moving parts for maintenance and wear reduction. They are typically used where powered pumps are impractical, allowing precise application of lubricant by an operator.
[F16N 13/10] with mechanical drive -- This subclass covers lubricating pumps that are powered by a mechanical drive, such as gears, belts or shafts, rather than by hydraulic or pneumatic means. It includes designs where the pump's operating force is transmitted directly from an engine, motor or other rotating device to move lubricant through the system.
[F16N 13/12] with ratchet -- F16N 13/12 covers lubricating pumps that incorporate a ratchet mechanism, allowing the pump to advance incrementally or lock in position during operation. The ratchet feature can facilitate controlled dosing of lubricant, prevent backflow, or enable manual resetting without continuous power. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering elements for lubrication systems.
[F16N 13/14] with cam or wobble-plate on shaft parallel to the pump cylinder or cylinders -- F16N 13/14 classifies lubricating-pump designs where a cam or wobble-plate is mounted on a shaft that runs parallel to the pump cylinder or cylinders, allowing the pumping element to be driven by the cam's reciprocating motion. This subcategory falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and lubricating.
[F16N 13/16] with fluid drive -- F16N 13/16 covers lubricating pumps that are powered by a fluid drive, meaning the pump's operating motion is generated by hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid-pressured mechanisms rather than an electric motor or manual input. This subclass groups designs where the pump and its drive are integrated to use the same fluid for lubrication and actuation.
[F16N 13/18] relative movement of pump parts being produced by inertia of one of the parts or of a driving member -- F16N 13/18 relates to lubricating pumps in which the motion of one component or a driving member is generated by its own inertia, causing relative movement of pump parts without external power input. This subclass covers designs where inertial forces drive the pump's internal elements, such as pistons or gears, to create fluid flow.
[F16N 13/20] Rotary pumps -- F16N 13/20 denotes rotary pumps used for lubricating applications, a subclass of lubricating-pump mechanisms within mechanical engineering. These pumps transfer fluid by rotating elements such as gears, lobes or screws, providing a continuous flow of lubricant to moving parts. They are commonly employed in engines, gearboxes and hydraulic systems to reduce friction and wear.
[F16N 13/22] with distributing equipment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pump with distributing equipment, whereby the distributing equipment is seen as mean to split a lubricant flow into multiple flows for different users or channels or pipes (the scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 15/00] Lubrication with substances other than oil or grease -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubrication with substances other than oil or grease such as e.g. with graphite or graphite-containing compositions or water (the scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lubrication of machines or apparatus working under extreme conditions -> F16N17/00
[F16N 15/02] with graphite or graphite-containing compositions -- F16N 15/02 classifies lubricating methods or devices that employ graphite or graphite-containing compositions instead of oil or grease to reduce friction in mechanical engineering elements. It resides under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → lubricating, focusing on non-oil/grease lubricants.
[F16N 15/04] with water -- F16N 15/04 pertains to lubrication methods using water as the substance, falling under the broader category of mechanical engineering and specifically addressing non-oil, non-grease lubricants. This classification is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European Patent Office (EPO) and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO).
[F16N 17/00] Lubrication of machines or apparatus working under extreme conditions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubrication of machines or apparatus working under extreme conditions such as e.g. at high temperature, at low temperature, or in vacuum or under reduced pressure (the scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 17/02] at high temperature -- This subclass concerns lubricating machines or apparatus that operate at elevated temperatures, specifying fluids, coatings, solid lubricants or other methods designed to maintain performance and limit wear under high-temperature conditions. It includes solutions for extreme-heat environments where standard lubricants would fail.
[F16N 17/04] at low temperature -- The F16N 17/04 subclass pertains to lubrication of machines or apparatus working under extreme conditions specifically at low-temperature, describing techniques, fluids or additives designed to maintain performance and reduce wear when operating in cold environments.
[F16N 17/06] in vacuum or under reduced pressure -- This CPC subclass concerns methods, devices, or systems for providing lubrication to mechanical components that operate in vacuum or low-pressure environments, where conventional oil film behavior is altered. It includes selection of lubricants, delivery mechanisms, and control techniques suitable for reduced-pressure conditions.
[F16N 19/00] Details of lubricators or lubrication systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubricant containers for use in lubricators or lubrication systems, such as e.g. indicating oil level or maintaining oil level (the scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 19/003] Indicating oil level  measuring liquid level in general  G01F -- F16N 19/003 covers devices that indicate the level of oil or other liquids, typically integrated into lubricating systems to monitor fluid quantity. This subclass falls under the broader categories of mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and lubricating, and relates to general liquid-level measurement techniques classified in G01F.
[F16N 19/006] Maintaining oil level  level control in general  G05D9/00 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for maintaining oil level such as float valve refill systems (The scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 2013/003] Flexible-wall pumps -- F16N 2013/003 designates flexible-wall pumps, a class of positive-displacement pumps that use a deformable wall or diaphragm to create suction and discharge. These pumps are typically employed for low-pressure liquid transfer in medical, chemical, food processing and similar applications.
[F16N 2013/006] Jet pumps -- F16N 2013/006 covers jet pumps, a type of hydraulic machine that uses a high-velocity stream of fluid to create a low-pressure region for suction and transport of liquids or gases, typically employed in marine, industrial and water-treatment applications.
[F16N 2013/063] with electrical drive -- The CPC subclass F16N 2013/063 covers devices for feeding, delivering, transporting, or handling that are powered by an electrical drive. It includes mechanisms where a motor or other electric actuator provides the force to move or position workpieces, tools, or materials, such as electrically driven feed rollers, conveyors, or positioning tables. This subclass groups inventions that integrate the drive unit directly with the handling apparatus.
[F16N 2013/066] with electromagnetical drive -- CPC F16N 2013/066 designates shafts, drive shafts or related transmissions whose propulsion is provided by an electromagnetic motor. It includes mechanical assemblies in which an electric or electromagnetic drive directly powers the rotating shaft, such as electric-motor-driven gear units or electromagnetic couplings.
[F16N 2013/205] Screw pumps -- F16N 2013/205 covers screw pumps, a type of positive-displacement pump that uses one or more helical screws to convey fluids. The classification includes designs where the screw rotates within a housing to create continuous flow, suitable for high-viscosity liquids and slurries.
[F16N 2021/005] Modular units -- F16N 2021/005 relates to modular units used in fluid-handling assemblies, such as interchangeable sections of pumps, valves, pipework or other hydraulic and pneumatic components that can be combined to form a complete system. The classification applies to parts designed for easy assembly, disassembly and re-configuration.
[F16N 2031/008] Drain plugs -- F16N 2031/008 relates to drain plugs used in pumps, covering devices that seal or open a passage to remove fluids from pump housings or cavities. The classification includes removable caps, plugs, and similar fittings designed to facilitate draining, cleaning or maintenance of pump interiors. It applies to both manually operated and automatically actuated plug mechanisms.
[F16N 2031/025] Oil-slinger -- F16N 2031/025 covers oil-slinger apparatus, which uses a swinging or rotating arm to fling oil onto a target area for purposes such as lubrication, spray cooling or fire suppression. The device typically consists of a reservoir, a pump and a mechanism that imparts centrifugal motion to the oil.
[F16N 2033/005] Flushing -- F16N 2033/005 covers flushing techniques and apparatus for hydraulic or fluid-power systems, including methods for cleaning, rinsing or purging pumps, compressors and associated pipelines by circulating fluid to remove contaminants or ready the system for operation.
[F16N 2037/006] Filling -- F16N 2037/006 covers apparatus and methods for filling containers, including devices that dispense liquids, powders, granules or pastes into bottles, cans, tubes or other packages. The classification addresses the mechanisms that control fill volume, flow rate, sealing during filling and the integration of sensors or automation for accurate dosing.
[F16N 2039/007] Using strainers -- F16N 2039/007 pertains to the use of strainers in fluid-handling systems, describing methods and apparatus for inserting, cleaning or removing strainers within pipelines, ducts or machinery to separate solid particles from liquids or gases. It includes arrangements for maintaining flow while the strainer is in place.
[F16N 2039/065] inlet foot filter -- A filter positioned at the foot of a pump's inlet to remove foreign particles and debris from the fluid before it enters the pump housing. It is designed for centrifugal or other types of pumps to protect internal components and maintain performance by preventing blockage or wear. This configuration is especially useful in industrial and water-treatment applications where inlet contamination is common.
[F16N 21/00] Conduits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Conduits; junctions; fittings for lubrication apertures such as e.g. lubricating nipples, nozzles for connection of lubricating equipment to nipples Covering members for nipples, conduits or apertures (The scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 21/02] Lubricating nipples -- Lubricating nipples are small, threaded fittings attached to the ends or junctions of hydraulic or pneumatic conduits to provide a convenient point for introducing grease or oil into the system. They allow periodic maintenance without dismantling the surrounding pipework, ensuring smooth operation of moving parts and preventing wear.
[F16N 21/04] Nozzles for connection of lubricating equipment to nipples -- F16N 21/04 covers nozzles that connect lubricating equipment to nipples, providing a passage for oil or grease to be delivered to moving parts. These fittings are designed for secure, leak-free attachment in machinery and are often made of steel or brass. They belong to the lubricating conduits subgroup within mechanical engineering.
[F16N 21/06] Covering members for nipples, conduits or apertures -- Covering members for nipples, conduits or apertures are protective elements that fit over the ends of pipes, fittings or holes in lubricating systems to seal and shield them from contaminants, leakage or damage. They are typically made of metal or polymer and may be removable for maintenance.
[F16N 2200/00] Condition of lubricant -- The F16N 2200/00 entry covers the condition of lubricants used in mechanical systems, including monitoring, evaluation, and classification of their physical and chemical state, degradation, contamination, and performance characteristics. It relates to methods and indicators for assessing lubricant health and suitability for continued operation.
[F16N 2200/02] Oxidation -- F16N 2200/02 covers the oxidation condition of lubricants, including methods for detecting, measuring or mitigating oxidative degradation in lubricating fluids used in mechanical systems. It also encompasses additives, monitoring techniques and treatments aimed at preserving lubricant performance.
[F16N 2200/04] Detecting debris, chips, swarfs -- F16N 2200/04 covers methods and apparatus for monitoring the condition of lubricants by detecting solid particles such as debris, metal chips or swarfs that may be present in the oil. It includes sensors, filters or analytical techniques that identify and quantify these contaminants to assess wear or contamination levels.
[F16N 2200/06] Film thickness -- F16N 2200/06 classifies patents that address the thickness of the lubricating film formed on moving or stationary mechanical parts. It includes methods for measuring, controlling or optimizing film thickness as a condition of the lubricant within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and lubricating topics.
[F16N 2200/08] Acidity, pH-value -- F16N 2200/08 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system addresses the acidity or pH value of lubricants, a critical parameter for assessing the condition and potential corrosiveness of the lubricant in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16N 2200/10] Temperature -- The code F16N 2200/10 designates subject matter concerning the temperature of lubricants used in mechanical systems. It includes methods for measuring, controlling, and specifying temperature-dependent ranges that affect lubricant performance and equipment reliability.

[F16N 2200/18] Detecting foaming -- The F16N 2200/18 subclass covers technologies and methods for detecting foaming in lubricating fluids, such as sensors, visual or acoustic monitoring, and analytical techniques used to identify gas bubbles or excessive foam formation. It is applied to condition-monitoring systems that assess lubricant quality and prevent equipment damage caused by foam.
[F16N 2200/20] Detecting water -- The F16N 2200/20 entry covers devices, methods and systems for detecting water in lubricants to prevent contamination that can impair performance. It includes sensors, test kits and monitoring techniques used in mechanical engineering applications where lubricant condition is critical.

[F16N 2210/08] Aircraft -- This CPC subclass covers technologies for the care of lubricants on aircraft, including methods and devices for supplying, maintaining, monitoring, or treating lubricating fluids in aviation applications. [F16N 2210/09] for inverted flight -- A subclass of lubricating care that addresses lubricants or lubrication systems designed for aircraft operating in inverted flight, focusing on oil retention, flow and performance when the craft is upside-down. [F16N 2210/10] Refrigerators -- This CPC subclass falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, lubricating, care of lubricants, and covers inventions related to the design, operation, maintenance or cleaning of refrigerating machines with respect to lubricant management, such as selection, supply, circulation, recovery or removal of lubricants in refrigeration systems.

The classification is applied to inventions that generate, analyze or act on such mapped data to improve lubricating efficiency.
[F16N 2230/10] Timing network -- The CPC entry F16N 2230/10 describes a timing network, which is a system of passages, valves or other control elements that coordinates the timing of lubricating-oil flow to moving parts. It ensures that oil is delivered at specific intervals or phases of operation, often to synchronize lubrication with the motion of gears, bearings or other mechanical components.
[F16N 2230/12] with pneumatic elements -- F16N 2230/12 covers lubricating systems that incorporate pneumatic components, such as air-driven pumps, valves or actuators, to deliver, control or circulate lubricant. [F16N 2230/14] with bimetallic elements -- F16N 2230/14 covers lubricating devices or systems that incorporate bimetallic elements, where two dissimilar metals are combined to enhance performance such as wear resistance, heat dissipation or corrosion protection. [F16N 2230/16] with capacitors -- F16N 2230/16 pertains to lubricating devices that incorporate capacitors, using the stored electrical charge for sensing, control or timing functions within the lubrication system. [F16N 2230/18] Switches -- F16N 2230/18 covers switches used to control the flow of lubricants within mechanical systems, including electrically, hydraulically or mechanically actuated devices that start, stop or modulate lubrication. The classification applies to valve-type and electronic switches designed for lubrication circuits in engines, gearboxes and other machinery where precise oil delivery is required. It is part of the broader mechanical engineering group for engineering elements and units related to lubricating.
[F16N 2230/19] Photo sensor -- A photo sensor in the context of F16N 2230/19 is an optoelectronic device incorporated into lubricating systems to detect the presence, level, or condition of lubricant, often by measuring light transmission or reflection. It provides feedback for automatic control or monitoring of lubrication processes, helping to ensure proper fluid distribution and prevent wear.
[F16N 2230/20] Reed relays -- A reed relay is an electromechanical switching device that contains one or more flexible metal reeds sealed in a glass envelope; when a magnetic field is applied the reeds bend together or apart to close or open an electric circuit. These components are used for low-power signal processing and switching tasks, offering fast response, low contact resistance, and reliable operation in compact, sealed packages.
[F16N 2230/22] using counters -- F16N 2230/22 covers inventions that employ electronic or mechanical counters to monitor, record, or control lubricating processes such as oil flow, pressure, or wear detection in mechanical engineering systems.

[F16N 2260/00] Fail safe -- F16N 2260/00, classified under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Lubricating, covers devices or systems designed to maintain operation when a primary lubricating component fails, providing a safe, secondary or emergency lubrication method that prevents damage or stoppage.
[F16N 2260/02] Indicating -- F16N 2260/02 covers devices that provide an indication of the operating condition of a fail-safe lubricating system, such as sensors, gauges or visual displays that show pressure, temperature, flow or lubrication level to ensure safe mechanical performance. [F16N 2260/04] Oil level -- F16N 2260/04 classifies devices and methods that detect, indicate or control the oil level in lubricating systems of mechanical engineering components, ensuring safe operation by preventing low-oil conditions. It covers sensors, gauges, float-type indicators and related safety mechanisms that monitor oil quantity in engines, gearboxes or hydraulic circuits.
[F16N 2260/05] Oil flow -- F16N 2260/05 relates to oil flow in lubricating systems that incorporate fail-safe features, within the mechanical-engineering domain. It covers designs and methods for controlling oil movement to ensure continuous lubrication even when a primary system fails, helping prevent wear and damage in engineered components.
[F16N 2260/06] Temperature -- F16N 2260/06 covers lubricating systems that incorporate temperature monitoring or control as a safety measure, ensuring that the lubricant operates within a defined temperature range to prevent failure. It relates to mechanical engineering elements where temperature data is used to trigger protective actions or maintain optimal lubrication conditions.
[F16N 2260/065] by means of colours or dye -- F16N 2260/065 relates to lubricating systems that incorporate colours or dyes as visual indicators, enabling users to detect the presence, flow or condition of lubricant and providing a fail-safe means of monitoring equipment performance. This subclass falls within mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for lubricating applications.
