[F16N 2260/08] Pressure -- F16N 2260/08 relates to pressure aspects of lubricating systems in mechanical engineering, focusing on elements that monitor, control or maintain pressure to ensure fail-safe operation of the lubrication circuit.
[F16N 2260/12] using warning lamps -- F16N 2260/12 addresses lubricating systems that incorporate warning lamps as part of a fail-safe arrangement, providing a visual signal when lubrication fails or a fault is detected. The subclass covers devices and methods that use illuminated alerts to indicate loss of oil, pressure drop, or other abnormal conditions, helping to prevent damage by prompting immediate corrective action.
[F16N 2260/14] using sound -- The F16N 2260/14 subclass covers lubricating methods that employ acoustic energy, such as ultrasonic vibrations, to reduce friction or enhance fluid film formation on moving surfaces. It includes devices that generate sound waves to clean, agitate, or distribute lubricant, improving performance in mechanical systems where traditional lubrication may be insufficient.
[F16N 2260/16] using recording -- This entry covers lubricating systems that incorporate recording means to monitor parameters such as oil flow, temperature, or wear. It includes devices or methods that capture and store data for analysis to ensure reliable operation and preventive maintenance.
[F16N 2260/18] necessity of changing oil -- F16N 2260/18 refers to literature that addresses the need to replace lubricating oil, covering criteria, timing and indicators for oil change in mechanical systems. It includes discussion of wear, contamination, temperature and service-interval considerations that determine when oil must be refreshed.
[F16N 2260/20] Emergency -- The F16N 2260/20 subclass covers emergency or fail-safe lubricating systems that automatically supply lubricant when normal lubrication is interrupted, ensuring continued operation of mechanical components under fault conditions such as loss of power, fire or system failure. It includes devices that detect a lubrication fault and trigger a reserve supply or alternative delivery method to prevent damage or shutdown.
[F16N 2260/21] limping home -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry F16N 2260/21, titled "limping home," pertains to mechanical engineering and lubricating systems. It specifically addresses fail-safe mechanisms designed to allow a system or vehicle to continue operating in a reduced capacity after a failure, ensuring safe operation until proper repairs can be made.
[F16N 2260/22] Rupture -- F16N 2260/22 designates rupture as a safety feature in lubricating systems, covering devices that deliberately break or release pressure to prevent damage in mechanical engineering components such as seals, reservoirs, or bearings. It is used to ensure that over-pressure or wear does not compromise the overall system performance.
[F16N 2260/24] using accumulator -- The F16N 2260/24 sub-class covers lubricating arrangements that employ an accumulator to supply pressure or fluid when the normal supply is interrupted, providing a fail-safe mechanism for mechanical elements.
[F16N 2260/30] Clogging filter -- The F16N 2260/30 class covers filters that are deliberately designed to become clogged as a fail-safe mechanism, halting fluid flow when debris or contaminants build up to protect downstream components. These clogging filters are used in hydraulic and lubricating systems to ensure automatic shutdown before damage occurs, and they belong to the mechanical-engineering hierarchy of engineering elements and units, lubricating.
[F16N 2260/32] Pump failure -- F16N 2260/32 designates technologies and methods dealing with pump failure, including detection, prevention, and safe-shutdown mechanisms within lubricated systems. It covers fail-safe features that protect equipment when a pump ceases to operate properly, such as overload protection, emergency lubrication supply, and fault-diagnostic devices.
[F16N 2260/40] Pre-lubrication -- F16N 2260/40 pertains to pre-lubrication methods applied to mechanical engineering elements, specifically lubricating components to ensure safe operation. It covers techniques that deposit lubricant before the machine starts, preventing wear and failure. This classification falls under the lubricating subclass of engineering elements and units.
[F16N 2260/50] After-lubrication -- F16N 2260/50 covers devices or methods that automatically supply lubricant to a mechanism after it has stopped or after a fault, providing a fail-safe means to protect moving parts. The entry includes systems that detect a shutdown or failure and deliver oil or grease to critical components to prevent wear and ensure safe restart.
[F16N 2260/60] Limping home -- The CPC entry F16N 2260/60 Limping home relates to lubricating systems or devices that provide a fail-safe or reduced-performance mode allowing machinery to continue operating briefly after loss or degradation of lubrication. It covers designs such as backup lubrication reservoirs, passive oil-feed paths or control strategies that enable a limp-home function. This falls under mechanical engineering.
[F16N 2270/00] Controlling -- The CPC subclass F16N 2270/00 covers devices and methods for controlling lubricating systems, such as regulators, sensors, and automatic distribution mechanisms that adjust oil flow, pressure or timing to ensure optimal lubrication of mechanical components. It includes both mechanical and electronic control solutions used in engines, gearboxes and other moving assemblies.
[F16N 2270/10] Level -- F16N 2270/10 covers level-control devices that integrate lubricating components to improve the movement of floats, pistons or other sensing elements. The classification includes mechanisms for measuring or maintaining a fluid level in tanks, reservoirs or pipelines where lubrication reduces friction and wear. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, lubricating, controlling.
[F16N 2270/12] using overflow -- The entry relates to methods and devices that control lubrication by diverting excess lubricating fluid, using overflow channels or reservoirs to regulate flow and pressure. It includes systems where overflow is deliberately employed to maintain optimal lubrication and prevent over-lubrication in mechanical components.
[F16N 2270/14] using float device -- F16N 2270/14 covers lubricating systems that use a float device to regulate the flow or level of lubricant, maintaining a constant oil level or pressure in hydraulic or mechanical assemblies. The float acts as a control element, adjusting delivery to prevent excess or shortage of lubricant during operation.
[F16N 2270/18] using overflow by filling -- The CPC class F16N 2270/18 addresses lubricating arrangements in which excess lubricant is deliberately allowed to overflow by filling a reservoir or cavity, using the overflow as a means of regulating the quantity supplied to the moving parts. It applies to hydraulic or mechanical lubrication systems that control flow through this filling-overflow method.
[F16N 2270/20] Amount of lubricant -- The "Amount of lubricant" subclass (F16N 2270/20) defines criteria for specifying, measuring or controlling the quantity of lubricant applied to mechanical components or systems. It covers guidelines on minimum, maximum, or optimal lubricant volumes needed for effective lubrication, as well as methods for monitoring and adjusting these levels.
[F16N 2270/22] with restrictions -- F16N 2270/22 covers lubricating control devices that include flow-restricting elements such as orifices, throttles or pressure-limiting valves. The classification applies to mechanisms that deliberately limit the amount or rate of lubricant supplied to moving parts.
[F16N 2270/24] using porous, felt, ceramic, or sintered material -- F16N 2270/24 relates to lubricating or controlling systems that incorporate porous, felt, ceramic or sintered materials to store, transport or release lubricants within mechanical elements. The classification addresses the use of these permeable structures to enhance lubrication of bearings, slides or other moving parts, improving wear resistance and performance. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Lubricating → Controlling.
[F16N 2270/26] variable -- F16N 2270/26 covers mechanisms that adjust the amount or rate of lubricant supplied to a machine or component in a variable manner. It includes devices or control systems that modulate lubrication flow according to operating conditions such as speed, load or temperature.
[F16N 2270/30] intermittent -- F16N 2270/30 covers devices that intermittently control the supply of lubricant to moving parts, such as timed or demand-based oiling systems used in machinery.
[F16N 2270/32] Fixed pulse, fixed length, fixed amplitude -- F16N 2270/32 covers lubricating-system control methods that use a fixed-pulse signal of constant length and amplitude to regulate fluid delivery. It pertains to mechanical-engineering elements that provide precise, repeatable lubrication timing and quantity. This subclass is part of the broader category of engineering elements and units for lubricating control.
[F16N 2270/48] pressure-controlled -- F16N 2270/48 covers pressure-controlled lubricating arrangements, where the flow or distribution of lubricant is regulated by hydraulic or pneumatic pressure. It includes devices, methods and control mechanisms that adjust lubrication rates in response to pressure changes, typically within mechanical engineering systems.
[F16N 2270/50] Condition -- F16N 2270/50 pertains to methods, devices or systems for assessing the condition of lubricants used in hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid-power systems, such as monitoring viscosity, contamination, temperature, wear particles or degradation. It falls under mechanical engineering → engineering elements and units → lubricating, and addresses ensuring proper lubrication by detecting lubricant quality or performance issues.
[F16N 2270/52] Viscosity -- F16N 2270/52 covers aspects of controlling the viscosity of lubricants in mechanical systems. It includes methods, devices and processes for measuring, adjusting or maintaining lubricant viscosity to ensure proper performance and wear protection.
[F16N 2270/54] pH -- F16N 2270/54 covers methods and devices for controlling the pH of lubricating fluids, including measurement, monitoring, and adjustment techniques to maintain optimal acidity or alkalinity and prevent corrosion or degradation in mechanical systems. It falls under Mechanical Engineering → Engineering Elements and Units → Lubricating → Controlling in the CPC hierarchy.
[F16N 2270/56] Temperature -- F16N 2270/56 designates features concerned with temperature in lubricating systems used to control mechanical elements. It covers methods, devices or parameters for measuring, regulating or managing the heat of lubricants within hydraulic, pneumatic or other fluid-driven components to ensure proper performance and protection of machinery.
[F16N 2270/60] Pressure -- F16N 2270/60 relates to the control of pressure within lubricating systems of mechanical engineering components. It covers devices, methods or arrangements that generate, regulate, monitor or maintain pressure to ensure proper lubrication of moving parts. The classification falls under Mechanical Engineering - Engineering Elements and Units - Lubricating - Controlling.
[F16N 2270/62] Limit -- F16N 2270/62 Limit falls under the Mechanical Engineering branch of the CPC, specifically the lubricating-controlling sub-group. It covers devices or methods that set a maximum amount, flow rate or pressure of lubricant supplied to a machine, ensuring the lubrication does not exceed a predetermined threshold.
[F16N 2270/64] Set-pressure -- F16N 2270/64 covers mechanisms that create and maintain a predetermined pressure in a lubricating system, using elements such as springs, pistons or other actuators to control fluid flow. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, lubricating, controlling, and applies to devices that keep a set-pressure for bearing or gear lubrication.
[F16N 2270/70] Supply -- F16N 2270/70 - Supply relates to systems or components that deliver lubricants to mechanical parts, ensuring controlled distribution and maintaining proper lubrication within engines, gearboxes or other moving assemblies.
[F16N 2270/72] on-off -- F16N 2270/72 relates to on-off control of lubrication functions, covering devices that start or stop the supply of lubricant to mechanical components, often used in hydraulic or pneumatic systems.
[F16N 2270/74] only during use -- F16N 2270/74 designates lubricating arrangements that are activated solely while the machine is operating. It covers devices or control methods that supply lubricant only during use, rather than continuously or during standby, and is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, lubricating, controlling.
[F16N 2280/00] Valves -- F16N 2280/00 covers valves specifically designed for lubricating systems, including devices that control, direct or regulate the flow of lubricants in machinery. It addresses valve constructions, operating principles and applications that ensure effective oil or grease delivery to moving parts, improving wear resistance and performance. This classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, lubricating.
[F16N 2280/02] electromagnetically operated -- F16N 2280/02 refers to valves that are activated by electromagnetic means, falling under the lubricating sub-section of engineering elements and units in mechanical engineering. This subclass covers designs where an electric coil or magnet drives the valve mechanism to control fluid flow, typically in lubrication or hydraulic systems.
[F16N 2280/04] Variable-flow or proportional valves -- F16N 2280/04 covers variable-flow or proportional valves used to regulate the amount of fluid passing through a lubricating system. These valves adjust flow continuously in response to control signals, enabling precise dosing of oil or other lubricants in mechanical equipment.
[F16N 23/00] Special adaptations of check valves -- F16N 23/00 covers specially adapted check valves that incorporate modifications such as enhanced sealing elements, flow-control features, auxiliary devices or unconventional materials designed to improve performance, reliability or suitability for particular operating conditions. It includes inventive variations beyond standard valve designs, focusing on structural or functional adaptations that address specific lubrication or mechanical requirements.
[F16N 25/00] Distributing equipment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Distributing equipment, whereby the distributing equipment is seen as mean to split a lubricant flow into multiple flows for different users or channels or pipes such as e.g. with reciprocating distributing slide valve or with rotary distributing member (The scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 25/02] with reciprocating distributing slide valve -- This CPC subclass covers lubricating systems that incorporate a reciprocating slide valve for distributing lubricant. It pertains to mechanical engineering components where the valve moves back-and-forth to regulate flow in distribution devices. The code F16N 25/02 classifies inventions featuring such slide valves within lubricating distributing equipment.
[F16N 25/04] with rotary distributing member -- F16N 25/04 covers lubricating distributing equipment that employs a rotary member to spread lubricant, such as rotary oil distributors, gear pumps, or rotor-type spray devices. The classification applies to mechanisms where rotation of the component ensures even delivery of oil or grease to moving parts.
[F16N 27/00] Proportioning devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Proportioning devices such as e.g. using restrictions or gating equipment (The scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 27/005] using restrictions -- F16N 27/005 covers proportioning devices that regulate the flow of lubricants by means of flow restrictions such as orifice plates, throttling valves or nozzles. The restriction creates a pressure drop that determines the quantity of lubricant delivered to a moving part, enabling precise control of film thickness and wear protection.
[F16N 27/02] Gating equipment -- Gating equipment comprises devices that regulate the admission, timing or quantity of a fluid, usually a lubricant, into a mechanical system. It includes valves, distributors or similar mechanisms that open or close passages to proportion lubricating flows as required.
[F16N 29/00] Special means in lubricating arrangements or systems providing for the indication or detection of undesired conditions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Special means in lubricating arrangements or systems providing for the indication or detection of undesired conditions (i.e excessive temperature, lack of lubricant, lubricant composition out of limits, excessive pressure, clogging, etc...); Use of devices responsive to conditions in lubricating arrangements or systems such as e.g. for influencing the supply of lubricant or enabling a warning to be given (The scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 29/02] for influencing the supply of lubricant -- F16N 29/02 pertains to mechanisms within lubrication systems designed to adjust or control the flow of lubricant, particularly in response to detected undesired conditions. This classification covers devices that ensure optimal lubrication by modifying the supply based on real-time operational needs or system feedback.
[F16N 29/04] enabling a warning to be given -- F16N 29/04 covers devices built into lubricating systems that sense abnormal operating states-such as low pressure, temperature spikes, or contamination-and generate a warning for the user. The focus is on the indication or detection means that enable an alert to be given before damage occurs.
[F16N 3/00] Devices for supplying lubricant by manual action -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements for supplying lubricant such as delivering oil (i.e. oil cans or oil syringes), as well delivering grease (i.e. grease guns)(the scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 3/02] delivering oil -- Devices for supplying lubricant by manual action, classified under F16N 3/02, cover hand-operated oil delivery systems such as manual oil pumps, grease guns, oil cans and similar tools that allow a user to dispense oil or grease directly onto mechanical components without the aid of powered mechanisms.
[F16N 3/04] Oil cans -- F16N 3/04 covers oil cans, hand-operated devices that dispense lubricant onto machinery. They typically feature a spout or simple pump that lets a user apply oil directly to moving parts for maintenance without the need for electric or pneumatic power.
[F16N 3/06] delivering on squeezing -- F16N 3/06 covers devices that deliver lubricants by manual squeezing action, such as hand-operated pumps or syringes for applying grease or oil to machinery.
[F16N 3/08] incorporating a piston-pump -- F16N 3/08 relates to manually operated lubricating devices that incorporate a piston-pump to draw and dispense lubricant. The piston-pump mechanism provides controlled flow of oil or grease during manual application, typically in machinery where precise lubrication is required. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, lubricating, and devices for supplying lubricant by manual action.
[F16N 3/10] delivering grease -- F16N 3/10 covers devices that deliver grease manually, such as hand-operated grease guns and similar apparatus used to apply lubricating grease to machinery. These tools are classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, lubricating, and specifically devices for supplying lubricant by manual action.
[F16N 31/00] Means for collecting, retaining, or draining-off lubricant in or on machines or apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for collecting, retaining, or draining-off lubricant in or on machines or apparatus, such as e.g. drain pans, drip trays, oil catchers, oil wipers (The scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Oil separators for separating oil from exhaust steam -> F22G
[F16N 31/002] Drain pans -- F16N 31/002 covers drain pans that collect, retain, or discharge lubricant from machines or apparatus. These components are part of lubricating systems designed to manage oil or grease runoff during operation or maintenance.
[F16N 31/004] combined with container -- F16N 31/004 covers lubricating devices that incorporate a container for collecting, retaining, or draining lubricant, providing an integrated system for storing and supplying oil or grease directly to moving parts of a machine. This sub-class combines the lubrication function with a built-in reservoir to simplify maintenance and reduce external piping.
[F16N 31/006] Drip trays -- F16N 31/006 covers drip trays, a type of lubricating device that collects, retains, or allows the drainage of lubricant from machinery or apparatus. These components prevent accumulation of oil on equipment surfaces, facilitating safe and efficient operation.
[F16N 31/02] Oil catchers -- F16N 31/02 falls under the CPC subclass for lubricating mechanisms in mechanical engineering, specifically covering devices that collect, retain, or drain oil from machines and apparatus. These oil catchers are designed to prevent oil leakage, improve lubrication efficiency, and protect surrounding components.
[F16N 3/12] Grease guns -- F16N 3/12 classifies manual grease-gun devices used to apply lubricants such as grease to mechanical parts. These tools belong to the lubricating subgroup of engineering elements within mechanical engineering and operate by hand-driven pressure to dispense lubricant.
[F16N 33/00] Mechanical arrangements for cleaning lubricating equipment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanical arrangements for cleaning lubricating equipment; Special racks or the like for use in draining lubricant from machine parts such as flushing and steam cleaners (The scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 35/00] Care of lubricants -- F16N 35/00 covers technologies for the care of lubricants, including methods and devices for monitoring, cleaning, storing, replenishing, and otherwise maintaining lubricant condition and performance in mechanical systems. It encompasses systems that detect contamination, adjust viscosity, or automate lubricant delivery to ensure optimal operation of moving parts.
[F16N 37/00] Equipment for transferring lubricant from one container to another -- Equipment for moving lubricant between containers, such as pumps, hoses, valves, and dispensing units, designed to handle oils, greases, and similar fluids. It includes mechanisms that control flow rate, pressure, and direction to ensure efficient and clean transfer. The devices are used in maintenance, manufacturing, and service settings where precise lubrication distribution is required.
[F16N 37/003] for filling bearings -- This CPC subclass describes equipment that transfers lubricant from a container into bearings, such as devices used for filling bearings with oil or grease during assembly or maintenance. It falls under mechanical engineering, lubricating equipment for moving lubricant between containers.
[F16N 37/02] for filling grease guns -- F16N 37/02 pertains to devices designed to fill grease guns, transferring lubricant from a storage container into the gun for precise application. It is classified under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, lubricating.
[F16N 39/00] Arrangements for conditioning of lubricants in the lubricating system -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements for conditioning (i.e. altering the viscosity) of lubricants in the lubricating system such as e.g. by deaeration, by evaporating or purifying, by cooling, by heating, by filtration or by diluting, e.g. by addition of fuel (The scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 39/002] by deaeration  degasification of liquids  B01D19/00 -- The F16N 39/002 entry covers arrangements within a lubricating system that condition lubricants by removing dissolved gases, i.e., deaeration or degasification of liquids. Such devices or methods improve lubricant performance and reliability by preventing gas-related cavitation, corrosion or foam formation in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16N 39/005] by evaporating or purifying  for heating or cooling of filters  B01D35/18 , e.g. comprising a vaporising unit  B01D35/185 -- F16N 39/005 covers arrangements in lubricating systems that condition lubricants by evaporating or purifying them for heating or cooling filters, typically using a vaporising unit. It is classified under Mechanical Engineering - Engineering Elements and Units - Lubricating - Arrangements for conditioning of lubricants in the lubricating system and cross-references B01D35/18 and B01D35/185.
[F16N 39/02] by cooling -- The subclass F16N 39/02 covers lubricating-system arrangements that cool the lubricant, including heat-exchangers, coolant passages, refrigeration loops or other means for reducing lubricant temperature to improve performance and protect components.
[F16N 39/04] by heating -- A method or device that heats lubricants as part of a conditioning arrangement in a lubricating system, used to reduce viscosity, improve flow and protect moving parts, typically integrated into pumps, reservoirs or circulation lines in mechanical engineering applications.
[F16N 39/06] by filtration -- F16N 39/06 covers devices that condition lubricants in a lubricating system by passing the oil through a filter. It relates to mechanical engineering elements that remove particles or contaminants to keep the lubrication effective and protect moving parts. This subclass falls under the broader category of arrangements for conditioning lubricants.
[F16N 39/08] by diluting, e.g. by addition of fuel -- F16N 39/08 covers arrangements for conditioning lubricants by diluting them, such as adding fuel to the oil, in order to modify viscosity or combustion properties within a lubricating system.
[F16N 5/00] Apparatus with hand-positioned nozzle supplied with lubricant under pressure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus with hand-positioned nozzle supplied with lubricant under pressure, or Nozzles or nozzle-valve arrangements therefor (the scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for supplying lubricant by manual action (draining equipment for liquid containers -> F16N3/00
[F16N 5/02] Nozzles or nozzle-valve arrangements therefor, e.g. high-pressure grease guns -- F16N 5/02 covers apparatus in which a hand-operated nozzle delivers lubricant under pressure, typically using a nozzle or nozzle-valve assembly such as those found in high-pressure grease guns. The classification relates to lubricating devices within mechanical engineering.
[F16N 7/00] Arrangements for supplying oil or unspecified lubricant from a stationary reservoir or the equivalent in or on the machine or member to be lubricated -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements for supplying oil or unspecified lubricant from a stationary reservoir or the equivalent in or on the machine or member to be lubricated such as e.g. with gravity feed or drip lubrication, splash lubrication, dip lubrication, mist lubrication, with feed by pumping action of the member to be lubricated or of a shaft of the machine, centrifugal lubrication, with feed by pumping action of a vertical shaft of the machine, and central lubrication systems (the scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 7/02] with gravity feed or drip lubrication -- F16N 7/02 covers lubrication systems that deliver oil or other lubricants from a fixed reservoir to the moving parts of a machine using gravity or a drip mechanism. These arrangements rely on the natural flow of the fluid without pumps or active pressure devices.
[F16N 7/04] with oil flow promoted by vibration -- This subclass deals with lubricating arrangements where oil is drawn from a fixed reservoir to a machine part and the flow is assisted or induced by vibration of the component or the supply system.
[F16N 7/06] Arrangements in which the droplets are visible -- F16N 7/06 covers lubricating arrangements that intentionally produce or permit visible droplets of oil or other lubricant, such as spray nozzles, misting devices or systems where the lubricant is applied as a visible spray onto machine parts, often to aid monitoring or to achieve a specific lubrication effect.
[F16N 7/08] controlled by means of the temperature of the member to be lubricated -- This subclass covers lubrication arrangements that draw oil from a stationary reservoir to a machine element and regulate the supply according to the temperature of the lubricated member, providing temperature-controlled flow for optimal lubrication.
[F16N 7/10] incorporating manually-operated control means, e.g. spindles -- This subclass concerns lubricating arrangements that use a manually operated control, such as a spindle, to regulate the flow of oil or other lubricant from a stationary reservoir to the machine part being lubricated. It falls under mechanical engineering elements dealing with oil-supply systems.
[F16N 7/12] with feed by capillary action, e.g. by wicks -- A lubricating system that supplies oil or other lubricant from a fixed reservoir to a machine part by capillary action, typically using wicks, ensuring continuous lubrication without mechanical pumps.
[F16N 7/14] the lubricant being conveyed from the reservoir by mechanical means -- This subclass covers lubricating systems where oil or other lubricant is transferred from a fixed reservoir to the point of use by a mechanical device such as a pump, gear or screw. It applies to arrangements that move the lubricant without using pressure from the fluid itself.
[F16N 7/16] the oil being carried up by a lifting device -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanisms to raise the elevation of lubrication such as scoops, the oil being carried up by a lifting device with one or more feed members fixed on a shaft, with one or more members moving around the shaft to be lubricated (i.e shaped as rings), or with discs, rollers, belts or the like contacting the shaft to be lubricated (the scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples)
[F16N 7/18] with one or more feed members fixed on a shaft -- The CPC subclass F16N 7/18 covers lubricating arrangements that draw oil or other lubricant from a stationary reservoir and deliver it to a machine or component, where the feed element or elements are mounted directly on a rotating shaft. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering path for engineering elements and units, specifically lubricating systems.
[F16N 7/20] with one or more members moving around the shaft to be lubricated -- F16N 7/20 designates lubrication arrangements that feed oil or other lubricant from a fixed reservoir to a rotating shaft using one or more moving members that travel around the shaft to distribute the fluid.
[F16N 7/22] shaped as rings -- F16N 7/22 relates to lubricating systems that deliver oil or other lubricant from a fixed reservoir to a machine or component using ring-shaped containers or delivery devices. The rings are arranged to store and release lubricant directly onto the part that requires lubrication.
[F16N 7/24] with discs, rollers, belts or the like contacting the shaft to be lubricated -- The CPC entry F16N 7/24 covers lubricating systems that draw oil or other lubricant from a fixed reservoir and deliver it to a shaft via discs, rollers, belts or similar contacting members, providing a continuous supply of lubricant to the moving part. This falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for lubricating arrangements.
[F16N 7/26] Splash lubrication -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of splash lubrication through projection of lubricant involving dipper, nozzle, sprinkling, spraying or diffusing device (the scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 7/28] Dip lubrication -- Dip lubrication is a method where a machine component or its housing is periodically immersed in a bath of oil or other lubricant to form a protective film on its surfaces. The process typically uses a stationary reservoir and may involve automated dipping mechanisms to ensure consistent coverage. This technique is commonly applied to bearings, gears and other moving parts that require regular lubrication.
[F16N 7/30] the oil being fed or carried along by another fluid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubrication means whereby, the oil being fed or carried along by another fluid (air, water etc…), whereby the other fluid being intentionally introduced. (For instance, an oil flow containing accidentally some air, either ahead of the oil or in the oil, i.e., bubbles, is not covered) (the scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 7/32] Mist lubrication -- Mist lubrication refers to systems that convey oil or other lubricants from a fixed reservoir to a moving component in the form of a fine mist, providing a thin lubricating film without direct liquid contact. Such arrangements are used in machines where precise, low-volume lubrication is needed to reduce friction and wear while minimizing fluid loss. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, specifically lubricating elements.
[F16N 7/34] Atomising devices for oil -- F16N 7/34 covers atomising devices that deliver oil or other lubricant from a stationary reservoir to a machine or component, typically by creating a spray or mist for precise lubrication. These arrangements are used in mechanical engineering where fine oil distribution is needed, such as in bearings, gearboxes or hydraulic systems.
[F16N 7/36] with feed by pumping action of the member to be lubricated or of a shaft of the machine -- The CPC subclass F16N 7/36 covers lubricating systems that supply oil or other lubricants from a stationary reservoir by means of a pumping action generated by the component being lubricated or by a rotating shaft of the machine. It is a narrower division within mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, lubricating.
[F16N 7/363] Centrifugal lubrication -- Centrifugal lubrication refers to a system that supplies oil or other lubricants from a fixed reservoir to a machine or component by using centrifugal force generated by a rotating part. The arrangement directs the lubricant outward through passages or nozzles, ensuring a continuous flow to bearings or other moving surfaces without external pumps. This method is classified under mechanical engineering lubricating elements for efficient, self-pressurising oil delivery.
[F16N 7/366] with feed by pumping action of a vertical shaft of the machine -- A F16N 7/366 arrangement supplies oil or other lubricant from a stationary reservoir to a machine or component by means of a pump driven by a vertical shaft of the machine. This vertical-shaft pumping action forms part of the lubricating system within mechanical engineering elements.
[F16N 7/38] with a separate pump -- This subclass covers lubricating arrangements that deliver oil or other lubricant from a stationary reservoir to the machine or component being lubricated by means of a pump that is separate from the main drive or other system functions. The separate pump may be dedicated solely to the lubrication circuit, providing independent control of flow and pressure.
[F16N 7/385] Central lubrication systems -- F16N 7/385 covers central lubrication systems that supply oil or unspecified lubricant from a stationary reservoir to machine parts, distributing the fluid through a network of supply lines, pumps or distribution devices to ensure continuous lubrication of bearings, gears and other moving components.
[F16N 7/40] in a closed circulation system -- F16N 7/40 covers lubrication arrangements that supply oil from a stationary reservoir through a closed-loop circulation. The system continuously pumps the lubricant around a sealed circuit, using return lines, filters or coolers to keep the oil circulating to the machine parts without exposure to the external environment.
[F16N 9/00] Arrangements for supplying oil or unspecified lubricant from a moving reservoir or the equivalent -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements for supplying oil or unspecified lubricant from a moving reservoir or the equivalent such as e.g. a reservoir on or in a rotary member and a reservoir on or in a reciprocating, rocking, or swinging member (the scope covered the by title of the corresponding group is not limited by these examples).
[F16N 9/02] with reservoir on or in a rotary member -- This subclass covers arrangements that deliver oil or other lubricant from a moving reservoir that is mounted on or integrated into a rotary member, providing continuous lubrication during rotation. It is classified under CPC code F16N 9/02 within the mechanical-engineering hierarchy for lubricating systems.
[F16N 9/04] with reservoir on or in a reciprocating, rocking, or swinging member -- F16N 9/04 covers lubricating systems where the oil reservoir is mounted directly on a part that moves back-and-forth, rocks or swings, allowing lubricant to be delivered to the bearing or gear as the member moves.
[F16N 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- F16N 99/00 is a residual class in the Cooperative Patent Classification for lubricating devices and methods that do not fit any other specific group within the lubricating subclass. It covers any mechanical-engineering inventions related to lubrication that are not otherwise classified, serving as a catch-all for novel or unconventional approaches.
[F16P] SAFETY DEVICES IN GENERAL; -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Safety systems allowing protection of the operator from a machine, a machine tool, a press. Safety systems designed specifically for certain machine/tool/application should be classified in the group related to this specific machine/tool/application if a group related to this safety system exists.
[F16P 1/00] Devices protecting or preventing injuries to people -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Safety systems which are independent from the machine, and which are meant to protect the operator by creating a physical barrier between the operator and a dangerous area. No interaction exist between the safety system and the machine.
[F16P 1/005] Guards for rolls in calendering or other roll machines, e.g. nip guards, finger guards -- F16P 1/005 covers safety guards designed for rolls in calendering or other roll machines, such as nip guards and finger guards, which protect operators from contact with moving or rotating rolls and help prevent injuries during machine operation.
[F16P 1/02] Fixed screens or hoods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: The document DE102007062813 discloses a screen (4) which provides a barrier in order to avoid an operator to enter a dangerous area. In use the barrier does not move, except that the panels (2) can be adjusted in height. Once these panels are adjusted, the screen generally does not move with respect to the machine.
[F16P 1/04] Screens or hoods rotating with rotary shafts -- F16P 1/04 covers safety screens or protective hoods that are mounted on a rotating shaft, allowing the barrier to move in sync with rotating machinery to shield operators from hazards. The devices are designed to rotate together with the shaft to maintain continuous protection.
[F16P 1/06] specially designed for welding -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: EP1908546
[F16P 3/00] Safety devices acting in conjunction with the control or operation of a machine -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Safety systems which depend on the machine by way of any possible interactions and which are meant to protect the operator from being injured by the machine.
[F16P 3/001] Safety devices for guarding the human operator of punch presses or like machine tools performing an opening and closing travel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: The document EP1990130 discloses a screen which depends on the functioning of the machine, in the sense that there is an interaction between the screen and the machine. Here the safety device performs an opening and closing travel in order to restrict access to the machine.
[F16P 3/003] by application of a specific control arrangement, e.g. specific switching systems or two-hand control -- F16P 3/003 covers safety devices that depend on a particular control arrangement, such as dedicated switching circuits or two-hand control schemes, to ensure safe operation of machines. These mechanisms intervene when the control configuration is engaged, preventing accidental activation or hazardous motion.
[F16P 3/005] having a movable screen or a sensor influencing the switching system -- The F16P 3/005 subclass covers safety devices for machines that incorporate a movable screen or a sensor that directly influences the switching system, thereby enabling shutdown or protective action in response to the presence, position, or movement of the screen or detected condition. It is part of the broader group of safety devices used in mechanical engineering to control machine operation.
[F16P 3/006] having a screen moving with the machine and moving the operator out of danger, or restraining the operator, without influencing the switching system -- A safety device that protects the operator by attaching a moving screen to the machine or by restraining the operator, thereby keeping the person out of hazardous zones while the machine runs. The device operates independently of the machine's control or switching system, ensuring safety without influencing its operation.
[F16P 3/008] Devices for directly stopping or interrupting the drive or gear in case of danger to the machine, e.g. devices with clutches -- F16P 3/008 covers devices that directly stop or interrupt the drive or gear of a machine when a danger is detected, such as clutch-based safety mechanisms. These are safety devices integrated with machine control to prevent injury or damage.
[F16P 3/02] Screens or other safety members moving in synchronism with members which move to and fro -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: EP2039960
[F16P 3/04] for machines with parts which approach one another during operation, e.g. for stamping presses -- F16P 3/04 designates safety devices for machines whose components move toward one another during operation, such as stamping presses, aimed at preventing accidents that could arise from the approach or contact of moving parts.
[F16P 3/06] in which body parts of the operator are removed from the danger zone on approach of the machine parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: US2004226424
[F16P 3/08] in connection with the locking of doors, covers, guards, or like members giving access to moving machine parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The activation or desactivation of the machine is linked to the state of the lock which is provided on the doors, covers guards or like members which give access to moving machine parts or by extension to the machine in general.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: EP2289672 if the door 3.1 is closed the machine 1 can be active. If the door 3.1 is opened the machine can not be active.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Safety locks for washing machines -> D06F37/42 - Locks in general -> E05B
[F16P 3/10] in which the operation of locking the door or other member causes the machine to start -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code F16P 3/10 pertains to safety devices that integrate with machine controls, specifically those where locking a door or component automatically initiates machine operation. This classification is part of a broader category covering general engineering elements and units, focusing on mechanical engineering and safety mechanisms.
[F16P 3/12] with means, e.g. feelers, which in case of the presence of a body part of a person in or near the danger zone influence the control or operation of the machine -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: FR2885401  (carpet 34, 36, 38 analysing the pressure exerted by an operator)
[F16P 3/125] Guards for rolls in calendering or other roll machines, e.g. nip guards, finger guards -- The subclass F16P 3/125 covers safety devices that protect the rotating rolls of calendering and similar roll machines. It includes nip guards, finger guards and other guards designed to prevent accidental contact with the roll surfaces during operation.
[F16P 3/14] the means being photocells or other devices sensitive without mechanical contact -- F16P 3/14 covers safety devices that operate in concert with a machine's control system and use non-mechanical sensing, such as photocells or similar contact-free detectors, to detect hazardous conditions and trigger protective actions. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering safety devices that intervene in machine operation without physical contact.
[F16P 3/141] using sound propagation, e.g. sonar -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Safety device using e.g. ultrasound. Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: US5323513
[F16P 3/142] using image capturing devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Using cameras: e.g. EP2282106  See also F16P3/14
[F16P 3/144] using light grids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group  Using a light grid: US2007069114
[F16P 3/145] using magnetic technology -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group  e.g. US2011050256
[F16P 3/147] using electro-magnetic technology, e.g. tags or radar -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group  e.g. WO2009050316
[F16P 3/148] using capacitive technology -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group  Using magnetic or capacitive sensors: e.g.US2011050256
[F16P 3/16] with feeling members moved by the machine -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: WO2010072193, the document shows feelers (20) which are provided on the moving arm parts (14, 16, 18).
[F16P 3/18] Control arrangements requiring the use of both hands -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group: US5396222
[F16P 3/20] for electric control systems -- F16P 3/20 is a CPC subclass that covers safety devices operating in conjunction with a machine's control or operation, specifically those that use electric control systems. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and safety devices in general.
[F16P 3/22] for hydraulic or pneumatic control systems -- The F16P 3/22 subclass concerns safety devices that operate together with hydraulic or pneumatic control systems to protect machines from dangerous conditions. It includes mechanisms that monitor pressure, flow or actuation and intervene-such as by limiting movement or triggering shutdown-when parameters exceed safe limits.
[F16P 3/24] for mechanical controls -- F16P 3/24 pertains to safety devices that are integrated with mechanical controls to influence a machine's operation, such as interlocks, limit switches and other control-actuated safety mechanisms.
[F16P 5/00] Emergency means for rendering ineffective a coupling conveying reciprocating movement if the motion of the driven part is prematurely resisted -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Safety systems which render ineffective a coupling conveying reciprocating movement if the motion of the driven part is prematurely resisted. The safety system is preventing the machine from being damaged.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Safety means disconnecting couplings -> F16D9/00
[F16P 5/005] Overload protection by energy absorbing components, e.g. breaker blocks, shear sections -- F16P 5/005 covers overload protection mechanisms that use energy-absorbing components such as breaker blocks or shear sections to disconnect or damage a reciprocating coupling when the driven part encounters premature resistance. These safety devices are intended to prevent damage by providing a controlled failure path.
[F16P 7/00] Emergency devices preventing damage to a machine or apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Safety systems preventing damages of the machine itself.
[F16P 7/02] by causing the machine to stop on the occurrence of dangerous conditions therein -- F16P 7/02 designates safety devices that automatically halt a machine when dangerous conditions arise, preventing damage or injury. It covers emergency-stop mechanisms that intervene to stop operation as soon as a fault or hazardous situation is detected.
[F16S] CONSTRUCTIONAL ELEMENTS IN GENERAL -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sheets, panels, or other members of similar proportions, without an application covered by any other class; Constructions comprising assemblies of such members, without an application covered by any other class  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Layered products -> B32B - Railway vehicles -> B61 - Motor vehicles -> B62D - Ships or other waterborne vessels -> B63 - Aircrafts; Cosmonautics -> B64 - Structural elements for building construction, e.g. beams, columns, panels for buildings -> E04C - Frames for doors, windows or the like -> E06B - Devices for fastening or securing general constructional elements or machine parts together -> F16B
[F16S 1/00] Sheets, panels, or other members of similar proportions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sheets, panels, or other members of similar proportions, without an application covered by any other class; Constructions comprising assemblies of such members, without an application covered by any other class  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Built-up gratings -> F16S3/00 - Layered products -> B32B
[F16S 1/02] designated for being secured together edge to edge, e.g. at an angle -- F16S 1/02 covers sheets, panels or similar members that are secured together along their edges, often at an angle, using methods such as welding, bolting, riveting or adhesives. The classification applies to mechanical-engineering constructions where the joining runs edge-to-edge to create a continuous structural element.
[F16S 1/04] produced by deforming or otherwise working a flat sheet -- F16S 1/04 classifies mechanical engineering elements that are made by shaping a flat sheet through deformation, such as bending, rolling, stamping or similar working processes, resulting in sheets, panels or members whose final geometry differs from the original flat form.
[F16S 1/06] by deforming only -- F16S 1/06 covers ways of joining or assembling sheets, panels or similarly proportioned members by deforming only, such as bending, folding, crimping or otherwise reshaping the parts to interlock without using adhesives, fasteners or additional material. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering and constructional elements for sheet-type components.
[F16S 1/08] by cutting or perforating, with or without deformation -- F16S 1/08 covers sheet, panel or similar constructional members that are produced by cutting or perforating, optionally combined with deformation processes. It includes methods that shape the material through punches, lasers or other cutting tools, with or without subsequent bending or forming.
[F16S 1/10] Composite members, e.g. with ribs or flanges attached -- The F16S 1/10 subclass covers composite structural members such as sheets, panels, or similar components that incorporate additional ribs, flanges or other reinforcing elements bonded or otherwise attached to the base material. These reinforced composites are used to improve stiffness, strength or load-bearing capacity while maintaining a lightweight form, and are applicable in various mechanical and constructional engineering contexts.
[F16S 1/12] of substantial thickness, e.g. with varying thickness, with channels -- F16S 1/12 relates to sheet-like or panel elements that have a substantial, often non-uniform thickness and include built-in channels or grooves. The classification covers structures where the thickness varies across the member to provide strength, stiffness or fluid passage, typically used in mechanical engineering components such as ducts, stiffeners or load-bearing panels.
[F16S 1/14] Assemblies of such members with members of forms covered by group -- F16S 1/14 relates to mechanical engineering constructions that join sheets, panels or similar members with other components whose shapes fall within the same group defined by the classification. It covers the combined assemblies of these proportionally similar elements, focusing on how they are integrated in a single structure.
[F16S 3/00] Elongated members, e.g. profiled members -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Elongated members, e.g. profiled members, without an application covered by any other class; Assemblies thereof, without an application covered by any other class; Gratings or grilles, without an application covered by any other class  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gratings or grilles formed from a sheet or the like -> F16S1/00 , F16S1/08 - Frames for doors, windows or the like -> E06B1/00 , E06B3/00
[F16S 3/02] composed of two or more elongated members secured together side by side -- F16S 3/02 covers profiles formed by joining two or more elongated members-such as strips, rods or shaped sections-side by side to create a single structural element. The classification includes methods of securing the members together and the resulting composite members used in mechanical engineering.
[F16S 3/04] designed for being joined to similar members in various relative positions -- This subclass covers elongated structural members such as profiles that are specifically shaped or provided with features to allow them to be connected to other similar members in a range of relative orientations, e.g., sliding, pivoting, or angled joints. It pertains to mechanical engineering - constructional elements - elongated members intended for modular assembly or adjustable frameworks.
[F16S 3/06] Assemblies of elongated members -- F16S 3/06 pertains to the design of assemblies formed from two or more elongated members such as profiles, rods, beams or similar structural elements. It includes the methods, joints, connectors and other features used to combine these members into a single functional unit within mechanical engineering.
[F16S 3/08] forming frameworks, e.g. gratings -- F16S 3/08 relates to processes and apparatuses for forming frameworks such as gratings from elongated or profiled members, covering methods of assembling, joining, shaping or otherwise constructing these structural elements. It falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and constructional elements in general.
[F16S 5/00] Other constructional members not restricted to an application fully provided for in a single class -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Other constructional members, without an application covered by any other class and not being either a sheet, a panel, an elongated member or a grating
[F16T] STEAM TRAPS OR LIKE APPARATUS FOR DRAINING-OFF LIQUIDS FROM ENCLOSURES PREDOMINANTLY CONTAINING GASES OR VAPOURS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Steam traps defined by devices or special automatic valves for discharging condensate and non condensable gases from live steam with less possible loss or consumption of live steam. Furthermore not only steam traps are covered but also all kind of apparatus or valves and component parts and accessories thereof for draining-off liquids from enclosures, gas lines, steam lines or containers containing gases or vapours. Said valves are controlled thermally (thermostatically, bi-metallically driven valves), by excess or release of pressure (thermodynamically driven valves), by floats (mechanically driven valves) or controlled without moving parts (Venturi-type or labyrinth-type).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Valves for removing gases or air from condensate -> B01D5/0069 - Degasification of liquids -> B01D19/00 - Treatment of water, waste water or sewage by degassing -> C02F1/20
[F16T 1/00] Steam traps or like apparatus for draining-off liquids from enclosures predominantly containing gases or vapours, e.g. gas lines, steam lines, containers -- F16T 1/00 classifies steam traps and similar devices designed to discharge liquids from enclosures that mainly contain gases or vapours, such as steam lines, gas pipelines or storage vessels. The entry covers mechanisms for draining condensate or other liquids while maintaining the flow of the primary gas or vapour.
[F16T 1/02] with valves controlled thermally -- F16T 1/02 covers steam-trap or similar drainage devices fitted with valves that open or close in response to temperature changes. These apparatuses are used to remove condensate or other liquids from pipelines, containers or other enclosures that primarily convey gases or vapours.
[F16T 1/04] by expansion rods -- F16T 1/04 covers steam-trap or similar draining devices that operate by means of expansion rods. The rod expands with temperature or pressure changes, moving a valve to discharge condensate while preventing loss of vapor in gas or steam lines, containers or other enclosures. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering elements for controlling liquids in predominantly gaseous or vapor-filled systems.
[F16T 1/06] by expansion tubes -- F16T 1/06 covers steam traps or similar devices that use expansion tubes to separate and discharge condensate from pipelines or vessels that carry primarily gases or vapours. The expansion tube expands when heated, creating a pressure change that opens the trap and allows liquid to flow out while preventing gas loss.
[F16T 1/08] by bimetallic strips or plates -- Steam traps that control the discharge of condensate from systems containing gases or vapours using the thermal expansion properties of bimetallic strips or plates, which bend as temperature changes to open or close the flow path. This mechanism enables automatic drainage without external power or manual adjustment. It is classified under F16T 1/08 within mechanical engineering for steam-trap apparatus.
[F16T 1/10] by thermally-expansible liquids -- The F16T 1/10 subclass covers steam traps or similar devices that discharge liquids from gas- or vapour-filled enclosures by using liquids that expand when heated. These thermally-expansible-liquid traps open or close in response to temperature changes to remove condensate from steam or gas lines.
[F16T 1/12] with valves controlled by excess or release of pressure -- Steam traps or similar devices that discharge condensate from pipelines or vessels mainly containing steam or other gases, in which the trapping valve is actuated by excess pressure of the fluid or by a pressure-release mechanism.
[F16T 1/14] involving a piston, diaphragm, or bellows, e.g. displaceable under pressure of incoming condensate -- F16T 1/14 relates to steam traps or similar drainage devices that employ a piston, diaphragm or bellows which move under the pressure of incoming condensate, enabling liquids to be expelled while gases or vapours remain sealed within the enclosure.
[F16T 1/16] involving a high-pressure chamber and a low-pressure chamber communicating with one another, i.e. thermodynamic steam chambers -- F16T 1/16 covers steam traps or similar devices that use a high-pressure chamber linked to a low-pressure chamber to discharge condensate while retaining the main gas or vapor flow. The pressure differential opens a valve or passage for liquid removal and closes when only gas remains.
[F16T 1/165] of disc type -- F16T 1/165 covers disc-type steam traps used to discharge condensate or other liquids from pipes, vessels or other enclosures that mainly contain gases or vapours such as steam or gas lines. The device relies on a flexible disc that opens under pressure differentials to allow liquid flow while retaining the gas phase.
[F16T 1/18] involving a vacuum chamber -- F16T 1/18 covers steam traps or similar devices that discharge liquids from enclosures primarily containing gases or vapours, where the operation involves a vacuum chamber. The apparatus are used in gas or steam lines, containers and related systems to remove condensate while preserving the vacuum environment.
[F16T 1/20] with valves controlled by floats -- F16T 1/20 covers steam traps or similar liquid-draining devices in gas or vapor lines that use a float-controlled valve; the valve opens when the float rises with accumulating liquid and closes as the level falls, allowing automatic removal of condensate without manual intervention. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering elements for draining liquids from enclosures predominantly containing gases or vapours.
[F16T 1/22] of closed-hollow-body type -- Steam traps of the closed-hollow-body type are devices that collect and discharge condensate or other liquids from pipelines, vessels or other enclosures that mainly contain gases or vapours, such as steam or gas lines. They operate by allowing liquids to flow into a sealed hollow chamber where pressure differences cause the liquid to be expelled while preventing gas back-flow.
[F16T 1/24] using levers -- F16T 1/24 pertains to steam traps or comparable devices that remove condensate from gas- or vapour-filled enclosures, where the trap's operation is controlled by a lever mechanism. It falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and specifically addresses lever-driven drainage of liquids.
[F16T 1/26] of upright-open-bucket type -- This subclass covers steam traps of upright open-bucket design, used to discharge condensate from pipelines or vessels that mainly contain steam or other vapors. The bucket is positioned vertically and opens upward, allowing liquid to collect and be released when pressure conditions change. It is applied in mechanical engineering for draining liquids from gas-filled enclosures.
[F16T 1/28] using levers -- F16T 1/28 covers steam-trap or similar devices that use lever mechanisms to open, close or regulate the valve that drains liquids from enclosures containing mainly gases or vapours such as steam or gas lines. The lever provides mechanical advantage for actuation, allowing precise control of flow and reliable shut-off under varying pressure conditions.
[F16T 1/30] of inverted-open-bucket type -- The F16T 1/30 subclass covers steam-trap devices that operate by an inverted open-bucket mechanism, where a bucket is suspended upside down and opens to allow condensate to drain while retaining the gas or vapour flow. These traps are used to remove liquids from pipelines, tanks or other enclosures that primarily contain gases or steam.
[F16T 1/305] using levers -- F16T 1/305 refers to steam traps or similar devices that remove condensate from pipelines or vessels containing gases or vapours, specifically focusing on designs that employ levers to control the opening, closing or flow-regulating actions of the trap.
[F16T 1/32] of rocking or tilting type -- F16T 1/32 designates steam traps or comparable devices of the rocking or tilting type that discharge condensate or other liquids from pipelines, containers or other enclosures in which gases or vapours predominate, such as steam or gas lines. This subclass falls under mechanical engineering, specifically engineering elements and units for draining-off liquids from gas-filled systems.
[F16T 1/34] without moving parts other than hand valves, e.g. labyrinth type -- F16T 1/34 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers steam traps or similar devices for draining liquids from gas or vapor enclosures that operate without moving parts except for hand valves, such as those of a labyrinth design.
[F16T 1/36] specially adapted for steam lines of low pressure -- F16T 1/36 covers steam traps and similar devices that are specifically designed for use in low-pressure steam lines. These apparatuses are intended to remove condensate or other liquids from pipelines that mainly carry gases or vapours, ensuring efficient flow and preventing water hammer. The classification falls under mechanical engineering, engineering elements and units, and steam-trap technology.
[F16T 1/38] Component parts -- F16T 1/38 covers the individual component parts used in steam traps or similar devices that remove condensate from enclosures mainly containing gases or vapours, such as pipelines, containers or steam lines. The classification pertains to the elements that make up these drainage systems.
[F16T 1/383] Valve closing members or valve seats -- F16T 1/383 covers valve closing members or valve seats used in steam traps or similar devices that remove liquids from vessels primarily containing gases or vapours. It falls under the mechanical engineering subclass for engineering elements and units related to steam trap technology. The category includes designs for sealing components that control flow in gas or steam line applications.
[F16T 1/386] Actuating mechanisms for lift valves -- F16T 1/386 covers the mechanisms that operate lift valves in steam traps or similar devices used to discharge liquids from chambers mainly containing gases or vapours. The entry includes mechanical, pneumatic, hydraulic, magnetic or electronic actuators that raise or lower the valve to regulate condensate flow and prevent back-pressure.
[F16T 1/40] Actuating mechanisms of ball valves -- F16T 1/40 covers the actuating mechanisms that operate ball valves used in steam traps or similar devices for draining liquids from vessels that mainly contain gases or vapours. It includes manual, pneumatic, hydraulic, electric and other drive systems that open, close or modulate the ball valve to control flow.
[F16T 1/42] Actuating mechanisms of slide valves -- F16T 1/42 covers the devices that move or control slide valves used in steam traps or similar liquid-draining apparatus. It includes hydraulic, pneumatic, mechanical or electromagnetic actuators that open, close or modulate the valve position to regulate flow of condensate from gas or vapor lines.
[F16T 1/45] Means for venting or aerating -- F16T 1/45 covers devices integrated in steam-trap or similar drainage apparatus that provide venting or aeration to remove non-condensable gases and improve liquid discharge from enclosures mainly containing gases or vapours. These means may include aerators, vents, or air-inlet passages that assist in draining-off liquids from gas lines, steam lines, or containers.
[F16T 1/48] Monitoring arrangements for inspecting, e.g. flow of steam and steam condensate -- F16T 1/48 covers devices and systems that monitor the operation of steam-trap or similar apparatus, detecting and inspecting the flow of steam and its condensate within gas- or vapor-filled enclosures such as pipelines and containers. It belongs to the mechanical-engineering subclass for steam-trap drainage units.
[F17] STORING OR DISTRIBUTING GASES OR LIQUIDS -- F17 addresses inventions concerning apparatus, methods and systems for storing or distributing gases or liquids, including tanks, containers, pipelines, valves, pumps and related control devices used in mechanical engineering.
[F17B] GAS-HOLDERS OF VARIABLE CAPACITY -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gas-holders for the storage of compressed, eventually liquefied or solidified gas, with large volume, and whose volume capacity can be changed by special means. Storage of gas is the only purpose of gas-holders.  Warning: This subclass is pending a reorganisation. Documents are continuously reclassified to subclass F17B and deep indexing scheme F17C .  **Glossary:** - Gas: Substance that is in gaseous state of matter at normal conditions (atmospheric pressure and normal ambient temperature). Substances that are in a gaseous state of matter but would not be gaseous at normal conditions are not considered in this subclass. Gas can be put in liquid (liquefied gas), or solidified state of matter, for storage.
[F17B 1/00] Gas-holders of variable capacity -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gas-holders for the storage of compressed, eventually liquefied or solidified gas, with large volume, and whose volume capacity can be changed by special means. Storage of gas is the only purpose of gas-holders.
[F17B 1/007] with telescopically movable ring-shaped parts -- F17B 1/007 covers gas-holder designs whose capacity is varied by means of telescopically movable ring-shaped sections that slide over one another to expand or contract the storage vessel, a common approach for variable-capacity gas tanks.
[F17B 1/013] with movables discs -- F17B 1/013 covers gas-holders of variable capacity that employ one or more movable disc elements to regulate the internal volume. The discs act as flexible partition plates, sliding or rotating to accommodate pressure changes and to maintain a seal as the holder expands or contracts.
[F17B 1/02] Details -- F17B 1/02 pertains to the detailed aspects and components of gas-holders with variable capacity, focusing on specific features that enable their expansion and contraction. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with mechanical engineering, specifically storing or distributing gases or liquids.
[F17B 1/04] Sealing devices for sliding parts -- F17B 1/04 covers sealing devices for sliding parts in gas-holders of variable capacity, such as piston rings, packed glands and other dynamic seals that maintain pressure while allowing movement.
[F17B 1/06] using sealing liquids -- The subclass concerns gas-holders whose capacity can vary and that employ sealing liquids to form a fluid barrier around moving walls or pistons, preventing gas escape while allowing the vessel to expand or contract.
[F17B 1/08] using resilient materials for packing, e.g. leather -- F17B 1/08 pertains to gas-holders of variable capacity that use resilient packing materials, such as leather, to provide a seal within the chamber. It is a subclass of mechanical engineering for storing or distributing gases or liquids, specifically focusing on flexible-capacity vessels.
[F17B 1/10] Guiding moving parts -- F17B 1/10 concerns devices that guide the movement of components inside gas-holders of variable capacity, such as guide rails, rollers, bearings or other mechanisms that keep lifting or floating parts aligned while the holder expands or contracts during filling and emptying.
[F17B 1/12] Gas admission or discharge arrangements -- F17B 1/12 covers arrangements for admitting or discharging gas in containers whose capacity can vary, such as pressure vessels, flexible bags or expandable tanks, including associated valves, manifolds and control devices that regulate flow during filling or emptying. This subclass is used for mechanical engineering solutions that manage gas storage and distribution in variable-capacity holders.
[F17B 1/14] Safety devices, e.g. prevention of excess pressure -- F17B 1/14 covers safety devices for gas-holders of variable capacity, such as pressure-relief valves, burst disks or other mechanisms that prevent excess pressure buildup in flexible or expandable storage vessels. The classification applies to equipment designed to protect the holder and surrounding system from over-pressurisation failures.
[F17B 1/16] of wet type -- F17B 1/16 refers to gas-holders of variable capacity that are of the wet type, typically involving a water seal or other liquid for maintaining an airtight enclosure as the volume changes.
[F17B 1/18] bell-shaped -- A bell-shaped gas-holder is a variable-capacity storage unit where a flexible, bell-like bladder or membrane is suspended within a sealed chamber or tank. As gas is introduced, the bell expands upward and outward, increasing the storage volume while the surrounding water or liquid provides a seal and structural support. This design is used to accommodate fluctuating gas quantities without requiring a rigid container.
[F17B 1/20] telescopic -- F17B 1/20 denotes telescopic gas-holders of variable capacity, where an inner cylindrical shell moves telescopically within an outer shell to change the volume of stored gas, allowing the vessel to accommodate pressure-induced expansion or contraction in mechanical engineering applications.
[F17B 1/22] spirally-guided -- This subclass covers gas holders whose pistons or diaphragms move in a spiral guide to maintain a seal while the volume changes. The spiral guide directs the vertical motion and supports the flexible enclosure, allowing efficient filling and emptying of the container.
[F17B 1/24] of dry type -- F17B 1/24 classifies dry-type gas-holders of variable capacity, where the storage volume changes by moving containers, pistons or separators rather than a water-filled cushion. These devices store gases such as compressed air or natural gas and adjust their internal volume through mechanical expansion and contraction without liquid sealing.
[F17B 1/26] with flexible walls, e.g. bellows -- This subclass refers to variable-capacity gas-holders that achieve volume change by means of flexible walls such as bellows. It includes devices where the containment envelope expands or contracts to store or discharge gas, typically used in compressors, pressure regulators, or safety vessels.
[F17C] VESSELS FOR CONTAINING OR STORING COMPRESSED, LIQUEFIED OR SOLIDIFIED GASES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Groups in the 2000-range:  The groups F17C2201/00 - F17C2209/00 cover features of the pressure vessel itself:  The groups F17C2221/00 - F17C2227/00 consider the fluid contained in the pressure vessel and its charging and discharging into and from the pressure vessel.  Layout of definition statements:  "Positive" examples have been chosen among patents to illustrate the definition statement and what is to be classified in the groups of the 2000 range. The qualifying features are marked in green in these drawings.  To illustrate the boundaries of the definition statement, "negative" examples have been chosen among patents to illustrate what would not lead to or even disqualify from a classification in a group. The corresponding features are marked in red in these drawings.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Storing fluids in natural or artificial cavities or chambers in the earth -> B65G5/00 - Variable-capacity gas holders -> F17B  **Glossary:** - Gas: substance that is in gaseous state of matter at normal conditions (atmospheric pressure and normal ambient temperature. Substances that are in a gaseous state of matter but would not be gaseous at normal conditions are not considered in this subclass. Gas can be put in liquid (liquefied gas), or solidified state of matter, for transport and storage - Pressure vessel: container containing gas for storage and/or transport - Fluid or handled fluid: fluid contained in, charged into or discharged out of the pressure vessel
[F17C 1/00] Pressure vessels, e.g. gas cylinder, gas tank, replaceable cartridge -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional aspects of vessel for containing gas under high pressure.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Autoclaves -> B01J3/04
[F17C 1/002] Storage in barges or on ships -- The subclass deals with storing compressed, liquefied or solidified gases in maritime containers such as barges, tank ships or other floating vessels, covering the design and arrangement of pressure-resistant storage tanks, safety systems and handling equipment needed for shipboard gas storage. It is part of the broader group on vessels for containing compressed gases within mechanical engineering.
[F17C 1/005] Storage of gas or gaseous mixture at high pressure and at high density condition, e.g. in the single state phase -- This subclass concerns devices and methods for storing gases or gas mixtures at pressures and densities high enough to keep the material in a single phase, such as compressed-gas cylinders or high-pressure tanks. It addresses the design and safety features required for such high-pressure containment.
[F17C 1/007] Underground or underwater storage -- F17C 1/007 covers vessels for storing compressed, liquefied or solidified gases that are placed underground or underwater, such as subterranean gas tanks, underwater cylinders and replaceable cartridges designed for sub-surface or sub-aquatic environments. This subgroup falls under mechanical engineering, storing or distributing gases or liquids, and specifically addresses pressure vessels used in these hidden or submerged locations.
[F17C 1/02] involving reinforcing arrangements -- The subclass F17C 1/02 concerns pressure vessels such as gas cylinders, tanks or replaceable cartridges that incorporate reinforcement arrangements-ribs, rings, hoops or external supports-to increase structural strength and safety when containing compressed, liquefied or solidified gases.
[F17C 1/04] Protecting sheathings -- The entry covers protective sheathings applied to pressure vessels such as gas cylinders or tanks, including sleeves, jackets, coatings or other layers that shield the vessel wall from corrosion, mechanical damage, temperature extremes or chemical attack, thereby enhancing safety and durability.
[F17C 1/06] built-up from wound-on bands or filamentary material, e.g. wires -- F17C 1/06 refers to pressure vessels that are built up by winding one or more bands, strips or filamentary materials such as wires around a central core, then joining the layers to form a stiff, gas-tight container. This construction method is typical for gas cylinders, high-pressure tanks and replaceable cartridges.
[F17C 1/08] Integral reinforcements, e.g. ribs -- This classification covers pressure vessels such as gas cylinders that incorporate integral structural reinforcements like ribs to increase strength and resist internal pressure. It applies to designs where the reinforcement is built into the vessel wall rather than added as a separate component.
